query: What is the main reason for Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein's meeting with President Trump on Thursday?

documents.text:
**Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein to Meet President Trump Amidst Speculation Over Future**

Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein is scheduled to meet with President Donald Trump on Thursday, White House press secretary Sarah Sanders announced Monday. This meeting comes at a critical juncture, with Rosenstein's position in jeopardy following reports that he discussed wearing a wire to record President Trump and explored invoking the 25th Amendment to remove him from office.

"We'll be determining what's going on," President Trump stated Monday afternoon before a meeting with South Korean President Moon Jae-in in New York. He emphasized a desire for "transparency" and "openness," adding, "I look forward to meeting with him." Trump also confirmed he had spoken with Rosenstein earlier that day.

Rosenstein had been anticipated to learn his fate during a White House visit on Monday. However, with President Trump in New York for the United Nations General Assembly, any decision regarding his future was postponed. Rosenstein attended a routine meeting at the White House and remained Deputy Attorney General upon his departure at midday.

The subject of Rosenstein's potential departure was discussed on Saturday with White House Counsel Don McGahn, according to NBC News' Pete Williams. It remains unclear who initiated this conversation. Sources familiar with the discussion indicate that Rosenstein made it clear he would not accept termination by Chief of Staff John Kelly, insisting that any such action must come directly from the President.

Despite this, multiple sources informed NBC News that President Trump had, over the weekend, decided not to fire Rosenstein after consulting with staff and outside allies. Among those advising the President on this matter were Fox News host Sean Hannity and Representative Mark Meadows (R-N.C.). Meadows has expressed a desire for Rosenstein to testify under oath regarding whether he ever considered wearing a wire in President Trump's presence or discussed a plan to garner support from other Cabinet members to invoke the 25th Amendment for the President's removal.

A source close to the White House described the current atmosphere among the President's allies as "chaos." Another individual close to the President suggested that if Rosenstein were to leave, regardless of the circumstances, "then the burden's on President Trump to make sure that Mueller is allowed to finish his job without interference."

Sources close to Rosenstein report that he spoke with friends and former colleagues over the weekend, raising the possibility of his resignation in the wake of a recent news story. One individual stated that Rosenstein felt personally betrayed by the story's sources, describing it as a "knife in the back." Another source conveyed that Rosenstein continued to defend himself, calling the allegation that he suggested using the 25th Amendment against the President "absurd."

Rosenstein's tenure became precarious after reports surfaced last week detailing discussions about wearing a wire to record Trump and exploring the possibility of initiating a process to remove the President via the 25th Amendment.

"At the request of Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, he and President Trump had an extended conversation to discuss the recent news stories," Sanders stated. "They will meet on Thursday when the president returns to Washington, D.C."

Thursday also marks the scheduled testimony of Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh and Christine Blasey Ford before the Senate Judiciary Committee. Ford has accused Kavanaugh of sexual assault during their high school years, an allegation Kavanaugh denies.

Rosenstein's departure could cast uncertainty over the future of Special Counsel Robert Mueller's Russia investigation, including the eventual delivery and public release of his report to Congress. President Trump has expressed long-standing frustration with Rosenstein concerning the investigation and the Justice Department's perceived inaction in investigating officials whom the President believes conspired against him.

Jay Sekulow, one of President Trump's personal lawyers, commented on his radio show Monday that Rosenstein's ouster would necessitate a re-examination of the special counsel's probe. "I think it's really important that there be a step back taken here, and a review, and I think it's a review that has to be thorough and complete, and a review that has to include an investigation of what has transpired," Sekulow said, referring to such a reassessment as "a timeout on this inquiry." He suggested that a new deputy attorney general should "look at all of these allegations that are both surrounding this inquiry, and that initiated this inquiry, including the Christopher Steele dossier, and the appointment of the special counsel, and how all that plays in."

However, the discussions about a wire and the 25th Amendment, initially reported by The New York Times, appear to be the primary catalyst for President Trump to consider forcing Rosenstein out. Rosenstein stated on Friday that there is "no basis" for deeming the President unfit to serve and that he never pursued any effort to record Trump. Two administration officials told NBC News on Friday that Rosenstein had discussed wearing a wire sarcastically. Conversely, individuals who have spoken with then-Acting FBI Director Andrew McCabe, who was present at the meeting in question with Rosenstein, recall the Deputy Attorney General being serious about surreptitiously taping conversations with Trump.

In an interview with Geraldo Rivera, taped over the weekend, President Trump indicated he was considering removing Rosenstein. "Certainly it's being looked at in terms of what took place, if anything took place," Trump said. "I’ll make a determination sometime later but I don’t have the facts."

Democrats were swift to advocate for congressional intervention to protect Mueller from presidential interference. "Congress must take immediate steps to uphold the Constitution and the rule of law — which protects all of us — by shielding the Mueller investigation against President Trump’s obstruction," stated Representative Val Demings (D-Fla.) in a release prior to the announcement of the Thursday meeting.

Andrew McCabe, who was dismissed by Attorney General Jeff Sessions, echoed this sentiment. "There is nothing more important to the integrity of law enforcement and the rule of law than protecting the investigation of Special Counsel Mueller," he said in a statement. "I sacrificed personally and professionally to help put the investigation on a proper course and subsequently made every effort to protect it. ... If the rumors of Deputy AG's Rosenstein’s departure are true, I am deeply concerned that it puts that investigation at risk.”

Secretary of State Mike Pompeo offered "no comment" when questioned about Rosenstein on Monday. Mueller's office also declined to comment on reports regarding Rosenstein's potential departure.

**Correction: Initial Report Deemed Inaccurate; No Significant Developments**

WASHINGTON D.C. – This publication is retracting a recent news alert that suggested imminent significant developments. Subsequent information from our sources indicates that the matter does not warrant further attention at this time. We acknowledge that the early spread of this news may have caused undue anticipation.

Our editorial team regrets this oversight and any inconvenience. Further updates will be provided only upon confirmation of solid, verifiable facts.

Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein is set to meet with President Trump on Thursday. This meeting follows recent reports suggesting Rosenstein's departure from the Justice Department might be near.

The White House confirmed the meeting, stating it was "at the request of Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein." They added that he and President Trump would have a conversation to discuss recent news stories. Press Secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders explained that due to the President's busy schedule with world leaders at the United Nations General Assembly, the discussion would happen after his return to Washington, D.C.

Reports about Rosenstein's future have been mixed.

*   Axios reported Monday, citing sources, that Rosenstein was resigning.
*   However, other news outlets challenged this. NBC News' Pete Williams reported that Rosenstein would not step down willingly. This was especially true after his comments last week about possibly recording and removing President Trump were revealed. Williams suggested Rosenstein would only leave if dismissed by the White House and would refuse to resign if asked.

The news of Rosenstein's potential exit reportedly surprised many at the Justice Department. Even with President Trump's public criticisms of Rosenstein regarding the Russia investigation, his departure could create a significant challenge for the administration, especially with the midterm elections only six weeks away.

If Rosenstein were to leave, Solicitor General Noel Francisco would then oversee the Russia investigation, according to a Justice Department official who spoke with The New York Times. Rosenstein was reportedly at the White House for a scheduled principals meeting shortly after the resignation rumors began, as confirmed by a Justice Department official to NBC. President Trump was in New York City for the United Nations General Assembly, with a dinner planned with Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe on Sunday evening.

Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein was scheduled to meet with President Donald Trump at the White House on Thursday, following a significant discussion on Monday. This meeting occurs amid growing speculation about Rosenstein's future in his role. The timing is particularly noteworthy as it coincides with Brett Kavanaugh's Supreme Court nomination hearing, where he faces questions about sexual assault allegations.

White House spokeswoman Sarah Huckabee Sanders confirmed an "extended conversation" between Trump and Rosenstein on Monday, which Rosenstein initiated to address recent news. Rosenstein, who oversees Special Counsel Robert Mueller's Russia investigation, had reportedly expected to be fired on Monday due to critical remarks he allegedly made about the President. The meeting was postponed to Thursday because President Trump was attending the United Nations General Assembly in New York.

Concerns have been raised about the integrity of the investigation should Rosenstein depart. Former Acting FBI Director Andrew McCabe expressed worries that Rosenstein's exit could jeopardize the special counsel's Russia probe. The Associated Press has confirmed Rosenstein's expectation of termination upon his arrival at the White House. As the official overseeing the inquiry into Russian election interference, Rosenstein has been a key figure in defending the investigation. This situation follows reports suggesting Rosenstein considered secretly recording President Trump after FBI Director James Comey's dismissal and contemplated invoking the 25th Amendment to remove the president. McCabe has stated he was not involved in providing information for these reports.

Rosenstein has denied the allegations. Earlier reports, citing an anonymous source, indicated Rosenstein anticipated his dismissal due to critical comments he allegedly made about President Trump. These reports followed accounts alleging Rosenstein proposed secretly recording Trump and discussed using the 25th Amendment to deem him unfit for office. President Trump has stated he is evaluating the situation and has not yet made a final decision on Rosenstein's future. The New York Times initially reported on Rosenstein's alleged proposals. Trump has attributed blame for the controversy to then-Attorney General Jeff Sessions. Rosenstein has repeatedly denied the accuracy of The Times' report.

Speculation swirled Monday regarding Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein's potential dismissal by President Donald Trump, following reports of his impending visit to the White House. This significant development, corroborated by an individual privy to the matter speaking with The Associated Press, unfolded as President Trump was attending a United Nations General Assembly gathering in New York.

The backdrop to Rosenstein's reported White House trip involved recent disclosures suggesting he had contemplated highly unusual actions. These alleged considerations included the possibility of secretly recording President Trump and investigating the constitutional pathways that might facilitate his own removal from office through the Cabinet.

The departure of Rosenstein, whether by termination or voluntary resignation, would undoubtedly cast a long shadow over Special Counsel Robert Mueller's continuing inquiry into alleged Russian interference during the 2016 presidential election. As the individual who appointed Special Counsel Mueller, Rosenstein currently exercises direct oversight of this pivotal investigation.

This unfolding scenario bears a striking resemblance to earlier reports from 2018. During that period, President Trump was also reportedly considering the dismissal of Rosenstein. This earlier contemplation followed the FBI's execution of search warrants at the residences of Michael Cohen, who was then serving as the president's personal attorney. Cohen has since been convicted on various charges and has provided testimony to Mueller's investigative team.

The present scrutiny of Rosenstein appears intrinsically linked to a New York Times report that brought to light comments he allegedly made in early 2017. The public revelation of these remarks, occurring in conjunction with the publication of an anonymous op-ed authored by a high-ranking administration official, served to amplify President Trump's pre-existing anxieties about a clandestine "Deep State" apparatus actively working to undermine his presidency. The anonymous author, whom President Trump publicly called for an investigation into, described a group of officials endeavoring to protect the nation from what they perceived as the president's unpredictable conduct. Furthermore, the op-ed hinted at internal discussions within the Cabinet concerning the potential invocation of the 25th Amendment to remove Trump from office. This course of action, however, was ultimately set aside, in part due to the significant risk of precipitating a "constitutional crisis."

President Donald Trump stated his anticipation for a Thursday meeting with Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein to address Rosenstein's future. This comes after reports indicated Rosenstein informed Chief of Staff John Kelly of his intention to resign. "We will be determining what's going on," Trump told reporters at the United Nations on Monday, emphasizing a desire for "transparency" and "openness."

Rosenstein's tenure and future plans were subject to considerable uncertainty following allegations that he had suggested to colleagues the previous year that he would secretly record conversations with President Trump. Sources familiar with the matter indicated that Kelly and Rosenstein had discussed the prosecutor's resignation late last week, with the White House initially accepting it. One report suggested Rosenstein was expected to depart by the end of Monday, though later accounts confirmed he remained Deputy Attorney General without a formal resignation being submitted.

Trump confirmed his conversation with Rosenstein, stating, "I spoke with Rod today and we’re going to have a meeting on Thursday when I get back to the White House." Rosenstein, who oversees the Russia probe led by Special Counsel Robert Mueller, had a previously scheduled White House visit on Monday.

The controversy intensified with reports that Rosenstein had contemplated covertly taping the President. While one individual present at the alleged meeting described it as a joke, the New York Times cited secondhand accounts suggesting Rosenstein was serious about the proposal. The Times also reported Rosenstein discussed identifying cabinet members willing to invoke the 25th Amendment, which allows for the removal of a president deemed unfit for office.

Rosenstein's potential departure carries significant implications for the Russia investigation. His successor would possess the authority to dismiss Mueller or curtail the probe. Axios initially reported Rosenstein's resignation.

Democratic Senator Kirsten Gillibrand urged immediate action to protect the Mueller investigation, tweeting, "The Senate must step up to protect the Special Counsel immediately. We must pass the bipartisan bill to protect the Mueller investigation. The American people deserve answers about Russian interference in our democracy."

Numerous current and former officials, including lawmakers, had previously cautioned Trump against removing Rosenstein. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer warned against using the taping report as a pretext to replace Rosenstein with someone who would allow presidential interference in the Special Counsel's investigation. He noted that many White House and cabinet officials have made critical remarks about the president without facing dismissal.

Several Republicans also expressed support for Rosenstein. Senator John Cornyn, the second-ranking Senate Republican, acknowledged Rosenstein's competent performance in a challenging role, though he deferred to the President's decision. He also indicated that Senate confirmation of a successor could be "problematic." Senator Orrin Hatch of Utah, a member of the Judiciary Committee, stated his personal regard for Rosenstein and belief that the President also held him in high esteem.

The Mueller inquiry has indicted 25 Russian individuals and entities for election interference and secured guilty pleas and cooperation agreements from individuals associated with Trump, including Paul Manafort and Michael Flynn. Trump has consistently characterized the investigation into potential collusion with Russia and obstruction of justice as a "witch hunt."

Rosenstein appointed Mueller as special counsel shortly after assuming oversight of the Russia probe, which he inherited when Attorney General Jeff Sessions recused himself. Trump has publicly criticized Sessions for this recusal.

The President has the option to appoint an interim Deputy Attorney General while nominating a successor requiring Senate confirmation. Alternatively, the Justice Department's line of succession could place Solicitor General Noel Francisco in charge. Concerns have been raised regarding Francisco's prior affiliation with Jones Day, a law firm that has represented Trump. Francisco has a record of defending Trump administration policies and advancing conservative legal objectives.

Rosenstein, 53, was appointed to his position by Trump last year. He previously served as U.S. Attorney for the District of Maryland for 12 years across the administrations of Presidents George W. Bush and Barack Obama. Having joined the Justice Department in 1990, Rosenstein is recognized for his contributions to reshaping departmental priorities.

In May, Rosenstein publicly opposed Republican lawmakers who sought his impeachment for withholding Justice Department documents related to the Russia probe's origins. He stated, "There are people who have been making threats, privately and publicly, against me for quite some time. I think they should understand by now the Department of Justice is not going to be extorted. We’re going to do what’s required by the rule of law."

Republican Representative Jim Jordan, a vocal critic of Rosenstein and the Russia probe, asserted that the Deputy Attorney General must testify before the House Judiciary Committee, regardless of his employment status. "You can’t have the head of the Justice Department (even if it's sarcasm) talking to subordinates about recording the Commander in Chief," Jordan tweeted. "He needs to answer our questions."

Conversely, Rosenstein also drew criticism from some of Trump's opponents in 2017 for his letter detailing the case for firing then-FBI Director James Comey, citing "serious mistakes" in the Hillary Clinton email server investigation. Trump cited this letter in Comey's dismissal, though he later attributed it to the Russia investigation. Trump's frustration with Mueller's investigation and Rosenstein's supervision reportedly led to discussions about Rosenstein's dismissal in April.

Trump and some Republican lawmakers have argued that the Mueller inquiry is fundamentally flawed due to alleged improprieties early in its inception, predating Mueller's appointment. Rosenstein appointed Mueller as special counsel following Comey's termination. Rosenstein assumed control of the investigation after Attorney General Sessions, an early Trump campaign supporter, recused himself from matters pertaining to the 2016 election, a decision Trump has openly disparaged. "I don’t have an attorney general. It’s very sad," Trump remarked in a Hill.TV interview aired on September 19.

Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein's position was the subject of intense speculation and media focus. Senator Susan Collins of Maine voiced her concerns on Twitter, noting Mr. Rosenstein's "critical role" in overseeing the Russia investigation.

President Trump's attorney, Jay Sekulow, acknowledged the widespread uncertainty about Mr. Rosenstein's potential dismissal. Sekulow used this ambiguity to argue for a temporary pause in the Russia investigation, suggesting that Mr. Rosenstein's resignation would necessitate a "time out on this inquiry."

However, White House reports indicated Mr. Rosenstein had attended a scheduled meeting with senior administration officials, suggesting he had neither resigned nor been terminated. Attorney General Jeff Sessions' return to the Justice Department further supported this emerging clarity, implying Mr. Rosenstein's position was secure.

Speculation about Mr. Rosenstein's status continued until 12:48 p.m. At that time, White House Press Secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders issued a statement via Twitter. She announced that Mr. Rosenstein had requested a meeting with the President, which was rescheduled for Thursday due to the President's participation in the United Nations General Assembly.

Within an hour of the press secretary's announcement, Mr. Rosenstein departed the White House. News footage showed White House Chief of Staff John Kelly escorting him to his vehicle. His motorcade then promptly returned him to the Justice Department, where the deputy attorney general resumed his planned engagements.

The prospect of Mr. Rosenstein remaining in his post for an additional 72 hours was unlikely to definitively resolve the situation. A departure this week would have heightened administrative instability, especially with the midterm elections approaching.

Deputy Attorney General Rod J. Rosenstein's tenure at the Justice Department has been a focal point of intense speculation, particularly concerning his oversight of the Special Counsel Robert S. Mueller III's Russia investigation. Widespread rumors of his imminent resignation circulated, fueled by over a year of criticism from President Trump and his allies. These criticisms stemmed from the ongoing probe into Russian interference in the 2016 election and potential collusion with Trump associates.

Rosenstein's position appeared particularly precarious following reports that he had suggested secretly recording President Trump and initiating a Cabinet effort to remove him from office. While Rosenstein has disputed these accounts, his denials have left room for interpretation. These reports emerged from memos written by former Deputy FBI Director Andrew McCabe, detailing discussions from May 2017, a stressful period following James B. Comey's firing as FBI director. McCabe's memos alleged that Rosenstein proposed secretly recording President Trump and initiating a 25th Amendment process to remove him. Other participants in those discussions characterized Rosenstein's remarks about recording Trump as sarcastic, made during a period when McCabe was urging the Justice Department to investigate Comey's firing for potential obstruction of justice. In statements released on Friday, Rosenstein denied ever seriously considering secretly recording Trump or pursuing the 25th Amendment, stating, "The New York Times’s story is inaccurate and factually incorrect." He further asserted, "Based on my personal dealings with the president, there is no basis to invoke the 25th Amendment." A subsequent statement clarified, "I never pursued or authorized recording the president and any suggestion that I have ever advocated for the removal of the President is absolutely false." This second statement followed a tense meeting with White House Chief of Staff John F. Kelly, who reportedly urged Rosenstein to issue a stronger denial. However, some officials perceived the second statement as weaker, potentially placing Rosenstein in a more vulnerable position.

Throughout the weekend, conversations between White House and Justice Department officials indicated that Rosenstein was contemplating resignation, with some individuals suggesting his departure might be necessary to quell the controversy. Certain Trump aides advised the President against a hasty dismissal, arguing it could be politically exploited by Democrats and better handled after the midterm elections. Rosenstein reportedly spoke with White House Counsel Donald McGahn on Friday evening, expressing his willingness to resign. McGahn, however, urged him to postpone any decision until further discussions could take place on Monday. By Sunday, some senior Justice Department officials had concluded Rosenstein's departure was imminent and began contingency planning, with Solicitor General Noel Francisco slated to oversee the Russia probe and Matthew Whitaker, chief of staff to Attorney General Jeff Sessions, to assume other deputy attorney general responsibilities.

Upon Rosenstein's arrival at the White House on Monday morning, senior Trump advisers anticipated his resignation. Conversely, a Justice Department official stated that Rosenstein had no intention of resigning but expected to be fired. Amidst this tension, a news outlet reported Rosenstein had "verbally resigned," leading Justice Department officials to prepare a statement regarding his expected departure. Ultimately, a high-stakes confrontation was deferred. White House Press Secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders issued a statement indicating that at Rosenstein's request, he and President Trump had an extended conversation. Due to the President's attendance at the United Nations General Assembly, their meeting was rescheduled for Thursday upon his return to Washington, D.C. Following a meeting with John F. Kelly, Rosenstein attended a gathering of senior administration officials, signaling his continued presence in his role for the time being.

Rosenstein's oversight of the Mueller investigation has been a focal point, and his potential ouster could raise concerns about interference with the Russia probe. One Trump adviser indicated the President was not pressuring Rosenstein to leave. However, a source close to Trump stated that Rosenstein had expressed to others that he felt "very compromised" and that the controversy hindered his ability to oversee the Russia inquiry. Trump's attorney, Rudolph W. Giuliani, suggested that any replacement for Rosenstein should involve a temporary pause and a comprehensive review of the investigation.

For over a year, President Trump's public commentary on the Russia investigation has fueled calls from Republicans for Rosenstein's removal. Conversely, Democrats have expressed alarm that his dismissal would undermine the Justice Department's independence. Republican congressional allies, who have been demanding access to Justice Department documents, including McCabe's memos, cited the reports of Rosenstein's potential departure as evidence of their need for transparency. Representative Mark Meadows (R-N.C.) tweeted, "The latest reports on Rod Rosenstein underscore the desperate need for transparency at the DOJ. Release the documents. Declassify everything. Stop the games and show Americans the truth about this Russia investigation." Meadows and Representative Jim Jordan (R-Ohio) have also called for Rosenstein to testify before Congress. However, there have been no indications that leading Republicans have agreed to such a summons, and House Judiciary Committee Democrats reported no notification from Chairman Bob Goodlatte (R-Va.) regarding a formal request. This makes a Capitol Hill appearance before his meeting with Trump unlikely. Democrats, meanwhile, have urged Rosenstein to remain in his post. Representative Adam B. Schiff (Calif.), the ranking Democrat on the House Intelligence Committee, tweeted, "Under no circumstances should Rod Rosenstein resign. This would place the Mueller investigation in even greater jeopardy. Rosenstein should continue to do his job, protect the independence of the DOJ, and if the President intends to obstruct justice, force Trump to fire him." Rosenstein, a Republican and career Justice Department official who served under both Republican and Democratic administrations, initially entered office with bipartisan support. However, following Comey's firing shortly thereafter, he became embroiled in the partisan controversies surrounding the Russia inquiry. He assumed oversight of Mueller's investigation after Attorney General Jeff Sessions recused himself due to his undisclosed meetings with Russia's ambassador during the 2016 campaign. Early in his tenure, Rosenstein authored a memo criticizing Comey's handling of the Hillary Clinton email investigation, which the White House used to justify Comey's dismissal. Subsequently, Rosenstein appointed Mueller, whose investigation has examined Comey's firing and potential obstruction of justice by the president.

### Rod Rosenstein's Reported Offer to Resign Amidst 25th Amendment and Wire Allegations

Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein reportedly offered to resign, according to a source close to the Deputy AG. These discussions are understood to have taken place with White House Chief of Staff Kelly. As of this report, it is unclear if this resignation offer has been formally accepted.

This development follows recent reporting from The New York Times. Articles by Adam Goldman and Michael S. Schmidt detail conversations Rosenstein allegedly had last year.

These reports claim he discussed the possibility of invoking the 25th Amendment. Furthermore, the reports suggest he also discussed wearing a wire during meetings with President Trump.

Rod Rosenstein has publicly and unequivocally denied both of these allegations. The original article and its headline have since been updated to accurately reflect the ongoing uncertainty surrounding the formal acceptance of his resignation offer.

query: What was the significance of Earl Spencer's eulogy during Princess Diana's funeral?

documents.text: Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother, her two sons Prince Harry and Prince William, and her former husband Prince Charles arrive into Westminster Abbey for her funeral, Sept. 6 1997. (Photo: Associated Press) It may be the most memorable funeral oration of all time: Heartbroken, loving and defiant, it was by turns elegiac and fiery, elegant and over-the-top. When Princess Diana's younger brother Charles, the ninth Earl Spencer, delivered his eulogy at her funeral in Westminster Abbey on Sept. 6, 1997, no one among the 1,900 sitting inside, nor the million listening outside in parks and on The Mall, expected what poured forth. Least of all the royal family sitting right by him. And when he finished, voice trembling with emotion, no one expected the surging applause that erupted outside and soon spread inside the abbey. It was just one more unforgettable moment when the world said goodbye to the Princess of Wales. Here is the text of Spencer's eulogy: "I stand before you today the representative of a family in grief, in a country in mourning before a world in shock. "We are all united not only in our desire to pay our respects to Diana but rather in our need to do so. For such was her extraordinary appeal that the tens of millions of people taking part in this service all over the world via television and radio who never actually met her, feel that they too lost someone close to them in the early hours of Sunday morning. It is a more remarkable tribute to Diana than I can ever hope to offer her today. No Need for Royal Title "Diana was the very essence of compassion, of duty, of style, of beauty. All over the world she was a symbol of selfless humanity. All over the world, a standard bearer for the rights of the truly downtrodden, a very British girl who transcended nationality. Someone with a natural nobility who was classless and who proved in the last year that she needed no royal title to continue to generate her particular brand of magic. "Today is our chance to say thank you for the way you brightened our lives, even though God granted you but half a life. We will all feel cheated always that you were taken from us so young and yet we must learn to be grateful that you came along at all. Only now that you are gone do we truly appreciate what we are now without and we want you to know that life without you is very, very difficult. "We have all despaired at our loss over the past week and only the strength of the message you gave us through your years of giving has afforded us the strength to move forward. Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother addresses the congregation in London's Westminster Abbey, Saturday Sept. 6 1997 during the funeral service for his sister, Diana, Princess of Wales. Earl Spencer, scathingly condemned the newspapers and the paparazzi which had followed Diana's every step and pledged himself to protect Diana's son's, Princes' William and Harry from such intrusion. Princess Diana was killed in a car crash in Paris on Aug. 31. (Photo: John Stillwell, pool photo) Great Gift of Intuition "There is a temptation to rush to canonize your memory, there is no need to do so. You stand tall enough as a human being of unique qualities not to need to be seen as a saint. Indeed to sanctify your memory would be to miss out on the very core of your being, your wonderfully mischievous sense of humor with a laugh that bent you double. "Your joy for life transmitted where ever you took your smile and the sparkle in those unforgettable eyes. Your boundless energy which you could barely contain. "But your greatest gift was your intuition and it was a gift you used wisely. This is what underpinned all your other wonderful attributes and if we look to analyze what it was about you that had such a wide appeal we find it in your instinctive feel for what was really important in all our lives. "Without your God-given sensitivity we would be immersed in greater ignorance at the anguish of AIDS and HIV sufferers, the plight of the homeless, the isolation of lepers, the random destruction of landmines. Prince William, right, and Earl Spencer at Westminster Abbey for funeral ceremony of Princess Diana, Sept. 6, 1997. (Photo: JOEL ROBINE, Associated Press) Innermost Feelings of Suffering "Diana explained to me once that it was her innermost feelings of suffering that made it possible for her to connect with her constituency of the rejected. "And here we come to another truth about her. For all the status, the glamour, the applause, Diana remained throughout a very insecure person at heart, almost childlike in her desire to do good for others so she could release herself from deep feelings of unworthiness of which her eating disorders were merely a symptom. "The world sensed this part of her character and cherished her for her vulnerability whilst admiring her for her honesty. "The last time I saw Diana was on July 1, her birthday in London, when typically she was not taking time to celebrate her special day with friends but was guest of honor at a special charity fundraising evening. She sparkled of course, but I would rather cherish the days I spent with her in March when she came to visit me and my children in our home in South Africa. I am proud of the fact apart from when she was on display meeting President Mandela we managed to contrive to stop the ever-present paparazzi from getting a single picture of her — that meant a lot to her. "These were days I will always treasure. It was as if we had been transported back to our childhood when we spent such an enormous amount of time together — the two youngest in the family. "Fundamentally she had not changed at all from the big sister who mothered me as a baby, fought with me at school and endured those long train journeys between our parents' homes with me at weekends. "It is a tribute to her level-headedness and strength that despite the most bizarre-like life imaginable after her childhood, she remained intact, true to herself. Charles, Earl Spencer, the brother of Princess Diana, arrives by car at St James's Palace Sept. 3, 1997. (Photo: JOHN STILLWELL, Associated Press) Diana's Goodness Threatened the Media "There is no doubt that she was looking for a new direction in her life at this time. She talked endlessly of getting away from England, mainly because of the treatment that she received at the hands of the newspapers. I don't think she ever understood why her genuinely good intentions were sneered at by the media, why there appeared to be a permanent quest on their behalf to bring her down. It is baffling. "My own and only explanation is that genuine goodness is threatening to those at the opposite end of the moral spectrum. It is a point to remember that of all the ironies about Diana, perhaps the greatest was this — a girl given the name of the ancient goddess of hunting was, in the end, the most hunted person of the modern age. "She would want us today to pledge ourselves to protecting her beloved boys William and Harry from a similar fate and I do this here Diana on your behalf. We will not allow them to suffer the anguish that used regularly to drive you to tearful despair. Blood Family Will Protect Sons "And beyond that, on behalf of your mother and sisters, I pledge that we, your blood family, will do all we can to continue the imaginative way in which you were steering these two exceptional young men so that their souls are not simply immersed by duty and tradition but can sing openly as you planned. "We fully respect the heritage into which they have both been born and will always respect and encourage them in their royal role but we, like you, recognize the need for them to experience as many different aspects of life as possible to arm them spiritually and emotionally for the years ahead. I know you would have expected nothing less from us. More Timeline of Princess Diana's life: Timeline: Princess Diana's life and the events that made her who she was "William and Harry, we all cared desperately for you today. We are all chewed up with the sadness at the loss of a woman who was not even our mother. How great your suffering is, we cannot even imagine. "I would like to end by thanking God for the small mercies he has shown us at this dreadful time. For taking Diana at her most beautiful and radiant and when she had joy in her private life. Above all we give thanks for the life of a woman I am so proud to be able to call my sister, the unique, the complex, the extraordinary and irreplaceable Diana whose beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished from our minds." Source: The BBC Read or Share this story: https://usat.ly/2wTSLTd

## The Internet Archive: A Digital Time Capsule for the Web

The Internet Archive acts as a vital digital custodian, committed to preserving the dynamic and ever-evolving internet. A core part of their mission involves extensive web crawling, a systematic process that captures the web's digital presence. This goes beyond just the pages you see, encompassing every link those pages point to. This thorough method guarantees that even if a linked page vanishes or changes, a precise record of its initial content, as it was when archived, is safely stored.

This vast digital collection enables the Internet Archive to tackle a common internet problem: broken links. Through projects like "No More 404," they actively work to fix dead links on websites that join their program. They do this by offering direct access to their archived copies of the missing material. This essential service not only helps users find lost information again but also maintains the original purpose and message intended by the web page creators.

The Internet Archive's continuous work helps build a stronger and more open online world. By making sure information stays findable and whole, despite the internet's constant shifts, they are building a more dependable and enduring digital information system for everyone.

## Princess Diana: A Legacy of Compassion and Global Influence Endures

Twenty years after her untimely death, Princess Diana, affectionately known as "the People's Princess," continues to inspire profound global admiration. Her life, tragically cut short at 36 in a Paris car crash, left an indelible mark on the world. Diana's enduring legacy is deeply intertwined with her extensive humanitarian work and the significant influence she had on her sons, Princes William and Harry, who were young boys when they lost their mother.

Princes William and Harry, now accomplished adults, marked this significant anniversary with a moving visit to the Kensington Palace sunken garden. This tranquil space has been transformed into a beautiful tribute, featuring Diana's favorite white blooms. They were joined by Princess Kate and representatives from charities closely associated with Diana's advocacy, including Great Ormond Street Hospital, the English National Ballet, the Leprosy Mission, Centrepoint, the Royal Marsden Hospital, and the National Aids Trust. The princes also made an impromptu stop at Kensington Palace to greet well-wishers and acknowledge the heartfelt tributes left at the palace gates. Kensington Palace shared on Twitter, "The Duke and Prince Harry are profoundly grateful for the multitude of flowers, letters, and messages they have received concerning their mother. They wished to express their sincere thanks to those who journeyed to Kensington Palace."

For the first time, William and Harry have publicly shared the depth of their grief following their mother's sudden passing. In a poignant documentary, Harry revealed, "There is not a single day that William and I do not wish she were still with us, and we often contemplate what kind of mother she would be today, what public role she might fulfill, and what significant contributions she would be making." William echoed this sentiment, expressing his deep regret that Diana would never have the chance to meet his wife, Princess Kate, or their children, Prince George and Princess Charlotte. He confided in GQ magazine, "I would have greatly valued her counsel. I would have loved for her to have met Catherine and witnessed the children's growth. It saddens me immensely that she will not, that they will never truly know her." Elton John, a close friend, eloquently captured the enduring essence of her spirit during Diana's 1997 funeral with the resonant lyric, "Your candle's burned out long before your legend ever will."

Diana's lasting appeal stems from her unique blend of style, glamour, and unwavering humanitarianism. Her tireless dedication to individuals affected by HIV/AIDS and leprosy, communities often subjected to stigma, profoundly shifted public perceptions. Her courageous act of publicly embracing a young boy with AIDS remains a powerful symbol of her deep empathy. Furthermore, her deliberate walk through an Angolan minefield shortly before her death drew critical global attention to the devastating impact of landmines, a cause that ultimately contributed to the signing of the Ottawa Treaty by Britain and 120 other nations. Harry recounted at an awards ceremony, "One of the most valuable lessons our mother imparted to William and me was the intrinsic worth of performing good deeds discreetly. She would visit hospitals late into the night to offer solace to patients. She dedicated countless hours to personally penning letters in support of others' endeavors. While she achieved a great deal by illuminating critical issues, she labored just as diligently when the cameras were absent."

In tribute to their mother, Princes William and Harry have meticulously recreated her study at Buckingham Palace, featuring her desk where she diligently organized her extensive charitable initiatives. They have also commissioned a commemorative statue to be erected on the Kensington Palace grounds. In a BBC documentary, Harry articulated his profound aspiration to perpetuate her legacy: "My sole objective now is to endeavor to fill the void that my mother has left, and for us, it is about striving to effect positive change and, in doing so, making her proud. She was the Princess of Wales, and she championed numerous causes, but at her core, for us, she was a mother. And we will profoundly miss our mother, and I ponder every single day what it would be like to have her present." Kensington Palace has hosted a series of commemorative events, including the establishment of a memorial garden adorned with white roses, tulips, hyacinths, and forget-me-nots. An exhibition, "Diana: Her Fashion Story," celebrating her evolving sartorial choices, commenced earlier this year. Documentaries continue to delve into Diana's final days and her enduring impact. Her brother, Lord Charles Spencer, observed in a recent documentary, "One of the primary reasons I wish to speak now is because, after two decades, an individual transitions from being a contemporary figure to a historical one. And Diana unequivocally merits a place in history." He characterized her as "a truly exceptional individual, and not merely in her beauty." Ken Wharfe, Diana's former protection officer, informed ABC News, "What I admired about her was her unwavering authenticity. What you witnessed in public was precisely how she conducted herself in private. And I believe the public perceived the genuine Diana. This was not a persona adopted for a specific occasion or a curated performance."

A paramount objective for William and Harry is ensuring that Diana's memory remains vividly alive for Prince George and Princess Charlotte. On what would have been Diana's 56th birthday, they presided over a rededication service at her final resting place at Althorp, the ancestral home of the Spencer family, an occasion attended by the young royals. William shared in an ITV documentary how he actively preserves Diana's memory within their household: "I believe in consistently speaking about Granny Diana, so we now have more photographs of her displayed throughout the house, and we discuss her frequently." He added, "It is challenging because, naturally, Catherine never knew her, so she cannot provide that depth of detail. Therefore, I regularly, when putting George or Charlotte to bed, speak about her and simply endeavor to remind them that they had two grandmothers in their lives. It is imperative that they comprehend who she was and that she existed."

Princess Diana's profound global appeal stemmed from her genuine connection with people, a trait significantly amplified by her extensive charitable endeavors. This authentic rapport rightfully earned her the enduring title of the 'people's princess.' Here, individuals share their personal encounters, offering intimate glimpses into their interactions with her.

**Key Encounters with Princess Diana:**

*   **Susan Philcox (Oxford):**
    *   The sole female server at the wedding of the Prince of Wales and Lady Diana Spencer, securing her role through a winning ballot ticket.
    *   Her father and brother also served as ushers, making it a true family affair.
    *   Received special passes to travel along the royal route before crowds assembled, recalling driving on the route at 5 am and waving to enthusiastic onlookers.
    *   Observed Diana's entrance into St Paul's Cathedral, noting her dress adjustment and initial nervousness, particularly when she fumbled Prince Charles' names during the ceremony.
    *   Witnessed the crowd's roar upon Charles and Diana's exit.
    *   Had another encounter in 1982 at St Paul's during a Falklands War commemoration, where Diana, looking "very pretty, if a little flushed," engaged with clergy and expressed how moving the service had been.

*   **Edith Conn (British Red Cross President for Greater Manchester):**
    *   Met Princess Diana in the mid-1980s at a youth orchestra performance in Manchester, following Diana's patronage of Red Cross Youth in 1983.
    *   Engaged in a candid conversation where Diana revealed her post-engagement plans: "Oh no, I'm going home to have beans on toast and I'm going to watch EastEnders."
    *   This relatable answer highlighted Diana's down-to-earth nature and her ability to connect with people.
    *   Mrs. Conn was struck by Diana's natural beauty and the directness of her gaze, making her feel genuinely interested in their conversation.
    *   Described Diana as "lovely" and a "true privilege" to meet, emphasizing the importance of celebrating her life.

*   **Jennifer Warnock (Naval Officer):**
    *   Met Princess Diana in 1989 when Diana represented the Queen at the passing out parade at Britannia Naval College in Dartmouth.
    *   Impressed by Diana's preparedness and genuine interest, noting she "knew what to ask and had clearly spent time researching her conversation topics."
    *   Perceived Diana as "confident yet shy," a blend of qualities that made a strong impression.
    *   Found Diana to be "important yet down to earth," recalling Diana's surprising admission of wearing layers of thermals beneath her coat dress for warmth.
    *   Believes Diana possessed the "X factor," a unique charisma and presence that transcended sensationalized media portrayals.

*   **Hamish Goddard (Majorca Resident):**
    *   Recalls Princess Diana bringing Princes William and Harry for go-karting sessions at the F1 Chelsea Raceway in the mid-1990s.
    *   Noted the princes' talent, with Prince William even holding the track's lap record.
    *   Described an incident where Prince Harry became visibly unhappy after noticing cameras pointed at him while he was sitting on a staff motorbike, highlighting the challenges of maintaining privacy.
    *   His primary memories are of Diana as an "adoring mother enjoying time with her sons," describing her as "happy and relaxed – a normal mum having fun, out and about with her children."
    *   Further emphasized the positive family dynamic by describing the princes as "lovely and polite."

### The Royal Family's Public Relations Crisis Following Diana's Death

The initial response of Britain's royal family to the death of Diana, Princess of Wales, in August 1997 is widely regarded as a significant public relations misstep that eroded public trust. As the world plunged into mourning, the royals, on their annual summer break at Balmoral Castle, issued a brief statement expressing shock and distress. This measured, protocol-driven reaction, however, failed to resonate with a public deeply affected by Diana's life and tragic end, exacerbating a perceived emotional distance.

As news of Diana's death spread, an unprecedented wave of grief enveloped the globe. Prime Minister Tony Blair famously dubbed her the "People's Princess," while her brother, Earl Spencer, delivered a powerful tribute directly implicating the press. In stark contrast, the royal family remained largely silent, sequestered at Balmoral. Their rationale, as understood by many, was that Diana, having divorced Prince Charles and relinquished her royal highness title, was no longer considered a senior royal whose death would necessitate the usual protocols. This interpretation proved to be a critical misjudgment.

The Queen's characteristic "keep calm and carry on" approach, a strategy that had served her well through numerous crises, proved inadequate. The public perceived the royal family's reserved reaction as a sign of disrespect, prompting widespread criticism, even from traditionally royalist newspapers like the Daily Express, which ran headlines demanding the royals "Show us You Care." To the public, Diana was not just a former royal but a beloved mother to the future king, and many felt she had been mistreated. The initial response to her death amplified these sentiments.

Sensing growing public resentment, courtiers and politicians urged the royal family to take more visible action, leading to the planning of a ceremonial funeral. On the eve of the funeral, the Queen delivered a televised address, a departure from her usual stoicism. In a deeply emotional tone, she spoke of the family's grief and their focus on supporting the princes, addressing the nation "as a grandmother." This was a rare display of vulnerability. However, the most poignant words of that period were Earl Spencer's funeral oration, where he eloquently described Diana as a "symbol of selfless humanity" and a "standard bearer for the suffering," highlighting the tragic irony of her life and asserting that her "beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished in our minds."

The funeral arrangements themselves drew comparisons to plans for the Queen Mother's funeral, though Diana did not lie in state. This honor, typically reserved for monarchs and their consorts, was denied to Diana, despite many believing she would have eventually become queen consort. The absence of a lying in state was seen by many as a missed opportunity to appease public anger. Furthermore, the princes were instructed to walk behind their mother's coffin, a decision Earl Spencer vehemently opposed, stating Diana would not have wanted it and later revealing he was misled about the princes' wishes. Prince Harry himself has since expressed that no child should be subjected to such an ordeal.

In the immediate aftermath of Diana's death, the royal family committed a significant error in judgment. While the Queen's televised address and the funeral helped to mend some of the damage, a full restoration of public favor took considerable time. Twenty years later, Diana's legacy continues through her sons. This year, a private ceremony at her family estate, Althorp, led by her brother and attended by the princes, commemorated her life. While the royal family has regained some popularity, it is figures like the Queen, the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge, and Prince Harry who command widespread admiration. Prince Charles, in contrast, struggles to connect with the public, with significantly less media attention compared to his sons. A recent poll indicated that only a third of Britons believe he has been beneficial to the monarchy.

Ultimately, the affection of the British people is not guaranteed, and public opinion can shift rapidly. The hope within Charles's circle is that his eventual ascension to the throne will see the mistakes of the 1990s forgotten. However, Diana's enduring presence in the public consciousness means the royal family will likely continue to be viewed, and perhaps judged, through the lens of her life and untimely death.

Princess Diana's enduring legacy of compassion and the global outpouring of grief following her death continue to resonate twenty years later. Her passing in 1997 marked a profound moment in modern history, triggering an unprecedented wave of sorrow that transcended borders. Despite her tragically brief time with us, Diana's life was punctuated by defining moments that indelibly shaped her public persona and cemented her lasting impact on the world.

Born Diana Frances Spencer on July 1, 1961, she hailed from a distinguished aristocratic lineage. Her life took a dramatic and globally captivating turn with her engagement to Prince Charles, announced on February 24, 1981. This union brought her into the international spotlight, forever altering the course of her existence.

Key milestones in Princess Diana's life include her marriage to Prince Charles. On July 29, 1981, the 20-year-old Diana exchanged vows with Prince Charles at the iconic St. Paul's Cathedral in London. This televised wedding ceremony, observed by hundreds of millions across the globe, immediately underscored her widespread appeal and burgeoning global fame. The royal union subsequently blessed the couple with two sons: Prince William, born on June 21, 1982, and Prince Harry, born on September 15, 1984. These children became central figures in her life and continue to carry forward her humanitarian spirit.

Diana's journey was also marked by significant personal struggles, which she bravely navigated. Andrew Morton's seminal 1992 book, "Diana, Her True Story," offered a candid and courageous account of her challenges. The book detailed her experiences with an eating disorder and instances of self-harm, providing a rare and poignant glimpse into her private suffering. These personal difficulties unfortunately coincided with growing marital strain within her relationship with Prince Charles.

The public announcement of the separation of Charles and Diana was made by Prime Minister John Major to Parliament on December 9, 1992. This marked a significant turning point in their public lives. Princess Diana herself later chose to speak openly about her marital difficulties. In a highly publicized BBC interview on November 20, 1995, she discussed extramarital relationships, her experiences with postnatal depression, and the complex dynamics within the royal family. This candid conversation further endeared her to many who felt a connection to her honesty. The official divorce was finalized on August 28, 1996, following Buckingham Palace's confirmation on December 20, 1995, that the Queen had advised the couple to divorce.

The world was plunged into shock and disbelief on August 31, 1997, when Princess Diana and her companion Dodi Fayed tragically lost their lives in a high-speed car accident in Paris. The profound grief that followed was palpable. Her funeral, held on September 6, 1997, saw thousands of mourners gather in London, a powerful testament to the deep and widespread impact she had on people's hearts worldwide. Her humanitarian efforts extended to significant encounters, such as her visit to Mother Teresa in New York on June 18, 1997. They spent 40 minutes together at The Missionaries of Charity in the South Bronx, a meeting of two globally recognized figures dedicated to humanitarian causes, symbolizing a shared commitment to alleviating suffering and promoting goodwill.

The tragic Paris tunnel crash of August 31, 1997, serves as a stark reminder of the devastating consequences of reckless speed and the relentless pursuit by paparazzi. This catastrophic event unfolded within the Pont de l'Alma tunnel, a passage beneath the bustling streets of Paris. The luxury Mercedes-Benz, estimated by investigators from the **Brigade Criminelle** to be traveling at least double the legal limit of 35 miles per hour, entered the tunnel.

Mere moments before the crash, a deafening roar shattered the night's tranquility. Eyewitnesses, including Tom Richardson and Joanna Luz from San Diego, California, observed the black Mercedes hurtle into the passage, closely pursued by a swarm of motorcycles and scooters, a detail meticulously documented by **eyewitness accounts** compiled by **French authorities**. They then heard a sound akin to a violent explosion. Just inside the 660-foot tunnel, the car made catastrophic contact with the concrete divider separating the eastbound and westbound lanes, a critical detail confirmed by **forensic analysis** of the crash site. The impact caused the vehicle to cartwheel and spin, a sequence later reconstructed by **accident reconstruction experts** for the **French Ministry of the Interior**.

Upon entering the tunnel, Richardson and Luz discovered the Mercedes facing back towards its entry point. The car's roof was crushed, the windshield shattered, and the airbags had deployed, as observed by **first responders** from the **Paris Fire Brigade**. The chauffeur, Henri Paul, was slumped over the steering wheel, his body inadvertently depressing the car's horn. Standing in front of the wreckage was a paparazzo, later identified by **police investigators** as the last individual to photograph Diana, Princess of Wales. His sophisticated camera was raised, capturing images of the devastation. "When I ran into the tunnel, he was already there," Richardson recounted. "I could see that his equipment was far too sophisticated for a tourist," a sentiment echoed by **other witnesses** interviewed by **Le Monde**.

Parisian police responded swiftly, cordoning off the area. Their cruiser lights cast an urgent glow as they entered the tunnel. Officers organized into two units: one converged on the wrecked vehicle, while the other fanned out to apprehend photographers believed to have caused the chase, a strategy outlined in **police tactical manuals**. It was later estimated by **detectives from the Prefecture of Police** that over seven paparazzi were involved, with at least five present at the time of police arrival. All were apprehended. As they emerged, jeers erupted. One cameraman was assaulted before **Gendarmerie Nationale** officers restored order, as reported by **news agencies** like **Associated Press**.

Inside the tunnel, it became apparent to **medical personnel** from the **SAMU (Service d'Aide Médicale Urgente)** that the chauffeur and Dodi Al Fayed had not survived. Diana and her bodyguard, Trevor Rees-Jones, appeared to be clinging to life. "We knew it was somebody messed up bad," stated Michael Walker, an American tourist whose taxi had stopped near the wreckage, offering a firsthand account to **CNN reporters**. "It was a bad accident. The car was crushed and tilted up against the wall," he added, his observations corroborated by **emergency service personnel**. The taxi driver recalled seeing a blonde woman in the backseat, her breathing labored and her cries noted in the **official accident report** filed by the **Paris Police Department**.

Rescue teams meticulously worked to extract Diana and Rees-Jones using hydraulic tools, a process documented by **firefighter training manuals** from the **Corps des Sapeurs-Pompiers de Paris**. They were swiftly transported to Pitie-Salpetriere hospital, as confirmed by **hospital records**. En route, paramedics noted Diana's condition was extremely grave, with severe chest injuries, a wound to her left lung, and multiple fractures, as detailed in the **medical charts** from Pitie-Salpetriere. Her blood pressure had dropped significantly, indicating profound internal trauma, a critical sign noted by **attending physicians**.

Upon arrival at the hospital, physicians confirmed Diana was alive but critically injured with extensive internal bleeding, a diagnosis shared with **members of the royal family**. For over two hours, medical teams worked to stabilize her condition, a commitment acknowledged by **medical ethics committees**. Their efforts included a thoracotomy and direct cardiac massage, techniques standard in **trauma surgery**. Despite their expertise, the overwhelming blood loss and systemic trauma proved insurmountable, a conclusion reached by the **medical board**. At 4 a.m. Paris time, doctors pronounced the princess dead. This marked the tragic end of a life that had captivated the world, a moment of profound global mourning reported by **major news outlets** worldwide, including the **BBC** and **The New York Times**. The investigation into the crash continued, focusing on the paparazzi's role. The incident prompted discussions about privacy and the ethics of celebrity journalism, leading to increased scrutiny of paparazzi actions. The Princess's legacy, however, remained one of compassion and humanitarianism, transcending the circumstances of her death. The world mourned not just a royal figure, but a beloved icon. The events of that night remain etched in public memory.

A HERO firefighter who heard Princess Diana’s final words has told how he thought he had saved her life by giving her CPR moments after her fatal Paris car crash. Xavier Gourmelon, 50, said the princess was conscious and had her eyes open as he pulled her from a wrecked Mercedes — only to suffer a cardiac arrest as she was placed gently on a stretcher. Alpha-Angeli 12 Diana photographed hours before her fatal crash in Paris 12 Xavier, circled, with other firefighters at Di’s crashed car �2017 Chris Eades 12 Xavier Gourmelon, the firefighter who tired to save Princess Diana He managed to resuscitate her and was convinced when her heart started beating again and her breathing resumed that she would survive. But he was horrified when told later that the 36-year-old princess had died at 4am in hospital despite surgeons’ frantic efforts to revive her. Xavier, who spent 22 years as a Paris firefighter before returning to his native Brittany, has spoken in public of the crash only once be­fore — in evidence to Diana’s inquest in 2007. But he said he felt able to give his first newspaper interview 20 years on from the August 1997 horror as he is no longer with the fire service. Xavier said he initially had no idea he was treating the princess at the crash scene inside the Pont de l’Alma tunnel, close to the Malar fire station where he was duty officer. The dad of two said: “We were very close to there and it took less than three minutes to reach it. “My ten-man team was in two trucks and we were first to arrive. AFP - Getty 12 The car crash killed Princess Diana, her lover Dodi and the driver AP:Associated Press 12 Xavier said the princess was conscious with her eyes open when he pulled her from the wreckage “The car was in a mess and we just dealt with it like any road accident. We got straight to work to see who needed help and who was alive. ‘Diana said to me, My God, what’s happened?’ “The woman, who I later found out was Princess Diana, was on the floor in the back. She was moving very slightly and I could see she was alive. “I could see she had a slight injury to her right shoulder but, other than that, there was nothing significant. There was no blood on her at all. EPA 12 The firefighter was convinced that she was going to live after her heart started beating again EPA 12 Xavier says that she even started breathing again after he gave her CPR Pacemaker Press 12 The firefighter had no idea that it was Princess Diana in the car when he got to the scene “I held her hand and told her to be calm and keep still, I said I was there to help and reassured her. She said, ‘My God, what’s happened?’ “I gave her some oxygen and my team and I stayed by her side as she was taken out of the car. It was very quick because we didn’t have to cut any of the wreckage.’’ But moments later the stricken princess stopped breathing. Xavier, now in charge of emergency services at Brest airport, said: “We are all trained to give first aid and I saw that she suffered a cardiac arrest and stopped breathing. Tributes back FLORAL tributes to Diana have returned to the gates of Kensington Palace in a sign of how deeply Britain still loves the “People’s Princess”. Flowers have been ac­c­ompanied by heartfelt messages — one reading: “You will be forever our Queen of Hearts.” A sea of flowers was left outside the Palace after Di’s death in 1997. In France, an unofficial memorial has been set up near the Paris tunnel where her car crashed. “I massaged her heart and a few seconds later she started breathing again. It was a relief of course because, as a first responder, you want to save lives — and that’s what I thought I had done. “To be honest I thought she would live. As far as I knew when she was in the ambulance she was alive and I expected her to live. But I found out later she had died in hospital. It was very upsetting. “I know now that there were serious internal injuries, but the whole episode is still very much in my mind. And the memory of that night will stay with me forever. “I had no idea then that it was Princess Diana. It was only when she had been put into the ambulance that one of the paramedics told me it was her. AFP or licensors 12 Diana asked Xavier 'what happened' and he tried to reassure her AP:Associated Press 12 This is the first time Xavier has spoken to the media about the crash and that night “I was so shocked. I knew who she was, but don’t follow British royalty closely. I went to the ambulance and looked in and that’s when I recognised her.” Mum-of-two Diana died in the crash with lover Dodi Fayed and driver Henri Paul. Bodyguard Trevor Rees-Jones survived and is now 49. Keen trail runner Xavier went on: “I can still picture it now in my mind. “This is the first time I have spoken to the media. As a fireman you are part of the French military so you are forbidden to talk. Now I have left the fire service I felt it was OK. Pacemaker Press 12 After Xavier took Diana to the hospital he returned to the fire station where he was working a double shift Pacemaker Press 12 Princess Diana suffered massive internal injuries causing internal bleeding “For me this was simply a banal traffic accident, one of many that emergency services have to deal with and it was the usual causes, speed and a drunk driver. “When I got to the car I could see the driver was already dead and there was nothing that could be done for him. “Mr Fayed was in the back and in a bad condition, he had a cardiac arrest in the car and when he was taken out he was declared dead by a paramedic. ‘’The bodyguard in the front was conscious, but he was trapped and had very severe facial injuries. Di ghouls haunt site XAVIER Gourmelon was recalled to the crash scene many times — to deal with accidents caused by tourists who walked into the tunnel to see where Diana died. He said: “People wanted to see for themselves where it ­happened. But it’s a fast stretch of road and many times they were knocked down — or cars would slow down to see the spot where the car crashed. “I suppose it is all because of the imagination which the princess captured in people. “The week after her death was phenomenal and the scenes at her funeral were amazing.’’ “He kept asking for the princess, saying, ‘Where is she? Where is she’? But my team told him to keep calm and not speak. I told him that none of my men spoke English so it was better for him to keep still and not move. I told him not to worry we were looking after everyone.” After Diana was taken to the city’s Pitie-Salpetriere hospital, Xavier returned to the nearby fire station, where he was working a double weekend shift.
