I found Kelvin Miyahira, a golf instructor in Hawaii with no ties to Yar who nonetheless had become one of its biggest fans. Miyahira had used a high-speed camera to compare the Oracle with other, more popular putters. In slow-motion videos he posted to YouTube, he showed that when he used the Oracle, it was more stable and rolled the ball more smoothly and with less sidespin than any of the other clubs he tried. Champions Tour player David Frost had once received an hour-long putting lesson from Dr. V and four days later had won a tournament by tying the lowest score ever recorded on that course. The information Dr. V had imparted to him was so valuable, Frost told me, that he wasn’t even willing to share it. Maybe if I’d had the same access, the Oracle would have remained as effective for me. But positive contagion, at least in my case, only seemed to work when I believed I was still infected. When I was under the impression that Dr. V was a brilliant engineer, my putting improved dramatically. As soon as I learned she had simply been a struggling mechanic, the magic was lost. Today, Dr. V’s Oracle is collecting dust in my garage. The other question to consider was if any of the lies actually mattered. Yes, Dr. V had fabricated a résumé that helped sell the Oracle putter under false pretenses. But she was far from the first clubmaker to attach questionable scientific value to a piece of equipment just to make it more marketable. Sure, her lies were more audacious than the embellishments found in late-night infomercials. But her ultimate intent — to make a few bucks, or, maybe, to be known as a genius — remained the same. Whatever the answers, Gary McCord would not be able to help me find them. The man who had once been so willing to talk stopped responding to my emails. Finally, a spokesperson at CBS told me that McCord had “nothing more to add to the story.” That left Jordan and Dr. V. I called them both, and realized that they had given me the same phone number. Dr. V had said the number was for her lab with the “switchboard personnel.” This time, though, no one answered and I heard the outgoing message. What sounded like a young girl’s voice filled the receiver: “Thank you for calling Essay Vanderbilt and family …” The next day I tried again. No answer still, but the recording had changed. Instead of a young girl, the voice was Jordan’s: “Hello, you’ve reached the offices of Yar Golf …” ♦♦♦ I was under the belief that what had transpired at Yar was ultimately harmless until I heard from a mysterious “silent investor” whom both Jordan and Dr. V had alluded to in our previous talks. His name was Phil Kinney. He was a retiree from Pittsburgh and he said he wasn’t the only one who had put money into the company. He had invested $60,000 — money that he believed he’d never see again. It wasn’t that Kinney didn’t love Yar’s putter or have high hopes for its future. He had loved it from the moment he met Dr. V at a convention four years ago. (Before I told him about her past, he told me that because of her height and vivid red hair, it was hard to miss the “pretty woman walking toward me in a miniskirt.”) He still loved the club enough to sell it to friends and clients, too. But he had also come to know the frustrations of working with Dr. V. Kinney had heard his own share of incredible claims. Dr. V had told him that she was a $1,000-an-hour consultant. She said she was one of the original designers of Bluetooth technology. She even suggested that her status as a Vanderbilt provided access to some exclusive company who could help Yar’s business. Kinney said Dr. V told him she was good friends with the Hilton family, and that the relationship would pay off in the form of putters sold at their hotels. Kinney also recalled a trip he had taken to Arizona where, in Dr. V’s house, she had shown him a computer that she said mirrored the one in Phoenix’s airport traffic control tower. For all her wild stories, though, what Dr. V was most, Kinney said, was a difficult person to deal with. “She would just explode. If you’re disagreeing with her while she had one of her headaches, you were in trouble.” And Kinney often disagreed with Dr. V. He tried to get her to change the design of the putter. She wouldn’t budge. He tried to get her to change Yar’s confusing website. She had the same reaction. He even tried to convince Dr. V to let well-known club designers like Bobby Grace, whom Kinney said wanted to invest, buy into the company. “She just told me, ‘We don’t need him.'” It seemed unlikely that Yar would ever deliver a return on Kinney’s investment. Maybe the most surprising thing about my conversation with Kinney was how calmly he took the news that the woman he thought was an aerospace engineer had once been a man, and a mechanic. “I’m pretty dang gullible, I guess,” he said. For all the hassle that came with his partnership with Dr. V, what had kept him going was the putter. That was what Kinney couldn’t understand. If Yar had simply been a scam, the story would have been much simpler. But the Oracle worked. And Dr. V seemed more interested in achieving fame as a club designer than in getting rich. “She could have took my money and ran,” he said. “But she didn’t. She took it and built a great product.” Kinney said he was worried that the putter’s excellence would be lost in the strange tale of Dr. V. He genuinely believed the Oracle was a superior product. But at one point near the end of our conversation, he had a thought that seemed to trouble him. “Maybe I liked it because she convinced me before I even hit it,” he said. “Maybe it’s not as good as I think. Someone tells me a story, I believe it.” ♦♦♦ The last time I heard from Dr. V she warned me that I was about to commit a hate crime. But before that, I received a voice mail from Jordan. Neither of them had contacted me in months, since I had sent an email trying to confirm what I had discovered, and Jordan wrote back to deny everything. “Your attack tale should be published in the National Enquirer,” Jordan wrote, “right next to the article on Martians … If I am to believe your diatribe, what you are telling golfers is that the most scientifically advanced Near Zero MOI putter, and the science of the Inertia Matrix was invented by a lesbian auto mechanic.” Now, Jordan’s message said she was calling to propose a deal. When I phoned her back, Jordan explained the offer. I could fly to Arizona and meet with Dr. V at her attorney’s office, where she would show me proof of her degrees from both MIT and the University of Pennsylvania. Dr. V then got on the phone and added another detail. Once I saw the documents I would have to sign a nondisclosure agreement barring me from revealing any of the details I’d learned about Dr. V’s past. The “deal” was one I could not accept, and when I explained this Dr. V got upset. “What is your intention?” she asked. “Are you being paid by someone to destroy Yar?” Dr. V’s anger made it so that what she said came out fast and with almost no interruption. I tried to record everything she said and ask the occasional question, but it was like yelling into a wind tunnel. When she finally had said her piece, she handed the phone back to Jordan. “Well, I guess you’re just going to print what you’re going to print,” Jordan said. “Try to lead a decent life. Have a good one.” Then she hung up. A few days later, Dr. V sent one final email. It had her signature mix of scattered punctuation and randomly capitalized words. Once upon a time I had brushed off these grammatical quirks, but now they seemed like outward expressions of the inner chaos she struggled to contain. “To whom this may concern,” it read. “I spoke with Caleb Hannan last Saturday his deportment is reminiscent to schoolyard bullies, his sole intention is to injure or bring harm to me … Because of a computer glitch, some documents that are germane only to me, were visible to web-viewers, government officials have now rectified this egregious condition … Caleb Hannan came into possession of documents that were clearly marked: MADE NON-PUBLIC (Restricted) … Exposing NON-PUBLIC Documents is a Crime, and prosecution of such are under the auspices of many State and Federal Laws, including Hate Crimes Legislation signed into Law by President Obama.” Over the course of what was now eight months of reporting, Dr. V had accused me of being everything from a corporate spy to a liar and a fraud. She had also threatened me. One of the quotes I was able to type down during our last conversation was this: “You have no idea what I have done and what I can do.” It’s not all that menacing when transcribed, but her tone made it clear she believed she could harm me. Yet despite all that, the main emotion I felt while reading her desperate, last-ditch email was sadness. Although there were times when I had been genuinely thrilled with the revelation that Dr. V’s official narrative didn’t line up with reality, there was nothing satisfying about where the story had ended up. People had been hurt by Dr. V’s lies, but she was the person who seemed to be suffering most. Not long after she sent her email, I got a call from a Pennsylvania phone number that I didn’t recognize. It was Dr. V’s ex-brother-in-law, who represented the closest I had gotten to finding someone who could tell me what she’d been like in her previous life. “Well, there’s one less con man in the world now,” he said. Even though he hated his former family member, this seemed like an especially cruel way to tell me that Dr. V had died. All he could tell me was what he knew — that it had been a suicide. A few weeks later a police report filled in the details. Around 11 a.m. on October 18, Jordan walked into the home office she shared with Dr. V and found pieces from her business partner’s jewelry collection laid out on a desk next to some handwritten letters. Each letter explained which friend or family member was to get which piece of jewelry in case of Dr. V’s death. Jordan then noticed that Dr. V’s car was missing. At first, Jordan explained to the police, she didn’t think much of the missing vehicle. Jordan prepared some breakfast and then drove to her nearby apartment. When Jordan arrived and reached her bedroom, she found Dr. V lying on the floor curled in a fetal position with a white plastic bag over her head; an empty bottle of pills sat on the kitchen counter. Writing a eulogy for a person who by all accounts despised you is an odd experience. What makes it that much harder is that Dr. V left so few details — on purpose, of course. Those who knew her in her past life refused to talk about her. Those who knew her in the life she had created were helpful right up to the point where that new life began to look like a lie. The only person who can provide this strange story with its proper ending is the person who started it. The words she spoke came during our last conversation, when she was frantically trying to convince me of things I knew couldn’t possibly be true. Yet though they may have been spoken by a desperate person at one of the most desperate times in a life that had apparently seen many, it’s hard to argue with Dr. V’s conclusions. “Nobody knows my life but me,” she said. “You don’t know what the truth is.”

The tragic suicide of Essay Anne Vanderbilt, a transgender woman whose life was the subject of Grantland's "Dr. V's Magical Putter," has ignited crucial conversations about journalistic ethics, particularly concerning the reporting on transgender individuals. This devastating event serves as a stark reminder of the profound influence of journalism and compels a critical examination of how sensitive personal narratives are handled.

### Ethical Breaches in Reporting

The Grantland article, initially lauded for its distinctive exploration of Vanderbilt and her golf putter, later drew significant condemnation. Following an in-depth review by Melissa McEwan, substantial and preventable errors committed by writer Caleb Hannan and Grantland were brought to light. Many believe these journalistic missteps contributed to Vanderbilt's decision to end her life, fundamentally shifting the article's focus from a golf club to a severe case of reporting, writing, and editing failures.

### The Narrative and its Contentious Aspects

Discussions surrounding the article have been extensive across platforms dedicated to trans* issues and journalism. While some have defended Hannan's actions, a significant number hold him accountable for Vanderbilt's death. Amidst this intense debate, the clear violation of key ethical boundaries has become a central point of contention.

Initially, the core of Hannan's reporting was not inherently problematic. Vanderbilt had presented herself with false credentials, including degrees from prestigious institutions like MIT and Penn, and claimed involvement in clandestine military projects. A journalist investigating such assertions would naturally endeavor to verify their accuracy. Hannan's discovery of Vanderbilt's transgender identity emerged incidentally during this verification process.

However, Hannan's decision to disclose Vanderbilt's transgender status to a Yar Golf investor represented a profound ethical transgression. This action stripped Vanderbilt of her autonomy over the disclosure of deeply personal information. As Christina Kahrl aptly observed, "revealing her gender identity was ultimately as dangerous as it was thoughtless."

### Dehumanizing Narrative Choices

Furthermore, specific narrative decisions, such as the detailed recounting of Vanderbilt's past and her suicide, coupled with the use of insensitive language, served to dehumanize her. The article framed her death as a consequence of a sensationalized fraud, rather than acknowledging the profound tragedy of a woman's suicide.

### Ambiguity and Editorial Deficiencies

Beyond these critical points, critiques have underscored the article's failure to provide adequate explanations for its choices. For instance, the text offers no concrete evidence of Hannan aggressively harassing Vanderbilt or threatening to out her. Instead, it suggests he uncovered information, offered her an opportunity to respond, and proceeded with his investigation. Conversely, the article also fails to demonstrate humane reporting by emphasizing his intent to verify facts without dictating her personal choices. Kahrl noted that, based on the published text, it is reasonable to infer that Hannan did not "reassure her that her gender identity wasn't germane to the broader problems he'd uncovered with her story."

This inherent ambiguity allows for a spectrum of interpretations. Journalists and editors might find themselves sympathetic to the writer's perspective, while transgender individuals and their allies may perceive the events through a distinctly different lens, a divergence stemming from fundamental writing and editing flaws.

### The Central Issue: Writing and Editing

Journalism encompasses reporting, writing, and editing. Hannan's primary reporting error appears to be the outing of Vanderbilt to an investor. While he gathered and sought to verify information, and at times paused his efforts, any omissions regarding his intentions concerning her transgender status are his to address, but cannot be definitively linked to her death without further insight. Directly attributing suicide to journalistic mistakes oversimplifies an exceptionally complex issue.

Clear writing and editing errors are undeniably present. By structuring the narrative as a chronological mystery, Hannan felt compelled to reveal Vanderbilt's transgender identity to fill perceived narrative gaps, inadvertently making it the emotional crux of the story. Steve Silberman noted that Grantland's editor-in-chief, Bill Simmons, indicated the story was largely complete before Vanderbilt's suicide, suggesting Hannan believed outing her was acceptable to bolster his claims and discredit her fabricated persona.

Ultimately, poorly conceived writing is published with editorial sanction, with the most serious issues residing in the editing process. Line editing problems include misgendering Vanderbilt in references to her past and emphasizing Hannan's "chill" upon learning she was transgender. Critics interpreted this as malicious ignorance, though it could also represent the moment the story's inconsistencies became apparent to Hannan. While not necessarily malicious, the narrative's disproportionate focus on her gender identity fostered this perception.

### A Fundamentally Flawed Concept and Missed Opportunities

More critically, the article's foundational concept is deeply flawed. Published three months after Vanderbilt's death, "Dr. V's Magic Putter" presented a writer grappling with the potential ramifications of his work under the most tragic of circumstances. The final article exhibits a striking lack of curiosity about Vanderbilt's death itself. At some juncture, the focus should have irrevocably shifted from a golf putter to a woman's life and death. If Hannan was unable to reframe the narrative, someone else should have. A story commencing with her death and meticulously exploring the events that precipitated it, including her gender identity, would have been profoundly different. Gerri Jordan's suggestion of focusing on "the most scientifically advanced Near Zero MOI putter, and the science of the Inertia Matrix... invented by a lesbian auto mechanic" highlights a potentially compelling narrative: an auto mechanic who fabricated credentials to achieve recognition for her innovative golf invention. How Grantland's editorial team overlooked these significant opportunities remains unclear, with sources at ESPN reportedly questioning the absence of respected journalist John Walsh.

### The "Grantland Problem" and Editorial Priorities

Bill Simmons's apology, while seemingly earnest, is characteristically self-absorbed, dedicating more space to his aspirations for the site than to Vanderbilt herself. This reflects Grantland's underlying premise that the allure of sports lies in the narratives crafted by the writers, often eclipsing the subjects themselves. This fixation leaves a palpable void in Grantland's apology, mirroring the unanswered questions inherent in the original article.

As a prestige publication, Grantland prioritized perceived quality over actual substance, functioning as a vehicle for Bill Simmons's vision, generously provided by ESPN to showcase his perceived value. Such editorial failures can manifest at any publication where editors lack sufficient attunement to transgender issues. However, this particular breakdown exemplifies the broader "Grantland problem," intrinsically linked to the "Bill Simmons problem." It reflects a philosophy where form supersedes function, perception outweighs substance, and widespread success serves as its own justification. ESPN president John Skipper reportedly covets awards and accolades for Grantland, aiming for the site to rival publications celebrated for sensational, unbelievable stories. Last week, Grantland found such a story, but it ultimately revealed more about Grantland's own shortcomings and the troubles they can engender than about Dr. V and her magical putter.

query: What reasons are given for Kim Kardashian and Kanye West's desire to have another child soon after the birth of North West?

documents.text:
### Kim Kardashian and Kanye West's Future Family Plans

Kim Kardashian and Kanye West are reportedly contemplating adding to their family in the near future, not long after welcoming their daughter, North West. Insiders suggest that conversations about having another child are not just casual but are actively taking place "right away."

Several elements appear to be shaping the couple's prompt consideration of another pregnancy. Kardashian, at 32, is reportedly aware of her age and her aspiration to have a larger family. She has also voiced a practical perspective on pregnancy, suggesting it could be more streamlined to navigate one extended period of pregnancy and associated weight changes, followed by a focused weight loss effort, rather than experiencing multiple cycles of weight gain and subsequent loss.

Kanye West is reportedly fully supportive of Kardashian, irrespective of her physical form, and is enthusiastic about the prospect of another child soon. The couple has been embracing the experience of parenthood since North's arrival, and West is described as a dedicated father. While immediate wedding plans are not on the horizon, their primary focus is on their expanding family.

The apparent ease with which Kardashian conceived their first child hints at the potential for a swift second pregnancy. This pattern of having children in close succession is not unusual among public figures. For instance:

*   Jessica Simpson became pregnant with her second child less than six months after her first.
*   Britney Spears welcomed children spaced only a few months apart.
*   Tori Spelling famously discovered her third pregnancy just one month after giving birth to her second child.

**Kim Kardashian and Kanye West are reportedly planning for another baby soon after welcoming daughter North West.**

Just four months after the birth of their daughter, North West, **Kim Kardashian and Kanye West are already discussing expanding their family.** A source close to the couple revealed to RadarOnline.com, "**They are discussing having another baby right away. It’s serious.**"

The apparent rush stems from Kardashian's desire to have a larger family. "**She is well aware that she’s getting older, and that if she wants to have a bigger family, then she needs to get it done,**" the source explained. Additionally, Kardashian is reportedly considering the logistics of multiple pregnancies. "**Kim is telling Kanye that she doesn’t think it makes sense to gain pregnancy weight, lose it, and then gain it all back again. She’s starting to think she might want to just suck it up and be big and pregnant in one long go and then lose the weight once and for all.**"

While Kardashian's appearance is not a concern for West, he is enthusiastic about the prospect of another child. "**Kanye likes Kim at any size, so that part doesn’t matter to him. But he loves the idea of having another baby soon,**" the source stated. The couple has been enjoying parenthood since North's arrival, despite having no immediate plans for marriage. "**Both of them are so blown away with her. And Kanye is a better father than anyone ever imagined he would be,**" the source added.

Although a definitive decision hasn't been made, the source believes it's only a matter of time before North has siblings. "**Kim and Kanye believe what will be will be, but since she got pregnant so easily the first time around, Kim could be pregnant again soon!**"

This rapid succession of children is not uncommon among celebrities. For instance, Jessica Simpson became pregnant with her son Ace less than six months after her daughter Maxwell was born. Britney Spears welcomed her son Sean in November 2005 and was pregnant with her son Jayden about three months later. **Tori Spelling holds the record, discovering she was pregnant with her son Finn just one month after giving birth to her daughter, Hattie.**

The highly anticipated union of Kim Kardashian and Kanye West is already sparking family discussions, with reports indicating that the couple's engagement has ignited a debate over who will escort Kim down the aisle. This unfolding drama, as revealed by an exclusive source to MailOnline, centers on a disagreement between Kim's mother, Kris Jenner, and her estranged husband, Bruce.

Kris Jenner reportedly favors her son, Rob Kardashian, to fulfill the ceremonial role, a sentiment stemming from her separation from Bruce. The source suggests that Kris believes it would be inappropriate for Bruce to walk Kim down the aisle, particularly in light of Kim's previous marriage to Kris Humphries. However, Kim and her sisters are said to be advocating for Bruce, viewing him as a paternal figure.

Adding another layer to the discussion, Kris Jenner apparently believes Bruce might prefer to avoid the public scrutiny that accompanied Kim's previous wedding. This sentiment acknowledges the intense media attention and "circus" surrounding Kim's short-lived marriage to the professional basketball player. The source also hinted that Kim and Kanye themselves might opt for a more private ceremony, with the possibility of an elopement being considered.

Kanye West proposed to Kim Kardashian on her 33rd birthday, orchestrating a grand gesture at AT&T Park in San Francisco. The proposal took place in the presence of their family and friends, following Kanye's earlier expression of his desire to marry Kim during a concert in Seattle. The surprise engagement means their wedding will occur sooner than anticipated.

This upcoming marriage will mark Kim Kardashian's third and Kanye West's first. The couple, who welcomed their daughter, North, in June, are reportedly already contemplating expanding their family. Concerns about Kim's age and the desire for a larger family are driving discussions about conceiving another child in the near future.

Those crazy kids went and got engaged. As E! Online was first to report, Kanye popped the question to Kim last night in front of 250 (more or less) of their closest friends and family at AT&T Park in San Francisco. Ya know, the stadium? According to everyone, Kanye rented out the baseball stadium and hired an orchestra of fifty musicians to serenade his betrothed. And all those witness to this momentous event from clydehairgod to livebythevk shared the good news via Instagram. Launch the gallery below to see the photos from the proposal to end all proposals.

Kris Jenner has consistently voiced her unwavering support for Kanye West, frequently emphasizing his dedication as a partner to her daughter, Kim Kardashian. Jenner has publicly lauded West, describing him as an "amazing boyfriend to Kim" and a "great dad." Beyond his familial roles, she has also acknowledged his significant creative abilities, characterizing him as a "wonderful person" and an "amazing human being."

A notable instance of Jenner facilitating West's public expression of his views occurred during his appearance on her talk show, "Hello Ross." This platform provided West with an opportunity to elaborate on his relationship with Kim. He described their connection as an immediate and profound "love at first sight" experience that had its origins years before. During this same television segment, West also shared the inaugural public photograph of their daughter, North. Jenner expressed her delight, stating that viewers were able to witness "the Kanye that I know at home."

In a separate conversation with E! News, Jenner spoke about the immense happiness her grandchildren bring into her life, referring to this current phase as a "delicious time." She also offered high praise for Kim, calling her "the best mom in the world." Jenner reiterated her belief that both Kim and Kanye are "really great parents." This perspective echoes Jenner's sentiments from over a year prior, when she had observed Kim's exceptional contentment and had begun contemplating potential wedding venue options.

'I would estimate that a diamond of this size and quality would be worth well over £1 million; Kim is a very lucky woman!' Their love only blossomed, however, after the star's second marriage and most infamous relationship collapsed. Her father was the first to share her image, doing so during an appearance on Kris Jenner's talk show.
