United Airlines subsequently clarified that the flight in question, identified as United Express flight 3411, was indeed overbooked. The airline initially sought volunteers to voluntarily relinquish their seats, offering compensation of $1,000 per seat. United explained that these seats were critically needed to accommodate their own crew members who were scheduled to work the flight. When no passengers initially volunteered to deplane, the airline informed several individuals that they would be required to leave the aircraft. The passenger who was forcibly removed reportedly refused to deplane voluntarily. Consequently, law enforcement officers employed by the Chicago Aviation Department were summoned to the gate and proceeded to remove him from the aircraft.

The DOT's official statement underscored its unwavering commitment to safeguarding consumer rights. The agency declared, "The Department of Transportation (USDOT) remains committed to protecting the rights of consumers and is reviewing the involuntary denied boarding of passenger(s) from United Express flight 3411 to determine whether the airline complied with the oversales rule." The Department further emphasized its pivotal role in ensuring that all airlines rigorously adhere to established consumer protection regulations, including the specific provisions of the oversales rule. While acknowledging the legal permissibility for airlines to involuntarily remove passengers from oversold flights when insufficient volunteers come forward, the DOT explicitly clarified that "it is the airline's responsibility to determine its own fair boarding priorities." This statement from the DOT, as reported by sources like the Associated Press, highlights the agency's focus on airline accountability.

United Airlines also issued its own statement to Business Insider, addressing the incident. The airline reiterated that flight 3411, operating from Chicago to Louisville, was overbooked. They elaborated that after their internal team actively sought volunteers, "one customer refused to leave the aircraft voluntarily and law enforcement was asked to come to the gate." United extended a formal apology for the overbooking situation and directed any further inquiries regarding the removed customer to the relevant authorities, as noted by CNN.

United CEO Oscar Munoz also released a personal statement following the incident, expressing deep regret. He conveyed that the event was "upsetting to all of us here at United." Munoz offered an apology for the necessity of re-accommodating customers under such circumstances. He assured the public that the airline's team was working with "a sense of urgency" to collaborate with authorities and to conduct a thorough internal review of the events that transpired. Furthermore, Munoz stated that United was actively reaching out to the passenger to engage in direct conversation and to "further address and resolve this situation," a commitment also reported by Reuters. The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) has also indicated it will be monitoring the DOT's investigation, according to a statement released by the agency.

United Airlines is facing severe backlash and accusations of "brand genocide" after a passenger was forcibly removed from an overbooked flight. Public relations experts have widely condemned the airline's response to the crisis, labeling it a "world-class debacle on an epic scale" and a "gruesome, epic-scale fail."

Reputation management consultant Eric Schiffer believes this incident is far more damaging than United's previous public relations misstep involving female travelers wearing leggings. He characterized the current situation as a "gruesome, epic-scale fail that follows their leggings crash landing," and suggested the airline's CEO appears "clueless about dealing with the public and the customers." Schiffer argues that United is actively engaged in "brand genocide," systematically dismantling a once-trusted brand that now evokes stress and apprehension. He controversially suggested that United's crisis management mirrors that of "the Donald Trump School of Media Relations."

The incident, captured on video and widely shared, shows security agents forcibly removing a passenger from Flight 3411. The passenger, identified as a doctor reportedly needing to reach his patients, refused to leave voluntarily after the flight was declared overbooked. United Airlines' initial statement attempted to distance the company, stating the removal was not by United employees. The Chicago Department of Aviation later confirmed an officer involved was placed on leave, acknowledging the action was "not in accordance with our standard operating procedure." While United apologized for the overbooking, they directed further inquiries about the removal to authorities.

Schiffer contended that United's critical flaw was removing a passenger who had committed no offense. "This man was a professional — this isn’t someone who was trying to get over on United," Schiffer explained. "He was seated and paid for a seat, and this is a discretionary call on their part and it’s the kiss of death to customer trust. When you go onto a United flight, you don’t think there will be blood and you don’t think you’re going to be slammed in the face." United CEO Oscar Munoz later apologized for the necessity to "re-accommodate these customers" and pledged a review. However, Schiffer remained critical, stating, "I think their CEO learned public relations from the Nazi party," and predicted significant negative repercussions for sales due to public outrage over the physical removal and the airline's "Nazi party-like PR responses," which he deemed "catastrophic for a brand’s trust." Schiffer added that while United might perceive itself as a monopoly, customers can choose competitors if they feel their safety is compromised.

Chad Kawalec, president of Brand Identity Center, echoed Schiffer's sentiments, calling CEO Munoz's initial statement insufficient. "The CEO is absolving himself of anything," Kawalec told TheWrap. "Instead of taking responsibility, he just said there’s an internal investigation that will take place. It’s going to be next to impossible to completely recover from this unless they do something much bolder than their CEO’s announcement." Kawalec emphasized that brand integrity is built on trust, which United's actions have severely undermined. He highlighted the incident's potential to happen to any passenger, fostering widespread anxiety. "The most devastating thing is that the passenger wasn’t being rowdy — this is an incident that could happen to you, it could happen to me," he stated. "He did nothing, that’s why it’s extra severe. Their brand is currently at the mercy of public opinion and they are not controlling the public opinion."

Adding to the controversy, one journalist characterized the passenger's removal as an "act of racial violence," noting the passenger was of Asian descent. Kawalec pointed out that this detail, combined with the passenger's professional status and apparent innocence, will undoubtedly harm United's reputation.

For remedial actions, Schiffer suggested the CEO should resign or face board condemnation. Michael Bilello, founder of Centurion Strategies, recommended a 24-hour communication blackout to prevent further alienation, followed by a clear plan for internal and external reviews and a more empathetic social media campaign. "I would shut down all communication for a 24-hour period — there’s no point to further insult people with those statements and it doesn’t matter what they say, their brand has been damaged," Bilello stated. Schiffer acknowledged that while the immediate impact will be severe, United's financial resources might allow them to weather the crisis through extensive advertising, though customer attrition is unavoidable.

Bilello expressed a more somber outlook: "In my humble opinion, this is beyond anything they can do." He believes recovery will require considerable time, sustained efforts, and substantial travel offers to demonstrate tangible improvements. Ultimately, he concluded, only genuine public endorsement will signal a turnaround, as "United’s word means absolutely nothing. They are, what I would call, brand bankrupt."

United Airlines faced significant public backlash after a video surfaced showing a passenger being forcibly removed from a flight. The widely shared footage depicted security officers dragging a seated passenger down the aisle, sparking immediate outrage on social media.

In response to the controversy, United Airlines CEO Oscar Munoz released a statement: "Our team is moving with a sense of urgency to work with the authorities and conduct our own detailed review of what happened." He continued, "This is an upsetting event to all of us here at United. I apologize for having to re-accommodate these customers." Munoz also confirmed the airline was in direct contact with the passenger to address the situation.

The incident drew sharp criticism from several prominent individuals. Filmmakers Joss Whedon and Ava DuVernay voiced their strong disapproval on Twitter. Whedon humorously questioned his own upcoming flight's seating and sarcastically proposed alternatives to overbooking. DuVernay shared the video, denouncing United for a breach of customer trust and labeling the police actions a "cowardly assault." Actor Josh Gad, a recent United passenger, sarcastically thanked the airline for not requiring him to "tweet this message from a hospital bed." Late-night host Jimmy Kimmel, on his April 10th show, humorously compared the airline's actions to being woken for school and questioned their seat management. Model and TV personality Chrissy Teigen also offered a critical comment on Twitter regarding the airline's apology.

The flight, a Sunday evening service from Chicago to Louisville, was reportedly overbooked. A United spokesman, Charlie Hobart, informed the Associated Press that the passenger was among four individuals randomly chosen to relinquish their seats. Hobart stated that this passenger declined the request, asserting, "We followed the right procedures."

query: What is Morgan Freeman's response to the allegations of sexual harassment made against him?

documents.text:
Morgan Freeman has responded to accusations of sexual harassment, describing his actions as "misplaced compliments or humor" and differentiating them from sexual assault. This recent statement follows an earlier response and has drawn a significant reaction from an individual featured in a CNN report.

Tyra Martin, whose interview was part of the CNN report, claims the network distorted her words and presented them without proper context. Speaking with TMZ, Martin clarified that she is not among those making accusations and has faced negative consequences due to this misrepresentation. She recounted an instance where, after she adjusted her skirt, Freeman remarked, "Oh, don't pull it down now."

Freeman conveyed profound regret, stating, "I am devastated that 80 years of my life is at risk of being undermined, in the blink of an eye." He stressed the distinction between his alleged behavior and "horrific incidents of sexual assault." While holding firm to his position, Freeman extended an apology, adding that he will "continue to apologize to anyone I might have upset, however unintentionally."

Morgan Freeman's legal team has formally requested CNN issue an apology and retract its May 24th report, which detailed multiple allegations of sexual harassment against the celebrated actor. In a letter addressed to CNN President Jeff Zucker, attorney Robert M. Schwartz argued that the article, which presented accusations from eight women concerning Freeman's alleged harassment and inappropriate conduct, was "used to unjustly attack him."

Schwartz further asserted that the report was characterized by "malicious intent, falsehoods, sleight-of-hand, an absence of editorial control, and journalistic malpractice." He specifically accused one of the article's authors, Chloe Melas, of "baiting and prodding" witnesses and alleged victims, suggesting personal bias as a motive. Melas, in her account, described experiencing harassment from Freeman during a junket interview for "Going in Style." During this interview, he allegedly commented on her pregnancy, calling her "ripe" and remarking on her physique.

According to Schwartz's letter, an internal investigation conducted by Warner Bros., the film's producer, found no wrongdoing. It is noteworthy that both Warner Bros. and CNN share the same parent company, Time Warner. Schwartz vehemently disputed Melas's recollection of the interview, stating, "The problem with Ms. Melas’ account, which infected everything that she and CNN thereafter did, is that her version of the interview is false." He elaborated that her claims were based on her perception rather than actual events. Schwartz asserted that "substantial evidence" indicates Ms. Melas either fabricated the incident or significantly exaggerated a non-malicious remark, thereby creating a basis to harm Mr. Freeman's reputation.

A CNN spokesperson responded to the lawyer's letter, referencing Freeman's initial public apology following the story's publication. The spokesperson stated, "The unfounded accusations made by Mr. Freeman’s lawyer are disappointing and are difficult to reconcile with Mr. Freeman’s own public statements in the aftermath of the story." CNN affirmed its commitment to its reporting and vowed to "respond forcefully to any attempt by Mr. Freeman or his representatives to intimidate us from covering this important public issue."

In a subsequent statement, Schwartz countered CNN's position by asserting that they had provided the network with "objective evidence, including videotapes and on-the-record denials by the claimed ‘victims,’ that the alleged incident that gave rise to the story never happened." He claimed to have proven "beyond any doubt, that the whole story was built on fakery," thereby undermining the credibility of CNN's entire report. Schwartz concluded that CNN's decision to disregard this evidence confirmed their concerns regarding the network's reporters, lack of oversight, and "gross misconduct in unjustifiably attacking Mr. Freeman." The CNN report was co-written by An Phung and Chloe Melas.

Freeman, a prominent figure in Hollywood for four decades, has starred in numerous acclaimed films. These include "The Shawshank Redemption," "Million Dollar Baby," and "The Dark Knight." Following the initial report, Freeman issued an apology to anyone he may have "upset" while simultaneously denying any accusations of assault. The 80-year-old actor has already experienced professional repercussions. Visa suspended a marketing campaign, and the city of Vancouver withdrew its offer to make him the voice of its public transit system. SAG-AFTRA is also considering revoking the lifetime achievement award it bestowed upon him earlier this year.

Schwartz's letter also pointed out that Tyra Martin of Chicago WGN-TV, identified in the article as one of Freeman's accusers, has stated that CNN misrepresented her remarks. While Melas was the only accuser named in the story, CNN reportedly interviewed 16 individuals for the piece. Eight of these individuals alleged harassment, and the other eight corroborated their accounts. Beyond challenging the reporting itself, Freeman's attorney is also demanding that CNN retract parts of the story pertaining to his producing partner, Lori McCreary. The article accused McCreary and Freeman of cultivating a "toxic" work environment at their production company, Revelations Entertainment. Within this environment, women were allegedly demeaned. It also referenced comments Freeman made about McCreary's attire at a 2016 industry conference.

Morgan Freeman's legal team has formally demanded CNN retract its investigative report detailing allegations of sexual misconduct against the esteemed actor. The actor himself has issued two public statements addressing the accusations. CNN, however, has affirmed its commitment to its reporting.

### Legal Demand for Retraction

A ten-page legal correspondence, obtained by The Hollywood Reporter, has been dispatched to Jeff Zucker, president of CNN. The letter asserts that the published story has subjected Mr. Freeman to defamation, causing "serious injury" to his professional standing and career trajectory. Attorney Robert M. Schwartz, representing Mr. Freeman, stated, "At a minimum, CNN immediately needs to issue a retraction and apologize to Mr. Freeman through the same channels, and with the same level of attention, that it used to unjustly attack him on May 24." The missive further contends, "CNN also needs to retract the portions of the story that concern Lori McCreary and apologize to her for defaming and injuring her."

### Challenging CNN's Reporting

Freeman's legal team has initiated an investigation into the veracity of CNN's report and has shared preliminary findings. Regarding the three individuals identified by CNN as "victims":

#### Chloe Melas Allegations

The letter argues that CNN's own reporter, Chloe Melas, lacked a reasonable basis to interpret Mr. Freeman's remarks or actions during a "Going In Style" interview last year as directed at her or as constituting harassment. The available videotape evidence, according to the letter, substantiates that his statement was unrelated to her and not of a harassing nature. Furthermore, an independent investigation by the Warner Bros. Human Resources Department reportedly concluded that her claim was unsubstantiated by factual evidence.

#### Tyra Martin's Statements

The legal document asserts that Tyra Martin has publicly stated on two occasions since the article's publication that CNN misrepresented her statements and that Mr. Freeman did not harass her.

#### Lori McCreary's Position

The letter indicates that Lori McCreary informed CNN that Mr. Freeman never harassed her. Additionally, concerning CNN's commentary about Ms. McCreary herself, the document states that an independent party investigated the claim when raised by CNN and deemed it meritless.

### Allegations of Reporter Misconduct

The attorney further alleges that Ms. Melas actively solicited negative commentary from purported "witnesses" and attempted to elicit confirmation of her preconceived bias against Mr. Freeman. This tactic, the letter suggests, compromises the credibility of all anonymous sources cited in the CNN article.

### CNN's Response and Freeman's Defense

In response to the legal demand, a CNN spokesperson issued a statement to The Hollywood Reporter: "The unfounded accusations made by Mr. Freeman’s lawyer are disappointing and are difficult to reconcile with Mr. Freeman’s own public statements in the aftermath of the story. CNN stands by its reporting and will respond forcefully to any attempt by Mr. Freeman or his representatives to intimidate us from covering this important public issue."

Attorney Schwartz elaborated in a subsequent statement to THR, "We presented CNN with objective evidence, including videotapes and on-the-record denials by the claimed 'victims,' that the alleged incident that gave rise to the story never happened. We proved to CNN, beyond any doubt, that the whole story was built on fakery. The credibility of the entire CNN attack on Mr. Freeman has now been undermined. And in choosing to ignore all of the evidence that we presented, CNN has confirmed our concerns about its reporters, its lack of oversight, and its gross misconduct in unjustifiably attacking Mr. Freeman."

### The Original Report and Its Fallout

The original CNN report on May 24 detailed accusations of sexual misconduct from eight women, describing various instances of alleged inappropriate behavior by Mr. Freeman throughout his career. In total, 16 individuals spoke with CNN for the investigation, comprising eight witnesses and eight individuals claiming to be victims. Chloe Melas, the CNN entertainment reporter who co-authored the article, was the sole accuser to speak on the record.

As previously reported by THR, Lori McCreary, co-founder of Freeman's production company Revelations, was the subject of a controversial remark by Mr. Freeman during a 2016 "Produced By" panel. He commented on her attire, stating, "She wants to be thought of as serious. But you can't get away from the short dresses." While THR's reporter observed that Ms. McCreary did not visibly react at the time, a former Revelations executive told CNN that Ms. McCreary was distressed by the comment, describing her as "clearly upset," "surprised," and finding it "hurtful and embarrassing," even "devastated."

Following the allegations of harassment toward female journalists and staffers at his production company, Mr. Freeman released two statements. The repercussions were immediate, with Vancouver's public transit system and Visa suspending marketing campaigns featuring the actor. SAG-AFTRA also announced it was reconsidering its Lifetime Achievement Award to Mr. Freeman. Entertainment Tonight also re-aired a video of the actor addressing his behavior.

### Freeman's Public Statements

In his initial statement on Thursday, Freeman stated, "Anyone who knows me or has worked with me knows I am not someone who would intentionally offend or knowingly make anyone feel uneasy. I apologize to anyone who felt uncomfortable or disrespected — that was never my intent."

Over the weekend, the actor issued a more comprehensive apology: "I am devastated that 80 years of my life is at risk of being undermined, in the blink of an eye, by Thursday’s media reports. All victims of assault and harassment deserve to be heard. And we need to listen to them. But it is not right to equate horrific incidents of sexual assault with misplaced compliments or humor. I admit that I am someone who feels a need to try to make women — and men — feel appreciated and at ease around me. As a part of that, I would often try to joke with and compliment women, in what I thought was a light-hearted and humorous way. Clearly I was not always coming across the way I intended. And that is why I apologized Thursday and will continue to apologize to anyone I might have upset, however unintentionally. But I also want to be clear: I did not create unsafe work environments. I did not assault women. I did not offer employment or advancement in exchange for sex. Any suggestion that I did so is completely false."

Film and TV producer Lori McCreary, a key figure in Hollywood and long-time business partner of Morgan Freeman, is facing allegations of making discriminatory remarks. A CNN report details accusations of harassment and inappropriate behavior by Freeman, with McCreary implicated for her own conduct towards a female job candidate and female employees with families. One accuser stated that McCreary witnessed Freeman's alleged behavior.

McCreary, 57, co-founded Revelations Entertainment with Freeman and currently leads the Producers Guild of America (PGA). She has not yet commented on the allegations detailed in the CNN report. Revelations Entertainment, the company she co-founded with Freeman, is behind the CBS series “Madam Secretary” and National Geographic shows such as “The Story of Us With Morgan Freeman” and “The Story of God With Morgan Freeman.” Their collaborative filmography includes titles like “Invictus,” “Mutiny,” “10 Items or Less,” and “The Magic of Belle Isle.”

The CNN report outlines that eight women, including co-author Chloe Melas, have come forward alleging they were victims of harassment or inappropriate behavior by Freeman. An additional eight individuals reported witnessing his conduct. These 16 individuals collectively described a pattern of inappropriate behavior by Freeman across various settings, including film sets, promotional events, and at Revelations Entertainment. The women reported feeling uncomfortable at work, with two alleging unwanted touching, including an incident where Freeman reportedly attempted to lift a woman's skirt. Fear for their jobs was cited as a reason for their delayed reporting. Morgan Freeman has issued a statement acknowledging that anyone who knows him would recognize he would not intentionally offend or make anyone feel uneasy, and he apologized to anyone who felt uncomfortable or disrespected, stating it was never his intent.

**McCreary's Alleged Discriminatory Remarks**

Beyond the accusations against Morgan Freeman, the CNN report also features accounts from former employees describing actions by Lori McCreary that created a difficult work environment for women, particularly those with families.

One former Revelations employee told CNN that during a phone call with a member of the Producers Guild of America, McCreary allegedly stated about a job candidate, "she’ll never be able to do a good job, she has a family." Furthermore, two other senior-level Revelations employees reported that McCreary frequently mocked women who needed to leave work for family commitments or school events. She reportedly suggested that some employees could not handle demanding workloads because they would "run home" to their families rather than stay late at the office. Despite publicly advocating for work-life balance, McCreary allegedly made "snide" remarks when women departed work early, according to these sources.

In response to these allegations, the Producers Guild of America issued a statement asserting its commitment as an Equal Opportunity Employer, emphasizing that marital or parental status is not a factor in their hiring practices. The PGA stated that upon notification from CNN, they conducted an investigation and found the allegations to be without merit. They described Lori McCreary as an "outstanding PGA President" and a "consistent, vocal, and proactive advocate for women and all who are underrepresented in our community."

**Support for #MeToo and 'Time's Up' Movements**

In her role as President of the Producers Guild of America, Lori McCreary has been a strong supporter of the #MeToo and "Time's Up" movements. Alongside co-president Gary Lucchesi, she was instrumental in banning Harvey Weinstein from the organization for life and establishing formal guidelines and best practices concerning sexual harassment. McCreary and Lucchesi have led the PGA since 2014.

McCreary highlighted the PGA's efforts in forming a diverse task force comprising members from various sectors of the industry, including studios, independent film, television, and streaming platforms. She noted the enthusiastic participation of busy producers in this initiative. McCreary also emphasized the PGA's focus on diversity and gender parity as long-term solutions to underlying cultural issues within the industry, stating that producers hold significant power and responsibility.

Speaking at the Produced By NY conference in October 2017, McCreary urged producers to adopt different approaches, suggesting that a more diverse film crew, mirroring the audience, would lead to a better industry and improved interpersonal conduct, preventing the perpetuation of negative issues.

**Long-Standing Partnership with Morgan Freeman**

Lori McCreary's professional relationship with Morgan Freeman dates back to 1993, when she produced her first film, an adaptation of the play “Bopha!” with Freeman directing. The pair co-founded Revelations Entertainment in 1996 with the stated mission of producing content that "reveals truth." Freeman has indicated that the company was established due to his frustration with the limited roles available for Black actors.

McCreary described their creative and financial alignment during discussions for “Bopha!,” noting their shared perspectives. She characterized Freeman as a man of integrity and guileless nature, highlighting their enduring partnership of over 20 years as one of Hollywood's longest-lasting.

In 2016, McCreary herself was reportedly the subject of comments by Freeman that were perceived as inappropriate. During a panel discussion attended by approximately 400 people, Freeman recounted his first meeting with McCreary, remarking on the brevity of her dress. The moderator interjected, and Freeman continued, suggesting that while McCreary desired to be taken seriously, her attire was noteworthy. According to The Hollywood Reporter, McCreary did not visibly react at the time. Freeman later characterized the comment as a joke made in jest when questioned about it on NBC's “Today” show. A former Revelations executive told CNN that McCreary was visibly upset and found the comments hurtful and embarrassing. At the panel, Freeman reportedly described his remarks as "sexist... but I'm not misogynistic," while also calling McCreary a "good person" and a "workaholic" with whom he shared a "great relationship." McCreary, in turn, used the words "trustworthy" and "integrity" to describe Freeman during the same panel.

**Educational Background and Early Career**

Lori McCreary, a native of California, grew up in Antioch. She graduated from UCLA in 1984 with a degree in Computer Science. Her early interest in theater began at age 8, with aspirations of opening her own theater.

In an interview with Luke Ford in 2002, McCreary discussed her upbringing in Antioch, a blue-collar town. Her mother was a homemaker and actress who had previously put her career on hold to raise her family. Her father worked at DuPont before becoming a real estate broker. McCreary has two younger siblings, one of whom, R. Dean McCreary, is an aspiring actor and writer whose screenplay has been optioned by Revelations Entertainment.

A significant experience in her youth involved the installation of a state-of-the-art computerized lighting board at a local theater. When the computer failed on opening night, she had to operate the show manually, an experience that influenced her decision to study computers. At UCLA, she was one of a small number of women in the Computer Science program. Her passion for theater remained, and she did not enter the film industry until 1985 after seeing the play “Bopha!” and believing it deserved a wider audience.

During her time at UCLA, McCreary co-founded CompuLaw, a successful software company that assisted lawyers with billing. She later sold the company, having worked as a programmer for several years. She described her career in programming as enjoyable but ultimately leading to boredom, which fueled her desire for a different path.

**Commitment to Social Justice and Untold Stories**

Lori McCreary has articulated a strong commitment to social justice, viewing it as central to her work as a producer and her leadership at Revelations Entertainment. Her primary goal is to bring to light stories that are rarely heard or are presented from underrepresented perspectives.

She explained that Revelations Entertainment focuses on stories that offer new insights into situations, people, or ways of life previously unseen. This guiding principle has shaped their work for approximately 25 years. McCreary and Freeman share an interest in diverse storytelling, and some projects they have held for two decades are now gaining traction, such as a project about Bass Reeves, the first Black U.S. Marshal west of the Mississippi.

In a March 2018 interview with the National Academy of Engineering, McCreary confirmed that social justice is indeed at the core of her professional endeavors. She elaborated that even when revisiting well-known stories, Revelations Entertainment seeks a fresh angle or perspective. For instance, while Nelson Mandela's story had been told, they focused on a different aspect, such as the South African rugby team, to reveal something new. McCreary expressed a desire to uncover and tell more American stories, citing films like “Hidden Figures” as examples of impactful narratives that deserve to be told.

Allegations of sexual harassment and unwanted touching have been leveled against actor Morgan Freeman. A comprehensive CNN investigation, published in May 2018, detailed accounts from eight individuals who described a consistent pattern of unwanted advances by the Oscar-winning actor on movie sets and at professional events. Freeman, then 80, has categorically denied these accusations.

In response to the CNN report, Morgan Freeman's legal counsel, Robert M. Schwartz, dispatched a 10-page letter to CNN president Jeff Zucker, demanding a retraction. Schwartz characterized the report as the result of "malicious intent, falsehoods, slight-of-hand (sic), an absence of editorial control, and journalistic malpractice." His firm also initiated an independent investigation into CNN's reporting methods and findings. The letter further requested an apology for both Freeman and his producing partner, Lori McCreary. Schwartz asserted that McCreary, co-founder of Revelation Entertainment and co-president of the Producers Guild of America, had informed CNN reporters she had not experienced harassment.

However, reporters Chloe Melas and An Phung wrote that McCreary had witnessed inappropriate behavior from Freeman and had been subjected to sexist remarks during a 2016 panel discussion. The Hollywood Reporter covered this event, quoting Freeman as commenting on McCreary: “She doesn’t want to be thought of as a pretty face. She wants to be thought of as serious. But you can’t get away from the short dresses.”

Schwartz also highlighted comments made by Chloe Melas during a Friday Headline News interview, where she explained her own experience with Freeman at a 2017 press junket for *Going in Style* prompted her investigation. Melas stated, "The impetus for the story and this whole investigation was actually my own experience with Morgan Freeman at a junket last year, for the movie Going In Style. Right when I walked into the room, he began making sexually suggestive comments to me. Now, as an entertainment reporter for over a decade, it was truly unlike anything I have ever experienced." She noted one comment, captured on tape, where Freeman allegedly said to her, “Boy do I wish I was there,” while looking her up and down. Melas, who was six months pregnant at the time, stated her co-stars, Alan Arkin and Michael Caine, were present and reportedly looked at Freeman when he made the comment. Melas emphasized the comment was recorded and pointed to Freeman's gaze in the clip as evidence.

Morgan Freeman has publicly stated, "I did not assault women."

Schwartz contended that a video recording of an interview featuring Freeman's co-stars, Michael Caine and Alan Arkin, "makes clear" Freeman was responding to an embarrassing anecdote where he complimented a pregnant woman who then revealed she was not expecting. He argued Melas's account was inaccurate and unduly influenced subsequent reporting. Schwartz further highlighted that two accusers, Tyra Martin and Lori McCreary, had explicitly denied being harassed. Martin has publicly stated on two occasions since the CNN article's publication that CNN misrepresented her words. Schwartz also accused Melas of prompting sources to "say bad things about Mr. Freeman and tried to get them to confirm her bias against him," questioning the credibility of anonymous sources. He referenced comments from Reddit and Twitter to support his arguments.

Melas and Phung maintained they interviewed 16 individuals, eight of whom reported experiencing or witnessing harassment or inappropriate behavior from Freeman. These accounts, they stated, collectively described a pattern of inappropriate behavior on set, during promotions, and at his production company, Revelations Entertainment.

Following an initial brief denial, Freeman issued a more detailed statement to The New York Times. He acknowledged, "All victims of assault and harassment deserve to be heard. And we need to listen to them." However, he distinguished between "horrific incidents of sexual assault" and what he termed "misplaced compliments or humor." Freeman admitted to attempting to make people feel appreciated and at ease through jokes and compliments, acknowledging his efforts "not always coming across the way I intended." He firmly stated, "I did not create unsafe work environments. I did not assault women. I did not offer employment or advancement in exchange for sex. Any suggestion that I did so is completely false."

Schwartz's demand for a retraction followed announcements by Visa and Vancouver's transit system suspending advertisements voiced by Freeman. CNN responded to the lawyer's letter, stating, "The unfounded accusations made by Mr. Freeman’s lawyer are disappointing and are difficult to reconcile with Mr. Freeman’s own public statements in the aftermath of the story. CNN stands by its reporting and will respond forcefully to any attempt by Mr. Freeman or his representatives to intimidate us from covering this important public issue."

Here's a breakdown of the allegations and responses concerning Morgan Freeman:

*   **CNN Investigation:** A May 2018 report detailed accounts from eight women alleging sexual harassment and unwanted touching by the actor.
*   **Freeman's Denial:** Morgan Freeman has categorically denied all accusations, stating, "I did not assault women."
*   **Legal Response:** Freeman's lawyer, Robert M. Schwartz, sent a 10-page letter to CNN demanding a retraction, calling the report "journalistic malpractice."
*   **Specific Accusations:**
    *   Reporter Chloe Melas described sexually suggestive comments from Freeman during a 2017 press junket for *Going in Style*, stating he looked her up and down while she was six months pregnant.
    *   Lori McCreary, Freeman's producing partner, was reportedly subjected to sexist remarks by Freeman during a 2016 panel discussion.
*   **Counterarguments and Denials:**
    *   Schwartz claimed a video of co-stars Michael Caine and Alan Arkin supported Freeman's account of the *Going in Style* incident.
    *   Tyra Martin and Lori McCreary are cited as accusers who explicitly denied being harassed. Martin has stated CNN misrepresented her words.
    *   Schwartz accused Melas of prompting sources and trying to confirm her bias.
*   **Freeman's Statement to The New York Times:** Freeman acknowledged that victims deserve to be heard but distinguished between "assault" and "misplaced compliments or humor," admitting his jokes and compliments may not have always been received as intended. He denied creating unsafe work environments or engaging in quid pro quo harassment.
*   **CNN's Stance:** CNN stands by its reporting and stated they would "respond forcefully to any attempt... to intimidate us."
*   **Impact:** Advertisements voiced by Freeman were suspended by Visa and Vancouver's transit system following the allegations.

Update, with CNN response CNN, in a response to Schwartz’s letter, is standing by its reporting. Read the network’s response here. Previous Here is the latest response from Morgan Freeman to the sexual harassment allegations alleged in a CNN investigative piece. Freeman, who has lost endorsements and has said he is devastated by the accusations, initially apologized and but then clarified. Now his attorney, Robert M. Schwartz of Irell & Manella, has emailed a 10-page letter to CNN chief Jeff Zucker, implying that this might become a lawsuit. Deadline has reached out to CNN and will post the network’s response when we have it. In the CNN report, multiple women accused Freeman of subjecting them to harassment or inappropriate behavior on set, while promoting his movies and at his production company Revelations Entertainment. One production assistant said she was subjected to unwanted touching and comments about her figure and clothing on a nearly daily basis, including one incident in which the 80-year-old actor allegedly “kept trying to lift up my skirt” while inquiring if she was wearing underwear. CNN also reported that three entertainment writers recounted inappropriate remarks made to them during press junkets. In his letter to Zucker, Schwartz writes, “It has been said that ‘A lie gets halfway around the world before the truth can get its boots on.’ In just the few days since CNN published the article on Mr. Freeman, it has traveled all the way around the world and back, millions of times. If CNN has any decency, or any allegiance to journalistic integrity, it will immediate retract the article and issue a public apology to Mr. Freeman.” One of the entertainment writers who says she was harassed by Freeman at a junket is CNN’s Chloe Melas. In Schwartz’s letter, the actor’s attorney claims Melas “had no reasonable basis to have interpreted what Mr. Freeman said or did at the Going In Style interview last year as having been directed at her or as any form of harassment. The videotape confirms that his statement had nothing to do with her and was not harassing.” In addition to disputing the harassment allegations, Schwartz questions CNN’s “journalistic integrity” in allowing Melas to work on the harassment story. “Under bedrock principles of journalistic integrity, CNN should not have allowed Ms. Melas to work on the story,” he writes. “As a supposed victim of the article’s subject, Ms. Melas lacked the requisite impartiality and objectivity to fairly cover the story she was chasing. Under those circumstances, CNN should not have allowed her to write it.” Here is Schwartz’s letter, in full, to Zucker: VIA E-MAIL Mr. Jeff Zucker President, CNN CNN Center 190 Marietta Street NW Atlanta, Georgia 30303 Re: Morgan Freeman Dear Mr. Zucker: This law firm represents Morgan Freeman. On Thursday, May 24, 2018, Mr. Freeman was the subject of an article that CNN published on its website by reporters Chloe Melas and An Phung. Given that Mr. Freeman is a world-renowned actor, and that the article sought to associate him with Hollywood actors and executives who have used their positions to trade sex for career advancement, it will come as no surprise to you that CNN’s article attracted explosive attention in newspapers and websites throughout the world. But no one who read CNN’s article about Mr. Freeman was told that it was the product of malicious intent, falsehoods, slight-of-hand, an absence of editorial control, and journalistic malpractice. I am writing to bring some of these issues to your attention. Our own investigation has just begun. But at just this early stage, our review confirms that: • Of the three people CNN identified as being a “victim,” the first, CNN’s own Chloe Melas, had no reasonable basis to have interpreted what Mr. Freeman said or did at the Going In Style interview last year as having been directed at her or as any form of harassment. The videotape confirms that his statement had nothing to do with her and was not harassing. And an independent third party, the Warner Bros. Human Resources Department, investigated her claim and concluded that it was not supported by the facts. • The second person CNN identified, Tyra Martin, has gone on record twice since CNN published the article to state that CNN misrepresented what she said to CNN and that Mr. Freeman did not harass her. • The third person CNN identified, Lori McCreary, told CNN that Mr. Freeman never harassed her. And as to CNN’s gratuitous sideswipe at Ms. McCreary herself, yet another independent party investigated the claim when CNN raised it, and found it to be meritless. • Ms. Melas baited and prodded supposed “witnesses” to say bad things about Mr. Freeman and tried to get them to confirm her bias against him. Thus, no reader of the article can have any confidence that any of the anonymous sources, which make up the balance of CNN’s article, can be relied upon at all. Based on those facts, and the additional information presented below, it is clear that CNN has defamed Mr. Freeman. CNN has inflicted serious injury on his reputation and career. At a minimum, CNN immediately needs to issue a retraction and apologize to Mr. Freeman through the same channels, and with the same level of attention, that it used to unjustly attack him on May 24. CNN also needs to retract the portions of the story that concern Lori McCreary and apologize to her for defaming and injuring her. CNN’s reporter, Chloe Melas, has admitted that she was inspired to write the story and, in fact, to conduct her “whole investigation” into Mr. Freeman, by her experience in 2017 while interviewing Mr. Freeman and his Going In Style co-stars. Here’s what she said when she appeared on CNN Headline News on May 25: The impetus for the story and this whole investigation was actually my own experience with Morgan Freeman at a junket last year, for the movie Going In Style. Right when I walked into the room, he began making sexually suggestive comments to me. Now, as an entertainment reporter for over a decade, it was truly unlike anything I have ever experienced. One of those comments was caught on tape. In this tape, he says to me, “Boy do I wish I was there,” while looking me up and down. I was six months pregnant at the time. And his co-stars Alan Arkin and Michael Caine were seated on either side of him and actually looked at him when he made this comment to me. Again, it was caught on tape. And take a note of Freeman’s eyes in this clip. The problem with Ms. Melas’ account, which infected everything that she and CNN thereafter did, is that her version of the interview is false. It is based on her imagining that Mr. Freeman had said or done anything to harass her. However, there is substantial evidence that Ms. Melas imagined an incident, or exaggerated a non-malicious remark wildly out of proportion to reality, to give her a basis to go after Mr. Freeman and cause him the grave harm that CNN’s story has inflicted. It is correct that, during the interview, Mr. Freeman said, “I wish I was there.” But Ms. Melas had no factual basis to have interpreted that as a statement about her, or as sexual harassment. The videotape makes clear that Mr. Freeman was in fact responding to a story that Michael Caine had just told. In that story, Mr. Caine had congratulated a woman on becoming pregnant, only to learn to Mr. Caine’s (and the woman’s) embarrassment that she was not pregnant. When Mr. Freeman said “I wish I was there,” any reasonable viewer would have known that the “there” to which he was referring was the conversation in which Mr. Freeman’s friend, Mr. Caine, had embarrassed himself. That is exactly what Mr. Freeman intended. Despite what should have been clear to Ms. Melas, she chose to interpret Michael Caine’s anecdote, and Mr. Freeman’s remark about it, as having something to do with her and as harassment. One cannot know if that was the product of something as innocuous as Ms. Melas’ having misheard what Mr. Freeman said, her runaway self-centeredness, or her search for a sexual harassment perpetrator to “expose” so that she could grab attention and advance her career. One also has to ask whether Ms. Melas would have had the same unjustified overreaction if the remark had come from Michael Caine or Alan Arkin. Regardless, nothing about what Mr. Freeman said—or any “look in his eyes”—supports Ms. Melas’ takeaway. Ms. Melas nonetheless made a conscious decision to treat Mr. Freeman’s comment as a form of sexual harassment, and then set out on a crusade to vilify him. As one person who saw Ms. Melas’ Going In Style interview reacted: I just can’t believe that not a single person at @CNN or @News Day watched that @Chloe_Melas video and didn’t think to tell her that Morgan Freeman was talking about Michael Caine and not her. Way to play the victim, @Chloe_Melas. Others have seen the video that served as the “impetus … for this whole investigation” into Mr. Freeman and reacted with equal disgust at how CNN has spun it against him: [Chloe Melas] is straight up lying about what was said to her and lying that he was even looking at her. Question is why is she still employed? Another person noted: I call BS on this story, especially the clip where Morgan Freeman says “I wish I was there” in response to a story Michael Caine just finished telling. It is not the responsibility of the victim of CNN’s sloppy and malicious journalism to do the fact-checking that CNN should have done before it ran this scandal-mongering hit piece on Mr. Freeman. But as explained below, it is clear at just this early point in investigation that CNN approached this story with a reckless disregard for the truth and with a malicious intent to harm Mr. Freeman. 1. Under bedrock principles of journalistic integrity, CNN should not have allowed Ms. Melas to work on the story. As a supposed victim of the article’s subject, Ms. Melas lacked the requisite impartiality and objectivity to fairly cover the story she was chasing. Under those circumstances, CNN should not have allowed her to write it. Numerous well-respected journalists, including current and former editors of The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, and The Washington Post have noted CNN’s breach of this basic rule of integrity and expressed surprise that CNN allowed this to happen here. As Leslie Wayne, a former business reporter at the New York Times, said bluntly, the breakdown in journalistic ethics that CNN inflicted on Mr. Freeman “would not be allowed” at The Times. Id. If CNN were committed to impartial reporting about Mr. Freeman, on a topic of extreme sensitivity and with the potential to destroy him, CNN should not have allowed that breakdown to occur. But CNN did. 2. CNN chose to accept Ms. Melas’ attack on Mr. Freeman even though all of the objective evidence undermined her account. The videotape of Ms. Melas’ interview with Morgan Freeman, Michael Caine, and Alan Arkin is objective evidence of what happened. It refutes her belief that Mr. Freeman’s remarks were directed to her or could have reasonably been viewed as offensive. As noted above, and as the videotape demonstrates, it is obvious—painfully so—that Mr. Freeman’s remark, “I wish I was there,” concerned Michael Caine’s story. Nothing about it could in any reasonable way be construed as a comment about Ms. Melas or as harassment. If CNN had not left this story to Ms. Melas to assemble, impartial editors would have viewed the objective evidence and told her that either: (a) she had made a mistake, or (b) she was over-reaching and trying to attack Mr. Freeman. CNN’s failure to provide this kind of check and oversight is a textbook case of irresponsible journalism. CNN disregarded additional objective evidence. As you know, Ms. Melas complained to the Warner Bros. Human Resources Department at the time. They investigated her accusations. They found her claim to lack sufficient factual support to merit pursuing it further. In other words, having reviewed the segment and having interviewed others who were present, the Warner Bros. Human Resources Department did not agree with how Ms. Melas was attempting to spin it. CNN’s article, however, deceptively downplays that independent third party’s conclusion that Ms. Melas had no basis to treat Mr. Freeman’s comments as harassment: Warner Bros. HR could not corroborate the account because only one of Freeman’s remarks was on video and the Warner Bros. employees present did not notice anything. That is indefensibly misleading. The remark that was captured on the video, and the remark that the Warner Bros. Human Resources Department personnel did see, was the key statement that Ms. Melas interpreted as harassment, namely, “I wish I was there.” The Warner Bros. Human Resources Department responded to Ms. Melas’ complaint. Its personnel did not The Warner Bros. Human Resources Department responded to Ms. Melas’ complaint. Its personnel did not believe that that statement constituted harassment of her because it was obviously not about her, or for other reasons. Based on these facts, an honest account required CNN to have said something more like this: After looking at the Going In Style interview, Warner Bros. HR Department personnel disagreed with Ms. Melas that the remark “I wish I was there” concerned her or constituted harassment. Other Warner Bros. employees were present while she taped the interview. Yet none of them heard or saw anything to substantiate Ms. Melas’ accusation that Mr. Freeman had said or done anything that constituted harassment. As a result, Warner Bros.’ HR Department declined to pursue the matter further. Such an account should and would have caused CNN to question whether there was even a story to pursue. But CNN ignored that. Even worse, that non-story caused CNN to give Ms. Melas a green light to use CNN’s considerable resources to launch a year-long witch hunt against Mr. Freeman. That is not something the editors of The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, or The Washington Post would have allowed to happen. But again, CNN did. 3. CNN urged people to claim they had been harassed, even when they said they had not. After getting CNN’s permission to pursue an unjustified and scandalous vendetta against Mr. Freeman, Ms. Melas cast her net far and wide. We believe that she created lists of thousands of people who worked on the motion pictures that Mr. Freeman had worked on over more than ten years. We also believe that she called hundreds of them, trolling to find anyone who would, by saying something negative about Mr. Freeman, confirm her bias against him. That is a shady journalistic tactic, particularly when CNN’s published article fails to disclose what the writer did. After one year’s work, Ms. Melas appears to have found a few people who were willing to say bad things about Mr. Freeman. CNN has kept their names out of the story, so we are at a disadvantage in trying to verify their assertions. But within hours after CNN published the article, many people that CNN contacted to generate the story came forward—unprompted—to criticize CNN publicly for its improper conduct and its outright misrepresentation of what they had said to CNN about Mr. Freeman. Tyra Martin, Senior Segment Producer at Chicago’s WGN News, is mentioned in the CNN article. In fact, she and Lori McCreary are the only two named people in the story, besides Ms. Melas, who supposedly claim to have been harassed. But Ms. Martin’s actual experience and Ms. McCreary’s (as shown below), do not support CNN’s story. Ms. Martin has interviewed Mr. Freeman many times over the years, without feeling that his interactions with her were inappropriate. The CNN article says, however, that “Martin felt that one incident crossed a line,” when she got up after an interview and adjusted her dress. When Ms. Martin discussed these matters on WGN on May 24, she stated unambiguously that CNN had misrepresented the facts: Mr. Richards: Not one of those eight women accusing Mr. Freeman, but included in that CNN story for her experiences with Mr. Freeman, is WGN senior segment producer Tyra Martin. Tyra has interviewed Morgan Freeman several times. What’s your take on the story, in general? Ms. Martin: I’m sorry for anyone who’s had an unfortunate experience or feels harassed or assaulted. That wasn’t my experience with Morgan Freeman. The interviews were always fun for me. Mr. Potash: In the wake of the #MeToo movement, does it change your view of that conversation [with Mr. Freeman]? I don’t know how many years ago it was. Ms. Martin: It doesn’t. I understand how for some people, not just women, men even, that kind of interaction might not be fun or easygoing. But it always felt like we were in on a joke, that we were just kind of exchanging like that. Ms. Baumgarten: Do you feel that you were mischaracterized in how you were presented in the [CNN] story? Ms. Martin: Yes. Some misreporting got out that I was uncomfortable or that he had made sexual remarks to me every time. That’s just not the case. I never said that. It got picked up. And here’s where I learned how careful we have to be, because something gets picked up. And then someone else picks it up. And then it gets somewhere else. And now it’s in print. And it looks like the truth. And it’s just not. When Ms. Martin’s colleagues asked her about the one incident that, according to CNN’s article, Ms. Martin had supposedly said “crossed the line,” she said something quite different: “I never felt uncomfortable or in danger.” Id. CNN’s omission of that key fact gives a profoundly negative and misleading impression about what Ms. Martin actually said about Mr. Freeman. Lest there be any doubt that CNN misrepresented what Tyra Martin said, in the last few days she was even more emphatic: A woman mentioned in the CNN story – Tyra Martin, reached out to TMZ and said, “Hey, still getting a lot of nasty messages from people who think I am one of the accusers. I’m not, never was. CNN totally misrepresented the video and took my remarks out of context.” Tyra Martin is not alone. CNN approached many others to prod them into saying negative things about Mr. Freeman. In the three days since the story broke, we have already discovered that many of those people, after reading the CNN article, were shocked by CNN’s unsavory news-gathering practices: I was one of the people contacted by Chloe Melas …. She was cold-calling everyone who had ever worked with him even if, as in my case, only for a few days. During the course of my conversation, it became abundantly clear that this was a personal vendetta and she asked me not to “tell anyone” about what we discussed. She is the lowest form of “reporter.” Given that CNN was spending many months calling people to attempt to dig up dirt on Mr. Freeman, rumors had spread that CNN was doing so. Word had spread to potential “witnesses” even before Ms. Melas or one of her CNN colleagues called them. As a result, when a reporter would call to ask about harassment, if the reporter said she was from CNN, many people knew that the reporter was going after Mr. Freeman—not because of anything he had done, but because they knew the game CNN was playing. And if the person CNN was interviewing identified someone other than Mr. Freeman, the CNN reporter tried to bait the person into trashing him: Several other times during this investigation, when a CNN reporter contacted a person who had worked with Freeman to try to ask them if they had seen or been subject to inappropriate behavior by an actor they had worked with – not even initially naming the person they were asking about – the person would immediately tell them they knew exactly who the reporter had in mind: Morgan Freeman. This one’s good: She asked me if sexually inappropriate behavior on a set rang a bell. We had very briefly hired Stephen Collins from Seventh Heaven PRIOR to his story coming out (he was fired minutes after the story broke), so naturally I mentioned him. She kept baiting me to name Morgan; repeatedly asking the question, who had I worked with. So briefly I forgot he [Mr. Freeman] was even in the film. Finally, she had to name him [Mr. Freeman] herself. (emphasis added.) Id. Other people have made similar comments: “Chloe Melas who is spearheading the sexual accusations against Morgan Freeman has a serious ulterior motive for slandering Morgan Freeman’s name.” “Seems like pure revenge by the journalist or she wanted her 15 minutes of fame.” It is not surprising that, after making dozens, hundreds, or some other vast number of phone calls, Ms. Melas was able to find people who claimed to have seen or experienced something bad. But in view of Ms. Melas’ bias against Mr. Freeman and her determination to pressure people into twisting non-harassing behavior into something much worse, there is ample evidence for one to be concerned that she acted similarly in her dealings with all of the sources mentioned in her story. In other words, CNN should be concerned that the entire story is the product of manipulative practices and not an honest account of anything, from its alleged skirt-lifting incident on down to the bottom of the page. 4. CNN baselessly attacked Lori McCreary to further attack Mr. Freeman. Our concern that CNN’s article was a hit piece is further reinforced by the article’s underhanded treatment of Lori McCreary, Mr. Freeman’s business partner of more than 20 years. As to Ms. McCreary, CNN’s article is a trifecta of misstatements. First, CNN is flat out wrong in stating that Ms. McCreary “witnessed” Mr. Freeman engage in harassment and, implicitly, did nothing about it. Second, CNN states that Mr. Freeman harassed Ms. McCreary by making comments about what she was wearing 20 years earlier. Ms. McCreary did not feel harassed. In fact, a writer for the Hollywood Reporter who, unlike CNN, was there, heard him make the remarks and believed that they were not harassing. CNN knew that before it ran the story. CNN’s third assertion, that Ms. McCreary ruined someone’s job prospects at the Producers Guild of America, is so wrong and so reckless that it warrants special attention. CNN wrote that one of its anonymous sources supposedly confirmed that, “on a phone call with a member of PGA, McCreary said of a candidate vying for a position at PGA East, ‘she’ll never be able to do a good job, she has a family.’” It was wrong for CNN to have published that statement: • Ms. McCreary never told the PGA not to hire someone because they had a family, or anything like that. It is completely false. • Even worse, CNN knew that it was false before it published the story. When CNN told the PGA about the assertion, the PGA conducted an investigation. It found the claim to be without merit. That should have ended the matter. Why would CNN include a false accusation that had been investigated by a professional organization and found to be without merit, unless CNN wanted to create a scandal where there was none? That’s malice. Worse still, none of this had anything to do with whether Morgan Freeman, the subject of the article, had done anything wrong. CNN’s inclusion of its baseless diversion about Ms. McCreary confirms that CNN had a malicious agenda and was willing to say anything to achieve it. CNN owes a separate retraction and apology to Ms. McCreary. 5. CNN has inflicted substantial harm on Mr. Freeman. In light of the article’s subject matter, CNN had to have known before publishing the article, that the article would have devastating consequences for Mr. Freeman. And it has. Because of CNN’s exploitation of the issue and the eternal memory of the Internet, Mr. Freeman is being viewed in the global court of public opinion as someone akin to notorious harassers and accused rapists, such as Harvey Weinstein and Kevin Spacey. CNN had no justification for doing that. Predictably, those who work with Mr. Freeman have felt the need to distance themselves and to consider suspending further engagement. Ms. Melas herself gloated about this on CNN. No doubt, you have seen similar reports concerning some of Mr. Freeman’s other employers. The damage CNN has inflicted is real. And given Mr. Freeman’s career and many motion picture and television commitments, it is substantial. It has been said that “A lie gets halfway around the world before the truth can get its boots on.” In just the few days since CNN published the article on Mr. Freeman, it has traveled all the way around the world and back, millions of times. If CNN has any decency, or any allegiance to journalistic integrity, it will immediate retract the article and issue a public apology to Mr. Freeman. This letter is not intended to constitute a complete statement of all of the facts related to CNN’s conduct. Mr. Freeman is hereby reserving all of his rights, claims, and remedies against CNN and its employees. We await your response. Very truly yours, Robert M. Schwartz for IRELL & MANELLA LLP cc: Morgan Freeman

**Review of Chloe Melas's Allegations Against Morgan Freeman**

In May 2018, CNN reporter Chloe Melas presented allegations of sexual harassment concerning actor Morgan Freeman. A subsequent examination of the video clip associated with her report, however, does not provide conclusive evidence to substantiate these specific claims. This discrepancy between Melas's assertions and the visual evidence raises questions regarding the precise nature of her accusations against Freeman as depicted in this particular segment.

The interview footage in question was originally aired by HLN/CNN, a subsidiary of Time Warner. This clip is being utilized under fair use principles for the purposes of news reporting and critical analysis. The entity distributing this material maintains no formal connection with HLN/CNN or Time Warner and does not assert proprietary rights over the video content. For any legal matters or inquiries, direct communication with the original content provider is recommended. This presentation of the video will occur without the inclusion of advertisements.

query: What evidence have scientists gathered that suggests the existence of gravitational waves, as proposed by Einstein?

documents.text:
The scientific community is on the cusp of a monumental discovery, eagerly anticipating the direct observation of gravitational waves, phenomena first theorized by Albert Einstein a century ago. These subtle distortions in the fabric of space-time, predicted by his 1916 general theory of relativity, propagate at the speed of light. For decades, their existence was inferred through indirect evidence, but a significant announcement from the U.S. National Science Foundation may soon confirm their direct detection. This potential breakthrough promises to revolutionize our understanding of the cosmos, unlocking secrets of the universe's earliest moments and illuminating the nature of extreme celestial objects like black holes and neutron stars.

Researchers from leading institutions, including Caltech, MIT, and the LIGO Scientific Collaboration, are preparing to unveil a "status report" detailing their relentless pursuit of detecting these elusive waves. Physicist Bruce Allen of the Max Planck Institute for Gravitational Physics aptly described the potential discovery as a "Nobel Prize-winning venture," predicting a dramatic shift in our cosmic perspective within the next decade. Abhay Ashtekar, director of Penn State's Institute for Gravitation and the Cosmos, provided a clear definition: "literally ripples in the curvature of space-time, caused by collisions of heavy and compact objects like black holes and neutron stars."

These cosmic undulations represent a fundamentally different form of cosmic messenger compared to electromagnetic radiation. As NASA astrophysicist Ira Thorpe explained, "They’re waves, like light... Except here what’s ‘waving’ is space and time itself." He further elaborated on their origin, stating, "When you’re moving masses, you get gravitational waves." The detection of these incredibly faint signals hinges on highly sophisticated instruments. The Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO), with its twin detectors in Livingston, Louisiana, and Hanford, Washington, along with a similar facility in Italy, are at the forefront of this endeavor. These precisely engineered instruments are vital for distinguishing genuine gravitational wave signals from the pervasive noise of terrestrial interference. The National Science Foundation plays a crucial role in funding this groundbreaking project.

Our current understanding of the universe is overwhelmingly derived from the study of electromagnetic waves. However, this reliance presents limitations, as these waves can be scattered or absorbed over vast cosmic distances, obscuring much of the universe's hidden phenomena. Gravitational waves, predicted to travel unimpeded through the cosmos, offer an unparalleled and unobstructed window into these otherwise invisible events, positioning them as a revolutionary source of new astrophysical data.

Neutron stars and black holes, objects of immense mass and density, present significant observational challenges. Yet, through the detection of gravitational waves, they hold the potential to yield profound insights. Bruce Allen highlighted the limitations of current observational methods, noting, "People don’t really know what’s going on inside neutron stars." These celestial bodies, compressed into volumes no larger than a city despite possessing masses far exceeding our sun, possess an extraordinarily dense internal structure. Gravitational wave observations could provide unprecedented detail about the processes occurring "inside or around the object that’s producing the waves." For binary black hole systems, which emit no detectable light, gravitational waves are the sole means of observation, making their detection indispensable for studying these enigmatic objects.

Beyond the study of extreme celestial objects, gravitational waves offer a unique pathway to understanding the universe's earliest moments. For the initial approximately 200,000 years of its existence, the universe was opaque to light. Bruce Allen emphasized this crucial aspect, stating that "gravitational waves can travel freely, back to very early times," thereby enabling direct observation of the universe's nascent stages. This extraordinary potential for profound cosmological and astrophysical insights underscores the transformative impact that the direct detection of gravitational waves is poised to have on our scientific endeavors.

The potential detection of **gravitational waves**, predicted by Albert Einstein a century ago, represents a major breakthrough in physics. These ripples in the universe's fabric, if confirmed, would offer the **ultimate validation** of his general relativity theory. A press conference from Caltech and MIT is expected to announce whether these subtle distortions, likely caused by colliding black holes, have been detected.

Einstein's theories profoundly changed our view of space, time, and matter. He proposed that space and time are linked in a four-dimensional continuum called **spacetime**. Mass warps this fabric, guiding object paths, which we experience as gravity. Decades of experiments have consistently supported his ideas.

Several key findings support Einstein's theories:

*   **The Photoelectric Effect:** Einstein won the Nobel Prize for explaining light as energy packets called **photons**, a core idea in quantum physics.
*   **Mercury's Orbit:** General relativity precisely explained oddities in Mercury's orbit by showing how the Sun's mass warps spacetime.
*   **Gravitational Lensing:** A 1919 solar eclipse showed the Sun's mass bends light, acting as a **gravitational lens**. This is now vital for astronomy.
*   **Frame Dragging:** Massive, spinning objects twist spacetime. This was confirmed by satellite data and NASA's Gravity Probe B.
*   **Time Dilation and Redshift:** Gravity affects time and light frequency. Light from strong gravity areas shifts to redder hues. This effect is **crucial for GPS accuracy**, requiring corrections for satellite clocks.

An artist's concept illustrates a quasar, or feeding black hole, on March 13, 2012. Gravitational waves are theorized to form around objects with great mass like black holes The first-ever detection of gravitational waves, which scientists could announce Thursday, would open a new window on the universe and its most violent phenomena. Scientists will hold a press conference Thursday to discuss the latest in their hunt for these waves, whose existence Albert Einstein predicted in his theory of general relativity 100 years ago, according to a statement from the National Science Foundation, which has funded the research. Scientists from the California Institute of Technology (Caltech), the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and the Laser Interferometer Gravitational Wave Observatory (LIGO) who have been working on the detection of these waves for years will participate. Press conferences are also simultaneously scheduled at Paris's National Center for Science Research (CNRS) and also in London. The announcement of a press conference revived rumors that have been circulating in the scientific community for months that the LIGO team may have indeed directly detected gravitational waves for the first time. These waves are produced by disturbances in the fabric of space and time when a massive object moves, like a black hole or a neutron star. Albert Einstein, a memorial of him seen on January 22, 2016 in Washington, DC, theorized that waves would appear like ripples in a pond that form when a stone is thrown in the water Einstein theorized that they would appear like ripples in a pond that form when a stone is thrown in the water, or like a net that bows under the weight of an object placed within—with the net serving as a metaphor for the bending of space-time. According to the rumors, the team may have observed the collision of two black holes and their fusion—leading to the detection of gravitational waves. Science magazine cited Clifford Burgess, a physicist at McMaster University in Canada and also a member of the Perimeter Institute for Theoretical Physics, as saying he deemed the rumors credible, even though he had not yet seen any documentation from LIGO. New look at universe The ability to observe these gravitational waves would offer astronomer and physicists a new look at the most mysterious workings of the universe, including the fusion of neutron stars and the behaviors of black holes, which are often found in the centers of galaxies. "The driving force of the universe is gravity," said Tuck Stebbins, Gravitational Astrophysics Lab Chief at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center. "These waves are streaming to you all the time and if you could see them, you could see back to the first one trillionth of a second of the Big Bang," he told AFP. "There is no other way for humanity to see the origin of the universe." Stebbins said he believes "we stand at a threshold of a revolutionary period in our understanding, our view of the universe." The LIGO detectors—one in Washington and one in Louisiana—can "measure changes of spacetime at the level of 1/1000 diameter of a proton," he added. Catherine Man, an astronomer at the Cote d'Azur Observatory in France, said the detection of these waves—if confirmed—would allow astronomers to probe the interior of stars and perhaps resolve the mystery of gamma rays, which are among the most powerful explosions in the universe and whose cause remains poorly understood. "Now we are no longer observing the universe with telescopes using ultraviolet light or visible light but we are listening to the noises produced by the effects of the gravitation of celestial bodies on the fabric of space-time, which could come from stars or black holes," she told AFP. "And since the star or black hole does not stop these waves, which move at the speed of light, they come right to us and we can therefore make models... to distinguish and detect their signatures." Previously, two Princeton scientists won the Nobel Prize for Physics in 1993 for discovering a new type of pulsar that offered indirect proof of the existence of gravitational waves. The LIGO team is collaborating with a French-Italian team on another detector, called VIRGO, that should become operational soon. Explore further: Announcement Thursday on Einstein's gravitational waves

**Media Advisory: Historic Gravitational Wave Detection Breakthrough to be Announced**

**Washington D.C. – February 8, 2016** – A monumental scientific announcement is imminent, poised to reveal significant advancements in the detection of gravitational waves, a phenomenon predicted by Albert Einstein a century ago. The National Science Foundation (NSF) will convene a press conference featuring leading researchers from Caltech, MIT, and the LIGO Scientific Collaboration (LSC) to unveil these pivotal developments. This event marks a profound moment in our understanding of the cosmos, directly stemming from the ongoing efforts to observe these elusive ripples in spacetime.

The press conference is scheduled for Thursday, February 11, 2016, at 10:30 a.m. EST, and will be held at the National Press Club. The focus will be on the groundbreaking work being conducted with the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory (LIGO). This highly sophisticated facility is engineered to detect the faintest cosmic tremors, thereby opening an entirely new observational window into the universe. The centennial commemoration of Einstein's revolutionary prediction has amplified global anticipation for this announcement, underscoring its profound scientific and historical significance.

LIGO represents a colossal scientific endeavor, comprising two identical, exceptionally sensitive interferometers. These observatories, conceived and meticulously constructed by dedicated researchers at MIT and Caltech, are made possible through funding from the NSF, with crucial support from numerous international partners. The detectors are strategically situated in Livingston, Louisiana, and Hanford, Washington, allowing for comprehensive observation. The LIGO Scientific Collaboration (LSC), an expansive international consortium that includes the GEO600 and VIRGO collaborations, plays an indispensable role in the rigorous analysis of the data generated by these advanced instruments.

Journalists are cordially invited to attend this landmark event. Seating in the main press room will be limited, and an overflow room will be available to accommodate all interested media. Subject matter experts will be present to provide interviews and engage in question-and-answer sessions, offering deep insights into the scientific implications of this discovery. Media personnel are kindly requested to RSVP in advance to ensure that press credentials are prepared for seamless access. A mult box and wireless internet access will be provided for convenience. For those unable to attend in person, the event will be simulcast live online, with dedicated efforts to address questions submitted remotely, ensuring broad accessibility to this historic announcement.

*   **When:** Thursday, February 11, 2016, 10:30 a.m. US EST
*   **Where:** The National Press Club, Holeman Lounge, 529 14th Street NW, 13th Floor, Washington, DC 20045
