His conviction in 2009 stemmed from his advocacy for democratic reforms, a petition that led to his arrest.

China’s most famous dissident, Liu Xiaobo, has died in custody at age 61. The cause was liver cancer. His death, confirmed Thursday evening local time on July 13 by Chinese justice authorities (link in Chinese) in the northeastern city of Shenyang, comes more than two weeks after prison authorities announced he was being moved to a hospital for treatment, news that was followed by pleas from around the world to allow him to travel abroad for care. Thursday’s brief statement didn’t mention the Nobel Prize awarded to Liu for a life dedicated to the memory of the protesters killed in Beijing’s Tiananmen Square in 1989. In April 1989, Liu, a literary critic and comparative literature professor, was in New York City as a visiting scholar at Columbia University when he heard of the protests in Tiananmen Square. He hurried back to Beijing and spent weeks among the student protesters. Two days after the Communist Party’s deadly crackdown on June 4, Liu was detained and jailed. When he was freed in 1991, he resumed his public demands for greater freedoms in China over the next two decades. In 2008, along with other dissidents, he drafted Charter 08, a document that called on the Chinese state to fundamentally change its character and abandon one-party rule. Soon after he was tried for subversion. According to freedom-of-speech advocacy group PEN America, he was convicted in 2009 for writing seven sentences, a total of 224 Chinese characters. He was given an 11-year sentence. On June 26, 2017, after serving more than seven years of his sentence, the prison holding Liu announced that he was being transferred to a nearby hospital to be treated for late-stage liver cancer—the same disease that killed his father. Here is a timeline of the life of a man who has been deeply admired, who is being deeply mourned, and whose last days have become for many a symbol for many of the darkness that clouds the story of China’s economic rise. 1955 Liu Xiaobo is born on Dec. 28 in northeastern China’s Jilin province, the third of five brothers. 1966 The Cultural Revolution begins. Millions of urban youth head to the countryside to be “re-educated” by peasants, following leader Mao Zedong’s exhortations to “go up into the mountains and down to the countryside (上山下乡运动)” (paywall). Liu’s grandmother, whom he is very close to, is designated a “landlord” and sent away. Meanwhile, his father, a Communist Party member, is busy, and his older brothers go away to become Red Guards. Liu remembers this time of turmoil as one of unexpected freedom according to his biography, Steel Gate to Freedom: “My parents were off ‘revolutionizing,’ schools stopped, and I was for a time able to be rid of the constraints of an ‘education.'” 1977 Liu is admitted to Jilin University and studies Chinese literature. During his university life, he organizes The Innocent Hearts (赤子心), a poetry group with his friends. 1982 Liu starts a two-year master’s program in Chinese literature at Beijing Normal University. After receiving his master’s, Liu stays at Beijing Normal University to teach and marries Tao Li, with whom he has a son, Liu Tao. 1987 Liu begins his doctoral studies and publishes literary criticism in dozens of magazines. In 1987 he publishes his first book, Criticism of the Choice: Dialogues with Li Zehou, which critiques a prominent intellectual. It becomes a bestseller. 1988 Liu works abroad at universities in Oslo and Hawaii and at Columbia University as a visiting scholar. 1989 While Liu is still in America, Chinese students break into massive pro-democracy demonstrations in Tiananmen Square. He flies back to stand staunchly on the side of students. Liu, as well as others, including Zhou Duo and Hou Dejian (a Taiwan-born singer), play pivotal roles, including organizing a hunger strike. Thanks to Liu’s efforts to persuade students to leave the square, many of them escape death. However, because of his leadership role in the protest, Liu is detained, fired from his job as a teacher, and his books are banned. He later divorces his wife Tao in order to protect his family, according to the Guardian newspaper. His son, then about six years old, goes to live with his grandparents; Liu will see little of him for the rest of his life, according to his biography. 1992 Liu doesn’t stop writing. His book The Monologues of a Doomsday’s Survivor, about his memories and evaluation of the Tiananmen Square protest, is published in Taiwan. In the book, he points out what he sees as the misjudgments of the movement. He says that it delayed China’s democratization, and that students and intellectuals had become “conceited—just as if we had reverted to the time of the Cultural Revolution.” Will Burgess/Reuters Pro-democracy activist Liu Xiaobo makes a point during a conversation in March 1995. 1996 Liu is sentenced to three years behind bars for a letter criticizing party rule. While still in the labor camp, he marries his current wife, fellow poet Liu Xia. According to Ian Johnson, writing for the New York Review of Books: They had met in the 1980s but became close only after Tiananmen. In 1995, Liu Xiaobo was detained for writing an open letter calling for basic rights and then formally sentenced in 1996 to three years in prison. Liu Xia traveled regularly up to the labor camp and—in a phrase that is now legendary—told the guards that “I want to marry that enemy of the state!” Eventually she did, while he was still in prison; their wedding banquet was in the prison cafeteria. David Gray/Reuters Liu Xia, wife of Liu Xiaobo, was placed under house arrest in 2010. 1999 Liu Xiaobo is released in October. 2001 Liu Xiaobo helps establish the Independent Chinese PEN Center, a “non-political, non-profit organization of writers that fights for the protection of freedom of expression and publication,” and serves as its president from 2003 to 2007. During this time he writes the essay “The internet is God’s present to China,” about his belief that the internet is making China freer (republished later in the Times of London). 2008 Liu Xiaobo co-authors Charter 08 with other dissidents, a political manifesto. The document appeals for a fundamental political transformation including a new constitution, separation of powers, and guarantee of human rights. In December 2008, he is detained. 2009 He is put on trial. On Dec. 25, he is convicted of “inciting subversion of state power.” According to PEN America, his conviction is largely for writing seven sentences, including this one: “One-party monopolization of ruling privileges should be abolished.” 2010 In October, Liu wins the Nobel Peace Prize while in custody, “for his long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China.” His wife Liu Xia is placed under house arrest in Beijing the same day. At the December ceremony (video), the medal and diploma are placed on an empty chair since Liu is still in prison. Norwegian actress Liv Ullmann reads the statement Liu had written prior to his sentencing: “I have no enemies and no hatred.” Heiko Junge/Scanpix/Reuters The empty chair where 2010 Nobel Peace Prize winner, jailed Chinese dissident Liu Xiaobo, would have sat during the ceremony at Oslo City Hall on Dec. 10, 2010. 2011 Liu’s father dies of liver cancer; he is briefly paroled to attend the funeral, according to Steel Gate to Freedom. 2017 On June 26, the Liaoning Prison Administrative Bureau prison makes the announcement that Liu is being transferred from prison in Jinzhou to a nearby hospital in the final stages of liver cancer. His lawyer confirms the news, setting off an outpouring of grief and anger among people who know and respect him globally. As Liu’s condition deteriorates, concerns over his treatment grow. The couple seeks to go abroad for treatment, news reports say, but his doctors in China say he is too ill to travel. China invites German and US doctors to visit him. Liu Xia is with her husband in hospital in his final days.

Germany and Chile are embarking on a significant collaboration to confront the dark legacy of Colonia Dignidad, a secluded community founded by former Nazi Paul Schaefer. This joint initiative aims to meticulously document the horrific abuses that transpired within its isolated borders.

A dedicated center for documentation will be established to chronicle the extensive abuses, alongside a commemoration for the victims who endured torture and ultimately perished. Founded in 1961 by Paul Schaefer, a former German medical orderly, Colonia Dignidad operated as an isolated sect governed by rigid, traditional principles. Schaefer, who inflicted sexual assault and torture upon numerous young individuals, wielded absolute authority over his followers, many of whom regarded him as divine.

During the brutal 1973-1990 dictatorship of Augusto Pinochet, Colonia Dignidad was infamously utilized as a holding and torture facility for political dissidents. This collaborative effort aligns with a broader trend in Latin America, where truth commissions and public monuments are increasingly employed to address past human rights violations as nations transition to democracy.

In a significant development in 2006, former members of the sect issued a public apology, seeking forgiveness for forty years of human rights transgressions. They attributed their past actions to being indoctrinated by Schaefer.

Despite its grim history, Colonia Dignidad continues to be a source of controversy. The settlement has been renamed as a Bavarian-style resort, a decision that survivors find deeply insulting to those who suffered there. Paul Schaefer died in 2010 while serving a two-decade prison sentence for sexual abuse.

Beijing has faced strong international condemnation for its refusal to allow democracy advocate Liu Xiaobo to seek overseas treatment for liver cancer. The Chinese government has consistently asserted that the global community should not interfere in its "domestic affairs," pushing back against international censure following Liu Xiaobo's death. This stance has been a recurring theme, with Beijing previously labeling the 2010 decision to award the late activist a Nobel Peace Prize as "a blasphemy."

Liu, who was 61, passed away from multiple organ failure. His death marked a somber historical parallel, as he became the first Nobel Peace Prize laureate to die in custody since German pacifist Carl von Ossietzky, who perished under surveillance in 1938 after enduring years in Nazi concentration camps. Prior to his death, Beijing had largely ignored international appeals for Liu to receive medical treatment abroad following his late-stage liver cancer diagnosis in May. Reports suggested this refusal stemmed from concerns that Liu might use his final days to denounce China's authoritarian rule.

The international outcry and China's defense of its actions highlight key points of contention. Geng Shuang, spokesperson for China's Ministry of Foreign Affairs, characterized the international condemnation as the work of "a few foreign officials." In stark contrast, Berit Reiss-Andersen, chair of the Norwegian Nobel Committee, accused Beijing of bearing "a heavy responsibility" for Liu's death. British Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson unequivocally stated that China's treatment of Liu was "wrong." Geng Shuang further asserted that "Foreign countries are in no position to make improper remarks," informing a daily press briefing that China had lodged representations with "certain countries" regarding their comments. He reiterated his criticism of the 2010 Nobel Prize award, calling it "a blasphemy of the peace prize," while simultaneously claiming that "all-out efforts" were made to treat Liu with "humanity and in accordance with the law."

Human rights activists and Liu's friends have vehemently refuted these official claims, deeming them fabrications designed to obscure Beijing's culpability in his demise. Liu had been sentenced to 11 years in prison for his involvement in pro-democracy protests. Hu Ping, who had known Liu since the 1989 pro-democracy protests, declared, "This is ultimately a political murder." Disturbing indications emerged that China's leadership sought to control Liu's funeral arrangements. Activist Hu Jia reported that authorities were pressuring Liu's family to cremate his body quickly, allowing only for a "simple farewell ceremony, under severe surveillance." Hu added that Liu's friends had been unable to obtain specific details about the arrangements. China's state-run media offered a brief report claiming Liu's funeral "would respect the wishes of his family and local customs, with relevant authorities providing assistance if the family requests it."

Further restrictions were imposed, including travel bans and visa refusals, aimed at stifling any public commemoration or dissent. Activist Ye Du stated that Chinese security services had warned him against traveling, imposing a "national ban" on activists who had hoped to attend Liu's funeral. Berit Reiss-Andersen revealed that the Chinese consulate in Oslo had refused her visa application to attend the funeral, citing an incorrectly filled form and the lack of a specific invitation. Internet censors actively removed references to the Nobel laureate from Chinese social media, blocking search terms such as Liu Xiaobo, LXB, and RIP. The phrase "I have no enemies," a well-known rallying cry associated with Liu, was also banned. China's state-run media largely omitted coverage of his death, a deliberate strategy to suppress discussion of his fate and ideas. An editorial in an English-language tabloid suggested Liu was a victim of Western "forces" seeking to undermine the Communist Party, rather than the party itself, questioning his political achievements and stating, "The west has bestowed upon Liu a halo, which will not linger."

Jerome Cohen, an expert in Chinese law and human rights at New York University, commented that Beijing's treatment of Liu and his wife exemplified "the Orwellian China that is developing under Xi Jinping." He predicted that Beijing would continue its efforts to suppress Liu's message of democratic change, even after his death. Cohen anticipated a funeral conducted under strict secrecy, with limited mourners and no public announcement of the burial site or services, in an attempt to "bury the whole thing with him." He expressed concern that Beijing's persecution of Liu's wife, Liu Xia, would persist as the government sought to prevent her from becoming a symbol of political resistance and free expression. He noted, "One-party dictatorships can’t allow free speech. That is why they tried to keep her quiet." Cohen speculated that Liu Xia might be kept under indefinite restrictions until she succumbs to illness. He further suggested that Beijing's prevention of Liu's travel and potential final denunciation of authoritarian rule was aimed at avoiding "an enormous public relations disaster." Friends and activists have urged Western governments to advocate for Liu Xia's freedom from China.

The death of Liu Xiaobo, China's most prominent political prisoner, on Thursday triggered an intense campaign by government censors to suppress online discussions of his legacy. Platforms like Weibo, a widely used Chinese microblogging service, saw the prohibition of candle emoticons and the phrase "R.I.P." Many websites displayed no search results when Mr. Liu's name was entered. Nevertheless, supporters of Mr. Liu devised ingenious methods to circumvent these restrictions. They employed coded language, visual media, and images to express unity and to voice disapproval of the government's actions concerning China's sole Nobel Peace Prize recipient. This is a summary of the responses: 'Heaven Is Watching'

The Nobel Peace Prize committee has expressed profound concern for Liu Xia, the widow of Liu Xiaobo, China's most prominent dissident, who passed away from liver cancer. The committee, alongside the United Nations, has called on China to release Liu Xia, who has been under house arrest without formal charges since 2010.

China has faced international condemnation for refusing to allow Liu Xiaobo, who was serving an 11-year sentence for "subversion," to receive medical treatment abroad. The Norwegian Nobel Committee, which awarded him the Peace Prize in 2010, stated that Beijing bears a "heavy responsibility" for his death. Berit Reiss-Andersen, the committee's leader, revealed that the Chinese consulate in Oslo declined to accept her visa application, preventing her from traveling to attend a potential funeral for Liu Xiaobo. She conveyed to the BBC her desire to pay respects to the dissident's family during this "tragic situation," lamenting that Beijing missed an opportunity for a "fantastic gesture" by not permitting her travel.

Amidst this controversy, attention has increasingly focused on Liu Xia, with growing worries about her mental well-being. The Norwegian Nobel Committee issued a statement expressing deep concern for Liu Xia's situation and urged Chinese authorities to "lift all restrictions they have put upon her." The committee emphasized that if she wishes to leave China, there is no justification for denying her that opportunity.

Liu Xia, a poet, is reportedly suffering from depression after years of house arrest and intense surveillance. She was permitted to visit her husband in the hospital. Ms. Reiss-Andersen described to the BBC that Liu Xia has been "contaminated by her husband's situation," having been isolated for years and not being a political activist herself.

Several nations, including Germany, the UK, France, the United States, and Taiwan, have appealed to China to permit Liu Xia to depart the country if she so chooses. This appeal was supported by the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, Zeid Ra'ad Al Hussein, who urged China to "guarantee Liu Xia's freedom of movement." The human rights organization Amnesty International has launched a petition for her release, asserting that it is "time the Chinese authorities stop cruelly punishing" the artist. The Chinese foreign ministry has stated that Liu Xia will be treated in accordance with the law.

Liu Xiaobo, a former university professor, transformed into a dedicated human rights advocate, was labeled a criminal by Chinese authorities and was imprisoned multiple times throughout his life. He is recognized for his role in helping to negotiate the safe exit of hundreds of demonstrators during the Tiananmen Square student protests in June 1989, which concluded with a violent government crackdown.

The 11-year prison sentence he was serving was imposed in 2009 after he co-authored the Charter 08 manifesto, a document advocating for multi-party democracy. Liu Xiaobo was convicted of attempting to overthrow the state. The Chinese foreign ministry previously rebuffed international criticism regarding its handling of Liu Xiaobo's case, asserting that it falls under China's internal affairs and that foreign countries are not in a position to make inappropriate comments.

Liu Xiaobo was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2010 for his "long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China," but he was prevented from traveling to Norway to accept the award. Chinese authorities announced last month that he had been diagnosed with liver cancer and transferred him from prison to a hospital in Shenyang, where he was under heavy guard. In his final days, Western countries repeatedly requested that China allow Liu Xiaobo to seek palliative care elsewhere, a request Beijing denied. While Chinese medical experts maintained he was too ill to travel, Western doctors who examined him held a different view. Liu Xiaobo died "peacefully" on Thursday afternoon, surrounded by his wife and other family members, according to his primary doctor, Teng Yue'e. His last words to his wife were: "Live on well."

In mainland China, international reports on Liu Xiaobo's death have been subject to censorship, with local media providing minimal coverage, primarily in English, according to correspondents. The Communist Party's English editorial in the Global Times described Liu Xiaobo as "a victim led astray" by the West. Online discussions in China regarding his death on social media also appear to have been censored. Friends of Liu Xiaobo in China have reportedly been instructed by authorities not to organize any memorial events, as stated by Germany-based activist Tienchi Martin-Liao, who also reported that "many have been detained already." Conversely, Chinese activists outside mainland China have openly mourned him, with hundreds gathering for a vigil in Hong Kong on Thursday night.

Beijing (CNN) The global outpouring of grief and anguish over Liu Xiaobo's death stands in marked contrast to the muted reaction in his homeland. The Nobel Peace Prize-winning writer and human rights activist died Thursday evening of liver cancer while in custody at a hospital in Shenyang in northeastern China. He was 61. Liu's funeral was held Saturday morning in Shenyang and attended by his "family members and close friends," according to a city government spokesman. Liu's body had been cremated, and his ashes had been dropped into the sea in a private ceremony, he said. "The authorities can't afford to see Liu's grave turn into a landmark and symbol where people gather to pay tribute," said Hu Jia, a leading Chinese human rights activist who has known Liu's wife for years and has served prison terms for his own advocacy. "They want to erase all traces of him." "But three-quarters of the Earth's surface is covered by water," he added. "His grave is now everywhere." In a government-organized news conference, Liu Xiaobo's eldest brother -- who Liu's supporters say had never approved his activism -- on Saturday afternoon expressed the family's "profuse gratitude" to the state for showing "humanity" in providing medical treatment and arranging "perfect" funeral services for Liu. He left without taking questions. The Chinese government, which had long banned Liu's work and even his name, continues to censor the story of his death, deleting social media posts mourning him -- including those simply displaying the image of a burning candle -- and blocking online searches containing variations of his name and famed quotes. CNN's broadcast is blacked out in China every time Liu's images or story appear. A poster of Liu Xiaobo is held up during a protest in Hong Kong. Domestic media outlets, all controlled by the ruling Communist Party, mostly have ignored the news. Dominating Friday's front page of the party mouthpiece, People's Daily, was a photo of a beaming President Xi Jinping meeting his Canadian counterpart. A few English-language outlets carried short reports on Liu's death, highlighting his "criminal" background and Chinese doctors' effort to save him. 'Inciting subversion' Before he was granted medical parole last month, Liu had been serving a 11-year prison sentence for "inciting subversion of state power." His conviction in late 2009 stemmed from his co-authorship of a manifesto calling for human rights and political reform in China. In 2010, while he was in prison, the Nobel committee awarded Liu the peace prize for "his long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China" -- a move that infuriated Beijing. In a harshly worded, English-language editorial published Friday by the nationalistic tabloid, The Global Times, the state-run newspaper painted Liu as "a victim led astray by West." "Liu lived in an era when China witnessed the most rapid growth in recent history, but he attempted to confront Chinese mainstream society under Western support," it said. "This has determined his tragic life." "The West has bestowed upon Liu a halo, which will not linger," it added. A protester holds up a picture of Chinese dissident Liu Xiaobo on June 30, 2017, in Hong Kong. The newspaper had posted a since-deleted message in Chinese on Sina Weibo, a platform similar to Twitter, mocking the international reactions to Liu's death: "The person's gone but a blockbuster tear-jerker is just on -- we'll sit back and enjoy the show." 'Improper comments' Amid an avalanche of condolences and condemnations from politicians and activists around the world, the Chinese government took the unusual step of issuing a statement shortly after 2 a.m. Friday to respond to what it called "improper comments" by foreign officials. "The handling of Liu Xiaobo's case belongs to China's domestic affairs, and foreign countries are in no position to make improper remarks," said foreign ministry spokesman Geng Shuang. "We call on relevant countries to respect China's judicial sovereignty and not to meddle in China's domestic affairs with this individual case." The statement was sent directly to foreign journalists and was nowhere to be found on the ministry's website -- just like all previous official responses to questions about Liu. China has conveyed its "strong dissatisfaction and firm opposition" to all governments and international entities that commented on Liu's death, including the United States and the United Nations' human rights commission, Geng said at a regular news briefing Friday afternoon. China's warning seems to have gone unheeded, with even the US President -- not known as a public champion of human rights activists -- expressing his sorrow over Liu's death and calling him a "political prisoner" in a statement. Echoing myriad other world leaders, Donald Trump made a point of mentioning Liu Xia, a poet and artist who married Liu Xiaobo in 1996 while he was serving an earlier prison sentence. Liu Xiaobo has become an icon of the democracy movement in Hong Kong. As her husband remained behind bars until recently, Liu Xia, 56, paid a heavy price for simply being his wife. Under de facto house arrest since his Nobel win, Liu Xia saw her communication with the outside world almost completely cut by the government. She has been suffering severe depression, according to close friends, especially after authorities sentenced her brother to 11 years in prison over what supporters call trumped-up charges of business fraud. Struggle continues Now, Liu Xia is increasingly the focus of an international campaign calling for her freedom and right to live abroad, which many friends and supporters say were her husband's dying wishes. "He loved her so much that he felt he owed it to her," said Liao Yiwu, a prominent Chinese writer and longtime friend of the Lius who now lives in Germany. "He didn't know about her condition and what had happened at home until not that long ago," Liao said. "That's why he agreed to go abroad with her." In a hastily arranged news conference in Shenyang after his death, Liu Xiaobo's Chinese doctors acknowledged for the first time that he had sought treatment overseas but insisted that his medical condition by then had made international travel impossible. They confirmed that Liu Xia was by her husband's bedside when he died -- and his last words urged her to "live a good life." Despite worldwide solidarity behind Liu Xia, many supporters sense the Chinese government's fear of her turning into an international symbol and rallying cry for human rights. They foresee an uphill battle for her ahead. "Few knew Liu Xiaobo's thoughts in the last eight years, but she did, and they talked during his last days," said Hu, the Chinese activist. "I think the authorities are afraid of seeing even the simplest last words from him get out and spread." Geng, the Chinese foreign ministry spokesman, repeatedly declined to say Friday whether the government would allow Liu Xia to leave China, insisting that Beijing would handle her case "in accordance with law." "Liu Xia is free," the Shenyang city government spokesman said Saturday, but needs privacy to mourn her painful loss. In government handout photos of her husband's funeral, a grief-stricken Liu Xia, wearing all black and sunglasses, can be seen bowing to the open casket and holding a portrait of Liu Xiaobo. The authorities later released videos that they said proved Liu Xia's consent to the burial arrangement. One clip purportedly shows a handwritten note by Liu Xia on Thursday requesting a private sea burial, while the other appears to be a hidden-camera recording of her saying the same thing. In a brief appearance before reporters, Liu Xiaobo's eldest brother said Liu Xia was unable to join him due to her "extremely weak condition" that may require medical attention. He did not respond to shouted questions about her whereabouts amid growing international concern over her freedom and welfare. Across the closely monitored cyberspace in China, some internet users have evaded censors to post lines from Liu Xia's poems that were dedicated to her late husband -- including verses from "Wind": "Walls make you suffocate / you are the wind, and the wind / never tells me / when it comes and when it goes."

query: What prompted the federal judge in Seattle to temporarily block President Trump's travel ban?

documents.text:
A Seattle federal judge's temporary halt to President Trump's travel ban on February 3rd marked a significant early challenge to presidential power. This judicial rebuke of executive authority, which stopped the executive order's enforcement, is uncommon and suggests ongoing legal disputes over presidential powers may continue. The judge's order temporarily suspended the entry ban, prompting airlines to prepare for allowing passengers from previously restricted nations to board. Government officials promptly contacted airlines to permit travel for those affected. The White House, however, announced the Justice Department would seek an emergency stay of the ruling.

Judicial interventions of this nature are rare. While this halt is temporary, the judge granted it based on the case's likelihood of success. Cases involving executive authority often proceed to the Supreme Court, which could reverse such decisions. Historically, courts have granted presidents considerable discretion. Experts note that presidents typically receive significant leeway, and rarely face court challenges for their actions.

During Barack Obama's presidency, courts challenged two notable executive orders. One involved halting deportations for millions of undocumented immigrants, a move the Supreme Court ultimately did not revive. Another instance saw a Texas federal judge block a Labor Department rule to expand the salary threshold for overtime pay. Both occurred in 2016. Additionally, the Supreme Court unanimously rejected Obama's 2012 recess appointments to the National Labor Relations Board, a key test of his executive power. Claims that Obama's executive authority was struck down numerous times have been disputed, as many cases did not directly concern his authority or began under a previous administration.

Experts observe that judicial checks on executive orders are generally infrequent, as courts tend to avoid "political" matters. However, President Trump's approach may differ. His early executive orders addressed contentious issues, and his past admiration for authoritarian leaders hints at a potential willingness to engage in such conflicts. The White House's initial strong reaction to the ruling further supports this idea. President Trump himself tweeted his response, stating, "When a country is no longer able to say who can, and who cannot , come in & out, especially for reasons of safety &.security - big trouble!"

The core question is whether this ruling will lead President Trump to adjust his use of executive authority or if he will continue to test presidential power limits. Many believe the latter is more likely, highlighting a key difference between past and present administrations. One expert noted, "One of the many differences between Obama and Trump is Obama understood limits of what executive order could be. I think the Trump people thought they could rule by edict, and they can't."

President Donald Trump's weekend at his Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach included a largely open schedule, highlighted by two important international phone calls.

This afternoon, the President was scheduled to speak with Italian Prime Minister Paolo Gentiloni at 4:15 p.m. This was to be followed by a call with Ukrainian President Petro Poroshenko at 4:45 p.m.

Earlier in the day, President Trump made an unplanned trip to his Trump International Golf Club. The club is situated across the Intracoastal Waterway, near West Palm Beach.

He arrived at the golf course at 9:33 a.m. While his golfing partners for this outing were not revealed, it's worth noting he previously played with Tiger Woods in December. An update later confirmed that Trump's motorcade left the golf club at 2:05 p.m., and he subsequently returned to Mar-a-Lago.

A federal judge's temporary suspension of a controversial travel ban targeting individuals from seven predominantly Muslim countries has led to a significant policy reversal by the United States government. The State Department announced its intention to reinstate approximately 60,000 revoked visas, a direct consequence of Judge James Robart's ruling that found substantial legal grounds to challenge the executive order's validity. President Donald Trump publicly criticized the Seattle judge's decision as "ridiculous" and vowed to contest it, seeking to reinstate the travel restrictions.

This development brought cautious optimism to those affected. Airlines began allowing previously barred individuals to board flights to the U.S. following the judge's Friday restraining order, which halted enforcement immediately. The State Department confirmed the reversal of visa cancellations, and Homeland Security personnel were instructed to comply with the court's directive. Customs officials subsequently notified airlines they could resume boarding travelers impacted by the executive order. Major international carriers, including Qatar Airways, Air France, Etihad Airways, and Lufthansa, affirmed their commitment to facilitating travel for these individuals.

The Trump administration maintained that the travel ban was essential for national security and indicated its intent to pursue an emergency stay to swiftly reinstate the restrictions. President Trump continued to voice strong objections via social media, reiterating his belief that the ruling was "ridiculous" and would be overturned. He expressed concern about a judge's authority to halt a national security measure, suggesting it could permit individuals with malicious intent to enter the country.

Personal accounts reveal a mix of relief and apprehension. Fuad Sharef, an Iraqi national with an immigration visa, expressed profound happiness at being able to travel to New York with his family, stating, "Finally, we made it." A cardiologist training in the U.S., who wished to remain anonymous, noted that while his Syrian wife had recently joined him, others might still hesitate to travel due to ongoing uncertainty. The emotional toll of such uncertainty is significant, as individuals face the possibility of their futures being abruptly altered.

Among the most compelling beneficiaries is four-month-old Fatemeh Reshad from Iran, suffering from a severe heart defect. Her ability to travel to the U.S. now allows her to undergo life-saving surgery, with American doctors pledging to treat her pro bono, a commitment highlighted by New York Governor Andrew Cuomo. This case underscores the critical need for timely medical care that transcends national borders and political policies.

The executive order, now temporarily suspended, had imposed an indefinite ban on refugees from Syria and a 90-day visa suspension for individuals from Iraq, Syria, Iran, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, or Yemen. The ban's initial implementation triggered widespread protests across the U.S. and in major international cities, with advocacy groups and citizens expressing solidarity with refugees and opposition to the policy.

Judge Robart's decision stemmed from his assessment that legal challenges presented by Washington and Minnesota were likely to succeed. Washington's Attorney General, Bob Ferguson, argued the ban was unconstitutional, unfairly denying visa holders the right to travel without due process. Washington's Solicitor General, Noah Purcell, emphasized the challenge focused on the order's discriminatory targeting of Islam. Courts in at least four other states were also hearing cases contesting the executive order, indicating a broad legal front against the restrictions.

Dr. Samuel Jacob, a Syrian-born physician in Pittsburgh, expressed frustration over his wife's inability to join him due to the ban. He planned to consult his attorney for clarity on the latest developments and his wife's travel prospects. Dr. Jacob emphasized the need for expert legal guidance to make informed decisions. He articulated a profound expectation that the United States, a nation he serves, should offer support and protection, allowing him to reunite with his family without enduring indignity and hardship. His experience highlights the personal cost of such policies on individuals and families building lives in a new country.

President Donald Trump voiced confidence that the Justice Department would prevail in its appeal of a federal judge's order that temporarily blocked his administration's controversial travel ban. This ban had halted entry for citizens of seven predominantly Muslim nations. The government quickly initiated a legal challenge following the order's suspension.

"We'll win. For the safety of the country, we'll win," Trump told reporters at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida, shortly after the Justice Department announced its intent to appeal.

Trump's public criticism of U.S. District Judge James Robart in Seattle raised concerns among some who argued that such personal attacks could undermine the judiciary's role. Trump had tweeted, "The opinion of this so-called judge, which essentially takes law-enforcement away from our country, is ridiculous and will be overturned!" He has consistently advocated for "extreme vetting" of immigrants and refugees to prevent terrorist attacks.

Throughout the day, Trump continued to voice his objections via Twitter, expressing dismay that the judge's decision, in his view, "opens up our country to potential terrorists and others that do not have our best interests at heart. Bad people are very happy!"

As the travel ban's suspension took effect, individuals from Iran, Iraq, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, Syria, and Yemen, who had been detained or prevented from traveling the previous weekend, rushed to secure flights to the United States.

The Justice Department did not specify a timeline for filing its appeal with the Ninth Circuit U.S. Court of Appeals regarding Judge Robart's ruling, which also suspended the ban on refugee admissions. Judge Robart, appointed by former President George W. Bush, had raised questions about the constitutionality of Trump's executive order. The panel tasked with deciding on an immediate block of the ruling includes appointees from George W. Bush, Jimmy Carter, and Barack Obama.

Legal experts noted that Trump's public commentary on the case could complicate the Justice Department's efforts. Jonathan Turley, a law professor at George Washington University, observed, "It’s hard for the president to demand that courts respect his inherent authority when he is disrespecting the inherent authority of the judiciary. That certainly tends to poison the well for litigation."

Immigration advocacy groups, including the American Civil Liberties Union and the International Refugee Assistance Project, issued a joint statement urging those with currently valid visas from the seven affected countries to "consider rebooking travel to the United States immediately," citing the possibility of the ruling being overturned or temporarily halted. A State Department email indicated that the department was preparing to admit refugees, including those from Syria, as early as Monday.

The ruling marked the initial phase of what is anticipated to be a protracted legal battle over Trump's immigration policies. The executive order had previously caused significant disruption at airports nationwide, leading to stranded travelers and widespread protests. Public opinion on the ban remains divided, with a Reuters/Ipsos poll indicating 49% in favor and 41% opposed.

Supporters of the travel pause, like Wes Parker, a retiree from Long Beach, California, expressed their reasoning: "If you were a new president coming in, wouldn’t you want what you feel safe with?" Conversely, rights groups, Democrats, and international allies have condemned the ban as discriminatory. Protests against the immigration restrictions occurred in major U.S. cities.

The sudden reversal of the ban created a window of opportunity for those seeking to enter the United States, though uncertainty about its duration persisted. In Erbil, Iraq, Fuad Sharef and his family were preparing to travel, expressing relief: "I am very happy that we are going to travel today. Finally, we made it."

The Department of Homeland Security stated it would revert to standard screening procedures while the Justice Department pursued an emergency stay of the order. Some travelers remained cautious, with Josephine Abu Assaleh, 60, from Damascus, stating, "I will not say if I have hope or not. I wait, watch and then I build my hopes."

The ban had also significantly impacted refugees, many of whom had spent years seeking asylum. The court's decision prompted refugee advocacy groups to assist individuals in the resettlement process. Iraqi refugee Nizar al-Qassab, 52, shared his family's experience of a canceled resettlement trip, remarking, "It’s in God’s hands."

## Mar-a-Lago: From Personal Retreat to Presidential Fortress Amidst Protest

Donald Trump's initial presidential working vacations at Mar-a-Lago were met with significant public dissent, notably a demonstration by approximately 2,000 individuals in West Palm Beach protesting his executive order on immigrant vetting. This protest coincided with Trump's attendance at the International Red Cross Ball, held at the resort, while the Red Cross itself was actively assisting refugees impacted by the president's temporary travel ban.

This period marked a profound transformation for Mar-a-Lago and its owner. Once a personal sanctuary for golf and ocean views, the estate evolved into a heavily secured presidential compound. Previously, Trump was known for his approachable demeanor with club members, often sharing his signature meatloaf. Longtime member Bo Dietl recalled Trump as someone who "talks to everyone and has the chitty-chatty talk. He just works the room, he walks around and talks to everyone." However, Dietl presciently cautioned that this accessibility was "before he was running for president… I don't think he'll be as accessible as he was."

The demands of the presidency have drastically altered Trump's accessibility at Mar-a-Lago. Enhanced Secret Service protocols now mandate rigorous screening for members and guests, including magnetometers and vehicle bomb checks, with access restricted to a side entrance. Coast Guard vessels patrol the waters, and local airspace faces temporary restrictions, creating a stark contrast to the estate's former, more relaxed atmosphere.

Prior to Trump's arrival, Democratic lawmakers raised concerns about potential conflicts of interest, urging the disclosure of Mar-a-Lago's membership roster. Senators Sheldon Whitehouse and Tom Udall highlighted in a letter that club membership dues had reportedly surged to approximately $200,000 annually following Trump's election, suggesting this increased demand was likely driven by the expectation of preferential access to the president. The letter also noted that the Mar-a-Lago Club's website continued to identify the property as "Donald J. Trump’s Mar-a-Lago Club," emphasizing his ownership.

While some members acknowledged an increase in visitor traffic, they also pointed out that Palm Beach ordinances limit club memberships, with few new memberships available that year. They further stated that the $200,000 fee was comparable to other exclusive Palm Beach clubs. Members also indicated that Mar-a-Lago is not conducive to presidential lobbying. One anonymous member explained, "As far as contact with Trump there, you can get your photo and you can talk to him. You're never really getting into any deep conversations. You're never really bending his ear as president." Another member added, "It’s a mistake for anyone to think that they’re going to be able to make a deal with Trump at Mar-a-Lago. Everyone knows that he doesn’t talk business there. He’s there to relax."

Trump's concept of relaxation deviates from the norm; he remains actively engaged, rarely at rest. Instead, he often retreats to his private quarters for television, golf, and discussions about politics, even cabinet appointments. Some members expressed apprehension about interacting with journalists, fearing scrutiny from Trump's political team. Beyond hosting duties, members are wary of his retaliatory nature. During the GOP presidential primary, for instance, the Jeb Bush campaign observed that Mar-a-Lago members were hesitant to publicly endorse Bush, fearing repercussions such as unfavorable seating arrangements.

Mar-a-Lago represents more than just a real estate asset for Trump. He claimed to have refined his inaugural address at the "Winter White House," following John F. Kennedy, the only other president to do so in Palm Beach. The estate symbolizes his negotiation prowess and was a strategic acquisition for his entry into New York's elite circles, where industry leaders and wealthy heirs traditionally spent their winters. Trump's dealings with Mar-a-Lago offer insight into his negotiation tactics. In his book, "The Art of the Deal," he detailed acquiring the historic property, built in 1927 by Marjorie Merriweather Post, for less than his initial offer. He purchased the estate for approximately $7 million in 1985, investing an additional $3 million in furnishings. Trump later engaged in legal disputes with the Palm Beach County tax assessor over the land's taxable value and with the town of Palm Beach regarding its conversion into a club open to all paying members.

This marked a significant shift for the historically exclusive island, where Jewish individuals had long been restricted to a single club, and even the Kennedy family faced scrutiny at another due to their Catholic faith. A former Republican official from the area described Palm Beach as "very WASP and back then it was even worse," noting that Trump's "boor, nouveau riche" persona initially faced resistance but that he has since become an accepted figure in Palm Beach society. Trump meticulously oversaw the mansion's restoration, offering guided tours and elaborating on the history of its furnishings and decor. His success in Palm Beach mirrored his earlier achievements in New York society and later within the Republican Party during his presidential campaign. His presidency solidified his status on an island populated by billionaires, including the Koch brothers. Another Republican involved in island politics observed, "These are rich people. They respect power. And Donald Trump is the most-powerful man in the world as president of the United States now." Chris Ruddy, a longtime friend of Trump and a club member, suggested another motivation for Trump's presidential candidacy: a desire to reduce aircraft overflights of Mar-a-Lago from Palm Beach International Airport (PBIA), a persistent issue for Trump.

Protesters had planned their demonstration along Flagler Drive in West Palm Beach, near the bridge to Palm Beach, commencing from the Trump Plaza condominiums. The initial organizer of the "March to Mar-a-Lago for Humanity" anticipated only a few hundred participants. However, with over 2,000 Facebook RSVPs, the organizer stepped down, and experienced organizers took over, opting against marching across the bridge for safety reasons. Alex Newell Taylor, a march organizer, acknowledged that Trump might not witness the protests directly but would likely view them on television, ensuring their message was conveyed. "We’re not going to let Trump relax at Mar-a-Lago," Taylor stated. "There are huge groups of people who don’t get to relax because of his executive orders, people fighting for their lives and their rights." Taylor highlighted the irony of the Red Cross hosting its gala at Mar-a-Lago while opposing Trump's refugee ban. The gala is the premier annual event in Palm Beach, attracting billionaires, ambassadors, and foreign dignitaries.

Roger Stone, a veteran adviser to Trump, expressed skepticism regarding the protests' effectiveness, suggesting Trump might even leverage them to his advantage if they intensified. However, Stone speculated on the potential impact of the presidency's pressures on Trump at Mar-a-Lago. "Donald used to come and go as he pleases, and now he can’t and he has protesters on top of that. He’s a man who does not like to be contained," Stone remarked. "Mar-a-Lago is like an oasis for him. But if feels he can’t go there to unwind, I wonder if it will make him go crazy."

A person walks outside the federal courthouse in Seattle carrying a sign that reads "I'm with Bob and Immigrants," in reference to Washington state Attorney General Bob Ferguson, Friday, Feb. 3, 2017,... (Associated Press) A person walks outside the federal courthouse in Seattle carrying a sign that reads "I'm with Bob and Immigrants," in reference to Washington state Attorney General Bob Ferguson, Friday, Feb. 3, 2017, during a hearing in federal court. A U.S. judge on Friday temporarily blocked President Donald Trump's... (Associated Press) WASHINGTON (AP) — The Latest on the reaction to a court order blocking U.S. President Donald Trump's ban on travelers and immigrants from seven predominantly Muslim countries (all times Eastern): 11:30 a.m. The Homeland Security Department says it's no longer directing airlines to prevent visa-holders affected by President Donald Trump's executive order from boarding U.S.-bound planes. That word follows a State Department announcement that it had reversed the cancellations of visas for foreigners after a federal judge put on hold Trump order on immigration. The department had said up to 60,000 foreigners had their visas "provisionally revoked" to comply with Trump's order. The two departments have not suspended enforcement of the president's order as the administration promises a legal appeal to the judge's ruling. Homeland Security says it has "suspended any and all actions" related to putting in place the terms of Trump's order. ___ 11 a.m. Uber CEO Travis Kalanick says his company is buying plane tickets for stranded drivers now that a federal judge has put a hold on President Trump's ban on travel to the United States by migrants from seven Muslim-majority countries. Kalanick tweeted Friday night that the head of litigation for the ride-hailing app is "buying a whole bunch of airline tickets ASAP!" U.S. District Judge James Robart in Seattle put a nationwide hold on Trump's executive order Friday night. It's not clear whether the ruling means that people from the affected countries will immediately start flying to the United States. Trump tweeted Saturday that the ruling "is ridiculous and will be overturned!" Kalanick quit Trump's council of business advisers Thursday. ___ 10:20 a.m. The State Department says it's reversed the cancellations of visas for foreigners after a federal judge put on hold President Donald Trump's executive order on immigration. The department had said up to 60,000 foreigners from seven majority-Muslim countries had their visas "provisionally revoked" to comply with Trump's order blocking them from traveling to the United States. The department says it acted to reinstate the visas after getting word from the Justice Department about the judge's ruling Friday in Washington state. For now, the department says people covered by the order and holding a valid visa may now travel to the United States. ___ 9:30 a.m. Seventy-two Iranian professors in Sharif University of Technology, one of the most reliable universities in Iran, have requested in a letter to the Iranian government to react in a different way to Trump's "improper action" on the visa ban. They proposed to President Hassan Rouhani and Foreign minister Mohammad Javad Zarif allow U.S. citizens come to Iran without obtaining a tourist visa and related formalities in the country and to issue visas for them at the Iran's airport with two-weeks validity during the next 90 days. They said that Americans can see the hospitality and goodwill of Iranians for themselves. ___ 9:30 a.m. The Middle East's biggest airline has joined its smaller Gulf rivals in confirming passengers from seven previously banned countries would be allowed to travel to the United States. Dubai-based Emirates said in a statement Saturday that under the direction of U.S. Customs and Border Protection, nationals of Iran, Iraq, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, Syria, Yemen holding valid visas or green cards could fly to the U.S. It says all refugees with visas would also be allowed to fly. It cautioned that "entry requirements to the U.S. may change, and Emirates will continue to comply with guidance provided by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection." Emirates offers daily flights from Dubai to several U.S. cities. Its rivals Qatar Airways and Abu Dhabi-based Etihad Airways issued similar guidance to passengers earlier Saturday. ___ 9:30 a.m. An Iraqi official at Baghdad international airport says the travel terminal was particularly crowded Saturday following news that U.S. President Donald Trump's travel ban on Iraqis and six other majority Muslim nations was blocked by a federal judge's ruling. Haider al-Rubaie, an official with the state-run Iraqi airways said flights from Baghdad to Dubai, Istanbul and Cairo were booked solid Saturday afternoon. While there are no direct flights to the US from Baghdad, al-Rubaie said many of the passengers were holding transit tickets to the US. Iraqi member of parliament Ibrahim Bahr Uloom praised the U.S. judge who ruled against Trump's ban and admonished the Iraqi leaders who were unable to achieve the same ends through diplomatic channels. "The U.S. justice system is better than Iraqi diplomacy," he said. "Today we thank the American judiciary." ___ 9 a.m. In Egypt, Cairo airport and airlines officials say they have received instructions from the U.S. Customs and Border Protection to suspend President Trump's executive order to ban travel to the United States by migrants from seven Muslim-majority countries: Somalia, Iraq, Syria, Iran, Sudan, Libya and Yemen. They say on Saturday there have not been any U.S.-bound migrants from those countries going through Egyptian airports since a federal judge on Friday blocked President Donald Trump's ban on admitting travelers from the seven countries or any refugees. ___ 7:30 a.m. President Donald Trump isn't happy that a federal judge has put on hold his executive order that applies to refugees and people from seven predominantly Muslim countries who want to enter the United States. The White House has promised a quick appeal and Trump has taken to Twitter to vent his frustration with the ruling. He says "the opinion of this so-called judge, which essentially takes law-enforcement away from our country, is ridiculous and will be overturned!" Trump also tweets that "when a country is no longer able to say who can and who cannot come in & out, especially for reasons of safety & security — big trouble!" ___ 8 a.m. Etihad Airways says it will begin accepting U.S.-bound passengers from the seven previously banned countries this week. The United Arab Emirates' national carrier said Saturday its decision follows guidance from the U.S. Customs and Border Protection unit at its hub in Abu Dhabi. The airline says it is monitoring developments in the U.S. and that passengers will be subject to screening measures in place before Trump's executive order was issued. U.S. border officials stationed in Abu Dhabi carry out passport and customs screenings before passengers board U.S.-bound flights under an existing pre-screening program. ___ 6:15 a.m. The U.S. embassy in Baghdad says they are still awaiting guidance following news of a court order blocking President Trump's ban on travelers from Iraq and six other predominantly Muslim countries. "We don't know what the effect will be, but we're working to get more information," the embassy told The Associated Press in a statement, adding that embassy staffers have received a large number of phone calls and inquiries from Iraqis eager to see if the visa restrictions had changed. Iraq's government spokesman says the prime minister's office is also waiting for the "official position of the U.S. administration." In a largely symbolic move, the Iraqi parliament called for a reciprocity measure last week increasing pressure on the country's government as it attempts to balance Iraq's alliance with the U.S. and powerful Iraqi political blocks with close ties to Iran. ___ 3:40 a.m. Qatar Airways has issued an advisory to passengers saying citizens of the seven Muslim-majority countries previously barred from entry who hold a valid U.S. visa or green card will be allowed to travel to the U.S. The airline cited a directive by U.S. Customs and Border Protection. Government-backed Qatar Airways is one of a handful of Mideast airlines operating direct daily flights to multiple American cities. Like other Gulf carriers, many of its customers are transit passengers whose journeys originated elsewhere. Its U.S. destinations from its Doha hub include New York, Chicago, Los Angeles, Miami and Washington. ___ 3 a.m. Cairo Foreign airlines operating in Iran have instructed travel agencies not to sell U.S.-bound flight tickets to Iranians holding U.S. visas in the wake of President Donald Trump's executive order banning visas for seven Muslim countries, including Iran. The move comes even though a U.S. judge on Friday temporarily blocked the ban, siding with two states that urged a nationwide hold on the executive order that has launched legal battles across the country. The directive does not come from U.S. airlines. In Tehran, the Kowsar travel agency told The Associated Press they had been instructed by foreign airlines not to sell tickets to Iranians with visas to enter the U.S. The agency said there was no problem for those who have a permanent resident card or a U.S. passport.

President Donald Trump's administration moved swiftly on Saturday to comply with a federal judge's order that halted his controversial travel ban. This action came even as President Trump himself publicly denounced the judge. The Department of Homeland Security announced it has suspended all efforts to implement the immigration order and will revert to standard traveler inspections, as was the practice before the ban's enactment.

However, the administration signaled its intent to legally defend the executive action. The Justice Department, expected to file an emergency motion to challenge the ruling, stated the need to contest the decision "at the earliest possible time." Acting DHS press secretary Gillian Christensen emphasized the order's purpose: "to protect the homeland and the American people, and the President has no higher duty and responsibility than to do so."

Despite these assurances, the nation remained in a state of uncertainty for the second consecutive weekend. The administration found itself on the defensive following the judge's ruling. A State Department official informed CNN that the department is reversing the cancellation of visas provisionally revoked after the President's executive order. This reinstatement applies to visas that were not physically stamped or marked as canceled.

The State Department transmitted a directive to all its posts on Saturday, instructing them to resume visa processing as it was prior to the executive order, according to two senior State Department officials. These officials clarified that individuals whose visas were physically canceled would need to visit an embassy or consulate for reinstatement. The majority of cancellations were electronic and have been electronically reinstated. The State Department indicated that fewer than 60,000 visas were revoked following the order's signing, but it remains unclear how many of those individuals will still be without their visas due to physical cancellation.

Prior to the government's announcements on Saturday, the International Air Transportation Association, a global airline industry trade group, advised its members to adhere to pre-order procedures, citing US Customs and Border Protection guidance. President Trump's order bars citizens of seven Muslim-majority countries—Iraq, Syria, Iran, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, and Yemen—from entering the US for 90 days. It also suspends all refugee admissions for 120 days and indefinitely halts refugees from Syria.

Federal Judge James Robart, appointed by George W. Bush and based in Seattle, issued a nationwide halt to the enforcement of Trump's order on Friday night. Judge Robart ruled in a lawsuit brought by the attorneys general of Washington and Minnesota, stating that the states had demonstrated they would suffer "immediate and irreparable injury" from the order's implementation. He noted the order's adverse effects on residents concerning education, employment, and freedom of travel.

Washington State Attorney General Bob Ferguson expressed his readiness to pursue the case to the Supreme Court if necessary, telling CNN's Anderson Cooper, "As a lawyer, it's why you go to law school."

The White House initially characterized Judge Robart's decision as "outrageous" before retracting the word in a subsequent statement. President Trump himself strongly criticized the judge on Saturday morning, tweeting, "The opinion of this so-called judge, which essentially takes law-enforcement away from our country, is ridiculous and will be overturned!" He followed with a series of tweets lamenting the ruling, stating, "When a country is no longer able to say who can, and who cannot, come in & out, especially for reasons of safety &.security - big trouble!"

Judge Robart's ruling was particularly impactful as it followed a more limited legal victory for the Trump administration earlier on Friday. A federal judge in Boston declined to extend a temporary restraining order that would have prevented the detention or removal of foreign travelers legally authorized to enter the Boston area. However, the sweeping nature of the Seattle ruling prompted a swift response from the federal government.

Customs and Border Protection (CBP) alerted airlines on Friday night that the US government would begin reinstating previously canceled visas. Airlines were also informed that refugees possessing US visas would be admitted. A CBP directive to major US airlines indicated that the government was in the process of reinstating visas and returning to "business as usual" conditions that predated last week's executive order. Airlines were expected to remove travel alerts from their websites and inform customers of the changes.

The possibility of further legal activity and appeals remained, and the timeline for visa reinstatement was uncertain. US airlines utilize an automated system linked to the CBP database to scan passports and visas for immediate boarding authorization. Until the government reinstates visas and airlines receive a "board" status, passengers may still be denied boarding. Airlines began adjusting their procedures on Saturday. Qatar Airways announced it would permit nationals from the seven affected countries and all refugees with valid, unexpired US visas or green cards to travel to the United States.

Refugee resettlement agencies across the US welcomed Judge Robart's ruling. Mark Hetfield, president and CEO of the Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society, stated, "President Trump's ban against refugees and Muslims was not only un-American, but Judge Robart found it to be unconstitutional." He added, "Thanks to Judge Robart's order protecting the Constitution, thousands of refugees who were bound for the US can finally find protection -- and a warm welcome -- here." Hetfield cautioned, however, "But this won't be the end of our fight to keep America's door open to refugees."

Due to the complex logistical coordination involved in organizing refugee arrivals, resettlement groups did not anticipate an immediate resumption of admissions. Once a refugee is vetted and approved, agencies work with the International Organizations for Migration to arrange placement with local chapters. The UN's Office of the High Commissioner for Refugees, along with the Departments of State and Homeland Security, and the refugees' country of origin, are involved in this process. Resettlement agencies have been coordinating with the State Department's Bureau of Population, Refugees, and Migration to determine when admissions can resume, while local branches prepare to receive new arrivals.

Daniel Smith, an immigration attorney in Seattle, predicted a "flood of people trying to enter the US over the next few days" due to the legal opening. He advised clients currently in the US to avoid leaving the country and suggested that those seeking entry should attempt to do so immediately and remain in the US once they arrive.

The controversy surrounding President Trump's criticism of the judge is likely to influence the Supreme Court nomination process for Neil Gorsuch. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer stated that Trump's remarks would be cited during the confirmation hearings, arguing that the President's "attack on Judge James Robart... shows a disdain for an independent judiciary that doesn't always bend to his wishes and a continued lack of respect for the Constitution." Schumer emphasized the importance of the Supreme Court serving as an independent check on the administration. Vermont Senator Patrick Leahy, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Judiciary Committee, echoed these sentiments, stating the need for a nominee "willing to demonstrate he or she will not cower to an overreaching executive" and that Judge Gorsuch must prove his ability to be an independent check and balance.

query: What modifications did Stephen Paddock make to his weapons to increase their lethality during the Las Vegas shooting?

documents.text: CLOSE Newly released body camera footage from the Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department offers a firsthand glimpse of the massacre as it happened. USA TODAY Mourners pay tribute at a makeshift memorial on the Las Vegas Strip for the victims of a mass shooting in Las Vegas on Monday. (Photo: Eugene Garcia, EPA-EFE) LAS VEGAS — Investigators say that a retired accountant's shooting rampage was meticulously planned and included specially modified weapons and surveillance cameras aimed to spy on any police intruders who would try and stop his murderous deluge. At a late-night press conference Tuesday, authorities said Stephen Paddock made his attack even more deadly by adding more lethal components to his weapons. He had devices attached to 12 semiautomatic rifles that allowed them to mimic fully automatic gunfire. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms Special Agent in Charge Jill Schneider said Paddock had nearly 50 guns — a combination of rifles, shotguns and pistols — in three locations. The gun attachment that mimics automatic gunfire is a little-known device called a “bump stock” that is not widely sold. The stocks have been around for less than a decade, and Schneider said officials determined they were legal. Paddock, who killed 58 people and eventually himself, also injured 527 others in what is the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history. He also used surveillance cameras to monitor police approaches to his room at the Mandalay Bay high-rise — including a camera he positioned in the peephole of the door. “I anticipate he was looking for anybody coming to take him into custody,” Sheriff Joseph Lombardo said. During the Sunday night rampage, a hotel security guard who approached the room was shot through the door and wounded in the leg. “The fact that he had the type of weaponry and amount of weaponry in that room, it was preplanned extensively,” the sheriff said, “and I’m pretty sure he evaluated everything that he did and his actions, which is troublesome.” While Paddock's motive remains unclear, authorities were putting together a more complete picture of his work history and his past. The killer worked as a letter carrier for the U.S. Postal Service, an IRS agent and in an auditing department over a 10-year period. A spokeswoman for the Office of Personnel Management told The Associated Press on Tuesday that Stephen Paddock’s employment included about two years as a mail carrier from 1976 to 1978. After that, he worked as an agent for the Internal Revenue Service for six years until 1984. And then he worked a defense auditing job for about 18 months. He graduated from college in 1977 from Cal State Northridge and also worked for a defense contractor in the late 1980s. President Trump, as he left Washington for a trip to storm-ravaged Puerto Rico, briefly referenced Paddock, whose carnage Sunday night left 59 people dead and more than 500 injured. "He was a sick man, a demented man with a lot of problems, I guess, and we are looking into him very, very seriously," Trump said. "We are dealing with a very, very sick individual." The president also said the administration would be "talking about gun laws as time goes by," and he lauded the efforts of Las Vegas law enforcement. "How quickly the police department was able to get in (to Paddock's hotel room) was very much a miracle," he said. "They've done an amazing job." Read more: Why motive matters Gun dealer: 'My gut fell out' Country music star Jason Aldean was performing when Paddock opened fire from the window of his 32nd-floor hotel room overlooking the Route 91 Harvest music festival. Aldean, who was not injured, tweeted a statement Tuesday saying he has been overwhelmed with emotion since the attack. "Something has changed in this country and in this world lately that is scary to see," Aldean wrote. But he also added that it is "time to come together and stop the hate." Stories of heroism continued to emerge. One minute Marine veteran Taylor Winston was dancing the two-step at the concert, the next he was commandeering a pickup and shuttling the wounded to hospitals before ambulances arrived. Dawn-Marie Gray, a concertgoer who happened to have seven years experience as a paramedic, found herself providing CPR and tying tourniquets. When Nick Jones, 30, of Vancouver, Wash., heard about the shooting, he drove 20 hours to help. He offered to donate blood, but the need had been met. He offered to volunteer at hospitals, but they had plenty of help. Even the family assistance center was overwhelmed with volunteers. “I was worried that no one would do anything,” Jones said Tuesday. “I was happy to be proven wrong.” Assistant Sheriff Todd Fasulo pleaded for patience during the investigation, saying authorities were "hunting down" every clue to learn more about Paddock. Several minutes after the carnage began, officers who blasted into the room found Paddock's body. His brother Eric, who lives in Florida, says his multimillionaire brother was a big spender at casinos and often received free meals and rooms there. Eric Paddock also said he believed his brother owned a couple guns. Police found 23 guns, including semiautomatic rifles, in Paddock's room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino. They found 19 more at his home in Mesquite, Nev., 80 miles northeast of here. And in yet another home owned by Paddock in Reno, authorities found five handguns, two shotguns, and a plethora of ammunition. CLOSE Las Vegas Metro Police have released footage from the body cameras worn by officers responding to the scene of the shooting Oct. 2, 2017. Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department A federal law enforcement official, who was briefed on the matter but not authorized to comment publicly, said police also found two tripods positioned at the hotel windows in what appeared to be a fully equipped sniper’s nest. While the investigation intensified, authorities touted the city’s resilience in the aftermath of the tragedy. Fully stocked blood banks were soon turning away would-be donors. A GoFundMe page collected $3.3 million from almost 50,000 donors in little more than 24 hours. Overall, donations for the victims have exceeded $3.7 million, Commissioner Stephen F. Sisolak said Tuesday afternoon. Pieces of normalcy slowly returned to the iconic Strip. Club promoters hawked 2-for-1 deals, tourists took selfies and police cracked down would-be criminals. “Do you know how disrespectful it is to come out here and deal drugs the day after 50 people died one block away?’’ a bike cop snapped at a man in handcuffs Monday night. “Can’t you take one day off?’’ Derek Koerner, who identified himself as a licensed club promoter, said he was encouraged by the sizable crowds — and particularly grateful for the SUV full of men interested in his 2-for-1 deals on strip clubs. A $40 cover for a two-drink minimum gets the buyer into the second strip club for free. “There’s more people out here than I thought there’d be,’’ said Koerner, 48. “People seem to be upbeat and have a good energy. People seem to be moving on with their lives.’’ But less than a mile away, the mood was noticeably different. Dozens of people, some arriving with candles and flowers, returned to the scene of the horrific crime, marked by yellow police tape. Herman Gold and his ex-wife, Sara, stood near the concert grounds and explained how they escaped the hail of bullets and why they came back. “I just want closure,’’ Herman Gold said. “Why? What’s the reason that on that particular night, one individual would sacrifice everyone else for his insanity?’’ Joe Larsen, standing at the same corner and wearing a VIP wristband from the Aldean concert, expressed gratitude. Larsen, 49, who flew in from New Jersey for the concert, said that he watched the Twin Towers fall in 2001, lost his wife to cancer in 2015 and, on Sunday night, feared he was about to leave his son and daughter without a parent. He said he saw a young woman die from a bullet to the head before he called his father during the bedlam. “I told him, ‘I don’t know if going to make it. Tell my kids to live a good life’ ” Larsen recalled. As he and others shared their stories and wiped away tears, police officers closely monitored pedestrians, especially the wayward ones. “Sir, get out of the street,’’ one officer barked. “It’s the last time I’m telling you.’’ Read more: Shooter may have used trigger crank Machine guns have long been a draw in Las Vegas Bacon reported from McLean, Va. Contributing: Kevin Johnson and Ashley May, USA TODAY; Brett Kelman, The Desert Sun; Megan Cassidy, The Arizona Republic; Mike Benner, KGW-TV, Portland, Ore.
