query: What specific elements of Rand Paul's op-ed appear to be plagiarized from Dan Stewart's article in The Week?

documents.text: Share On more Share On more Share On more Share On more Sections of an op-ed Kentucky Sen. Rand Paul wrote on mandatory minimums in The Washington Times in September appear nearly identical to an article by Dan Stewart of The Week that ran a week earlier. The discovery comes amid reports from BuzzFeed that Paul plagiarized in his book and in several speeches. Paul also delivered testimony before the Senate Judiciary Committee on Sept. 16, 2013, that included the copied sections. Here's how Stewart wrote his introduction to his article on "Rethinking mandatory sentencing": It's the automatic imposition of a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s as a response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, and over the decades has put hundreds of thousands of people behind bars for drug possession and sale, and other non-violent crimes. Since mandatory sentencing began, America's prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Is that a good thing? Most public officials — including liberals, conservatives, and libertarians — have decided that it's not. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. And here's how Paul wrote it a week later with the text bolded that appears copied: Mandatory-minimum sentences automatically impose a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory-sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Since mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s in response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, America's prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Drug offenders in the United States spend more time under the criminal justice system's formal control than drug offenders anywhere else in the world. Most public officials — liberals, conservatives and libertarians — have decided that mandatory-minimum sentencing is unnecessary. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory-sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. Another section of the article related to John Horner, a father of three who sold some pain pills to a police informant. Here's how The Week wrote it, again with bolded text on the copied sections: When a friend asked John Horner if he could buy some painkillers, the 46-year-old father of three didn't see a problem. The Osceola County, Fla., resident had been taking prescribed painkillers for years after losing his eye in an accident, and agreed to sell his friend, "Matt," four unused bottles. After the pills exchanged hands, Horner discovered that "Matt" was in fact a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. At the advice of his public defender, Horner pleaded guilty, and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in jail. He will be 72 by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. "Matt," who turned out to have a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months after informing on Horner, and is now free. And here's how Paul wrote it: John Horner was a 46-year-old father of three when he sold some of his prescription painkillers to a friend. His friend turned out to be a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. Horner pleaded guilty and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in prison. John will be 72 years old by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. The informant, who had a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months, and is now free. "We've always known that the audience of The Week consists of smart, busy people who want to feel even smarter, including a lot of people on Capitol Hill," said Bill Falk, the editor in chief of The Week. "We'd like to thank Sen. Paul for his endorsement.'' Paul's office did not return a request for comment.

This document provides an update to a Street Sweeper article originally published on March 16, 2011, detailing legal proceedings against Steve T. Kinder, Cornelia Joyce Kinder, and their associated caviar companies for the illegal harvesting and misrepresentation of paddlefish eggs.

On March 14, 2011, a federal indictment was unsealed in the Southern District of Ohio. Steve T. Kinder, 51, and Kinder Caviar Inc. were charged with illegally harvesting paddlefish from Ohio waters. They were further accused of falsely reporting to the Kentucky Department of Fish & Wildlife Resources that these fish were caught in Kentucky.

Cornelia Joyce Kinder, 53, along with Kinder Caviar Inc. and Black Star Caviar Company, faced charges for providing false information to the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service. This deception was aimed at obtaining permits to export paddlefish eggs to foreign customers. The false information pertained to the quantity of eggs, the identities of the fishermen, and the locations where the paddlefish were harvested.

The Kinders, husband and wife, jointly owned and operated both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar. These companies were engaged in the business of exporting paddlefish eggs, marketed as caviar, to international clients. The alleged illegal activities spanned from March 2006 to December 2010.

If convicted on all counts, the Kinders each faced a maximum penalty of five years in prison and a $250,000 fine. The companies could have been fined up to $500,000 per count. It is important to note that these charges were accusations, and the defendants were presumed innocent until proven guilty.

**Sidebar: The American Paddlefish and the Caviar Market**

The American paddlefish (Polyodon spathula), also known as the Mississippi paddlefish or spoonbill, is a freshwater species predominantly found in the Mississippi River drainage system. Its eggs are commercially valuable and sold as caviar. Historically, paddlefish were abundant throughout the Midwest. However, a global decrease in other caviar sources, such as sturgeon, has amplified demand for paddlefish caviar. This increased demand has unfortunately led to overfishing, contributing to a significant decline in paddlefish populations.

Paddlefish are afforded protection under both federal and Ohio state laws. Harvesting paddlefish in Ohio waters is illegal. However, legal harvesting is permitted in Kentucky waters.

Despite the criminal allegations, the Kentucky Department of Agriculture's website featured promotional material for Kinder Caviar, touting "Caspian Sea style caviar made right here in the Bluegrass!" and claiming American caviar was comparable to that of the Caspian Sea. The site noted that as Caspian Sea caviar catches and quotas decline, prices would inevitably rise, positioning Kinder Caviar as a choice for discerning connoisseurs.

Further information from Kentucky.gov reveals that in October 2008, Kentucky Governor Steven L. Beshear appointed five members to the state's Aquaculture Task Force. Among these appointees was Joyce Kinder, identified as the owner of Kinder Caviar, Inc., representing wholesalers of aquaculture products.

The October 2008 edition of the Legislative Record documented a meeting of the Interim Joint Committee on Agriculture and Natural Resources. During this meeting, Representative McKee noted that Ms. Joyce Kinder had signed up to address concerns regarding paddlefish administrative regulations. Ms. Kinder acknowledged the necessity of regulations but sought amendments to certain provisions. She specifically highlighted concerns about the revocation of a fisherman's license or permit due to inaccurate water boundaries caused by GPS positioning, deeming such revocations excessively harsh. She also noted disagreements regarding snagging methods, stating that under current regulations, all snagging methods must cease. Finally, Ms. Kinder expressed disagreement with the size limit, indicating a desire for the committee to re-examine the regulation without causing undue delays.

**Update: Plea Agreements and Sentencing**

On January 17, 2012, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder, along with their caviar companies, entered guilty pleas in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Ohio. They admitted to trafficking in and falsely labeling illegally harvested paddlefish.

According to the plea agreement, Cornelia Joyce Kinder confessed to making false statements on CITES Export Registration Forms for paddlefish eggs. On or about March 15, 2007, for Kinder Caviar, she misrepresented the quantity of legally harvested paddlefish eggs she could document, and she misidentified both the fishermen and the harvest locations. Similar misrepresentations were made for Black Star Caviar Company on or about December 18, 2010. In that instance, she used the name of a subordinate employee and forged their signature to conceal her own involvement as the applicant.

The plea agreement also stipulated that both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting each other in harvesting paddlefish. They acknowledged knowing these fish were harvested in Ohio waters, in violation of Ohio law, through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline, on or about May 5, 2007. They subsequently transported these paddlefish to Kentucky with the intent to sell them.

As part of their plea agreements, Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company each agreed to pay a $5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation. During this probation, both companies are prohibited from applying for or receiving CITES Export Permits.

Furthermore, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder each agreed to a three-year term of probation. This probation includes 100 hours of community service for each individual. They are also prohibited from fishing in any part of the Ohio River that forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky. Additionally, they are barred from applying for or receiving CITES Export Permits, either for themselves or on behalf of others.

In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders face potential suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck utilized in furtherance of the Lacey Act violations have been forfeited.

Mandatory-minimum sentencing laws, particularly those stemming from the "war on drugs," have had a devastating and disproportionate impact on minority communities, especially Black Americans, by permanently disenfranchising millions and contributing to mass incarceration. This analysis delves into the injustices of these policies, highlighting their role in perpetuating racial disparities and ruining lives.

The consequences of mandatory-minimum sentencing are stark. Millions of Americans, many of them nonviolent offenders who have served their time and pose no further threat, are forever barred from voting. This disenfranchisement is a direct result of drug convictions, a consequence amplified by the "war on drugs."

Racial disparities are evident throughout the criminal justice system. A report by the American Civil Liberties Union indicates that Black individuals are four to five times more likely to be convicted for drug possession. While some argue this reflects higher rates of drug use, The New York Times reported in June that arrest rates for marijuana possession are disproportionately high for Black Americans, despite similar usage rates between Black and white individuals.

Further investigation into these disparities reveals systemic issues. A study by The San Jose Mercury News, analyzing hundreds of thousands of criminal cases, found that similarly situated white defendants were more successful in plea-bargaining negotiations than their Black and Hispanic counterparts. This suggests that the system itself contributes to unequal outcomes.

The United States incarcerates more people than any other nation, and the racial disparities in arrest rates have had a profound and damaging effect on the Black community. Professor Michelle Alexander has aptly described the "war on drugs" as "the New Jim Crow," a sentiment that resonates with the experiences of countless individuals.

The impact extends beyond race, affecting thousands of young people whose lives are irrevocably altered by drug convictions. Consider the case of a man in Kentucky who, decades after a minor marijuana offense in college, remains unable to vote, own a firearm, or secure employment due to his felony record.

Another poignant example is John Horner, a father of three who received a mandatory minimum sentence of 25 years for selling prescription painkillers to a friend. His friend, who was an informant with a history of drug offenses, received a significantly lighter sentence. Horner will be released at age 72, having missed his children's upbringing.

Many judges oppose mandatory-minimum sentencing because these laws strip away judicial discretion, failing to account for the unique circumstances of each case. While not advocating for leniency for all offenders, the argument is that federal mandates should not prevent judges from exercising their professional judgment.

Mandatory-minimum sentences, enacted in the 1970s to combat a perceived drug and crime epidemic, require a fixed prison term for specific offenses, typically drug-related. This approach has led to a quadrupling of the U.S. prison population to 2.4 million, with the nation incarcerating a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, at an annual cost of $80 billion.

Drug offenders in the U.S. face longer periods of formal control within the criminal justice system than anywhere else globally. A broad consensus among public officials, spanning the political spectrum, now views mandatory-minimum sentencing as an ineffective and detrimental policy.

At least 20 states have reformed their mandatory-sentencing laws, and bipartisan efforts are underway in Congress to address federal crimes. Currently, approximately 1.3 million individuals, over half of the total prison population, are incarcerated for nonviolent offenses, and federal prisons are operating at 40% over capacity.

Anthony Papa of the Drug Policy Alliance aptly stated, "It’s a waste of tax dollars and human lives." It is imperative that these unjust laws are repealed.

In response, on March 20, 2013, Senator Rand Paul introduced the Justice Safety Valve Act of 2013 with Senator Patrick J. Leahy. This bipartisan legislation, co-sponsored by Senators Carl Levin, Angus King, and Kristen Gillibrand, and Representative Robert C. "Bobby" Scott in the House, aims to provide judges with the authority to impose sentences below statutory mandatory minimums under specific conditions.

Mandatory-minimum sentencing has proven ineffective in addressing drug abuse while causing immense harm by destroying lives. The Justice Safety Valve Act represents a crucial step toward restoring fairness and discretion to the nation's courtrooms, ensuring that each case is judged on its individual merits.

A section within Rand Paul's 2012 book, *Government Bullies*, has drawn scrutiny for apparent plagiarism, specifically from a Forbes article published earlier that same year. This discovery follows previous reports by BuzzFeed highlighting instances of uncredited copying from The Heritage Foundation and the Cato Institute within the book. Similar to those earlier cases, where material from The Heritage Foundation and a Cato scholar was incorporated without clear attribution, Paul's text includes a link to the Forbes article in its footnotes. However, it fails to explicitly acknowledge that not only the source but the very wording was lifted from Forbes.

The Forbes article in question details the case of Steven and Cornelia Joyce Kinder, owners of a Kentucky-based caviar company. The couple pleaded guilty to harvesting paddlefish in Ohio waters with the intent to sell them in Kentucky, a violation of the Lacey Act.

The Forbes article states: "According to the plea agreement, both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting paddlefish that they should have known were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of Ohio law through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline, on or about May 5, 2007, and, thereafter, transporting the paddlefish to Kentucky with the intent to sell them when. As part of a plea agreement, both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit. Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform 100 hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else. In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited."

Rand Paul's book presents the following text, with the directly copied sections highlighted in bold: "The Justice Department's plea agreement stated that both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting the fish. The agreement stated that they should have known these paddlefish were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of state law, through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline. This happened on or about May 5, 2007, and thereafter the Kinders had transported the paddlefish to Kentucky with intent to sell them. Now, if you're like me, obviously we are getting down on our hands and knees to thank our federal government for addressing this pressing matter. But it gets even more ridiculous. **As part of a plea agreement, both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $ 5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit. Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform one hundred hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River, where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else. In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders also face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited.**"

Paul's office initially did not provide immediate comment on this specific instance. However, they previously stated that "there have also been occasions where quotations or typesetting indentations have been left out through errors in our approval process." The office of the Kentucky senator further elaborated that "a new approval process implemented by Sen. Paul will ensure proper citation and accountability in all collaborative works going forward."

Bill Singer, the author of the Forbes article, expressed surprise when asked if he was aware of the Senator's use of his work, stating, "no, absolutely not." He also noted that his reporting heavily relied on a press release from the Justice Department. Singer observed, "It would appear whoever wrote the senator's book copied my language not the press release. Plagiarism is a very sincere form of flattery." He further pointed out that even the more critical commentary within his article appeared in Paul's book, though both authors employed different examples for their sarcastic remarks regarding the government's prosecution.

Singer's commentary read: "Ain't it grand? The world is best by terrorism, civil war, earthquakes, tsunamis, and nuclear catastrophe but we still have time to go after alleged caviar bandits in the murky waters of Ohio and Kentucky." Paul's book included a similar sentiment: "We have a trillion-dollar deficit hole in our budget. We have bridges literally falling down and an infrastructure that is crumbling. We have a military that is stretched entirely too thin. We have a broken entitlement system, and a bunch of Washington 'leaders' too scared or inept to do anything about it. We have a border so porous that illegal immigrants and terrorists alike could be coming across it at any moment. We are told it cannot be fixed because we don't have the manpower. Yet somehow, some way, we have the manpower and resources to prosecute people for the following major 'crimes.'"

Senator Rand Paul has acknowledged errors in the sourcing of his published writings, following accusations of plagiarism. He specifically addressed a column previously printed by The Washington Times, taking personal responsibility for the oversight. His team indicated that background materials supplied by staff were not consistently footnoted or attributed correctly.

Paul suggested the media is applying a more rigorous standard to his work compared to other politicians. "The standard I’m being held to is a little different than everybody else," he remarked on CNN, adding, "They’re now going back and reading every book from cover to cover and looking for places where we footnoted correctly and don’t have quotation marks in the right places or we didn’t indent correctly." This suggests a perception of heightened scrutiny.

The Washington Times confirmed that an independent review led to a correction for Paul's September 20th column, which failed to attribute a passage from The Week magazine. Subsequently, the newspaper and Senator Paul mutually decided to conclude his weekly column.

John Solomon, Editor of The Washington Times, stated, "We expect our columnists to submit original work and to properly attribute material, and we appreciate that the senator and his staff have taken responsibility for an oversight in one column." He also noted, "We also appreciate the original insights he has shared with our readers over the last few months and look forward to future contributions from Sen. Paul and any other members of Congress who take the time to help educate our readers."

Previous reports from BuzzFeed highlighted potential issues, including a section of Paul's 2012 book, "Government Bullies," that appeared to draw heavily from a Forbes article. BuzzFeed also reported that Paul incorporated parts of an opinion piece on mandatory sentencing from The Week into a Washington Times article.

Doug Stafford, an advisor to Paul, asserted that the senator's core ideas are his own, explaining that he relied on staff for "supporting facts and anecdotes — some of which were clearly not sourced or vetted." He assured, "Going forward, footnotes will be available on request." The specific sentence in question from The Washington Times column, "By design, mandatory sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances," was found by BuzzFeed to have previously appeared in an article by Dan Steward in The Week.

While Stafford initially characterized the scrutiny as a "witch hunt," further examples emerged, suggesting a pattern. Senator Paul's recent admission appears to be a strategic effort to address these controversies and restore confidence in his published work's integrity. This situation underscores the critical importance of accurate and transparent attribution for public figures.

Mandatory sentencing laws, a key component of "tough on crime" strategies, mandate fixed minimum prison terms, significantly reducing a judge's ability to consider individual cases. These laws, primarily enacted to address drug offenses, aim to ensure harsh penalties regardless of specific circumstances. Introduced in the 1970s during a period of rising drug-related crime, mandatory sentencing has resulted in the imprisonment of hundreds of thousands for non-violent drug offenses. This era saw the U.S. prison population quadruple to 2.4 million, making the nation the leader in incarceration rates globally, with an annual cost of $80 billion.

A growing bipartisan agreement now questions the effectiveness and fairness of mandatory sentencing. As a result, at least 20 states have implemented reforms, and federal legislation is being considered. U.S. Attorney General Eric Holder has instructed federal prosecutors to use their judgment and avoid triggering mandatory sentences for offenders with minor criminal records, stating, "Too many Americans go to too many prisons for far too long, and for no truly good law-enforcement reason."

The origins of mandatory sentencing can be traced back about 40 years to New York State, prompted by a severe heroin epidemic. Governor Nelson Rockefeller's "Rockefeller Drug Laws" of 1973 imposed 15-year-to-life sentences for possessing even small amounts of narcotics, immediately drawing criticism for treating recreational use the same as trafficking. Other states soon adopted similar strict measures. Michigan's 1978 "650-lifer" law mandated life sentences for possessing over 650 grams of narcotics. In 1986, the federal government enacted its own mandatory sentencing law, the Anti-Drug Abuse Act, following the fatal cocaine overdose of basketball player Len Bias. This law established a five-year mandatory minimum for possessing 5 grams of crack cocaine and a ten-year minimum for 50 grams, with 20-year sentences for involvement in a "continuing criminal enterprise."

The effectiveness of these laws is still debated. While some law enforcement officials credit them with removing major drug traffickers and contributing to the crime reduction in the 1990s, critics argue they have not stopped drug trafficking and have disproportionately imprisoned people for non-violent offenses. Currently, about 1.3 million individuals, over half of the prison population, are jailed for non-violent crimes, and federal jails are operating at 40% over capacity. Anthony Papa of the Drug Policy Alliance calls this "a waste of tax dollars and human lives." The demographic impact is substantial, with African-American men making up a majority of those jailed for drug convictions, despite being only 13% of the population. They are four times more likely than white individuals to be arrested for marijuana possession and receive sentences that are, on average, 20% longer. This mass incarceration has deeply affected black communities, with one in nine black children having an incarcerated parent. Ron Daniels of York College notes that the "War on Drugs" has "disrupted families, decimated communities, and rendered America's 'dark ghettos' zones of desperation, desolation, and despair."

Reform advocates support ending mandatory sentences for non-violent offenses, calling for the return of judicial discretion. States like Ohio, Georgia, Pennsylvania, Hawaii, New York, and North Dakota have changed their sentencing laws. California voters repealed the "three strikes" law, which required life sentences for a third felony conviction, regardless of its seriousness. Even conservative states are exploring alternatives; Texas has shifted prison construction funds to drug courts, electronic monitoring, and improved parole supervision for non-violent offenders, leading to the closure of three prisons and a historic low crime rate. Senator Rand Paul (R-Ky.) states, "Mandatory minimums are costly, unfair, and do not make our country safer," adding, "The American people are ready to make sentencing reform a priority." John Horner, a 46-year-old father of three, illustrates the consequences of these laws. After agreeing to sell four unused bottles of prescribed painkillers to a friend, he learned the individual was a police informant. Advised to plead guilty, Horner received a mandatory 25-year sentence for drug dealing, facing release at age 72. The informant, who had a history of drug offenses, received a reduced 18-month sentence for cooperating.

Rand Paul's campaign is currently under intense scrutiny regarding the sourcing of his published works. He recently addressed a specific passage within his Washington Times article, explaining that it was adapted from a speech. Paul acknowledged that this adaptation process unfortunately led to a lack of thorough vetting. He emphasized that he is taking this situation with far greater seriousness than any previous disclosures.

One notable instance occurred last week on MSNBC's "The Rachel Maddow Show." Maddow brought attention to a speech delivered the previous Monday, which contained portions directly lifted from the Wikipedia description of the 1990s science fiction film "Gattaca." This movie, for context, portrays a future society where an individual's social standing is rigidly determined by DNA testing. The following day, Paul contended that this particular instance did not technically qualify as plagiarism. He clarified his stance, stating, "Trying to say someone commits plagiarism, you’re saying that someone is dishonest." He further elaborated, "And, well, it would be dishonest if I tried to say, ‘Oh, I had this great idea for a movie, and this is my idea, and this is a story I wrote in college called ‘Gattaca.’ ”

Paul attributed the unattributed passages found in his Washington Times article to the demanding and fast-paced environment associated with being a newly prominent national political figure. Kevin Madden, a communications aide for Mitt Romney's presidential campaign, offered his perspective on the escalating pressure Paul is experiencing. Madden commented, "This is what it looks like when you move from having just a Kentucky profile or just a Capitol Hill profile to building a national profile." He further suggested that this heightened scrutiny is unlikely to abate anytime soon.

Adding another layer to the controversy, BuzzFeed uncovered a passage in Forbes magazine that also appeared in Paul's book, "Government Bullies." In some instances, the text was reproduced verbatim. While this material was indeed cited in the book's appendix, it was not clearly identified as such within the main body of the text. Paul, without directly referencing this specific Forbes passage, explained that his book generally signaled cited material through a distinct, narrow typeset. He conceded that in certain cases, these indentations were regrettably omitted when they should have been present.

Paul indicated that further disclosures might surface, given the sheer volume of his collected writings and speeches. He expressed concern about the enduring nature of this issue, lamenting, "That’s why it’s like, how does this ever go away?" He concluded his remarks by stating, "The only way it goes away is that people have to accept what I’m telling them or not accept what I’m telling them — that we’ve never intended to deceive people, and we’ve made mistakes."

Last night on her show, Rachel Maddow addressed Rand Paul's comments from over the weekend when the Senator, while talking to George Stephanopoulos on ABC's This Week, expressed a desire to duel Maddow over her recent claims that Paul plagiarized large parts of his books and speeches from Wikipedia and other sources. (The accusations, by the way, are undeniably true.) "If dueling were legal in Kentucky, if they keep it up, you know, it would be a duel challenge," Paul said. "But I can’t do that, because I can’t hold office in Kentucky then.” Advertisement Now Maddow has responded to the Kentucky Republican's wimpy half-threat and, no, her reply wasn't "Pistols at dawn." Instead, she did what real adults do: She told Paul to grow up already and face the charges. "Senator Rand Paul wants to shoot at me or stab me with a sword or something for reporting something true that he has done wrong as a politician," Maddow told her audience. "Responding to the person rather than the charge is a time-tested tactic. Honestly, it's a symptom of immaturity in our political discourse." Visit NBCNews.com for breaking news, world news, and news about the economy Sponsored Worth noting: Senator Paul was charged again with plagiarism Monday night, this time for an op-ed that he wrote for the Washington Times in which he appears to have lifted lines directly from the Dan Stewart article "Rethinking Mandatory Sentencing."

query: What incidents led to the fatal stabbings on the MAX train in Portland?

documents.text:
A harrowing incident on a MAX train in northeast Portland saw two individuals tragically lose their lives and another sustain injuries. This occurred on a Friday when witnesses reported that the victims intervened to defend two young girls. The defense was prompted by a man who had begun shouting hateful messages at the girls. In the aftermath, one of the young women targeted by the hate speech has expressed her profound gratitude to those who stepped in to protect her.

Destinee Mangum, a 16-year-old, recounted her experience to FOX 12. She and her 17-year-old friend were passengers on a MAX train when a man, later identified as 35-year-old Jeremy Joseph Christian, approached them. He began yelling what witnesses and authorities have described as hate speech or bias-motivated language.

Mangum, who is not Muslim, stated that her friend is. The aggressor directed hateful remarks at them, telling them to "go back to Saudi Arabia" and that they "shouldn't be here, to get out of his country." He continued with dehumanizing statements, implying they were worthless and should end their own lives.

Feeling scared, Mangum and her friend decided to move to the back of the train. It was at this point that a stranger intervened. According to Mangum, this individual, a white male from the back of the train, confronted the aggressor, questioning his behavior towards the young ladies and stating that he could not disrespect them in such a manner. This confrontation quickly escalated into an argument involving multiple people.

The events that followed are etched in Mangum's memory. As she and her friend prepared to disembark the MAX train, they turned to witness the unfolding fight. Suddenly, the aggressor began stabbing people. Mangum described a scene of chaos and bloodshed, recalling that "it was just blood everywhere and we just started running for our lives."

Dyjuana Hudson, Mangum's mother, expressed her immense debt to the two men who were killed and their grieving families. She conveyed her deepest thanks, acknowledging the unimaginable pain of losing a loved one and expressing sorrow that it occurred at the hands of her children.

Mangum herself has a heartfelt message for the strangers who became heroes. She wishes to express her sincere gratitude to those who sacrificed their lives to protect her and her friend. She emphasized that these individuals did not know them but lost their lives because of their appearance and the prejudice they faced. Mangum concluded by stating her appreciation for them and their families, believing that without their intervention, she and her friend might not have survived.

The Internet Archive actively archives the digital world by conducting thorough web crawls. This process meticulously captures digital content as it appears and preserves the linked resources it points to. This proactive strategy guarantees that even if a referenced webpage changes or disappears from the live internet, a stable link to the version that existed when the original page was created remains available.

As a result, the Internet Archive aims to replace broken links with reliable references to these archived versions. They also provide additional links that help clarify the original author's intent. The main goal is to systematically fix all broken hyperlinks found online, with a special emphasis on supporting "No More 404" projects.

Journalist Dan Rather has urged President Trump to publicly address the fatal stabbing on a Portland MAX train, an act of extreme violence that resulted in the deaths of Ricky John Best, 53, and Taliesin Namkai-Meche, 23. These men were killed while bravely intervening against a white supremacist who was espousing hate speech. Their courageous actions aimed to protect two young women, who appeared to be Muslim, from the man's vitriolic tirade. Authorities later confirmed that Micah David-Cole Fletcher sustained injuries during the attack. The suspect, Jeremy Christian, 35, who has documented ties to white supremacist ideologies, was apprehended and faces aggravated murder charges.

Rather, former anchor of the CBS Evening News, suggested this attack could be classified as terrorism. He specifically honored Best and Namkai-Meche, expressing concern over President Trump's apparent silence on the incident, even two days later, with no public statement or social media activity from the White House.

Rather believes this tragedy may not align with narratives President Trump frequently promoted. He noted the contrast between the victims' motivations and the typical targets of the President's rhetoric, writing, "They were not killed by an undocumented immigrant or a 'radical Islamic terrorist.' They were killed in an act of civic love, facing down a man allegedly spewing hate speech directed at two teenage girls, one of whom was wearing a hijab." This highlights the unique motivation behind the attack: the selfless act of protecting others from hate speech.

The brutality of the Portland attack, occurring in a public transit setting, sent national shockwaves. It was particularly disheartening that the victims were targeted for confronting hate speech. Reports of increasing hate crimes, especially against Muslim individuals, have been a growing concern since President Trump's campaign. Rather acknowledged that many experts do not consider this rise in hate crimes a mere coincidence. While he clarified he does not directly blame the President for this specific incident, he emphasized the significant influence a President's rhetoric, associations, and public tone can have on national discourse and citizen behavior.

Rather implored President Trump to acknowledge Best and Namkai-Meche by name or share a message of condolence. President Trump, having recently returned from the G7 conference, had sent over a dozen tweets on various subjects since the attack. In contrast, Hillary Clinton promptly addressed the attack on Twitter, expressing her heartbreak and solidarity. This disparity underscored Rather's concerns about presidential leadership and the importance of fostering inclusive public discourse, especially in the face of rising hate-motivated violence. The incident serves as a somber reminder of the power of words and the responsibility of public office.

The recent indictment of Roger Stone, a longtime associate of the President, underscores a disturbing trend: an increasing number of individuals with White House connections are facing criminal charges. This pattern within the President's close circle warrants serious attention.

While the justice system upholds the presumption of innocence, the sheer number of accusations against those linked to the President naturally raises concerns. Law enforcement probes frequently start with such signals, hinting at broader problems. The rising count of indictments and guilty pleas among the President's inner circle suggests these are not isolated occurrences.

To understand the gravity of these developments, one might consider Watergate. However, the current situation could be more concerning due to the potential involvement of foreign adversaries. This international dimension sets the present circumstances apart.

Legal experts have drawn parallels between the President's public remarks, particularly on social media, and the methods employed by organized crime figures. His public threats and indirect references to informants are seen by legal professionals as possible witness intimidation. For many, the President's assertions of ignorance or non-involvement have already diminished his credibility, and this latest indictment further erodes public trust.

This situation is not a baseless "witch hunt." Instead, it resembles a "which hunt": Which associate will be targeted next? What new details will emerge? This period is shaping up to be a somber chapter in American history. The bedrock principle of "innocent until proven guilty" is vital to American justice. Nevertheless, the accumulating evidence and the character of the allegations necessitate thorough scrutiny.

The lone survivor of Friday’s vicious attack on a crowded MAX train may owe his life in part to another Good Samaritan who rushed to the man’s side to render critical medical aid. Micah David-Cole Fletcher, 21, on Sunday continued to recover from knife wounds to his neck. A Portland man with known extremist views verbally assaulted a teenage girl wearing a hijab and her friend on the train, then turned his rage on Fletcher and two others, an Army veteran and a recent Reed College graduate. Jeremy Joseph Christian, 35, cut all three men in the neck. Rick Best, 53, of Happy Valley, and Taliesin Myrddin Namkai-Meche, 23, died from their injuries. Fletcher, an aspiring poet, is slowly improving in an area hospital, said his girlfriend, Miranda Helm. “He’s getting a little bit stronger,” she said Sunday. “He’s getting good rest. He’s finally able to eat now.” Helm said Fletcher wants to meet the man he credits with helping to save him. The unidentified man rushed to Fletcher’s side and used a cloth to apply pressure to his wound. He also found Fletcher’s cell phone and looked through the contacts to find someone to call. He saw a contact for “mom,” said Helm. The man told Fletcher's mother that he was a veteran and was familiar with first aid. "He was trying to reassure her that he was capable and confident" in what he was doing, Helm said. The man didn’t describe the true nature of Fletcher’s injuries, saving Fletcher’s mother from worry as she raced to the hospital. It was a gesture of compassion that Fletcher’s family appreciated, Helm said. He said her son had been “stabbed in the arm so we would not panic as much,” Helm said. She said by the time Fletcher’s family arrived at the hospital, the medical staff said he would survive his injuries. “We were able to go into the ER much more composed than we would have otherwise,” she said. Helm said the mother of one of the girls that Fletcher, Best and Namkai-Meche were trying to protect also reached out to Fletcher. Fletcher's friends on Saturday posted new photos of the 21-year-old, including one of him flashing a "thumbs up'' from his hospital bed on a "Tri Met Hero Recovery'' GoFundMe site. A second photo showed the large wound to the left side of his neck, stretching from just below his chin up to under his left ear. The site is to raise money to cover his hospital and recovery costs. "Micah is recovering, but it's going to be a lengthy process,'' a message with the posted photos said. "He is overwhelmed by the outpouring of love and support he's received.'' Meanwhile, new details surfaced Sunday about Christian. He allegedly started a fight with a woman at a MAX station the day before the deadly altercation, officials said. KGW reported that Christian threw a plastic bottle at a black woman, who in turn sprayed him with mace. Portland police were aware of the altercation and seeking video of the incident. More details also emerged Sunday about Christian's criminal history. During an 8-year prison stint for robbery, Christian racked up a lengthy disciplinary record, according to Oregon Department of Corrections officials. Those violations included assault and disobedience. He moved around in the state's prison system, serving time at four institutions in all. Upon his release, he served his post-prison supervision with Multnomah County. His probation ended in January 2014, state officials said Sunday. Like other acquaintances and friends of Christian, Lindsey Self says she noticed a drastic change in his behavior after he served nearly eight years in prison. “I am still in shock from this news,” Self wrote in a Facebook message. “Jeremy was a friend of mine since we were 17. We worked together at Pietro's Pizza, our first job out of high school. He was a good guy -- strange at times, but funny and quirky.” Self, a registered nurse, said she’d had little or no contact with Christian the past five years. “I've seen his rants on Facebook,” she wrote. “That seemed odd for him. Something in him changed over the recent years. He didn't do drugs. He was mentally ill. He needed help and I feel so sad that we all lost touch and didn't reach out to him. He was a good guy at one point. “All I have to say is I hope this brings attention to the need for mental health facilities and more outreach. Jeremy was another victim of the criminal justice system: let out with no resources, no guidance, a child trapped in a man's body. I'm heart broken.” Tomica Carr said she has known Christian since elementary school. She said she was surprised to learn people call Christian a racist. Carr is black and said Christian had a lot of black friends, she said. "He never disrespected me," Carr said. Her daughter heard Christian using the N-word at Montavilla Park in April. "I was shocked," Carr said. Christian told her about an elementary school reunion he planned, and many other black people were on the invitation list, she said. In elementary school, Christian was the class clown, Carr said. "We both got into trouble," she said. "He was acting out all the time, every day." Echoing a sentiment others have shared, Carr said Christian emerged from prison a changed man. "Prison took the real him away," she said. Afterward, she said, he was "more nutty." Three separate fundraising efforts for the victims and their families, meanwhile, raised nearly $800,000 as of Sunday evening. The largest initiative had raised more than $350,000 from more than 7,000 contributors. Nick Zukin, a Portland man who said he doesn't know the victims, started his Gofundme campaign because he was looking for a place to direct his own donation. "These guys stood up for what was right and paid the ultimate price," he wrote on the site. "I'm glad we are helping their families as a community." Staff writers Allan Brettman, Kale Williams and Carli Brosseau contributed to this report. -- Noelle Crombie ncrombie@oregonian.com 503-276-7184; @noellecrombie

In the wake of a horrific anti-Muslim hate crime on a Portland light-rail train, the Muslim community has expressed profound gratitude for the outpouring of support. The incident, which occurred on the first day of Ramadan, saw two men tragically lose their lives after intervening when a suspect began spewing hateful slurs at two young women, one of whom was wearing a hijab.

Wajdi Said, president and co-founder of the Muslim Educational Trust, spoke at a news conference, highlighting the unity displayed in the aftermath. "I am very thankful as a Muslim, I am very thankful as a Portlander ... that we stand together here as one," stated Muhammad A. Najieb, an imam at the Muslim Community Center. He emphasized that the two women could have been the primary targets, but instead, "three heroes jumped in and supported them."

A fundraising initiative established for the families of the deceased, a surviving victim, and the two young women had already garnered over $300,000 by Sunday evening, demonstrating significant community solidarity.

Authorities are investigating the suspect, identified as Jeremy Joseph Christian, 35, for what appears to be an extremist ideology. His social media activity reportedly indicates an affinity for Nazism and political violence. Christian faces charges including aggravated murder, attempted murder, intimidation, and illegal possession of a weapon. He was apprehended shortly after the attack when confronted by other individuals.

One of the targeted young women, 16-year-old Destinee Mangum, recounted the terrifying experience. She and her 17-year-old friend, who is Muslim, were subjected to hateful remarks, including being told to "go back to Saudi Arabia" and that they "shouldn't be here, to get out of his country." Mangum described the assailant's words as demeaning, suggesting they were "basically weren't anything and that we should kill ourselves." The girls, frightened, moved to the back of the train.

The intervention of bystanders proved fatal. Alvin Hall, who had just exited the train, witnessed the unfolding violence and pursued the suspect. He, along with Chase Robinson and Larry Blackwell, confronted Christian, who then attacked them with a knife. Hall described the suspect's aggressive challenge: "You want some of me, you're a snitch, come on after me, you want some of this?" Police arrived shortly thereafter and took Christian into custody.

The victims have been identified as:

*   **Ricky John Best**, 53, of Happy Valley, Oregon. Mayor Ted Wheeler noted Best was an Army veteran and a city employee.
*   **Taliesin Myrddin Namkai Meche**, 23, of Portland. Meche held a bachelor's degree in economics from Reed College and was employed by the Cadmus Group.

A third victim, Micah David-Cole Fletcher, 21, of Portland, was also stabbed and is in serious but not life-threatening condition. Fletcher, a student at Portland State University, was on his way to work when the attack occurred. Notably, Fletcher had previously won a poetry slam competition in 2013 with a poem condemning prejudice against Muslims.

Police Sergeant Pete Simpson confirmed that the assailant was using "hate speech or biased language" during the incident. The FBI is currently assessing whether the slayings qualify as a federal hate crime, though Christian is facing state intimidation charges, the equivalent of a hate crime.

Reports indicate Christian had previously attended a free speech march in late April, where he was seen with a baseball bat and shouting nihilistic slogans. Video evidence from that event has been confirmed by police to be of Christian. His online presence also reportedly showed support for Nazis and Timothy McVeigh.

Jeremy Christian, the suspect in the fatal MAX train stabbings in Portland, Oregon, had a prior confrontation at a TriMet station the day before the tragic events. This earlier incident involved an altercation with a woman at the Interstate Rose Quarter Station.

According to an anonymous source, Christian allegedly threw a bottle of Gatorade at a Black woman late Thursday night. The woman then reportedly used mace against Christian. Detectives investigating the fatal stabbings are aware of this preceding incident, the Portland Police Bureau has confirmed.

Christian, 35, faces serious charges including aggravated murder, attempted murder, intimidation, and being a felon in possession of a weapon. He was apprehended shortly after the Friday attack.

Authorities have stated that the stabbings on Friday occurred after Christian directed racial slurs at two young women, one of whom was reportedly wearing a hijab. Several bystanders attempted to intervene and de-escalate the situation. The victims of the fatal attack were identified as Rick John Best, 53, and Taliesin Myrddin Namkai Meche, 23. A third individual, Micah David-Cole Fletcher, 21, was injured but has since been released from the hospital.

Portland, Oregon, is currently experiencing heightened tensions following a tragic double murder that has amplified the city's existing political divides. The situation has become particularly charged with the planned continuation of an "alt-right" rally, despite the organizer's refusal to cancel it. This defiance has spurred antifascist groups to pledge a strong counter-response.

The incident that ignited these tensions involved the fatal stabbing of Taliesin Myrddin Namkai-Meche, 23, and Rick Best, 53, on a city train. They had intervened after a man, identified as Jeremy Christian, allegedly directed hate speech at two women. Christian, 35, is suspected of being a white supremacist and is awaiting court proceedings. Another individual was injured during the attack.

Despite the gravity of the murders and condemnation from city officials, "alt-right" organizers, led by Joey Gibson of the Patriot Prayer group, intend to proceed with a "free speech rally" in downtown Portland. In response, self-described "anti-fascist" activists are preparing to confront the event. Gibson, a YouTube personality, has organized several rallies in the Portland area this year, which have often been confrontational and have sometimes resulted in violence. Christian was reportedly filmed at a previous Patriot Prayer event making fascist salutes and using racial slurs.

Gibson has strongly denied any connection to Christian, stating that Christian was not invited and that his group does not know him. He claims that "antifa and liberals in Portland" are using images of Christian to falsely associate his group with white supremacy. Gibson asserts he is not racist, believing in equality and supporting women's and LGBT rights. He expressed uncertainty about the "alt-right" label and framed his political choices as a matter of "freedom" versus "tyranny."

Rose City Antifa, a prominent antifascist group, is a key organizer of opposition to Gibson's rallies. A spokesperson for the group dismissed Gibson's disavowals, emphasizing that the attendees define the nature of an event. They highlighted Portland's history of white supremacist activity, citing past incidents and the persistent presence of extremist groups. Rose City Antifa has identified several white supremacists who attended the April 29th event, suggesting Christian was not an isolated case. They claim that individuals with KKK and Identity Europa affiliations, as well as other white supremacists, have attended previous rallies, and that some were allowed to remain at the April event despite displaying swastika pins. The group argues that the presence of such individuals and the escalating violence undermine Gibson's claims of promoting free speech, asserting that the impact and conduct at a rally are more significant than its stated purpose.

Gibson anticipates a hostile reception at the upcoming rally, attributing it to perceived mischaracterizations of his group's involvement with Christian. He also referenced a Department of Homeland Security report labeling anti-Trump protests involving "black bloc" anarchists as "domestic terrorist violence." Rose City Antifa is mobilizing for a significant turnout, distributing flyers and expressing confidence that Portlanders are "tired of this circus" and the excuses for these rallies. They anticipate a large counter-demonstration, potentially continuing the political confrontations that have intensified in the city.

query: What evidence is authorities focusing on in their investigation of the Brussels attacks?

documents.text: Brussels, Belgium (CNN) In grainy images from surveillance footage, a man wearing light-colored clothes and a hat pushes a baggage cart through the airport. It's one key piece of evidence authorities are looking at as they search for suspects after two explosions at the Brussels airport and another at a busy subway station in the Belgian capital Tuesday killed at least 30 people and wounded 230 others. ISIS claimed responsibility for the coordinated attacks, but authorities said it's too soon to say for sure whether the terror group was behind the blasts. So far, police have released photos of three men they say are suspects tied to the airport attack, standing side-by-side. Police say they are looking for the man on the right in connection with the Brussels attack. Two of the men, wearing black in surveillance images, are believed to be suicide bombers who died in the explosions in the airport's departure lounge. But investigators believe the one in light-colored clothing planted a bomb at the airport, then left. Authorities called him a wanted man and asked for the public's help tracking him down. "The third man left a bomb in the airport, but it didn't explode. ... And we are now looking for this guy," Belgium's Interior Minister Jan Jambon said. A photograph released by investigators shows the three suspects side-by-side. Federal Prosecutor Frederic Van Leeuw said the two men wearing black in the photograph were likely the suicide attackers. Video shows the men exiting a taxi and moving through the airport, according to two U.S. officials. The man dressed in white left the airport after accompanying the other two, they said -- a move the officials said appeared to be planned. Taxi driver tip sparks raid A break in the investigation may have come from a taxi driver who took the suspects to the airport. The driver contacted authorities after seeing surveillance footage and gave them the address where he picked the men up, according to two U.S. officials briefed on the investigation. That information prompted authorities to raid a residence after the attacks, the officials said. Investigators found a nail bomb, chemical products and an ISIS flag during a house search in the northeast Brussels neighborhood of Schaerbeek, Belgium's federal prosecutor said in a statement. Hours later, they were still combing through the building for evidence. Security was high. At one point, a helicopter hovered overhead, carrying a sniper with a weapon trained on the building. As masked, armed officers stood guard outside the building, the burst of camera flashes inside could be seen from the street below. Officers left the building carrying bags of evidence they loaded onto vehicles. Ties to Paris attacks? A Belgian government representative told CNN that 10 people were killed and 100 wounded at Brussels' international airport. At least 20 people died and 130 were wounded at the Maelbeek metro station, officials said. The blasts sent wounded people fleeing into the streets, spurred evacuations of nuclear plants and transit hubs and led to raids in some areas as authorities searched for suspects and evidence. Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels An injured woman leaves the airport in Brussels, Belgium, after two explosions rocked the facility on Tuesday, March 22. There was also an explosion at a subway station in the city. Hide Caption 1 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Windows are blown out after the deadly attack at the airport. Hide Caption 2 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A police officer directs passengers in a smoke-filled airport terminal. Hide Caption 3 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Two wounded women are seen in the airport. Hide Caption 4 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels This photo from inside the airport was shared by Jef Versele on Facebook. Hide Caption 5 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Subway passengers walk along the tracks following a blast at the Maelbeek metro station. Hide Caption 6 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A private security guard helps a wounded woman outside the Maelbeek metro station. Hide Caption 7 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Wounded people are treated outside the subway station. Hide Caption 8 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Rescue teams evacuate the subway station. Hide Caption 9 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Police officers guard the area around the subway station.
