Hide Caption 22 of 22 Authorities in Belgium have been trying to crack down on terror threats for months as they raided homes in the area in search of suspects. Tuesday's violence came just days after investigators closed in on Europe's most wanted man, Paris attacks suspect Salah Abdeslam, who was hiding out in a Brussels suburb. The "working assumption" is that the attackers came from the network behind November's massacres in Paris , which left 130 dead, Belgian security sources said, while cautioning it is very early in the latest investigation. ISIS also claimed responsibility for those attacks. As doctors treat the wounded and authorities search for suspects, a key question remains unanswered: Could the attacks have anything to do with Abdeslam's arrest On Tuesday, Belgian Prime Minister Charles Michel deflected a question about whether there is any link between the attacks and the Belgium-born French citizen's capture, saying it is too early to tell. Michel said Tuesday he had "no information" about who was responsible for the attack, adding that authorities will find that out, but now their focus is on caring for the victims. Two senior U.S. officials told CNN they believe the Belgium attack is tied to the same network as Abdeslam. Fears realized One of the two airport explosions happened outside security checkpoints for ticketed passengers and near the airline check-in counters, an airline official briefed on the situation said. The subway station blast happened about an hour later in the Brussels district of Maelbeek, near the European Quarter, where European Union institutions are based. "We were fearing terrorist attacks," Michel told reporters Tuesday. "And that has now happened." Belgium is no stranger to terror . "The Belgians have been sitting on a ticking time bomb," a U.S. counterterrorism official said, given all those who have traveled from the small European nation to Syria and Iraq to join ISIS , then possibly come back home. But for survivors of Tuesday's blasts, the repeated warnings from officials in recent months didn't dull the shock of seeing the carnage. "You cannot believe it; you cannot believe it," said Jef Versele, who was in the airport's departure hall when bombs exploded there. "It was so insane. Not in my backyard." The second blast inside the airport blew out windows, created a lot of smoke and caused parts of the ceiling to fall, he added. "People were on the floor," Versele said, estimating he saw 50 to 60 who were thrown to the ground and didn't seem to be able to walk. Anthony Barrett saw the wounded carried out on stretchers and luggage carts as he watched from his hotel across from the terminal building. "I could see people fleeing," he said. 'We remain united' After the attacks in Brussels, the home of NATO and the capital of the European Union, leaders inside Belgium and beyond vowed not to back down in their fight against terror. In Belgium, where officials declared three days of national mourning, Michel offered a resolute message to those who supported and cheered the attackers. "To those who have chosen to be barbarous enemies of freedom, democracy and fundamental values ... we remain united as one," Michel said. "We are determined to defend our freedoms and to protect our liberties." In its message claiming responsibility, ISIS noted that Belgium is one of the nations "participating in the international coalition against the Islamic State." A Twitter post widely circulated by prominent ISIS backers Tuesday featured the words, "What will be coming is worse."

BRUSSELS — Bombs packed with nails terrorized Brussels on Tuesday in the deadliest assault on the European heartland since the Islamic State’s attacks on Paris four months ago, hitting the airport and subway system in coordinated strikes that were also claimed by the militant extremist group. The bombings paralyzed Brussels, the headquarters of the European Union and NATO, prompted international travel warnings to avoid Belgium and reverberated across the Atlantic to the United States, where New York and other major cities raised terrorism threat levels. Anxieties intensified about the inability to prevent mass killings at relatively unprotected places. At least 30 people were killed by two blasts at the Brussels airport departure area around 8 a.m. and one in a subway station shortly after 9. The police found at least one other unexploded bomb in a search of a Brussels house hours later. And Europe’s most wanted person suddenly became an unidentified man in a white coat and dark hat seen pushing a luggage cart in an airport surveillance photo taken just before the bombings. Two other men in the photo, each wearing a black glove on his left hand, were identified by Belgian prosecutors as suspected suicide bombers who appeared to have died in the explosions. “To those who have chosen to be the barbaric enemies of liberty, of democracy, of fundamental values, I want to say with the greatest strength that we will remain assembled and united,” the Belgium prime minister, Charles Michel, said at a news conference Tuesday evening, declaring a three-day mourning period.

On **Tuesday, March 22, 2016**, Brussels was struck by a series of **devastating attacks** at its airport and subway system. These events, which resulted in **at least 13 fatalities** and numerous injuries, occurred shortly after a significant figure connected to the November Paris attacks was apprehended in the city.

At the **Brussels airport**, the departure hall was plunged into chaos. **Two explosions** within the hall caused **falling debris and widespread harm**. All air traffic was **immediately suspended**, with incoming flights diverted. Belgium's national terror alert was elevated to its **highest level**, and residents were instructed to **remain indoors**, effectively halting city operations. Security measures were also **considerably enhanced** at all Paris airports.

European security agencies had been anticipating a major assault for weeks, with intelligence pointing to **active planning by the Islamic State**. The recent capture of **Salah Abdeslam** in Brussels intensified these concerns, as investigators noted that many individuals involved in the **deadly November 13 Paris attacks** (which claimed 130 lives) remained at large.

Following Abdeslam's arrest, Belgian Foreign Minister Didier Reynders indicated that authorities had discovered he had established a **new network** and possessed **multiple weapons**. However, at that juncture, there was **no direct evidence** linking him or the Islamic State to the day's assaults.

Belgian media confirmed **13 deaths at the airport**. It remained unclear if any casualties occurred in the subway explosion. Zach Mouzoun, who had just arrived on a flight from Geneva, described the harrowing scene. He recounted the **second, more powerful explosion** causing ceilings to collapse and pipes to rupture, mixing water with victims' blood. "It was **horrific**. The ceilings fell," he stated. "There was **blood everywhere, injured people, scattered luggage**." He likened the scene to a "war zone" as they navigated through the wreckage.

Near the entrance to the **Maelbeek subway station**, close to the European Union's headquarters, rescue workers established a temporary medical post. Dazed commuters emerged from the metro entrances as police secured the area. Alexandre Brans, 32, described the moment of the explosion: "The Metro was leaving Maelbeek station for Schuman when there was a **very loud blast**. It was **panic all around**. There were many people on the Metro." Francoise Ledune, a spokesperson for the Brussels Metro, indicated that there appeared to have been **only one explosion**, occurring inside a stopped train car at Maelbeek. First responders were observed carrying two individuals on stretchers from the station, their clothing visibly torn.

The airport explosions took place during its **busiest travel period**. Smoke billowed from the terminal building. Amateur footage showed passengers, including a child, fleeing the terminal with their belongings. Another image depicted a security officer in an interior hall where panels had been blown out and ceiling insulation lay on the floor. Denise Brandt, an American, recognized the sound of an explosion from past experiences. "I felt the blast, the way it impacts your body," she said. "And we just looked at each other and I said 'Let's go this way.' It was over there. There was just this **instinct to get away from it**. Then we saw people running, crying, toward us. So I knew we were heading in the right direction and away from it."

The airport, a vital hub connecting Europe's capital to 226 global destinations, handled close to **23.5 million passengers in 2015**. Following the attacks, passengers were led onto the tarmac, and the crisis center advised the public to **avoid the airport**.

*Associated Press writers Raf Casert in Brussels and Angela Charlton and Lori Hinnant in Paris contributed to this report.*

Image copyright AFP Image caption Police are hunting for the man pictured in the far right of this CCTV image of three suspected attackers More than 30 people are believed to have been killed and dozens injured in attacks at Brussels international airport and a city metro station. Twin blasts hit Zaventem airport at about 07:00 GMT, with 11 people reported killed. Another explosion struck Maelbeek metro station near EU headquarters an hour later, leaving about 20 people dead. Brussels police have issued a wanted notice for a man seen pushing a luggage trolley through the airport. He was pictured in CCTV footage with two other suspects who are believed to have died in the blasts. The Islamic State (IS) group said it was behind the attacks in a statement issued on the IS-linked Amaq agency. Belgium has raised its terrorism alert to its highest level. Three days of national mourning have been declared. Prime Minister Charles Michel called the latest attacks "blind, violent and cowardly", adding: "This is a day of tragedy, a black day... I would like to call on everyone to show calmness and solidarity". 'The worst thing I've seen' Two blasts tore through the departures area of Zaventem airport shortly after 08:00 local time (07:00 GMT). A suicide bomber was "probably" involved, the Belgian prosecutor said. Eleven people were killed and 81 wounded in the blasts, Belgian Health Minister Maggie de Block said. Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption James Firkin was at Brussels airport when the blast happened More about the attacks Live: The latest updates What we know so far Why was Brussels attacked? Crisis information Some witnesses reported hearing shots fired and shouts in Arabic before the two explosions. Others said that people fled the first blast, only to get caught in the second. A third bomb failed to detonate and was destroyed by the security services. A local firefighter, Pierre Meys, described seeing "war injuries". "I think this is the worst thing I've ever seen in my career," he said. Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Debris litters the floor of Brussels airport after two explosions hit the building Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption People leaving the Brussels metro following the blast At the scene - By Gavin Lee, news correspondent Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption People fled Brussels airport after two explosions went off "People were running over others that had fallen, I couldn't breathe. I can't believe I'm alive," 15-year-old Antoine told me as he walked with his school friends in a line of hundreds being escorted on foot and by bus to a sports hall, now a makeshift reception centre. Three hearses passed, heading towards the ruins of the airport departure hall. People sobbed at the sight of them. Several tourists were asking police what they should do now, where it was safe to go - and when the airport might reopen. The police were confused too, but offered what little information they had, telling people to head to a reception centre. An officer repeatedly shouted: "Quickly, keep moving, evacuate, this is serious." Carnage on the Metro The metro blast occurred shortly after 08:00 GMT during the rush hour at Maelbeek station. It struck the middle carriage of a three-carriage train while it was moving away from the platform. Alexandre Brans told AP: "The metro was leaving Maelbeek station when there was a really loud explosion. It was panic everywhere. There were a lot of people in the metro." Brussels Mayor Yvan Mayeur said "about 20" people had died and more than 100 had been injured, 17 of them severely. Eyewitnesses recall explosions In pictures: Brussels explosions Full coverage The station is close to EU institutions. The European Commission, the EU's executive arm, has told employees to stay indoors or at home. All meetings at EU institutions have been cancelled. Ryan McGhee, a catering worker at a college in Brussels, told the BBC: "The entire city is in lockdown. People are calm at the moment but the atmosphere is tense." Security raised Local and international travel has been suspended or disrupted and security tightened across Europe. All flights have been cancelled. The airport is due to reopen on Wednesday. Eurostar has cancelled all trains to and from Brussels. The Thalys France-Benelux train operator says the entire network is closed. In the UK, security has been stepped up at Gatwick and Heathrow airports. The UK Foreign Office has advised British nationals to avoid crowded areas in Belgium. UK Prime Minister David Cameron chaired a meeting of the Cobra response committee on Tuesday. In France the cabinet has held an emergency meeting. There is also extra security at Dutch airports. Image copyright Horst Pilger Image caption Security has been stepped up after the explosions Image copyright Ralph Usbeck via AP Image caption Passengers react in the smoke-filled aftermath at the airport 'Outrageous' attacks There has been strong international condemnation: US President Barack Obama called the blasts "outrageous attacks against innocent people". The 28 EU leaders said the bombings were an "attack on our open democratic society" in a joint statement. "The terrorists have struck Belgium but it is Europe that was targeted," said French President Francois Hollande. Russian President Vladimir Putin called the attacks "barbaric". Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Brussels airport worker: 'My colleague hid in the luggage carousel' Belgium's Interior Minister Jan Jambon had said on Monday that the country was on the highest level of alert for possible revenge attacks after the capture on Friday of Salah Abdeslam, the main surviving suspect from the Paris attacks in November. Mr Jambon told Belgian radio: "We know that stopping one cell can... push others into action. We are aware of it in this case." Gaps in intelligence: By Chris Morris, Europe correspondent European security experts had been braced for another attack for months. But it is always a huge shock when it actually happens. If this was "revenge" for the arrest of Salah Abdeslam on Friday, it will be a source of considerable concern that a functioning terrorist network was able to respond so quickly and with such devastating effect. It is possible that a cell linked to Abdeslam brought forward the timing of a future attack because they thought he might blow their cover. Either way, it shows how advanced the planning was in terms of logistics, explosives, weapons and people willing to carry out such attacks on civilian targets. The priority now will be to ensure that anyone else who poses an imminent threat to the public is apprehended as soon as possible. But it is clear that there are still huge gaps in intelligence, and Brussels is seen as a soft target. In the words of French President Francois Hollande, the response from Europe will need to be "calm, lucid and determined" - and it will have to last for a long time.

VTM Nieuws uses cookies to enhance your online experience and safeguard your privacy. We employ cookies to understand site traffic and user interests, enabling us to offer free content, personalize your visits, and display relevant advertisements. For comprehensive details on the cookies we utilize, their specific functions, and how to manage your preferences, please refer to the "More information" link.

### How VTM Nieuws Uses Cookies

Cookies are fundamental to a user-friendly website. They can be placed by us or by third parties, such as advertising networks. Crucially, cookies **do not collect personal information** like your address or phone number, meaning they cannot be used for direct marketing purposes.

Our transparent cookie policy is designed to:

*   **Protect your privacy:** We handle your data with utmost care.
*   **Improve your experience:** Making our site easier to navigate and use.
*   **Keep content accessible:** Advertising revenue allows us to provide news at no cost to you.
*   **Maintain content quality:** Helping us deliver high-standard news consistently.

To adjust your cookie settings, click "Settings." By selecting "Continue to website," you consent to our use of cookies for an optimized browsing experience.

### Types of Cookies Used by VTM Nieuws

#### Essential Cookies

These cookies are **critical for our website's functionality**. They assist with:

*   Remembering your preferences, such as language and search history.
*   Ensuring smooth video playback.
*   Securely storing your login credentials.
*   Adapting the site to your browser's settings.
*   Detecting and preventing misuse of the site.
*   Maintaining the overall smooth operation of the website.
*   Enabling features like user comments.
*   Managing items in your shopping cart.
*   Saving your order details during the checkout process.
*   Efficiently processing data for forms and orders.

**Key Essential Cookies:**

*   **Adblock detect:** Identifies if an ad blocker is active.
*   **Adobe Typekit:** Ensures correct font display.
*   **DFP (Doubleclick):** Manages the delivery of advertisements.
*   **Drupal (post-authentication):** Supports site functions after a user logs in.
*   **Facebook connect:** Allows users to log in using their Facebook accounts.
*   **Freewheel:** Manages the delivery of video advertisements.
*   **Gigya socialize:** Handles user registration and login processes.
*   **Google IMA Ads:** Integrates Google's advertising software development kit.
*   **Google Maps GEO:** Collects geographical location data.
*   **Google Publisher tags:** Facilitates ad management through Google's platform.
*   **Google Recaptcha:** Protects against automated bot activity.
*   **Google Save Frame:** Assists in the display of advertising content.
*   **Hello Bar Smartbar:** Displays site-wide announcements.
*   **Myfonts Counter:** Tracks the usage of specific fonts.
*   **Sourcepoint:** Manages user consent and detects ad blockers.
*   **SSO Sign on:** Enables single sign-on functionality.
*   **SUMO Smartbar:** Displays site-wide notices.
*   **Usersnap:** Collects bug reports and user feedback.

#### Analytics Cookies

These cookies help us understand how you interact with different sections of our site. The data gathered allows us to refine our site's structure, navigation, and content. We receive anonymous statistics from third parties regarding visitor numbers and popular content. This data **is not linked to individual users**.

Analytics cookies help us:

*   Count unique visitors to our site.
*   Measure the duration of your page visits.
*   Analyze your navigation patterns across the site.
*   Identify areas where improvements can be made.
*   Enhance overall website performance.

**Key Analytics Cookies:**

*   **Boomerang:** Collects statistics on user behavior.
*   **Chartbeat (+ mab / video):** Provides insights into content and video engagement.
*   **CIM / Gemius:** Offers audience measurement data.
*   **CO2stats:** Tracks the website's carbon footprint.
*   **comScore Audience measurement:** Measures audience characteristics.
*   **Content Insights:** Displays data on editorial performance.
*   **Conviva:** Gathers analytics for video streaming.
*   **Crazy Egg:** Generates heatmaps and scrollmaps of user interaction.
*   **Formissimo:** Analyzes the submission of forms.
*   **Google Analytics:** Provides detailed analysis of website traffic.
*   **Google Tag Manager:** Assists in managing tracking tags.
*   **Hotjar:** Offers heatmaps and user feedback tools.
*   **Moat:** Measures ad viewability.
*   **Optimise A-B:** Supports A/B testing for site optimization.
*   **Snowplow Javascript Tracker:** Collects event data for analysis.
*   **Stats service:** Gathers general website statistics.
*   **UserZoom:** Aids in user experience research.
*   **Visual Measures:** Collects data on visual engagement.
*   **Visual Website Optimizer (VWO):** Enables A/B testing for site improvements.

For information on the data practices of third-party providers, please consult their respective privacy policies.

#### Personalization Cookies

Our goal is to deliver content that is highly relevant to your interests. These cookies help us tailor website content and advertisements to your specific preferences. Without them, you will still see ads, but they **will not be personalized**.

These cookies allow us to:

*   Suggest articles based on your reading history.
*   Recommend videos based on your viewing habits.
*   Propose topics based on your inferred interests.

**Key Personalization Cookies:**

*   **Froomle Recommendation:** Powers content suggestion algorithms.
*   **Gravity Recommandation:** Helps personalize content delivery.
*   **Trackuity iframes:** Manages advertisements within embedded content.

For details on third-party cookies, please refer to their privacy statements.

#### Advertising Cookies

A significant portion of our content is **provided free of charge, supported by advertising**. Some advertisements utilize cookies, which may be placed by us or by advertising networks. To personalize ads, we analyze your browsing activity to infer your interests, thereby displaying ads that are more likely to be relevant to you. For instance, a sports enthusiast will see different ads than someone interested in travel. Third parties may combine this data with your past interactions.

These cookies help us:

*   Record visits to infer user interests.
*   Share browsing data with other sites for advertising purposes.
*   Utilize third-party services for ad delivery.
*   Prevent the repeated display of advertisements.
*   Track ad clicks for billing purposes.
*   Monitor ad-driven sales for billing.

**Key Advertising Cookies:**

*   **Adhese:** Manages targeted advertising campaigns.
*   **Advertorials script:** Supports sponsored content.
*   **Facebook Pixel:** Enables retargeting through Facebook.
*   **Google Ad Words pixel:** Facilitates retargeting via Google Ads.
*   **Index Exchange:** Operates as an ad exchange platform.
*   **Krux:** Provides ad data management services.
*   **Ligatus:** Offers native advertising solutions.
*   **OnRecruit:** Tracks the performance of advertising campaigns.
*   **Sharethrough:** Distributes native advertisements.
*   **Shoppingminds:** Focuses on e-commerce advertising.
*   **Sticky Ads:** Delivers persistent ad formats.
*   **Teads:** Specializes in video advertising.

For information on third-party advertising cookies, please consult their privacy statements.

#### Social Media Cookies

You have the ability to share articles and videos directly through social media buttons. These buttons use social media cookies to recognize you when you share content.

These cookies enable:

*   Social networks to identify users for actions such as liking or sharing.
*   More relevant advertisements based on your social media activity.
*   Direct content sharing for users logged into their social media accounts.

For details regarding social media cookies, please review their respective privacy policies.

**Key Social Media Cookies:**

*   **Bluekai Social:** Assists in collecting social data.
*   **FB social plugins:** Enables sharing content via Facebook.
*   **Google+ Social sharing:** Supports sharing content through Google+.

Australian Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull has voiced concerns regarding European security, indicating a perceived weakening prior to the recent attacks in Belgium. When questioned about the possibility of Australia experiencing a similar terrorist event, Turnbull stated, "You cannot guarantee that there will be no terrorist incident."

He assured the Australian public that the nation's security apparatus, encompassing border management and internal safety protocols, is significantly more robust than that of Europe. Turnbull suggested that Europe's security challenges were connected to recent difficulties the continent has encountered.

Key takeaways from Turnbull's remarks include:

*   A decline in European security measures.
*   Australia's security framework is superior to Europe's.
*   The complete exclusion of a terrorist incident in Australia is not feasible.

In reaction, federal opposition leader Bill Shorten commented that it was premature for the Prime Minister to critique Belgium's response so soon after the tragic events. Shorten emphasized that the immediate focus should be on acknowledging the loss of innocent lives, with assessments to follow. Australia's current national terrorism threat level is classified as "probable."

Here's a revised account of Turkey's 2016 coup attempt, focusing on a direct timeline of events and employing more varied language for enhanced clarity and distinctiveness.

**Turkey's 2016 Coup Attempt: A Chronological Breakdown**

This timeline details the pivotal moments of the attempted coup in Turkey on Friday, July 15, 2016, based on Reuters reporting. All times are presented in Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).

**Early Hours: Unrest and Conflicting Declarations**

The initial hours of July 15, 2016, were marked by escalating unrest and a flurry of conflicting declarations. By 19:29 GMT, key Istanbul arteries, the Bosphorus and Fatih Sultan Mehmet Bridges, were obstructed, with Dogan News Agency footage showing traffic rerouted. Gunfire echoed in Ankara by 19:50 GMT, accompanied by the observation of military aircraft in the skies over both Ankara and Istanbul.

Prime Minister Binali Yildirim announced an ongoing coup attempt at 20:02 GMT, urging public calm and stating that a military faction sought to overthrow the government, with security forces mobilized. However, at 20:25 GMT, the Turkish military issued a statement via email and television, claiming control to preserve democratic order and international ties. Amidst this confusion, CNN Turk reported at 20:38 GMT that President Tayyip Erdogan was safe.

Further complicating the situation, Anadolu Agency reported at 20:47 GMT that the Turkish chief of military staff was among hostages at Ankara military headquarters. A presidential source stated at 20:49 GMT that the military's declaration lacked command authorization. Internet monitors noted restrictions on major social media platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube in Turkey by 20:57 GMT. A TRT correspondent told Reuters at 20:58 GMT that soldiers had entered the Turkish state broadcaster's Ankara premises. The head of the ruling AK Party's Istanbul branch reported at 21:02 GMT that soldiers had entered their building, demanding evacuation. By 21:05 GMT, the Turkish state broadcaster aired a military statement detailing plans for a new constitution, accusing the government of undermining democratic and secular principles, announcing a "peace council" for governance, and imposing martial law and a curfew.

**Mid-Evening: Escalation and Government Countermeasures**

As the evening progressed, the situation escalated, prompting government countermeasures. A presidential source confirmed at 21:18 GMT that the president and government remained in power. The Turkish Prime Minister tweeted at 21:22 GMT that all necessary measures, including fatalities, would be taken to quell the coup, mentioning sieges at undisclosed significant buildings and urging continued public composure.

President Tayyip Erdogan appealed to the public at 21:26 GMT to resist the coup by a minority military faction. Via a cellphone video link to CNN Turk, he vowed a "necessary response" and announced his imminent return to Ankara. Turkish state broadcaster TRT ceased transmissions at 21:35 GMT, later resuming from London. Eyewitnesses reported a military helicopter firing over Ankara at 21:51 GMT, with an explosion heard in the capital.

The Turkish justice minister identified elements within the army linked to U.S.-based cleric Fethullah Gulen as instigators at 22:03 GMT. Tanks surrounded the Turkish parliament and opened fire at 22:08 GMT, with gunfire also audible at Istanbul airport. Two distinct explosions struck the Turkish capital at 22:26 GMT. The commander of Turkey's First Army described the plotters as a minor faction at 22:37 GMT, assuring there was "nothing to worry about."

The Prime Minister denounced the coup as terrorism by gangs and illicit formations at 22:47 GMT, reaffirming government authority and urging citizens to take to the streets. The special forces commander declared treason by a group that would ultimately fail at 22:51 GMT, affirming the military's non-endorsement of the coup. Turkish broadcaster NTV reported at 22:59 GMT that a fighter jet downed a coup plotters' helicopter over Ankara. Anadolu Agency reported at 23:04 GMT that 17 police officers were killed at Ankara special forces headquarters, a claim unconfirmed by independent sources.

**Late Evening: International Response and Apparent Resolution**

The late evening saw international responses and an apparent resolution to the crisis. The White House stated at 23:05 GMT that President Obama and Secretary of State Kerry agreed all parties should support Turkey's democratically elected government, urging restraint and preventing bloodshed. A group associated with Fethullah Gulen vehemently denied involvement at 23:13 GMT, calling accusations "highly irresponsible," condemning military interference, and expressing concern for civilian safety.

U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry emphasized "absolute support for Turkey's democratically elected, civilian government and democratic institutions" at 23:20 GMT in a call with the Turkish foreign minister. Anadolu Agency reported at 23:39 GMT that a bomb struck the Turkish parliament in Ankara, with a Reuters witness corroborating an explosion in Istanbul. The Turkish Prime Minister declared the situation under control at 23:52 GMT, attributing events to the Gulen movement and announcing a no-fly zone over Ankara. A U.S.-based organization linked to Gulen had previously disavowed participation.

On **March 22, 2016**, Brussels, Belgium, was the target of **coordinated terrorist attacks**. Explosions at **Brussels Airport** and on a **metro train** near the European Union's headquarters plunged the city into **chaos and fear**. These tragic events resulted in at least **31 deaths** and numerous injuries.

At **Zaventem Airport**, the departure lounge was devastated by two explosions, filling the air with shattered glass and debris. Belgian Health Minister Maggie de Block reported **11 fatalities and 81 wounded** at the airport. A survivor described the second blast as **catastrophic**, likening the scene to a **"war zone."** An airport worker noted the explosions happened near the Swissport counters and a Starbucks, describing a disorienting sound and falling paper. Amateur video showed passengers fleeing the terminal. At least one **Kalashnikov rifle** was found in the departure lounge, though its direct use was unconfirmed.

Approximately an hour later, a third explosion occurred on a **rush-hour subway train** near the Maelbeek station, close to the EU's administrative center. This blast tragically killed **20 people and injured over 100**, according to Brussels Mayor Yvan Majeur. The event caused **panic everywhere**. Both the airport and the metro system were **completely shut down**.

These attacks led to **immediate and extensive security enhancements** across Europe. Airports intensified protocols, and flights to Brussels faced significant disruption, with over **200 diversions or cancellations**. Belgian Prime Minister Charles Michel declared Belgium's **terror alert at the highest level**.

The attacks took place amid **heightened security concerns** in Europe, with intelligence suggesting the **Islamic State group** was planning strikes. The recent arrest of a key suspect in the November Paris attacks amplified fears of a broader network. While no group immediately claimed responsibility, Prime Minister Michel stated there was **no immediate evidence linking Salah Abdeslam**, the Paris suspect, to the Brussels bombings. Abdeslam had reportedly informed authorities of his involvement in planning further attacks.

query: What sparked Amazon Studios to investigate allegations against Jeffrey Tambor?

documents.text: (UPDATED with Jill Soloway statement) EXCLUSIVE: Three weeks after Amazon Studios boss Roy Price resigned over sexual harassment claims, the company is conducting an investigation into allegations against Transparent star Jeffrey Tambor. An Amazon spokesperson confirmed to Deadline that the company has initiated an investigation. In a statement, Tambor himself calls the claims “baseless.” In its early stages, the probe primarily stems from allegations by Tambor’s former assistant Van Barnes of implied inappropriate behavior on the part of the Emmy, Golden Globe and SAG Awards winner. The investigation into the claims by the transgender Barnes, which she made online in a private Facebook post, began earlier this week, we hear. In the post, she claims that her former boss, whom she does not identify by name, had repeatedly propositioned her, made lewd comments, groped her and threatened to sue her if she spoke up. “I am aware that a former disgruntled assistant of mine has made a private post implying that I had acted in an improper manner toward her,” Tambor told Deadline today. “I adamantly and vehemently reject and deny any and all implication and allegation that I have ever engaged in any improper behavior toward this person or any other person I have ever worked with. I am appalled and distressed by this baseless allegation.” Amazon’s stance on such allegations is to investigate promptly and thoroughly, a source says. The investigation is in the process of speaking to members of the Transparent production and Tambor personally. The Jill Soloway-created series, Amazon’s flagship comedy, is currently not in production. “Anything that would diminish the level of respect, safety and inclusion so fundamental to our workplace is completely antithetical to our principles,” Soloway said in a statement today. “We are cooperating with the investigation into this matter.” As well as working for Tambor on Transparent, Barnes has also appeared on E! now-shuttered I Am Cait with Caitlyn Jenner and the Amazon documentary anthology miniseries This Is Me back in 2015. This investigation into Tambor comes as the Jeff Bezos-founded studio has seen a near exodus of executives in recent weeks, led by studio head Price, who was suspended and subsequently resigned after details became public about how he had lewdly propositioned The Man in the High Castle executive producer Isa Dick Hackett during San Diego Comic-Con in 2015. Kristina Cohen, who has appeared in various films and television series, alleges that she was raped in 2014. At the time of the alleged incident, she was visiting Westwick with her then-boyfriend, a producer who was acquainted with the actor. The boyfriend's name has not been disclosed.

The allegations, which emerged on Wednesday, state the incident took place when Haim was 13 and Sheen was 19. Following a wave of sexual assault allegations against former *Entourage* star Jeremy Piven, advertising executive Tiffany Bacon Scourby has come forward with her own account, detailing an alleged assault by the actor. She claims to have first met him in October 2003 during a work event in New York City. Scourby, now 39, described Piven, 52, as "very nice" when they met at a club. He mentioned an upcoming appearance on *Late Night with Conan O’Brien* and invited her to attend as his guest, providing his phone number.

Scourby stated that nothing untoward occurred that evening. The following morning, after her work meeting, she met Piven around 11 a.m. at the Trump International Hotel & Tower, where he was staying. She recalled wearing business attire, specifically a "white ribbed turtle neck and brown slacks," and sitting beside him on a couch. After a brief conversation, during which Piven mentioned waiting for his publicist to join them, Scourby alleges he "jumped on top of me." She attempted to resist, but he allegedly "forced me to the ground." Scourby claims Piven exposed himself and rubbed against her while she remained clothed. She states that during the approximately 15 minutes she was in his suite, he "held down my hands" and ejaculated onto her turtleneck.

Following the alleged assault, Scourby recounted, "I pushed him off of me and ran down the short hallway to get to the door." She then "rubbed my chest against the wall to get his [semen] off of my sweater." She immediately hailed a cab and "burst into tears," crying for the entire journey back to her hotel. From the cab, she called a close friend, who has since corroborated Scourby's account to PEOPLE. Upon returning to her hotel, she showered before catching a train back to Washington, D.C., continuing to cry throughout the journey.

In the 14 years since the alleged incident, Scourby married and became a mother. She stated she "never thought about saying anything" until she witnessed Piven's strong denial of Bellamar's accusations. This prompted her to believe, "It couldn’t have just happened to me and couldn’t have just happened to her."

In a statement to PEOPLE, Piven categorically denied Scourby's account, stating, "Let me be absolutely clear, this simply did not happen. I would never force myself on a woman, ever. I cannot speak as to why a person would create a story like this." A representative for Piven added that a polygraph test would "clearly proven this allegation to be entirely fabricated, a complete lie," and that the actor was "looking at legal options."

On Thursday, Piven issued a statement via his Twitter account, asserting that all claims against him are "completely fabricated." He began, "Let me begin by saying that the accusations against me are absolutely false and completely fabricated. I would never force myself on a woman. Period. I have offered to take a polygraph to support my innocence. I keep asking myself, ‘How does one prove something didn’t happen?’" Piven continued, expressing his inability to "speculate as to the motivations of these women." He conveyed compassion for victims of such acts but expressed perplexity regarding the "misdirection of anger with false accusations against me," hoping they do not detract from legitimate stories. The actor concluded by writing, "We seem to be entering dark times — allegations are being printed as facts and lives are being put in jeopardy without a hearing, due process or evidence. I hope we can give people the benefit of a doubt before we rush to judgment." He further stated, "Continuing to tear each other down and destroy careers based on mere allegations is not productive on any level. I hope we can use this moment to begin a constructive dialogue on these issues, which are real and need to be addressed."

### Portia de Rossi Recounts Disturbing Audition Experience with Steven Seagal

Portia de Rossi, an actress recognized for her work on "Arrested Development" and "Ally McBeal," has shared a disturbing account of alleged sexual misconduct involving action star Steven Seagal. Upon arriving at the hotel, Margulies found Seagal alone and recalled him displaying a firearm. She managed to leave the situation without harm.

These testimonies contribute to a broader pattern of sexual misconduct allegations against Steven Seagal, a trend that gained significant public attention following the Harvey Weinstein scandal. query: What steps are Congressional Republicans taking to dismantle the Affordable Care Act?

documents.text: (Sarah Parnass/The Washington Post) Congressional Republicans on Wednesday launched their long-promised effort to dismantle the Affordable Care Act, even as they acknowledged that they may need several months to develop a replacement along conservative lines. Signifying how enormous a priority the issue is for the incoming administration, Vice President-elect Mike Pence met privately to discuss it with House and Senate Republicans. He offered no details afterward about what a new health-care law might look like but vowed to unwind the existing one through a mixture of executive actions and legislation. Meanwhile, President Obama made a rare Capitol Hill appearance, meeting behind closed doors with Democrats from both chambers. He urged members of his party not to help the GOP devise a new health-care law. The dueling high-level visits, on the same day that the Senate opened debate on a budget resolution that would begin rolling back the law, highlighted the sharp political fault lines that surround the future of the government’s health policies. The president, who was accompanied by Senate Minority Leader Charles E. Schumer (D-N.Y.), House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) and Rep. Frederica S. Wilson (D-Fla.) as he entered the Capitol, took no questions from reporters before or after the nearly two-hour meeting. But participants said he told members of his party that they did not have to “rescue” Republicans and that they should “stay strong” as the GOP strives to replace the law. (Reuters) Pence told reporters that he and Trump would pursue a “two-track approach” to chip away at the ACA through executive powers and legislation. Trump is “working on a series of executive orders that will enable that orderly transition to take place,” Pence said, and is eyeing other policies that can be reversed. [With Obamacare, GOP faces the ‘Pottery Barn rule’: You break it, you own it ] While Pence did not identify other policy targets, Republicans from Western states urged him Wednesday to undo some of the public land protections Obama has created through the 1906 Antiquities Act. Other executive actions, including those providing new safeguards for LGBT Americans and curbing greenhouse gas emissions linked to climate change, also could come under fire. According to a lobbyist in touch with congressional aides on the ACA issue, the Trump transition team has been considering ways to strip down the health benefits that insurers must provide in plans that they sell to individuals and small businesses. This list of “essential health benefits” was envisioned by the law but was defined in a regulation written by the Department of Health and Human Services. As a result, the incoming administration could alter it without help from Congress. [Why Obamacare is unlikely to die a swift death] Trump took to Twitter on Wednesday morning to urge Republicans to “be careful in that the Dems own the failed Obamacare disaster.” In a dig at Schumer and his allies, Trump added: “Don’t let the Schumer clowns out of this web.” Less than half an hour after Trump’s social media messages, Schumer tweeted: “Republicans should stop clowning around with America’s health care. Don’t #MakeAmericaSickAgain.” Schumer and other Democrats echoed Obama, saying they did not feel any responsibility to craft a substitute health-care bill. “If you are repealing, show us what you’ll replace it with. Then we’ll look at what you have and see what you can do,” said Schumer, who met briefly with Pence on Wednesday. (Reuters) White House press secretary Josh Earnest said that Obama told Democrats that they are well positioned to defend the law, which has extended insurance to more than 20 million Americans. However, Obama also told lawmakers that he recognized that he had not succeeded in selling the law to the public. “There was an acknowledgment that so many features were so popular, but there was a failure to communicate that,” Sen. Jeff Merkley (D-Ore.) said. [Donald Trump, meet your toughest hurdle: The U.S. Senate] Pence, who was greeted by applause as he entered the House GOP meeting with Reince Priebus, Kelly Anne Conway and several other future White House aides, made it clear that Trump, starting on the first day of his presidency, intended to embark on an aggressive campaign to reverse executive actions made by Obama. “They didn’t enumerate a list, just that which came by the pen can die by the pen,” said Rep. Mark Sanford (R-S.C.). The first bill Republicans introduced in the new Senate that convened Tuesday was budget legislation with instructions for House and Senate committees to begin repealing the Affordable Care Act. The bare-bones spending outline requires members of four committees — Ways and Means and Energy and Commerce in the House; and Finance, as well as Health, Education, Labor and Pensions, in the Senate — to produce bills just seven days after Trump’s inauguration that each would save $1 billion over a decade by slashing elements of the heath-care law. [Democrats: Trump will make America sick again] House Republicans tasked with writing the repeal legislation said that no final decisions have been made on what it will include. Rep. Patrick J. Tiberi (R-Ohio) who chairs a House subcommittee that oversees health care, called the incoming administration’s Jan. 27 deadline “a challenging goal.” Among the unanswered questions is whether Republicans will immediately end health-care taxes, such as an additional 0.9 percent Medicare payroll tax and a 3.8 percent investment income tax, that were created to help pay for the Affordable Care Act. Some Republicans have insisted that the taxes should be eliminated immediately, even if lawmakers decide to delay the repeal of other parts of the law. Others worry that repealing the taxes will make it impossible to pay to keep Obamacare afloat while a replacement is finished. “We’re not sure yet,” Tiberi said. “There’s just not consensus on this.” Rep. Chris Collins (R-N.Y.), a member the Trump transition team, said in an interview that “we have six months to work out the replacement plan” for the sweeping health-care law. Senate rules allow budget resolutions to pass by a simple majority — a maneuver that guarantees that the chamber’s Democratic minority will not have enough votes for a filibuster to block repeal. Only changes related to taxes, spending or the long-term federal budget are eligible for the simple-majority treatment, however. Other parts of the law, such as the structure of the insurance marketplaces, probably would require a filibuster-proof margin of 60 votes in the Senate, a trickier task since the new Senate has 52 Republicans. (The Washington Post) Since its passage by Congress in the spring of 2010 — entirely with Democratic votes — the ACA has spurred the most significant changes to U.S. health policy since the creation of Medicare and Medicaid during the Great Society legislation of the 1960s. It also has faced sustained opposition from Republicans, leading to two Supreme Court cases and a lawsuit over cost-sharing subsidies that is now before the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit. The ACA is best known for having expanded insurance coverage, starting in 2014, in two ways: new marketplaces selling private health plans to Americans who do not have access to affordable health benefits through a job, and an expansion of Medicaid in about three-fifths of the states. The marketplaces got off to a shaky start because of computer dysfunction with HealthCare.gov, the federal enrollment website. [Trump just met with super-influential health-care executives you’ve probably never heard of] Three years later, the U.S. Census reported that the nation’s uninsured rate had declined to 9.1 percent, with most of a recent decrease coming from people who bought insurance on their own and more who had joined Medicaid. The president’s visit to the Hill was part of a broader, last-ditch effort by his administration to preserve a central element of his domestic legacy. On Wednesday morning, New York Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo (D) drew attention to the stakes for his state by issuing a county-by-county breakdown of the estimated 2.7 million New Yorkers who would lose coverage if the law were repealed. He pointed out that New York would lose $595 million this year in federal money that has helped the state expand its Medicaid program. HHS officials have been trying to use to their advantage the fact that the change in administrations will take place while the fourth year’s enrollment period for ACA health plans is underway. On Wednesday, HHS released HealthCare.gov enrollment figures showing that 8.8 million Americans chose or were automatically re-enrolled in ACA health plans between Nov. 1 and Dec. 31 in the 39 states that rely on the federal exchange website. That is about 200,000 more than at the same time a year ago, although the proportion of new customers was lower. The new drive to unwind the health-care law will take time. Senate leaders must allow Democrats to offer a nearly unlimited number of amendments before a final budget vote. Democrats plan to use the process, known as a “vote-a-rama,” to offer a long string of potentially toxic amendments that could make it difficult for Republicans to vote for the final legislation, Democratic leadership aides said. Mike DeBonis, Ed O’Keefe, Sean Sullivan and David Weigel contributed to this report. Read more at PowerPost

Democrats on Capitol Hill are rallying under the slogan "Make America Sick Again" as they fight to preserve Obamacare. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and his progressive colleagues are deeply concerned about a potential national healthcare catastrophe should the Affordable Care Act be repealed without a viable replacement. Republicans, however, are eager to fast-track a repeal bill as a top priority for the 115th Congress. The challenge of devising a replacement policy that ensures millions of Americans remain insured is significant, leading some to speculate about a "repeal in name only" scenario.

### What is Obamacare?

The Affordable Care Act (ACA), commonly known as Obamacare, is a federal health insurance exchange. It established marketplaces where individuals without health insurance could purchase government-regulated health plans offering essential benefits. These marketplaces also allowed uninsured citizens to determine their eligibility for government subsidies.

### Key Features and Mandates

Under the ACA, Americans are required to obtain health insurance or face a penalty, unless they qualify for an exemption. For instance, the annual penalty for uninsured Americans in 2016 was $695 per adult. The ACA, signed into law by President Obama in 2010, aimed to expand health insurance coverage by broadening Medicaid eligibility and increasing its funding to assist low-income individuals. Those already satisfied with their private, Obamacare-compliant insurance plans had the option to retain them, renew them, or switch to plans available through the marketplace.

### Coverage Expansion

As of February 2016, approximately 20 million individuals had gained health insurance through various ACA provisions, including marketplace enrollment, Medicaid expansion, and the ability for young adults to remain on their parents' plans. Despite this expansion, the Kaiser Family Foundation estimated that 28.5 million non-elderly Americans were still uninsured at the end of 2015.

### Republican Opposition

A primary concern for Republicans is the cost associated with the ACA. Conservatives who oppose tax increases object to the Medicaid expansion and the subsidies that help lower-income individuals afford insurance, viewing it as a burden on higher-income taxpayers. Some younger adults expressed frustration at being compelled to purchase healthcare they felt they could not afford.

Furthermore, proponents of free-market principles argue that the ACA's standardized regulations stifle medical innovation. Critics have also blamed Obamacare for hindering job growth by imposing costs on larger businesses. The sheer complexity of the legislation, spanning 2,700 pages, made it an easy target for criticism. The rollout of the Healthcare.gov website in October 2013 was plagued with technical issues, and in late October 2016, an announcement of an average 25% premium increase across 39 states served by the federal marketplace further fueled public dissatisfaction.

### The Path to Repeal and Replace

With Republicans controlling both the White House and Congress, the repeal of Obamacare appears feasible. Previous attempts to declare the ACA unconstitutional failed in 2015, with the Supreme Court upholding it in a 6-3 decision. Since 2010, Republicans have made over 60 unsuccessful attempts to repeal the law. An effort to dismantle significant portions of the ACA reached President Obama's desk last year but was vetoed.

The current political landscape, with President-elect Donald Trump in office, presents a different scenario. However, the process of replacing the ACA could prove complicated. Following his election, Trump indicated a willingness to preserve key ACA provisions, specifically mentioning the allowance for young adults up to age 26 to remain on their parents' insurance and the prohibition against insurers denying coverage for pre-existing conditions.

### Proposed Alternatives

While a definitive replacement plan remains elusive, President-elect Trump has nominated Tom Price, a Georgia congressman, to lead the Department of Health and Human Services. Price has previously proposed the "Empowering Patients First Act," which would offer tax credits to individuals without insurance to help them purchase private coverage. These credits would be age-based rather than income-based, a feature Democrats argue could disadvantage lower-income Americans. Price's proposal would also eliminate the Medicaid expansion, a provision that has provided coverage to approximately 14 million low-income Americans under Obamacare.

### Transition Timeline

A swift repeal of the ACA could leave an estimated 22 million Americans uninsured, according to the Congressional Budget Office, a scenario that would likely be politically detrimental. Therefore, any transition to a new healthcare law is expected to be gradual, spanning two to four years. Alternatively, Republicans might adopt a "repeal and delay" strategy, postponing the full repeal until a more satisfactory healthcare law option is developed.

Donald Trump's approach to the Affordable Care Act (ACA), often called Obamacare, shows how campaign promises can differ from governing realities. During his 2016 presidential run, Trump vowed to "repeal and replace Obamacare." However, as his inauguration neared, he adopted a more measured tone, advising Republican leaders to proceed cautiously. This shift is consistent with his history of changing policy views; he reportedly took 15 new stances on nine issues after the election, following 141 distinct positions on 23 major topics during his campaign. His evolving thoughts on the ACA, noted eight times since he announced his candidacy, highlight the challenges of turning campaign pledges into law.

Early in his campaign, Trump acknowledged the effectiveness of single-payer healthcare systems, referencing Canada and Scotland. He suggested a private U.S. system but stressed removing state-based barriers to encourage competition and ensure care for those who couldn't afford it otherwise. He also committed to universal coverage, stating, "I am going to take care of everybody."

A notable shift occurred in February 2016 when Trump expressed support for the ACA's individual mandate, explaining, "I don't want people dying on the streets." This suggested an understanding of its role in protecting individuals with pre-existing conditions. However, during a debate, his replacement strategy was vague, offering only general ideas about "many different plans" and "competition."

Later, his published healthcare plan leaned towards traditional Republican ideas. The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB) estimated this plan could lead to millions losing insurance and cost billions. It proposed unspecified block grants to states for Medicaid, leaving questions about coverage for vulnerable populations.

By November 2016, Trump indicated openness to amending the ACA rather than a full repeal, specifically mentioning keeping protections for pre-existing conditions and allowing young adults on parents' plans. He stated, "Either Obamacare will be amended, or repealed and replaced."

Leading up to his inauguration, Vice President-elect Mike Pence signaled that ACA repeal would be an early priority. Yet, the details of a replacement plan remained unclear. Trump himself then advised Republican lawmakers to "be careful" when dismantling the ACA, suggesting it might collapse on its own and urging them to avoid blame.

On January 3, 2017, a political event on Capitol Hill brought the debate over the Affordable Care Act (ACA) into sharp focus. Following a mock swearing-in ceremony for Republican Representative Marsha Blackburn of Tennessee, administered by House Speaker Paul Ryan, Blackburn initiated an online exchange that underscored the deep divisions surrounding the landmark health-care law.

A vocal critic of the ACA, also known as Obamacare, Representative Blackburn used Twitter to poll her followers on the prospect of repealing the legislation. Her poll directly asked: "Do you support the repeal of Obamacare? RT if you do, and share what you want to see as the replacement." This initiative aimed to gauge public sentiment and build support for repeal.

However, the poll quickly drew significant opposition, with far more respondents indicating they favored keeping the ACA than repealing it. This unexpected result posed a considerable obstacle to Blackburn's efforts to generate momentum for repeal. Reports from The Hill.com suggested that actions from the White House may have influenced the poll's reception.

On the same day the poll was launched, White House Deputy Press Secretary Eric Schultz retweeted Blackburn's poll to his substantial following. This occurred as President Barack Obama, Schultz's superior, was actively working to defend the ACA, a law he viewed as his signature legislative achievement. President Obama visited Capitol Hill that Wednesday to rally Democratic support against Republican attempts to dismantle and replace the law.

Meanwhile, President-elect Donald Trump, who had campaigned on repealing the ACA, issued a note of caution to his fellow Republicans. In a series of tweets, Trump advised prudence regarding ACA repeal and replace efforts. He suggested that Democrats were responsible for the law's perceived flaws and warned that Republicans could face political consequences if repeal led to more uninsured individuals or higher insurance costs.

**Fact Check: Examining Claims About the Affordable Care Act (ACA)**

The Affordable Care Act (ACA), commonly known as Obamacare, remains a focal point in healthcare policy debates, with strong opinions on its impact. Supporters, predominantly Democrats, champion its role in expanding access and affordability. Conversely, Republicans generally advocate for its repeal, citing perceived negative consequences. This analysis delves into key arguments from both perspectives regarding the ACA's effects, drawing on available data and expert analyses.

**Understanding ACA Premium Dynamics**

A frequently cited claim is that the ACA has led to "massive premium increases." While some individuals purchasing insurance through ACA marketplaces have indeed experienced significant price hikes, the reality is more nuanced. The Kaiser Family Foundation (KFF) indicates that the average benchmark plan on these marketplaces saw a nationwide increase of approximately 25%. However, this national average masks considerable state-level variations. For instance, Arizona witnessed an average premium surge of 116%, a stark contrast to Indiana and Massachusetts, where premiums actually declined. Crucially, many marketplace enrollees benefit from government subsidies that substantially lower their out-of-pocket expenses. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) estimates that, even without these subsidies, marketplace plans were, on average, about 10% less expensive than typical employer-sponsored plans. The perception of higher costs can also be influenced by how employer plans are subsidized by companies, making them appear less costly to employees than their true value.

**Assessing Insurance Choices Under the ACA**

Another common assertion is that under Obamacare, individuals are "stuck with one option" for health insurance. While there has been a reduction in marketplace competition, this doesn't represent the experience of the majority of consumers. The number of insurers participating in ACA marketplaces has decreased, leading to fewer choices for some. Data from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) reveals that this year, one in five marketplace customers had access to only a single insurer, a notable increase from just 2% in 2016. Nevertheless, nearly 60% of customers still have a choice of three or more insurers. This decline in competition, which can contribute to price increases, is more prevalent in rural areas and certain Southern states. Insurers have cited various challenges, such as lower-than-expected enrollment of younger, healthier individuals, as contributing factors. Proposed solutions to bolster competition have included more generous subsidies or stronger enrollment penalties. Former President Obama also explored the concept of a public insurance option to supplement existing private plans.

**Impact on the Healthcare System and Coverage**

Claims that "the health care system has been ruined, dismantled under Obamacare" are not substantiated by the data. Healthcare costs were already on an upward trajectory before the ACA's enactment. The majority of Americans under 65 continue to obtain health insurance through their employers, although this percentage has gradually diminished. While the costs of employer-sponsored coverage have risen since the ACA's passage, the annual rate of increase was considerably higher in the decade preceding the law. Some of the savings from slower premium growth have been offset by increases in deductibles, as noted by the American Medical Association (AMA).

Democrats contend that repealing the ACA would yield far more detrimental outcomes. Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer has stated, "Instead of working to further ensure affordable care for all Americans, [Republicans] seek to rip health care away from millions of Americans, creating chaos in our entire economy." He has also voiced concerns that repealing Obamacare would severely impact the financial stability of rural hospitals.

The assertion that ACA repeal would "rip health care away from millions" holds true if protections or replacements are not effectively implemented. The ACA has demonstrably extended health coverage to approximately 20 million Americans through subsidized individual policies, the expansion of Medicaid, and by allowing young adults to remain on their parents' insurance plans until age 26. The uninsured rate has consequently fallen to a historic low of around 10%, according to the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS). This figure would be even lower if all 19 states that have not expanded Medicaid had done so. Repealing the ACA without a viable substitute could jeopardize coverage for a significant portion of the population. Millions more purchasing insurance on marketplaces could lose coverage if the market becomes unstable. Estimates from organizations like the Urban Institute suggest that up to 30 million people could lose health coverage, effectively doubling the uninsured rate. Republicans have pledged an "orderly transition" with a replacement plan, and the effective date of repeal could be delayed. However, insurers may exhibit hesitancy to participate if the individual market is perceived to be in a state of phase-out, as reported by industry analysts.

**Addressing Concerns for Rural Hospitals**

The concern that rural hospitals could face significant challenges if the ACA is repealed outright is a valid one, and it is intrinsically linked to the ACA's original legislative design. The potential impact on these facilities stems from a deliberate trade-off made during the ACA's enactment. The federal government reduced payments to hospitals for treating Medicare patients and the uninsured, operating under the assumption that increased insurance coverage would offset these reductions. Hospitals express concern that if repeal leads to a decrease in insurance coverage without a restoration of these payment structures, they could experience a substantial rise in uncompensated care. Both the American Hospital Association (AHA) and the Federation of American Hospitals have formally urged lawmakers to either preserve existing insurance coverage levels or reinstate the prior hospital payment structures, as documented in their public statements.

Republicans are utilizing budget reconciliation, a streamlined legislative procedure, to repeal core components of the Affordable Care Act. This process, though sometimes perceived as complex, has a proven track record of enabling major national laws to be enacted over the past 35 years. The Congressional Research Service, a non-partisan research group for Congress, has described reconciliation as "likely the most powerful budget enforcement tool available to Congress for a significant part of the budget." This guide offers a basic overview of how it works.

**Understanding Budget Reconciliation**

Budget reconciliation provides Congress with a way to speed up legislative action on changes to tax codes or government spending. This is especially relevant for programs like Medicare and Medicaid. While originally intended to reduce federal budget shortfalls, the process has also been used for tax cuts and to create new initiatives that can, in turn, raise spending and increase deficits. A key benefit in the Senate is that a reconciliation bill usually needs only a simple majority to pass. This is a significant difference from the 60-vote requirement often needed for other bills to move past debate limits and reach a final vote.

## Obamacare Marketplace Sees Strong Enrollment for 2017 Coverage

As of last Saturday, nearly 8.8 million individuals have enrolled in health insurance plans offered through the federal Obamacare marketplace for the 2017 coverage year. This figure represents an increase compared to the approximately 8.6 million plan selections recorded on HealthCare.gov during the same period for the previous open-enrollment season.

### HealthCare.gov Reach and State-Run Marketplaces

HealthCare.gov facilitates enrollment for consumers in 39 states. The remaining states operate their own state-run marketplaces to serve their residents.

### Secretary Burwell Highlights Demand for Coverage

U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Secretary Sylvia Burwell emphasized the significance of these numbers, stating, "With 8.8 million Americans signed up for coverage through HealthCare.gov, more than last year at this time, it is clear that Americans want and need this vital coverage."

### Deadline for Uninsured Individuals

Secretary Burwell also reminded those who remain uninsured that the deadline to sign up for coverage starting February 1st is January 15th.

### Breakdown of Enrollment

Of the total plan selections, approximately 6.55 million were made by returning customers. The remaining 2.2 million enrollees were new to the HealthCare.gov marketplace.

query: What is the significance of Florida in the context of the U.S. presidential election?

documents.text: Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Donald Trump follows a time-honoured electoral tradition in Tampa Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump have been campaigning in Florida, a key battleground state that could tip Tuesday's US presidential election. Florida is the largest swing state and is seen as a must-win for Mr Trump. The Republican candidate later flew to North Carolina and will then head west to Nevada. Opinion polls in recent days have suggested Mr Trump is gaining support but he still remains behind Mrs Clinton in most surveys. In Florida, the contest appears to be tight. Real Clear Politics' poll average puts the Democratic candidate ahead, but poll analysis website FiveThirtyEight says Mr Trump has a 52.6% chance of winning the state. US President Barack Obama won Florida in 2012 by a margin of just 0.9% over Republican Mitt Romney. Candidates need 270 electoral college votes to win the presidency. Florida is worth 29. Some 37 million early voters have already cast their ballots. Reports suggest many more Latino voters are turning out early in key states including Florida, Arizona and Nevada compared to past elections. Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Beyonce and Jay Z turn out for Hillary Clinton Florida has a significant Latino population, including many Cubans. Mr Trump told a rally in Tampa: "I say to the Hispanic community living in the inner city, to the African-American community, I say: what the hell do you have to lose?" He said Cubans supported him. He said he would provide jobs and solve crime for inner-city communities. Mrs Clinton courted voters in a Haitian-American district of Miami before holding a rally in Pembroke Pines. Her short address there ended in a downpour of rain. She will later make an appearance in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, alongside pop star Katy Perry. Florida's Latino vote Image copyright Reuters Image caption Some Latinos support Trump, despite his harsh rhetoric targeting immigrants Hispanics make up 16% of registered voters in Florida this election, the Associated Press reports, up from 13.5% in 2012. American Hispanics mostly vote Democrat but Florida's large Cuban population - which make up 31% of the state's Hispanic vote - have traditionally voted Republican. Donald Trump recently began to speak out against the US lifting its economic embargo on Cuba in a play for older voters, analysts say. But they also point out that as more younger Cubans have settled in Miami, support for warming US-Cuba relations - which Mrs Clinton backs - is growing among the community. Polls now suggest that more Miami Cubans are against the embargo than for it. Mr Trump's divisive and insulting comments about immigrants from Mexico and elsewhere have also angered many Latinos. Both candidates held rallies in Ohio and Pennsylvania on Friday. In Cleveland, Mrs Clinton ended the day's campaigning at a concert, where she was joined by the singer Beyonce and her husband, rapper Jay Z. The Clinton campaign is putting on several events with high-profile figures from the entertainment world as it tries to energise young and minority voters. Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Voters describe what's the worst that could happen if the other side wins. Rocker Jon Bon Jovi will later appear with Democratic vice-presidential candidate Tim Kaine in St Petersburg, Florida. On Friday night, Mr Trump told supporters in Hershey, Pennsylvania, that he "didn't have to bring J-Lo or Jay Z" to draw crowds. "I am here all by myself. Just me. No guitar, no piano, no nothing," he said. In other campaign developments: Documents alleging to show that Mr Trump's wife, Melania, worked illegally as a model in the US for a brief period two decades ago before she obtained the legal right to work have been obtained by the Associated Press. The National Enquirer, a US tabloid, paid $150,000 (£120,000) to a former Playboy model for exclusive rights to the story of an alleged affair she had with Donald Trump from 2006-2007, the Wall Street Journal reports. But the Enquirer, which supports Mr Trump, then killed the story, it says. The National Enquirer has denied paying to suppress the story and the Trump campaign says the alleged affair did not occur. Democratic campaign chief John Podesta said Mr Trump should ask the governor of New Jersey, Chris Christie, to step down as the head of his transition team after two of his aides were found guilty of orchestrating traffic jams in New Jersey as an act of political retribution against a local Democratic mayor. Mr Christie has denied any involvement. Who is ahead in the polls? See full poll tracker Mrs Clinton has faced a torrent of negative headlines after the FBI said last week it was looking into emails that may be connected to her use of a private email server while she was secretary of state. The Clinton camp have questioned the timing of the announcement. Two senior Democrats have now called for an investigation into the role of former New York mayor Rudy Giuliani, a Trump surrogate, after he appeared to suggest he knew about the inquiry before it was announced. Separately, US authorities have said they are assessing the credibility of information on a possible al-Qaeda extremist attack before election day. New York City, Texas and Virginia were said to be possible targets but a police spokesman said the information "lacks specificity". Officials say they regularly assess all possible threats before major events. Predict the president Who will win? Play our game to make your call

**Pro-Trump Websites Operated from a Macedonian Town: A Digital Gold Rush Fueled by Misinformation**

In a surprising revelation, BuzzFeed News uncovered over 100 pro-Trump websites being managed from a single town in the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia. These sites, often featuring sensationalist headlines and fabricated stories, have become a significant source of misinformation, particularly for American conservatives and Trump supporters.

One such site, WorldPoliticus.com, published a baseless story, citing unnamed FBI sources, claiming Hillary Clinton would be indicted in 2017 for her email scandal. This fabricated news generated over 140,000 shares, reactions, and comments on Facebook, demonstrating the power of sensationalism to drive engagement.

Meanwhile, thousands of miles away in Veles, a town of 45,000 in Macedonia, a digital gold rush was underway. Locals launched at least 140 US politics websites with American-sounding domain names like TrumpVision365.com and USADailyPolitics.com. These sites primarily published aggressively pro-Trump content.

The young Macedonians behind these websites admitted their motivation was purely financial. They recognized that a US Facebook user is worth significantly more to advertisers than a user outside the US. The fraction-of-a-penny-per-click revenue from US display advertising, a declining market for American publishers, proved highly lucrative in Veles.

These individuals learned that the most effective way to generate traffic on Facebook was to publish sensationalist and often false content that appealed to Trump supporters. As a result, this Macedonian hub of pro-Trump sites has played a substantial role in propagating the kind of false and misleading content identified in recent analyses of hyperpartisan Facebook pages.

These operations highlight the economic incentives behind producing misinformation specifically for lucrative advertising markets, particularly on platforms like Facebook and through online advertising networks such as Google AdSense. A university student from Veles, who wished to remain anonymous, explained the rationale: "**Yes, the info in the blogs is bad, false, and misleading but the rationale is that 'if it gets the people to click on it and engage, then use it.'**"

BuzzFeed News identified over 100 active US politics websites run from Veles, with some boasting Facebook pages with hundreds of thousands of followers. Another 40 domains registered in Veles are no longer active.

The Macedonians interviewed stated their primary reason for launching these sites was financial. "**I started the site for an easy way to make money,**" shared a 17-year-old who runs a site with four others. "**In Macedonia the economy is very weak and teenagers are not allowed to work, so we need to find creative ways to make some money.**" He added that the revenue from a small site is enough to afford many things, unlike in his home country.

Most of the content on these sites is aggregated or plagiarized from fringe and right-wing US websites. The Macedonians would find a story, create a sensationalized headline, post it, and then share it on Facebook to drive traffic and earn ad revenue. While some experimented with left-leaning content, Trump-related stories consistently performed better on Facebook. "**People in America prefer to read news about Trump,**" stated a 16-year-old operating BVANews.com.

BuzzFeed News' research also found that the most successful posts from these Macedonian sites were overwhelmingly false or misleading. For instance, a highly successful post from ConservativeState.com, claiming Hillary Clinton praised Donald Trump in 2013, garnered an astounding 480,000 Facebook interactions. This story was sourced from TheRightists.com, a site that admits to publishing both real and fake content. While Clinton did express a desire for more successful business people in politics in a private speech, she did not mention Donald Trump, making the headline quote false.

Four out of the five most successful posts identified by BuzzFeed News from these Macedonian sites were false. These included claims that the Pope endorsed Trump and that Mike Pence called Michelle Obama the "most vulgar first lady we've ever had." These four posts alone generated over 1 million shares, reactions, and comments on Facebook, leading to significant traffic and ad revenue for the site owners, while misinforming countless readers.

The Macedonians interviewed noted that the market has become crowded, making it harder to earn money. Those who started their sites in early 2016 are seeing the most significant financial returns. One university student mentioned a friend earning "$5,000 per month, or even $3,000 per day" during viral hits. The 16-year-old running BVANews.com, along with a partner, also operates health websites and stated their politics site averages 1 million page views per month.

The 17-year-old and his partners were still awaiting Google AdSense approval for their site, earning only about 800 views daily with no revenue. The university student, who launched his site in August, stopped updating it to focus on a more successful health and well-being site, estimating "thousands" of such sites operate out of Veles. He explained, "**I stopped because I didn't really enjoy doing it and we didn't actually make any money from it since there are so many people posting already. The people who started early are the ones reaping the rewards.**"
