This cognitive dissonance is not entirely unexpected from Moss, who, despite appearing as a capable and dedicated individual, has consistently declined to discuss Scientology, a religion in which she was raised and reportedly remains a member. Reporter Tony Ortega has documented Moss's extensive involvement, including a course in 1999 that placed her significantly along Scientology's "bridge" of spiritual progression. Moss has cited privacy as the reason for her reticence, stating in a *Guardian* interview, "It is weird for me to be put in the position where I am like, ‘No, I can’t. I don’t really want to talk about this.’ You feel kind of like, I am a nice person who likes to talk about stuff. I also get the curiosity. I get the fascination."

Her stance was consistent in a 2014 interview with Willa Paskin at Vulture, where she implied that critics of Scientology often lack understanding. "I’m not going to talk about it anymore," she stated firmly. "I said what it meant to me, and anyone can go and look at that if they want to know what I feel. But now it’s private, off limits." She has previously described the church as personally beneficial, not anti-gay, and "grossly misunderstood by the media." Moss reportedly avoids discussing Scientology even with close friends, appearing comfortable with the discomfort it generates in others. "I would feel the same way, honestly," she remarked. "I think if there was something that I didn’t know and didn’t understand, I would probably feel as opinionated. You know how you’re opinionated about when someone breaks up? Celebrities break up and you just feel like you know what happened?"

While personal privacy is a valid concern, it becomes problematic when juxtaposed with the disturbing allegations detailed in the book *Going Clear*, the A&E series by Leah Remini, and numerous accounts from former Scientologists. These testimonies describe patterns of coercion, control, and physical abuse attributed to church leader David Miscavige, allegations that Scientology representatives deny. The prolonged absence of Miscavige's wife, Shelly, from public view has also raised concerns. Reports from ex-members describe severe disciplinary measures at a facility known as "The Hole," characterized by confinement and humiliation, though Scientology disputes these accounts as exaggerated.

Furthermore, Scientology's historical views on women, as articulated by L. Ron Hubbard in the 1950s, relegated women to domestic roles. While these passages have been removed from some newer editions, former members detail systemic abuses, including alleged forced abortions within the Sea Org, as reported by the *Tampa Bay Times*. Danny Masterson, a prominent Scientologist, is currently under investigation for sexual assault, with accusers alleging pressure from the church to remain silent.

It is understandable that Elisabeth Moss may wish to maintain privacy regarding her religious beliefs and any alleged improprieties within the church. However, this reticence, coupled with the cast's collective avoidance of discussing the show's political and feminist dimensions, appears less like an effort to achieve universality and more like an anxious attempt at sanitization. Hulu's promotional efforts, including a package sent to female reporters containing a signed novel, a Handmaid's sweatshirt, and an invitation to a Facebook group called #Maidez, further complicate this narrative. Moss's accompanying letter, referencing the eradication of rights for "women, LGBTQ people and those of diverse faiths" by a "newly formed theocratic dictatorship," offers a remarkably pointed, albeit uncharacteristic, description of the show's relevance. She describes it as "a story as relevant now as the day it was first written," yet invites discussion within a framework of "positive discussion among solution-oriented people who believe in the power of sharing ideas and personal connections," a call to action conspicuously devoid of political specificity.

The motivation behind such a carefully curated apolitical stance remains unclear. The impulse to distance the narrative from its potent feminist implications is baffling, particularly for those who deeply appreciate the novel's message. Ultimately, the cast's apparent fear of engaging with the show's inherent political and feminist dimensions raises questions about their motivations and perceived audience. Pretending that *The Handmaid's Tale* is merely a fictional narrative, detached from the pressing realities it reflects, offers no genuine protection in our current socio-political landscape.

The recent television adaptation of Margaret Atwood's "The Handmaid's Tale" has ignited a fervent discussion about its perceived relevance to today's political landscape. While many hail its message as "unexpectedly timely," a closer examination reveals this claim to be largely unfounded. Though the novel offers a potent, albeit fictional, depiction of a dystopian future, its direct parallels to our current era warrant critical scrutiny.

My own initial encounter with "The Handmaid's Tale" involved deep dives into its themes and even attempts to emulate Atwood's distinctive prose. Two decades have passed since I last engaged with the book, my copy now a relic of a bygone era, much like my velvet chokers and Suzanne Vega CDs. The current wave of popularity, propelled by the television series, compels a re-reading and a critical reassessment of its supposed timeliness.

The assertion that the television adaptation of "The Handmaid's Tale" is "unexpectedly timely" in the current political climate is, frankly, unsubstantiated. The future that might unfold under present political shifts bears little resemblance to Atwood's Gilead. A re-reading confirms my initial assessment: the premise of the book and show serves as a stark warning. However, comparing it to modern-day Pyongyang or claiming it mirrors Huxley's "Brave New World" misses the mark.

My critique is not directed at the novel's political themes or its realism. Expecting strict plausibility from dystopian fiction is unrealistic. Among dystopian works, only Orwell's "1984" approaches a sense of verisimilitude, largely due to Orwell's direct observation of totalitarian regimes. Fictional dystopias often function as the negative image of propaganda from actual totalitarian states, showcasing masses in uniform attire and precise formations.

While regimes like North Korea and Nazi Germany did feature such mass rallies, individuals typically returned to ordinary lives, albeit altered by fear and scarcity, not fundamentally transformed into something inhuman. Atwood herself has stated that all details in her book are based on real-world occurrences or historical accounts. The concept of "handmaids," women compelled to bear children for elite men in a theocracy, is rooted in the biblical story of Rachel and Jacob.

Despite these historical referents, the overall effect of "The Handmaid's Tale" is unrealistic. Atwood has collaged details from numerous oppressive regimes, creating a composite that strains credulity. A theocracy that embraces extramarital sex, infanticide, and Tibetan prayer wheels, for instance, presents a dramatic, yet ultimately implausible, scenario. This deviates from what Mary McCarthy identified as the "essential element of a cautionary tale": recognition, a shock of surprised recognition that prompts a genuine warning.

Real-world dystopian regimes, while sharing common features, are not interchangeable. They are remarkably specific and culturally embedded. Even as they proclaimed a new world order, individuals within these societies continued to marry, form families, and maintain varying degrees of religious observance. Nationalist sentiments often resurfaced, even within ostensibly internationalist ideologies.

Consider the Taliban; while a real entity, it could not gain traction in America due to a lack of popular support and a historical absence of practices like widespread burqa-wearing. Similarly, a movement gaining power along the lines Atwood outlines in the United States is highly improbable. While Atwood is praised for detailing the potential emergence of her fictional state, the mechanism, as written, lacks coherence.

The inciting incident, a thinly veiled Reichstag fire, is historically inaccurate in its depiction of societal transformation. A decade after the Reichstag fire, German society remained largely unchanged from its 1925 state. While not excusing Nazi atrocities, Hitler's regime did not instantaneously overhaul the economy, eradicate all competing religions, or completely reorganize society within a few years. Much of the existing economy and culture remained intact.

Atwood's narrative requires rapid and sweeping societal changes to facilitate her plot, specifically the need for a handmaid to recall the pre-Gilead world while remaining fertile. This structural necessity leads to an unrealistic depiction of societal transformation. The novel is not an accurate predictor of how current political shifts will transform America. The United States lacks a unified theocratic tradition comparable to the early Massachusetts Bay Colony, and contemporary descendants of Puritans are more likely to be found advocating for environmental causes than establishing a theocracy.

The closest modern parallel, the dominance of the Latter-day Saints in Utah, bears little resemblance to Gilead and lacks the potential to unite the nation's fractured religious traditionalists, many of whom do not even recognize Mormons as Christian. Furthermore, even if such a movement were to coalesce, current political figures are unlikely leaders; the most religious states were precisely those where opposition was greatest.

These points are not necessarily flaws within the novel itself. Dystopian fiction's purpose is to highlight the conflict between the individual and society through stark contrast, not to achieve photorealistic accuracy. However, while one can forgive a speculative novelist for taking liberties with reality, the same leniency cannot be extended to critics who claim current events have rendered the story more relevant.

These critics overlook the inability to enact even more modest campaign promises, such as drastically restricting immigration or reshaping international alliances. There is a lack of control over Congress and the courts, and no command of the mass movements that propelled other dystopian governments to power. Even a mass movement is not a guaranteed path to such a regime.

Historical examples like Stalin's Russia, Hitler's Germany, Mao's China, the Khmer Rouge, and the Taliban all emerged from catastrophic circumstances, such as colonialism or war, and exploited power vacuums created by failing institutions. While the current political climate is concerning, "congressional gridlock" and "declining wages for unskilled men" are not comparable to the disasters that facilitated the rise of history's most notorious tyrants.

Moreover, societal trends are moving in the opposite direction. Women are gaining economic power, and the nation is becoming less religious. "The Handmaid's Tale" appears increasingly implausible as a future scenario, despite feminist insistence on a slippery slope from the overturning of Roe v. Wade to state-sanctioned breeding programs.

The question of whether the implausibility of this misogynistic theocracy's rise, or the distinct nature of any dark future that might presage, truly matters, is significant. While enjoying fictional implausibilities is harmless, hysterically declaring them reality is problematic. This risks a dangerous confusion, obscuring the real dystopias that may be emerging and rendering our warnings unheard by a world fatigued by alarmism.

**Key points regarding "The Handmaid's Tale" and its relevance:**

*   **Perceived Timeliness:** The novel's popularity has surged due to a new TV adaptation, with many finding its message "unexpectedly timely."
*   **Questionable Direct Relevance:** The author argues that the novel's dystopian vision, Gilead, does not directly mirror the current political climate or potential futures.
*   **Historical Roots vs. Fictional Composite:** While Atwood bases details on real-world events and biblical stories, the novel creates an unrealistic composite of oppressive regimes.
*   **Implausibility of Rise:** The mechanism for Gilead's rise, particularly the rapid societal transformation, is presented as historically inaccurate and lacking coherence.
*   **Societal Trends:** Current societal trends, such as increasing female economic power and declining religiosity, make the novel's scenario appear less plausible.
*   **Critique of Critics:** The author criticizes those who claim the novel is more relevant now, arguing they overlook the limitations of current political power and the vastly different circumstances that historically led to totalitarian regimes.
*   **Danger of Alarmism:** Hysterically declaring fictional dystopias as reality risks obscuring genuine emerging threats and leading to alarm fatigue.

Hulu's adaptation of *The Handmaid's Tale* has ignited a national conversation about the term "feminist." While some cast members, like Samira Wiley and Ann Dowd, readily label the series as feminist, others, including lead Elizabeth Moss, express reservations, preferring to frame it as a universal human story. This hesitation likely stems from a concern that the "feminist" label might alienate potential viewers or negatively impact the show's commercial appeal.

The apprehension surrounding the word "feminist" is understandable, particularly if it's perceived as solely tied to specific political ideologies that have recently faced public scrutiny. In marketing, associating with perceived "losers" can be a significant risk when targeting a broad audience. While movements like the Women's March and widespread support for reproductive rights are important, there's a pragmatic tendency to align with popular sentiments. This can lead to a dilution of core messages; for instance, a focus on racial diversity, while crucial, might overshadow the original text's engagement with eugenics and white supremacy, and the specific plight of women's rights.

Elizabeth Moss, for example, has stated that while *The Handmaid's Tale* is recognized as a seminal feminist novel, she views it as a broader exploration of human rights. This suggests a concern that labeling it solely as "feminist" might reduce its scope and universal relatability. This dichotomy between "feminist" and "human" is particularly striking, given that the dystopian society depicted in the series was facilitated by a societal reluctance to defend women's rights specifically, not just general human rights.

Margaret Atwood herself has articulated a nuanced perspective on what constitutes a "feminist" book. She clarifies that if "feminist" implies an ideological tract where women are purely angelic or utterly victimized, then her novel does not fit. However, if it signifies a narrative where women are depicted as complex human beings, with varied behaviors and motivations, and where their experiences are central to the story's themes, then yes, it aligns with that definition. This distinction feels somewhat anachronistic in an era where television increasingly showcases complex, unlikable, and deeply human female protagonists, moving beyond simplistic archetypes.

The anxiety surrounding the "feminist" label for *The Handmaid's Tale* can be seen as a product of living within a society steeped in ambient misogyny. Narratives perceived as overtly female-centric can be unfairly dismissed as niche, mean-spirited, or frivolous. This cultural tendency devalues women's stories, much like "chick flicks" are often relegated to a lower tier due to their expected audience. For the entertainment industry, "feminist" can unfortunately become a shorthand for something preachy and lacking in entertainment value, a perception that *The Handmaid's Tale* series, with its horrifying and beautiful execution, actively defies.

The series risks being overwhelmed by the discourse surrounding its political implications, a common fate for shows with predominantly female casts. Accusations of virtue-signaling or self-indulgence, which rarely plague shows about men, can overshadow artistic merit. The sentiment that characters might be performing rather than acting authentically, driven by a desire to "look good," mirrors a broader cultural suspicion of female expression, especially when it carries a message.

Figures like Moss and Atwood, who have become unintentional feminist icons, might express caution. This phenomenon, described by some as the "All Lives Matter-ing" of a feminist text, highlights the challenging cultural landscape in which such works are produced. The pervasive societal filters and biases make it difficult to engage directly with the core of a story about women, leading to a mediation that obscures the "Thing Itself." The focus on the political reception inadvertently contributes to the fear that the show's politics will eclipse its artistic achievements.

The dilemma is a Catch-22: labeling the show as feminist risks erasing its other qualities, while avoiding the label can lead to the same outcome. While many critics have successfully delved into the show's substance, the pervasive "analytical angst" surrounding its reception is a hurdle that must be overcome. As Offred observes, "Given our wings, our blinkers, it's hard to look up, hard to get the full view... But we can do it, a little at a time." The power of *The Handmaid's Tale* lies in its ability to be dissected and understood incrementally, allowing for a broader appreciation of its artistic and thematic complexities beyond the immediate political storm.

Margaret Atwood's "The Handmaid's Tale" serves as a potent literary warning, particularly concerning the erosion of reproductive rights and escalating environmental anxieties. First published in 1985, the novel emerged as these issues gained prominence. Atwood envisioned a future where a rigid, theocratic regime exerts control over fertile women, compelling them into state-sanctioned reproduction. The book's themes resonate with alarming contemporary relevance, as women's bodily autonomy faces renewed threats and ecological crises intensify. This enduring impact, akin to George Orwell's "1984," underscores Atwood's ongoing concern for its significance, especially with the advent of a new Hulu series adaptation.

The Hulu series, developed both before and after the 2016 election, has significantly amplified the novel's modern resonance. Atwood herself observed a surge in public interest as the cast recognized they were portraying a potential reality, not mere fiction. While the iconic red robes are fictional, the tangible loss of rights once taken for granted has instilled widespread unease, rendering the novel's depiction of societal collapse even more chilling. Critics, such as Sarah Lyall in *The New York Times*, have lauded the series as "unsettlingly timely."

Atwood also scrutinizes the contemporary invocation of "traditional values." She posits that compelling women to bear children without adequate social support, such as orphanages or financial assistance, leads to detrimental societal outcomes. This echoes historical precedents, like Nicolae Ceaușescu's Romania, where mandated births resulted in overcrowded orphanages, a point highlighted by historians like Mary Ellen Fischer. Atwood cautions against policies that force impoverished women to have children they cannot afford, thereby perpetuating cycles of hardship.

The reception of "The Handmaid's Tale" has evolved over time. Early American readers perceived it as a distant, albeit disturbing, possibility. Canadians, conversely, often felt a more immediate sense of "could this happen here?" Today, younger women, who have grown up with established rights, engage with the novel with palpable alarm, recognizing the fragility of their freedoms. This was vividly demonstrated when women donned handmaid attire to protest reproductive rights legislation in Texas, mirroring scenes from the book and series, as reported by outlets like the *Austin Chronicle*.

The proliferation of high-quality streaming programs has revolutionized the adaptation of literary works, particularly lengthy narratives that are challenging to condense into films. The Hulu series, with its extended format, facilitates a more profound exploration of the narrative. It also allows for the development of characters who were peripheral or absent in Atwood's original work, offering a broader, and perhaps more terrifying, perspective on the Gilead regime. Atwood believes this adaptation is more effective at generating fear than the book or the earlier film, a sentiment shared by numerous viewers and critics, including those at *Vanity Fair*.

Atwood explicitly connects Gilead's stringent reproductive mandates to tactics employed by historical dictatorships. She references the brutal actions of Argentina's military junta, which executed pregnant women post-childbirth—a period documented by organizations like the Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo. She also draws parallels to Hitler's appropriation of "Aryan" children, a subject explored by Holocaust historians such as Raul Hilberg. While the U.S. has historically maintained a separation between private and public life, Atwood contends that women's bodies have been an exception, subject to state control even within a democracy. She likens this to the state's conscription of men for military service, linking bodily autonomy to civic obligation. The notion that unwanted infants can be readily adopted is also questioned, as Atwood points to the limitations of adoption systems, a concern frequently voiced by child welfare organizations.

In "The Handmaid's Tale," Canada symbolizes safety and liberty, echoing its historical role as a refuge for enslaved individuals during the American Civil War, a parallel often noted by historians of the Underground Railroad. Atwood, a Canadian herself, offers a distinctive perspective on power dynamics. She acknowledges that nations often judge others more critically than they are judged themselves, suggesting that while Canadians might believe they possess unique insights, this self-perception could be a form of self-deception. Nevertheless, Canada's history as a sanctuary for diverse groups, such as Vietnamese refugees in the 1970s as documented by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada, provides an alternative viewpoint, even if its complete veracity is not always assured.

The novel's contentious status, facing bans in some educational institutions while being mandated in others, underscores ongoing debates regarding age-appropriateness and the necessity of addressing difficult subjects. Atwood observes the historical inclination to shield young people from explicit content, a practice debated by organizations like the American Library Association. However, she firmly asserts that students are capable of engaging with complex issues. She recounts instances of students successfully advocating for their right to study the novel, demonstrating their capacity for reasoned discourse, as evidenced in school board meeting records.

Atwood characterizes the current political climate not as the genesis of misogyny, but as a potent catalyst. She argues that when a world leader's actions and rhetoric signal endorsement of such views, it emboldens others to express them more openly. The current administration's influence on women's movements is evident, fueling momentum for ongoing activism, a trend noted by political scientists like Kathleen Hall Jamieson. The potential enactment of certain legislation could precipitate significantly altered societies, a concern frequently articulated by policy think tanks such as the Brookings Institution.

Beyond reproductive rights, Atwood emphasizes the critical nexus between environmental stewardship and women's health. Ecological degradation directly impacts families and overall well-being, and the novel illustrates the devastating consequences of environmental harm on human fertility. Atwood cites studies linking diminished male fertility to plastic and chemical contamination in water sources, published in journals like *Environmental Health Perspectives*. She warns that disregarding these findings will yield severe repercussions, a sentiment echoed by environmental organizations such as the Natural Resources Defense Council.

Recalling her conversations with physicians who treated women seeking self-induced abortions prior to Roe v. Wade, Atwood illuminates the grim realities that could resurface: increased orphanages, fatalities from unsafe procedures, and infertility resulting from botched illegal abortions. These past events, she argues, are not merely historical footnotes but potential outcomes if legal and safe reproductive options are curtailed, a concern frequently raised by health advocacy groups like Planned Parenthood. The fundamental questions of who conceives, the number of children, and accountability remain paramount. Atwood notes that historical figures like Napoleon and Hitler promoted "have-more-babies" initiatives for strategic purposes, demonstrating how reproductive control has long been wielded for political objectives, a practice examined by military historians.

Atwood acknowledges receiving a substantial volume of hate mail over the years, anticipating an increase with the television series. Nevertheless, she maintains that her work is not intended to advocate for specific policies but rather to serve as a cautionary tale regarding the potential ramifications of certain legislative actions. She urges careful consideration of the consequences of prohibiting birth control, restricting access to reproductive information, or reducing funding for related services, posing the question of whether society is prepared to accept and bear the costs of such choices, a query often posed by public health ethicists.

In response to a hypothetical inquiry, Atwood addresses the "abandonment of science." She perceives a societal inclination to prioritize profitable scientific advancements over those that warn of potential dangers, a trend discussed by sociologists of science like Robert K. Merton. This selective application of scientific knowledge, she suggests, aligns with broader societal patterns evident in her work, a perspective frequently shared by science communicators.

"The Handmaid's Tale" offers a chillingly prescient look at societal decline, a theme that feels alarmingly relevant decades after Margaret Atwood's groundbreaking novel first appeared. In the third episode of Hulu's acclaimed series, protagonist Offred, powerfully portrayed by Elisabeth Moss, articulates this central message with stark clarity: "Now I'm awake to the world. I was asleep before. That's how we let it happen." This profound realization dawns as she walks in a somber procession with another handmaid, their iconic crimson robes a stark contrast against the oppressive watch of armed authorities.

### The Subtle Erosion of Freedoms

Offred's inner thoughts reveal the terrifying, incremental nature of her society's downfall. "When they slaughtered Congress, we didn't wake up. When they blamed terrorists and suspended the Constitution, we didn't wake up then either," she recalls. "They said it would be temporary. Nothing changes instantaneously. In a gradually heating bathtub, you'd be boiled to death before you knew it." This potent metaphor vividly illustrates how significant societal shifts can occur so gradually that people fail to recognize the looming danger until it's too late to fight back.

### Modern Parallels and Collective Indifference

In the context of current sociopolitical discussions, especially following major political changes, the series' timeliness is undeniable. Samira Wiley, who plays Moira, emphasized this point in a recent CNN interview: "I think it's scarily relevant right now. I think one of the things we can see -- especially through the flashbacks [in the show] -- is how it doesn't happen all at once. And that's the thing that's so scary."

### The Gradual Path to Authoritarianism

The show's flashbacks meticulously detail a society's descent into totalitarian rule. Before Gilead's rise, the former United States was a place where Offred and Moira shared everyday moments, finding comfort in simple pleasures like running and coffee. Their lives were irrevocably altered as women were systematically stripped of their basic rights, including their careers and financial autonomy. These freedoms weren't taken away suddenly but were dismantled piece by piece through legislative actions and insidious societal changes.

Wiley further elaborated on this dangerous progression: "I think right now we think that, 'Well, this can't happen.' And then it does. And then we think, 'Well, that next thing can't happen.' And it does," she noted. "People in the show -- and I think it's dangerous now -- become complacent and [aren't] vigilant." This widespread complacency, she warns, is a significant and perilous weakness.

### The Actors' Deep Connection to Their Roles

Wiley was drawn to Moira's resilient spirit, a departure from her earlier role as Poussey in "Orange is the New Black." "She's just a badass," Wiley stated. Elisabeth Moss, on the other hand, found a deep connection with Offred's enduring strength. The role became even more personally significant for Moss after the events of November 2016. "When you do roles and you do characters, you always try to personalize it and make it relevant for yourself, obviously. With something like this, it became more personal than anything I've ever done in a way that was unavoidable," she explained.

Moss, having recently finished her impactful work on "Mad Men," was the first actor to join "The Handmaid's Tale" project, having read the script in April 2016. She also serves as a producer on the series. Bruce Miller ("ER") is credited as the creator, with veteran television executive Warren Littlefield also holding an executive producer role. Moss views her producing position as crucial for creative input. "I think if you are going to commit that time, you do want to make sure you're going to have some creative say," she said. "And you know, as a woman in this business, I so admire other women who have taken that step themselves and taken some ownership of their career and gotten either behind the camera or gotten involved as a producer. Those are the women I look up to."

As an actor, Moss prefers characters who display genuine humanity over idealized heroic figures. She admired Peggy Olson from "Mad Men" for her "belief in herself." "For me as an actor, I don't look for heroes. I don't look for someone who is inspiring or some sort of iconic person, I look for someone who is human and who is real," Moss elaborated. "[Offred] is a hero in the end, but because she has to be."

Hulu released the first three episodes of "The Handmaid's Tale" on Wednesday, with new episodes following weekly.

Elisabeth Moss delivers a tour-de-force performance in Hulu's *The Handmaid's Tale*, arguably the television event of the year. Best known for her compelling portrayal of Peggy Olson in AMC's acclaimed drama *Mad Men*, Moss now embodies Offred, a woman subjugated to a life of forced servitude and valued solely for her reproductive capacity within the chilling alternate reality of the series. This dystopian world is brought to life from Margaret Atwood's seminal 1985 novel.

The adaptation frequently employs extreme close-ups on Moss's face, leaving no room for artifice. Every subtle eye movement, every flicker of a facial muscle, is palpable. Moss's masterful navigation of these intense demands is a testament to her profound talent. She demonstrates a deep understanding of the technical nuances of embodying Offred, such as how the character's distinctive costume influences her physicality. Simultaneously, Moss is keenly attuned to the narrative's potent political undercurrents, particularly how the show's society has perverted sex into an act of brutality rather than intimacy. As a producer on the series, she played an integral role in shaping and constructing this unsettling world.

In a conversation prior to the series' premiere, Moss discussed the thematic connections between Peggy Olson and Offred, the concept of accidental feminists, and the experience of being constantly under the camera's gaze. The following dialogue has been edited for conciseness and clarity.

**Todd VanDerWerff:** Actors often speak about how costumes inform their performances. This seems particularly true for *The Handmaid's Tale*. How did donning the Handmaid's attire impact your portrayal of Offred?

**Elisabeth Moss:** Immensely. The dress itself was a significant undertaking, serving as the foundational design for all Handmaids. We needed it to be universally applicable, with strict specifications, such as avoiding revealing elements. Yet, we also didn't want it to be an unflattering, shapeless garment. My primary desires were for it to be comfortable, given its constant wear, and something I could genuinely feel invested in putting on each day. Ane Crabtree, our costume designer, delivered on both fronts. While it represents something deeply negative, I found myself appreciating the dress. It fundamentally alters one's posture and movement, creating a distinct feeling compared to everyday wear.

The addition of the cloak, which restricts arm movement, and the bonnet, referred to as "wings," further intensifies this sense of confinement. The bonnet significantly dulls hearing, akin to covering one's ears, and severely limits peripheral vision, necessitating deliberate head turns. This restriction proved invaluable for camera work, allowing for nuanced portrayals where only one or both eyes might be visible. Every head movement became intentional and specific, which was crucial in a world where overt expression is forbidden or highly controlled.

**Todd VanDerWerff:** The series features numerous flashbacks where Offred is depicted in what could be considered more conventional attire.

**Elisabeth Moss:** Much of that was my own clothing, so I'm relieved you didn't describe it as "freakish."

**Todd VanDerWerff:** What did you discover about navigating the world in your own clothes versus the highly restrictive Handmaid costume?

**Elisabeth Moss:** The freedom of choice and individuality inherent in modern dress is entirely absent for the Handmaids. Two fundamental rights stripped away are privacy and individuality. Privacy is eliminated because one's attire immediately signals their social class and reproductive status. Uniformity eradicates any possibility of personal expression. It was fascinating to contrast this with the flashback attire, exploring the flexibility of modern dress, even in terms of gender presentation. The Handmaid's dress is deliberately designed to be conventionally "feminine" within a very narrow societal definition. In flashbacks, June, Offred's former name, could appear more androgynous, transitioning from a delicate spring dress to jeans and a t-shirt or a baseball tee. This reflects the sartorial freedom we possess in contemporary society, a freedom not universally available.

**Todd VanDerWerff:** Many reviews highlight the show's contemporary relevance, particularly concerning women's rights globally. While the novel has always been timely, when you're actively portraying Offred, do you focus on the story's political dimensions, or is it more of an emotional, intuitive process that later incorporates political reflection?

**Elisabeth Moss:** Politics inevitably intrudes. It's impossible to be an aware woman in today's world and not recognize the infringement of rights. This is something I've always felt personally and have actively advocated for. It's difficult not to internalize these issues more deeply when portraying such a character, and it fosters a sense of responsibility towards other women, a responsibility I embrace and feel privileged to undertake.

**Todd VanDerWerff:** You previously inhabited another iconic television role for an extended period. Do you perceive a connection between Peggy Olson and Offred, or is their primary similarity simply your shared appearance?

**Elisabeth Moss:** I see numerous parallels. Fundamentally, both are depicted as ordinary women. I often describe Peggy as an "accidental feminist." She wasn't familiar with the term "glass ceiling"; she encountered information about pay disparities and, finding it unfair, sought a raise out of simple incomprehension. This blend of stubbornness and almost naive conviction exemplifies many women of that era who paved the way for progress in the 1960s.

I observe the same qualities in Offred. She is a wife, a mother, a woman with her own challenges and a job. She isn't initially conceived as a heroine. Her profound realization is that heroism resides within all of us, that we possess the power to effect change. This is a crucial lesson for her. Both characters are flawed, which I find compelling, as no one is perfect. Yet, both possess an inherent strength.

**Todd VanDerWerff:** You characterized Peggy as an accidental feminist. Similarly, Offred in the novel likely wouldn't have identified as a feminist, but her circumstances force her to confront what she once took for granted. Do you believe Offred has undergone a form of radicalization that Peggy perhaps did not?

**Elisabeth Moss:** I do. This phenomenon is observable in many women, including those in my generation and younger. We grew up assuming certain inalienable rights, like the permanence of Roe v. Wade, were secure. The notion of losing the right to choose seemed inconceivable; we expected someone would always defend these rights. While not entirely taking them for granted, we anticipated their continued existence.

In recent years, there's been a palpable awakening among my generation, younger generations, and even teenagers, realizing that these rights are not immutable. We may have amplified June's feminist leanings in flashbacks compared to the book, but her radicalization is a natural consequence of having all her rights systematically removed. It compels a fight-or-flight response that she might not have anticipated. For us, as women in 2017, the imperative is to act now, rather than waiting for such circumstances to arise.

**Todd VanDerWerff:** *The Handmaid's Tale* portrays sex as inherently political, with Offred's rape sanctioned by the state and her clandestine flirtations serving as acts of rebellion. Yet, filming sex scenes is often described as tedious and technical for actors. Did this technical aspect overshadow the story's broader political messages surrounding sex?

**Elisabeth Moss:** It was paramount that the "Ceremony" be unequivocally depicted as sexual assault, without any attempt to soften that reality. It is central to the narrative. Fertility and procreation are the ultimate goals for everyone, regardless of their societal standing. We were committed to portraying this with brutal accuracy, emphasizing its clinical and perfunctory nature, while also incorporating the distinct emotional responses of the three individuals involved. This contrasted sharply with typical sex scenes, which can be awkward or mundane. This was a performance scene, driven by the extreme circumstances.

Furthermore, Gilead enforces a desexualization of intimacy. Men and women are forbidden from experiencing sexual pleasure; orgasm is not to be enjoyed. As seen in subsequent episodes, this becomes a significant struggle for June. The right to experience sexual pleasure, a fundamental human right, is eradicated. Reclaiming this ownership is crucial, representing yet another right that has been systematically denied.

**Todd VanDerWerff:** The camera frequently lingers on your face, capturing even the slightest eyebrow movement, leaving no space for inauthentic moments. Is this an exhilarating aspect of the role?

**Elisabeth Moss:** It's phenomenal. It significantly enhances my work. When portraying a character who cannot speak freely, the camera can register internal thoughts. A swallowed breath, a subtle tension – these become visible. Having reviewed the episodes multiple times, I'm often struck by involuntary facial twitches or neck movements that weren't consciously performed but rather an organic manifestation of internalized emotion, of feelings suppressed or unexpressed.

This intimate relationship with the camera transcends scene context. Even with a distant shot, a connection exists. However, with such proximity, it feels as though the camera operator, director of photography, or director Reed Morano becomes an extension of oneself. The matte box effectively isolates the frame, focusing attention inward. Their movement in sync with yours creates a sense of unified entity. I find this deeply rewarding. While wider shots were also utilized, we consistently returned to what we termed the "Offred lens." Whenever it was employed, I felt a sense of relief and validation, knowing I could truly convey my character's internal state.

*The Handmaid's Tale* is currently available for streaming on Hulu.

## "The Handmaid's Tale": A Reflection of Present-Day Concerns

The recent Hulu adaptation of Margaret Atwood's novel, *The Handmaid's Tale*, has prompted a notable reaction. Some viewers, upon seeing the trailer, mistakenly assumed it was a new critique of the Trump administration, unaware of its origins in a book written decades prior. This confusion is understandable, as the series powerfully taps into current anxieties. The disturbing images of children separated from their families, the persecution of those deemed "unnatural," and the forced pregnancies of victims all feel uncomfortably familiar.

The enduring power of *The Handmaid's Tale* lies in its timeless exploration of dystopia. Beyond the overt atrocities, the series masterfully exposes the pervasive fear women experience due to everyday sexism. Even before the oppressive Handmaid era, we witness casual cruelty, like a coffee shop cashier's insults directed at Offred and her friend Moira. This moment mirrors a world where societal breakdown emboldens misogynists, echoing online trolls empowered by anonymity and real-world incidents of hateful expression.

While the series depicts violence and sexual assault, it is the more insidious forms of misogyny that resonate deeply. We see the boss offering a perfunctory apology while laying off female employees, or the soldier holding a door for departing women who will not return to their jobs. These instances highlight the creeping sexism many women encounter – the condescending gesture rather than outright harassment.

Atwood's novel, and the series, powerfully illustrate how personal anxieties surrounding masculinity fuel the political structure of this dystopian society. The very idea of male infertility is forbidden, and women are barred from reading, lest intellectual pursuits distract them from childbearing. Even well-meaning men become unwitting participants in patriarchal systems. When Offred learns women's bank accounts have been seized and transferred to male relatives, her husband assures her he will "take care of" her. This dismissive promise highlights his inability to grasp the systemic crisis, framing it as a personal matter he can control, thereby reinforcing his own perceived power.

Ultimately, *The Handmaid's Tale* is a terrifying exploration of sexism, not just in its extreme forms, but in its everyday, insidious manifestations. It is not solely about a society where politicians dehumanize pregnant women or where public figures refuse to dine alone with women. It is about the erosion of trust, not just in government, but in the men closest to us. The series reveals the danger that lies not only in overt male disdain for women but also in the apathy and self-serving attachment to privilege exhibited by "good" men. These are the dual horrors that women have long understood.

Margaret Atwood's 1985 novel, *The Handmaid's Tale*, has become a focal point for discussions about contemporary American society, with many drawing parallels between its fictional dystopia and current events. This renewed interest, significantly boosted by the popular Hulu miniseries, has fueled extensive commentary in major publications like *The Washington Post*, *The San Francisco Chronicle*, and *The New York Times*. These outlets have frequently described the novel as both "timely" and "chillingly real," often connecting its themes to the prevailing political climate, particularly during the Trump administration. Rebecca Nicholson, writing for *The Guardian*, noted a growing trend of readers identifying concerning links between the fictional Republic of Gilead and present-day occurrences. This phenomenon was underscored when the book surged to the top of Amazon's bestseller list, coinciding with the Women's Marches, where signs famously urged, "Make Margaret Atwood fiction again."

Atwood herself has weighed in on this cultural moment, sharing her thoughts in an interview with the *Los Angeles Times* on April 19th. She suggested that Donald Trump's election had, in her opinion, blurred the lines between fiction and reality. "The election happened, and the cast woke up in the morning and thought, we’re no longer making fiction — we’re making a documentary," she stated, articulating a sentiment of profound unease shared by many. This feeling is echoed in various analytical pieces. For instance, *The New Republic* suggested that certain American states, such as Texas and Indiana, and potentially the entire nation under a Vice Presidency like Mike Pence's, were exhibiting characteristics increasingly reminiscent of Gilead. While this comparison is striking, it warrants a closer look at the specific factual basis.

However, a more detailed examination of this widespread discourse reveals a tendency to overlook or oversimplify specific factual details that complicate the direct equation of contemporary America with Gilead. For example, the broad assertion that Republicans universally oppose reproductive rights is challenged by historical evidence of their support for accessible over-the-counter birth control options. Furthermore, the financial landscape of organizations such as Planned Parenthood, which reportedly receives substantial annual taxpayer funding, is often presented in a manner that lacks nuance. Even Supreme Court Justice Neil Gorsuch, during his rigorous confirmation hearings, indicated a personal stance that would have opposed any directive to overturn the landmark Roe v. Wade decision. These verifiable points, which offer a more complex picture, are frequently sidelined in the broader, more emotionally charged narrative that equates current events with Atwood's dystopia.

The prevailing feminist discourse, as it intersects with the discussions surrounding *The Handmaid's Tale*, sometimes inadvertently implies a deficit in personal agency for women, framing them as passive recipients of reproductive outcomes rather than active decision-makers. This narrative frequently overlooks the widespread existence and accessibility of modern contraception, a crucial tool that empowers individuals to make informed choices about their reproductive health. It also tends to downplay the inherent capacity for individual decision-making that women possess. Paradoxically, despite advocating passionately for bodily autonomy, some feminist arguments appear to suggest that women are incapable of managing their reproductive health effectively without extensive government intervention. This perspective, which can be perceived as paternalistic, ironically mirrors the very control mechanisms and infantilizing assumptions that Atwood so powerfully depicts in her dystopian vision of Gilead.

The intense focus on figures like Vice President Mike Pence, whose personal habits and stated beliefs have been scrutinized as indicative of a potentially Puritanical mindset, often serves to distract from more complex and multifaceted societal dynamics. The insinuation that women inherently lack personal agency is a recurring theme within this discourse, a notion that persists even when discussing a country where, in many states, minors can obtain IUDs without parental consent. This stands in stark contrast to the ongoing political debates surrounding taxpayer funding for organizations like Planned Parenthood, an organization that, it is worth noting, paid its CEO a significant salary in 2014. The portrayal of America as being on the precipice of a totalitarian regime, where women are purportedly "forced" to reproduce against their will, often seems detached from the tangible realities of available reproductive healthcare options and the exercise of individual choice. The simplistic notion that babies simply "happen" to women fails to acknowledge the profound role that contraception and personal agency play in family planning decisions. The assertion that birth control is on the verge of being banned, while simultaneously remaining heavily subsidized, is a factual claim that is frequently left unaddressed amidst the widespread panic and alarm. Ultimately, while the discourse surrounding *The Handmaid's Tale* effectively taps into legitimate anxieties about reproductive rights and societal control, it often oversimplifies the intricate and nuanced interplay of public policy, individual autonomy, and the complex landscape of reproductive rights in contemporary America. The novel's enduring power lies in its ability to provoke thought, but its contemporary reception sometimes risks flattening the multifaceted reality it is being used to interpret.

query: What new features are expected in the upcoming iPhone models, particularly the high-end model?

documents.text:
**iPhone 8 Leaks: Infrared Facial Recognition and Bezel-less Design Revealed**

New details about Apple's upcoming iPhone models have surfaced, thanks to a leaked HomePod firmware update. This accidental release offers developers a preview of the next generation of iPhones, including a premium model alongside the expected standard "S" versions for the iPhone 7 and 7 Plus.

**Infrared Facial Recognition Confirmed**

A key revelation from the firmware code points to the integration of an **infrared facial recognition system**. Developer Steve Troughton-Smith has corroborated reports of this biometric feature within Apple's BiometricKit framework, referencing code like "BKFaceDetect." The "BK" prefix likely stands for Biometric Kit, a common naming convention in Apple's developer tools. This indicates the new iPhone will be able to **detect faces using infrared technology**, even in low-light conditions. Previous reports from Bloomberg had suggested Apple might use an infrared camera for 3D facial scanning, but its inclusion across all new iPhone models was uncertain.

**A Glimpse of the Bezel-less iPhone Pro**

The leaked firmware also appears to contain an embedded image showcasing a **novel, bezel-less design for the high-end iPhone**. This model, speculated to be named "iPhone Pro," "iPhone 8," or "iPhone X," is expected to feature a display that extends nearly to the device's edges. Notably, this screen seems to curve around the speaker and camera sensors at the top. Unlike previous potentially fabricated leaks, this visual evidence is directly integrated into the HomePod's code, making it more credible.

**Touch ID Status Unclear, Facial Scanning Takes Priority**

The leaked information also addresses ongoing speculation about Touch ID, the iPhone's fingerprint sensor. For months, rumors have debated its placement: under the display, on the back, in the power button, or omitted entirely. While the definitive status of Touch ID remains unconfirmed, the firmware leak strongly suggests the new iPhone will support unlocking through **facial scanning**. The fundamental visual profile of the premium model, characterized by its edge-to-edge display, has also been disclosed.

The prospect of a $1,400 iPhone, a topic explored by Christopher Mims in The Wall Street Journal, compels a deeper dive into Apple's pricing strategies and its esteemed brand. While concrete details about the upcoming flagship iPhone remain elusive, industry whispers and supply chain intel point towards a starting price hovering around $1,000, with the possibility of reaching $1,400. This would mark a considerable leap from the current iPhone 7 models, which retail between $649 and $969. Apple now stands at a critical juncture, tasked with either elevating its brand prestige further or risking the alienation of its devoted customer base.

Apple's brand equity is undeniably robust, valued by Forbes at an impressive $170 billion, a figure that significantly eclipses competitors like Google. This valuation underpins Apple's substantial $250 billion cash reserve, a sum that rivals the market capitalization of industry giants such as Netflix, Tesla, and eBay. While a specialized, high-end "halo" product might hold a certain allure, Apple's historical experiences, exemplified by the 20th Anniversary Macintosh, serve as a cautionary tale. Industry analyst Jon Gruber posits that a "$1,200-$1,300 'Pro' model is feasible," yet he views it as a niche offering rather than a mainstream device. The bedrock of Apple's enduring success lies in what can be described as "obtainable aspiration"—the compelling desire for high-quality, desirable products that are within reach, rather than purely unattainable luxury.

Several key factors weigh on Apple's decision-making process, including the strategic role of halo products, the unwavering loyalty of its customer base, and the market's overall receptiveness. Marketing professor Janell Townsend highlights that halo products can indeed serve as valuable testing grounds for nascent technologies. However, Apple has a well-established pattern of integrating innovations into its primary product lines, a strategy evident in the adoption of the iPad Pro stylus and the dual-lens cameras on larger iPhone models. Apple consistently delivers meaningful upgrades to its standard iPhones, often without accompanying price hikes, thereby incentivizing upgrades. Conversely, a hypothetical iPhone 7S priced at $649 without substantial new features would likely face diminished adoption. Similarly, confining advanced functionalities to a significantly more expensive model could alienate a considerable portion of its loyal customer base. Apple's history with the iPad further illustrates that while price increases can be justified by enhanced features, maintaining robust sales hinges on a keen understanding of market receptiveness. The iPhone's remarkable success is rooted in steady, incremental improvements that render each iteration desirable, much like consumers purchasing new computers for enhanced performance at a comparable cost.

Supply chain intricacies, frequently cited by industry observers like Jon Gruber and Christopher Mims as a catalyst for price escalations, emerge as a recurring concern each year. Nevertheless, Apple consistently demonstrates an impressive ability to scale production post-launch, effectively navigating initial shortages. This resilience underscores the sophisticated engineering and manufacturing prowess required for each iPhone. The sourcing of novel components or the production of intricate parts undoubtedly present challenges. However, CEO Tim Cook's expertise in procurement and manufacturing is widely acknowledged. The anticipated integration of an OLED screen in the next iPhone, for instance, exemplifies Apple's success in securing substantial supplies from Samsung. Historically, Apple has maintained stable pricing, deliberately avoiding premium charges for early access, a critical aspect emphasized by analyst Rakesh Agrawal. This deliberate approach is fundamental to their overarching brand and business strategy.

While Apple may harbor ambitions to elevate its Average Selling Price (ASP) for iPhones, a drastic price hike is unlikely to represent the most effective or sustainable strategy. The iPhone's ASP has demonstrated remarkable consistency, generally fluctuating between $550 and $700, with predictable seasonal variations. Apple adeptly manages this through a diverse product portfolio, which includes more accessible options like the iPhone SE alongside its premium offerings. Nevertheless, a substantial price increase is neither a necessity nor a strategically prudent move. Fundamental economic principles dictate that elevated prices typically correlate with diminished demand.

The assumption that a significant iPhone price increase would not substantially impact sales carries considerable risk. While some propose that an anniversary iPhone could evolve into a Veblen good—a rare category where demand paradoxically increases with price—a consumer product selling over 200 million units annually with a starting price of $649 is highly unlikely to fit this peculiar economic classification. Veblen goods, such as ultra-luxury automobiles, derive their value from exclusivity and high price points that function as potent status symbols; a reduction in their price would inevitably diminish their appeal. Giffen goods, where demand rises with price due to a lack of affordable alternatives, are entirely irrelevant to Apple's premium market segment. A substantially higher iPhone price would almost certainly lead to a reduction in sales volume, a scenario Wall Street typically views with considerable disfavor. Furthermore, Apple faces intensifying competition, particularly in burgeoning markets like China, where affordable domestic smartphones are rapidly gaining traction. A scenario where Chinese consumers reject significantly more expensive iPhones could easily offset any short-term financial gains. A hypothetical 10% price increase met with a corresponding 10% drop in sales could attract significant analyst criticism and potentially undermine the app and service ecosystem, a crucial growth engine for Apple.

While the introduction of a $1,400 iPhone variant or a higher entry price is not entirely out of the realm of possibility, it represents an improbable and strategically questionable direction for Apple. The company already possesses substantial financial resources and should logically prioritize the expansion of its user base over the sole augmentation of its cash reserves. A super-premium iPhone might offer a temporary profit boost, but it fundamentally deviates from the core principles that have cemented Apple's position as a leading technology innovator.

This September, Apple is poised to unveil its latest iPhone lineup. Alongside the expected standard models, a **special 10th-anniversary edition** is widely rumored to be part of the release.

This **ultra-deluxe model** is anticipated to showcase **cutting-edge technology**. Among the potential key advancements are:

*   An **OLED screen**
*   Advanced **3-D imaging** capabilities
*   A sophisticated **retina scanner** for enhanced security

Industry analysts foresee this premium device carrying a **significant price tag**. Current estimates place its cost in the range of **$1,000 to $1,400**. This high anticipation has already fueled considerable excitement within the tech community and among consumers.

There are just days until the launch of the iPhone 8 - in fact, the evidence now strongly suggests it'll be called the iPhone X - and hype is reaching almost unbearable levels. Rumours suggest the iPhone 8 will feature a larger display, made possible by removing the Home Button and moving the fingerprint scanner - if Apple can overcome the technical challenges. It is also said to feature wireless charging and Face ID, a new form of facial recognition that will support Apple Pay. In this article, we round up all the rumours about the new iPhone 8: its UK release announcement (12 Sept) and onsale date (probably 22 Sept) - UK price, specs and new features, and what it's going to look like. We also cover leaked photos and videos. For advice on the current iPhone range, read our iPhone buying guide and best cheap iPhone deals UK. Or, if you'd like to look even further into the future, read iPhone 9 and beyond. Release date The iPhone 8 will be announced on 12 September, as confirmed by Apple's sending of press invites on 31 August. We'll save you from the mad interpretations people make from the invite designs, but there is a suspiciously curved edge in that light colour... no, we'll stop. But it's going to be great, isn't it? Preorders are likely to begin on 15 Sept with an onsale release date of 22 Sept. Read more about the Apple September 2017 event here. Delays & shortages Some sources have suggested there may be longer-than-usual delays between the iPhone 8 announcement and products hitting shops, or that there may be initial supply shortages. Deutsche Bank analysts predict that "key component shortages and technical challenges could delay the release of a high-end iPhone 8", while IHS Markit analyst Brian Huh claims Samsung is having trouble meeting demand for the OLED screens. Ming-Chi Kuo is among the analysts to state that problems have now been resolved and iPhone 8 mass production will begin in mid September as planned - but while the flagship device will now go on sale at the same time as the two cheaper models, he warns that there may be a shortage at first. DigiTimes too predicts that "the supply of the OLED version [iPhone 8] could fall short of demand." What will the new iPhone be called? We expect Apple to announce three new handsets: the first two will be simple updates to the 7 and 7 Plus (they may even be thicker), but we'll all be focused on the flagship model and its wireless charging, 3D scanning, AR and edge-to-edge OLED screen. One developer reckons he's found the names of these handsets. Steve Troughton-Smith, combing through the latest leaked firmware, has found references to iPhone 8, iPhone 8 Plus and iPhone X. (The latter makes sense because it's the Roman numeral for 10, and 2017 is the iPhone's 10th anniversary.) iPhone 8 iPhone 8 Plus iPhone X pic.twitter.com/OVLfhLxTdT — Steve T-S (@stroughtonsmith) September 9, 2017 This fits with an earlier report from iCulture (in Dutch), citing "a reliable source", which predicted the same thing. There had been a claim by 9to5Mac that the phones would be called the 8, 8 Plus and iPhone Edition, but X is now looking the strong favourite. Macworld poll: What do you think Apple will call its next iPhone? iPhone 8 design & leaked images Noted tech blogger Robert Scoble has posted an exhaustive list of predictions for this year's iPhone, and he expects big things. "It's the 10th anniversary of the iPhone," he writes. "It's the first product introduction in Apple's new amazing headquarters. It's a big f**king deal and will change this industry deeply." Here's what we expect from the new design, and the leaked images we've seen to back it all up. Edge-to-edge screen The iPhone 8 will have a screen running almost edge to edge, with a slimmed-down bezel of just 4mm on each side. An image of this design, also showing the loss of the Home button and the rumoured 'notch' along the top edge, has been spotted in the HomePod's firmware. (It also offers clues about face-unlocking features, the lack of a Home button, a new Apple Watch-esque 'tap to wake' feature and much more.) This is mirrored by the leak of what is believed to be the user guide from the iPhone 8 packaging: And an alleged leak of the iPhone 8's front glass, posted on Weibo: Similarly, Macworld has been sent an iPhone 8 dummy from the case maker Olixar, which has already started making cases for the device. (It's very detailed and the buttons press in, but contrary to reports you may have seen elsewhere, this is absolutely not a "working prototype".) "It's the dummy that Olixar has been using to design their cases for the iPhone 8," said our contact. "Apart from some minor details that might still change in the final design stages we believe this is what the iPhone 8 will look like!" We're told that the company obtained these details from "a regular group of contacts that help them to source these items for the production of their accessories"; while we'd rate the authenticity as unproven, it does seem to fit in with the currently popular rumours. Note the cut-out in the display at the top, to allow for the speaker and sensors: we think this section will be used for the status bar, which is split into two groups of icons anyway. This is a theme that's repeated in other concept illustrations and leaks we've seen - such as this one from Forbes. Here's a render with measurements for that edge-to-edge screen: And this tweet shows the measurements of the top status bar area, as revealed in the HomePod firmware leak: These are the metrics used by the status bar on the edge-to-edge iPhone, including notch height and ear width. Designers, have fun pic.twitter.com/lPD2RbEJFA — Steve T-S (@stroughtonsmith) August 10, 2017 Finally, one bright spark appears to have got semi-confirmation from Apple itself, by designing iMessage stickers depicting this design and having them rejected from the App Store for being "too similar to iPhones". So, I tried to submit an iMessage sticker pack. pic.twitter.com/HNziE4SSRx — Saif Aslam (@msaifaslam) August 17, 2017 No Home button It's also believe that there will be no Home button on the front of the iPhone 8 (we'll discuss the implications of this for Touch ID in a moment). The HomePod firmware leak mentioned previously contains code referring to a new variable called "deviceHasHomeButton" (which could be tagged with a yes or no), which seems like a strong hint that this is going ahead. @stroughtonsmith And the death of the home button is confirmed. RIP. pic.twitter.com/w638s4Tgzn — Guilherme Rambo (@_inside) August 1, 2017 A June 2017 leak backs up the 'no Home button' theory. As you can see, that's a massive screen, with no physical home button in sight. Aside from Touch ID, this also removes one of the traditional ways of accessing Siri - but source code suggests this may now be done by holding down the power button instead (presumably for less time than it would take to turn the iPhone off). And you'll still be able to use Hey Siri, of course. Home button function area If the physical Home button is ditched, Apple is likely to replace it with a software Home button. In February 2017 Ming Chi Kuo predicted that the iPhone 8 would have a function area, somewhat like the Touch Bar on the 2016 MacBook Pro, which will take the place of the lower bezel and Home button and contain "virtual buttons". Near the end of August 2017 Bloomberg revealed further details of how the iPhone 8's interface will work, citing "images of the new device viewed by Bloomberg News and people familiar with the gadget". The site claims that the Home button of previous handsets will be replaced by new gesture controls. "Across the bottom of the screen there's a thin, software bar in lieu of the home button," Bloomberg writes. "A user can drag it up to the middle of the screen to open the phone." Further gestures, many of them context-specific, will enable users to close apps, multitask and return to the home screen. These stunning mockups by Gabor Balogh give an idea of how the function area could be used, and how it could interact with the also-rumoured new augmented reality features. Looks great, right? But not so fast. The developer Steve Troughton-Smith, "supported by API evidence", has posted a summary of what the Home button area will and won't be able to do - and it seems that there will be no ability for developers to change the colour of the area to fit in with their apps. We know some facts re iPhone 8 home button area: • it resizes • indicator can be hidden • no API to change color • tab bars extend under it — Steve T-S (@stroughtonsmith) August 10, 2017 "Fullscreen video does hide it," he adds, but "there is no evidence to suggest any app UI moves to the home button area at all. No toolbars, no other junk." Initially at least, the area will remain a comparatively standardised area that either shows the Home button in its conventional design or gets out of the way entirely for full-screen video, gaming and so on. What happens to the Touch ID fingerprint sensor? Developer betas of iOS 11 suggest Touch ID will remain in some form this autumn: the lock screen still displays its icon when trying to access locked notifications. But if Apple gives the iPhone 8 with a larger screen and ditches the Home Button, what happens to the fingerprint sensor? There's four main options: a scanner built into the screen; a scanner on the back of the phone; a scanner built into the power button; and no scanner at all, with Touch ID replaced by Face ID. No scanner Right now it seems most likely, based on multiple leaks, that the iPhone 8 won't have a fingerprint scanner - and that the Touch ID code in iOS 11 is there for existing handsets and possibly the iPhone 7s and 7s Plus. In this scenario Touch ID's security functions will be covered by Face ID. Scanner under the screen iDrop News, citing an unnamed source, reported that Apple has been testing fingerprint technology beneath the glass display, but that this isn't ready for prime time this year. Technology is moving forward quickly; Qualcomm, for example, has come up with new sensors that can recognise fingerprints through OLED display stacks of up to 1200um. But it seems these breakthroughs may have come too late for the iPhone 8. KeyBanc Capital Markets analyst Andy Hargreaves has written that it's "entirely unclear if Apple will be able to fix the problem" within time for the launch, and Ming-Chi Kuo says Apple has "cancelled" plans to embed Touch ID in the iPhone 8's screen. Scanner on the back Because of the technological obstacles preventing in-screen Touch ID, iDrop News predicts that the scanner will go on the back of the device. This was backed up by a video that leaked in late August 2017, and purportedly shows a factory worker in China testing the fingerprint scanner on an iPhone 8. We're not totally convinced, to say the least, and it could easily show testing on an iPhone clone by a different company, or an early prototype that did have rear Touch ID. Further 'evidence' of this came in the form of leaked images in a tweet by Sonny Dickson, which appears to feature a space at the back of the iPhone to accommodate a scanner. iPhone 8 looks to be taking a new direction pic.twitter.com/mG19bcDYiC — Sonny Dickson (@SonnyDickson) April 19, 2017 There's also this (suspiciously blurry) photo, posted on a Chinese forum and supposedly from a Foxconn assembly line. You can see the circular cutout for the fingerprint scanner just below the Apple logo: It's also apparent in an August 2017 leak which appears to show off the rear-facing fingerprint scanner. The video shows an Apple leather case for the iPhone 8, although it's widely believed to be a fake - the details on the case are shoddy, and the acting is a little cheesy too. The designs have not gone down well with people critical of Samsung's similar solution to the same problem on the S8+. Scanner in the power button Our last suggestion is that Touch ID could be integrated into the power button. Forbes has posted a mock-up based on CAD files leaked by case designer Nodus which shows what appears to be a bigger lock button on the side of the iPhone, which could be in order to house Touch ID. Vertically aligned twin-lens camera The third big expected change to the design affects the rear-facing camera, which looks to have twin lenses arranged vertically (as opposed to the side-by-side layout on the iPhone 7 Plus). Forbes' Gordon Kelly is worried that this will stick out at the rear of the phone and make it wobble when placed on a flat surface. The vertical camera design was illustrated in an early July 2017 leak showcasing an alleged iPhone 8 back panel. Two short videos, which have been posted on Chinese social media in August 2017 and uploaded to YouTube by the Dutch site TechTastic, purportedly show the iPhone 8 production line - and while you can't see much, it's quite clear that the devices have vertically aligned camera lenses. And if you're yet to be convinced that all this is the finalised design of the iPhone 8, you need look no further than infamous (and remarkably reliable) leaker OnLeaks' Twitter account. More specifically, a tweet posted on 23 June shows what appears to be the next-generation iPhone 8, which can be seen below. Glass front and back 9to5Mac has got hold of a report from the ever-quotable and usually reliable Ming-Chi Kuo of KGI Securities - a report that predicts, based on the popularity of the scratch-prone Jet Black iPhone 7, that the iPhone 8 will have a (more scratch-resistant) glass front and back. Glass would also make it easier to implement wireless charging. "If Apple does follow through with what KGI suggests, an all-glass design could extend the glossy finish to all colours of the iPhone lineup depending on how Apple handles the design," 9to5Mac says. The rumour was also backed up by Benjamin Geskin on Twitter, a source that has provided many iPhone 8 leaks over the past few months. He claims that one of the colour options for the iPhone 8 will be a mirror-like finish. Slashleaks has posted a video allegedly showing metal iPhone 8 rear shells being assembled in a Foxconn factory. But it's possible this actually shows iPhone 7 or 7s Plus handsets, which may not get the all-glass design of the top-end model. (Or the cases may, as OnLeaks observes, be intended for clone handsets that are nothing to do with Apple!) Colour options The iPhone 7 handsets are available in six colours: silver, gold, Rose Gold, black, Jet Black and (Product) Red. (The red option was not added until several months after launch, however.) Things look to be different for the iPhone 8. Benjamin Geskin's source says the iPhone 8 will feature a mirror-like finish plus only three other colour options (most likely silver, gold and black); while analyst Ming-Chi Kuo predicts that the mirror option has been canned so we'll get just silver, gold and black. But hold up! Geskin has tweeted again since then to offer an entirely new colour option, which he refers to as Blush Gold and which looks like a sort of coppery reddish-pink. This appears to be the same finish MyDrivers referred to nearer the start of August, admittedly in translation, as "champagne gold". Geskin acknowledges, however, that this name is based on a working translation from the Chinese and may not be accurate. He says the information comes from a leaked Foxconn report, and was posted on the Weibo Chinese social media site. New features That covers design. But what new features should we expect in the iPhone 8? Face ID The loss of the Home button means the Touch ID fingerprint scanner is in design limbo - with separate theories claiming it will move to the back of the device, or be embedded under the glass, or be removed entirely. But many of its functions may be replaced by a new feature tentatively known as Face ID. The HomePod firmware mentioned earlier contains references to facial recognition and expression detection that could be used to unlock the device. There's also a lot of new references to facial expression detection pic.twitter.com/8PsPVj1QqU — Guilherme Rambo (@_inside) August 1, 2017 Face detection/unlock has been expected for a while. Back in December 2014, USPTO awarded Apple a patent relating to a "personal computing device control using face detection and recognition", and US Patent 20170076077, published in March 2017, describes a method for "Locking and unlocking a mobile device using facial recognition". DigiTimes has predicted that Apple is likely to launch iPhones equipped with iris-recognition technology in 2018; but at the end of August 2016 the site went further, reporting that Taiwan-based Xintec is expected to provide iris scanners to Apple for the iPhone launching in 2017. JPMorgan analyst Rod Hall backed up this theory with his own prediction that the iPhone 8 will have a 3D laser scanner for face recognition, replacing Touch ID. (If you'd like to know how powerful a mere rumour can be, check this out: the share price of the UK company expected to provide laser wafers for this feature, IQE, has tripled this year.) A photo of this scanner appears to have leaked, too: And unlocking the phone might not be the only thing facial recognition is used for. Code in the HomePod firmware leak indicates that it will also be able to authenticate Apple Pay and be accessible to third-party apps; and that it will also serve to mute notifications if the user is perceived to be 'paying attention' already. About Pearl ID: 1 - The software definitely supports it for payments 2 - 3rd party apps can use it 3 - You can add multiple faces pic.twitter.com/aUotHwD64f — Guilherme Rambo (@_inside) August 9, 2017 Augmented reality and VR Apple debuted ARKit at WWDC 2017, showcasing how developers can implement AR abilities into iPads and iPhones. Surely the iPhone 8 will arrive with cool new features that take advantage of that? We previously spotted an Apple patent application that appears to back up all this speculation. Patent 9,488,488, for 'Augmented reality maps', describes the use of a mobile device to view live video of whatever is in front of the user, and to then superimpose images related to nearby places of interest on top of the video. Loup Ventures analysts speculate that a company named Finisar may be providing lasers for AR depth mapping in the iPhone 8. You can read more in our Apple VR and augmented reality rumours article. Wireless charging Apple has been offering wireless (inductive) charging for a while now on the Apple Watch, and we're hearing increasingly confident predictions that the feature will come to the iPhone in 2017. One strong hint came from Robert Hwang, the CEO of Apple manufacturer Wistron, who (in an apparent slip-up) said "new features like waterproof and wireless charging" will appear in the next generation of Apple handsets; and SlashLeaks has posted what it says is the charging coil that will be used to deliver this feature. Benjamin Geskin, a well-known leaker, has posted what he says are images of components in the iPhone 8, and these include a similar wireless charging module: #iPhone8 Foxconn Production Photos. Shows wireless charging, stacked two-layer circuit board and “L” shaped batteryhttps://t.co/zVLHCWJmIX pic.twitter.com/M0oCpDXNqZ — Benjamin Geskin (@VenyaGeskin1) August 5, 2017 And here are more leaked photos of the wireless charging pads: Such speculation is not new. Renders based on CAD files obtained by Engadget in May from "a reliable source", for example, claim to show that the new iPhone will have a glass back incorporating a wireless charging coil. (Mind you, in July 2015 Qualcomm announced a wireless charging breakthrough that can work through metal.) Foxconn - one of the large manufacturing firms that assembles iPhones for Apple - has been reportedly testing wireless charging modules that will be included in some or all of the 2017 iPhones if the tests prove satisfactory. And in February 2017 it emerged that Apple had joined the Wireless Power Consortium industry group, as spotted by 9to5Mac.
