This stance aligns with his executive order on immigration and refugees, as detailed by the Department of Homeland Security.

Despite criticisms of exacerbating racial and religious divisions, Trump made a clear effort to counter these perceptions. He condemned anti-Semitic threats and a hate crime in Kansas City that resulted in the death of one Indian man and injuries to another. These actions were acknowledged by civil rights organizations like the Southern Poverty Law Center.

**A Vision for Healthcare**

Trump reiterated his goal to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA), drawing negative reactions from Democratic lawmakers, including Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer. He expressed a desire for new laws that would:

*   Expand choice
*   Increase access
*   Lower costs
*   Provide better healthcare

He criticized the ACA's "mandate" but assured that individuals with pre-existing conditions would retain coverage and that no one would be excluded from Medicaid. This promise drew scrutiny from healthcare policy experts at the Kaiser Family Foundation.

However, most experts, including those at the Congressional Budget Office, believe achieving such an ideal outcome is improbable, especially in a divided Congress.

**Improved Fact-Checking**

Critics have often pointed to President Trump's tendency to misrepresent facts. For instance, he recently claimed the largest Electoral College victory since Ronald Reagan, a statement later challenged by fact-checkers at PolitiFact. On Tuesday, however, there was a noticeable improvement in his factual accuracy. His comments on the 2015 murder rate and violent crime in Chicago were accurate, a point highlighted by the FBI's Uniform Crime Reporting program. He also cited a 116 percent premium increase in Arizona for ACA plans, a figure supported by a Department of Health and Human Services report.

While some interpretations of his remarks on immigration's economic impact or his credit for job announcements may be debated, Trump largely avoided factual missteps. As Glenn Thrush of The New York Times noted, the speech appeared to have undergone nominal fact-checking, a process potentially influenced by advice from communications director Sean Spicer.

Washington (CNN) President Donald Trump reached for poetry and conjured a vision of common national purpose Tuesday during his first address to Congress, shifting his tone from the dark, searing approach of his previous big speeches to the nation. Trump adopted a statesmanlike cadence, hitting notes of inspiration. For once, this most unorthodox of politicians struck a conventional presidential posture as he sought to stabilize his administration after a tumultuous five weeks in office. Though his language was more lofty and unifying than normal, Trump gave little quarter on the substance of his policies on issues ranging from trade, defense, immigration and counterterrorism. The result was a populist, nationalistic prescription that he said would yield "a new chapter of American greatness." "From now on, America will be empowered by our aspirations, not burdened by our fears, inspired by the future, not bound by failures of the past, and guided by a vision, not blinded by our doubts," Trump said, from the Speaker's rostrum in the House of Representatives. During a vitriolic campaign and a raucous start to his term, Trump has done little to reach beyond his base of deeply committed voters who revile the kind of political elites that the President was staring down as he spoke on Tuesday. But, beset by the lowest approval ratings of any new commander-in-chief of modern times, Trump made a palpable effort to court voters who didn't support him with an offer to lay down the battles of the past. In fact, his address ticked almost all the boxes of a traditional State of the Union style appearance. 'Embrace this renewal' "I am asking all citizens to embrace this renewal of the American spirit. I am asking all members of Congress to join me in dreaming big, and bold, and daring things for our country," Trump said. "I am asking everyone watching tonight to seize this moment. Believe in yourselves. Believe in your future. And believe, once more, in America." Hide Caption 22 of 22 It was an uplifting and unifying message that many Americans have rarely heard from Trump, who argued "the time for trivial fights is behind us." While Trump is not solely responsible for the coarsening of political life, his brash, Twitter-fueled approach has rocked the nation's politics. The question now is whether the President was previewing a new, more sober political persona or whether he will return to his old habits. The change in his tone was evident from the first moments of his speech when he condemned the recent spate of threats against Jewish community centers, vandalism at Jewish cemeteries and the shooting of two Indian men in Kansas. He said the violence was a reminder that "while we may be a nation divided on policies, we are a country that stands united in condemning hate and evil in all its very ugly forms." The comment followed heavy criticism of Trump for not addressing such violence. The new President entered the House chamber to thunderous applause and spoke of the "renewal of the American spirit." A senior White House official said Trump wrote the speech himself with input from almost every member of his presidential team of advisers. In the emotional high point of the speech, Trump turned to the first lady's box and acknowledged Carryn Owens, the widow of a US Navy Special operator, William "Ryan" Owens, who was killed in an anti-terror raid in Yemen in the first major military engagement of the new administration. "Ryan died as he lived: a warrior, and a hero -- battling against terrorism and securing our nation," Trump said as the House floor erupted in a prolonged standing ovation. Owens, with tears streaming down her face, looked to the Heavens and joined in the applause. Less explosive style welcomed His less explosive presentation style was welcomed by many lawmakers. JUST WATCHED McConnell: American's expect Obamacare repeal Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH McConnell: American's expect Obamacare repeal 01:10 "Donald Trump did indeed become presidential tonight, and I think we'll see that reflected in a higher approval rating," Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, R-Kentucky, said on CNN after the speech. "The Donald Trump I heard tonight was a lot more focused and disciplined and subdued, and it was a lot more uneventful in a good way," moderate House Republican Charlie Dent told CNN's Tom LoBianco. "There were not a lot of distractions tonight, this speech was much better than the inaugural speech." North Dakota Democratic Sen. Heidi Heitkamp, who is up for re-election next year in a state that Trump carried, said the President delivered a "very good speech." "It was delivered with a sense of 'this is who I am, this is what I want to accomplish' and I think the goals are great," she said. "How we get there is the $10,000 question." Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer said on CNN that Trump's "speeches and the realities are very, very far apart." "Until his reality catches up with his speeches, he's got big trouble," Schumer said. Though his rhetoric was soaring, Trump still struck hardline notes. He overruled national security adviser H.R. McMaster, according to a senior administration official, to warn of "radical Islamic terrorism." Hitting themes familiar from his campaign, Trump vowed to restore "integrity and the rule of law to our borders." "We will soon begin the construction of a great, great wall along our southern border," Trump said, drawing Republican cheers even as he didn't mention his earlier promise that Mexico would pay for construction. "As we speak tonight, we are removing gang members, drug dealers, and criminals that threaten our communities and prey on our very innocent citizens. Bad ones are going out as I speak, and as I promised throughout the campaign" Sending shockwaves through Washington While such language could please conservatives, Trump sent shockwaves through Washington earlier Tuesday by telling reporters he wants to pass an immigration reform bill that could grant legal status to millions of undocumented immigrants living in the US. "The time is right for an immigration bill as long as there is compromise on both sides," Trump said at the White House. But he did not provide further clarity on that position during his address. So far there is little sign that the new President's legislative agenda, which includes repealing and replacing Obamacare, a big tax overhaul, and a $1 trillion infrastructure program , is anywhere near coming to fruition. That explains why he devoted a considerable portion of the address to touting his achievements so far. He argued that his election alone had convinced big firms like Ford, Sprint, SoftBank and Intel to invest billions of job-creating dollars in the US. He noted that stocks have put on $3 trillion in value since his election and claimed to have saved hundreds of millions of taxpayer dollars, including on the new F-35 jet fighter. Trump said he also kept his word by cutting government regulations, clearing the way for the Keystone and Dakota Access pipelines and pulling out of the Trans Pacific Partnership trade deal. The President also laid down clear principles for the repeal and replacement of Obamacare, a key policy goal that is threatening to become overwhelmed by the complications of writing health policy. A new system, he said, must retain coverage for Americans with pre-existing conditions, should offer plans backed by tax credits and expanded health savings accounts and should preserve Medicaid expansion in the states. Trump also vowed to bring down the high price of drugs "immediately." 'Obamacare is collapsing' "Obamacare is collapsing -- and we must act decisively to protect all Americans," he said, "Action is not a choice -- it is a necessity." House Democratic Leader Nancy Pelosi, who was instrumental in passing the law, shook her head as Trump condemned it. The President also signaled action on another key piece of his agenda -- tax reform, promising "massive" relief for the middle classes and cuts in corporate tax. Yet Trump also pushed for his $1 trillion infrastructure plan and spoke of his effort to boost military spending. Given that he has also said he will protect entitlements, questions will be asked about how the administration can finance its ambitious plans. Trump's speech was closely watched around the world, given that his pronouncements on foreign policy have caused alarm and confusion. There were, however, few details on national security policy to clear up misconceptions. Trump recommitted himself to a strong "America First" foreign policy, but also backed NATO -- as long as its members pay their dues -- while bemoaning trillions of dollars the US has spent in foreign wars abroad. "My job is not to represent the world. My job is to represent the United States of America," said Trump. "But we know that America is better off when there is less conflict, not more. We must learn from the mistakes of the past. We have seen the war and the destruction that have ravaged and raged throughout the world." Despite some positive reviews for Trump in the room on Tuesday, the official Democratic response by former Kentucky Gov. Steven Beshear accused the President of deserting the working people who voted for him by picking a cabinet of millionaires and billionaires. "That's not being our champion. That's being Wall Street's champion," Beshear said. "Real leaders don't spread derision and division. Real leaders strengthen, they unify, they partner, and they offer real solutions instead of ultimatums and blame," said Beshear, accusing Trump of waging war on refugees and immigrants and endangering US security by reaching out to Russia.

During President Donald Trump's February 28, 2017, address to Congress, Democratic lawmakers used a variety of subtle, non-verbal cues to signal their disagreement with his policies and statements. While they avoided overt protests, like the "pussyhats" seen at the Women's March, their opposition was evident through understated actions.

These quiet displays included turning thumbs down, maintaining neutral expressions, and keeping hands folded. Democrats shared a moment of laughter when President Trump declared he would "drain the swamp." Conversely, audible groans came from their side of the chamber when he announced a new office to aid victims of crimes committed by immigrants.

Upon President Trump's arrival, several Democratic representatives intentionally did not shake his hand. Empty seats on the Democratic side also visually represented their dissent. For instance, Rep. Eliot Engel of New York chose not to sit in his usual center aisle seat, a spot he had occupied for nearly thirty years during presidential addresses.

In a symbolic move, Democratic women in the House wore white. This color choice honored the suffragist movement, highlighting their commitment to women's issues. This sartorial statement offered a different visual message than the "pussyhats." House Democratic leader Nancy Pelosi explained on Twitter that those wearing white supported women's rights, "in spite of a @POTUS who doesn't!"

Many Democratic senators and House members also used their invited guests in the galleries for a political statement. These guests often included individuals affected by President Trump's immigration policies or those who depended on the Affordable Care Act, a healthcare program President Trump opposed. President Trump seemed to subtly address the Democrats' dissent by gesturing toward them and saying, "the time for trivial fights is behind us."

Donald Trump's recent address signaled a notable change, widely seen as his most impactful speech yet, characterized by a more unifying and traditional style. This shift towards a more inclusive and presidential demeanor, previously elusive, was effectively demonstrated in this particular address.

The speech skillfully integrated a clear condemnation of prejudice, a hopeful perspective, and a call to the American story. Trump also thoughtfully featured individuals in the audience who evoked empathy and concluded with the strong assertion that "God created us equal." These elements, requiring no additional expense, enabled him to convey his central message in a way more suited to the presidency.

A significant question now arises: if Trump can sustain this more measured approach and decrease ongoing controversy, will his policies truly connect with voters across party lines? The strong backing from Republicans for points on protectionism and infrastructure investment was remarkable, highlighting the significant influence Trump has exerted on the party.

Despite the speech's favorable reception, questions remain about Trump's exact policy positions. He approached endorsing a border adjustment tax with caution, stopping short of a firm commitment. Likewise, while he mentioned a $1 trillion figure for infrastructure, he did not definitively confirm increased spending. His comments on replacing the Affordable Care Act were also indistinct, with support for a tax credit unlikely to substantially sway the congressional discussion.

Additional insights from the speech include a clear inclination to make ambitious promises, even within the usual scope of presidential discourse. Apart from the appointment of Gorsuch, themes favored by social conservatives were largely absent. While immigration was highlighted, its prominence as a top issue was doubted even by those who prioritize it. The political environment was reshaped by a Republican president discussing job recovery, leading Democrats to largely remain quiet. Trump appears to be a broad deregulator, and his comments on FDA reform were particularly well-received.

Ultimately, this speech likely served Trump well. However, his continued success will depend on his capacity to maintain this more balanced tone and show concrete advancements on his declared goals.

query: What significant milestone could Rashida Tlaib achieve if she wins the election in November?

documents.text: Rashida Tlaib Rashida Tlaib could become one of the nation’s first Muslim women in Congress if she wins in November. Former Michigan state legislator Rashida Tlaib won the Democratic primary on Tuesday for a U.S. House seat in the state’s 13th Congressional District. She beat out five other Democratic candidates seeking to fill the seat of Rep. John Conyers, who resigned in December amid allegations of sexual harassment. As the Democratic nominee in a district that has historically voted Democratic ― and with no Republican challengers ― Tlaib is all but guaranteed a seat in Congress in November’s general election. If she wins the seat, she could be one of the nation’s first Muslim women in Congress ― she may be joined in the historic feat by one or more other candidates also running in 2018. Tlaib will, however, be the only one to claim the status of the first Palestinian-American U.S. congresswoman. Breaking barriers will be nothing new to the Detroit-born mother of two, who already made history in 2008 as the first Muslim woman to serve in the Michigan Legislature. “People think it’s corny ― I do want to change the world, and I want people like me to have a seat at the table,” the daughter of Palestinian immigrants told HuffPost last month. Tlaib is part of a record wave of Muslim candidates running for office in 2018, alongside unprecedented ﻿numbers of women running since Donald Trump’s election ― and part of a movement of progressive candidates seeking to push the Democratic Party establishment further left. Rashida Tlaib Rashida Tlaib beat out five other﻿ Democratic candidates seeking to fill the seat of Rep. John Conyers, who resigned amid allegations of sexual harassment. Tlaib, who grew up the eldest of 14 children, is running on a progressive platform, including support of Medicare for All and a $15 per hour minimum wage. She was endorsed by the Justice Democrats ― the progressive political group that backed Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez in New York in her now-famous upset win. Like Ocasio-Cortez, Tlaib doesn’t accept money from corporate PACs, and she told HuffPost she’s “going to abolish ICE” ― a leftist stance that has gained traction among some leading Democrats since Ocasio-Cortez ran on that idea. Running in Michigan ― a state with a significant Muslim and Arab-American community ― Tlaib’s candidacy comes at a particularly trying time for Muslim Americans. Over the past couple years, the Muslim community in the United States has faced a spike in hate crimes amid Trump’s campaign, Islamophobic rhetoric and policies from the White House, and a Trump administration ﻿travel ban upheld by the Supreme Court that targets mostly Muslim-majority countries. “People are ready for someone like me,” Tlaib said.

The Ohio 12th District special election results have illuminated a surprisingly competitive contest, with Republicans narrowly maintaining a lead over Democrats. This race, initially projected as a comfortable Republican win, has instead highlighted significant Democratic enthusiasm as the party gears up for the November midterm elections. As of late Tuesday, Republican state Sen. Troy Balderson held a slender advantage over Democrat Danny O’Connor, with the margin hovering below one percent. With over 3,300 provisional ballots yet to be counted, the final outcome remains uncertain and may require several days for official confirmation. While Republicans may have narrowly avoided a significant electoral setback, the close nature of this election serves as a potent warning for the party as it strives to preserve its 23-seat majority in the House.

Concurrently, Tuesday's elections also showcased a robust performance by female candidates across a spectrum of contests, reinforcing the prevailing narrative that 2018 could indeed be the "Year of the Woman." The influence of President Trump's endorsements, alongside those from progressive figures such as Sen. Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, proved to be varied and their ultimate impact remained somewhat ambiguous.

Here are the key takeaways from Tuesday's elections:

**GOP Maintains a Lead, Yet Democrats Exhibit Encouraging Momentum**

Despite trailing in the Ohio 12th District special election, Democrat Danny O’Connor's strong showing underscores the formidable nature of the Democratic challenge. They have successfully transformed a district that has been reliably Republican-held for decades into a closely contested race, signaling potential difficulties for President Trump with suburban voters in this key Ohio district. O’Connor's campaign strategy capitalized on his youth, energetic grassroots outreach, and a deliberate approach focused on appealing to moderate voters without directly confronting President Trump. This tactical resemblance to the successful methods employed by Rep. Conor Lamb in a Pennsylvania special election earlier this year is noteworthy. Republicans attempted to leverage House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi as a central campaign theme, aiming to associate O’Connor with her. However, O’Connor largely managed to deflect these attacks and maintain a tight race, even after acknowledging in an MSNBC interview his eventual support for his party's leadership.

**Suburban Districts Pose a Growing Challenge for the GOP**

The Ohio race serves as a stark illustration of a mounting concern for Republicans: their diminishing standing in suburban districts. Democrats are actively targeting well-educated suburban voters, particularly women, who have historically leaned Republican but are now expressing palpable dissatisfaction with the current administration. These voters are actively seeking congressional representation that will provide a more effective check on presidential power. There are 68 other Republican-held seats considered more favorable to Democrats than Ohio's 12th District. Significantly, President Trump secured 45 of these seats with a smaller margin than he achieved in Ohio. An additional 23 seats are held by Republicans but were won by Hillary Clinton in the 2016 presidential election. Democrats are strategically concentrating their efforts on many of these districts, often situated in the suburbs surrounding major metropolitan areas, particularly within the Midwest and Rust Belt, as they strategize to regain control of the House.

**Trump's Electoral Impact Remains a Mixed Factor**

President Trump swiftly claimed credit for Balderson's narrow lead following a last-minute campaign rally. While his appearances can effectively galvanize the Republican base, their broader effectiveness in attracting moderate and suburban voters remains a subject of considerable debate. Furthermore, Trump's inclination to endorse more ideologically extreme candidates, sometimes even over the preferences of established Republican figures, raises pertinent questions about their general election viability. In Michigan, Trump's endorsement of military veteran John James in a Senate primary proved successful, with James winning his race. James presents a compelling narrative as a Black Republican candidate in a Senate currently lacking significant Black representation from the GOP. However, Trump's endorsement of conservative figure Kris Kobach in Kansas's gubernatorial primary over incumbent Gov. Jeff Colyer yielded a different outcome. While that race remained too close to call, Kobach could face a significant challenge in the general election, even within a traditionally Republican state.

**The Influence of Sanders and Ocasio-Cortez is Still Developing**

The primary campaigns of Sen. Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez garnered substantial attention. However, their definitive impact on general election results is not yet firmly established. In Michigan, their preferred gubernatorial candidate, Abdul El-Sayed, ultimately lost to Gretchen Whitmer. Similarly, in Missouri's 1st District, an Ocasio-Cortez-backed candidate, Cori Bush, failed to unseat the incumbent Rep. Lacy Clay. Sanders and Ocasio-Cortez have emerged as prominent voices for the progressive movement, and their influence on Democratic primaries has been closely observed. Nevertheless, persistent questions remain regarding whether their message will resonate effectively with voters in more rural and suburban areas, particularly within the Midwest. In a Democratic House primary in Kansas's 3rd District, another candidate supported by the duo, Brent Welder, held a slight lead early Wednesday, though the race had not yet been officially called. Both Welder and his challenger, Sharice Davids, campaigned on progressive platforms, presenting a key test for policies like "Medicare for All" in a competitive race against incumbent Rep. Kevin Yoder.

**Another Strong Performance by Female Candidates**

The "Year of the Woman" narrative continued to unfold robustly on Tuesday, with numerous female candidates securing significant primary victories. This trend was particularly evident in Michigan, where women achieved notable success across the ballot. Gretchen Whitmer secured a decisive win in the state's gubernatorial primary, decisively defeating two male opponents. In two competitive House races in Michigan, Democrats Gretchen Driskell and Elissa Slotkin won their primaries, positioning them as strong contenders against Republican incumbents Tim Walberg and Mike Bishop, respectively. In races to replace retiring Rep. David Trott, both the Democratic and Republican nominees are likely to be women. Lena Epstein, a staffer on Trump's Michigan campaign, held a lead in the Republican primary, while Haley Stevens, endorsed by Hillary Clinton, had a narrow lead in the Democratic primary. Furthermore, in the race to succeed former Rep. John Conyers Jr., former state Rep. Rashida Tlaib secured the Democratic nomination in a heavily Democratic district, making her highly likely to become the first Muslim woman elected to Congress. In Washington state, women also appeared poised for victories in two key House races.

The Missouri Senate race stands as a crucial battleground in the upcoming midterm elections, capturing national attention due to its potential impact on the balance of power in the U.S. Senate. This highly anticipated contest pits incumbent Democratic Senator Claire McCaskill against Republican challenger Josh Hawley, the current Missouri Attorney General. Both candidates have successfully navigated their respective primary elections, setting the stage for a fiercely competitive general election. The significance of this race is amplified by Missouri's recent electoral history, which has shown a clear trend towards the Republican Party in presidential contests. This was notably demonstrated in the 2016 presidential election, where Donald Trump secured a decisive victory in the state, winning by a substantial 20-point margin. This prevailing political climate places Senator McCaskill in a precarious position, making her one of the most vulnerable Democratic incumbents in the 2018 election cycle.

The Republican Party has strategically identified the unseating of Senator McCaskill as a top priority. McCaskill, who initially won her Senate seat in 2006 and was subsequently re-elected in 2012, faces a considerable challenge in a state that has increasingly leaned Republican. The GOP is largely rallying behind Josh Hawley, a 38-year-old conservative who currently holds the office of Missouri Attorney General. Hawley has been actively emphasizing his conservative credentials throughout his campaign. However, certain public statements he has made have drawn considerable attention and have played a role in shaping his public image. One such statement involved a recent assertion connecting the sexual liberation movements of the 1960s and 1970s to the issue of sex trafficking.

In an effort to prevent a repeat of the 2012 election, where the Republican candidate Todd Akin's campaign was derailed by controversial remarks, the party views Hawley as a strong contender. His academic background, having graduated from Stanford and Yale, is considered a valuable asset. The Missouri Republican Party has demonstrated robust support for Hawley, even permitting the Republican National Committee to channel campaign funds to him prior to his primary victory. Nevertheless, Hawley's campaign has encountered its share of obstacles. These include the lingering effects of the scandal-plagued resignation of former Governor Eric Greitens. Furthermore, reports have indicated internal friction within the conservative base, stemming from concerns about Hawley's perceived slow fundraising pace and his relatively low public profile. These issues have led to apprehension among national Republicans regarding a potential opportunity for Senator McCaskill to capitalize.

As of mid-July, Hawley's campaign reported having $2.3 million in cash reserves. This figure represents only one-third of the available funds held by Senator McCaskill, highlighting a significant financial disparity between the two campaigns. As the general election campaign gains momentum, Hawley has initiated targeted attacks against McCaskill. These attacks have focused on criticizing her opposition to Brett Kavanaugh's nomination to the Supreme Court, with the aim of portraying her as too liberal for the state of Missouri. Political analysts widely agree that the outcome of this particular Senate race will have substantial implications for the overall balance of power within the U.S. Senate. The race is therefore being closely watched by political observers across the nation.

The Republican primary for Kansas governor was a closely contested election, with Secretary of State Kris Kobach and Governor Jeff Colyer in a very tight race. As votes were counted, the candidates remained neck and neck, each securing around 40% of the vote. The final outcome was uncertain due to a substantial number of uncounted ballots from Johnson County.

This contest attracted significant national attention, partly due to President Trump's endorsement of Kobach. This support went against the Republican Governors Association's preference to remain neutral in the primary. Kobach's national visibility, often associated with contentious policy positions, raised questions about his viability in a general election.

Several elements shaped this primary:

*   **Kansas's Republican Leanings:** The state has a strong history of supporting Republican candidates. In 2016, Donald Trump won Kansas by a considerable margin, indicating a clear preference for Republican leadership in statewide races.

*   **Democratic Opportunity:** The Democratic Party perceived an opportunity. Recent demonstrations concerning education funding may have mobilized voters against the incumbent Republican leadership, potentially shifting Kansas's typical party advantage.

*   **Kobach's Divisive Record:** Kobach's advocacy for unsubstantiated claims of widespread voter fraud from the 2016 election, coupled with his firm stances on immigration, presented challenges. These views risked alienating moderate Republicans, especially in key suburban areas around Kansas City and Wichita.

Voters cast their ballots among an array of electronic voting machines in a polling station at the Noor Islamic Cultural Center, Tuesday, Aug. 7, 2018, in Dublin, Ohio. Two-term state Sen. Troy Balderson,... (Associated Press) Voters cast their ballots among an array of electronic voting machines in a polling station at the Noor Islamic Cultural Center, Tuesday, Aug. 7, 2018, in Dublin, Ohio. Two-term state Sen. Troy Balderson, is fighting off a strong challenge from Democrat Danny O'Connor, a 31-year-old county official,... (Associated Press) WESTERVILLE, Ohio (AP) — The Latest on primaries in Missouri, Kansas, Michigan and Washington state and a congressional special election in Ohio (all times local): 12:30 a.m. A Washington state representative who resigned his chairmanship following allegations of inappropriate conduct is in third place in early primary returns. Democratic Rep. David Sawyer on Tuesday trailed Democrat Melanie Morgan and Republican Terry Harder. Under Washington's primary system, the top two vote-getters advance to November, regardless of party. Sawyer represents a district that includes parts of Tacoma. In June, Sawyer resigned as the chairman of the House Commerce and Gaming Committee a day after an outside investigation found he violated the chamber's policies on harassment, decorum and ethics. Sawyer apologized and said in an email at the time that it was "clear that I messed up and that it's time for me to acknowledge some personal mistakes." ___ 12:25 a.m. A Kansas official says long lines at polling places delayed election results in the state's most populous county as Gov. Jeff Colyer and Secretary of State Kris Kobach were locked in a tight race for the Republican nomination for governor. State elections director Bryan Caskey said Tuesday night that some polling places in Johnson County in the suburbs of Kansas City remained open until about 8 p.m. to accommodate people who were in line to vote when polls officially closed at 7 p.m. He says that led local officials to delay reporting their first results, from votes cast in advance. Johnson County has nearly 408,000 registered voters, or almost 23 percent of the state's total of 1.8 million. ___ 11:45 p.m. A black Ferguson city councilman leads longtime St. Louis County prosecutor Bob McCulloch in a contest some see as a referendum on McCulloch's handling of the investigation into Michael Brown's death. With the vote counted from nearly 90 percent of precincts, the county reported Wesley Bell leading the 67-year-old McCulloch by a 55 percent to 45 percent margin in Tuesday's Democratic primary. No Republicans are running for prosecutor. McCulloch, who is white, is seeking an eighth term. Bell is a 43-year-old attorney and former municipal judge and prosecutor. He was elected councilman in 2015 as protests raged over Brown's death. Brown, an unarmed black 18-year-old, died Aug. 9, 2014, in a street confrontation with white officer Darren Wilson. A St. Louis County grand jury declined to indict Wilson, who later resigned. Some critics accused McCulloch of skewing the investigation in favor of the officer. ___ 11:40 p.m. Democrat Danny O'Connor is trying to rally his supporters as he's locked in a close race with Republican Troy Balderson in Ohio's congressional special election. O'Connor says, "We're not stopping now." The candidates were locked in a razor-thin contest Tuesday night. There were at least 3,367 provisional ballots left to be reviewed. That's enough for O'Connor to potentially pick up enough votes to force a recount. The Associated Press does not declare winners in races subject to an automatic recount. Balderson, meanwhile, was celebrating, saying he's ready to get to work in Congress. He says, "America is on the right path and we're going to keep it going that way." The winner takes the seat previously held by Republican Pat Tiberi, who resigned in January to take another job. ___ 11:35 p.m. President Donald Trump is already taking credit for helping elect his favored candidate in a special House election in Ohio, though the race is too close to call. In a tweet Tuesday night, Trump declared "a great victory" for Republican Troy Balderson over Democrat Danny O'Connor. While Balderson has a slight lead, the race is close enough that there could be a mandatory recount. There are also at least 3,367 provisional ballots left to be reviewed. Trump says Balderson had been far behind in early voting before he hosted a rally for the candidate Saturday night in the suburban Columbus district. The president says that after his speech "there was a big turn for the better." Trump offered another prediction — that Balderson will "win BIG" in November's general election. ___ 11:30 p.m. Republican Susan Hutchison has gained a top-two finish in Washington state's primary to advance to the November general election. The former television newscaster and GOP state party chairwoman was finishing second in the voting in Tuesday's contest. Under Washington's primary system, the top two vote-getters go on to November, regardless of party. Hutchison is the former state Republican Party chairwoman. She is making a longshot bid to oust the Democratic incumbent, Sen. Maria Cantwell, who is seeking a fourth term. ___ 11:25 p.m. Sen. Maria Cantwell has advanced to the November ballot in Washington's primary election. The Democrat seeking her fourth term easily outpaced all other candidates. Under Washington's primary system, the top two vote-getters go on to November, regardless of party. Cantwell is Washington's junior senator and the ranking member of the Energy and Natural Resources Committee. Cantwell will likely face Republican Susan Hutchison in November. Hutchison is the former state Republican Party chairwoman. ___ 11:20 p.m. The last special election of the year for the U.S. House is too close to call. With election officials in Ohio done counting Tuesday night, Republican Troy Balderson has a slight lead over Democrat Danny O'Connor in the special election in the state's 12th Congressional District. But there are at least 3,367 provisional ballots left to be reviewed. That's enough for O'Connor to potentially pick up enough votes to force a mandatory recount. The Associated Press does not declare winners in races that go to an automatic recount. The race in suburban Columbus was one of the most-watched contests in Tuesday's primaries as O'Connor tried to pick up a seat long held by Republicans. ___ 11 p.m. The deadline has passed for turning in primary ballots in Washington, a vote-by-mail state. The contest getting the most attention is a U.S. House seat Democrats hope to capture in November for the first time since the district east of Seattle was created in 1980. Voters began receiving their state primary ballots in the mail weeks ago, and Tuesday was the last day to get them in or postmarked for mail delivery. In some of the more competitive races, results may not be known for days as most counties will update vote counts only once a day. In Washington's 8th District, Republican U.S. Rep. Dave Reichert is retiring after more than a decade. Republican Dino Rossi, a former state senator, is expected to advance along with one of three Democrats. ___ 10:40 p.m. Missouri voters have rejected a right-to-work law banning mandatory union fees in workplace contracts. The vote Tuesday marked a major victory for unions, which poured millions of dollars into a campaign to defeat Proposition A. The right-to-work law originally was enacted in 2017 by Missouri's Republican-led Legislature and governor. But it never took effect, because unions gathered enough petition signatures to force a public referendum on it. Unions argued the measure would have led to lower wages, while business groups claimed it could have led to more jobs. Economic studies showed mixed and sometimes conflicting results. Twenty-seven other states have similar laws against compulsory union fees, including five Republican-led states that have acted since 2012 — Indiana, Michigan, Wisconsin, West Virginia and Kentucky. ___ 10:20 p.m. Kansas Secretary of State Kris Kobach (KOH'-bahk) and Gov. Jeff Colyer are locked in a close Republican primary race for governor. Colyer and Kobach topped a seven-candidate GOP field in Tuesday's election. President Donald Trump tweeted a full endorsement of Kobach on Monday. Kobach is nationally known for advocating tough policies on illegal immigration and strict voter identification laws. He served as vice chairman of Trump's now-disbanded commission on election fraud after advising Trump's 2016 campaign and the White House. Colyer had endorsements from Kansas political icon Bob Dole and the National Rifle Association in his quest to remain in office after becoming governor in January. In the Democratic primary, state Sen. Laura Kelly of Topeka held a lead over former Wichita Mayor Carl Brewer and ex-Kansas Agriculture Secretary Joshua Svaty. ___ 10:15 p.m. A veteran Kansas legislator has won the Democratic primary for governor after stressing her Statehouse experience and fending off questions about her voting record. State Sen. Laura Kelly of Topeka defeated former Wichita Mayor Carl Brewer, former Kansas Agriculture Secretary Joshua Svaty and two other candidates Tuesday. The 68-year-old Kelly has served 14 years in the Senate and is the top Democrat on the budget committee. She stressed those credentials in running and suggested that she was best able to fix problems created by Republican policies. She faced criticism from Svaty and Brewer for votes she made in representing a GOP-leaning district for looser gun laws and for some of the nation's toughest voter identification requirements. But she also had the backing of former two-term Democratic Gov. Kathleen Sebelius. ___ 10:05 p.m. Detroit-area businessman and Iraq War veteran John James has won the Republican nomination to run against Democratic Sen. Debbie Stabenow this fall. James is a political newcomer endorsed by President Donald Trump. He defeated Sandy Pensler in Tuesday's primary. Stabenow is seeking a fourth Senate term and has easily fended off past challengers, but Trump's narrow 2016 victory in Michigan has the GOP hopeful it can flip the seat. The 37-year-old James is an executive at his family's automotive logistics companies and a West Point graduate who flew helicopters during the Iraq War. He is Michigan's first black Republican nominee for a major statewide office in more than three decades, but he has said he only wants to be assessed on his character. ___ 9:55 p.m. Gretchen Whitmer has won the Democratic nomination for Michigan governor, besting two competitors to advance to the November race to succeed term-limited Republican Gov. Rick Snyder. Whitmer, a former legislative leader, defeated chemical-testing businessman Shri Thanedar (shree TAN'-eh-dahr) and ex-Detroit health director Abdul El-Sayed in Tuesday's primary. She will face Attorney General Bill Schuette (SHOOT'-ee), who defeated Lt. Gov. Brian Calley, state Sen. Patrick Colbeck and Dr. Jim Hines in the GOP primary. Whitmer was considered the favorite because of her political experience and broad support from organized labor and other key groups. El-Sayed especially courted the party's more liberal, pro-Bernie Sanders wing, as did Thanedar, who spent millions of dollars of his own money on his campaign. The Michigan governorship is a top target for the Democratic Party. ___ 9:50 p.m. U.S. Rep. Roger Marshall has won the Republican primary in the sprawling rural 1st District of western and central Kansas. He advances to a November matchup against Democrat Alan LaPolice of Clyde in the heavily Republican agricultural district. Marshall, a Great Bend physician, first gained national attention in 2016 for knocking off then Rep. Tim Huelskamp in the Republican primary for the seat. Democrats did not have a candidate in 2016, but LaPolice launched a long-shot bid as an independent. LaPolice, an educator, is taking another shot at it this year running as a Democrat. While the district strongly supported President Donald Trump in 2016, some worry tougher immigration policies make it harder to fill agricultural jobs. Marshall wants to couple border-security measures with changes in visas for guest farm workers. ___ 9:30 p.m. Missouri Attorney General Josh Hawley has won the Republican nomination in one of the nation's most hotly contested Senate races. Voters on Tuesday picked Hawley as expected over 10 other GOP challengers in the race for Democrat Claire McCaskill's seat. He was the only candidate to have previously won a statewide election and had considerably more money than the other Republicans in the field. He's backed by President Donald Trump. Republicans are eyeing the now-Democratic seat as a prime pickup opportunity in a state Trump won by nearly 19 points. A McCaskill-Hawley matchup is expected to be one of the nation's top showdowns. McCaskill is running as a moderate in the red state. Hawley is campaigning largely on support for Trump. He is attempting to paint his rival as a liberal obstructionist. ___ 9:25 p.m. Sen. Claire McCaskill of Missouri has won the Democratic primary in her campaign for a third term. Voters picked McCaskill on Tuesday as expected over six other Democratic challengers. Republicans are eyeing the Democratic seat in a state that President Donald Trump won by nearly 19 points in 2016. Missouri Attorney General Josh Hawley was favored in an 11-candidate Republican primary for the right to face McCaskill in November. A McCaskill-Hawley matchup is expected to be one of the most hotly contested races in the nation. McCaskill is running as a moderate in the red state. Hawley is campaigning largely on support for Trump and is attempting to paint his rival as a liberal obstructionist. ___ 9:20 p.m. State Attorney General Bill Schuette (SHOOT'-ee) has won the Republican nomination for Michigan governor, defeating three other candidates vying to succeed term-limited GOP Gov. Rick Snyder. Schuette beat Lt. Gov. Brian Calley, state Sen. Patrick Colbeck and Dr. Jim Hines on Tuesday. Democrats running were former legislative leader Gretchen Whitmer, ex-Detroit health director Abdul El-Sayed and entrepreneur Shri Thanedar. Schuette, a former congressman, state lawmaker and appellate judge, was endorsed by President Donald Trump. He says he would cut the state income tax, like Trump reduced federal taxes. The Michigan governorship is a top target for the Democratic Party, which is eager to make gains in the Midwest, where Republicans have dominated state governments and which helped President Donald Trump take the White House in 2016. ___ 9 p.m. Polls have closed in Kansas and Michigan primary elections that are testing anew President Donald Trump's political clout. Tuesday's races, like dozens before them, are pitting the strength of Trump's fiery supporters against the Democratic Party's anti-Trump resistance. The results will help determine the political landscape before the GOP defends its congressional majorities across the nation. One of the top races is in Kansas, where Secretary of State Kris Kobach (KOH'-bahk) is trying to unseat Gov. Jeff Colyer. Should Kobach win the primary, some Republican operatives fear he could lose the governor's seat to Democrats this fall. In Michigan, three mainstream Democrats are among those vying for a chance at retiring Republican Rep. Dave Trott's seat. The field includes Fayrouz Saad (fay-ROOZ' sahd), who would be the first Muslim woman in Congress. ___ 8:10 p.m. Democrat Danny O'Connor has jumped out to a significant lead over Republican Troy Balderson in early returns in Ohio's congressional special election. Voters on Tuesday were choosing between Balderson, a state senator, and O'Connor, the Franklin County recorder, to complete the term of a Republican who retired in January. The race is a test of voter sentiment before the general election in November, when Balderson and O'Connor will battle again for the full two-year term. President Donald Trump campaigned for Balderson, arguing Republicans need to control Congress and casting the midterms as a referendum on himself. A Balderson victory would buoy Republicans concerned about how Trump might be playing in political battleground states. An O'Connor win would elate Democrats hoping for a Trump backlash. ___ 8 p.m. Polls have closed in Missouri and are beginning to close in parts of Kansas and Michigan on a night of primary elections. Tuesday's races, like dozens before them, pitted the strength of President Donald Trump's fiery supporters against the Democratic Party's anti-Trump resistance. The results will help determine the political landscape — and Trump's standing within his own party — just three months before the GOP defends its House and Senate majorities across the nation. One of the top races of the night is in Kansas, where Secretary of State Kris Kobach (KOH'-bahk) was trying to unseat Gov. Jeff Colyer. Kobach has been backed by the president. Should the polarizing Kobach win the primary, some Republican operatives fear he could lose the governor's seat to Democrats this fall. ___ 7:30 p.m. The polls have closed in Ohio, where a Republican state senator backed by President Donald Trump is competing with a Democratic county official in a special election for an open U.S. House seat long held by the GOP. Voters Tuesday were choosing between Sen. Troy Balderson and Franklin County Recorder Danny O'Connor to complete the term of a Republican who retired in January. The race is a test of voter sentiment before the general election in November, when Balderson and O'Connor will battle again for the full two-year term. Trump campaigned for Balderson, arguing Republicans need to control Congress and casting the midterms as a referendum on himself. A Balderson victory would buoy Republicans concerned about how Trump might be playing in political battleground states. An O'Connor win would elate Democrats hoping for a Trump backlash. ___ 7:15 p.m. A St. Louis County election official blames a "comedy of errors" for some voters being turned away in one Missouri town. The St. Louis Post-Dispatch reports that turnout in the St. Louis region for Tuesday's primary election was brisk, with about 35 percent of registered voters going to the polls. But there were sporadic problems, most notably at Holman Elementary School in Berkeley. Democratic elections director Eric Fey says a Republican poll worker had to leave because of a family emergency. The other two went to lunch, apparently got lost and didn't return. It was unclear how many people were turned away during the 45 minutes it took to get additional Republican poll workers to the polling place. Elsewhere in St. Louis County, some poll workers mistakenly gave voters nonpartisan ballots that included only issues, not party primary races. ___ 6:20 p.m. Battleground Ohio is deciding the year's final special election, a congressional faceoff that tests anew President Donald Trump's political clout and the appeal of his signature tax cuts. Seventy-one-year-old retiree Gregg Schirra voted for Hillary Clinton in 2016 and Democrat Danny O'Connor on Tuesday in Westerville. He says, "We gave Trump a fair chance and now it's time to get the legislature working and get back on track." At Delaware Township Hall north of Columbus, 71-year-old retiree Katheryn Terry voted for Republican Troy Balderson, saying she felt the 31-year-old O'Connor was just too young and she wasn't happy with anything about his candidacy. She says: "I'm trying to give Trump the benefit of the doubt, and so I'm trying to be as supportive as I can, because things have turned around."

Michigan's 2018 gubernatorial primary results are official, setting the stage for a major contest between Republican nominee Bill Schuette and Democratic contender Gretchen Whitmer. Both secured their party's nominations by Tuesday's deadline, paving the way for the general election on November 6th. This election will decide the future leadership of Michigan.

Following spirited primary campaigns, both Schuette and Whitmer reached out to their former rivals, stressing party unity. This sentiment was echoed by second-place finishers: Republican Lieutenant Governor Brian Calley and former Detroit health director Dr. Abdul El-Sayed. Early signs pointed to strong voter turnout across the state. Ballot shortages were reported in areas like Oakland County, prompting the Michigan Secretary of State's Office to advise voters to stay at their polling places to ensure their ballots were counted.

Here's a breakdown of the primary results:

*   **Democratic Primary:**
    *   Gretchen Whitmer: 52%
    *   Dr. Abdul El-Sayed: 30%
    *   Shri Thanedar: 18%
*   **Republican Primary:**
    *   Bill Schuette: 51%
    *   Brian Calley: 25%
    *   Senator Patrick Colbeck: 13%
    *   Dr. Jim Hines: 11%

Schuette, the current Michigan Attorney General, declared his victory in Midland, stating, "I am proud to be your Republican nominee for governor. Michigan, it's time to win again." He quickly framed Whitmer as a "tax-and-spend liberal" whose policies would harm the state's economy.

In Detroit, Whitmer spoke of rejecting "policies of division" and restoring a Democratic governor. She highlighted key issues:
*   Improving public education
*   Addressing the Flint water crisis
*   Investing in aging infrastructure

Whitmer stated, "As a child, I never once worried about literacy or drinking water and infrastructure, but our leaders have failed us."

Political consultant Ed Sarpolus noted that both Whitmer and Schuette face the challenge of distinguishing themselves from past candidates and party legacies. He advised Whitmer to "run as herself" to avoid being seen as a successor to former Governor Jennifer Granholm or a parallel to national figures like Hillary Clinton. Sarpolus also suggested Schuette carefully manage his relationship with President Donald Trump and Governor Rick Snyder to appeal to a wider electorate.

The primaries previewed the general election battle. Schuette labeled Whitmer a "Granholm clone," warning of a return to the "lost decade" of the 2000s. Whitmer portrayed Schuette as beholden to special interests, suggesting he would not champion middle-class voters.

Michigan Republican Party Chairman Ronald Weiser expressed confidence in Schuette, saying, "He will lead our state into another decade of prosperity." Democratic Governors Association Chairman Jay Inslee called Michigan a "top target for pickup this cycle" and praised Whitmer as a "terrific candidate."

The Republican primary featured sharp exchanges. Brian Calley criticized Schuette for alleged ethical lapses, including hiring Republican activists for civil service jobs and using state resources for political purposes. Calley's campaign focused on Governor Rick Snyder's record of reducing business taxes and regulations. In his concession, Calley stated, "we took our shot" and "we left everything out there on the field."

Schuette's campaign, advised by Stu Sandler, attacked Calley's past involvement with a failed ballot initiative for a part-time legislature and his role in creating the Michigan Business Tax. Schuette emphasized his experience as a congressman, state department director, state senator, and appellate court judge, advocating for economic growth and lower personal income tax.

Both parties are planning "unity rallies." Vice President Mike Pence will attend a Republican event in Grand Rapids, while Democrats will hold a rally in Detroit.

In the Democratic primary, Whitmer, an East Lansing attorney, secured endorsements from unions and the Detroit Regional Chamber, positioning her as the establishment choice. She focused on her track record, including expanding Medicaid under the Affordable Care Act, rather than advocating for single-payer healthcare.

Dr. Abdul El-Sayed was the progressive frontrunner, endorsed by figures like Senator Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. He gained national media attention and drew large crowds. In his concession, El-Sayed endorsed Whitmer and called for Democratic unity.

Shri Thanedar, a scientist and entrepreneur, ran an extensive campaign with significant personal investment and TV advertising. He supported progressive policies like single-payer healthcare and universal pre-school. Despite scrutiny over past support for Republican candidates, he pledged, "Absolutely, a Democrat must win the governor’s office in 2018."

Whitmer welcomed El-Sayed and Thanedar, stating, "I extend a hand out to them, there is a place for you as we move forward. I appreciate your energy and ideas — let’s work together to get this done." Detroit Mayor Mike Duggan cited Whitmer's "leadership in 2013" and her role in the Healthy Michigan plan as reasons for his support.

In the Libertarian race, Bill Gelineau won with 58% of the vote, defeating John Tatar.

Preliminary data suggests voter turnout in the 2018 primary may have exceeded the previous midterm primary record of 23.3% set in 2002. The significant increase in absentee ballots supports this trend:
*   694,129 absentee ballots sent out (a 53% rise)
*   527,050 absentee ballots returned (a 50% rise)

This heightened engagement signals strong interest in the gubernatorial contest.

The August 2018 special elections served as a critical barometer of the political climate, with particular attention focused on battleground Ohio and deep-red Kansas. These contests, marked by significant campaign spending, offered a crucial test of President Donald Trump's influence and the growing momentum of the Democratic Party's anti-Trump movement.

In Ohio's 12th Congressional District, the race between Republican Troy Balderson and Democrat Danny O'Connor was exceptionally close, with the Associated Press unable to declare a winner due to the possibility of a recount. Despite the tight margin, President Trump declared victory, attributing Balderson's performance to his endorsement. O'Connor, a 31-year-old county official, pledged to continue his challenge through the November general election, setting the stage for a spirited rematch. This district, long held by Republicans, showed a notable shift in voter sentiment, with Democratic candidates performing more strongly than in prior elections. Data indicated that 79 House races in the upcoming fall elections were considered more competitive than this Ohio district, based on Trump's 2016 performance. O'Connor's vote total significantly exceeded that of his Republican predecessor's Democratic opponent in 2014, while Balderson's vote count was lower than the previous Republican incumbent's. The two candidates are slated to compete again in November for the seat in the 119th Congress.

The Ohio contest underscored the district's evolving demographics, often characterized as a "Whole Foods" district with a more affluent and educated electorate than typical Trump strongholds. The race also became a referendum on Trump's tax cuts, with O'Connor criticizing the plan as benefiting the wealthy and potentially harming Medicare and Social Security. Balderson and his supporters countered by focusing on criticism of House Democrat Nancy Pelosi. Campaign finance reports revealed a substantial difference in local advertising spending, with O'Connor's campaign investing $2.25 million compared to Balderson's $507,000. However, Republican campaign committees and allied super PACs collectively spent over $4 million to support Balderson.

Meanwhile, Kansas experienced a heated gubernatorial primary. Secretary of State Kris Kobach, who had Trump's endorsement, was in a close race with incumbent Republican Governor Jeff Colyer. Some Republican operatives expressed concern that a Kobach victory could jeopardize the governorship for the GOP in the general election, especially with the potential for independent candidate Greg Orman to enter the race. Trump had publicly endorsed Kobach, tweeting, "He is a fantastic guy who loves his State and our Country - he will be a GREAT Governor and has my full & total Endorsement! Strong on Crime, Border & Military."

In other races, the Democratic primary in Kansas's 3rd Congressional District saw a competitive field vying to challenge Republican Rep. Kevin Yoder. Labor lawyer Brent Welder campaigned with prominent figures like Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders and New York congressional candidate Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. Also competing were Native American attorney Sharice Davids and former teacher Tom Niermann.

Beyond Ohio and Kansas, these elections set the stage for several other key contests. In Michigan, three mainstream Democrats competed for the seat of retiring Republican Rep. Dave Trott, including Fayrouz Saad, who aimed to become the first Muslim woman in Congress. In suburban Seattle, Democrats vied for the seat of another retiring Republican, Rep. Dave Reichert. Senate races also took shape, with Democratic Sen. Claire McCaskill securing her party's nomination in Missouri against Republican Josh Hawley, and in Michigan, Democratic Sen. Debbie Stabenow preparing to face military veteran and business executive John James, who won the Republican nomination. James's potential victory could make him one of the few Black Republican senators. President Trump had also weighed in on James's candidacy, calling him "a potential Republican star."

These special elections, occurring in the lead-up to the midterm elections, provided early insights into voter sentiment and the effectiveness of campaign strategies, particularly concerning President Trump's influence and the Democratic Party's mobilization efforts.

query: What was Sen. Ted Cruz's response when asked if he would support Sen. Mitch McConnell as Senate leader?

documents.text:
Following the Republican takeover of the Senate and John Boehner's expanded House majority, the GOP is poised to demonstrate its governing capabilities. Prior to the election, Republican strategists and lawmakers outlined a plan to secure early legislative wins before the 2015 presidential election cycle intensifies. This strategy, championed by Boehner and other leaders, aimed to pivot from crisis management to a proactive agenda.

A primary objective is to pass a comprehensive funding bill that would sustain government operations through most of the following year. This would clear legislative bandwidth for other priorities, such as a Republican-authored budget and a long-term highway infrastructure bill. Congressional activity is slated to resume in earnest after Thanksgiving, with both parties focusing on organizing for the new partisan landscape in Washington. Capitol Hill is expected to become fully operational by December 1st, marking the potential for significant legislative action.

Republicans are keen to avoid fiscal confrontations in early 2015. Consequently, they intend to utilize the initial weeks of December to enact a long-term spending measure, ensuring government continuity through the majority of the next year. Government funding is set to expire on December 11th, coinciding with the expiration of the authority for President Barack Obama's administration to train and equip moderate Syrian rebels in their fight against the Islamic State. This specific provision could introduce complexities to the passage of a smooth spending bill.

Beyond appropriations, Congress will also aim to address a backlog of expired targeted business tax provisions and renew a federal program designed to protect insurance companies against terrorist attacks. Furthermore, depending on the Senate's final composition, Democrats may seek to confirm a new Attorney General in the closing months of 2014, following Eric Holder's anticipated retirement. The overarching hope is that this concentrated legislative push will free up the calendar for the first half of 2015, enabling Congress to tackle a long-term highway and infrastructure bill, raise the debt limit, and pass a budget.

With Republican control of both chambers, the budget process presents a significant opportunity for the party to influence the Affordable Care Act. This is particularly true if Senate Republicans employ budget reconciliation, a procedure requiring only 51 votes. Congress is also expected to address the Sustainable Growth Rate, a contentious formula dictating Medicare reimbursements to physicians. However, the legislative landscape could be complicated by GOP Senators Ted Cruz of Texas and Mike Lee of Utah, who have publicly committed to obstructing any legislation deemed non-essential during the lame-duck session. While senior aides acknowledge the potential for shifting priorities, it is clear that major initiatives such as immigration reform, tax reform, or large-scale fiscal agreements will not be addressed during this period.

Equally crucial to year-end legislating are the internal party meetings scheduled to occur. A Republican Senate majority will need to establish committee structures and determine the ratio of Democrats to Republicans on these panels. A notable shift will be the appointment of new GOP committee chairmen, many of whom are expected to challenge the Obama administration's agenda. Senator Jim Inhofe of Oklahoma, a vocal critic of the administration's energy policies, is anticipated to chair the Environment and Public Works Committee. Senator John McCain of Arizona is likely to lead the Senate Armed Services Committee, a prominent platform for his critiques of Obama's foreign policy. Senator Jeff Sessions of Alabama, known for his conservative stance, is in line to chair the Senate Budget Committee.

The expanded Republican majority in the House will necessitate considerable organizational adjustments on Capitol Hill. New members will convene for orientation and leadership elections on November 12th, including the selection of new Democratic and Republican chairmen for Senate campaign committees. The House will also elect new chairmen for its key committees. Representative Paul Ryan of Wisconsin is set to lead the Ways and Means Committee, which is preparing to revise the tax code. Representative Mac Thornberry of Texas, a seasoned legislator, will take the helm of the Armed Services Committee. The Oversight and Government Reform Committee, Congress's primary investigative body, will see new leadership as Representative Darrell Issa (R-Calif.) reaches his term limit. Representatives Jason Chaffetz of Utah and Mike Turner of Ohio are considered leading candidates for this position. One chairmanship that is not expected to face a competitive challenge is that of the National Republican Congressional Committee. Despite some House Republicans expressing dissatisfaction with Representative Greg Walden's leadership during the fall, the election results are anticipated to quell these concerns.

Following the Republican Senate victory on Tuesday night, Texas Senator Ted Cruz declared the "era of Obama lawlessness is over." Speaking to supporters in Austin, Cruz emphasized the significant responsibility Republicans now hold to govern effectively, especially if they achieve a national majority.

He stated, "If and when we retake the majority nationally, it will be incumbent on Republicans to lead." Cruz outlined a Republican vision centered on presenting a "bold positive, optimistic agenda to turn this country around, to bring back jobs and growth and opportunity."

Cruz further remarked, "The American people have risen up and frankly, at the national level, they have given Republicans another chance."

The senator, often cited as a potential 2016 presidential candidate, detailed a broad conservative platform for the upcoming congressional session. His proposals included significant changes to tax structures and regulatory policies.

He also advocated for an end to what he termed "amnesty" for undocumented immigrants. Additionally, Cruz stressed the vital importance of protecting religious freedoms and upholding the Second Amendment right to bear arms.

A primary objective for the senator was the determined repeal of the Affordable Care Act, commonly known as ObamaCare. Cruz urged Republicans to "do everything humanly possible to repeal ObamaCare."

The 2014 U.S. midterm elections significantly reshaped the political landscape, most notably with the Republican Party achieving a majority in the Senate. This victory fulfilled their objective of controlling the Senate, requiring a net gain of six seats. While final tallies in some states, like Alaska, were pending, the overarching Republican goal was realized. Early successes, such as Cory Gardner's win in Colorado, signaled a competitive election, but the results ultimately underscored a widespread Republican triumph across the nation.

Several pivotal Senate races cemented the GOP's control. In Kansas, Senator Pat Roberts successfully defended his seat against independent challenger Greg Orman. North Carolina's House Speaker, Thom Tillis, unseated incumbent Democrat Kay Hagan. The Virginia Senate race was particularly noteworthy; had a third-party candidate not been a factor, Ed Gillespie might have defeated incumbent Democrat Mark Warner.

Democrats faced a difficult electoral environment in 2014, with many Senate contests concentrated in former Confederate states. The historical trend of the party holding the presidency experiencing losses in midterm elections also adversely affected Democrats. They lost seats even in states previously considered Democratic strongholds. The election also saw Republicans win governorships in key states, with Maryland electing a Republican governor for the first time since 1969, and Rick Scott retaining the Florida governorship for the GOP.

With Republicans leading Congress for the first time in a decade, attention turned to how they would exercise their legislative authority. Mitch McConnell, set to become Senate Majority Leader, voiced a desire for collaboration, stating, "Just because we have a two-party system doesn't mean we have to be in perpetual conflict." However, the political realities of Washington suggested that conflict might persist among certain factions.

Kentucky's other Senator, Rand Paul, offered a different perspective, indicating that a Republican Senate would aim to counter the President's agenda, including efforts to repeal the Affordable Care Act. Despite this sentiment, McConnell's influence would be limited. The projected Republican majority of 53 or 54 seats fell short of the 60 votes needed to overcome a filibuster, meaning Democrats could still impede or shape the Republican legislative agenda.

Internal dynamics within the new Republican Senate could also pose challenges for McConnell. Senators like Tom Cotton, who defeated Mark Pryor in Arkansas, had previously supported government shutdowns, a tactic McConnell had sought to avoid. New Senators Joni Ernst from Iowa and Thom Tillis from North Carolina might also prove to be independent voices, not always adhering strictly to party directives.

The Senate's internal dynamics were expected to become clearer before the new members were sworn in. A significant portion of the government was operating under a continuing resolution set to expire on December 11th, making it likely that McConnell would prioritize avoiding another shutdown. Potential areas for bipartisan agreement with the President might include trade policy, corporate taxes, and increased visas for skilled workers. However, those anticipating immediate solutions to major issues due to the Senate's partisan shift should recognize the complexities of divided government, which often leads to protracted negotiations and gradual progress. The success of the Republican majority would ultimately depend on internal party unity, the procedural leverage of the Democrats, and the President's willingness to engage. The ensuing months would reveal the extent of Republican influence and their capacity to navigate Washington's intricate political processes.

**Harry Reid Congratulates Mitch McConnell on Senate Leadership Following Republican Victory**

Following a significant Republican victory in the Senate, Senator Harry Reid of Nevada has congratulated Senator Mitch McConnell of Kentucky on his upcoming role as the new Senate Majority Leader. This transition marks a notable shift in the chamber's political balance, as Republicans have secured a majority in key Senate races.

In a statement, Senator Reid acknowledged the voters' clear message, stating, "The message from voters is clear: they want us to work together." He expressed his readiness to collaborate with Senator McConnell, adding, "I look forward to working with Senator McConnell to get things done for the middle class."

Senator Reid's remarks come as the Democratic party reflects on the election results. While some internal discontent exists, no formal challenge to his leadership has emerged, and he intends to remain in his role.

The focus now shifts to the upcoming lame-duck session, which precedes Senator McConnell's official assumption of Senate leadership. Key items on the agenda include nominations and the approval of a comprehensive spending bill, representing the immediate priorities for the Senate during this period of transition.

The recent Republican electoral success has significantly reshaped the nation's political landscape, ushering in an era of legislative gridlock and governmental stagnation for President Obama's final two years. This outcome, which granted Republicans full control of Congress for the first time since 2006, prompts an examination of how the party plans to wield its newfound power and what this signifies for American governance. The GOP successfully tapped into widespread public frustration with Washington D.C.'s perceived ineffectiveness, achieving a complete congressional takeover. This victory not only bolstered their House majority but also secured crucial wins in several key governor's races.

Senator Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, a seasoned politician, is set to become the Senate Majority Leader following his successful re-election. After his win, McConnell expressed a desire for bipartisan cooperation, stating, "We have a duty to work together on issues where we can agree. I think we have a duty to do that." However, this sentiment contrasts with the Republican Party's legislative history since regaining the House in 2010, a period marked by a general reluctance to advance significant legislation. Therefore, even with McConnell's stated goals, substantial legislative achievements in the coming years appear limited.

This political climate suggests that critical national issues, such as comprehensive immigration reform, decisive action on climate change, addressing income inequality, and modernizing infrastructure, will likely remain stalled. In this context, President Obama may increasingly rely on his veto power to block legislation from the Republican-controlled Congress. The future of the Affordable Care Act remains uncertain, as it was not a primary campaign focus for many Republican candidates.

The Republican Party's rise, particularly their Senate takeover, could empower more conservative factions, potentially shifting the party's platform further right. For instance, Senator Ted Cruz initially expressed reservations about endorsing McConnell for Majority Leader. House Speaker John Boehner did not explicitly prioritize immigration reform for the incoming Congress but acknowledged the significant responsibility entrusted to them by voters, remarking, "We are humbled by the responsibility the American people have placed with us, but this is not a time for celebration. It’s time for government to start getting results and implementing solutions to the challenges facing our country, starting with our still-struggling economy."

The loss of the Senate also significantly curtails a key presidential power Obama had largely maintained after Republicans took the House in 2010: his ability to confirm judicial nominees. This development signals prolonged political disputes over any potential Supreme Court vacancies. The implications for the 2016 presidential election are complex. While Republicans' increased ability to obstruct Obama's agenda could further fuel voter dissatisfaction, potentially boosting their desire for a White House change, this strategy might also backfire. If Republicans are seen as overreaching or excessively catering to their base, they could alienate a broader electorate, appearing to be part of the problem they campaigned against.

This election night outcome mirrored the significant Republican gains of 2010, a result President Obama himself called a decisive "thumping" of the Democrats. Exit poll data indicated that the results were largely driven by the President's declining approval ratings and widespread dissatisfaction with an economic recovery that, despite statistical improvements, many Americans felt did not translate into tangible benefits. On a positive note for Republicans, they achieved notable successes by electing two women to the Senate, along with one Black senator and one Black female representative. Key Democratic Senate incumbents lost in Arkansas, Colorado, and North Carolina, while Republicans also secured Senate seats in Montana, West Virginia, Iowa, and South Dakota. Many of these Senate races were expected to be highly competitive. Even on election night, there was a possibility that Senate control would remain undecided for weeks, with potential runoffs and an independent senator yet to declare his party affiliation for caucus purposes. A panel discussion highlighted the Republicans' historic House majority and potential challenges Speaker John Boehner might face in managing more ideologically driven colleagues.

However, states like Kansas, Arkansas, Georgia, Kentucky, Iowa, and Colorado appeared to shift decisively towards the GOP in the campaign's final days. Republican candidates effectively targeted the President in their messaging, a tactic that proved highly successful. The NBC News national exit poll revealed the President's approval rating at 45%, a significant nine-point drop from his re-election two years prior. Concurrently, 70% of voters expressed dissatisfaction with economic conditions, with only 29% believing the economy was performing well.

In closely watched gubernatorial contests, Wisconsin Governor Scott Walker defeated Democrat Mary Burke, while Ohio's John Kasich secured re-election. These victories position both Walker and Kasich as potential 2016 presidential contenders. In Florida, Republican Governor Rick Scott narrowly won against Democrat Charlie Crist. Texas saw Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott achieve a decisive victory against Democratic State Senator Wendy Davis. Illinois elected Republican Bruce Rauner, unseating incumbent Democrat Pat Quinn. In a notable exception for Democrats, businessman Tom Wolf ousted the unpopular Republican Governor of Pennsylvania, Tom Corbett.

The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) Sen. Ted Cruz spent the final weekend of the midterms on the far edge of the country trying to help fellow Republican Dan Sullivan win a race the GOP is counting on in its effort to retake the Senate. It’s a team-player role the tea party firebrand from Texas has filled a handful of times this fall — but one he plans to abandon if Republicans win control of both congressional chambers. In an interview at the Hotel Captain Cook here between campaign stops for Sullivan, Cruz made it clear he would push hard for a Republican-led Senate to be as conservative and confron­tational as the Republican-led House. Piggybacking on what House leaders have done, Cruz said the first order of business should be a series of hearings on President Obama, “looking at the abuse of power, the executive abuse, the regulatory abuse, the lawlessness that sadly has pervaded this administration.” (Live coverage: On the campaign trail) 1 of 13 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × New faces to Washington?
