Or bigger roles? View Photos Hopes among these politicians are high that they’ll be headed to the nation’s capital – or upgrading from House digs to the Senate side of the Capitol. Caption An eclectic group is coming to Congress — or moving across the Capitol from the House to the Senate. Here are a few. Mia Love — Utah Love, a former small-town Utah mayor and fitness instructor from Brooklyn, became the nation’s first black, female, Republican member of Congress. She lost a House race in 2012 to incumbent Democrat Jim Matheson, who is retiring. Rick Bowmer/AP Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. Cruz also would like the Senate to be as aggressive in trying to repeal the Affordable Care Act as the House, which has voted more than 50 times to get rid of the law. Republicans should “pursue every means possible to repeal Obamacare,” Cruz said, including forcing a vote through parliamentary procedures that would get around a possible filibuster by Democrats. If that leads to a veto by Obama, Cruz said, Republicans should then vote on provisions of the health law “one at a time.” And when asked whether he would back Sen. Mitch McConnell of Kentucky for Republican leader, Cruz would not pledge his support — an indication that there are limits to how much of a partner he’s willing to be. At the heart of Cruz’s shift from the insular approach that defined his first year in office is a belief that he can use his popularity with conservatives to expand his influence in the Senate and improve his standing as he considers a 2016 presidential campaign. Cruz’s desire to turn his party further right in the coming months is one of the challenges already facing McConnell should Republicans regain the Senate, with tea party leaders inside and outside the Capitol spoiling for a number of hard-line moves. “Senator Cruz has been rather quiet over the past few months,” said Ron Bonjean, a spokesman for Trent Lott when the Mississippian was the Senate Republican leader. “That time seems to be coming to an end. I understand why he’s eager to go after Obamacare. But the reality is that it’ll take 60 votes to repeal it and Republicans will have nowhere near that amount. If Obamacare remains the focus, he will certainly get the base jazzed up about what he’s doing, but he won’t get rid of the law.” Cruz has gained some traction in terms of shaping the contours of what a Republican Senate would do, in part because McConnell and House Speaker John A. Boehner (R-Ohio) have not offered their own definitive vision of what a Republican-led Congress would look like. View Graphic Election Lab: See our current forecast for every congressional race in 2014 Two weeks ago, Cruz wrote an opinion piece in USA Today laying out 10 conservative priorities he thinks Republicans should pursue, including moving toward a flat tax and drawing a hard line on illegal immigrants. In the interview here, Cruz reiterated some of those points, such as approving the Keystone XL pipeline. McConnell has mostly been coy about what he would like to accomplish other than adding amendments to curb federal regulations to spending bills as a means of putting pressure on the president. “It’s never a good idea to tell the other side what the first play is going to be,” McConnell said at an event last month. Cruz is not interested in adding amendments that may put indirect pressure on Obama. He favors direct political combat. That way, either the president gives in, or, Cruz said, “you have clear accountability. It becomes transparent to everyone that it is the Democrats blocking meaningful progress.” Rep. Peter King (R-N.Y.), a Long Island moderate, said Republicans should be wary of Cruz’s guidance. “He is the last one we should listen to,” King said in an interview Sunday. “Don’t forget — a year ago he brought Republicans over the cliff.” Still, Cruz’s help on the trail has improved his image among some Republicans, while easing a measure of the anger aimed at him from some of the donors and colleagues he infuriated in October 2013 when he led a shutdown of the government. Cruz should be able to count on a handful of new friends, if not allies, when the Senate convenes next year. In recent weeks, he has campaigned for Senate contenders who beat Cruz-admiring insurgents in Republican primaries, from businessman David Perdue in Georgia and state Sen. Joni Ernst in Iowa to Sullivan and Sen. Pat Roberts, Kansas’s embattled incumbent. If she wins, Ernst is poised to be a powerful player in the run-up to the Iowa caucuses, the first nominating contest in the 2016 race for the GOP presidential nomination. Perdue, who has weak ties to his red state’s GOP base, could hew close to Cruz on some votes to keep conservatives in Georgia at bay. Sullivan, for similar reasons, could do the same. Cruz is not the only conservative Republican with national ambitions who is working to show skeptical Republicans that they can do more than squabble. Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) has cut ads for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, one of the groups that opposed tea party candidates in Senate primaries, and stumped for moderate former Massachusetts senator Scott Brown, who is running for Senate in New Hampshire. Cruz declined to say whether he’s going to run for president, but was dismissive of moderates in his party, particularly those who may challenge him for the 2016 Republican nomination. Of Jeb Bush, for instance, Cruz said he likes and respects him, “but I think we have seen election after election that when Republicans fail to draw a clear distinction with the Democrats, when we run to the mushy middle, we lose.” “At some point,” Cruz continued, “after Gerald Ford and Bob Dole and John McCain and Mitt Romney . . . we shouldn’t keep making the same mistakes over and over again. “One of the reasons Republicans have lost elections recently is that we have failed to engage in a meaningful way on the great issues of the day,” Cruz said. “We’ve played a prevent defense. You don’t win elections that way.”

query: What significant developments are expected to emerge from the Paris climate talks next month?

documents.text:
The upcoming Paris climate talks are poised to culminate in a landmark international accord aimed at curbing greenhouse gas emissions from both developed and developing nations. This potential achievement could be hailed as a significant diplomatic triumph for President Barack Obama and his contemporaries, showcasing their efforts to address global warming. However, a notable contributor to the progress being made in Paris is George W. Bush.

This assertion may be surprising, given the former Republican president's skepticism towards global warming science and his administration's legal challenges to Environmental Protection Agency climate policies. Yet, Bush played a pivotal role in initiating the very negotiations now nearing fruition. This might seem counterintuitive, especially considering the current Republican stance, which often seeks to impede the Paris negotiations and dismantle Obama's environmental initiatives. Nevertheless, Bush's presidency left a substantial mark, particularly during late 2007 negotiations held in Bali, Indonesia.

Although President Bush himself was not present, his administration's delegation became a focal point during the two-week talks. Al Gore, fresh from receiving his Nobel Peace Prize, delivered a speech criticizing Bush and urging negotiators to reserve space in their agreements for the next U.S. president. The atmosphere at the conference was tense, with Bush's officials facing vocal disapproval during the closing sessions.

Despite the contentious environment, the outcome of the Bali conference represented a significant breakthrough in international climate discussions. For the first time, nations of all economic capacities committed to addressing global warming. The resulting "Bali Action Plan" provided a foundational framework for future negotiations. Crucially, it established a precedent by securing commitments from rapidly developing countries like China, Brazil, India, and South Africa to submit their own emission reduction plans. This inclusion was instrumental in dismantling a major political obstacle to international climate action.

The momentum generated in Bali has faced numerous challenges. In 2009, ambitious expectations surrounding President Obama's leadership at the Copenhagen summit, where he and over 100 world leaders aimed to finalize a binding treaty, ultimately fell short. It took two more years, at the Durban climate conference, to re-establish the trajectory set by the Bali Action Plan.

While the Paris talks will address intricate details, such as providing financial and technical assistance to developing nations for clean energy adoption and climate adaptation, the core principle established in Bali—collective global engagement in combating climate change—underscores the strong likelihood of a successful outcome in Paris. Yvo de Boer, former top UN climate official, described Bali as a "turning point" that is often overlooked.

Navigating the complexities of UN climate conferences can be challenging, with their technical jargon and political maneuvering. These annual summits, held since 1995, often aim for tangible outcomes to justify the extensive efforts. While host countries may highlight similar achievements year after year, each conference builds upon previous agreements, supported by ongoing work in side meetings and at UN climate headquarters in Bonn.

The foundational treaty for climate action was negotiated at the 1992 Rio Earth Summit, signed by George H.W. Bush. In contrast, the subsequent "Berlin Mandate" in 1995, under President Bill Clinton, stipulated that only developed nations were legally bound to reduce emissions, while developing countries received exemptions. The 1997 Kyoto Protocol, negotiated with the assistance of Al Gore, formalized this distinction. Despite U.S. efforts to introduce flexibility, including broader coverage of greenhouse gases and a cap-and-trade system, the Kyoto Protocol faced significant opposition in the U.S. Senate, which had previously passed a resolution against ratifying any treaty that would harm the U.S. economy or exempt developing nations.

Reflecting on Kyoto, some former Clinton negotiators acknowledged the difficulty in securing commitments from developing countries, who often cited historical Western emissions as the cause of climate change. Stuart Eizenstat, who led the U.S. delegation, described the hostility in the room, stating, "We completely struck out with developing countries." Daniel Bodansky, a former Clinton-era climate coordinator, characterized Kyoto as a "false start." Although Clinton signed the protocol, he did not submit it for Senate ratification.

George W. Bush subsequently withdrew the U.S. from all talks related to the Kyoto agreement. International criticism intensified amid reports of the Bush administration censoring climate science and his reversal of a campaign pledge to cap power plant emissions. Bush's substantive engagement on climate issues didn't resume until 2007, a period marked by Democratic control of Congress and growing international pressure for an alternative to Kyoto.

Harlan Watson, a lead Bush administration climate negotiator, noted the persistent question: "When Bush came in and said we’re not going to do Kyoto, the obvious question was: What’s your alternative?" The Bali negotiations, following all-night sessions, resulted in an agreement where developed nations committed to assisting developing countries with climate adaptation and clean energy infrastructure. This agreement also significantly advanced the concept of breaking down the barriers between developed and developing nations.

George David Banks, a former senior Bush White House environmental aide, recalled the skepticism from environmentalists and Democrats, who viewed the Bali agreement as less ambitious than Kyoto. However, he emphasized the pragmatic necessity of a new approach, stating, "If you were concerned about the climate issue, just because of the math and the increase in emission levels [counting the world’s major economies], you had to do something different."

The post-Bali negotiations have been complex. The Copenhagen summit in 2009, while not yielding a legally binding treaty, resulted in an accord where nations pledged to continue developing environmental plans, deferring the establishment of a formal legal framework until Paris. Banks credits the Obama administration for building upon the 2007 goals, acknowledging that their success would not have been possible without Bush's groundwork. Kalee Kreider, an environmental group veteran who was present at the Bali talks, agreed that Bush's team "deserve credit, coming from a terrible place, undermining the system, to getting things done," calling Bali a "tipping point moment."

The irony of Bush's foundational role in setting the stage for Paris is lost on many current Republican lawmakers, who often reject climate science and oppose Obama's environmental agenda. Their recent actions, including resolutions to overturn emissions rules for power plants, stand in stark contrast to the architecture of the Paris agreement, which Nigel Purvis, a Clinton-era climate diplomat, notes is "the architecture that conservatives said we needed."

The images of more than 500 people are being projected onto the facade of France’s National Assembly building during the 2015 U.N. Climate Change Conference (COP21) in Paris to “remind leaders that the world is watching,” in the words of the artists behind the project. The Standing March is the collaborative effort of the French artist known as JR and the American filmmaker Darren Aronofsky. The “representation of humanity” is, the artists said, a message to the 25,000 world leaders who have gathered in Paris to negotiate a plan that will limit the rise of the global temperature to 2°C (3.6°F). “We expect decisions, we expect commitments, and we expect time frames,” Aronofsky, the controversial filmmaker behind Requiem for a Dream and Black Swan, said in a statement on the project’s website. “It’s about people, from Tuvalu to New Orleans, whose lives are threatened by climate change. The world depends on a solution.” The conference and the exhibition — which will travel around Paris for seven days after a weekend on the National Assembly building — come less than three weeks after the terrorist attacks that left 130 people dead. “For security reasons, marches are forbidden in Paris. But our art piece is a silent march,” JR said in a statement. “And we are marching, backed by the Assemblée Nationale, the heart of the French democracy. We must think about our future, the future of our environment and this is our answer to those who want to control our present.”

## The Environmental Footprint of Climate Diplomacy: A Look at COP21

The **2015 Paris climate talks, COP21**, were a pivotal moment in global climate discussions. This major event brought together approximately **50,000 people** to address the critical issue of climate change. The attendees included 22,000 official delegates from 195 countries, along with thousands of representatives from non-governmental organizations, businesses, activists, and the media.

The sheer scale of COP21 resulted in a significant environmental impact, largely due to **travel**. As over 140 world leaders convened in Paris for crucial treaty negotiations, the urgency of the climate crisis took center stage. This gathering, officially the 21st Conference of the Parties to the Kyoto Protocol (#COP21), aimed to address a planet grappling with record-high carbon dioxide levels and escalating emissions, a stark reality underscored by the fact that this year is likely the warmest since the dawn of agriculture.

For instance, Peru's Environment Minister, Manuel Pulgar Vidal, a prominent figure in climate negotiations, shared a link to a document titled, "Scenario note on the twelfth part of the second session of the Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform for Enhanced Action," a title far from inspiring.

The ADP2 process, which has involved representatives from nearly every nation gathering periodically, primarily in Bonn, Germany, over the past four years, laid the groundwork for a draft agreement. This foundational work stemmed from a 2011 agreement in Durban, South Africa, to establish the first global climate treaty by 2015. Key sticking points in these negotiations include:

*   **Emission Reduction Targets:** The foundational United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC), signed in 1992, established the principle of "common but differentiated responsibility," acknowledging that developed nations, such as the United States, Japan, and Germany, with historically higher emissions, should undertake more significant reductions. While voluntary pledges, known as Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs), have been submitted, they are insufficient. A central focus in Paris is establishing a mechanism to accelerate the pace of emissions cuts.

*   **Climate Finance:** A crucial element for fostering climate-friendly development involves financial assistance from developed to developing nations. Developed countries have pledged $100 billion annually by 2020, but the funding sources remain unclear. Many developing nations have integrated their financial needs into their INDCs, recognizing that international support is vital for a swift transition to renewable energy.

*   **Loss and Damage:** Even with aggressive emissions reductions, the planet faces continued warming due to the thermal inertia of oceans and the inherent lag in the climate system. This means that climate-related disasters will likely intensify, disproportionately affecting vulnerable countries. These nations are advocating for a mechanism to secure aid and support for adaptation to future extreme weather events.

*   **Ratchet Mechanism:** Recognizing that the Paris talks alone will not solve climate change, a significant debate revolves around the frequency of countries announcing more ambitious targets. Developed nations generally favor a decadal review, while developing countries push for five-year intervals.

To circumvent the challenges faced in previous summits like Kyoto and Copenhagen, the U.N. climate talks operate by consensus, with a strategy of making provisions voluntary. However, the European Union, with France at the forefront, desires a legally binding agreement. This presents a significant hurdle, as U.S. approval would require ratification by the Republican-controlled Congress, a prospect deemed highly unlikely. Consequently, the U.S. is strongly opposing legally binding language in the Paris agreement.

Organizations advocating for the most robust climate action include the Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS), the Vulnerable Countries Forum (V20), and the Least Developed Countries (LDC). ### COP21: A Global Effort to Combat Climate Change

The 21st Conference of the Parties (COP21), a crucial summit under the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCC), is underway near Paris. This significant meeting, commencing Monday and concluding on December 11th, aims to forge a universal accord to address the escalating threat of climate change.

The primary objective of COP21 is to establish a legally binding agreement to limit global temperature increases to below 2 degrees Celsius (3.6 Fahrenheit) above pre-industrial levels. Scientific consensus highlights that surpassing this threshold will exacerbate extreme weather events, leading to more severe droughts, intensified storms, and devastating floods, thereby imperiling our planet's future.

This pivotal conference brings together representatives from 196 nations, including prominent leaders such as President Obama and China's President Xi Jinping. Their participation is particularly vital, given that the United States and China are the world's largest emitters of greenhouse gases. The gathering also includes a broad spectrum of stakeholders, such as delegates, politicians, business leaders, scientists, environmental advocates, and journalists. Public climate demonstrations planned in Paris and other French cities have been canceled in light of recent terrorist attacks.

A central agenda item involves the review of national commitments to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 2020. Countries like the US and China, currently reliant on fossil fuels, have pledged to transition towards cleaner, low-carbon energy sources. Previous climate negotiations, such as the Copenhagen summit in 2009, encountered difficulties in establishing equitable frameworks for emission reduction responsibilities between developed and developing nations.

The human and economic toll of climate change is substantial. The UN reports that over the past two decades, weather-related disasters have resulted in 606,000 fatalities and 4.1 billion injuries. Furthermore, the UN estimates that approximately 90% of major disasters, including floods, storms, heatwaves, and droughts, are linked to extreme weather phenomena, incurring annual costs between $250 billion and $300 billion.

Despite the inherent complexities, there is a palpable sense of optimism regarding the potential for a successful agreement at COP21. Extensive preparatory work has been undertaken, enhancing the likelihood of a binding action plan. A positive indicator is the submission of Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs) by 178 out of 196 participating countries. These INDCs publicly outline each nation's proposed strategies and methodologies for emission reduction.

The stage is set for a potential breakthrough in global climate change negotiations, but significant hurdles remain. As Secretary of State John Kerry noted last week, "there are all these wrinkles and hurdles at the last minute." This sentiment underscores the complex and often challenging path toward a unified global response to climate change.

### A History of Global Climate Agreements

The pursuit of a comprehensive global climate treaty is a long-standing endeavor. Since 1992, United Nations negotiators have convened annual summits, with two notable instances where a deal seemed within reach:

*   **The Kyoto Protocol (1997):** This landmark agreement established legally binding greenhouse gas emission reduction targets for major industrialized economies. However, it encountered two critical limitations. Firstly, it exempted developing nations such as India and China, which have since become significant contributors to global emissions. Secondly, the United States, under President Bill Clinton, opted not to submit the protocol for Senate ratification due to the unlikelihood of its passage, thereby excluding the world's largest economy from its commitments.

*   **The Copenhagen Accord (2009):** Intended as a successor to the Kyoto Protocol, this pact sought to incorporate commitments from developing countries. Nevertheless, it failed to secure the unanimous consent required for legal enforceability, ultimately functioning more as a voluntary declaration of intent rather than a binding agreement.

### What Distinguishes the Current Climate Talks?

Several key factors differentiate the current climate negotiations from their predecessors, offering a renewed sense of optimism:

*   **The US-China Breakthrough:** A pivotal development is the agreement reached between the United States and China, historically the world's two largest carbon emitters and significant obstacles in past negotiations. The U.S. has pledged to reduce its emissions by up to 28 percent below 2005 levels by 2025, a goal supported by forthcoming environmental regulations on power plants and vehicles. China, in turn, has committed to launching a national cap-and-trade system by 2017 to curb industrial emissions and has stated that its emissions will begin to decline no later than 2030.

*   **Broad International Participation:** The current talks boast unprecedented international engagement, with over 170 countries submitting proposals. These nations collectively account for more than 90 percent of global carbon emissions. These submitted plans are anticipated to form the bedrock of a new agreement, signaling a departure from the brinkmanship that characterized previous rounds of negotiations.

*   **Managed Expectations:** In contrast to the high-profile pronouncements that surrounded the Kyoto Protocol and the Copenhagen Accord, negotiators in Paris are adopting a more measured approach to expectations. The anticipated agreement is not being presented as a definitive solution to climate change but rather as a crucial foundational step in a longer-term process.

### The Paris Agreement: A Foundation, Not the Final Destination

The overarching objective of global climate policy is to limit the increase in global average temperatures to 3.6 degrees Fahrenheit above pre-industrial levels. Exceeding this threshold carries the risk of severe and irreversible consequences. While the current national plans, if fully implemented, would still permit a warming of up to 6 degrees Fahrenheit, this represents a substantial improvement compared to the projected 8-degree increase that would occur without such policies.

*   **Current Trajectory:** Without the implementation of the policies outlined in the Paris Agreement, scientists warn of a far more destructive trajectory of global temperature increase.

*   **Progress Achieved:** Although the submitted national plans are not yet sufficient to meet the ultimate temperature goals, they are expected to "bend the temperature curve," according to Janos Pasztor, UN Assistant Secretary-General for Climate Change. He emphasized that this moment represents a "turning point" and that the agreement should serve as a "floor, not the ceiling" for future climate action.

### The Challenge of "Ratcheting Up" Commitments

A central concept in the Paris talks is the idea of "ratcheting," which implies that countries will reconvene periodically, typically every five to 10 years, to strengthen their emissions reduction plans. This mechanism, however, faces significant domestic political challenges, as implementing even more stringent policies may prove difficult for lawmakers to gain public acceptance for. While some nations favor a 10-year interval for these reviews, the U.S. is advocating for five-year cycles to ensure more frequent reassessment and potential strengthening of commitments.

### Financial Commitments and Persistent Disagreements

The issue of climate finance remains a substantial obstacle. During the Copenhagen negotiations, developed nations pledged to provide $100 billion annually to developing countries by 2020 to support clean energy transitions and climate change adaptation efforts. However, a 2015 OECD report indicated that only approximately $62 billion in climate finance was transferred in 2014. Developing nations at the Paris talks are seeking more substantial and binding commitments from wealthier countries, with India's environment minister asserting that the cost of climate action runs into "trillions," and the $100 billion figure represents merely "reparation."

### Verification and the Crucial Element of Trust

Ensuring that governments adhere to their pledges without infringing upon national sovereignty presents another critical point of contention. China's recent disclosure of burning 17 percent more coal annually than previously reported has understandably raised questions about the credibility of its emissions reporting and is expected to be a significant point of discussion and potential contention during the negotiations.

### Legally Binding Agreements vs. The Power of Peer Pressure

Historically, climate treaties have been legally binding instruments, requiring national ratification by member states. However, given the current political landscape in the United States, a traditional treaty is unlikely to secure the necessary approval from the Senate. Consequently, negotiators are employing what has been described as "legal jujitsu," utilizing voluntary plans to circumvent the legal definition of a treaty. The underlying hope is that international peer pressure, particularly from major economic powers, will serve as a powerful incentive for compliance, akin to a classroom assignment where students are expected to present their work to their peers.

### Potential Pitfalls and the Risk of Derailment

The United Nations' consensus-based decision-making process inherently means that a single country possesses the power to derail an agreement. Just as a few nations ultimately opposed the Copenhagen Accord, a "wild-card" nation in Paris could potentially undermine the entire negotiation process, highlighting the fragility of multilateral climate diplomacy.

## Hilary Duff's Tinder Strategy: Lessons from the Queen of Swiping

The internet was abuzz when a **Hilary Duff Tinder profile** surfaced, initially shared on Reddit. This sparked curiosity, quickly escalating when the star herself confirmed her presence on the popular dating app. While some suspected an imposter, the profile's direct link to Hilary's verified Instagram account strongly suggested its legitimacy. Given her fame and charisma, her impact on Tinder isn't surprising. But beyond her celebrity status, what can average users learn from her approach? Examining her profile and subsequent revelations offers several **key takeaways for improving your own Tinder experience.**

**Her Culinary Preferences:**
Regarding her ideal breakfast, Duff cited bacon as a staple, admitting to its frequent preparation and consumption in her household, often accompanied by coffee, eggs, and oatmeal. She noted that her son also shares a predilection for bacon, solidifying its status as a household favorite.

**Advice to Her Younger Self:**

She finds immense joy in observing his cognitive development, his inquisitive nature, and his journey of becoming an individual. For Luca's cough, she employs natural cough syrups, utilizes a humidifier in his room, and applies baby vapor rub to his chest and feet during sleep.

**Television Show Recommendations and Personal Favorites:**
Duff admitted to limited television viewing time, making it difficult to assess current shows' messages for young audiences. However, she expressed a fondness for dramatic series, citing *Orange Is the New Black*, *Empire*, and *Scandal* as personal favorites.

**Music Versus Acting and Album Significance:**

**Appreciation for Her Appearance:**

**A Favorite Taylor Swift Track:**
When asked to name her favorite Taylor Swift song, Duff deliberated before selecting "Welcome to New York."

A mutual pal of DiCaprio’s and Gossip Cop tells us these stories are completely “insane” and “untrue.” The bogus rumor began in Star, and was then quickly picked up by a slew of lemming-like outlets that didn’t bother to fact-check the story. According to the original report, at Coachella, “Leo confessed that he was using the dating app under the name Leonard.” The somewhat unreliable tabloid then quotes a so-called “source” as saying DiCaprio is “obsessed with swiping on girls and seeing who’s out there.” Before we totally blast this fictitious report out of the water, let’s examine a few things. One, the story emanated from Star, a tabloid Gossip Cop has corrected before about DiCaprio, including a false report that he and Cameron Diaz had a “no strings attached” relationship. Two, how reliable could a source who was partying at Coachella be? Three, if a creative guy like DiCaprio were actually going to use Tinder, don’t you think he would come up with a little less obvious screen name than “Leonard”? Four, why would one of Hollywood’s most guarded stars tell a random person, who just happened to be a Star “source” that he’s “obsessed” with Tinder? Five, DiCaprio seems not to have a problem attracting women, especially those who are leggy, blonde models. Of course, the story is wholly fabricated, but unfortunately dozen of outlets were more than happy to try to gain Google traffic by shoving together the words “Leonardo DiCaprio” and “Tinder.” Regardless, Gossip Cop actually fact-checked the story, and a source close to the actor tells us exclusively the claim is “untrue.” The DiCaprio insider further joked to Gossip Cop, “One day they say he has 844 models and the next he needs a dating app? It’s insane.”

query: What are the impacts of the recent ransomware attack on companies across the globe?

documents.text:
A significant global ransomware attack is currently disrupting companies and organizations worldwide, with widespread reports of compromised computer systems. This sophisticated digital assault, whose origins are still under investigation, encrypts user data and demands an untraceable ransom payment in Bitcoin.

British advertising giant WPP has confirmed substantial IT system disruption. Ukrainian entities were among the first to report being affected, including the state power company and Kiev's main airport. The Chernobyl nuclear power plant was forced to revert to manual radiation monitoring after its Windows-based sensors were disabled by the malware.

US government agencies are actively investigating. The US National Security Council stated the US is "determined to hold those responsible accountable." The Department of Homeland Security advises victims against paying the ransom, noting no guarantee of restored file access.

Kaspersky Lab reported approximately 2,000 attacks, primarily in Ukraine, Russia, and Poland. Interpol is "closely monitoring" the situation and collaborating with member countries.

Initial analysis suggested the malware might be a variant of Petya, an earlier ransomware, updated as Petrwrap. However, Kaspersky Lab later revised its assessment, deeming the malware entirely new and naming it "NotPetya." Suspected attacks have been detected in Poland, Italy, Germany, France, and the US, in addition to the UK, Russia, and Ukraine.

The profitability of ransomware offers a strong incentive for cybercriminals. Recorded Future spokesperson Andrei Barysevich noted a South Korean hosting firm recently paid $1 million for data recovery. A Bitcoin wallet linked to the outbreak has received over 3.5 bitcoins. German email provider Posteo blocked the blackmail demand email address, halting further attacker communication.

This ransomware attack impacts a wide array of industries:

*   **Manufacturing and Logistics:** Aircraft maker Antonov, Danish shipping firm Maersk (affecting container shipping, oil, gas, and drilling), and Netherlands-based TNT (requiring system "remediation").
*   **Energy:** Russia's largest oil producer, Rosneft.
*   **Infrastructure:** A Mumbai port halted operations. Kiev's metro reportedly stopped accepting payment cards, and several petrol station chains suspended services.
*   **Healthcare:** Heritage Valley Health System in Pennsylvania reported network downtime, causing operation delays, though direct confirmation of this attack's involvement is pending.
*   **Food and Beverage:** US food giant Mondelez, including a Cadbury factory in Tasmania.
*   **Construction:** French company St Gobain.
*   **Pharmaceuticals:** US maker Merck Sharp & Dohme.
*   **Legal:** DLA Piper offices, with staff instructed to power down laptops.

This global ransomware assault follows the recent WannaCry incident, which significantly disrupted the UK's National Health Service. Experts suggest the current malware exploits similar Windows vulnerabilities used by WannaCry, potentially affecting organizations that hadn't fully patched these weaknesses.

Industrial firms, often challenged in applying patches due to operational continuity needs, are also affected. "These organizations typically have a challenge patching all of their machines because so many systems cannot have down time," noted one expert. "Airports also have this challenge."

Analysis of virus samples indicates limited detection by security vendors, suggesting many unpatched systems relying solely on traditional antivirus remain vulnerable.

Ukraine appears particularly hard-hit. The Kiev metro ceased payment card acceptance, and petrol stations suspended operations. Ukraine's deputy prime minister shared an image of affected government systems. The UK's National Crime Agency is also investigating.

## Global Cyberattack Disrupts Businesses and Governments

A significant ransomware cyberattack, dubbed "NotPetya," has wreaked havoc across the United States and Europe, locking down computer systems and leaving victims unable to access their files, even if they pay the ransom. The attack has particularly impacted Ukraine's government, financial institutions, and electricity grid, but its reach extends to major companies in France, Denmark, and even Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

### Widespread Impact and Notable Victims

The "NotPetya" ransomware has caused severe disruption at prominent organizations worldwide. Among the hardest hit are:

*   **Advertising giant WPP:** Several of its subsidiary companies experienced system outages, prompting an internal memo warning employees to disconnect all Windows machines due to a "massive global malware attack."
*   **French construction materials company Saint-Gobain:** This firm also fell victim to the widespread attack.
*   **Russian steel and oil firms Evraz and Rosneft:** These major Russian corporations reported significant system impacts.
*   **Danish shipping and transport giant AP Moller-Maersk:** The attack crippled all of Maersk's business units, including container shipping, port operations, oil and gas production, and oil tankers, affecting seventeen container terminals. The company confirmed its IT systems were down across multiple sites.
*   **Food giant Mondelez:** This multinational food holding company also reported its systems had been compromised.
*   **Legal firm DLA Piper:** The global law firm was among the organizations affected by the ransomware.
*   **Heritage Valley Health System:** This Pittsburgh-based healthcare provider, which operates hospitals and care facilities, confirmed its systems were hit by the malware.

### The "NotPetya" Ransomware and Its Mechanics

Infected computers display a message demanding a Bitcoin ransom, initially valued at $300, with instructions to confirm payment via email. However, the email provider, Posteo, has shut down the designated address, stating, "We do not tolerate any misuse of our platform." This action effectively prevents victims from contacting the attackers to receive a decryption key, rendering payment futile.

Cybersecurity experts, like Ryan Kalember, senior vice-president of cybersecurity strategy at Proofpoint, noted that this indicates the attackers are not "experienced ransomware operators."

### Devastation in Ukraine

The attack first surfaced in Ukraine, where it severely affected the government, banks, the state power utility, and critical infrastructure in Kyiv, including the airport and metro system. The radiation monitoring system at Chernobyl was also taken offline, forcing employees to rely on handheld counters to measure radiation levels in the exclusion zone.

Ukraine's Prime Minister, Volodymyr Groysman, described the attack as "unprecedented" but assured that vital systems remained operational. He stated, "Our IT experts are doing their job and protecting critical infrastructure. The attack will be repelled and the perpetrators will be tracked down." Despite the assurances, the attack temporarily shut down the country's main airport and disrupted metro services.

The Ukrainian central bank confirmed an "unknown virus" was responsible for the difficulties in client services and banking operations. The country has previously blamed Russia for cyberattacks, including a 2015 incident that affected its power grid, though Russia has denied these accusations.

### Technical Details and Spread

While initially reported as a variant of the "Petya" ransomware, analysts at Kaspersky Labs identified it as a new form they call "NotPetya." This new ransomware has reportedly affected over 2,000 users across Russia, Ukraine, Poland, France, Italy, the UK, Germany, and the US.

Security experts confirmed that the ransomware utilizes the same exploit, EternalBlue, as the WannaCry attack that occurred the previous month. EternalBlue, leaked by the Shadow Brokers hacker group, is believed to have originated from the US National Security Agency. To spread rapidly within organizations that had patched against WannaCry, "NotPetya" appears to employ additional methods, targeting network administrator tools. The initial infection vector remains unclear, but unlike WannaCry, it doesn't seem to have spread primarily through email.

### A Growing Threat Landscape

The "NotPetya" attack follows the widespread WannaCry ransomware incident, which impacted over 230,000 computers globally. The increasing frequency and sophistication of cyberattacks highlight a growing global concern. Nicolas Duvinage, head of the French military’s digital crime unit, likened the situation to a "flu epidemic in winter," predicting more such attacks in the coming months. This escalating threat has led to a surge in cybersecurity spending, with the global market now estimated to be worth approximately £94 billion ($120 billion) annually.

A significant ransomware attack swept across numerous private businesses on Tuesday, with many incidents appearing to stem from the same malicious software. This widespread digital disruption bears a striking resemblance to the recent WannaCry attacks, which previously crippled thousands of computer systems globally.

Motherboard has collated multiple accounts of infections shared by affected entities on social media platforms. While independent verification of every claim is ongoing, several cybersecurity researchers and firms have corroborated the reports of these attacks. Costin Raiu, a security expert at Kaspersky Lab, noted in an online exchange that "We are observing several thousand infection attempts at this time, comparable in scale to WannaCry's initial hours."

Visual evidence shared on social media and provided by sources depicts many of the reported attacks featuring ransomware that displays stark red text against a black background. This ransomware demanded a ransom of $300 in bitcoin, with a message stating, "If you see this text, then your files are no longer accessible, because they are encrypted." It further cautioned, "Perhaps you are busy looking for a way to recover your files, but don't waste your time. Nobody can recover your files without our decryption service."

Initially, Kaspersky Lab hypothesized that the ransomware was a variation of the well-known Petya. However, Raiu later informed Motherboard that "it appears to be a new development indeed." Kaspersky Lab has since officially designated this new strain as NotPetya. Researchers at MalwareHunterTeam, an organization dedicated to combating ransomware, also indicated via direct message on social media that they believed the attack originated from the same malware family as initially identified by Raiu.

Consistent with other ransomware operations, the malware observed on Tuesday encrypts files on a compromised system. The perpetrators claim they will furnish the decryption key in exchange for bitcoin. According to a post from antivirus company Avira, the attacks leveraged the EternalBlue exploit, which was previously disclosed by the group known as The Shadow Brokers. Symantec, another prominent cybersecurity firm, subsequently tweeted that its analysts had "confirmed" the outbreak's propagation via EternalBlue. Motherboard was unable to independently verify this assertion at the time of this report.

EternalBlue, the same exploit that fueled the WannaCry attacks, targets a vulnerability within the SMB data-transfer protocol. Microsoft has since issued a patch for this vulnerability, but the extent of its adoption among users remains a point of concern.

An image of the alleged ransomware interface was provided to Motherboard. Spanish publication El Confidencial reported that hackers targeted the Madrid office of DLA Piper, a global law firm. A source with direct knowledge of the incident shared a photograph with Motherboard showing an infected computer at DLA Piper's Washington D.C. office, with claims that employees were instructed to vacate their workstations. Neither the D.C. nor Madrid offices provided immediate comment when contacted. A client of DLA Piper reported an inability to access the portal used for sensitive documents, a portal which Motherboard confirmed was indeed inaccessible.

A Ukrainian media company also fell victim to the attack, according to a local report from 24tv. UK-based global advertising and public relations firm WPP tweeted on Tuesday that it had experienced a "cyber attack." A source informed Motherboard that MediaCom, a UK entity owned by WPP, had been infected with ransomware. MediaCom did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The individuals controlling the email account listed in the ransomware message also did not respond to inquiries.

Cybersecurity researchers from Kaspersky Lab reported that the ransomware's impact extended to Russia, Ukraine, Spain, and France, among other nations. Numerous individuals on social media shared accounts of witnessing or hearing about the outbreak in their locales and across diverse industries. Companies worldwide also reported significant computer system disruptions.

Several hours after the initial outbreak, Rob Wainwright, the executive director of Europol, tweeted that the European law enforcement agency was "urgently responding" to "another major ransomware attack" unfolding across Europe. Chris Sistrunk, a security researcher at Mandiant, commented that the situation appeared to be "another global outbreak attack."

This is a developing situation, and further updates will be provided as more information becomes available. This report has been revised to accurately reflect Kaspersky Lab's evolving analysis; the firm initially suggested the ransomware was a Petya variant but now considers it a distinct entity.

A significant cybersecurity threat, dubbed "Petya Ransomware," emerged in early 2016, first making its appearance on the Heisse Security board. This particular strain of ransomware distinguished itself from its predecessors through a novel infection methodology that resulted in a complete system lockout for the user. Historically, since 2010, ransomware attacks predominantly relied on remaining covert on a victim's system, meticulously encrypting all files using robust encryption algorithms before demanding payment. The primary avenues for data recovery without succumbing to the ransom demand typically involved either maintaining a reliable backup or employing file recovery software.

Petya ransomware diverged from these conventional tactics in two critical aspects. Firstly, its distribution vector was identified as the Dropbox network, a cloud storage service. Secondly, and more alarmingly, Petya actively overwrites essential boot files required for Windows to initiate, thereby rendering the computer entirely inaccessible to the user. The initial infection vector often involved a seemingly legitimate, business-related email, purportedly from a job applicant. Recipients were enticed to access a Dropbox link, which contained the applicant's curriculum vitae and associated documentation. Upon attempting to open these files, a self-extracting executable would launch, harboring a Trojan horse virus. This malicious payload would then proceed to disable any installed antivirus software and initiate a remote download of the Petya ransomware.

Once embedded within the system, Petya would systematically overwrite the Master Boot Record (MBR) of the entire hard drive. This action would subsequently trigger a system crash, manifesting as a Blue Screen of Death (BSoD), effectively halting the Windows operating system. When the user attempted to restart their computer, the compromised MBR would prevent a normal Windows boot sequence. Instead, a ransom demand message would be displayed. This message characteristically began with a red, ASCII-rendered "pirate" skull, followed by detailed instructions for payment. The Petya ransomware asserted the use of military-grade encryption for file locking and stipulated payment in Bitcoins, to be transferred to a TOR-based site, a common characteristic of many ransomware operations. Some reports also indicated that Petya did indeed proceed with file encryption. However, unlike many traditional ransomware variants that operate discreetly during the encryption phase, Petya's modus operandi involved enforcing the system lockdown first, and then commencing the encryption process without further concealment. To mitigate the impact of such an attack, immediate power-down of the affected computer is strongly advised.

A major global cyberattack is currently unfolding, fueled by a new ransomware strain called Petya, also identified as Petrwrap. This malicious software has rapidly spread across the globe. Early reports indicate significant disruption to Ukraine's essential services, impacting power providers, airports, public transport networks, and the nation's central bank.

Beyond Ukraine, international entities have also fallen victim, including the Danish shipping conglomerate Maersk and the Russian oil firm Rosneft. Numerous organizations in India, Spain, France, and the United Kingdom have also reported Petya infections.

Petya's rapid spread is largely due to its exploitation of the EternalBlue vulnerability, a tool originally developed by the U.S. National Security Agency (NSA). Security experts have confirmed Petya uses EternalBlue to propagate, drawing comparisons to the recent WannaCry ransomware. Microsoft had issued a patch for EternalBlue in March, before WannaCry's emergence, which secured some systems. However, Petya's extensive damage suggests many organizations neglected to install these vital security updates, leaving systems, including older ones like Windows XP, exposed. While the widespread attention has led many administrators to prioritize patching, Petya's continued use of EternalBlue underscores persistent patching difficulties. Experts believe Petya may utilize additional methods for further distribution.

Petya functions in two primary stages. First, it targets a computer's master boot record and attempts to encrypt its master file table. If this initial stage fails, a secondary component, Mischa, encrypts all files on the hard drive, a common ransomware approach. Upon infection, users see a ransom demand for $300 in bitcoin to restore their files, similar to WannaCry's payment model. The origin and perpetrators of this attack remain unidentified.

A worrying characteristic of Petya is its apparent absence of the amateur errors that limited WannaCry's reach and ransom collection. Unlike WannaCry, which possessed a "kill switch" that experts could activate to slow its spread, Petya lacks a similar feature, making it harder to stop its activity. While increased patching efforts following WannaCry might prevent an outbreak of similar magnitude, the number of Petya infections is still substantial. This attack is considered particularly severe, and though not as widespread as WannaCry, it is undeniably significant. The attacks have generated a considerable amount of bitcoin, with ongoing updates anticipated from cybersecurity agencies.

A massive global cyberattack is unfolding, with Ukraine appearing to be the primary target, leading to extensive disruptions. This event represents the most significant cyberattack Ukraine has ever faced. It has severely impacted essential services, including banks, power grids, and the metro system. Despite the prime minister calling the attack "unprecedented," he also stated that "vital systems haven't been affected." Ukraine has a well-documented history of cyber incidents and hacking attempts in recent years. The country has previously blamed Russia for similar attacks, such as a 2015 power grid incident, according to The Guardian. However, Russia has consistently denied any involvement.

The cyberattack's reach extends beyond Ukraine, affecting government bodies and private companies internationally. Globally, major corporations like Russia's Rosneft oil company and Denmark's AP Moller-Maersk have confirmed they were compromised. Security experts believe the malware used is a variant of the "Petya" ransomware. Analysts are already comparing this incident to the May WannaCry ransomware attack. That earlier attack infected approximately 230,000 computers in over 150 countries. Some experts worry that the current Petya attack could potentially be larger in scope than WannaCry.

The Petya ransomware, also known as Petwrap, emerged as a significant cybersecurity threat, rapidly spreading across Europe and Ukraine. This attack, occurring shortly after the WannaCry virus, similarly exploited the SMBv1 vulnerability for rapid network propagation. However, Petya's method of holding infected computers hostage was notably more destructive.

Petya's impact was widespread, affecting a diverse range of organizations including corporations, power suppliers, and banks. Significant outbreaks were reported in numerous countries, with Russia, Ukraine, Spain, France, the UK, and India experiencing notable infections. Within just 72 hours, Petya compromised over 300,000 computers, with attackers demanding a $300 ransom in Bitcoin.

Unlike typical ransomware that encrypts individual files, Petya operates by rebooting infected machines and then encrypting the Master File Table (MFT) and rendering the Master Boot Record (MBR) inoperable. This process effectively locks down the entire system by corrupting the essential data that dictates file locations. Petya then overwrites the MBR with its malicious code, displaying a ransom note and preventing the computer from booting.

Microsoft had previously released patches for the exploited vulnerability in April, even for unsupported systems like Windows XP, highlighting the critical importance of system updates. The company strongly advises disabling the outdated and vulnerable SMBv1 file sharing protocol. This is a simple yet highly effective security measure, particularly as more ransomware exploits utilizing leaked NSA hacking tools continue to surface.

For Windows 10 and Windows 8.1 users, disabling SMBv1 can be achieved through the Control Panel. Navigate to Programs and Features, select "Turn Windows Features on or off," and uncheck the box for "SMB 1.0/CIFS File Sharing support." A reboot is required to apply these changes. There is no compelling reason to continue using SMBv1, and Microsoft intends to remove it in a future update. This incident serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with outdated, unpatched systems and the ongoing consequences of leaked hacking tools.

**Petya Ransomware Re-emerges, Disrupting European Systems**

The **Petya ransomware** is back, causing major IT problems across Europe. Initial findings show significant effects in **Ukraine, Russia, England, and India**. The Swiss Reporting and Analysis Centre for Information Assurance (MELANI) confirmed Tuesday that Petya is spreading fast by using the **SMB (Server Message Block) flaw**.

While MELANI has not yet heard of any Swiss firms being hit, the group is watching this cyber threat closely. This new attack is similar to the big problems caused by Petya in 2016.

The wide impact of this Petya attack is clear in Russia. **Rosneft**, the country's top oil company, reported a large cyberattack on its systems Tuesday. At the same time, Ukraine saw disruptions at several banks and its main airport, showing how widespread this ransomware event is.

Security firm Kaspersky Lab said the attack has hit around 2,000 computers so far in around a dozen countries. The most affected organisations are located in Russia and the Ukraine, with systems in the UK, Germany, France, Italy, the US and Poland also hit. The researchers confirmed that one of the ways the virus spread was using the Eternal Blue tool, but that there are likely other ways too. The company added that the ransomware might not be a variation of Petya but a new strain of the virus. "Kaspersky Lab's analysts are investigating the new wave of ransomware attacks targeting organisations across the world. Our preliminary findings suggest that it is not a variant of Petya ransomware as publically reported, but a new ransomware that has not been seen before," the researchers said.

## Remembering Jessica Starr: A Beloved Meteorologist's Life and Legacy

Paul's mother described the agonizing struggle of witnessing her son's relentless pain. "We were at a loss for how to help him," she recounted. Initially, pain relievers were suggested, and Paul would cautiously take them. However, he was not inclined to rely heavily on medication. Tragically, as the effects of the painkillers subsided, he reported an intensification of his suffering. This cycle left him frequently with his eyes closed, a poignant indicator of his discomfort.

The tragic suicide of Ontario man Paul Fitzpatrick, a consequence of over two decades of excruciating eye pain, has brought to light a devastating, albeit rare, complication of laser eye surgery: corneal neuralgia. This severe side-effect, meticulously investigated by W5, can result in significant nerve damage. This damage, in turn, can manifest as intense, chronic pain that incapacitates individuals, rendering them unable to work and, in the most extreme and heartbreaking instances, driving them to contemplate ending their lives. Paul's family, in their profound grief, has bravely chosen to share his story, aiming to illuminate the realities of this debilitating condition and prevent others from enduring similar suffering.

Paul's family firmly believes he suffered from undiagnosed corneal neuralgia for more than 20 years, a silent torment that shadowed his adult life. The onset of his ordeal traces back to 1996, when, at the age of 32, Paul underwent photorefractive keratectomy (PRK). This procedure represented an early iteration of laser eye surgery, undertaken to correct his lifelong dependence on corrective lenses. His mother, Christine Fitzpatrick, recounted how Paul had diligently researched his available options, ultimately selecting PRK. He was drawn to its perceived less invasive approach to the corneal tissue when compared to the more commonly performed LASIK procedure. PRK, in essence, involves the removal of the eye's outermost protective layer, the epithelium, before a laser is employed to precisely reshape the underlying corneal tissue. Following this reshaping, the cornea is expected to naturally regenerate its surface within a matter of days.

While the vast majority of patients experience no adverse effects or complications from PRK and other forms of laser eye surgery, Paul's journey took a tragically divergent and agonizing path. His father, Gene Fitzpatrick, poignantly described the profound and devastating impact of Paul's condition: "it ruined his life, and it also left a lot of people around him suffering." In the immediate aftermath of the procedure, Paul began to experience debilitating migraines and persistent, unrelenting dry eyes. His quest for relief became an arduous and international undertaking, as Canadian medical professionals were unable to offer a definitive solution or effective treatment. Despite being presented with and even undergoing further PRK treatments in his desperate search for comfort, his pain only intensified, deepening his despair.

His brother, Kevin Fitzpatrick, offered a vivid and heart-wrenching account of Paul's relentless agony. He described the searing sensation with chilling clarity: "The burning pain in his eyes, the needles in his eyes, that was how he described the pain." Kevin further emphasized the critical lack of awareness surrounding his condition, stating, "Had he been aware (of his condition)…he definitely would not have continued with subsequent surgeries." In the final months of his life, Paul's pain reached such an unbearable crescendo that he spent the majority of his time with his eyes closed, seeking any semblance of respite. He even relied on a cane for support when he needed to move, a testament to his physical debilitation. His mother, Christine, articulated the family's profound sense of helplessness: "We didn’t know what to do for him." The pain medication prescribed offered only fleeting, temporary relief, and often, the subsequent rebound effect of the pain returning with even greater intensity once the medication's effects waned, only added to his torment.

On October 6th, a mere two weeks before the W5 report detailing his case was scheduled to air, Paul, then 54 years old, tragically died by suicide. In a deeply moving and poignant note left for his family, he meticulously detailed the unbearable agony he had endured for years. His words conveyed the depth of his suffering: "I cannot experience any type of pleasure anymore. Just the pain of burning eyes inside my head and throughout myself… Since 1996 Pain, pain and more pain, please forgive me for not being strong enough to cope. The past few months have been unbearable." His obituary, a somber testament to his struggle, noted that his "suffering abruptly ended" following his prolonged and ultimately unsuccessful search for relief from the persistent post-surgical eye pain that had consumed his life.

In the wake of his tragic death, Paul's family discovered a meticulously kept collection of lenses and extensive medical records. These documents served as a stark and painful testament to his years of fruitless attempts to find effective help and alleviate his suffering. Paul's suicide is believed to be the first documented case in Canada directly linked to complications arising from laser eye surgery. Paula Cofer, the founder of the online support group Lasik Complications, reported that there are approximately 10 similar documented cases in the United States, underscoring the potential for this devastating outcome. Paul's family is now courageously sharing his harrowing story with the world, aiming to shed light on the potential risks associated with elective surgical procedures. "I think these stories are what helps people understand what the potential consequences are of elective surgeries," Kevin stated, his voice heavy with emotion. "Having known what Paul has gone through, he would expect us to tell his story." This crucial report was made possible through the dedicated efforts of CTV News’ medical affairs specialist Avis Favaro and producer Elizabeth St. Philip. Further insights were drawn from respected organizations such as the American Academy of Ophthalmology and critical research from the Cornea Society, with valuable contributions from esteemed experts like Dr. Mark Bland, who provided essential medical context and understanding.

Depression and Suicide after LASIK Dr. Edward Boshnick: "Over the years I've had the misfortune of examining hundreds of patients who have lost quality vision and suffered severe depression as a result of LASIK surgery. Two of these patients were so depressed by their post-surgical vision loss that they attempted suicide. In fact, there are several documented cases of post-lasik suicides." Source: A Few Words About LASIK Dr. Arthur B. Epstein: “... many of us in the contact lens community have spent untold hours trying to help patients who have had their lives literally destroyed by LASIK.” Source: Review of Optometry, November 2006 OSN Supersite 5/19/2009: Dr. Lawless said his practice has amended its essential clause to include language about the risk of possible psychological damage from an ideal or less-than-ideal refractive surgery procedure in response to last year's U.S. Food and Drug Administration panel meeting on LASIK safety, which examined quality-of-life issues as well as reported psychological damage resulting from poor LASIK outcomes in patients. We are presenting this information because people, particularly prospective LASIK patients, need to know that LASIK complications may have a profound negative impact on quality of life. If you are experiencing depression or suicidal thoughts after LASIK, please get professional help. If you are in crises and need immediate help, click here, call 1-800-273-8255, or call 911. Want to share your feelings with others who've been there and understand? Join the discussion on FaceBook. If you experienced a bad outcome from LASIK with associated depression or thoughts of suicide, please file a MedWatch report with the FDA online. Alternatively, you may call FDA at 1-800-FDA-1088 to report by telephone, download the paper form and either fax it to 1-800-FDA-0178 or mail it to the address shown at the bottom of page 3, or download the MedWatcher Mobile App for reporting LASIK problems to the FDA using a smart phone or tablet. Read a sample of LASIK injury reports currently on file with the FDA. ARE YOU CONSIDERING LASIK OR ANOTHER FORM OF VISION CORRECTION SURGERY? Please consider this. There have been several Lasik-related suicides and countless anecdotal reports of Lasik-related depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress. In 2008, the FDA called a special public hearing on Lasik after injured Lasik patients complained to the agency. In the weeks leading up to the hearing, the media reported stories of Lasik-related suicides and poor outcomes. At the hearing, leading Lasik surgeons, in a concerted effort to protect the public perception of Lasik, lined up to testify that Lasik cannot not lead to depression or suicide. The Lasik industry even hired a psychiatrist, Jennifer Morse, MD, to testify that patients who are depressed after Lasik were already depressed before Lasik, and that there is no scientific evidence of any direct link between Lasik and depression or suicide. Morse was even quoted in an October, 2008 issue of EyeWorld magazine saying that unhappy Lasik patients have good Lasik results but defective brains (David Laber. Depressed patients can struggle coping with post-Lasik. EyeWorld. October 2008.). Physicians in every other medical specialty must acknowledge and address surgical complications or face lawsuits, discipline by state medical boards, and loss of medical license. In the field of Lasik, surgeons readily lie to protect themselves, their colleagues, and the Lasik gravy train. Below is a link to a February, 2016 review of the body of medical literature on surgical complications (all types of surgery) and their impact on patients’ psychosocial well-being. The conclusion states, "Surgical complications appear to be a significant and often long-term predictor of patient postoperative psychosocial outcomes. The results highlight the importance of attending to patients’ psychological needs in the aftermath of surgical complications." Be warned, if you experience problems after laser eye surgery, your surgeon will almost certainly downplay or dismiss your concerns. If you continue to complain, you will likely be met with animosity and eventual abandonment. Because Lasik is completely unnecessary and carries significant risk to a vital sensory organ, the Lasik industry will do whatever is necessary to conceal the problems. Please read the following article and consider the psychological toll that surgical complications take on patients. Lasik is no different. Are you willing to accept this hidden risk of Lasik? Pinto et al. Surgical complications and their impact on patients’ psychosocial well-being: a systematic review and meta-analysis. BMJ Open. 2016 Feb 16;6(2):e007224.
