EyeWorld, March 2012 From the article: "[LASIK] Patients go to their ophthalmologists, some of them suicidal because of the pain, and their eyes on the slit lamp look perfectly normal," said Perry Rosenthal, M.D., founder, Boston Foundation for Sight. "Doctors send them to a psychiatrist because they think the [patients are] exaggerating. It just adds to the patients' burden." Before you dismiss this patient as a crazy hypochondriac and send her on her way, consider corneal neuropathic disease, an extremely rare condition that causes intense pain along corneal nerves. Link to article EyeWorld, February 2012. Ectasia, topography readings hot topics among military refractive surgeons From the article: Corneal neuralgia is a newly described disease process refractive surgeons wish was mythological. Lt. Cmdr. John B. Cason, M.D., cornea, external disease, and refractive surgery, Naval Medical Center, San Diego, gave an overview of the symptoms, which many patients find excruciating. "The hallmark of this is how uncomfortable these patients are," he said. "But when you examine them, you don't see anything causing it. These patients are extremely difficult to treat; they keep coming back to your clinic. All the therapies you give them fail, and because of this, many of us think they're making it up." The pain these patients are feeling, however, is very real. Some patients are so uncomfortable and so despondent over failed treatments that they become suicidal. As one patient Dr. Cason had in fellowship said, "I want my eyes taken out or I want to die." Link to article Dr. Edward Boshnick testifies at FDA hearing regarding LASIK complications, depression and suicidality Beth Kotsovolos speaks on behalf of families affected by post-LASIK depression and suicide 4/25/2008 Diana Zuckerman, PhD, President, National Research Center for Women & Families wants more information about the Emory study of suicide rate among cornea donors (Go to 4:44). Dr. Zuckerman: "The possibility of a higher suicide rate among [LASIK] patients has been raised and will be raised. More research and really good quality objective scientific research is needed. I tried to get that information. I contacted Emory University but was not able to get better information about that research which, it has not been published." Patient with post-LASIK ectasia suffers depression and thoughts of suicide, now wearing speciality contact lenses Patient considers suicide after disastrous monovision LASIK Excerpt: "My life as a respected Police officer and my hobbies of fishing, scuba diving, surfing, working out at the gym, bicycling and living my life happily are now over and its been all Doom and Gloom for me and I have even considered suicide but a very close and loving friend has kept me from doing this. I would rather go blind and live in pain and anguish than to not see her again. At least that's what I feel today but I came very close a few different nights." Read story Perils of Lasik: Double Vision, Eye Pain and even a Suicide - Lawyers and Settlements 4/28/2008 From the article: Colin Dorrian was a promising law school student when he sought relief from a chronic dry eye condition by undergoing Lasik laser eye surgery. However, rather than improving his vision and freeing him from his contacts, the procedure left him with so much eye pain and fuzzy vision that after six years, he took his own life... Dorrian, who ended his own life six years after having Lasik surgery, should have been disqualified from having the surgery. His pupils were overly large, and he suffered from extreme dryness of the eyes—two conditions that should have disqualified him from having the procedure. And yet he was cleared for it. Link to article Some link depression, failed lasik. Patients with impaired sight turn suicidal; surgeons reject any connection The News & Observer 2/3/2008 Sabine Vollmer, Staff Writer From the article: "Frustration and even sorrow can follow any unsuccessful surgery, but when the procedure leaves a patient with unremitting eye pain or permanently impaired vision, the emotional toll can be particularly severe... Scientists at the Emory Eye Center in Atlanta reviewed suicides among organ donors who had had laser eye surgery. Preliminary results suggested the suicide rate might be four times as high among cornea donors who had had lasik as among cornea donors who had not... Christine Sindt, an optometrist and associate professor of clinical ophthalmology at the University of Iowa in Iowa City, Iowa, has encountered the psychological effects that patients experience when they have trouble seeing. "Depression is a problem for any patient with a chronic vision problem," she said. But in the case of post-lasik patients, she said, the depression is compounded by remorse. "It's not just that they lose vision," she said. "They paid somebody [who] took their vision away." Sindt specializes in treating ectasia, a bulging of the eye that is considered the most severe and rarest LASIK complication. She sees a few dozen patients with ectasia; all of them show signs of depression, she said." Link to article Severe Eye Pain After LASIK May Lead To Thoughts Of Suicide From the article: "The cornea is the most powerful pain generator in the human body, said Dr. Rosenthal. The density of corneal pain receptors has been estimated to be 40 times that of dental pulp. He explained that the damaged nerve fibers in the cornea, the sensory fibers, cause all the symptoms, whether or not the initial disease is severe dry eye or corneal neuropathy. The intensity and constancy of corneal neuralgia can be incapacitating and even induce thoughts of suicide, said Stephen C. Pflugfelder, MD. Dr. Pflugfelder, who is professor of ophthalmology and director of the Ocular Surface Center at the Baylor College of Medicine in Houston, had a patient with so much corneal neuropathy following LASIK that he begged to have his eyes enucleated."... "Jayne S. Weiss, MD, professor of ophthalmology and pathology at Kresge Eye Institute in Detroit, recalls such a case. A young man presented to her with excruciating corneal pain and evidence of a healed lamellar keratotomy. “Confocal microscopy revealed abnormal collections of unusually tortuous corneal nerves consistent with corneal neuropathy. Although vision eventually recovered to 20/20, the patient had asked at one point if he could have retrobulbar alcohol injected to dull the pain,” she said." Source: Miriam Karmel. Addressing the Pain of Corneal Neuropathy. EyeNet, Jul/Aug 2010. The CORS Survey From the abstract: A total of 517 responses were submitted. After exclusion criteria, 392 responses were coded resulting in 36 separate subjective complaint categories. 195/392 (50%) responded to the three follow-up questions. 58 subjects reported suicidal ideation as a result of the surgical procedure, and 83% (48/58) of this group were referred to as successes by their surgeon. 115 subjects reported severe depression as a result of the surgical procedure, and 76% (87/115) of this group were referred to as successes by their surgeon. IS LASIK A CRIMINAL ENTERPRISE? THE RELEASE OF THE CORS SURVEY. Link Effect of LASIK complications on quality of life reported to the FDA through the MedWatch program "There seems to be no solution. No one seems to understand the impact that this loss of vision has had on my life. My eye doctor talks as if i should be grateful because using the eye chart i can see around -. 05 in both eyes with a slight astigmatism. However, the crazy images i see have led me to be depressed to the point of considering suicide at times. I have not received a good explanation for why my vision is deteriorating." Read report "Due to my declining visual ability and debilitating depression, I am on [redacted by FDA], wherein I receive a monthly stipend of [redacted by FDA]. I have attempted suicide more than 5 times since my LASIK." Read report "Had LASIK eye surgery on [redacted], 2010. Now cannot see to do any of my daily tasks. Near and mid-vision are gone. I only see far away. Was only told I would need readers for the computer. Local doctor can't believe how farsighted they made me. I am preparing to commit suicide after I get me affairs in order for my children. Only those who have gone through this nightmare understand the guilt and shame. You spend thousands of dollars and have no recourse whatsoever when the outcome is bad. It is a great scam for the LASIK centers. Where on earth could you buy a product and then not have any recourse if it does not work??? Please put a stop to this before others die or ruin their lives!!! Please listen to us!!!" Read report "Bilateral Intralase LASIK performed in [redacted] 2010, using the VISX CustomVue system... Three months post surgery complications - pain in the right eye accompanied by headaches, dry eyes, appearance of many dark floaters, starbursts and halos around bright light sources -in both low and moderate lighting conditions-, reduced contrast sensitivity - reduced ability to see well in low lighting-, induced astigmatism. These conditions result in reduced ability to drive at night, reduced ability to concentrate while on the job, reduced ability to participate in activities and everyday tasks in low light settings; increased sensitivity to bright light, increased depression, increased anxiety." Read report "My quality of life has been ruined. I no longer can drive, work, and enjoy the things I loved so much, like reading, photography, cooking and so many other things. I am a prisoner in my home and have a hard time doing simple tasks... Therefore, this has not only cause a great hardship for myself, but my family as well... I am hoping you can prevent him from doing this to another patient and ruining another life." Read report "It has been 21 months since the surgery and my vision is still poor in the both eyes... I tried to get help from lasik plus, but all they could say to me was , we are sorry... If it were not for my strong relationship with Jesus Christ and many Christian brothers and sisters praying for me daily, would have most likely taken my life." Read report "I feel like my life has been ruined. And the thought of living the rest of my life with this pain is horrible." Read report "I have not had one happy moment since I started having complications. I am in a severe depression." Read report "It ruined my vision, and gave me permanent eye pain and dryness... It affects my work, my hobbies, every aspect of my life is pain and difficulty... I never knew depression until lasik." Read report "My vision has progressed to the point where my left eye is legally blind... This surgery has destroyed my life." Read report "The immediate effect of my lasik and dry eye was severe depression that endured for three years post-surgery... My mental state required me to make several treatment attempts including medication and therapy. I had no prior history of depression." Read report "Can you please finally do something about this before more people end up like me, hurt, injured, etc. Some people end up committing suicide... AMA and FDA: are you listening? What happened to ethics and the standard of care? The bar must be raised so that others don't have to experience this hell." Read report More » How does the LASIK industry respond to reports of LASIK-related depression and suicide? In response to reports of LASIK-related suicides, LASIK industry representatives made statements in the media and at an FDA hearing denying any connection between a bad outcome from LASIK and risk of depression or suicide... “There is simply no scientific basis to support a direct connection between a sub-optimal outcome from eye surgery and suicide.” Lindstrom, Richard. (2008, March 12) Letter to the News & Observer “There is no scientific evidence of any direct link between LASIK and the development of depression or suicide.” Morse M.D., Jennifer (2008, April 25) FDA Special Hearing on Post-LASIK Quality of Life “There's no cause and effect.” Schallhorn, Steven C. As quoted by Vollmer, S. "Some link depression, failed LASIK." News & Observer [Charlotte, NC] 3 February, 2008. "It would be an error to conclude that poor results of LASIK are the cause of suicide." R. Doyle Stulting, MD. (2008, March 5) Letter to the Chicago Tribune from Richard Lindstrom. Shocking videos of prominent LASIK surgeons performing comedy skits mocking damaged patients who are depressed or suicidal Watch Parag Majmudar, M.D.'s performance as "Dr. I. M. Suicial" at a convention of the American Society of Cataract and Refractive Surgery (ASCRS), singing of "pushing the boundaries of ethics". Below, skip to 42 seconds to watch comedy skit starring Dr. I. M. Suicial (Parag Majmudar, M.D.), William Trattler, M.D. and Jodi Luchs, M.D. Study finds link between visual impairment and risk of suicide Lam BL, Christ SL, Lee DJ, Zheng DD, Arheart KL. Reported visual impairment and risk of suicide: the 1986-1996 national health interview surveys. Arch Ophthalmol. 2008 Jul;126(7):975-80. Excerpts: Increased mortality risks also have been noted in adults with visual impairment and disabling eye disease... However, reported visual impairment indirectly increased suicide risk significantly by 5% through poor self-rated health and by 12% through the number of nonocular conditions. The combined indirect effects of reported visual impairment operating jointly through poorer self-rated health and a higher number of reported nonocular conditions increased the risk of suicide significantly by 18%. When we examined the combined indirect effects of the other covariates in the model, only older age produced stronger associations, providing further support for the importance of the indirect effects of visual impairment as a contributor to increased risk of suicide... Up to two-thirds of people who commit suicide have some type of physician contact in their last month, and physician education is effective in reducing suicide rates... In summary, we observed that reported visual impairment increased suicide risk, particularly indirectly via reported health status and health conditions. Link to abstract Reported cases of post-LASIK depression in the medical literature Disappointment of patients on antidepressant therapy after excimer laser treatment. Cataract Refract Surg. 2006 Oct;32(10):1775-6. Celik L, Kaynak T, Ozerdem A, Kocak N, Kaynak S. Excerpts: Case 1 A 46-year-old woman was admitted with complaints of severe dry-eye symptoms, blurred and double vision, halos around objects, and inability to look at oncoming car lights. She had had excimer laser surgery 2 years earlier for myopia, which she said was around −10.00 diopters (D) and −11.50 D in the right and left eyes, respectively... Severe dry eye and visual disturbances due to a mid-dilated pupil were diagnosed. The prescription included frequent instillation of artificial tears and pilocarpine drops 4 times daily. Punctum plugs were also advised, but the patient did not want another ocular procedure. She called the office 4 days later from a psychiatry clinic where she was hospitalized because of intolerable visual disturbances resulting from longstanding pupil dilation. The patient did not return for a follow-up visit. Case 2 A 34-year-old woman reported dryness of the eyes and visual disturbances after LASIK 11 months earlier. According to her statements, the refraction in her spectacles was approximately −8.00 D and −7.00 D. She was receiving antidepressant therapy with seroxate; one reason she had LASIK was to improve her mood and increase her self-confidence. She reported that the result of the surgery was a “catastrophe,” “worse than anything I could imagine,” and that “life became a burden” for her. I've reached the end Originally posted on Dr. Gerald Horn's board at http://www.chicagolasercenter.com Posted on Wednesday, 23 May 2001, at 8:13 p.m. Excerpt: I cannot battle this anymore, there is no current and there will be no future hope for me. I am so sad that my life has been stolen from me this way. How did this nightmare happen? Is this real? Did I really do this to myself? How did my eyes end up so screwed?...My pain now is not from my eyes, but from the thoughts of how devasted my family will be from this. I made the biggest mistake of my life, the biggest mistake ANYONE COULD POSSIBLY make. In one instant my life was destroyed and now my family and friends will suffer to. I only hope that they know this is to end my suffering. Read entire post LASIK and Quality of Life "When compared with a hearing-impaired control group, impaired sight alone can acutely affect otherwise psychologically healthy individuals." Source: Psychosomatics, Jul-Aug 1999 Read article “Functional vision for the normally sighted person is so basic to everyday living that sudden, or even gradual, vision loss can have a devastating impact on quality of life.” Source: Cleveland Clinic website [2009, February 24] Link Robert Latkany, M.D.: "I have patients from around the world. They’re desperate and crying out for help,” he said. “It’s borderline scary. Numerous times I’ve heard people say they want to commit suicide. To the eye doctor, [dry eye] is just a nuisance [when], in fact, these patients are really suffering.” Source Tuisku I, Tervo T, Belmonte C.: "It is important to comprehend that these individuals may be suffering from “phantom eye pain,” which does not have a psychiatric or “central pain” etiology. The recent discussion of mental depression up to suicide after LASIK suggests that we need to pay more attention to the pain issue after this therapy and analyze whether this problem is associated with unrealistic expectations, poor results, or development of peripheral neuropathic pain." Source: Letters to the Editor. J Refract Surg. Oct 2008, pg 772. College baseball player, Trevor Tyre, finds healing after LASIK "depressing" - Dothan Eagle 5/19/2009 From the article: He had the eye surgery in December and had to put in eye drops every day for three months to regain the strength in his eyes. He could see, but not well enough... He saw an eye doctor who recommended the LASIK surgery, but Tyre still wasn’t sure. “I had my disbeliefs,” Tyre said. “Some people say you see halos around car lights and there’s only a 50 percent chance it works. But my doctor assured me that with my young age, it wasn’t as bad as it could be.” “I couldn’t pick up the rotation or spin on the ball,” Tyre said. “It was real depressing." Source

Last night, the devastating news of meteorologist Jessica Starr's passing reached us. We are **deeply shocked** and heartbroken to learn that our **wonderful, bright, and intelligent colleague** has taken her own life. The entire FOX 2 team is struggling to comprehend this loss.

Our **thoughts and prayers** are with Jessica's family and friends as we navigate this profound grief together.

If you or someone you know is experiencing distress, please reach out for help. The **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline** offers **free and confidential emotional support 24/7**. You can call them at **1-800-273-8255** or text **741-741**.

Jessica was a proud **Michigan native**, born in Southfield and raised in Commerce Township. Her passion for meteorology led her to earn **two degrees** in the field from **Michigan State University** and **Mississippi State University**.

query: What did House Speaker John Boehner state about raising the debt ceiling during his interview?

documents.text: The House won't raise the debt ceiling without attaching other demands to the action, Speaker John Boehner said Sunday. "We're not going to pass a clean debt limit increase," the Ohio Republican said on ABC's "This Week." " I told the president, there's no way we're going to pass one. The votes are not in the House to pass a clean debt limit. And the president is risking default by not having a conversation with us." Boehner's comments come after reports last week that he's privately said he would hold a no-strings-attached vote to raise the debt ceiling in order to avoid default and its cascading negative effects. "We're not going down that path" of passing a clean bill to raise the debt ceiling," he said. "It is time to deal with America's problems. How can you raise the debt limit and do nothing about the underlying problem?" "We've spent more than what we've brought in for 55 of the last 60 years," he continued. "This year, the federal government will have more revenue than any year in the history of our country, and yet we're still going to have a nearly $700 billion budget deficit. We're squandering the future for our kids and our grandkids, by not dealing with this problem." Read more about: Barack Obama, John Boehner, Debt Ceiling

The Washington Redskins' team name has been a focal point of significant controversy, with widespread calls for its change due to its offensive nature. This debate has been amplified by prominent figures weighing in on the issue, highlighting the deep-seated objections.

Journalist Cokie Roberts, a self-proclaimed long-time fan, shared her views on ABC's "This Week." While humorously admitting her primary concern was the team's performance, she directly addressed the problematic team name. Roberts asserted that employing racial slurs for team mascots is unequivocally unacceptable, drawing a clear parallel: "We wouldn't call a team the Yellowskins or the Brownskins. It would be absolutely unacceptable." She underscored the critical importance of heeding the concerns of Native Americans who find the name offensive, stating, "And if people who are Native Americans are offended by it, we should pay attention to that.”

The controversy gained renewed national attention when President Barack Obama commented that he would consider changing the NFL team's name if he were its owner, acknowledging the pervasive objections. Former broadcast journalist Soledad O'Brien emphasized the intensity of public sentiment surrounding the issue, noting that "someone walking into a bar 'would find that people have strong opinions about it.'" O'Brien stressed the significance of validating the feelings of those who are offended, stating, "and that 'the people who are offended are offended.'" She further elaborated, "Even if that's not a majority of Americans or Native Americans, 'I think that matters.'"

Steve Rattner, formerly the car czar, drew a comparison between Redskins owner Dan Snyder and House Speaker John Boehner, suggesting Snyder might adopt a "hang tough until you can’t anymore" strategy. Rattner also proposed that Snyder could consider reviving the Washington Senators' former name. However, Rattner cautioned that this alternative might be "even more" offensive. As the discussion concluded, host George Stephanopoulos offered his own thought, musing that perhaps "the bigger problem these days might be the name Washington in front of it." The ongoing controversy surrounding the Redskins' team name continues to be a prominent subject of public discourse.

Treasury Secretary Jack Lew issued a stark warning on Sunday, asserting that Congress is "playing with fire" by risking a failure to raise the nation's debt ceiling. Lew emphasized the critical juncture, stating, "I'm telling you that on the 17th, we run out of our ability to borrow, and Congress is playing with fire."

He further elaborated on the precipitous consequences, explaining, "If they don't extend the debt limit, we have a very, very short window of time before those scenarios start to be played out."

This imminent fiscal cliff underscores the profound implications of legislative inaction on the nation's borrowing capacity. The Treasury Department's ability to meet its financial obligations hinges on Congress's timely action.

Failure to raise the debt limit could trigger a cascade of negative economic events, impacting everything from government services to the broader financial markets. The consequences of such a default would be far-reaching and severe.

Treasury Secretary Jack Lew responded on Sunday to House Speaker John Boehner's assertion that there weren't enough votes in the House to pass a "clean" continuing spending resolution by challenging him to put the bill on the floor for a vote. "Why doesn't he put it on the floor and give it a chance?," Lew asked on CBS' "Face the Nation." I worked for Speaker (Tip) O' Neill who believed deeply the one thing he believed the American people won't tolerate is obstructionism. He put things on the floor, and sometimes he won and sometimes he lost. But that's the right thing to do." "Let the Congress vote," Lew said. Boehner, a Republican from Ohio, said earlier Sunday on the same show that there weren't enough votes in the House to pass a clean CR. Read more about: John Boehner, Speaker Of The House, Government Shutdown, Jack Lew, Continuing Resolutions

House Speaker John Boehner indicated on Sunday that a quick end to the government shutdown and the approaching debt ceiling crisis is unlikely. Speaking on ABC's "This Week," Boehner expressed doubt about when these linked issues would be resolved, stating, "If I knew, I would tell you."

Boehner made it clear he would not support "clean" bills, which would only address the shutdown and debt ceiling increase without any attached conditions. This position differs from the approach favored by Democrats and President Obama, who prefer such simple measures. Instead, Boehner emphasized his desire for direct negotiations.

"I'm ready for a conversation," Boehner said. "I'll take anybody on the Democrats' side who wants to seriously sit down and begin to work out this problem. I'm a reasonable guy. But I didn't come to Washington to be a congressman. I came here to do something on behalf of my country." This view contrasts with President Obama's consistent stance that he will not negotiate, arguing that Republicans should not be allowed to "exact a ransom" for performing basic government functions.

Key points from Boehner's remarks include:

*   **Uncertainty:** A swift resolution to the shutdown and debt ceiling standoff is not expected.
*   **No Unconditional Bills:** He will not back legislation that only deals with the shutdown and debt ceiling increase without other provisions.
*   **Open to Talks:** Boehner is prepared for direct discussions with Democrats to find a solution.
*   **Purpose of Service:** The Speaker stated his motivation for public service was to achieve significant results for the country.

Senator Ted Cruz has identified the debt ceiling debate as a crucial opportunity to address and counteract the impacts of President Barack Obama's signature healthcare legislation, the Affordable Care Act (ACA). During an appearance on CNN's "State of the Union," Cruz articulated his belief that Congress has a responsibility to actively seek out and implement measures aimed at diminishing the negative consequences associated with Obamacare.

He further elaborated on the historical role of the debt ceiling, highlighting its function as a significant tool for Congress to exercise oversight and control over executive branch decisions. Cruz argued that this inherent leverage should be thoughtfully utilized as a means to challenge and modify the president's key healthcare initiative.

Beyond the specific focus on the ACA, Cruz proposed that the debt ceiling negotiations could also serve as a platform to advocate for a broader fiscal strategy. This strategy, he suggested, should encompass a robust plan for decreasing overall government spending and preventing the introduction of any new tax burdens on citizens. Cruz firmly stated that the President's stance, which insists on an unconditional increase to the nation's borrowing limit, is an unacceptable and unsustainable position.

Senator Ted Cruz views the recent **criticisms from Democrats** as a **direct consequence of his effective strategy** concerning the **Affordable Care Act (ACA)**, also known as Obamacare. He expressed on CNN's "State of the Union" that the **"nasty partisan jabs"** and **"ad hominem inflammatory attacks"** from Democrats, including Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid, signal that his **message is resonating**. Cruz interprets these attacks as evidence that **"we're winning the argument -- Obamacare isn't working."**

These statements from the Texas Republican came after President Barack Obama commented on freshman senators, including Cruz, who are pursuing national recognition. President Obama suggested that in today's media environment, taking **controversial stances** and **rallying extreme elements of one's base** can swiftly garner attention and funding. However, he warned that this tactic is **"not good for government."**

Senator Rand Paul has strongly condemned President Barack Obama's administration for even **discussing the possibility of the U.S. government defaulting on its debt**. Speaking on NBC's "Meet the Press," the Republican senator from Kentucky labeled such contemplation as **"irresponsible,"** emphasizing that **"there is no reason for us to default."** He argued that the administration's rhetoric is precisely what is **"scaring the marketplace."**

Paul explained his stance by pointing out the federal government's financial capacity. He stated that the U.S. government collects around **$250 billion in taxes monthly**, while the interest payments on the national debt are approximately **$20 billion**. "Tell me why we would ever default," he challenged, questioning the rationale behind such a scenario. Additionally, Paul cited the **Full Faith and Credit Act**, asserting that it **mandates the president to prioritize interest payments on the debt.**

He further characterized the administration's approach to the debt ceiling debate as a **political tactic**, comparing it to "closing the World War II Memorial" rather than a reflection of a genuine crisis.

These remarks followed Treasury Secretary Jack Lew's own appearances on Sunday news programs, including "Meet the Press." Lew had warned that the nation is **"on the verge of going into a place we’ve never been."** He contended that if the government depletes its cash reserves for payments, there would be **"no option that permits us to pay all of our bills on time."** Lew concluded that a failure by Congress to raise the debt ceiling would **"for the first time put us in a place where we’re defaulting on our obligations as a government because of Congress’s failure to act."**

Senator Chuck Schumer has issued a direct challenge to House Speaker John Boehner regarding the funding of the government. Following Boehner's statement on Sunday that there weren't enough votes in the House for a simple continuing resolution, Schumer, appearing on ABC's "This Week," proposed, "Put it on the floor Monday or Tuesday."

Schumer voiced strong conviction about the potential outcome.

He asserted, "I would bet there are the votes to pass it."

He further elaborated on the expected support, noting, "We have just about every Democrat." Additionally, he highlighted, "Twenty-one Republicans have publicly said they would [vote for it]." Schumer also indicated that "there are many more Republicans who have said that they privately would."

To conclude his remarks, Schumer directly addressed the Speaker, urging, "So, Speaker Boehner, just vote. Put it on the floor and let's see if you're right."

query: What were some significant events and milestones in Queen Elizabeth II's life leading up to her 90th birthday?

documents.text:
Queen Elizabeth II, Britain's longest-reigning monarch, ascended to the throne on **February 6, 1952**, marking the beginning of an era defined by her steadfast dedication and service. Born **Elizabeth Alexandra Mary on April 21, 1926**, in London, she was named after her mother. Her early life included a christening at Buckingham Palace on May 29, 1926, and the birth of her sister, Princess Margaret, in 1930. A significant shift occurred in **1936** when her uncle, King Edward VIII, **abdicated**, positioning her father, King George VI, as monarch and herself as heir presumptive.

During World War II, she was evacuated to Windsor Castle for safety. Her education was private, covering constitutional history, law, and religious studies. She met Prince Philip in 1934 and learned to drive in 1945. The couple **married Prince Philip**, Duke of Edinburgh, at Westminster Abbey on November 20, 1947. They resided in Malta from 1949 to 1951 while he served in the Royal Navy. Her **ascension to the throne** followed her father's death in February 1952. She is the mother of four children: Charles, Anne, Andrew, and Edward.

Throughout her extensive reign, Queen Elizabeth II undertook **five official state visits to the United States** and responded to **over 3.5 million pieces of correspondence**. Her beloved corgis numbered **more than 30**, starting with her first, Susan. She famously described Prince Philip as her "**strength and stay**." They were the first royal couple to celebrate **60 years of marriage**. Her **Diamond Jubilee in 2012** saw her receive over 120,000 cards and gifts. Notably, her coronation was the **first to be televised**. Her great-grandson, Prince George, affectionately calls her "Gan-Gan." The oldest recipient of a message from the Queen was a 116-year-old Canadian man in 1984. She carried cash solely for Sunday church donations.

Her coronation ceremony, held at Westminster Abbey in June 1953, was marked by rain. Post-coronation events included extensive tours across the UK and a naval review. During her **Silver Jubilee in 1977**, she toured 36 counties across the UK and Northern Ireland, as well as Australia, New Zealand, and Papua New Guinea, covering an estimated 56,000 miles. She initiated a chain of celebratory beacons by lighting one in June 1977, and approximately 4,000 street parties were held nationwide. In **2002**, she celebrated **50 years on the throne**, visiting every region of the UK and numerous other countries. That year featured concerts with prominent artists and a "Children's Party at the Palace" for her 80th birthday. For her **60th wedding anniversary**, she revisited her honeymoon location and recreated wedding photos. The Queen's Diamond Jubilee Trust supported initiatives like Queen's Young Leaders.

Queen Elizabeth II's favorite animals were **horses and dogs**. She regularly attended horse racing events and owned and bred thoroughbred horses. She also engaged in Scottish country dancing and supported nearly 40 animal welfare organizations and almost 90 medical charities. Her birthday is observed on two occasions: April 21 and in June, a tradition for monarchs. She met with the British Prime Minister **weekly for an hour**.

Fluent in French, Queen Elizabeth II was the 40th monarch since William the Conqueror. During her reign, **twelve U.S. presidents** served, and **seven Roman Catholic popes** held office. As a child, she was a member of the Girl Guides and Sea Rangers and has **30 godchildren**. She became the **first British monarch to visit China in 1986**, and her **first email was sent in 1976**. Her reign saw **six Archbishops of Canterbury** serve. She developed the "**dorgi**" breed and once demoted a footman for adding alcohol to a corgi's water. She is the **oldest individual to celebrate 60 years on the throne**. In 2005, she inaugurated the first children's trail in the Buckingham Palace garden. The British Monarchy YouTube channel, launched in 2007, has garnered millions of views. She is the **only person in the UK exempt from needing a driver's license or license plates** and does not require a passport for international travel. During World War II, she operated a truck and trained as a mechanic. Whales, dolphins, porpoises, and sturgeons are classified as "**Fishes Royal**" and belong to the monarch if caught within three miles of the UK coast. She donated rare animal gifts, such as jaguars, to the London Zoo and used rationing coupons to buy materials for her wedding dress.

Her extensive travels included visits to **over 115 countries**. She referenced meeting Game of Thrones actors in her Christmas message after visiting the set. Her affinity for dogs stems from Queen Victoria. Only five other British monarchs have reigned for 50 years or more. She visited Edinburgh nearly every year during Holyrood Week and gave royal assent to **over 3,500 Acts of Parliament**. She visited the Vatican on three occasions, meeting Pope Francis in 2014. The Royal Yacht Britannia traveled over 1 million miles during its service. She undertook **more than 250 overseas journeys** and attended every State Opening of Parliament except for two instances when she was pregnant. The Royal Family's official website was launched in 1997. She was the **first reigning monarch to visit the Republic of Ireland in 2011**. Her reign saw the **introduction of the internet and mobile phones**. She was the **longest-reigning female head of state in history**. Her coronation was the first to be filmed. She was the **first British monarch to send an email** and the first to visit Australia more than once. Her reign spanned **15 Prime Ministers**.

Queen Elizabeth is the longest-reigning monarch in British history. Look back at moments from her life so far. Queen Elizabeth is the longest-reigning monarch in British history. Look back at moments from her life so far. I was covering the Golden Jubilee in 2002 and we'd asked the Palace if on the opening day, she would adjust her schedule by a few minutes to help the broadcasters. The Palace agreed. So when I met Her Majesty, I thanked her for changing her schedule. Unfortunately, I pronounced it "SKedule" not "SHedule. I'd used the American pronunciation in front of the Queen AND in her Castle. Brilliant move! She gently corrected me and then laughed. The King was right. I still remember our meeting, which is why, even during my years living in America, I never use the American pronunciation. And if any one questions me about it, I tell them to ring Buckingham Palace for an explanation! Stage presence When you meet the Queen, she tries to put you at ease. But it's still nerve wracking. After I'd shaken her hand, I thought about all those world leaders, pop stars, sports legends and others who've shaken THAT hand. Despite being 5'4" tall, the Queen has enormous presence when she enters the room. It's a most unusual feeling. My first encounter with Her Majesty came during her Silver Jubilee in 1977. She visited my hometown of Hull in the North of England. I wonder if she'd forgiven the city for rising up against one of her predecessors, King Charles I. Hide Caption 10 of 10 The protests had ignited the English Civil War that led to the King losing his head -- and all future monarchs ruling not through the Divine Right, but with the blessing of their people. That first sight of the Queen was one I'll always remember. We didn't see her often in those days before 24 hour news. Sure, she was on our stamps, our coins and her picture hung in the school assembly hall. But to see her in the flesh was quite something. I was with hundreds of choirboys that day singing to her. I really hope she doesn't remember that! Patience - more than a virtue Over the years, I've seen the Queen in action on many Royal visits. God only knows how she does it. She travels all over the world, meets countless people, tries to ensure a routine day for her is a special day for those she meets. One event after another, listening to and giving speeches, shaking endless outstretched hands and all with a smile on her face. It must be tough because, in truth, some of the speeches can be dull and some of the events are pretty boring. But she can't exactly yawn when she's bored or, indeed, tired. And if she is bored or tired, you'd never know it. Little is known about her JUST WATCHED The contentious Brexit issue has even reached the Queen Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH The contentious Brexit issue has even reached the Queen 01:37 Despite being one of the most famous people in the world, very few of her subjects really know much about her. She's never given an interview, never written an autobiography. We know she likes horses, corgis and the outdoors. We know also that she's a deeply religious person and has a sense of humor. Apparently she's an excellent mimic. But we know nothing about her politics or her views on anything remotely controversial. Sense of duty Some commentators have criticized her for being a bit remote as a mother. Yet when she put her grandmotherly responsibilities to Princes William and Harry first in the days after Princess Diana died, she was criticized for that too. I remember that time very well and the angry mood of the British people -- it was unprecedented. That week saw the biggest wobble of her reign. What a remarkable turnaround that just five years later, millions of people around the Commonwealth came onto the streets to celebrate her Golden Jubilee. They met at school and even shared a house with others while students. Hide Caption 34 of 34 So why is the Queen so popular? JUST WATCHED The world's longest serving monarchs Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH The world's longest serving monarchs 01:23 Well for a start she's not a politician. She also stands for something and does not do knee-jerk reactions to some poll or focus group. Again, not a politician. She's seen as genuine, somebody who cares about her country, her Commonwealth and her people. In times of sadness and despair, people look to the Queen and her family for words of support. I remember her doing just that after the London bombings of 2005. She and her family are also there in times of joy to celebrate with us. Remember that dramatic arrival with James Bond at the opening of the London Olympics? The Queen also provides a sense of continuity in a changing world. Sure, there are people who would like to see an end of monarchy but they are few in number compared to those who, day in day out, welcome the Queen to their towns, cities and homes around the world. Her realms and territories extend across the globe. She's monarch in places ranging from New Zealand in the south to Scotland in the north, taking in Australia, Canada and a host of Caribbean countries. She's also Head of the Commonwealth -- a 53-nation club whose membership numbers almost a third of the world's population. On those occasions when millions turn up outside Buckingham Palace and sing God Save the Queen, they truly mean it. She wears the Imperial State Crown and the Robe of State, also known as the Parliamentary Robe. Her gown is of white silk with bands of gold and silver embroidery. (Photo credit /AFP/Getty Images)

The enduring fascination with portraying the Queen on stage and screen intensifies as she approaches her 90th birthday. This widespread interest begs the question: what draws so many performers to embody one of the world's most recognizable figures, and what are the challenges involved?

For many actresses, stepping into the role of the Queen might seem daunting. However, two performers who have embraced this challenge on stage highlight a crucial element for embodying the monarch: the wig. Emma Handy, who portrayed the 1980s iteration of the Queen in the touring production of Moira Buffini's play *Handbagged*, found the transformative power of costume invaluable. The play itself imagines conversations between older and younger versions of the Queen and Margaret Thatcher during their weekly audiences. Handy states, "The wig and make-up and clothes do so much. It's extraordinary." She also observed, "She seems to have two faces – the public and the private face. There is very little footage of her private life, but she is known to be enormously mischievous – and these are things I have in common with her." Handy emphasized her focus on capturing the Queen's "essence" rather than resorting to mere impersonation, finding that sitting "incredibly straight-backed" aided her portrayal. She noted, "She's probably the most photographed person in the world, so everyone has an idea of her. Every year you see the Christmas broadcast, but that's not who she is. There is a massive side of her people don't know." Marion Bailey, who played the older Queen, referred to as 'Q', in the West End production, described the role as "irresistible." She recounted an instance where someone commented on her lack of resemblance to the Queen, to which she confidently replied, "Oh, but I do." Bailey confessed to often looking in the mirror during makeup sessions and thinking, "You know what? I could do the Queen." In preparation for the London run of *Handbagged*, Bailey immersed herself in the Queen's Christmas messages and any other available online footage. The audience's reaction was profoundly rewarding. She recalled, "Some nights I would walk out and there would be gasps. It was to do with the look of it and the history, and I tried to get as many of the mannerisms as I could. On the West End transfer, I heard an American tourist whisper: 'Is that the Queen?'"

The Queen's presence in popular culture is undeniable, with numerous portrayals across various media. Film: Peter Morgan's 2006 film *The Queen* starred Helen Mirren as the monarch, focusing on the public reaction to Princess Diana's death. Theatre: Morgan's play *The Audience* also featured Helen Mirren in its West End and Broadway runs, with Kristin Scott Thomas taking on the role in London. Television: Channel 4's *The Queen* featured five actresses, including Emilia Fox and Samantha Bond, depicting the monarch at different life stages. The upcoming Netflix series *The Crown*, written by Morgan, stars Claire Foy and chronicles six decades of the Queen's life. Music: Songs such as "Her Majesty" by The Beatles and "God Save the Queen" by the Sex Pistols have been written about the Queen. Literature: Alan Bennett explored the Queen's life in his book *The Uncommon Reader* and his play *A Question of Attribution*, which includes a scene with the Queen and spy Anthony Blunt. Lookalikes: Queen lookalike Jeannette Charles built a career impersonating the monarch, appearing in films like *The Naked Gun* and *Austin Powers in Goldmember*.

Professor Philip Murphy, director of the Institute of Commonwealth Studies at the University of London, suggests that the 1969 documentary *Royal Family* offered the public their closest glimpse of the actual Queen. However, he notes, "The Royal Family effectively suppressed it. They decided it reduced the mystique around them. In a way, that creates a carte blanche for writers and satirists to make up personalities." He elaborates, "That's the problem the Royal Family has – they want to appear human but they also want to appear somehow removed and keep this dignified distance." Professor Murphy also points out the challenge of "mixing fact and fiction" when writing about the Queen, as "the public have very little sense of what actually goes on. But a plausible fictional representation can almost seep into historical record." He acknowledges that portrayals have not always been flattering, citing *Spitting Image* as an example of satire that "was an attack on British deference around authority figures." John O'Farrell, a lead writer for *Spitting Image*, recalls a shift in public perception: "If you cast your mind back to the 1980s, there was an enormous reverential style. It was seen as poor form to laugh at the Queen's expense... But what we did was show her to be an ordinary person in an ordinary family, which is where it struck a comic nerve." He adds, "The tabloids faked outrage, but people loved it... At times it was crude and disrespectful – but I think that's warranted." Artist Alison Jackson has also courted controversy with her work, which uses lookalikes to create seemingly candid photographs of celebrities in private moments. Jackson's images depict "the Queen" engaging in everyday activities like feeding her corgis, taking selfies, and doing the washing-up. Jackson explains her motivation: "I thought it would be really good to get her doing normal things. She is a person, after all. And she does do normal things – she reads, she drives a Land Rover, she gets her hands dirty – she's quite tomboyish, really. She's not a diva and seems to come across as a normal woman, even though of course she's not." She notes that her photograph of the Queen on the toilet is her least published, suggesting that "people think breaking that taboo is tasteless, which I find interesting. But it's designed to be thought-provoking, rather than controversial." While these fictional representations may be thought-provoking, they arguably do not bring us closer to understanding the Queen's true self. The sole instance of her playing herself was in a James Bond sketch alongside Daniel Craig for the London Olympics opening ceremony in 2012. Nevertheless, this will not deter continued efforts to capture the essence of this elusive, yet ever-present, figure.

### Prince William Reflects on Queen Elizabeth II's Unwavering Support After Princess Diana's Death

Prince William has shared a deeply personal account of the crucial support he received from his grandmother, Queen Elizabeth II, in the wake of his mother, Princess Diana's, tragic passing. Speaking in an interview with Sky News, conducted prior to the monarch's 90th birthday, the Duke of Cambridge emphasized the Queen's role as a vital "strong female influence" and a steadfast pillar of strength during his formative teenage years.

"Having lost my mother at a very young age, it has been particularly important for me that I had somebody like the Queen to look up to and who has been there and has understood some of the more complex issues when you lose a loved one," William stated, his voice tinged with evident emotion. He was a mere 15 years old when Princess Diana tragically died in a Paris car crash in 1997. During this intensely challenging period, the Queen and the Duke of Edinburgh faced considerable public scrutiny for their decision to remain at Balmoral with William and his younger brother, Prince Harry, rather than immediately returning to Buckingham Palace. This sensitive time, marked by overwhelming public attention and profound personal grief, is a subject William has seldom addressed, making his reflections particularly poignant and significant.

The Queen's counsel proved instrumental as he navigated the immense waves of grief and the relentless glare of public attention. Her reassuring presence offered a crucial sense of stability and continuity during a period of profound upheaval and uncertainty. Her ability to provide quiet, unobtrusive support, coupled with her deep, lived understanding of loss, was profoundly cherished and provided a bedrock of comfort.

### A Childhood Lesson in Caution at Balmoral

Beyond the profound moments of grief, Prince William also recounted a memorable and somewhat humorous childhood anecdote from his time at Balmoral, the Royal Family's cherished Scottish estate. He vividly recalled an incident where he and his cousin were engaged in a spirited chase on quad bikes, playfully pursuing Zara, who was riding a go-cart. In their youthful exuberance and perhaps a touch of recklessness, they steered Zara perilously close to a lamppost, resulting in her fall and a near miss with serious injury.

"I remember my grandmother being the first person out and running across the lawn in her kilt," William recounted with a smile, recalling the scene. "She came charging over and gave us the most almighty b*****king." This unforgettable experience served as a significant and lasting lesson, instilling in him the paramount importance of greater caution and the critical need to avoid causing harm to others. The Queen's immediate and decisive concern for Zara's well-being, coupled with her firm reprimand, powerfully underscored her protective instincts and unwavering commitment to her grandchildren's safety. This incident became a lasting illustration of her character and deeply held values, teaching him invaluable lessons about responsibility and the tangible consequences of impulsive actions.

### Balancing Royal Duties, Family, and a Meaningful Career

Prince William also confirmed that he has his grandmother's unwavering support for his ongoing efforts to strike a balance between his demanding royal duties, his cherished family life, and his fulfilling role as an air ambulance pilot. "Surprisingly, she is very supportive of what I'm doing at the moment," he stated, acknowledging her understanding. "She understands that I'm a family man and I want to be around for my children as much as I can." He described the Queen as being "very much at the helm," highlighting her continued active engagement and dedication even in her later years. He also graciously acknowledged the significant contributions of his father, the Prince of Wales, and the wider royal family, anticipating a natural and appropriate increase in responsibilities for other senior royals as time progresses.

The Duke of Cambridge expressed his readiness to embrace a more prominent role in royal duties should the need arise. However, his current priorities remain firmly fixed on his young children and his "incredibly worthwhile and meaningful job" as an air ambulance pilot. He firmly believes that these commitments, alongside fulfilling his duties to the Queen, represent a "good balance" for his life. The Queen's profound understanding of his deep desire to be present for his children's milestones fosters the essential flexibility he needs to dedicate quality time to his family while diligently fulfilling his royal obligations. Her forward-thinking perspective on modern family dynamics and the evolving nature of public service has been a source of considerable encouragement and inspiration.

**A Photographic Journey Through the Remarkable Life of Queen Elizabeth II**

Queen Elizabeth II, the longest-reigning monarch in British history, has lived a life of extraordinary significance. This collection of photographs offers a glimpse into pivotal moments, from her early years to her enduring reign, showcasing a journey marked by duty, family, and unwavering public service.

Born on April 21, 1926, in London, Elizabeth Alexandra Mary was the daughter of Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon and Prince Albert, Duke of York. Her father would later ascend to the throne as King George VI. Early photographs capture Princess Elizabeth as a young child, often alongside her mother and sister, Princess Margaret, her only sibling. These formative years, including visits to Balmoral, Scotland, and attendance at theatrical events, laid the groundwork for her future role.

The path to the throne was significantly shaped by her uncle, Edward, Prince of Wales. His abdication in 1936, after a brief reign as King Edward VIII, meant that Elizabeth's father became King, and she, at just ten years old, became the heir presumptive. This pivotal event marked a profound shift in her destiny, preparing her for the immense responsibilities that lay ahead.

During World War II, a 14-year-old Princess Elizabeth participated in her first radio broadcast on October 13, 1940, conveying a message of cheerfulness and courage from England's children. She also took on honorary military roles, being made an honorary colonel in the Grenadier Guards by her father in 1942. These experiences demonstrated an early commitment to national service.

Her personal life also saw significant milestones. On April 21, 1947, her 21st birthday, she was photographed in South Africa's Natal National Park. Later that year, in July, her engagement to Prince Philip of Greece, a lieutenant in the British Navy, was announced. He became a naturalized British citizen, renounced his Greek titles, and was granted the title His Royal Highness Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh. Their wedding took place on November 20, 1947, with the royal couple later seen waving from the balcony of Buckingham Palace.

The ascension to the throne occurred in February 1952, following the death of her father, King George VI, from lung cancer. Her first official engagement as Queen was a ball in Edinburgh in June 1952. The coronation ceremony, a grand spectacle, took place on June 2, 1953, at Westminster Abbey, making her the sixth female monarch to reign in Britain.

Throughout her reign, Queen Elizabeth II undertook numerous royal tours, including a visit to Australia in March 1954, where she was photographed at Melbourne's Government House. Family life remained central, with photographs capturing her with her children, Charles and Anne, and later her son Andrew. Public duties continued, including appearances at the state opening of Parliament and significant national events.

The investiture of her eldest son, Prince Charles, as Prince of Wales in 1969, was a notable occasion. The Queen and Prince Philip were seen departing Tokyo in May 1975. A portrait taken at Windsor Castle for her 50th birthday on April 21, 1976, marked another personal milestone. Royal tours continued, including a visit to New Zealand in 1977, where she met the public.

The wedding of Prince Charles and Lady Diana Spencer on July 29, 1981, was a globally televised event. Personal moments, such as Queen Elizabeth taking photographs of her husband at a horse show in Windsor in May 1982, and her reaction to an elephant at a charity event in London in June 1987, offer glimpses into her life beyond official duties. She also participated in ceremonial events, including firing a rifle during a visit to the Army Rifle Association in July 1993.

The tragic death of Princess Diana in 1997 prompted a period of national mourning. Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip viewed floral tributes at Buckingham Palace, and the Queen addressed the nation on the eve of Diana's funeral. Later, in April 2005, she was present at the wedding of Prince Charles to Camilla, Duchess of Cornwall.

Significant anniversaries and events marked her later reign. In October 2009, she and Prince Philip attended a commemoration service for the end of combat operations in Iraq. On April 29, 2011, she greeted crowds from the Buckingham Palace balcony following the wedding of her grandson, Prince William, to Catherine Middleton. She was also seen driving her Range Rover at the Windsor Horse Show in May 2011 and signed the visitors' book at the Irish President's official residence in Dublin.

The year 2012 was particularly significant, marking her Diamond Jubilee, celebrating 60 years as Queen. Events included a service at St. Paul's Cathedral and a reception for members of the International Olympic Committee. A wax figure of the Queen was unveiled at Madame Tussauds London in May 2012. The Diamond Jubilee concert saw Prince Charles kiss his mother's hand on stage. She also toured the Foreign and Commonwealth Office in December 2012.

Further public engagements continued, including a visit to Edinburgh Castle in July 2014 and her speech during the state opening of Parliament in May 2015. In September 2015, she officially became the longest-reigning British monarch in history. Her 90th birthday in 2016 was commemorated with a special family photograph featuring four generations of the House of Windsor, commissioned by the Royal Mail for a series of stamps. An official photograph released on June 10, 2016, showed her with Prince Philip, taken at Windsor Castle. Throughout these years, Queen Elizabeth II remained a constant and unifying presence, embodying dedication to her role and her country.

To celebrate **Her Majesty The Queen's 90th birthday**, a series of special engagements will take place in Windsor. These events highlight significant anniversaries and the enduring legacy of The Queen's reign.

On **Wednesday, April 20th**, The Queen's first engagement is a visit to the **Royal Mail Windsor delivery office** on William Street. This visit commemorates the **500th Anniversary of the Postal Service**. Her Majesty, accompanied by **The Duke of Edinburgh**, will meet **Mrs Moya Greene, the Chief Executive Officer of Royal Mail**, and **Mr Peter Long, the Chairman of the Royal Mail Group**. They will observe the operations of the **Caller's Office**, where customers collect undelivered items, and learn about modern tracking technology. The royal couple will also witness how **postmen and women sort mail** and meet dedicated staff. A highlight will be viewing an exhibition detailing the **history of Royal Mail over the last 500 years** and unveiling a commemorative plaque. The **Royal Mail choir**, comprised of frontline staff and formed for a BBC television series, will perform.

Following this, The Queen and The Duke of Edinburgh will visit **Alexandra Gardens** for the official opening of the new **Bandstand**. They will be welcomed by **Mr James Puxley, Lord Lieutenant of Berkshire**. An exhibition will showcase the Bandstand's design, influenced by workshops with local schoolchildren. The Queen will meet **students from six participating schools** and the bandstand's designers and builders. A **children's choir** will perform, followed by a Shakespearean performance as part of the School's Shakespeare Festival. Her Majesty will then unveil a plaque marking the occasion.

On **Thursday, April 21st**, The Queen and The Duke of Edinburgh will embark on a **walkabout in Windsor**. They will proceed from the **Henry VIII Gate of Windsor Castle** towards the **Statute of Queen Victoria**. Here, The Queen will unveil a plaque marking **The Queen's Walkway**. This **6.3km self-guided trail** connects 63 significant points in Windsor, symbolizing the moment Her Majesty became Britain's longest-reigning monarch. The Walkway draws inspiration from London's **Jubilee Walkway**, opened by The Queen in 1977. The **bronze EIIR crested markers, embossed panel, and promotional app** have now inspired a **parallel network of Walkways across the Commonwealth**, recognizing The Queen's service as Head of the Commonwealth. Several Walkways are in development, with the first complete route launched in Malta in November 2015. By 2018, the aim is to have **100 Commonwealth Walkways**, connecting 5,000 points of significance.

In the evening, The Queen, The Duke of Edinburgh, **The Prince of Wales**, and **The Duchess of Cornwall** will light a beacon. This principal beacon will initiate a chain reaction of **over 900 beacons lit across the country and worldwide** to celebrate The Queen's 90th Birthday.

**June events** are also scheduled to mark Her Majesty's official birthday.

LONDON — Queen Elizabeth II, a figure of remarkable endurance, marks her 90th birthday on Thursday. This milestone is celebrated by a nation that has witnessed her reign through profound societal shifts. She has served under 12 prime ministers, starting with Winston Churchill, and has navigated the twilight of the British Empire, enduring personal and national tragedies. As of September 9th, she surpassed Queen Victoria as the longest-reigning monarch in British history, with her tenure now extending to 64 years. Biographer Douglas Hurd, a former foreign minister, lauds her composure, describing her as "The Steadfast." For seven decades, she has maintained an enigmatic presence, invariably attired in her signature pastel ensembles and vibrant hats. Royal observers suggest this is a deliberate choice to ensure she is easily identifiable in public gatherings.

In true British fashion, the occasion is being commemorated with characteristic pageantry. This includes:

*   Extended pub hours
*   Equestrian displays
*   An appearance by Oscar-winning actress Helen Mirren, who previously portrayed the monarch.

Demonstrating her characteristic dedication, Elizabeth commenced her birthday festivities on Wednesday with a visit to a Royal Mail delivery center. There, accompanied by her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, she observed postal workers as a choir serenaded her. On Thursday, she is scheduled to ignite the first of over 900 celebratory beacons. Tributes continue on Friday, with President Obama set to extend birthday wishes at Windsor Castle. The celebratory period extends beyond the week, with pub opening times in England and Wales extended until 1 a.m. on June 10th and 11th, coinciding with the official June celebrations for ceremonial purposes.

A unique tribute comes from a British artist who, honoring her service as an Army mechanic during World War II, has crafted a monumental sculpture of the Queen's head from 800 car parts, featuring a crown fashioned from spark plugs. A new series of first-class stamps also commemorates the birthday, featuring four generations of the royal family, including young Prince George. The commercial sector is capitalizing on the event, with retailers offering a range of commemorative items. A bespoke 22-karat gold music box retails for $1,425, while more accessible souvenirs, such as a $50 waistcoat emblazoned with the Queen's image, are also available. Reflecting the multicultural evolution of Britain during her reign, Nadiya Hussain, a Muslim baker and winner of the "Great British Bake Off," will prepare an orange drizzle birthday cake. This culinary nod is particularly poignant, as a 68-year-old slice of the Queen's wedding cake previously fetched $719 at auction.

The Queen's renowned self-restraint, honed during wartime, is frequently cited as a cornerstone of her success in unifying the nation. Constitutional scholar Vernon Bogdanor commends her ability to maintain her views privately, suggesting this enigmatic persona has been crucial to her symbolic role. He contrasts her upbringing with contemporary expectations of emotional expression. Bogdanor recalls only two notable missteps: a 1994 comment about St. Petersburg being "not such a nice place," and more significantly, the public's perception of a delayed response following Princess Diana's death in 1997, when she initially remained at Balmoral.

Maintaining the relevance of a venerable institution presents ongoing challenges. An ITV documentary, "Our Queen at 90," offered a rare glimpse into royal life, revealing that her great-grandson, Prince George, affectionately calls her "Gan-Gan." Prince William noted that some individuals are so overcome by meeting his grandmother that they faint.

Born on April 21, 1926, Elizabeth was initially third in line to the throne. Her marriage to Prince Philip in 1947 was a union she pursued against her parents' wishes, following a childhood infatuation. She ascended to the throne in 1952 upon the death of her father, King George VI, whose elder brother, King Edward VIII, had abdicated in 1936 to marry Wallis Simpson. Cultural commentator Peter York posits that the Queen's perceived "unbridled blandness" represents a form of "human bondage." Yet Britons reportedly appreciate her legendary frugality, with anecdotes of her conserving energy by turning off lights and enjoying simple meals from Tupperware. In private, she is said to possess a sharp wit. York suggests her longevity provides Britons with a sense of continuity, also noting instances of barely perceptible annoyance, such as her 2008 inquiry at the London School of Economics questioning the lack of foresight concerning the financial crisis.

While her mother lived to 101, and the Queen shows no signs of diminishing her duties, the author of "Royalty Inc.: Britain's Best-Known Brand," Stephen Bates, observes a taboo: the word "abdication" is considered taboo within Buckingham Palace. He expresses concern that the presumed successors—Charles, Prince of Wales; Prince William, Duke of Cambridge; and Prince George—may not possess the same charisma as the Queen, describing Prince William as having become "just another middle-aged bald bloke in a suit." Despite these potential challenges, the Queen appears to have fulfilled a promise made on her 21st birthday: "I declare before you all, that my whole life, whether it be long or short, shall be devoted to your service."

query: What key points did Director of National Intelligence James Clapper make regarding the intelligence community's findings on Russian involvement in the hacking during the 2016 election?

documents.text:
U.S. intelligence leaders have firmly stated that senior Russian government officials were behind the acquisition and distribution of sensitive emails during the 2016 presidential election, directly addressing President-elect Donald Trump's skepticism.

Director of National Intelligence James Clapper, speaking to the Senate Armed Services Committee, distinguished between healthy doubt and outright dismissal. He stressed the importance of public trust in the intelligence community. Clapper, along with CIA Director John Brennan and FBI Director James Comey, was scheduled to brief Trump on the cyber intrusions targeting Democratic organizations that supported Hillary Clinton. Trump had previously expressed doubts about the intelligence agencies' findings, citing the difficulty of proving hacking and acknowledging past intelligence errors.

However, Clapper assured the Senate panel that the intelligence agencies' confidence in their conclusions is now at a "very high" level. He stated they are "even more resolute" about Russian involvement than when their initial public assessment was released on October 7th. Clapper also clarified that, in his view, the Russian hacking did not influence the election's outcome. He testified alongside Undersecretary of Defense for Intelligence Marcel Lettre and National Security Agency Director Admiral Michael Rogers.

The hearing, initiated by Senator John McCain, revealed differing perspectives between President-elect Trump and some Republican foreign policy figures. While Trump has publicly praised Russian President Vladimir Putin, Senator McCain and other prominent Republicans advocate for stronger sanctions against Russia, which has consistently denied any hacking involvement. Senator Lindsey Graham declared, "Putin is up to no good" and urged decisive action. He advised Trump, "Mr. President-elect, when you listen to these people you can be skeptical, but understand they’re the best among us and they’re trying to protect us."

Clapper acknowledged that while finding common ground with Russia can be useful, "there’s a threshold of behavior that’s unacceptable." Trump, via social media, questioned the timing of his intelligence briefing and referenced WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange. Assange had suggested a teenager could have been responsible for the breaches and that Russians had not provided him with information. Both Clapper and Rogers dismissed Assange as a credible source, citing his past actions that endangered U.S. lives by publishing classified material. Clapper noted that the intelligence community holds "a whole lot of respect for him" and that Trump's public comments "hardly help" morale.

President Barack Obama expressed hope that Trump would gain confidence in the intelligence agencies after his briefings. Beau Woods, deputy director of the Atlantic Council’s Cyber Statecraft Initiative, commented that while skepticism is valuable, outright dismissal of intelligence assessments is "unhelpful for finding the truth."

Senator McCain described Russia's hacking as an "unprecedented attack" on U.S. democracy, urging vigilance. The intelligence officials' prepared remarks stated that Moscow "poses a major threat" to U.S. networks and possesses a "highly advanced offensive cyber program." They anticipate future Russian cyber operations will continue to target the U.S. for intelligence gathering, decision support, influence operations, and to prepare for contingencies.

Clapper announced plans for private congressional briefings and the release of an unclassified version of their review to the public, aiming for transparency. White House Press Secretary Josh Earnest confirmed the White House has the review, commissioned by President Obama, and referred to intelligence community members as "patriots" dedicated to accuracy.

Following the hearing, Trump tweeted that the intelligence report had been politicized, questioning its origins and release timing. This followed an NBC News report detailing cyberattacks beyond the Democratic National Committee, including targets like the White House and Joint Chiefs of Staff. Trump also claimed the DNC obstructed the FBI's access to its computer information, referencing a BuzzFeed News story about the FBI's reliance on a third-party analysis by CrowdStrike due to the DNC's refusal of direct server access.

Intelligence officials highlighted the Kremlin's increasingly "aggressive cyber posture" in recent years, noting sophisticated phishing and impersonation tactics. While U.S. government systems are being strengthened, officials acknowledged that over 30 nations are developing offensive cyber capabilities, and groups like ISIS pursue sensitive information.

Washington (CNN) The leading US intelligence official said Thursday that his foreign counterparts have expressed concern that President-elect Donald Trump is disparaging the US intelligence community, potentially damaging public confidence. "I do think public trust and confidence in the Intelligence Community is crucial" both in the US and in other countries that rely on US intelligence, James Clapper, the Director of National Intelligence, told the Senate Armed Services Committee. "I've received many expressions of concern from foreign counterparts about the disparagement of the US Intelligence Community, or I should say, what has been interpreted as disparagement of the US Intelligence Community." Clapper was speaking at a hearing on global cyberthreats that focused almost exclusively on Moscow's alleged hacking during the presidential elections. The hearing gave lawmakers and senior US intelligence officials the chance to draw a line in the sand for Trump, presenting a united front on their conclusion that Russia is a major threat to the United States and was behind election-related hacking -- a conclusion the President-elect has refused to accept. "We assess that only Russia's senior-most officials could have authorized the recent election-focused data thefts and disclosures, based on the scope and sensitivity of the targets," Clapper, Undersecretary of Defense for Intelligence Marcel Lettre and the commander of US Cyber Command, Michael Rogers, wrote in a joint statement to the Senate Armed Services Committee. Clapper said at the hearing that there was a line between critical thinking about assessment and undermining those gathering the intelligence. "I think there is an important distinction here between healthy skepticism which policymakers -- to include policymaker No. 1 -- should always have on intelligence, but I think there's a difference between skepticism and disparagement," he said. JUST WATCHED Conway defends Trump's Russian hack comments Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Conway defends Trump's Russian hack comments 01:20 And he noted that some intelligence agents die in service for their country. "You only need to walk into the lobby and look at the stars on the wall or the NSA and the number of intelligence people who have paid the ultimate price for their country," he said. Rogers told the committee that he was concerned about morale within the Intelligence Community and that having political leaders who are confident in intelligence agencies is "crucial to that." "I don't want to lose good, motivated people because they feel there's not room for them to contribute," Rogers said. "Without that confidence, I just don't want a situation where our workforce decides to walk because that's just not a good place for us to be." Sen. Lindsey Graham, a South Carolina Republican, said he wanted to convey to Trump that "It's okay to challenge the intel, you're right to do so, but what I don't want you to do is undermine those who are serving our nation in this arena until you're absolutely sure they need to be undermined. "And I think they need to be uplifted," Graham added, "not undermined." In his opening questions to Clapper, Committee Chairman John McCain noted the Intelligence Community's conclusion that the cyberthefts and disclosures were intended to interfere with the US election process and could only have been authorized by Russia's most senior levels. "We stand actually more resolutely on the strength of that statement that we made on the seventh of October," Clapper said. After the election, the Intelligence Community concluded that at least one Russian motive had been to help Trump win. Clapper said that the hacking did not succeed in changing any vote tallies, but that it was impossible for intelligence to assess how the information released from the breaches affected voters' attitudes. McCain emphasized the Russian role and delivered an implicit rebuke to Trump, who has urged people to "move on" from the issue of Moscow-directed hacking. "Every American should be alarmed by Russia's attacks on our nation," the Arizona Republican said. "There's no escaping the fact that this committee meets today ... in the aftermath of an unprecedented attack on our democracy." McCain added that the "goal of this review is not to question the outcome of the election nor should it be." It's crucial that the country move forward with full knowledge about what happened and that Congress take bipartrisan steps, he said. Russia reacted almost immediately, with Kremlin spokesman Dmitr Peskov telling CNN that Moscow is "sick and tired of those irresponsibly blaming everything on our country. If there is a need for an enemy, why not to try someone else?" "We have suggested cooperation on combating cyberthreats numerous times," Peskov said. "It was rejected." JUST WATCHED Is Putin the most powerful man in the world? Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Is Putin the most powerful man in the world? 02:00 But both Clapper and Rogers said in their testimony that the US has been trying to create international norms for cyber-related conflict. "We continue to engage with partners around the world about what is acceptable and unacceptable behavior in cyberspace, " Rogers said. "We are clearly not where we want to be in this regard." Trump has suggested that the Intelligence Community's conclusion on Russia was driven by political interests. Clapper, who enlisted in the Marine Corps in 1961, stressed that he has served every president "in the trenches" since John F. Kennedy. "I am apolitical," he said, adding that it was a priority to supply "unvarnished" and "untainted" policy recommendations to policy makers. McCain asked Clapper whether Assange had endangered men and women serving the US with his earlier release of State Department cables. "Yes, he has," Clapper said. McCain then asked whether Assange should be accorded any credibility -- another implicit rebuke of Trump. Clapper responded, "No, he should not."
