The intelligence leaders stressed before the Senate that instead of "moving on," Russia requires vigilance. "Russia is a full-scope cyber actor that poses a major threat to US government, military, diplomatic, commercial, and critical infrastructure and key resource networks because of its highly advanced offensive cyber program and sophisticated tactics, techniques, and procedures," they said. After Thursday's public hearing, Clapper, Lettre and Rogers may brief lawmakers in a closed session to discuss classified material, Senate aides said. Clapper is due to brief Trump Friday on Russia's actions in a private meeting. President Barack Obama ordered a full review of Russian meddling into the election a month ago and received the report Thursday morning. Clapper, who will brief Trump on the findings on Friday, said the report will speak to Russia's motivations. He also told lawmakers that an unclassified version will be released to the public. "My hope is that when the President-elect receives his own briefings and is able to examine the intelligence as his team has put together and they see how professional and effective these agencies are, that some of those current tensions will be reduced," Obama said. "I can speak to my own experience -- it's going to be important to make sure the President and the intelligence communities are both working on the best possible information," Obama continued. Trump "is more skeptical of conclusions from raw data, rather than intelligence and raw data provided," Spicer said. "That's why he's looking forward to meeting on Friday." Intelligence officials describe increasing dismay within their community about Trump's attacks and refusal to believe them. "It's a sad day when politicians place more stock in Vladimir Putin and Julian Assange than in the Americans who risk their lives daily to provide objective non-partisan intelligence analysis," one official told CNN. In Thursday's hearing, Connecticut Democratic Senator Richard Blumenthal said Trump's "demeaning and dismissive" comments about intelligence officers were unacceptable and praised their service. "I want to thank you on behalf of all the women and men in the intelligence community," Clapper said. "I want to thank you for that."

**George Mitchell: Trump's Doubts on Russian Election Interference Intelligence Are Unfounded**

George Mitchell, a former Senate Majority Leader, expressed on Thursday that President-elect Donald Trump's skepticism concerning U.S. intelligence reports about Russian interference in the election is misplaced. Mitchell, a veteran Democrat and former diplomat, advised Trump on CNBC's "Squawk Box" to place greater trust in American intelligence assessments. He suggested Trump should not prioritize the statements of Russian President Vladimir Putin or WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange over these findings.

A Senate Armed Services Committee hearing on Thursday was set to address Russia's alleged role in influencing Trump's election. Mitchell warned that Trump's apparent leaning towards Russia could result in a "rude awakening." He asserted that Putin holds animosity towards the United States. Mitchell further explained, "He views the United States as the cause of a major tragedy for the Soviet Union and Russia. He has stated that the collapse of the Soviet Union was the most significant catastrophe in recent times."

President Barack Obama was expected to get an update on Thursday. This update would concern an intelligence report he commissioned. The report focuses on potential Russian cyberattacks on elections, with a history dating back to 2008. Trump was scheduled to receive a presidential intelligence briefing on Friday in New York.

## McCain and Graham: Challenging Trump on Russian Interference

For eight years, Senators John McCain and Lindsey Graham consistently challenged President Barack Obama's foreign policy, often questioning his administration's approach to global affairs. Now, with Donald Trump preparing to enter the White House, the tables have turned, and the two Republican senators are poised to scrutinize his administration, particularly concerning cyberthreats and Russian interference in the U.S. election.

### A Stance on Foreign Policy

McCain and Graham, known for their independent streaks and bipartisan collaborations, are set to address the issue of cyberthreats to the United States, with a specific focus on Russian interference in the recent election. This comes despite President-elect Trump's repeated downplaying of Russian meddling in his campaign.

"You can expect I’ll do what I’ve been doing: I worked with Obama, though I didn’t agree with him, I certainly was a check," Graham stated in an interview. He elaborated on his alignment with Trump on domestic policy and certain foreign policy issues like Iran and China, but expressed uncertainty regarding Trump's stance on Russia.

### Investigating Cyberthreats

The Senate Armed Services Committee, chaired by McCain, is initiating an extensive investigation into cyberthreats posed by Russia and other hostile nations. While the committee's mandate is cybersecurity, the hearings are expected to heavily focus on Russian intrusion into the U.S. election. Key intelligence officials, including Director of National Intelligence James Clapper and NSA chief Adm. Mike Rogers, are slated to testify. These public hearings may be followed by classified sessions.

This investigation, which includes a new cybersecurity panel led by Graham, could represent a significant challenge to Trump's skepticism of the U.S. intelligence community within a Republican-controlled Congress.

### Navigating Trump's Skepticism

Despite a strong consensus within the intelligence community regarding Russia's efforts to influence the election, President-elect Trump has consistently expressed doubt, referring to the events as "so-called Russian hacking." McCain and Graham have acknowledged Trump's right to his own opinions but have also expressed a desire to understand the basis of his doubts.

While many Republicans in Congress have maintained a critical stance on Russia, Trump's admiration for Vladimir Putin and his desire to reset U.S.-Russia relations have created a complex dynamic. Other Senate committees are also examining Russian activities, but many Republican leaders have been hesitant to publicly criticize Trump's questioning of intelligence assessments.

### A Call for Scrutiny

In contrast to the more cautious approach of some colleagues, Graham and McCain have joined forces with Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer in advocating for a dedicated select committee to investigate Russian hacking and other cyberthreats. Both Republicans have voiced concerns about Trump's nominee for Secretary of State, Rex Tillerson, citing his perceived close ties to Putin and Russia. McCain has indicated that Tillerson's views on the Russia hacking will be a crucial factor in his decision to support his nomination.

### An Independent Path

McCain and Graham's push for a select committee was ultimately unsuccessful due to opposition from Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell. McCain, however, has maintained that his primary responsibility is to defend the nation, rather than to act as a direct check on Trump.

For Democrats seeking bipartisan support for their criticisms of Trump, the voices of McCain and Graham have been invaluable. Senator Martin Heinrich (D-N.M.) described them as "intellectually honest" and noted that their stance on Russia has remained consistent. Senator Claire McCaskill (D-Mo.) suggested that other Republican senators are hesitant to criticize Trump due to fear, contrasting them with the courage displayed by McCain and Graham.

While some Republicans, like Senator David Perdue (R-Ga.), have expressed hope for a unified congressional front, they have also acknowledged the independence and capability of McCain and Graham. Senator Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) described their questioning as "healthy."

However, few other Republican senators have publicly supported a select committee on cybersecurity, with Senator Marco Rubio (R-Fla.) being another notable exception in raising concerns about Tillerson's Russian ties. This leaves McCain and Graham as somewhat isolated figures within the Republican Party for their willingness to challenge Trump. McCain has humorously suggested that forming a new group of Senate contrarians might be a "kiss of death," implying a reluctance among others to join such a dissenting faction.

**Intelligence Chief Differentiates Skepticism from Disparagement Regarding Election Interference**

Washington D.C. – The nation's **Director of National Intelligence, James R. Clapper Jr.**, drew a clear distinction on Thursday between **"skepticism"** of intelligence findings and **"disparagement"** of the intelligence community. This statement was notably directed towards President-elect Donald J. Trump, who has publicly questioned the intelligence that **Russia interfered with the American election**.

Clapper made these remarks while testifying before the **Senate Armed Services Committee**. During the hearing, **both Republicans and Democrats rallied to defend Clapper and his colleagues**, a stark contrast to Mr. Trump's ongoing questioning of foreign involvement in the election.

**Key takeaways from the hearing included:**

*   Intelligence officials asserted that **Julian Assange, the founder of WikiLeaks, should not be afforded credibility**.
*   Senator Lindsey Graham criticized President Obama's approach to Russian interference, stating the President had thrown a **"pebble"** while Graham himself intended to use a **"rock"**.

The alleged Russian interference in the U.S. presidential election has raised significant concerns, with Senator John McCain famously labeling Russia's actions as "an act of war." While he later clarified this did not necessarily imply a declaration of military conflict, the statement underscored the gravity of the situation.

Official U.S. intelligence community statements, corroborated by media reports from mid-December, support this assessment. These reports indicated that Russian President Vladimir Putin was "personally involved" in orchestrating cyberattacks aimed at disrupting the U.S. presidential election. In a December 15 interview with NPR, President Barack Obama vowed a U.S. response, stating it would occur "at a time and place of our own choosing." He added, "Mr. Putin is well aware of my feelings about this, because I spoke to him directly about it."

On December 29, President Obama enacted concrete measures. Sanctions were imposed on two Russian intelligence services, the GRU and the FSB, believed to be involved in the hacks. Additionally, 35 Russian officials identified as intelligence agents were expelled from the U.S. Despite threats of retaliation from Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov, President Putin ultimately chose not to respond in kind.

The question of whether Russia's hacking of the Democratic National Committee (DNC) and other entities was previously known is relevant. The recent intelligence assessment aimed to provide official details on both the perpetrators and their motivations. Prior to mid-December, Putin himself had not been directly implicated. However, in October, Director of National Intelligence James Clapper neared this conclusion, stating that "based on the scope and sensitivity of these efforts... only Russia’s senior-most officials could have authorized these activities."

Further developments emerged through separate intelligence leaks reported on December 9. For the first time, these reports claimed the hacking's intent was to sway the election in favor of Donald Trump, a shift from the prior understanding that the goal was merely to sow generalized distrust. It is crucial to note that it has not yet been suggested that these cyberattacks successfully altered the actual vote tally for either presidential candidate. This intelligence assessment now represents the official stance of the U.S. intelligence community.

Information regarding the specifics of these events has been disseminated gradually. As early as mid-June, the Democratic National Committee reported a breach of its computer network, with the cybersecurity firm CrowdStrike publicly attributing the intrusion to Russian hackers. In July, after emails stolen from the DNC appeared on WikiLeaks, Democratic members of Congress also pointed to Russia. Clinton campaign manager Robby Mook alleged, "It was the Russians who perpetrated this leak for the purpose of helping Donald Trump and hurting Hillary Clinton."

It wasn't until September that anonymous federal officials confirmed to The New York Times the intelligence community's "high confidence" in Russian government involvement in the hack. This confirmation did not definitively include the subsequent leak, leaving doubts as to whether the hacks were merely "routine cyberespionage" or specifically intended to influence the election. Then, in October, Director of National Intelligence James Clapper officially blamed Russia, specifying government actors rather than independent Russian cybercriminals. He cited "the scope and sensitivity of these efforts" as evidence and declared that these actions "were intended to interfere with the U.S. election process." Days later, emails stolen from Clinton campaign chairman John Podesta were published on WikiLeaks.

U.S. intelligence leaders have presented a confidential report to the White House concerning foreign interference in American elections. A U.S. official confirmed that President Barack Obama has received this report. President-elect Donald Trump is slated to be briefed on its contents shortly. The Director of National Intelligence has indicated that an unclassified version of the report may be made public early next week.

Intelligence officials view Russia as a substantial and growing risk to U.S. government, military, diplomatic, and business activities, as well as vital national infrastructure. They also noted that Russia is one of several countries developing cyberattack abilities.

President-elect Trump has expressed doubt regarding Russia's role in election hacking, but U.S. intelligence agencies stand by their assessments. Specifics from the foreign influence report are not expected before President Obama's briefing.

Earlier, senior intelligence officials were scheduled to appear at a Senate hearing regarding claims of Russian interference in the U.S. presidential election, intended to help Donald Trump win. This hearing was arranged before the president-elect received briefings from the CIA, FBI, and the director of national intelligence about the probe into Russia's alleged hacking. Trump has been critical of these findings, even seeming to back Julian Assange's claim that Russia did not provide him with hacked Democratic emails. Those set to testify included:

*   National Intelligence Director James Clapper
*   Undersecretary of Defense for Intelligence Marcel Lettre
*   National Security Agency chief Adm. Michael Rogers
