The controversy stemmed from Trump's remarks, particularly his assertion that there was blame "on both sides," which underscored the profound influence of presidential statements on the corporate landscape.

Following the events in Charlottesville, President Trump held a press conference on August 15, 2017. While initially condemning white supremacists, he later stated that blame for the violence rested "on both sides" and characterized counter-protesters as the "alt-left." This statement drew widespread and sharp criticism from various political figures and the public. Notably, former KKK leader David Duke publicly thanked Trump on Twitter for his "honesty & courage to tell the truth."

In direct protest of Trump's "blame on both sides" comment, Campbell's Soup CEO Denise Morrison announced her resignation from the President's American Manufacturing Council on August 16, 2017. Morrison explicitly cited Trump's remarks on Charlottesville as the reason for her departure, stating, "Racism and murder are unequivocally reprehensible and are not morally equivalent to anything else that happened in Charlottesville." She stressed the importance of the President being "unambiguous on that point." Morrison was not the first executive to leave the council; 3M CEO Inge Thulin and Merck CEO Kenneth Frazier had also stepped down prior to her resignation, alongside leaders from Under Armour, AFL-CIO, and Intel.

Within minutes of Morrison and Thulin's resignations, President Trump announced via Twitter that he was dissolving both the Manufacturing Council and the President's Strategic and Policy Forum. He tweeted, "Rather than putting pressure on the businesspeople of the Manufacturing Council & Strategy & Policy Forum, I am ending both. Thank you all." Earlier that day, Campbell's had issued a statement indicating Morrison's belief in the importance of "having a voice and providing input on matters that will affect" her industry and company. This preceded Trump's reiteration of his "blame on both sides" stance regarding the deadly white supremacist rally, where he stated, "You had a group on one side that was bad and you had a group on the other side that was very violent. And nobody wants to say that."

The following business leaders were part of the American Manufacturing Council before its disbandment or their prior resignation: Bill Brown (Harris Corporation), Michael Dell (Dell Technologies), John Ferriola (Nucor Corporation), Jeff Fettig (Whirlpool Corporation), Mark Fields (Ford Motor Company), Kenneth Frazier (Merck), Alex Gorsky (Johnson & Johnson), Greg Hayes (United Technologies Corp.), Marilynn Hewson (Lockheed Martin Corporation), Jeff Immelt (General Electric), Jim Kamsickas (Dana Inc.), Klaus Kleinfeld (Arconic), Brian Krzanich (Intel Corporation), Rich Kyle (The Timken Company), Thea Lee (AFL-CIO), Andrew Liveris (The Dow Chemical Company), Mario Longhi (U.S. Steel), Denise Morrison (Campbell Soup Company), Dennis Muilenburg (Boeing), Elon Musk (Tesla Motors), Doug Oberhelman (Caterpillar), Scott Paul (Alliance for American Manufacturing), Kevin Plank (Under Armour), Michael Polk (Newell Brands), Mark Sutton (International Paper), Inge Thulin (3M), Richard Tumka (AFL-CIO), and Wendell Weeks (Corning).

### Corporate America's Response to President Trump's Charlottesville Remarks

President Donald J. Trump's statements following the Charlottesville events triggered significant reactions from the business community, culminating in the dissolution of key presidential advisory councils. While the stock market experienced minor gains—the S&P 500 rose 0.2% to 2,468.63, the Dow Jones Industrial Average increased 0.1% to 22,029.79, and the Nasdaq Composite climbed 0.2% to 6,344.86—the spotlight turned to the notable withdrawals of prominent business leaders.

The most impactful development was the disbandment of the President's manufacturing council. This action followed the earlier decision of the Strategic and Policy Forum, which comprised CEOs from leading companies such as General Motors, BlackRock, and IBM, to dissolve. Several key figures from the manufacturing council, including the chief executives of 3M and Campbell Soup, also announced their resignations.

The primary trigger for these departures was Merck CEO Kenneth Frazier's decision to step down from the manufacturing council. This move occurred after President Trump's initial response to the Charlottesville events, which broadly attributed blame to "many sides." His subsequent remarks, which defended white nationalist marchers by stating some were "very fine people," proved to be the decisive factor for many in the corporate sector.

These events underscore the inherent risks and potential rewards tied to a company's reputation. President Trump has utilized Twitter to comment on these departures, suggesting Merck's Frazier could instead focus on reducing drug prices and labeling the departing CEOs as "grandstanders." Amazon.com's stock experienced a decline following a presidential tweet that criticized the company's effect on other retailers.

Academic studies corroborate the connection between a company's reputation and its financial performance. Research indicates that robust reputational capital can result in:

*   More favorable contract terms
*   The ability to command premium pricing
*   A reduced cost of capital

Conversely, companies with negative reputations often face penalties. Under Armour's CEO, Kevin Plank, experienced this firsthand when his support for the President led to public backlash. Plank was among the initial leaders to withdraw from the manufacturing council, highlighting the business imperative to avoid alienating customers.

**Trump Disbands Business Advisory Councils Amidst Charlottesville Fallout**

In the wake of intense criticism over President Donald Trump's response to a deadly neo-Nazi rally in Charlottesville, Virginia, two prominent CEO-led advisory groups—the Manufacturing Council and the Strategy & Policy Forum—were abruptly dissolved on Wednesday. This decision followed a significant wave of business leaders resigning from the Manufacturing Council, citing personal conscience and ethical concerns.

President Trump announced the dissolution himself via Twitter early Wednesday afternoon, stating, "Rather than putting pressure on the businesspeople of the Manufacturing Council & Strategy & Policy Forum, I am ending both. Thank you all!" This public statement appeared to precede formal announcements, as members of at least one council were reportedly already planning their departures. Administration officials indicated that council members had been in recent discussions with White House officials regarding their roles, though President Trump's direct involvement remained unclear. The CEOs involved had reportedly held a conference call to discuss their continued participation.

The exodus from the Manufacturing Council began Monday with Merck CEO Kenneth Frazier, who described his departure as a "matter of personal conscience." President Trump responded to Frazier's resignation with a critical tweet, suggesting the Merck CEO would now have "more time to LOWER RIPOFF DRUG PRICES!" Despite this public criticism, Frazier's exit was swiftly followed by similar decisions from the CEOs of Intel and Under Armour, as well as representatives from the labor union AFL-CIO. By Wednesday, Campbell Soup CEO Denise Morrison and 3M CEO Inge Thulin had also joined the growing list of prominent American chief executives stepping down from the advisory group, which the White House had established earlier in the year.

The mounting pressure from the business community stemmed directly from President Trump's widely criticized initial reaction to the violence at a white supremacist rally in Charlottesville the previous weekend. While the President eventually acknowledged and condemned the hate groups, including neo-Nazis and the Ku Klux Klan, he later suggested in a press conference that counter-protesters shared some responsibility for the tragedy, which resulted in three deaths and numerous injuries.

CEOs who had initially remained on the councils often cited the importance of engaging with the White House to influence policy and advocate for their industries. However, public outcry and the growing momentum for business leaders to distance themselves from the President, amplified by the Twitter hashtag #Quitthecouncil, proved overwhelming.

As resignations from the Manufacturing Council increased, CEOs on the Strategy & Policy Forum, though not yet having resigned by Wednesday morning, also faced questions from activists and reporters about their intentions. The ongoing media attention and the rising number of departures from the manufacturing group prompted the CEOs on the policy forum to consider their own future involvement.

In a statement released through the private equity firm Blackstone, whose CEO Stephen Schwarzman chairs the group, the forum explained their decision. "We believe the debate over forum participation has become a distraction from our well-intentioned and sincere desire to aid vital policy discussions on how to improve the lives of everyday Americans," the statement read. "As such, the President and we are disbanding the forum."

The mass resignations by these chief executives may also be linked to broader business considerations, according to Bill Klepper, a professor of management at Columbia Business School. Klepper suggested that business leaders increasingly find the "cost of alignment with Trump is too high." He explained the concept of a "social contract" that businesses have with their stakeholders, emphasizing core values, ethical principles, and societal contributions. Klepper argued that "lots of things Trump has been doing or not doing come close to violating the social contract."

Furthermore, questions about the effectiveness of these advisory councils had been raised since their creation. Some critics viewed them as mere public relations efforts, designed to boost the President's image and his promise of restoring American jobs. Richard Trumka, President of the AFL-CIO, who resigned from the manufacturing council, noted that the council had "yet to hold any real meeting" since its formation. He expressed doubts about the council's ability "to deliver real policy that lifts working families."

query: What hairstyle technique was highlighted by hairstylist Amit Abraham in Bibhu Mohapatra's spring 2013 ready-to-wear show?

documents.text: At Bibhu Mohapatra's spring 2013 ready-to-wear show, hairstylist Amit Abraham worked with this season's beloved braids to create a look that was on-trend while entirely pushing forward the artform. Inspired by the luna moth, he coiled fishtails into a sleek bun supposed to mimic the insect's cocoon. "The unexpected shape of the rectangular bun commands attention,” he says. Here he gives us the step-by-step lowdown on how to easily master this elegant runway look on your own.Begin by creating a dramatic center part from your forehead to the nape of your neck.Spritz hair with shine spray. Abraham used L'Oréal Professionnel Mythic Oil Reinforcing Milk on the roots to create a super-flat graphic part. Then separate your hair into two ponytails.Working with each ponytail, create a tight fishtail braid using L'Oréal Professionnel Architexture Matte Defining Paste , which keeps braids smooth as you go.To create a fishtail braid, simply divide the ponytail into a left and right section. Then, beginning at the top, take a small section from the outside of the left and pass it over to the right. Repeat on the right side by passing a section from the outside right to the left. Continue alternating left to right sections until you have braided the entire length of the ponytail.Interlace the two ponytails into a horizontal rectangular shape, making sure to stay two inches above the nape of neck and to keep the shape no wider than your hairline. Place bobby pins strategically around the bun to hold it in place.To finish, spray Aussie Instant Freeze Hair Spray and Garnier Fructis Style Brilliantine Shine Glossing Spray throughout hair for strong hold and ample shine. Photo: Diane Bondareff/Invision for L'Oréal Professionnel/AP Images

Hillary Clinton's private emails offer a **unique and revealing perspective** on her time as Secretary of State, providing **intimate glimpses** into her decision-making processes and her **management of critical global events**. These communications shed light on her involvement in the complex political landscape of the Arab Spring, her responses to the 2012 Benghazi attacks, and her diplomatic efforts to broker peace in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. Beyond these high-stakes foreign policy challenges, the correspondence also offers **fascinating insights into the personal life** of a prominent figure in American politics.

The unearthed emails vividly illustrate Clinton's dedication to **navigating an exceptionally demanding public service schedule**. This involved constant adjustments to flights, meals, and personal commitments, a necessity given her extensive travel, which covered over 950,000 miles and spanned numerous time zones. The approximately 7,800 pages of emails, disclosed from her private server as mandated by a court order, underscore the **persistent scrutiny** surrounding her email practices. Concerns regarding the security of government information and proper record-keeping were ongoing. Inspectors general identified some emails as classified, and multiple Senate committees, in conjunction with the FBI, continued their investigations into Clinton's email arrangement and the security protocols of her private server. To date, **two-thirds of her work-related emails have been made public**, though many of these contain significant redactions.

A notable segment of the released emails addresses Clinton's health challenges, including a **severe stomach virus in 2012**. This illness necessitated hospitalization and resulted in a concussion, forcing her to postpone crucial testimony before the House Foreign Affairs Committee concerning the Benghazi attacks. During her recovery, she received **messages of encouragement** from notable figures such as Hollywood mogul Harvey Weinstein and Dorothea Hurley, the wife of rock star Jon Bon Jovi. Her health quickly became a focal point of political discourse, with Republicans questioning her fitness for office. Her staff actively worked to counter this speculation. Top aide Philippe Reines, for instance, enlisted the support of individuals like former Senator Bill Frist and the NFL commissioner to push back against critical commentary.

Although not a candidate in the 2012 presidential election, Clinton and her supporters closely monitored the political landscape. Emails reveal her **candid observations on the Republican primaries**, where she playfully referred to Mitt Romney as "Mittens" and Newt Gingrich as "Grinch." She also expressed concerns about the potential ramifications of primary outcomes on ballot access for state Republican parties.

Several emails have been re-examined in the context of her presidential campaign, particularly those pertaining to the **September 11, 2012, attack on the U.S. consulate in Benghazi, Libya**. An email sent to her daughter, Chelsea, shortly after the incident indicated that two officers, including the ambassador she had appointed, had been killed, and the attackers were described as belonging to an "al-Qaida-like group." Republicans on the House Benghazi committee cited this email as evidence that Clinton was aware of the terrorist nature of the attack early on, a point that contrasted with earlier suggestions by then-UN Ambassador Susan Rice that the attack was primarily motivated by a controversial video. Clinton's aides, including Jake Sullivan, assured her that her public statements had been carefully crafted to avoid definitively characterizing the attack as "spontaneous" or attributing its motives with certainty.

The emails also highlight Clinton's commitment to **maintaining robust relationships with key Democratic Party donors and strategists**. She engaged with figures such as Samuel Kaplan, a significant donor and former U.S. ambassador to Morocco, discussing potential future alliances. Former aide Lauren Jiloty shared an anecdote where investor Warren Buffett expressed his admiration for Clinton. She also stayed connected with political strategists from her husband's administration, including John Podesta and Gary Gensler, who would later play pivotal roles in her 2016 campaign.

As her tenure as Secretary of State drew to a close, Clinton's aides and supporters offered her **effusive praise**. Messages lauded her appearance and her policy initiatives. Deputy chief of staff Huma Abedin noted how "fabulous" she looked during congressional testimony, and one supporter described the "twitterverse abuzz with Hillary-kvelling." When political consultant Mark Penn suggested that Clinton might have appeared rattled during a hearing, aide Philippe Reines strongly defended her, asserting that she appeared "real" rather than rattled. Jake Sullivan echoed this sentiment, recalling that Penn had offered similar advice during her 2008 presidential campaign, a moment Clinton had characterized as "BINGO!"

New releases of Hillary Clinton's emails, totaling 78,000 pages from 2012 and 2013, have been scrutinized for any new details concerning the Benghazi attack. These documents, however, do not present fresh evidence that incriminates her regarding the incident. Instead, the emails primarily detail the former Secretary of State's communications with her staff, offering a glimpse into how her team managed public criticism following Clinton's Benghazi testimony. The exchanges also shed light on how staff addressed the aftermath of Clinton's concussion and responded to common technological inquiries.

One particularly noteworthy email, sent on Christmas Eve 2012, features Clinton humorously referencing her "cracked head" in a message to senior advisor Philippe Reines. This communication followed Reines's contact with NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell and former Senate Majority Leader Bill Frist, which had caused some initial confusion. Clinton initially believed Reines was offering medical advice, but he clarified that he was seeking their assistance to counter media speculation surrounding her head injury.

Following Clinton's initial testimony on Benghazi, campaign advisor Mark Penn suggested that Clinton's remark, "What difference, at this point, does it make?" was an ill-advised statement. Clinton shared this feedback with her team, who promptly dismissed Penn's assessment, considering her testimony to have been "flawless."

The released emails also provide insights into Clinton's engagement with popular culture and political commentary. In one exchange with Sidney Blumenthal, Clinton shares her opinions on the Florida GOP primary, referring to the candidates as "Mittens" and "Grinch." In a separate instance, Reines proposed that Clinton undertake extensive travel in her final months in office, aiming for her to surpass the travel records of previous Secretaries of State. This suggestion appeared to be positively received by countries such as Albania.

Furthermore, the documents highlight a consistent pattern of Clinton seeking assistance from her aides for personal matters. While she reportedly required help identifying her cable provider, one email indicates she attempted a complex hairstyle herself rather than consulting her professional styling team. Although Clinton frequently directs inquiries about her tenure to her memoir, *Hard Choices*, one email contains a one-page summary of her achievements, which was entirely redacted.

The recent release of emails from Hillary Clinton's time as Secretary of State offers a **direct look into her private communications**, providing insights into her political views, personal thoughts, and the demanding nature of her role. These documents offer a **more detailed perspective** on the former diplomat, even as the controversy surrounding her private email server has largely faded.

The collection, containing nearly 8,000 pages of correspondence primarily from 2012 and 2013, sheds light on Clinton's final year leading the State Department. Her inbox reveals a range of messages, from **colorful nicknames for Republican rivals** to candid stories about a concussion and issues with TV reception.

Here are some notable exchanges:

1.  **"Mittens and the Grinch"**: After Newt Gingrich won the South Carolina primary, Clinton's confidant Sid Blumenthal sent an email to Secretary Clinton. Clinton referred to Gingrich as "Grinch" and Mitt Romney as "Mittens." In a January 22, 2012, message, she wrote, "If Mittens can't beat Grinch in Florida, there will be pressure on state Republican parties to reopen or liberalize ballot access especially in the caucuses, which as we know are creatures of the parties' extremes. Tried calling but no answer, so hope you and Jackie are somewhere fun," referencing Blumenthal's wife.

2.  **Senator Graham's Endorsement**: In January 2011, State Department official Michael Posner told top aide Jake Sullivan about a conversation with Senator Lindsey Graham. Posner reported that Graham "expressed strong support for Secretary Clinton," despite potential political backlash in his home state of South Carolina. Graham, a former GOP presidential candidate, had previously worked well with Clinton. Posner documented, "He expressed strong support for Secretary Clinton and said the main reason he wants to serve on Foreign Ops is to protect our budget requests against those who will advocate for severe cuts. I thanked him for his committment and encouraged him to pursue this committee assignment. He said this won't help him politically in South Carolina but these are the issues that matter most to him." Sullivan then forwarded this communication to Clinton.

3.  **"I am really devastated"**: Anne-Marie Slaughter, Clinton's former Director of Policy Planning, expressed significant distress in an October 2012 email exchange. This followed Clinton's reported disapproval of Slaughter's influential *Atlantic* article, "Why Women Still Can't Have It All," in which Clinton had apparently suggested some women are not suited for high-demand careers. Slaughter emailed Cheryl Mills and Huma Abedin, asking, "Is she really talking about me? I have been 500 percent supportive and loyal in every possible way I can be? Can I at least talk to her? I AM advocating for women!!" The subject line read, "I am really devastated." Mills and Abedin advised her not to worry too much. The Clinton campaign disputed POLITICO's account of Clinton's reaction. Abedin wrote, "There is a lot going on here and [senior adviser Philippe Reines] has been pushing back hard. I know cheryl is also calling you. Happy to talk." Slaughter later thanked Abedin, saying she was recovering but hoped to discuss the matter with Clinton after her time at the State Department. Abedin forwarded the thread to Clinton, noting Reines would speak with Slaughter.

4.  **"your sparkling yellow pants suit"**: In December 2012, long-time family friend Lanny Davis sent Clinton an email previewing an op-ed about her service as Secretary of State and First Lady. He also sent well wishes after her fall and subsequent concussion. Davis wrote, "I hope you enjoy the memories - especially mom remembering your sparkling yellow pants suit! I sent you my best wishes thru cdm when I heard about your fall. Happened to me once - faint and hard knock on marble floor. Scary. But your great husband and my great wife agree we are well protected - hard-headed. Take care of yourself - and look forward to a long rest as I wrote at the end."

5.  **In search of "Homeland"**: On a Sunday afternoon in October 2012, Clinton emailed Philippe Reines with the subject "Stupid question," asking about the broadcast channel for the Showtime series "Homeland." Reines provided the Comcast channel numbers and offered to find the information if she didn't have Comcast. Clinton replied, "You won't be surprised to hear I'm not sure." Reines offered more help and followed up after the show to confirm she had watched it.

6.  **Assisting a "cracked head"**: In the immediate aftermath of her concussion, Clinton congratulated Senator John Kerry on his nomination to succeed her. On December 21, 2012, she requested an email from Reines. Reines sent a statement about Kerry and added, "Hi! Miss you!" Clinton replied, "And I miss you too! Having a cracked head is no fun at all." Reines offered to help with her "cracked head," mentioning he had contacted NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell and former Senate Majority Leader Bill Frist. Clinton declined medical help, stating she was fine. Reines clarified he wanted to use their influence to counter criticism about her health, which had led to her absence from Benghazi testimony. He detailed his communication with Laura Ingraham and his drafting of a similar message for John Bolton to address their comments.

7.  **"Publicly and directly puncture conspiracy fever"**: In the week after CIA Director David Petraeus resigned and one month after the Benghazi attacks, Sid Blumenthal gave Clinton strategic advice on managing public scrutiny, along with relevant news articles. He discussed the Petraeus scandal and a conversation with British ambassador Peter Westmacott about Jill Kelley. Blumenthal advised Clinton, "Publicly and directly puncture conspiracy fever on Benghazi before any closed hearing, in response to press question that you make yourself available to."

8.  **Not POLITICO?**: After her Benghazi testimony, Blumenthal shared a blog post titled "How to deal with a mansplainer starring Hillary Clinton in GIFs," which Clinton found amusing. Blumenthal also commented on his wife Jackie's stylish glasses.

9.  **Give ... Me .... A .... Break**: Mark Penn, a former Clinton campaign strategist, sent Clinton an email that she forwarded to Reines, Sullivan, and Mills. Penn criticized her testimony, suggesting that while Republicans focused on Libya, her emotional presentation during the hearing might have been unhelpful. Clinton called this the "first discordant note." Reines responded, "I say this as someone who has never had a problem with Mark or held him as accountable as other people do - but... Give Me A Break. You did not look rattled. You looked real. There's a difference. A big one."

10. **Daughters**: In an August 2012 email chain addressed to Clinton and her daughter, Chelsea (using the pseudonym Diane Reynolds), Reines analyzed the potential electoral disadvantage for Mitt Romney due to his lack of daughters. He noted a historical pattern of presidents having daughters. Chelsea, under the codename "Energy," found this observation "fascinating." Reines further suggested that Romney's 20-point deficit among female voters fit this pattern. He indicated plans to research past losing candidates who did not have daughters.

11. **Paging Atticus Finch**: In a separate email, Lanny Davis shared Gallup survey data showing Clinton's "near record-high favorability numbers." He offered to write a column explaining the reasons for this. Clinton responded that any commentary from him might seem biased due to their long-standing relationship. Davis then said he would not write the piece but would have described her as a hard worker, fact-based, and understanding of different viewpoints, comparing her ability to understand others to Atticus Finch from "To Kill a Mockingbird." He also called her a gracious person, essential for diplomatic roles. Clinton asked for a printed copy of the email.

12. **Counting trips and asterisks**: A June 2012 email from Reines to Clinton and Abedin detailed the Secretary's travel schedule. With seven months left in her term, Reines set a goal of visiting 110 countries, listing the 94 remaining destinations. Asterisks marked countries visited before her appointment as Secretary of State but not during her tenure.

13. **Stargazing**: In December 2012, Clinton emailed former U.S. Ambassador Joseph Wilson, stating, "I met Naomi Watts last weekend and we talked about you and Valerie and how amazing you both are!" Watts, who played Valerie Plame in the 2010 film "Fair Game," had attended the Kennedy Center Honors the previous weekend.

14. **How to get a job with Hillary**: Gary Gensler, former Chairman of the Commodities Futures Trading Commission, sent Clinton at least six emails seeking advice as she prepared to leave the State Department. Gensler, who later served as her campaign's chief financial officer, initially asked for a meeting to discuss career possibilities. After not receiving a response, he sent well wishes after her concussion, recommending chicken noodle soup and advising her not to worry about professional matters. He later expressed happiness at her recovery and reiterated his interest in connecting.

15. **Spielberg and Israel**: Ahead of a meeting with Israeli President Shimon Peres in March 2012, director Steven Spielberg expressed a desire to speak with Clinton. Andy Spahn, associated with DreamWorks SKG, emailed Clinton's Chief of Protocol, Capricia Marshall, to...

Hillary Clinton Hillary Clinton emails: latest release reveals love of Homeland Messages from private server include discussions of Arab spring, Benghazi – and once-hot TV drama Hillary Clinton at a Democratic party event on Sunday. Photograph: Mary Schwalm/Reuters Lampooned for its exaggerated plot twists and, most recently, accused of racism, Homeland has not worn the mantle of must-watch television drama for some time. Back in October 2012, however, it was still a hot enough property that Hillary Clinton was eager to catch the latest episode while enjoying some downtime from her then role as US secretary of state. The latest tranche of emails to be released from Clinton’s private server includes an exchange in which, under the subject “stupid question”, she emails a senior adviser, Philippe Reine, to announce: “Hey, I need some help.” “Do you know what channel on the TV in DC is the program listing? And, specifically, what channel number is Showtime,” asks Clinton, who adds minutes later: “Because I want to watch ‘Homeland’.” Reine comes to the rescue around an hour later, providing channel listings for both standard and high definition. Diligently, he offers: “If you don’t have Comcast, I can look it up.” The exchange is one of a number contained in the latest emails which provide a glimpse of Clinton’s private side, in addition to others showing her dealing with the politics of the Arab spring, fending off questions about her role in the deadly 2012 Benghazi attacks and attempting to navigate an intensifying conflict between Israel and Palestine. Clinton’s notes show her searching for videos on how to do a “fishtail bun” hairstyle and scheduling and rescheduling flights, meals and hairstyling appointments. The roughly 7,800 pages of emails released on Monday were part of a court-ordered disclosure of correspondence sent from the private server Clinton used while she was secretary of state. Clinton, the frontrunner for the 2016 Democratic presidential nomination, has faced questions about whether her unusual email setup was sufficient to ensure the security of government information and retention of records. Included in the most recent batch was an email that the intelligence community inspector general, I Charles McCullough III, and the State Department inspector general, Steve Linick, deemed classified in July. At least two Senate committees are still investigating Clinton’s email arrangement and seeking the release of correspondence from her top aides. The FBI is also investigating the security of Clinton’s private email setup.

This collection showcases web crawls performed as a pre-inauguration snapshot for the End of Term Web Archive. This joint effort aims to safeguard the U.S. federal government's digital footprint during each administrative change.

The archived materials include publicly available government websites. This covers sites on .gov and .mil domains, as well as related non-.gov domains. Government social media content is also included in this preservation initiative.

The web archiving occurred in the Fall and Winter of 2016. The objective was to document these websites right before the presidential inauguration on January 20, 2017. For more information, please see http://eotarchive.cdlib.org/.

This collection features web crawls conducted as part of the pre-inauguration archiving for the End of Term Web Archive. This collaborative initiative is dedicated to preserving the U.S. federal government's online presence during each administration's transition.

The archived content encompasses:

*   Publicly accessible government websites hosted on .gov and .mil domains.
*   Relevant non-.gov domains that host government content.
*   Government social media materials.

These web archives were captured in the Fall and Winter of 2016, specifically to document websites before the January 20, 2017 inauguration.

For further details, please visit: http://eotarchive.cdlib.org/.

### Declassified Emails Reveal Insights into Hillary Clinton's Private Communications

The U.S. State Department has recently declassified and released approximately 7,800 emails from Hillary Clinton's private server. This release offers a unique window into her personal and professional communications during her tenure as Secretary of State. While a comprehensive analysis will undoubtedly require more time, initial reviews have already brought several noteworthy details to light. Some of these revelations have quickly gained significant traction across social media platforms.

Among the newly accessible emails are messages from various associates offering support and well wishes following a health-related incident that occurred in 2012. Although the majority of these communications directly pertain to her responsibilities as Secretary of State, a select few touch upon the events surrounding the Benghazi attack. For individuals seeking to understand the chronological progression of these conversations, it is advisable to review the messages from the most recent to the oldest. Within this latest batch of released emails, Clinton is depicted discussing the Benghazi attack with her daughter, Chelsea. Notably, Chelsea utilized the pseudonym "Diane Reynolds" in these exchanges. An email from a long-time ally, Sidney Blumenthal, references press coverage that he asserts "refutes Libya smear." Furthermore, several months after the Benghazi incident, Clinton is also shown questioning the absence of official U.S. advisories that might have recommended American citizens evacuate Libya.

Beyond the scope of her official duties, the extensive email archive also contains a wealth of personal and informal exchanges. These communications provide glimpses into Clinton's more casual interactions. For instance, Clinton is recorded referring to Mitt Romney by the nickname "Mittens" and Newt Gingrich as "The Grinch." She also characterized political caucuses as "creatures of the parties' extremes." In one particularly relatable instance, she expressed difficulty in locating the Showtime channel, as she was trying to watch the popular series 'Homeland.' U.S. Senator Barbara Mikulski, a Democrat from Maryland, is also mentioned in an email, where she is noted for complimenting Clinton's appearance at a particular event.

The released emails also illuminate private conversations between Clinton and other key figures, including Melanne Verveer and Capricia Marshall. These exchanges are described within the context of "ladies-only communication." It appears that Clinton's staff played a role in assisting with her international travel arrangements. Diplomat Philip Gordon is also mentioned for alerting Clinton to a prank. Further insights are provided through expressions of well-wishes from Dorothea Hurley, the wife of musician Jon Bon Jovi, regarding Clinton's health. In a moment that highlights the evolving digital landscape, Clinton's staff is shown attempting to recall a time before the widespread existence of Twitter. Philippe Reines is also documented responding sarcastically to an offer from al-Shabaab rebels. This offer, rather unusually, proposed trading poultry for information concerning Clinton's whereabouts.

query: What are the main goals of A Day Without a Woman and how do the organizers plan to achieve them?

documents.text:
On March 8th, International Women's Day, a nationwide strike called "A Day Without a Woman" is scheduled to take place across the United States. While the intention behind this event is commendable, it's likely that the participants will primarily be those from more privileged backgrounds, a consequence of an initiative lacking a clearly defined purpose or a deep understanding of feminist history. This anticipated follow-up to January's Women's March risks falling short of expectations.

Organizers have outlined specific guidelines for the strike: abstaining from both paid and unpaid labor for the day, refraining from shopping, and wearing red as a symbol of solidarity. The stated objective is to "highlight the economic power and significance that women have in the US and global economies, while calling attention to the economic injustices women and gender nonconforming people continue to face," according to the strike's website.

### A Noble Idea, With Practical Hurdles

The underlying concept of the strike is indeed noble. Who wouldn't advocate for economic equality for all? However, the practical reality for many American women makes opting out of either paid or unpaid labor an unaffordable choice. This limitation, combined with the strike's broad aims, raises significant concerns. In terms of messaging and strategy, "A Day Without a Woman" appears to function more as a secondary Women's March than a strategically coordinated national labor action.

The slow build-up of momentum for the strike is a critical factor. Momentum is essential for capturing and sustaining media attention, but it also provides a crucial buffer for women hesitant about taking time off from their jobs. Experienced union organizers understand that collective power is paramount. Without this, a substantial number of working-class women may choose not to participate, perceiving the risks of standing out to outweigh any potential benefits.

It's important to note that women constitute nearly two-thirds of minimum-wage workers in the US, with women of color making up over half of those earning minimum wage or less. An Oxfam America study revealed that systemic sexism in the workplace has relegated millions of women to low-paying jobs with minimal benefits and often unpredictable schedules. This trend is projected to worsen over the next decade, meaning tens of millions of women lack the financial security or flexibility to take a day off for protest.

While organizers acknowledge that many women will be unable to strike due to economic insecurity, stating, "Many women in our most vulnerable communities will not have the ability to join the strike, due to economic insecurity. We strike for them," the initiative still feels like a protest of the privileged and, frankly, unrealistic.

### The Unseen Labor of Women

The notion of refusing to participate in unpaid labor is also unfeasible for many, particularly those without extensive resources. Unpaid labor, such as caregiving, is still labor. Individuals shouldering these responsibilities cannot simply abandon children, elderly family members, or other dependents without a readily available support network to step in. For a mother with a partner working long hours, the idea of stopping childcare responsibilities to participate in the strike is an impossibility. With family members living far away and friends also managing their own children, hiring help would be necessary. However, this directly contradicts the premise of "A Day Without a Woman," as many babysitters are themselves women. While one could theoretically opt out of cooking and cleaning, this would likely result in increased expenses and household chaos.

### Learning from Past Actions

To approach such a strike more effectively, organizers could benefit from examining the successes and limitations of previous women's movements. The Women's Strike for Equality March of 1970, organized by the National Organization for Women, began in the late afternoon to accommodate working women. More recently, Icelandic women reduced their workdays by two hours to visually represent the gender wage gap, effectively simplifying a complex issue into tangible terms. These strategies fostered greater participation and amplified the impact of their actions.

Iceland's history of women's strikes is notable. On October 24, 1975, an astonishing 90% of Icelandic women refused to work, cook, or care for children. While the sheer scale of participation was remarkable, it did not immediately resolve the nation's gender inequalities, as evidenced by a similar, though less extensive, strike in 2016.

Strikes, while empowering, tend to be most effective when focused on achieving specific policy objectives. Poland offers a compelling modern example, where tens of thousands of women striking on October 3, 2016, successfully protested a restrictive abortion ban, leading to its rejection by parliament. In contrast, the primary outcome for Icelandic women in 1975 and American women in 1970 appeared to be increased visibility.

### Beyond Visibility: The Need for Concrete Action

While demonstrating economic power in the face of discrimination serves as a powerful call to action, visibility alone is insufficient. Iceland now ranks highly on the World Economic Forum's Global Gender Gap Report, while the US lags behind at 28th out of 145 countries. Despite increased awareness of issues like paid leave, affordable childcare, maternal profiling, and the wage gap, women remain undervalued and underpaid.

Visibility is undeniably crucial for the women's rights movement globally. However, when confronting systemic and institutional inequality, concrete follow-up steps, particularly policy changes, are essential. This is particularly challenging in the US, where women remain underrepresented in government at all levels. Until parity is achieved, male allyship will be a significant component of the solution.

Ultimately, women require more than symbolic gestures like wearing red. While raising awareness about the issues and policies contributing to women's economic inequality is vital, advocacy must extend beyond public demonstrations. Providing resources such as fact sheets, suggested language for contacting elected officials, and tips for effective lobbying can empower women who cannot participate in the strike but still wish to make their voices heard.

## A Day Without a Woman: A Call for Equity and Recognition

March 8th is observed as A Day Without a Woman, a day to honor the vital contributions women make to our society. This observance stems from the spirit of unity and liberation that characterized the Women's March. Despite their essential roles, women frequently encounter lower pay, persistent inequities, and a greater risk of discrimination, harassment, and job instability. We also recognize that transgender and gender nonconforming individuals face amplified discrimination, social oppression, and political targeting. Our fundamental aim is gender justice for everyone.

### Ways to Participate

Anyone can join A Day Without a Woman on March 8th by taking part in one or more of these actions:

*   **Take Time Off:** Women are encouraged to step away from both paid and unpaid work for the day.
*   **Refrain from Shopping:** Avoid making purchases throughout the day. An exception can be made for small businesses owned by women and minority entrepreneurs.
*   **Wear Red:** Display your support and solidarity by wearing the color red.

### Our Foundations and Inspirations

A Day Without a Woman reinforces our dedication to the Principles of Unity, which were developed collaboratively for the Women’s March. We are inspired by recent impactful actions, such as the "Bodega strike" initiated by Yemeni immigrant store owners in New York City and the Day Without Immigrants observed nationwide. We also applaud initiatives like #GrabYourWallet for holding corporations accountable for their practices.

The Women's March stands with the organizers of the International Women's Strike, feminists of color, and global grassroots groups advocating for equity, justice, and human rights. The collective power demonstrated when millions united in January, driven by love, proved far stronger than fear, greed, or hatred.

Let us unite our voices again to assert that women's rights are human rights. This principle applies universally, irrespective of a woman's race, ethnicity, religion, immigration status, sexual identity, gender expression, economic standing, age, or disability.

### Additional Resources

For more information about A Day Without a Woman, please refer to our [FAQs](link to FAQs).

To notify your employer of your participation, you can use this [template letter](link to employer letter template).

An [out-of-office auto-response email template](link to out-of-office template) is also available.

Social media content and graphics can be found on [this page](link to social media page).

New Yorkers can find details about a local gathering for A Day Without a Woman [here](link to New York gathering information).

Organizers of the Women's March are orchestrating a significant demonstration for International Women's Day, March 8, aiming to underscore the profound economic and societal influence of women's labor. This initiative seeks to illustrate the tangible consequences of a day devoid of women's contributions.

Participants are encouraged to engage in several key actions:

*   **Work Stoppage:** Abstain from attending paid employment.
*   **Consumer Boycott:** Minimize commercial expenditures, with a specific exemption for enterprises owned by women and minority groups.
*   **Symbolic Attire:** Don attire in the color red to signify collective support.

Further particulars are accessible via the event's official online platform. Although the precise turnout remains undetermined, the preceding Women's March on January 21 drew substantial crowds, indicating a strong potential for widespread participation.

To better comprehend the ramifications of a day without women's participation, consider the following:

*   **Workforce Absence:** A substantial segment of the national labor force would be unavailable. In 2015, women constituted over half of the U.S. working-age demographic.
    *   Women: 51.7%
    *   Men: 48.3%

The upcoming "A Day Without a Woman" strike presents a critical question: how can a single day of action translate into enduring societal transformation? History offers compelling precedents. In 1975, Iceland witnessed a women's strike that ultimately paved the way for the nation's first female president. More recently, a similar demonstration in Poland in 2016 is widely credited with contributing to the defeat of a stringent abortion ban.

This week's planned strike encourages women to abstain from all paid and unpaid labor for 24 hours. Participants are also advised to refrain from spending money, with the exception of supporting small, women- and minority-owned businesses. The initiative aims to illuminate the substantial socioeconomic contributions of women, while simultaneously highlighting persistent issues such as wage disparities, vulnerability to discrimination, the prevalence of sexual harassment, and job insecurity.

Beyond these immediate concerns, the strike also seeks to foster a more equitable distribution of domestic responsibilities. The "A Day Without a Woman" website addresses how men can participate, suggesting they reflect on the daily care and support provided by women in their lives—whether family or colleagues—and consider how they can reciprocate this support. Organizers encourage men to actively engage in care work and housework, and for those partnered with women, to initiate conversations about the division of domestic labor. Men are also urged to advocate for equal pay in the workplace, champion their female colleagues, and critically examine workplace expectations, such as whether women are implicitly tasked with post-meeting cleanups or ordering food.

Addressing the often-contentious topic of housework and the division of labor within households, licensed professional counselors Don and Carrie Cole of the Gottman Institute's Center for Relationship Wellness offer valuable insights. They acknowledge that discussions about domestic responsibilities can be fraught with long-standing resentments and misunderstandings regarding the perceived burden of labor. The Coles emphasize that approaching these conversations with a focus on connection and positive change, rather than conflict, is paramount.

"Research consistently demonstrates that successful couples maintain a positive or neutral tone for approximately 80 percent of their conversations, even when discussing problems," Don Cole explains. He highlights the power of statements like "I appreciate everything you do" and "I understand where you're coming from." According to him, the most effective communication from a man to a woman is often "That makes sense," while for a woman to a man, it's "This isn't all your fault."

Carrie Cole elaborates on this, stressing the importance of introspection and avoiding direct criticism of one's partner. Instead of focusing on what a partner does or doesn't do, she advises articulating one's own feelings and experiences. For instance, framing a concern as "I feel like I'm an inferior person in the relationship when I'm doing these tasks by myself" is more constructive than stating, "You never help."

At couples workshops, the Coles introduce a structured approach for initiating difficult dialogues: "I feel, about what, and I need." This formula allows individuals to express their emotions, specify the issue, and clearly state their needs. Don Cole provides an example: "I feel frustrated about being alone in the kitchen, and I really need some help in here."

The Coles strongly caution against keeping score, whether internally or externally, as it perpetuates negative dialogue and detracts from the core issues. They posit that underlying meanings often drive the importance we place on certain tasks, linking them to childhood experiences, aspirations of good parenting, or other deeply held values. Consequently, even if one partner believes they are contributing equally, the other may perceive a greater imbalance.

Therefore, centering conversations around how the division of labor impacts each partner's feelings about their role within the relationship is crucial. Both men and women are encouraged to approach these discussions with an open mind and a shared objective: to ensure both partners feel equally respected and supported. The ultimate goal, as with the strike itself, is to achieve lasting change, not merely a temporary resolution.

Carrie Cole suggests that on Wednesday, men can initiate these conversations by asking their partners open-ended questions such as: "How are you feeling about the way we divide child care?" "Are there some chores we don't split equally?" and "Are there some divisions that are working well?" Don Cole emphasizes the importance of actively listening to the responses. If the current distribution feels fair and equitable, he advises to continue as is. However, if imbalances exist, the couple should collaboratively brainstorm solutions to achieve a more equal footing.

Regardless of the outcome, tuning into these conversations is vital. "When you turn toward your partner and say, 'I'm listening. This is important,' that is one of the strongest trust-building moments in a relationship," Don Cole concludes. The positive effects of such genuine engagement, he notes, extend far beyond a single day.

The organization behind the Women's March on Washington is calling for a powerful demonstration of women's economic influence on March 8, 2017. This event, known as "A Day Without a Woman," urges women to refrain from all forms of labor and spending to highlight their essential contributions to society.

Following the massive turnout for the Women's March on Washington in January, where over 2 million people protested concerns about civil, human, and reproductive rights under a new administration, organizers aim to harness that energy again. "A Day Without a Woman" is planned for International Women's Day, March 8, as a showing of economic solidarity through walkouts, rallies, and marches.

**What is International Women's Day?**
Celebrated annually on March 8, International Women's Day recognizes the social, economic, cultural, and political achievements of women. It also serves as a call to action for greater gender equality. The first observance of women's day occurred in 1909, when 15,000 women marched in New York City advocating for better pay, reduced working hours, and the right to vote.

**What is the International Women's Strike?**
The International Women's Strike is a global movement involving women in over 50 countries. It aims to address critical issues affecting marginalized women, including gender-based violence, reproductive freedom, labor rights, and environmental concerns.

**What is "A Day Without a Woman"?**
Organizers of the January Women's March are leveraging International Women's Day for a day of action. "A Day Without a Woman" seeks to spotlight the immense economic power and value of women, acknowledging their contributions in both paid and unpaid work. The event aims to draw attention to economic injustices such as wage gaps, gender discrimination, sexual harassment, and job insecurity. Furthermore, it champions gender justice, recognizing the discrimination and marginalization faced by trans and gender non-conforming individuals.

**Are the International Women's Strike and "A Day Without a Woman" the same?**
The organizers of the January Women's March and "A Day Without a Woman" are collaborating in solidarity with activists participating in the International Women's Strike.

**How can you participate?**
Women are encouraged to abstain from all work, whether paid or unpaid. Participation also involves refraining from shopping, with an exception made for local small businesses and women-owned companies supporting the event. Participants are also urged to wear the color red.

**Why wear red?**
Organizers have designated red as the color of "revolutionary love and sacrifice." Historically, red has also been associated with the labor movement.

**Can men participate?**
Yes, men are encouraged to participate by taking on caregiving and domestic responsibilities. They are also invited to rally in support of equal pay and other workplace equity issues for women.

**What if you cannot take the day off?**
Organizers acknowledge that economic insecurity may prevent some women from striking. "Many women in our most vulnerable communities will not have the ability to join the strike, due to economic insecurity. We strike for them," the organizers state on their website. For those unable to strike, wearing something red is a way to show solidarity.

**Can businesses participate?**
Businesses can support "A Day Without a Woman" by closing for the day or allowing female employees to take the day off. Companies are also encouraged to review their policies on pay equity and paid leave.

**What about private households?**
Household heads are encouraged to provide a paid day off for caregivers, nannies, and housekeepers.

**Are there any rallies you can attend?**
Numerous rallies are scheduled across the country, including events in: New York; San Francisco; Philadelphia; Pittsburgh; Washington, D.C.; Ann Arbor, Mich.; St. Petersburg, Fla.; Raleigh, N.C.; and Portland, Ore. Educational institutions like Rutgers University in New Jersey are also organizing walkouts and marches.

Advancing women's rights requires sustained momentum, and the Women's March in January served as a crucial initial step toward achieving gender equality. The upcoming "A Day Without Women" protest, drawing inspiration from the "A Day Without Immigrants" movement, powerfully highlights the indispensable role women play in society. It is truly astonishing that in 2017, the fundamental principle that women's rights are indeed human rights remains a point of contention for many.

Organizers of the Women's March have provided specific details for "A Day Without A Woman," a general strike planned to coincide with International Women's Day. These guidelines offer a spectrum of participation options, thoughtfully designed to accommodate individuals who can and cannot take the day off work. Understanding that many women face financial constraints and cannot afford to forgo a day's pay, the strike proposes alternative avenues for demonstrating solidarity. For those unable to remain home, options include abstaining from shopping or exclusively patronizing businesses owned by women and minorities. Wearing the color red is also a recognized symbol of support for the movement. Essentially, any activity you typically undertake as a woman to contribute to the economy, which you can refrain from on March 8th, serves as a powerful act of solidarity.

Recent events and reports underscore the pressing need for women and gender nonconforming individuals to actively assert their rights. These include allegations of widespread sexual harassment within the tech industry, the tragic violence experienced by trans women, the repeal of a bathroom bill that protected trans children, and the persistent, pervasive wage gap. When some individuals claim that sexism is a relic of the past, these contemporary issues serve as a stark and undeniable reminder of why "A Day Without Women" is not just relevant, but absolutely necessary. As the iconic Madonna aptly articulated, "We are living in a dream world if we think that Women have equal rights."

The sentiments shared on social media vividly illustrate the ongoing struggles and the urgent calls for change:

*   **Hypocrisy in Discourse:** A user expressed profound disbelief at conservative women who voiced concerns about sexism directed at Kellyanne Conway, yet simultaneously supported a political candidate facing accusations of misogyny. This highlights a perceived double standard in public discourse.
*   **Equal Opportunities Needed:** One tweet powerfully emphasized the critical need for equitable opportunities for women with disabilities, advocating for fair compensation and advancement prospects within the workforce.
*   **Striking for Rights:** A determined participant declared their intention to participate in the strike on March 8th, specifically to protest policy repeals, to stand in solidarity for "a day without women," and to demand basic human rights for all.
*   **Reproductive Rights Concerns:** A significant point raised referenced a statement suggesting that women should face punishment for seeking abortions, starkly underscoring deep-seated concerns regarding reproductive autonomy and rights.
*   **Fear Silences Reporting:** Data revealed that a substantial percentage of women opt not to report instances of workplace sexual harassment. This reluctance is frequently attributed to employers' inadequate compliance with established reporting procedures, fostering an environment of fear and impunity.
*   **Violence Against Trans Women:** Tragically, the early months of 2017 saw a disturbing number of trans women murdered. This grim reality highlights the urgent and critical need to actively combat the pervasive violence directed at trans women, particularly trans women of color.
*   **"Dream World" of Equality:** Directly quoting Madonna, this sentiment reiterates the belief that to perceive women as having achieved equal rights is to exist within a "dream world," suggesting a significant disconnect between perception and reality.
*   **Honoring Trailblazers:** One user shared a heartfelt tribute to their mother, a woman who served with distinction in WWII and the Korean War. This individual was recognized as a trailblazer who, despite her significant contributions, never received equal pay for her work.
*   **Accessible Participation:** This perspective underscored the inclusive nature of "A Day Without Women," emphasizing that participation is accessible to everyone, irrespective of their economic standing. Individuals were encouraged to request time off or utilize sick days to join the movement.
*   **Societal Bias Against Women:** This tweet starkly contrasted the societal presumption of innocence afforded to men with the immediate labeling of women as liars. This illustrates a deeply ingrained societal bias that actively discourages women from coming forward and reporting injustices.

If any of these sentiments resonate with your own experiences or observations, it powerfully underscores the profound necessity of participating in "A Day Without A Woman." It is an opportune moment for women, and indeed for all who stand in solidarity with women and non-binary individuals, to #BeBoldForChange on International Women's Day.

On March 8, International Women's Day, a global women's strike is planned to protest President Donald Trump and advance women's equality. This action, driven by grassroots activists, moves beyond typical observances that simply highlight women's rights.

Feminist scholars and activists view the current political climate as a prime opportunity for such a strike. They cite the significant success of the Women's March on Washington as proof of widespread discontent with the Trump administration's perceived misogyny and ongoing attacks on women's rights. This international strike, scheduled in over 30 countries, has organizers designating March 8 as "A Day Without a Woman." Women are encouraged to take the day off work if possible, or to show support in other ways.

While a women's strike in the U.S. faces hurdles due to a less robust labor movement and the inherent difficulty of general strike tactics, it clearly signals growing opposition from women. Organizers have already shown their ability to build sustained movements from single events, with "A Day Without a Woman" being one of many planned actions.

Key guidelines for participating in "A Day Without a Woman" include:

*   **Abstaining from paid and unpaid labor.**
*   **Avoiding shopping**, with exceptions for small, women- and minority-owned businesses.
*   **Wearing red** in solidarity.

Male allies are encouraged to support by:

*   Managing household duties.
*   Advocating for family-friendly workplace policies.

Collective participation is expected to have a significant impact. Some businesses and organizations, including NARAL Pro-Choice America, are closing in observance. Several school districts have also canceled classes due to anticipated teacher absences.

The strike's overall impact is hard to predict, with questions about public event visibility and participant numbers. Organizers note that a strike differs from a march; it's typically more distributed and local, with individuals acting independently rather than gathering in large public forums. However, opportunities for public protest exist, with resources available for finding local meetings and detailed suggestions for involvement. Organizers anticipate visibility through social media and the widespread wearing of red, symbolizing "revolutionary love and sacrifice" and historical labor connections. Other countries are using black to reference Poland's "Black Monday" protest.

The strike emphasizes women's absence to illustrate society's functioning without their labor. It aims to highlight the undervaluation of women's work in all areas, from the home to the boardroom. This includes disproportionate handling of chores and childcare, often for less pay or no pay. By refusing their usual labor for a day, women aim to underscore the importance and underappreciation of their contributions.

Sociology professor Elisabeth Clemens suggests that while the "International Women’s Strike" may function more as a protest or boycott, it can be a powerful tool for drawing attention to women's rights and fostering global solidarity. When many people share grievances, it can create a positive feedback loop, attracting more activists.

The strike advocates for a "feminism of the 99 percent," stressing the need for both economic and social equality. Organizers argue that the issues contributing to Trump's rise long predate his administration. They believe women's conditions, particularly for women of color and marginalized groups, have worsened over decades due to financialization and corporate globalization. They contend that strong labor organizing, rather than a focus on corporate success, is vital for improving the lives of women facing poverty, low-wage jobs, and the struggle to balance work and family. The strike aims to highlight women's economic power and significance while drawing attention to ongoing economic injustices.

Labeling "A Day Without a Woman" a "general strike" aligns with other recent protests, though past general strikes have seen limited participation. General strikes, intended to halt daily life and draw attention to grievances, are much harder to execute in the diverse United States, especially with declining union membership. The lack of legal protections for political strikes in the U.S. poses personal risks for participants, raising concerns about class and privilege.

Questions arise about the meaning of a strike in 2017, given advancements in women's education and careers. Yet, persistent inequalities make empathy across diverse struggles more complex. While past strikes made invisible work visible, defining a specific problem for female workers to address through striking is more intricate today. Conversely, some argue that labeling a strike as "privileged" is misguided. They suggest strikes have historically been tools for the least privileged, drawing parallels to the conditions that led to International Women's Day. They propose that while a strike might reflect elite concerns, it can also foster connection and solidarity between elite and marginalized women. Organizers emphasize that those able to strike do so on behalf of those who cannot, representing each other.

March 8 strikes continue an international trend of global solidarity. Women in various countries have staged strikes and walk-outs, addressing issues from abortion rights to gender-based inequality. The concept of organizing as "women" is effective, encompassing diverse struggles. Organizers argue that combating "violence" against women should extend beyond domestic or sexual violence to include systemic issues like market forces, debt, capitalist property relations, discriminatory policies, state criminalization of migration, mass incarceration, and institutional violence through abortion bans and lack of healthcare access. This intersectional approach, recognizing how different problems disproportionately harm various groups, proved successful at the Women's March, motivating a diverse range of first-time protesters.

Participating in "A Day Without a Woman" is a powerful act of solidarity. To encourage broader involvement, it's essential to understand the strike's goals and how to communicate them effectively. This guide offers talking points for common questions and reactions you might encounter.

When discussing your participation in "A Day Without a Woman," expect a range of responses. Some will offer strong support, viewing you as an ally. Others may simply be curious, asking, "What is 'A Day Without a Woman'?" For these individuals, directing them to an FAQ page is a good starting point. If more explanation is needed, provide brief answers, remembering that readily available information doesn't always require your personal, in-depth teaching.

You might also face skeptical questions, such as, "I'm not against feminism, but how will taking a day off work fix anything?" Similar to those who are uninformed, pointing them to relevant resources can be helpful. Keep the conversation focused on the strike's specific aims and the issues it addresses, rather than getting drawn into a broad debate about feminism. While educating genuinely curious individuals is valuable, maintaining focus on the strike's goals is more effective. In instances of hostile or sexist remarks, characterized by offensive language or dismissive attitudes, it's often best to disengage. Your energy is better spent on constructive actions than on arguing with someone unwilling to listen.

If someone understands the basic concept but questions the method, consider these points. A common criticism is the "privilege argument": "This initiative seems privileged. Many working women can't afford to take the day off. There are better ways to fight for equality that include economic justice." Acknowledge that participating in the strike may be easier for some than others. Emphasize that participation can take many forms beyond not working, such as making conscious consumer choices (e.g., not buying, buying less, or supporting businesses owned by women). The media attention generated can highlight systemic discrimination affecting all women. While inclusivity is a goal, dismissing the strike entirely is unproductive. Use the event as an opportunity to learn how to make future actions more inclusive. Remember, this strike doesn't preclude other forms of activism. If someone is deeply concerned about economic justice for less fortunate women, they are encouraged to initiate their own efforts.

Some may present as "demanding inquirers," stating, "I have many questions because I want to learn. However, if you can't give perfect, smart, and acceptable answers to every single one, or if you show any impatience, I'll think you're ignorant." Answering endless questions and enduring comments constitutes emotional labor—unpaid and often expected of women. You are not obligated to provide free education. Suggest they seek information through formal channels like community college classes or libraries. You have the right to end any conversation at your discretion.

The "ineffective protest" argument might surface: "This won't significantly impact the economy, change laws, or remove sexist politicians. You're wasting your time." This perspective overlooks the power of collective action. Strikes, marches, and social media campaigns amplify voices often unheard in mainstream discussions. Engaging people directly in issues that affect them is crucial, especially between elections. Sustained civic involvement beyond voting is important, and attention-grabbing actions like strikes draw more individuals into the movement.

Finally, the "not feminist enough" question arises: "If feminism is about equality between men and women, why is a strike just for women considered feminist? Shouldn't we treat everyone the same?" This argument often misses the core issue. Women's specific struggles and systemic disadvantages necessitate focused action. Addressing these unique challenges is a vital step toward achieving broader equality.

For more specific queries, consider these responses. The designated color for this strike is red. While donating to organizations like Planned Parenthood is valuable, it differs from direct action. Civic involvement extends beyond financial contributions; it requires active participation. Supporting women's causes financially is commendable, but it is not equivalent to actively joining the strike. If you genuinely cannot participate on the day itself due to prior commitments, avoid making a significant announcement or seeking approval. Participate in future events. Reflect on your priorities if such conflicts are frequent. For inspiration, historical accounts like "Newsies" might offer relevant narratives.

query: What significant milestone did Facebook achieve in its live streaming feature according to the recent news article?

documents.text:
Facebook's investment in live streaming is showing promising results. A significant portion of videos shared on the platform, specifically one-fifth, are now live broadcasts. This data point was recently shared by Fidji Simo, Facebook's head of video, via a Facebook post.

The introduction of live broadcasting capabilities for all users a year ago has led to a substantial increase in engagement. Daily watch time for Facebook Live broadcasts has more than quadrupled during this period. This growth is particularly noteworthy given the intense competition in the live streaming space.

While competitors like Twitter made significant moves into video, including the acquisition of the livestreaming app Periscope, Facebook has successfully captured the attention of younger demographics with its innovative live streaming features. Simo highlighted these advancements, stating, "We’ve added live masks and new creative effects, built features that give publishers more control and flexibility over their broadcasts, and rolled out exciting new formats like Live 360 or Live Audio."

The escalating popularity of Facebook Live, especially among younger demographics, positions it as a valuable asset for businesses. Understanding how to effectively utilize this tool to connect with audiences is paramount. Integrating Facebook Live into a digital marketing strategy can tap into a substantial social media community.

Before implementation, it's crucial to familiarize yourself with the platform's functionalities and how they can be leveraged for business benefit. Once a comprehensive understanding is achieved, businesses can explore diverse marketing applications. Whether it involves promoting an upcoming event or offering a sneak peek of a new product, Facebook Live offers a powerful avenue for making a tangible impact.

**Migrant Activist Hugo Castro Safely Located After Disappearance in Mexico**

San Diego-area migrant activist Hugo Castro has been safely located following his disappearance in Mexico last Thursday. His partner, Gaba Cortes, confirmed his safe return via a Facebook announcement on Tuesday afternoon. "Communicating to our friends, family, that Hugo has been found alive. That’s what we can share. We thank those who intervened," Cortes stated.

Castro's disappearance on Thursday marked four days since his last known contact. He had previously issued a public plea for help, providing clues to his whereabouts through a Facebook Live broadcast. Mexican and U.S. authorities, alongside Castro's friends and family, had actively participated in the search efforts. Concerns for the Border Angels volunteer's well-being escalated after he stated on Facebook Live that he had encountered a "group of criminals" intending to harm him and expressed distrust in local law enforcement. In a 20-minute recording of his Facebook Live broadcast on Thursday afternoon, Castro conveyed his distress: "I was threatened. They want to kill me. I’ve been walking for a long time, they don’t want to sell me anything, and they’re looking at me as if I were a dog. I need help."

Castro's unsettling disappearance generated significant concern, with a scarcity of official details fueling widespread speculation and rumors across social media platforms. Many individuals turned to his Facebook Live broadcast for any potential clues regarding his situation. The events began when Castro departed from his residence in Tijuana on April 9, intending to travel to the Mexican state of Chiapas. He was scheduled to rendezvous with a caravan of individuals assisting migrants journeying from Central America to seek asylum in the United States, as reported by The San Diego Union-Tribune.

During his journey, Castro reported being followed and cornered by individuals in trucks near Puebla. It was at this critical juncture that he initiated his Facebook Live broadcast to solicit assistance. The video commences with Castro standing on the shoulder of a busy highway situated between Puebla and Mexico City. He meticulously described visible landmarks, including a water storage tank and the highway marker Kilometer 37, where he urgently pleaded for someone to pick him up. He mentioned that local residents were refusing to accept his U.S. dollars and that he had encountered a "group of criminals" who had been pursuing him. "I really need help. I can’t move from here. My battery is going to die. I’m not going to move from here," he stated in the video, which served as his last known contact before his disappearance on Thursday.

San Diego-based Border Angels activist Hugo Castro was officially declared missing on Thursday, April 13, 2017, following his desperate Facebook Live plea. An image distributed in a bulletin shared on social media depicted him. As of Monday morning, Castro remained unfound, but his friends and relatives continued to post heartfelt appeals for his safe return on his Facebook profile. "Hugo Castro, we ask God that you soon return home," read one such post. His video and a missing person bulletin were widely disseminated across social media platforms. Over the weekend, supporters organized prayer vigils at Chicano Park in San Diego. Messages of support and pleas for help, articulated in both Spanish and English, circulated extensively on Facebook.

Following Castro's disappearance, his partner and fellow Border Angels activist, Gaba Cortes, utilized Facebook Live to solicit help and disseminate details about his journey. She indicated that he was traveling by bus to Chiapas and appeared to be in good spirits the night before he went missing. "It’s very hard to talk about this," she expressed. In a separate video recorded on Friday, Cortes implored the public to refrain from spreading rumors and speculation concerning Castro's fate. On Sunday, a woman identifying herself as Cortes' sister established a PayPal account and requested donations via Facebook to fund her trip to Mexico City to aid in the search for Castro. Enrique Morones, founder of the immigrant rights group Border Angels, informed KPBS that Castro had received death threats in recent months while assisting deported Haitian migrants in Tijuana. "These people that threatened him were trying to take advantage of the Haitians and Hugo was defending them," Morones told KPBS. It remains undetermined whether these prior threats are connected to his recent disappearance.

Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption WATCH: 'Our hearts go out to the family' Facebook's chief has paid his respects to the family of a man whose killing was filmed and posted onto its site. "Our hearts go out to the family and friends of Robert Godwin Sr," said Mark Zuckerberg near the start of Facebook's annual F8 developers conference. His social network had been criticised over the amount of time it had taken to take the clip offline. About an hour before the event got underway, police had revealed that the murder suspect had killed himself. Steve Stephens had been the subject of a national manhunt. He was believed to have uploaded a video to Facebook showing his killing of 74-year-old Mr Godwin in Cleveland on Sunday and then boasting on subsequent Facebook Live streams that he had killed others.
