query: What role did Mahalia Jackson play in the delivery of Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech?

documents.text: Without Mahalia Jackson, Martin Luther King Jr.'s famous "I Have a Dream" speech might never have happened. Jackson, known as the Queen of Gospel, was a musical legend who helped bring gospel from church to mass audiences. She mentored Aretha Franklin and Della Reese, and in 1961 was the first gospel singer to win a Grammy. She was also instrumental to the civil rights movement, especially as a good friend of King's. Shortly after meeting King at the National Baptist Convention in 1956, Jackson agreed to sing at a fundraising rally for the Montgomery bus boycott. After that, she frequently accompanied King to perform at rallies and events. Her voice became "the soundtrack of the civil rights movement," as NPR's Sonari Glinton put it. Jackson was devoted to King, and accompanied him into the most hostile parts of the segregated South for rallies and demonstrations. Even in moments when King felt discouraged, he would call Jackson on the phone just to hear her sing. This bond of mutual inspiration and respect between King and Jackson came at a pivotal moment during the 1963 March on Washington. "Tell them about the dream, Martin! Tell them about the dream!" King had struggled with his speech, which was supposed to be kept to five minutes. His advisers argued over which themes he should include. King himself was torn between two metaphors he liked, figuring he only had time for one. There was the image of a "bad check," representing America's failure to deliver on her promises of freedom to her black citizens. And then there was the idea of King's "dream" for a nation undivided by racial tensions, which he had used in speeches throughout the previous year in cities like Detroit and Birmingham, Alabama. Check out the Detroit version of the speech here — it has a lot in common with the much more famous March on Washington version, but the rhetoric is a bit less soaring and the grievances a bit more specific. King originally thought the speech should be lower-key, since he was speaking to a broad audience about controversial themes. So the "bad check" image won out — at least in the original printed version of the speech, which doesn't even mention the word "dream." (Can you imagine generations of schoolchildren being taught about MLK's "Insufficient Funds" speech?) But during delivery, King started improvising a bit when he reached a sentence that felt clunky. Instead of calling on the crowd to "go back to our communities as members of the international association for the advancement of creative dissatisfaction," he went with: "Go back to Mississippi; go back to Alabama; go back to South Carolina; go back to Georgia; go back to Louisiana; go back to the slums and ghettos of our Northern cities, knowing that somehow this situation can and will be changed." It was at that moment, says King's adviser Clarence Jones, that Mahalia Jackson cried out: "Tell them about the dream, Martin! Tell them about the dream!" It was, Jones said, "one of the world’s greatest gospel singers shouting out to one of the world’s greatest Baptist preachers." Jones, who was standing about 50 feet away from King during the speech, recalled that King looked over at Jackson briefly after she shouted. "Then he takes the text of the written speech that's been prepared, and he slides it to the left side of the lectern, grabs the lectern, looks out on more than 250,000 people there assembled." Jones remembers turning to the person next to him and saying, "These people out there, they don’t know it, but they’re about ready to go to church." Then King started speaking completely off the cuff. That ad-lib became "I Have a Dream." Jones isn't sure, but he thinks Jackson must have heard one of King's earlier versions of the "dream" speech, and that she knew the moment called for it. Jones said when Jackson called out to King it was like a "mandate to respond," and King's body language transformed from lecturer to preacher. "I have never seen him speak the way I saw him on that day," Jones said. "It was as if some cosmic transcendental force came down and occupied his body. It was the same body, the same voice, but the voice had something I had never heard before." It's no wonder Jackson was King's favorite gospel singer, and that he would be so inspired by her at just the right time. Here's Jackson singing "I Been 'Buked and I Been Scorned" at the March on Washington right before King spoke. She would have had her place in civil rights history with this performance even without what came next. For more on other amazing women entertainers and activists who shaped the March on Washington and the civil rights movement, check out this great slideshow from the Root.

On September 20, 1958, a pivotal moment in civil rights history unfolded in Blumstein's Department Store in Harlem. Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., then 29 years old, was engaged in a book signing for his influential work, "Stride Toward Freedom," which chronicled the pivotal 1955 Montgomery Bus Boycott he had led.

A seemingly ordinary encounter took a terrifying turn when Izola Curry, a 42-year-old woman, approached the esteemed reverend. After confirming his identity, Curry uttered a chilling phrase: "I've been looking for you for five years." In that instant, she violently plunged a letter opener into Dr. King's chest.

Upon the arrival of law enforcement, they discovered the civil rights leader seated, the letter opener's ivory handle starkly visible just below his collar. The immediate concern was the blade's perilous proximity to Dr. King's heart. Officer Al Howard, recognizing the extreme danger, cautioned him, "Don't sneeze, don't even speak."

As Curry was apprehended, Dr. King was swiftly transported to Harlem Hospital. There, a critical surgical team, including chief of thoracic and vascular surgery John W.V. Cordice, Jr., and trauma surgeon Emil Naclero, was urgently assembled. Dr. Naclero, having been en route from a wedding, arrived prepared for the life-saving operation, still in his tuxedo.

But King and fellow organizers with the Southern Christian Leadership Conference saw misery in the North as well. Southern blacks who had moved to Chicago in search of better jobs and better lives instead met firebombings and racist housing covenants that squeezed them into crowded slums on the city's South and West sides.

As Martin Luther King Jr. Day approaches, it's important to think about heroism in social justice. Historian Dixon Wecter noted in 1941 that "Hero-worship answers an urgent American need," linking it to patriotism and our desire for figures of hope. While Dr. King's role in the Civil Rights Movement and his heroic image are clear, we should look closely at how we remember leaders. This ensures the collective work of the movements they led isn't forgotten.

Dr. King's holiday honors his life and impact. However, the simplified stories often told about his legacy need more thought. This history can shape how we see leadership, especially in the Black community. It can hide the key roles of many others—including queer activists, women, and working-class organizers—who were vital to the movement's success.

Easy stories and strong figures are important for showing a movement's spirit, attracting people, and getting them involved. But in America, there's a trend to make one leader stand for the whole movement. This puts a lot of pressure on one person. Dr. King's message, his calm manner, strong voice, and commitment to non-violence, offered simple phrases and images that media and white audiences liked. For a community often shown in harmful ways, having a visible representative who gained some white acceptance was important.

Today's activism, especially among younger people, favors shared power and local organizing, moving away from focusing on one hero. Movements like Occupy Wall Street and Black Lives Matter show this change. Black Lives Matter's spread-out nature, sometimes seen as a weakness, can be a democratic way for different people to work together. This shift might come from lessons learned during President Obama's time, when big policy goals faced tough political realities.

Whether this new way of organizing movements works is still debated. Occupy Wall Street's lack of a clear leader led to disorganization. Black Lives Matter, however, has succeeded by stating clear goals and having many voices, shifting focus from speakers to the movement's main message.

Technology helps this shift, allowing movements like Black Lives Matter to share many voices. But working in a structured society as a spread-out group has challenges. Scholar Barbara Ransby has explained the complexities of less structured movements. She notes that the idea of "leaderless" movements often comes from misunderstanding Ella Baker's ideas. Baker famously said, "Strong people don't need [a] strong leader." She wasn't against leadership itself, but against systems that weakened ordinary people and helped those already in power. Ransby stresses, "Baker was not against leadership. She was opposed to hierarchical leadership that disempowered the masses and further privileged the already privileged."

While it's hard to imagine the Civil Rights Movement without Dr. King, it's also vital to celebrate the contributions of figures like Ella Baker, Bayard Rustin, A. Phillip Randolph, and many others who worked hard. As we remember Dr. King, we must ensure history recognizes everyone who paved the way. This Martin Luther King Day, let's honor Martin the man, but always remember that movements need dedicated participants, not worshipped figures.

The establishment of Martin Luther King Jr. Day as a national holiday was a complex and protracted process, a testament to persistent advocacy and evolving political dynamics. Despite Dr. King's profound impact, this significant observance, now a cornerstone of January, faced considerable opposition and debate before its eventual enactment.

The movement to honor Dr. King with a national holiday began shortly after his assassination. Early legislative attempts to recognize his birthday failed to gain momentum. A decade later, in September 1979, a bill introduced by Representative John Conyers Jr. did not pass, drawing renewed public attention. During this period, musician Stevie Wonder released his song "Happy Birthday," a powerful and direct plea for the holiday's establishment, explicitly addressing its opponents.

In 1981, President Ronald Reagan initially expressed reservations. His administration, as reported by TIME in February 1982, grappled with a "sensitivity gap" regarding minority groups. While a committee was proposed to address these issues, TIME suggested considering the MLK Day matter. Laurence I. Barrett of TIME noted the White House's noncommittal stance, suggesting that embracing the holiday might appear condescending given the administration's perceived indifference to other racial matters.

By the summer of 1983, however, the passage of a holiday bill seemed likely. Representative Katie Hall had reintroduced the legislation in July. Walter Isaacson of TIME observed that "Faced with inevitable congressional passage of a bill to make Martin Luther King‘s birthday a national holiday, Reagan swallowed his longstanding objections... and decided that he would support the measure." This shift was also influenced by the approaching election season, as a gesture like establishing MLK Day could appeal to moderate white voters.

That fall, Congress passed the bill, designating Dr. King's birthday as a national holiday, observed on the third Monday in January, beginning in 1986. This honor had previously been bestowed only upon George Washington. The legislative journey was contentious. Senator Jesse Helms actively campaigned against the bill, citing Dr. King's alleged communist sympathies. Helms threatened a filibuster, sought to unseal Dr. King's FBI files, and estimated the economic cost of a new holiday at $12 billion in lost productivity.

President Reagan's nuanced position was further highlighted in a letter to New Hampshire Governor Meldrim Thomson, another opponent. Reagan acknowledged his own reservations about Dr. King's communist sympathies but stated it was ultimately irrelevant, as public perception often prioritized image over reality. On November 2, 1983, President Reagan signed the bill into law.

Interestingly, some TIME readers suggested that the very difficulty in establishing the holiday had a positive outcome. Retta and Charles Gray of North Carolina wrote, "As supporters of the King holiday bill, we thank Senator Jesse Helms for helping to secure the bill’s passage. Helms reminded us by his behavior of the freedoms the Rev. Dr. King fought for." This perspective underscores how the intense debate ultimately highlighted the principles of freedom and civil rights that Dr. King championed, solidifying the significance of his enduring legacy.

To commemorate Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and the national holiday observed in his honor each year on January 18th, let's explore nine key facts about this pivotal figure in the American civil rights movement. Born Michael King on January 15, 1929, in Atlanta, Georgia, his name was later changed to Martin Luther in honor of the Protestant Reformation leader by his father, Reverend Martin Luther King, Sr. Dr. King, the eldest son of Reverend King and Alberta Williams King, became a beacon of hope and a driving force in the fight for racial equality and human rights.

Here are nine key facts about Dr. King and the national holiday observed in his honor:

*   **A Name Change with Historical Significance:** While born Michael King, his father, Reverend Martin Luther King, Sr., renamed him Martin Luther King Jr. in tribute to the influential German theologian. This renaming connected him to a lineage of significant historical figures.

*   **Coretta Scott King: A Partner in the Movement:** Dr. King's wife, Coretta Scott King, hailed from Perry County, Alabama. She met Dr. King while pursuing her vocal studies at Boston University. The couple married on June 18, 1953, and together they raised four children: Yolanda Denise, Martin Luther III, Dexter Scott, and Bernice Albertine.

*   **Pastor at Dexter Avenue Baptist Church:** Dr. King served as the pastor of Alabama's Dexter Avenue Baptist Church from 1954 to 1960. It was during this period that he became deeply involved in the burgeoning civil rights movement. In 1955, he was chosen as the spokesperson for the Montgomery Improvement Association, a role that placed him at the forefront of the Montgomery bus boycott, sparked by Rosa Parks' courageous act of defiance.

*   **"Letter from a Birmingham Jail": A Powerful Testament:** Throughout his activism, Dr. King was arrested an astonishing 30 times. One notable arrest occurred for a minor traffic violation: driving 30 mph in a 25 mph zone. It was during a 1963 incarceration in Birmingham, Alabama, that he penned one of his most profound and enduring works, the "Letter from a Birmingham Jail." This eloquent defense of nonviolent resistance remains a cornerstone of civil rights literature.

*   **The Iconic "I Have a Dream" Speech:** In 1963, Dr. King delivered his legendary "I Have a Dream" speech from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial in Washington, D.C. An estimated 250,000 people gathered to hear his powerful words. This speech was later recognized as the top American speech of the 20th century in a 1999 poll conducted by scholars of public address. Following this historic address, *Time* magazine named him its Man of the Year. A year later, at the age of 35, he became the youngest recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize.

*   **Assassination and Legacy:** Dr. King's assassin, James Earl Ray, a native of Illinois, tracked Dr. King across several states. Ray purchased a Remington Game Master Model 760 rifle in Birmingham and used it to carry out the assassination in Memphis, Tennessee, on April 4, 1968. Dr. King was laid to rest on April 9 in Atlanta, near Ebenezer Baptist Church. James Earl Ray, who confessed to the murder, died in prison in 1998 at the age of 70.

*   **The Lorraine Motel and the National Civil Rights Museum:** Dr. King was assassinated as he was leaving the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee, where he had been supporting striking African American sanitation workers. The room he occupied was subsequently preserved and transformed into a memorial. The hotel itself was later renovated and reopened in 1991 as the National Civil Rights Museum, a significant landmark dedicated to the struggle for equality.

*   **Stevie Wonder's "Happy Birthday": A Musical Tribute:** In 1980, Grammy Award-winning artist Stevie Wonder released the song "Happy Birthday" as a tribute to Dr. King. Wonder was a dedicated advocate for the establishment of the King holiday and played a key role in organizing a large rally in Washington, D.C., on January 15, 1981, to further the cause.

*   **The Martin Luther King Jr. National Holiday:** Fifteen years after Dr. King's assassination, President Ronald Reagan signed legislation establishing the third Monday of January as the Martin Luther King Jr. National Holiday. The proposal for this holiday was first introduced in late 1968 and was reintroduced annually, facing considerable opposition, including a filibuster by Senator Jesse Helms of North Carolina, before its eventual passage in 1983. The holiday officially went into effect in 1986.

Information for this document was drawn from resources including the Encyclopedia of Alabama and the Martin Luther King, Jr. Center for Nonviolent Social Change (The King Center).

Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s legacy is often simplified to a message of love and equality. However, this common portrayal can obscure the more radical and complex dimensions of his activism. King was not just a preacher; he was a minister who actively defied unjust laws and a determined protester who consistently challenged societal norms. He championed resistance and strength with the same passion he expressed for love, yet these crucial elements of his mission are frequently overlooked. To genuinely honor King's memory, we must explore the full range of his beliefs and his multifaceted character. His words offer deep insights into activism and the persistent pursuit of social justice, especially when examined through the lens of his less-highlighted quotes, which reveal a more complete and authentic picture of the man, prompting deeper reflection.

King's dedication to direct action is evident in his numerous arrests, totaling approximately thirty between 1955 and 1965. Despite his steadfast commitment to nonviolence, he actively combatted oppression and refused to tolerate injustice. His advocacy extended beyond racial equality to a profound concern for the poor, recognizing the inherent connection between race and economic standing. This understanding drove his leadership of the Poor People's Campaign, a significant initiative aimed at securing economic fairness throughout America.

During the 1960s, King strongly opposed superficial remedies for deep-seated oppression, asserting that temporary fixes were inadequate to dismantle the systemic inequalities that sustained poverty and racism. He emphasized the need to understand the root causes of social divisions to achieve lasting change. King firmly believed in the capacity of every individual, irrespective of their background, age, or education, to effect a real difference. He did not see nonviolence as passive; instead, he clearly demanded freedom through both his potent words and resolute actions.

While a well-known quote speaks to his dream of brotherhood, King also understood the difficulty of changing individual hearts. He recognized that laws, though they might not alter personal beliefs, could effectively modify behavior and advance racial justice. Often seen as a moderate, King himself was frequently labeled an extremist during the Civil Rights Movement, a label he eventually embraced, famously asking in his "Letter From Birmingham Jail," "Was not Jesus an extremist for love?" This viewpoint challenges those who selectively interpret his legacy, particularly white moderates who agreed with his objectives but not his methods or timing. To truly honor King, we must embrace his complete self, including the challenging aspects that urge us to confront difficult truths.

Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s legacy extends far beyond his powerful oratory; he was a masterful architect of change, deeply invested in the economic underpinnings of his vision for America. While his soaring speeches are widely celebrated, King's strategic thinking was profoundly shaped by financial realities. He meticulously considered how to fund the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) and, more broadly, how to dismantle the economic injustices faced by marginalized communities across the nation. This perspective positions King not just as a civil rights leader, but as a significant economic thinker. He understood that lasting societal transformation required sustainable, even macroeconomic, strategies—moving beyond immediate aid to empowering individuals with the means for self-sufficiency. As King himself articulated, he aimed not to be a mere "Good Samaritan" on the roadside, but to prevent people from being attacked in the first place. Beyond his role as a revered minister, King's engagement with economic principles warrants deeper examination. While he may not be listed alongside economists like John Maynard Keynes or Friedrich von Hayek, it's worth noting that Adam Smith, the foundational figure of classical economics, was a professor of moral philosophy at Glasgow University, a field that encompassed rhetoric, philosophy, ethics, religion, empathy, and justice—all topics central to King's discourse. This Martin Luther King Jr. holiday offers an opportunity to explore three key areas where economics informed Dr. King’s message and actions.

Firstly, King masterfully employed economic rhetoric to make his message accessible and resonant. He frequently utilized familiar financial metaphors to ensure his vision could be broadly understood and embraced. In his iconic "I Have a Dream" speech, he powerfully illustrated economic injustice through the metaphor of a bounced check. He translated a complex societal problem into relatable "pocketbook" terms: "We’ve come to our nation’s capital to cash a check," he declared, referencing the Constitution and Declaration of Independence as a "promissory note." He lamented that instead of honoring this obligation, America had issued a "bad check" to Black citizens, marked "insufficient funds." Yet, he affirmed, "We refuse to believe that bank of justice is bankrupt." This strategic use of language, as highlighted by scholars like Dr. Clayborne Carson, director of the Martin Luther King, Jr. Research and Education Institute at Stanford University, was crucial in galvanizing public support.

Secondly, King and his fellow civil rights leaders were astute strategists, adept at orchestrating events that would capture national attention and drive change. Recognizing that violent protests would alienate potential allies and exacerbate racial tensions, King focused on economic leverage. He and his team championed and organized boycotts, leveraging the collective purchasing power of African Americans. The Montgomery bus boycott, which commenced on December 1, 1955, and concluded on December 20, 1956, significantly impacted Montgomery City Lines, a bus service already facing declining ridership and revenue, as documented by historian Taylor Branch. This economic pressure intensified the company's financial woes and provoked a strong reaction from city officials, leading to King's arrest. As King demonstrated, impactful change could be achieved by targeting the economic bottom line, rather than resorting to physical confrontation.

Thirdly, King's policy objectives were intrinsically linked to his fight against poverty, racism, and militarism. He made significant strides in combating institutional racism, contributing to the passage of landmark legislation such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965. He was also a vocal critic of the Vietnam War, condemning the violence America was perpetuating in Southeast Asia, a stance that drew criticism from President Lyndon B. Johnson's administration, according to accounts from the LBJ Presidential Library. However, his ambition to eradicate poverty remained a central, albeit less realized, goal. King observed, "This country has socialism for the rich, rugged individualism for the poor," underscoring his critique of economic inequality. He increasingly advocated for policies such as a bill of rights for workers and federal anti-poverty legislation. In the weeks leading up to his assassination, King was actively organizing the "Poor People’s Campaign," a planned march on Washington D.C. Although the march proceeded with an estimated 50,000 participants, the movement lost momentum following King's death, and the economic legislation he championed was never enacted. Consequently, King's dream of economic justice remains, in many respects, unfulfilled. It requires continued efforts from those who recognize this great American figure not only as a political leader but also as a profound economic strategist. Kabir Sehgal, author of the New York Times bestseller "Coined: The Rich Life of Money And How Its History Has Shaped Us," and a former vice president at J.P. Morgan, further elaborates on these themes in his work.

query: What inspired Ryan's parents to start the Ryan ToysReview channel?

documents.text: PewDiePie and Justin Bieber (and every other online video creator in the world) have nothing on a charismatic four-year-old with a propensity for Disney Cars and Thomas The Tank Engine. PewDiePie, YouTube’s most-viewed and most-subscribed star of all-time, and Bieber, another digital goliath who was seeing unbelievable views even months after the release of his latest studio album Purpose, have both been eclipsed by nascent channel Ryan ToysReview at the top of our Tubefilter charts — which track the world’s most-viewed channels on a weekly and monthly basis with data provided by OpenSlate. Having teetered at the top of these charts since last November, four-year-old Ryan — who has emerged as a standout within the humongously popular children’s toy unboxing space — currently resides as YouTube’s most-watched channel in the world, with more than 585.3 million views in July alone. What makes this feat even more stunning is the fact that Ryan ToysReview, which boasts a relatively straightforward premise and homespun production values, was established just over one year ago. Since March 2015, Ryan has amassed more than 4 million subscribers and over 6.5 billion lifetime views. To give those stats some context, since Ryan ToysReview’s rise to prominence on about November 20, 2015, the channel has amassed about 5.6 billion views for an average of 19.4 million views per day, 807,090 views per hour, 13,541 views per minute, and 224.2 views per second. By comparison, PewDiePie scored 2.71 billion views at a rate of 9.3 million views per day, 386,861 views per hour, 6,448 views per minute, and 107 views per second during the same time frame. We spoke with Ryan’s parents — who wished to remain anonymous — about why they decided to start making videos, how they’ve spun the pastime into a full-fledged family business, and what Ryan makes of his new-found viral fame. Check out the interview, which has been condensed and edited for clarity, below: Tubefilter: What inspired you to start the Ryan ToysReview channel? Mom: Ryan was watching a lot of toy review channels — some of his favorites are EvanTubeHD and Hulyan Maya — because they used to make a lot of videos about Thomas The Tank Engine, and Ryan was super into Thomas. One day, he asked me, ‘How come I’m not on YouTube when all the other kids are?’ So we just decided — yeah, we can do that. Then, we took him to the store to get his very first toy — I think it was a lego train set — and it all started from there. Dad: Another reason we started is because Ryan has a lot of extended family outside of the U.S., so YouTube was a great way to share childhood memories with them. It’s also a great way for us to spend more time together as a family and to bond with him. TF: At what point did you realize that you had a viral phenomenon on your hands? Dad: About four months after we started, we began to see a serious spike in viewership, and, after that point, views started doubling month after month. Our largest demographic is children aged three to seven years old. Most of our viewers are in the U.S., but Ryan also has a sizable following in the U.K. and in the Philippines. The most popular video on our channel, titled 100+ cars toys GIANT EGG SURPRISE OPENING Disney Pixar Lightning McQueen kids video Ryan ToysReview (below), has about 480 million views. I don’t know why so many people love that video — if I did, I’d make a lot more just like it. What we’ve also noticed is that that video is carrying a lot of the traffic flow to the rest of the channel. TF: Has this become a family business? Mom: I was a high school chemistry teacher and my husband is a structural engineer, but I left my job to focus on the channel full-time. We had no prior experience on YouTube — I’d never uploaded a video in my life — and we’ve learned everything as we’re going. We post a new video every day, and we typically film two to three videos at a time two to three times per week. We try not to interfere with Ryan’s pre-pre-school schedule, so a majority of the filming takes place during the weekend, and then we’ll edit while he’s in school. TF: How do you determine which toys to feature? Dad: Disney Cars and Thomas The Tank Engine were the main brands we were focused on when we first started because we noticed that those were keywords on a lot of other popular videos. But now, it’s mainly what Ryan wants to do. When we go to the toy store, we ask him what he wants to play with, and we let him guide us. Recently, we’ve started to get into the business of branded videos, but 99% of the videos on our channel aren’t branded. And we pay for all of the toys that Ryan reviews ourselves. TF: You must have a ton of toys. Mom: One room in our house is completely dedicated to toy storage, and another is used as a filming studio. But Ryan doesn’t keep all the toys he gets — we give a lot of them away to friends and family, and also a lot of them away to charity. TF: What does Ryan make of all that he’s accomplished thus far? Dad: He’s only four, so he doesn’t fully understand everything yet. But when we’re out in public — either at a toy store or Target or grocery shopping — people recognize him and he gets very excited. He thinks they’re his friends and he wants to play with them. Mom: Right now, he loves making videos. Every time I tell him we’re going to film, he gets so excited. As long as he’s loving it and it doesn’t disrupt his daily routine, we plan on continuing. But the moment he’s not having fun any more, that’s when it will be time to stop.

Ryan's World is a popular YouTube channel where kids can watch toy reviews. The star, Ryan, shows off many different kinds of toys.

Some of Ryan's favorite toys include:

*   Toy cars and trains, like Thomas and Friends

Beyond toys, Ryan's World also features simple science experiments for children. Many of the toys shown are donated to charity. These toys are often part of Ryan's Toys & Clothing lines sold at Walmart and Target.

You can find more on Ryan's World:

For business inquiries, email: ryantoysreviewbiz@gmail.com

EK Doodles brings you the animated escapades of Emma and Kate, Ryan's younger siblings. These humorous cartoons for children provide a window into the lives of the twin sisters. Emma is known for her bold and adventurous spirit, while Kate is characterized by her self-reliance and nurturing disposition.

The series chronicles Kate and Emma's exploration of their world and the challenges they encounter.

Empowered by their enchanted stamp, the twins set out on expeditions filled with discovery and amusement.

Audiences can witness the unique ways Kate and Emma engage with their environment and one another.

Forbes BrandVoice offers marketers a unique way to engage directly with the Forbes audience. This innovative platform empowers brands to create their own content and join discussions across Forbes' digital publishing channels.

Each BrandVoice campaign is developed independently by the marketer. This ensures that the content is tailored to the brand's specific goals and messaging.

For those interested in learning more about BrandVoice or starting a campaign, please visit brandvoice.com.

At just seven years old, Ryan, a burgeoning entrepreneur, has achieved remarkable financial success through his popular YouTube channel, Ryan ToysReview. This family-operated venture, dedicated to showcasing and reviewing toys, reportedly amassed an impressive $11 million in earnings before taxes in 2017, as highlighted by Forbes' esteemed list of top-earning YouTubers. This substantial income positioned Ryan ToysReview in an eighth-place tie with the well-known comedy duo Smosh on the prestigious Forbes ranking.

The genesis of the channel dates back to March 2015. At the tender age of four, Ryan, already an avid viewer of toy review content, expressed a keen desire to create his own videos. While the channel experienced a gradual growth trajectory initially, a pivotal video uploaded in July 2015 catapulted it to viral fame. This particular video showcased Ryan's unboxing and enthusiastic review of over 100 toys from the beloved Pixar "Cars" franchise, ultimately garnering nearly 800 million views.

Presently, the channel's content extends beyond toy reviews to include new toy releases and children's food products. Ryan's videos are widely recognized for his genuine enthusiasm and candid reactions, with his parents providing essential guidance behind the scenes. The Verge has reported that the channel's expansive audience, which boasts 10 million subscribers, generates approximately $1 million each month solely from advertising revenue.

Witness the delightful experience of this fortunate young child as he reviews a DIY candy dispenser in the accompanying video.

YouTube has become a powerful platform for creators to build careers and achieve significant success. Many prominent YouTubers, like Daniel Middleton, known as "DanTDM," have turned their online presence into substantial personal brands.

A primary source of income for these creators is through sponsored content and advertising revenue. This allows them to monetize their viewership and build a sustainable livelihood.

Beyond ad revenue, some top YouTubers have expanded their reach into traditional media. They have secured lucrative television deals with major streaming services, including Netflix and YouTube's own premium service, YouTube Red.

This success naturally leads to the question of which YouTube creators are earning the most. Forbes, a respected financial publication, recently published its analysis of the highest-earning YouTube stars for 2017. Their rankings were based on careful estimations of pretax income.

The following list highlights these top earners. It's worth noting that Nathan McAlone and Maya Kosoff contributed to an earlier version of this report.

**Ryan: The Unlikely YouTube Phenomenon Redefining Childhood Entertainment**

Ryan, a typical five-year-old, enjoys toy cars, tricycles, and water slides. His mother, like many parents, documents his childhood. However, Ryan's life is far from ordinary. His everyday moments, from unboxing a new action figure to visiting Chuck E. Cheese, are broadcast to hundreds of millions of children worldwide, making him an unlikely YouTube phenomenon.

**The Genesis of a YouTube Sensation**

Ryan's family launched their YouTube channel, **Ryan ToysReview**, in March 2015. Initially, viewership was modest. But a pivotal video uploaded four months later sparked exponential growth, with views doubling each month. Ryan's mother, a former high school chemistry teacher, left her profession to dedicate herself full-time to the channel's success.

**Dominating the YouTube Landscape**

For an unprecedented 18 consecutive weeks, **Ryan ToysReview** has held the top spot as the most popular channel in the US and ranks as the second largest globally. This remarkable achievement surpasses even established giants like PewDiePie, Justin Bieber, BuzzFeed, The Tonight Show, and the WWE. This immense viewership translates into an estimated **$1 million per month in advertising revenue alone**.

**The Rise of Toy Review Channels**

"He is undoubtedly the youngest YouTube star we've ever encountered," states Josh Cohen, an industry analyst and founder of TubeFilter. Ryan was merely three years old when the channel debuted. Cohen elaborates, "This genre of programming, garnering billions of weekly views on YouTube, is incredibly significant. While often overlooked, its impact becomes astonishingly clear upon closer examination."

The concept of reviewing toys on YouTube isn't novel. As early as fall 2013, DisneyCollecterBR, a channel run by an anonymous adult woman, achieved top-10 status. Her videos featured softly spoken toy unboxings and play sessions, filmed from a static close-up perspective. By summer 2014, she consistently led the most-viewed channels in the US. The genre truly exploded when children took center stage. In March of the previous year, coinciding with Ryan's channel launch, media reports highlighted another family that achieved considerable success by sharing videos of their children playing with toys. A report by The Guardian indicated that 20 of YouTube's top 100 channels are dedicated to toys, collectively amassing over 4.5 billion monthly views.

**From Viewer to Star: Ryan's Journey**

Before stepping in front of the camera, Ryan was an avid viewer. "Ryan was watching many toy review channels – some of his favorites were EvanTubeHD and Hulyan Maya – because they produced numerous videos about Thomas The Tank Engine, and Ryan was incredibly fond of Thomas," his mother shared in an interview with TubeFilter. "One day, he asked me, 'Why am I not on YouTube when all the other kids are?' So, we decided – yes, we can do that. We then took him to the store to buy his very first toy – I believe it was a Lego train set – and that's how it all began."

While Ryan's channel is part of a larger trend, its scale is unprecedented. In less than two years, **Ryan ToysReview** boasts 5.5 million subscribers, exceeding the combined subscriber counts of the two channels that inspired it. His mother produces the channel, mastering the art of this unique genre – a fusion of personal vlogs and "unboxing" videos, blending innocent childhood moments with pervasive consumerism.

**The Evolution of Toy Reviews**

The channel's premise, as the name suggests, is Ryan reviewing toys. His inaugural video, a demonstration of his thoughts on Lego Duplo blocks at age three, is a slow, static, ten-minute recording of him unboxing, setting up, and playing. He concludes with a simple wave and "see you next time."

However, the nature of toy reviews has evolved significantly. The second video featured two toys, and subsequent episodes have showcased dozens. The channel's most popular video presents Ryan with a hundred toys simultaneously. He interacts with each for mere seconds before being engulfed in a mountain of newly opened, quickly discarded toys. This video alone has garnered 568 million views.

In more recent content, the pretense of Ryan actively playing with toys has diminished. In one clip, Ryan recites lines with a noticeable lack of genuine emotion: "I wonder what's inside it. I'm so excited." The video then shows brief close-ups of a toy car that Ryan never interacts with, followed by his repeated, smiling requests for viewers to subscribe. Videos frequently feature four or five toy names in their titles, accompanied by lengthy descriptions and numerous links to branded products. "His parents are incredibly astute," notes Cohen. "They feature toys Ryan enjoys, but also strategically select popular items across YouTube."

**YouTube Stars Driving Retail Sales**

The toy industry closely monitors stars like Ryan. "When a product achieves ten or twenty million views, and you see that Ryan, or other children, love it, it has a profound impact on retail sales," explains Jim Silver, CEO of Toys, Tots, Pets, and More. "He represents the youngest success story we've witnessed. Typically, children in reviews are six years or older, due to vocabulary and maturity requirements for a review." Silver even paid Ryan's family to attend his company's winter showcase, highlighting the common practice of toy manufacturers and retailers sponsoring videos or providing free products to popular YouTubers. "When a child resonates with other children, it can truly take off, especially given how much screen time children have on their phones at home."

**Subliminal Advertising at its Finest**

The distinction between toy manufacturers and independent reviewers is increasingly blurred. "It's a novel, highly creative, and effective form of advertising for toy companies, even if it operates on a subliminal level," observes Jason Moser, a toy industry analyst at Motley Fool. "I anticipate this trend will continue to grow." While Ryan's family asserts they purchase all reviewed toys, his father has indicated the channel is venturing into branded content. FTC-mandated disclaimers now appear on sponsored episodes.

This content format aligns perfectly with YouTube's strategic objectives. "The algorithm's design complements the inherent viewing habits of the Ryan ToysReview audience," explains Cohen. "YouTube's algorithm prioritizes longer watch times. Children who view these videos often do so for extended periods, unlike typical YouTube viewers. A three or four-year-old with an iPad is likely to watch the entire video, which YouTube highly values." Consequently, Ryan's success translates into increased revenue for numerous larger corporations.

**Uncharted Territory: Child Labor and YouTube**

Before the YouTube era, an ordinary child's antics could not be commercialized to reach hundreds of millions weekly without formal agreements with major broadcasters. Today, an average family can operate a highly profitable business from their living room. This raises significant questions, particularly concerning child labor laws. Traditional film and television productions have strict regulations on the working hours for child performers. However, these protections are largely absent for YouTube creators like Ryan.

"Child performer laws are regulated at the state level, with the federal government deferring to them," states Toni Casala, CEO of Children in Film. "Seventeen states have virtually no child labor laws pertaining to performance." For instance, the Texas Workforce Commission notes that "When a business is owned or operated by a parent or legal custodian, the parent or custodian may employ their own children at any age to work any hours, so long as the work is non-hazardous (not prohibited) and the child works under the parent or custodian’s direct supervision."

**A Parent's Perspective and Ethical Considerations**

Ryan's parents appear to adopt a balanced approach. His mother shared that they post a new video daily and film two to three videos at a time, two to three times per week, prioritizing Ryan's pre-school schedule. Filming primarily occurs on weekends, with editing done while he's at school.

However, the channel's scope extends beyond toy reviews. Whether documenting trips to Disneyland, haircuts, or even illness, Ryan's parents control what is filmed and shared. This raises critical questions: Would Ryan feel comfortable asking his parents to stop filming, or possess the vocabulary to articulate his feelings about constant documentation? How does a five-year-old process fear when their parent intervenes, yet the camera remains active? We are navigating uncharted territory, where video views translate into millions of dollars – a substantial incentive to maintain the status quo.

**The Tempting Bargain for Families**

How should parents view Ryan's channel? On one hand, the constant influx of toys could be seen as spoiling a child. Conversely, the channel's "About" page states that most toys are donated to charity. While Ryan's on-camera behavior can occasionally seem rehearsed, he generally appears to be a child enjoying himself.

It's easy to adopt a cynical perspective on the family's rapid success. Yet, for many parents, balancing work and family life is a perpetual challenge. If securing your children's financial future were possible simply by playing with them on camera, wouldn't you consider it? The Border Adjustment Tax (BAT) is a debated tax idea that could greatly affect U.S. companies and shoppers. Supporters, like House Speaker Paul Ryan and Ways and Means Chairman Kevin Brady, see the BAT as a way to boost the economy. They argue it would encourage more U.S. manufacturing by making overseas operations less attractive. They also estimate it could bring in about $1 trillion each decade, allowing for lower corporate tax rates.

However, critics, including Senators David Perdue and Tom Cotton, view the BAT as a tax increase that would ultimately cost consumers more. Their main worry is that businesses would pass on higher expenses to shoppers, leading to pricier everyday items. Senator Perdue has stated the BAT would "hammer consumers" and hinder economic growth.

The BAT proposal has drawn strong reactions from different business sectors. Companies that export a lot, such as Boeing, Merck, and Dow Chemical, generally support the plan. On the other hand, retailers that rely heavily on imports, like Target, Nike, and Walmart, strongly oppose it, expecting it to hurt their profits. The oil industry also seems to be against the BAT, with groups like the Koch brothers actively campaigning against it.

Right now, opposition to the BAT seems to have more public support. A key reason is the BAT's complexity, which makes it hard to explain its economic effects simply. Talking about its advantages often involves detailed discussions about currency changes, buying power, and global tax systems. Critics, like Senator Tom Cotton, suggest this complexity hides weak economic ideas.

While the arguments for the BAT might have merit, they struggle against warnings that consumers could end up paying for corporate tax changes. An anti-BAT group, Americans for Affordable Products (AAP), has stressed that the BAT acts like a consumer tax, essentially funding other corporate tax cuts. AAP member Brian Dodge calls it a "pay-for," rejecting claims that it supports an "America First" approach or fixes trade gaps. AAP is actively lobbying and reaching out to the public, including talking with officials, to inform lawmakers and reduce support for the BAT.

Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has voiced significant apprehension about a potential U.S. import tax, cautioning that such a policy could inflict considerable damage on the economic well-being of both Canada and the United States. During discussions with prominent energy executives in Houston, Prime Minister Trudeau articulated his deep concerns. He stated unequivocally, "Anything that creates impediments at the border, extra tariffs, new taxes is something we’re concerned with." He further elaborated on the potential repercussions, emphasizing, "You’re going to be hurting not just the Canadian economy but the American economy as well."

This firm stance from the Canadian Prime Minister directly confronts a proposed border adjustment tax championed by U.S. Republicans. The core objective of this Republican proposal is to incentivize American exports while simultaneously discouraging imports, forming a key component of a larger initiative to overhaul the U.S. tax code. While this particular tax strategy has encountered considerable resistance from businesses that heavily depend on imported goods for their operations, it has simultaneously found favor among major exporting industries, particularly within the manufacturing sector.

In parallel, Canada's Minister of Natural Resources, Jim Carr, has signaled a receptive attitude towards exploring potential adjustments to the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA). Speaking at a press conference also held in Houston, Minister Carr conveyed Ottawa's perspective that trade agreements, by their very nature, "every now and then should be refreshed." This statement was made in direct response to questions regarding the longevity and relevance of the existing NAFTA pact. This viewpoint appears to be in alignment with the publicly stated objective of U.S. President Donald Trump, who has expressed a clear desire to renegotiate the terms of this long-standing agreement, which has been in effect for more than two decades.

Minister Carr also affirmed Canada's preparedness to actively engage in discussions with the U.S. administration concerning the proposed border tax. However, he made a notable observation regarding the sentiment expressed by energy executives at the CERAWeek energy conference. According to Carr, these executives did not convey any perceived need for imposing a tax specifically on energy imports. "They want a free movement of goods and services," Carr reported. He added, "We have to do a better job as Canadians of reminding our American friends how integrated this relationship is." While he deliberately avoided making predictions about specific policy changes that might emerge from the new U.S. administration, Minister Carr reiterated Canada's unwavering commitment to "expand and deepen the network of people we do business with in the United States." This underscores Canada's strategic approach to maintaining and strengthening its economic ties with its southern neighbor.

Following the legislative setback of the American Health Care Act (AHCA), House Speaker Paul Ryan and the Trump administration have shifted their legislative focus to tax reform. This change presents a significant challenge, as the planned spending cuts from the AHCA, which were meant to help offset tax reductions, are now absent. Republicans had intended to use budget reconciliation, a procedural tool allowing legislation to pass with a simple majority and bypass Democratic filibusters, for their tax reform initiative. A key requirement of this process is revenue neutrality. Without the planned spending reductions, enacting substantial tax cuts—a central goal for President Trump and Speaker Ryan—without widening the national deficit is a considerable hurdle.

Speaker Ryan's "Better Way" initiative outlines a broad restructuring of the U.S. tax system. Key proposals include reducing the corporate tax rate from 35% to 20%, eliminating certain corporate deductions such as those for net interest, and introducing a border adjustment tax (BAT). The BAT would represent a major change in tax policy by taxing imported goods while offering tax relief for exported goods. Supporters contend that any added costs for domestic importers would be balanced by a stronger U.S. dollar, generating enough revenue to meet budget reconciliation requirements and satisfy fiscal conservatives.

However, the proposed BAT faces significant opposition. Large domestic importers, including Walmart, are actively campaigning against it, as is the influential Koch network. Retail leaders, like the CEO of Kohl's, have warned of a "massive" impact on consumers, predicting considerable price hikes and potential job losses in affected sectors. Smaller importers reportedly express greater concerns about job security than their larger counterparts. While Republican leadership has suggested openness to finding solutions, specific exemptions for small businesses remain undefined.

Many economists doubt the U.S. dollar will strengthen enough to fully compensate for import taxes, warning that this potential shortfall could hinder overall economic growth. The White House's position on the BAT has been inconsistent, with President Trump publicly voicing disapproval while also making potentially supportive remarks. Gary Cohn, former head of the National Economic Council, has offered non-committal statements. Furthermore, there are concerns that the BAT might conflict with World Trade Organization (WTO) regulations, which generally require identical tax treatment for similar goods regardless of their origin. Tax experts from White & Case note that the current proposal could permit deductions for domestic goods while denying them for identical imported products, creating potential compliance issues. Analysts at Morgan Stanley consider the border tax crucial for the success of tax reform, significantly shaping its structure. Their economists do not foresee immediate economic gains from a border tax, even with regulatory adjustments, due to anticipated currency shifts. Concurrently, their corporate credit team anticipates substantial risks arising from uncertainty in business models and instability in corporate finances.

**Germany Issues Strong Warning to US on Trade War, Threatens WTO Action**

German Economy Minister Brigitte Zypries has issued a stern warning to Washington, urging the US to abandon plans for a trade war. She stated that Germany would pursue legal action at the World Trade Organization (WTO) if the US proceeds with its proposed import tax. Zypries expressed confidence that "reason and the courts" would prevent a damaging trade conflict.

This warning comes as US Republicans advocate for a significant overhaul of the corporate tax system, including a new "border adjustment" mechanism. This system would reportedly impose a 20 percent tax on US imports while exempting export revenues. Zypries pointedly remarked that if the US border tax violates WTO rules, it would not be the first time President Trump has faced legal setbacks, referencing the repeated rejections of his immigration ban by US judges.

In 2016, the United States was Germany's largest export market, with US imports of German goods totaling 107 billion euros ($115 billion), while German imports from the US were only 58 billion euros. During his election campaign, Trump specifically targeted German luxury car manufacturer BMW, threatening border levies if the company proceeded with building a plant in Mexico. The US President has also accused Germany and China of engaging in unfair trade practices, including currency manipulation to make their exports cheaper.

However, Germany has consistently maintained that its products are simply superior and that the US should focus on improving its own manufacturing competitiveness. Zypries acknowledged Germany's substantial trade surplus with the US as a "problem" and assured that Berlin is actively working to reduce it. She also emphasized that "Americans need our machines and industrial plants" for the foreseeable future. Zypries predicted that Trump's advisors would caution him that imposing such import taxes would be a self-defeating move.

The European Union and other US trading partners are already laying the groundwork for a legal challenge against the proposed US border tax. This could potentially escalate into the largest case in WTO history. The Trump administration has criticized WTO rules that allow countries with VAT-based tax systems to offer export rebates, arguing that income-based systems like the US's are disadvantaged.

White House trade advisor Peter Navarro recently stated that the "unequal treatment of the US income tax system under biased WTO rules is a grossly unfair subsidy to foreigners exporting to the US and a backdoor tariff on American exports to the world that kills American jobs and drives American factories offshore."

Chad Bown, a WTO trade dispute expert, estimates that a US defeat at the WTO could trigger global trade retaliation worth $385 billion annually, a figure nearly 100 times larger than any previous WTO ruling. Bown further warned that if the US disregards a WTO ruling, as President Trump has threatened, it could undermine the international system designed to prevent trade wars.

The House Republican tax plan proposes a significant shift to a destination-based cash flow tax (DBCFT). This system would incorporate border adjustments, meaning exports would be exempt from taxation, while imports would be subject to taxes. The economic impact of these border adjustments hinges on how exchange rates respond, a point of considerable confusion and uncertainty.

**Understanding the DBCFT and Border Adjustments**

The DBCFT is a variation of a value-added tax (VAT), but it allows for the deduction of wages, unlike a VAT. Like a VAT, the DBCFT functions as a consumption tax, exempting normal investment returns. It primarily taxes consumption funded by specific capital income, such as returns from past investments and above-normal returns from new investments.

Border adjustments are logical for domestic consumption taxes like VATs because goods consumed domestically should face the same tax rate, regardless of origin. Exports, not consumed domestically, are excluded, while imports, consumed domestically, are taxed. This creates a level playing field for all goods consumed within the U.S., distinct from a selective tariff. All developed nations except the U.S. already employ border-adjusted VATs, rebating taxes on export production and taxing imports. Implicit border adjustments are also present in U.S. state-level retail sales taxes, where goods produced in one state and sold in another are taxed by the destination state. For a cash flow tax, the border adjustment would also be implicit, excluding export revenues and disallowing deductions for imported goods' costs. This approach aims to eliminate incentives for companies to shift profits or operations abroad by effectively disregarding foreign transactions.

**Potential WTO Compatibility and Economic Theory**

The proposed border adjustment may not comply with World Trade Organization (WTO) rules, which generally require equal treatment for imports and domestically produced goods. The DBCFT taxes the full value of imports while only taxing the portion of domestically produced goods related to above-normal capital returns. This could potentially be resolved by restructuring the DBCFT into a standard VAT combined with a wage subsidy.

Basic economic theory suggests the exchange rate should appreciate immediately by the full amount of the tax. This can be understood through the demand and supply of dollars: exempting exports increases demand for U.S. dollars, while taxing imports reduces their global supply. These effects would strengthen the dollar. Alternatively, national income accounting implies that if saving and investment remain unchanged, the trade balance must also remain unchanged. If export and import quantities don't change, the dollar's value must adjust to maintain equilibrium.

If the exchange rate fully adjusts, the border adjustment would have no impact on the trade balance, export/import volumes, domestic prices, or the profitability of importers and exporters. However, this full adjustment would lead to a significant wealth transfer from Americans holding foreign assets to foreigners holding U.S. assets and could create difficulties for emerging-market countries with dollar-denominated debt.

**Skepticism and Complicating Factors**

Many economists are skeptical about a full and immediate exchange rate adjustment due to several factors. These include potential influences on capital flows, the DBCFT's effect on government saving, exchange rate volatility, the dominance of asset flows over trade flows, government intervention, pre-existing contracts, hedging strategies, the prevalence of dollar invoicing for U.S. trade, and differential tax rates for corporations and pass-through entities.

**Consequences of Incomplete Exchange Rate Adjustment**

If the exchange rate does not adjust fully and immediately, the effects of the border adjustment would differ significantly. The trade balance would likely improve, with exports increasing and imports decreasing. Consumer prices would rise due to higher import costs, disproportionately affecting lower-income households. Exporters would see increased profits, while importers would face challenges. Short-term revenue might decline, and the wealth transfer to foreigners would be reduced, lessening risks for emerging-market countries with dollar debt.

**Empirical Evidence and Outlook**

Convincing empirical evidence on the precise exchange rate effects of border adjustments is scarce, as financial markets can anticipate legislative changes. Some data from smaller countries with similar taxes suggest a full exchange rate offset. While rapid and significant exchange rate adjustments, potentially close to full, are probable, a non-trivial risk remains that this adjustment may not be complete, which is a primary concern for opponents of the plan.

It would amount to a tax on imports, and in turn on the American family. Sen. Tom Cotton, R-Ark., in Washington on Jan. 10, 2017. (Photo: Chris Kleponis, AFP/Getty Images) The U.S. tax code is a mess, and Congress should eliminate all the special-interest loopholes in the code and use that money to cut taxes for everybody — including American businesses. Right now, some of my colleagues are working on a tax reform bill that would do just that. But I have grave reservations about one idea that’s being considered. It’s called a border adjustment tax, and here’s how it would work: We’d cut the corporate tax rate, and to make up for the lost revenue, we’d tax businesses whenever they bought something from another country. So, for instance, every time Ford bought an auto part from Canada, it would pay a 20% tax. Or every time your local grocery store brought in bananas from Guatemala, it would pay a 20% tax. And whatever money businesses made from selling their products in other countries would be exempt. But what all of this would really amount to is a 20% tax on imports. The proponents of this tax contend it will stop businesses from leaving our country — because right now, some are moving overseas to avoid paying our corporate tax rate, which is the highest in the modern, industrial world. Under this proposal, it wouldn’t matter where you put your headquarters. You’d be taxed according to what you bought, not where you put down stakes. The hope is this arrangement would mean more headquarters and more factories — and the jobs that come with them — would stay in America. Which of course is a desirable goal. But I don’t think this is the best way to do it. Consider this: it’s estimated that this one change alone would produce an estimated $100 billion a year in additional revenue. That’s a lot of money, and someone has to pay it. That someone is going to be working Arkansans and Americans who’ve been struggling for decades. A tax on imports is a tax on things working people buy every day — and I’m not talking about caviar and champagne. I’m talking about T-shirts, jeans, shoes, baby clothes, toys, groceries. I’ve heard from thousands of Arkansans who are already struggling just to get by. Why would we make the goods they buy at Walmart more expensive? Its supporters say this tax won’t increase the cost of imports. They argue exports will be cheaper because we’ll no longer be taxing them. They say more people overseas will buy them, meaning they’ll need more dollars with which to purchase them, which will ultimately raise the value of the dollar. According to their logic, imports won’t be more expensive because you’ll be able to buy more of them with a stronger dollar. Even with the new tax added on, you’ll still come out right where you were before. POLICING THE USA: A look at race, justice, media But this is a theory wrapped in a speculation inside a guess. Nobody knows for sure what will happen because currency markets fluctuate daily based on millions of decisions and events. Just because an economist slaps an equation on a blackboard doesn’t make it real. I’m concerned that these predictions simply won’t pan out. If we increase the costs of goods, people obviously won’t be able to buy as much, which will hurt retail sales — and retail jobs too. The retail industry is the largest employer in the U.S., and its impact is broad. In Arkansas, for instance, retail supports one in every five jobs. Are we really going to impose a huge tax on the livelihoods of so many and say, “Oh, don’t worry; it’ll all work out in the end”? That’s not a gamble many Arkansans I know would take. That’s why we have to take a hard look at this proposal now. I support fundamental tax reform and will reserve judgment on any final bill until I read it. But we must take a long, hard look at any plan that includes a border-adjustment tax. A bill that raises working families’ taxes and increases the price of the things they buy every day is hardly the way to create opportunity for all Americans. Sen. Tom Cotton, R-Ark., is a member of the Senate Armed Services Committee. You can read diverse opinions from our Board of Contributors and other writers on the Opinion front page, on Twitter @USATOpinion and in our daily Opinion newsletter. To respond to a column, submit a comment to letters@usatoday.com. Read or Share this story: http://usat.ly/2nwMBQY

President-elect Donald Trump has expressed reservations about a central component of the House Republicans' corporate tax plan. This proposal, which was put forth as an alternative to his own ideas on import tariffs, now highlights another point of divergence between the president-elect and his Republican colleagues in Congress.

The specific provision causing concern is known as border adjustment. Its aim is to tax goods brought into the country while exempting goods sent out. This mechanism is part of a broader effort to encourage businesses to create jobs and manufacturing facilities within the United States.

In his first public comments on the matter, Mr. Trump stated that he found the border adjustment to be "too complicated."

House Republicans are pushing a significant legislative idea: a border adjustment tax. While President Trump initially had doubts, he seems to be rethinking his position. This policy could be a key part of his upcoming speech to Congress. If it becomes law, it would mark a major change with big effects for American shoppers. This complex subject needs a closer look.

**What Exactly is a Border Adjustment Tax?**

Simply put, a border adjustment tax would give tax breaks to U.S. companies that export goods. At the same time, it would reduce tax benefits for companies that import products into the country.

**What's the Goal?**

The main aim, as President Trump might say, is to "bring jobs back." This tax is meant to encourage more manufacturing in the U.S. It would remove the financial incentive for companies to move production overseas just to save on taxes. The idea is to make exporting cheaper and importing more costly.

**The Dollar's Big Role**

Whether this policy works as planned depends heavily on the U.S. dollar strengthening significantly. For the border adjustment tax to achieve its goals, the dollar would need to rise by about 20% quickly. This is a big risk. Even small daily changes of 1% in the dollar's value are seen as major market shifts.

The economic theory behind this suggests that encouraging domestic manufacturing would boost demand for U.S.-made goods, thus increasing the dollar's value. If the dollar did indeed climb by 20%, the financial upsides and downsides for importers and exporters would theoretically cancel out, returning them to where they were before the policy. However, companies would still be less likely to move jobs abroad for tax reasons.

On the other hand, if the dollar doesn't appreciate by the predicted 20%, studies, like one from the New York Federal Reserve, indicate that consumers would face higher prices. The National Retail Federation, a strong opponent of the plan, predicts a notable increase in the cost of everyday items.

**Why Supporters Back It**

Supporters believe the dollar will indeed rise by 20%, leading to more U.S. production. They argue that a stronger dollar would give American companies that import goods more buying power, preventing price increases for shoppers. For companies that export, the policy would lower the costs of making and shipping products for sale overseas.

**President Trump's Changing Views**

President Trump's stance on the border adjustment tax has clearly evolved. He first expressed doubt, saying in January, "Anytime I hear border adjustment, I don't love it." But more recently, he has hinted at a change, telling Reuters, "It could lead to a lot more jobs in the United States." While not a full endorsement, this is a significant shift from his pre-presidency views. It's worth noting that Trump has also suggested other actions, like a 20% tax on goods from Mexico and threats of tariffs on China. The border adjustment tax is seen as a less confrontational alternative.

**Criticisms and Worries**

Opponents, especially in the retail industry, are very concerned. Economists at the Federal Reserve share these worries, forecasting significant price jumps for essential goods like clothes, electronics, and phones. The National Retail Federation estimates that prices could go up by 15%, costing the average American family an extra $1,700 each year.

Many economists doubt the border adjustment tax will work. They question if the dollar will strengthen by the needed 20% or do so fast enough. The dollar's value is affected by many things, making its future hard to predict. This complexity is often compared to a difficult golf shot, where success depends on skill and unpredictable factors like wind. Similarly, the dollar's value is influenced by monetary policy (like Federal Reserve interest rate changes), commodity prices, and the overall health of the U.S. economy, not just tax policy.

Additionally, there's concern that a 20% stronger dollar could make American exports too expensive for international buyers. This worry is highlighted by the fact that the U.S. manufacturing sector faced a downturn in 2015, partly due to a rapid rise in the dollar's value.

Finally, the international legality of a border adjustment tax is unclear. If the World Trade Organization (WTO), which the U.S. belongs to, ruled against the policy, other member countries could take legal action against the United States.

query: What is the main premise of Pokémon Go and how does it integrate augmented reality with the real world?

documents.text:
Boon Sheridan, a Holyoke, Massachusetts resident, found his home unexpectedly turned into a virtual hotspot. This happened after he started playing Pokémon Go, a popular augmented-reality game. The game prompts players to explore real places to find and catch digital creatures. Players can also meet up at specific spots, like public art or well-known buildings, for battles at virtual "gyms."

Sheridan's house is a converted 19th-century church. At first, he didn't pay much attention to the game, having only caught one creature. But the next morning, he saw a gym seemingly placed right over his house. "And I thought, that can’t be right," Sheridan recalled. Soon after, he noticed strangers gathering on his sidewalk, all looking at his house on their phones. His former church had indeed become a virtual gym in the game, drawing about 40 visitors throughout the day without his knowledge or permission.

This situation highlights the complex link between augmented reality and the real world, especially concerning the game's location data. Pokémon Go overlays a game world onto real maps, using streets and landmarks. However, the exact origin of this mapping data isn't entirely clear.

The game shares traits with Ingress, another augmented-reality game from Niantic Labs, the same company behind Pokémon Go. In Ingress, players helped gather information on local points of interest. Some of this data seems to have been used in Pokémon Go, with Ingress "Portals" becoming "Pokéstops." It's not confirmed if Ingress provided all the data for Pokémon Go.

Additionally, the source of the game's basic street map isn't explicitly stated. Mapping data is valuable. Since Niantic came from Alphabet (formerly Google), it suggests a possible use of Google's mapping data. However, Pokémon Go's legal documents don't name any mapping sources, which is unusual as digital map services usually require credit. Another idea is the use of the U.S. government's TIGER map, though its limitations might make it less suitable for the game.

Niantic representatives have been open to discussion but haven't revealed the definitive source of Pokémon Go's data. Sheridan's experience isn't isolated. After he shared his story online, other homeowners reported similar issues with their former churches becoming virtual gyms. These cases show the unexpected ways augmented reality can affect daily life and raise questions about data sources and privacy. The blending of virtual and physical spaces, as seen with Pokémon Go, requires a better understanding of the underlying data and its impact on people. The game's popularity has brought these issues to light, sparking conversations about responsible augmented reality development. As these technologies evolve, the lines between digital and physical will likely continue to blur, presenting both opportunities and challenges.

Pokémon Go has rapidly ascended to become a global mobile gaming phenomenon, captivating players worldwide shortly after its July 6th U.S. debut. This groundbreaking augmented-reality application leverages a smartphone's GPS and camera, enabling users to discover, capture, and engage in battles with digital creatures within their actual surroundings. The game's immense popularity has already placed a strain on Nintendo's servers and significantly propelled the Japanese game maker's stock value.

The game's swift ascent is marked by several key milestones. Within its inaugural week, Pokémon Go claimed the top download spot in Apple's App Store, as reported by App Annie. By Sunday morning, Nintendo's market capitalization had surged by an impressive $7 billion, a dramatic increase that commenced Friday evening following the game's U.S. launch on Wednesday, according to Bloomberg.

User engagement with Pokémon Go has escalated at an unprecedented pace, transforming it into a cultural touchstone. Players are actively pursuing Pokémon and congregating at real-world locations, designated as "gyms," for virtual confrontations. Data from SimilarWeb indicates that by Sunday, over 60 percent of Android users who downloaded the game were actively playing it daily, a level of engagement that could rival Twitter's active Android user base.

As Pokémon Go downloads continue their upward trajectory, a series of unusual and sometimes concerning incidents have emerged. These include controversial locations like the Westboro Baptist Church and Trump Tower being identified as "gyms," drawing players to these sites. Disturbingly, a 19-year-old in Wyoming discovered a dead body while searching for Pokémon near a river. Furthermore, CNN reported that armed robbers allegedly exploited the app to lure unsuspecting players into isolated areas.

While the long-term success and player retention of Pokémon Go remain subjects of speculation, its immediate impact is undeniable. The game is a colossal hit, prompting investors to ponder whether this extraordinary performance will translate into sustained revenue for Nintendo. The company had previously faced considerable challenges, with its stock plummeting nearly 50 percent in the year preceding Pokémon Go's release, largely attributed to sluggish Wii U console sales.

Nintendo operates within a fiercely competitive market, contending with rivals such as Microsoft's Xbox and Sony's PlayStation, alongside the escalating allure of mobile applications that have diverted a new generation of gamers from traditional consoles. However, Nintendo's strategic foray into mobile gaming with Pokémon Go could prove highly advantageous. Despite being free to download, the game incorporates in-app purchases, a monetization strategy that investors hope will yield millions in consistent, daily revenue for the company.

GOOCHLAND, Va. (AP) — Searching for Pokemon on a popular new smartphone game is not a valid excuse for trespassing. That's the warning from a central Virginia sheriff's office. In a post on its official Facebook page, the Goochland Sheriff's Office links a rise in reports of trespassing and suspicious activity over the weekend to Thursday's release of the popular Pokemon Go game. The "augmented reality" game encourages players to wander in the physical world to find and catch new Pokemon on their screens. Deputies say they have found people on business, church, and government properties late at night when the grounds are closed. Authorities say those actions constitute trespassing and put both members of the public and law enforcement officers at risk.

Pokémon Go, an augmented reality game, rapidly achieved global recognition. Within just two days of its July 8th debut in the U.S., Australia, and New Zealand, it had been installed on over 5% of Android devices in the United States. This adoption rate surpassed that of Tinder, which was on just over 2% of Android devices, according to Similarweb. The game's daily active users also neared, and likely exceeded, Twitter's user count.

Developed by Niantic and distributed by Nintendo's Pokémon division, Pokémon Go utilizes augmented reality to integrate virtual creatures into the real world, fostering an immersive experience. Players can engage with others nearby, and participate in battles, training, and trading of their Pokémon within this blended digital and physical environment. The app is free to download, with optional in-app purchases available to enhance gameplay.

Similarweb's data highlights Pokémon Go's exceptional consumer engagement, with 60% of U.S. users continuing to play the app daily. On average, players dedicate over 43 minutes per day to the game, exceeding the time spent on popular applications such as WhatsApp, Instagram, and Snapchat.

This remarkable success has significantly benefited Nintendo shareholders. Following a robust opening weekend, Nintendo's stock experienced a substantial surge on Monday, closing up 24.5% on the Tokyo Stock Exchange. These gains boosted the company's market value to approximately $28 billion, an increase of nearly $11 billion since the game's launch the preceding week.

Pokémon Go's triumph marks a significant positive turn for Nintendo, a company that had previously encountered challenges with its Wii U console, disappointing software sales, and less successful mobile ventures. With its first major mobile hit, Nintendo appears poised for a resurgence. The company is also preparing for the March launch of its new hardware, codenamed NX, which will coincide with a new installment in The Legend of Zelda series. These forthcoming projects indicate a promising outlook for Nintendo in the coming year.
