He later expressed uncertainty about whether women should cease serving, stating, "something has to be happened," but offered only vague solutions.

Trump also discussed his interactions with foreign leaders, claiming his trip to Mexico was successful due to the finance minister's resignation. He praised Russian President Vladimir Putin as a superior leader to Barack Obama, citing Putin's high approval rating and characterizing their relationship as mutually beneficial.

Clinton emphasized her extensive foreign policy experience and defended her tenure as Secretary of State, acknowledging her use of a private email server as a "mistake." She asserted that there was no evidence her system was hacked or that classified information was exchanged, noting that no charges were filed. An email exchange released prior to Clinton's appearance revealed that former Secretary of State Colin Powell had advised her on bypassing State Department servers. Clinton expressed regret for her actions and her past support for the Iraq War, noting that Trump also supported the invasion but had denied it. Trump, conversely, falsely claimed to have opposed the war from its inception.

Clinton also pointed out that Trump's support for intervention in Libya mirrored her own. Trump criticized the Obama administration's foreign policy and asserted he possessed greater knowledge of the Islamic State than military generals. When pressed for his strategy, Trump declined to elaborate, stating he did not want to broadcast his plan to potential adversaries. Recent polling indicates Trump holds a lead among military and veteran voters, while Clinton has secured endorsements from national security experts who consider Trump too dangerous. Other surveys suggest voters place more trust in Clinton's approach to terrorism.

“I have a lot of experience dealing with classified material,” Hillary Clinton told NBC's Matt Lauer. | Getty Clinton, Trump on defensive in national security forum Neither candidate did much to advance their case to voters, while Democrats raged at the event's tight format. NEW YORK — Hillary Clinton spent a third of the time fending off questions about her emails. Donald Trump struggled to explain his secret plan to defeat the Islamic State. Both presidential candidates walked into Wednesday night’s national security forum seeking to prove themselves ready to serve as commander in chief. Clinton sought to showcase her superior experience, and Trump to indict her foreign policy along with President Barack Obama’s. Instead, both almost immediately found themselves on the defensive. Story Continued Below The Democratic nominee, appearing first at the “Commander-in-Chief Forum” held by NBC News and a veteran’s group, was hit with a barrage of detailed questions about her use of a private email system as secretary of state, as well as her initial support of the Iraq war — both of which she has called mistakes. Her Republican counterpart, meanwhile, faced broader queries about his qualifications for the Oval Office, his many past controversial statements about the state of the military, and even his apparent admiration for Russian President Vladimir Putin. The result was a scattered hour at the Intrepid Sea, Air, and Space Museum that set the stage for a particularly bitter lead-up to the first presidential debate in under three weeks — where the candidates will not be constrained by the guidelines put in place on Wednesday by moderator Matt Lauer that they should refrain from attacking each other. The evening began on shaky footing for Clinton almost immediately, as she carefully weighed her answer to Lauer’s opening question about the FBI’s conclusion that she was “extremely careless” in handling classified information. “I have a lot of experience dealing with classified material,” Clinton said defensively, her jaw setting. “Classified material has a header which has ’top secret,’ ’secret,’ ‘confidential.’ Nothing — and I will repeat this, and this is verified in the report by the Department of Justice, none of the emails sent or received by me had such a header,” she explained. It took Clinton nearly one-third of her allotted half-hour to work in a shot at her GOP rival, but her opening answer — that a commander-in-chief must have “absolute rock steadiness” — was also a potential dig at a man her campaign has portrayed as erratic and untested. Clinton noted that Trump, who appeared at the forum separately, supported invading Iraq, but found herself forced to answer yet again why she had voted to give President George W. Bush the green light. "I think the decision to go to war in Iraq, and I have said that my voting to give President Bush that authority was, from my perspective, my mistake,” she said, promising yet again not to commit ground troops to Iraq “ever again” and ruling out additional ground troops in Syria. “I also believe that it is imperative that we learn from the mistakes like after-action reports are supposed to do, so we must learn what led us down that path so it never happens again,” she said. Trump, appearing after Clinton, insisted to Lauer that he had opposed the war — despite telling radio host Howard Stern in 2002 that he supported it — quickly turning to criticize the current state of American foreign policy under Obama. Presented with his past statement that he knows more about ISIS than “the generals,” Trump responded that those same generals he would have to work with “have been reduced to rubble” under Obama’s leadership. And he repeated his lament that the United States failed to “take the oil” in Iraq, an idea that national security experts across the political spectrum have panned as ludicrous. Thought Lauer missed an opportunity to corner Trump on his support for the war, he did press the Republican nominee on an apparent contradiction between his declaration that he would have top generals present him with a blueprint to defeat ISIS with his longstanding intimation that he already has a secret plan to do so. “I have a substantial chance of winning, but if I win I don’t want to broadcast [the plan],” Trump explained. “The fact is, we have had the worst — and you could say the dumbest — foreign policy." Trump was perhaps most emphatic in defending his past comments about Putin, despite the fodder those remarks have provided for attacks from Clinton and her allies. “I think when he calls me brilliant I’ll take the compliment, but it’s not going to get him anywhere,” Trump told Lauer, who had just listed a roster of controversial Russian moves, including Moscow’s reported culpability for the hack of the Democratic National Committee earlier this year. Trump responded that Putin has an 82 percent approval rating in Russia: “He’s been a leader far more than our president has been a leader.” And, as part of his answer to a question posed by a father who said his daughter was concerned about rising incidents of sexual assault in the military, Trump also stood by a past tweet suggesting that allowing women to serve — rather than lax enforcement of military discipline — was to blame. If Clinton appeared defensive and cautious in answering the specific, pointed questions posed to her, Trump also stumbled with the broader queries Lauer offered him. At one point, he corrected a veteran who had lost two friends to suicide about veteran suicide statistics — when she in fact had the right number, shaking her head at his retort. His defense of Putin was politically dodgy, too — the Russian leader’s American approval rating is in the single digits, according to an NBC News survey conducted even before the DNC hack that his government is widely believed to be behind. And his insistence that the generals currently serving had “been reduced to rubble” neared crossing a line in military circles because of the implication that he would replace officers who are not political appointees. Tensions boiled over between Democrats and NBC News, as well, as they questioned Lauer's questioning: "@NBCNews it is irresponsible to allow Trump to go on a 60 second rant about a military intervention that he supported w/o clarifying," tweeted Clinton's traveling press secretary, Nick Merrill. The forum landed in the wake of new NBC News/Survey Monkey polling showing that Trump holds a substantial lead over Clinton when it comes to military and veteran voters — a crucial voting bloc in states like Virginia -- 55 percent to 36 percent overall. Even so, as the two campaigns spar over the numbers of military endorsers each has, Trump’s poll numbers in the military community are lower than either Mitt Romney's in 2012 or John McCain’s in 2008 relative to Obama’s. After a summer of Clinton bombarding Trump’s fitness for the office and a stretch in which he saw his support waver after clashing with the parents of a deceased soldier, however, this week’s pitches are not just targeted at the significant military community: They are each campaign’s latest attempt to appeal to middle-class voters concerned about national security and their own safety. While Trump has gone after Clinton aggressively over her emails, she has responded by pointing out some of his policy inconsistencies on the trail and specifically identifying his ties to Russia as particularly troubling. Prior to the event, members of the Trump team spent pieces of the morning playing defense after Clinton’s super PAC, Priorities USA Action, promoted an ad that prominently featured the candidate declaring his love for war, the latest in a series of spots from both the campaign and super PAC making the case that Trump is far too inconsistent and unpredictable to sit in the Oval Office or have control of the nuclear codes. But on Wednesday, the man who once promised to “bomb the hell out of ISIS" spit the accusation right back at his opponent. “The current strategy of toppling regimes, with no plan for what to do the day after, only produces power vacuums that are filled by terrorists,” Trump said earlier in the day at a speech in Philadelphia. “Immediately after taking office, I will ask my generals to present me with a plan within 30 days to defeat and destroy ISIS." Even as he called for a massive increase in the military budget, Trump suggested he would more judicious in using force -- upending the traditional partisan battles lines on national security. “Unlike my opponent, my foreign policy will emphasize diplomacy, not destruction. Hillary Clinton’s legacy in Iraq, Libya, and Syria has produced only turmoil and suffering,” he said in Pennsylvania, skipping over his own one-time support for intervention in some of those conflicts. “Sometimes it has seemed like there wasn’t a country in the Middle East that Hillary Clinton didn’t want to invade, intervene or topple. She is trigger-happy and unstable when it comes to war,” he said.

During a September 7th NBC News "commander-in-chief forum," presidential candidates Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump discussed critical national security and foreign policy issues. Each candidate faced a 30-minute segment of questions from moderator Matt Lauer and audience members. This review examines questionable claims made by both candidates during the event. As is our practice, we do not assign "Pinocchios" during live broadcasts.

**Hillary Clinton's Claims:**

Regarding her private email server, Clinton stated, "It was a mistake to have a personal [email] account." The core issue involved her use of a private server instead of her designated State Department account, which would have preserved emails for Freedom of Information Act requests. She asserted, "Classified material has a header which says ‘top secret,’ ‘secret,’ ‘confidential.’ Nothing — and I will repeat this, and this is verified in the report by the Department of Justice — none of the emails sent or received by me had such a header." Initially, Clinton claimed, "I did not email any classified material to anyone on my email. There is no classified material." As the State Department began retroactively classifying some emails, her statement shifted to, "I’m confident that this process will prove that I never sent nor received any email that was marked classified." She has since specified that headers indicating "top secret" and similar designations were absent. This specificity is notable because FBI Director James Comey disclosed that three emails sent to Clinton's server contained the marking "(c)" for "confidential" but lacked a formal header. The State Department indicated two of these emails were erroneously marked. It is important to note that classification markings do not definitively render information classified, nor does their absence render it unclassified. Comey further revealed that 110 emails, separate from the 2,000 retroactively classified, were found to "contain classified information at the time they were sent or received." He also stated, "there is evidence to support a conclusion that any reasonable person in Secretary Clinton’s position, or in the position of those government employees with whom she was corresponding about these matters, should have known that an unclassified system was no place for that conversation." Therefore, Clinton's focus on the presence or absence of a "header" appears to be a misdirection.

Clinton claimed, "There is no evidence my system was hacked." While the FBI did not uncover evidence of Clinton's emails being compromised, their report highlighted numerous attempts to access her private server and noted its lack of expected safeguards for sensitive communications. Furthermore, an aide to Bill Clinton, using the Clinton private server, was indeed hacked. The report also indicated that "hostile foreign actors" successfully infiltrated the email systems of close personal aides to Hillary Clinton, acquiring emails.

In response to a question about veteran suicide rates, Clinton stated, "Twenty — twenty suicides a day." This aligns with the updated, accurate statistic. Trump, however, reverted to the outdated and misleading figure of 22 veteran suicides per day. The updated statistic is derived from the most comprehensive, data-driven study on veteran suicides to date, whereas the older figure was a rough estimate based on incomplete data. The Department of Veterans Affairs, in collaboration with the Department of Defense and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, conducted a study of veteran mortality records, compiling 55 million records over 35 years. This study found that in 2014, an average of 20 veterans died by suicide daily. Of these suicides in 2014, 65 percent involved veterans aged 50 and older. The study also revealed that from 2001 to 2014, the suicide rate among veterans utilizing VA services increased by 8.8 percent, compared to a 38.6 percent increase among veterans who did not use VA services. It is crucial to provide context: veterans generally exhibit higher suicide rates than the civilian population. Clinton's presentation of the statistic lacked this essential context.

Clinton asserted, "I will not let the V.A. be privatized. And I do think there is an agenda out there, supported by my opponent, to do just that. I think that would be very disastrous for our military veterans." This is a misleading assertion. She has previously referenced proposals by Concerned Veterans for America to reform the Department of Veterans Affairs. These proposals would offer veterans the option of subsidized private care and establish a new, nonprofit government corporation to manage VA medical facilities. While these plans incorporate elements that open veteran healthcare to the private sector, the term "privatization" typically implies a complete transfer of government services to private industry, which does not accurately describe the group's proposal. Furthermore, Trump has stated he does not support such a privatization of the VA. In a July 2016 speech, he declared, "veterans should be guaranteed the right to choose their doctor and clinics, whether at a VA facility or at a private medical center." Trump's position is to provide veterans with the option of private healthcare if they reside far from a VA facility or face lengthy appointment wait times. This mirrors a provision in the 2014 Veterans Choice Act.

Clinton stated, "Here at home, for goodness’s sakes, we have to finally pass a law prohibiting people on the terrorist watch list from being able to buy a gun in the United States of America." While Democrats frequently highlight that individuals on the terror watch list can acquire firearms, the legislative proposals they have put forth do not automatically ban such purchases. Senator Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) has introduced legislation granting the attorney general the authority to determine if a suspected terrorist can purchase a firearm. Individuals subjected to a federal terrorism investigation within five years of attempting to buy a gun would be flagged in the background check system. Membership in a terrorist organization or inclusion on a watch list does not inherently prevent an individual from purchasing a gun. An additional disqualifying factor under federal or state law, such as a felony conviction or illegal immigration status, must be present.

Clinton correctly stated, "Now, my opponent was for the war in Iraq. He says he wasn’t. You can go back and look at the record. He supported it. He told Howard Stern he supported it." There is no evidence that Trump opposed the Iraq War at its outset. He reiterated his false claim during the forum.

Clinton accurately noted, "With respect to Libya, again, there’s no difference between my opponent and myself. He’s on record extensively supporting intervention in Libya, when Gadhafi was threatening to massacre his population." This is correct, with video evidence of Trump advocating for intervention.

Clinton remarked, "They are not going to get ground troops. We are not putting ground troops into Iraq ever again. And we’re not putting ground troops into Syria. We’re going to defeat ISIS without committing American ground troops." This statement is perplexing given the presence of approximately 3,000 U.S. troops in Iraq at the time, serving in advisory and training capacities. Clinton's comment drew widespread mockery on Twitter. While she appeared to be referring to combat troops, her phrasing was imprecise. Prior to this remark, Clinton seemed to acknowledge the existing troops by stating that Arab forces engaged against the Islamic State would receive support from the United States, including "special forces, as you know, they have enablers, they have surveillance, intelligence, reconnaissance help."

Clinton stated, "When I became secretary of state, we had 200,000 troops deployed in Iraq and Afghanistan." This figure is close to accurate. Troop levels in Iraq were declining when President Obama took office, while he initiated a surge in Afghanistan, increasing troop numbers. In June 2010, there were still 88,000 troops in Iraq, down from 150,000 prior to Obama's presidency. The Congressional Research Service reported an average of 186,300 troops across both theaters in fiscal year 2009.

**Donald Trump's Claims:**

Trump asserted, "I happened to hear Hillary Clinton say that I was not against the war in Iraq. I was totally against the war in Iraq. You can look at Esquire magazine from ’04. You can look at before that." This claim is difficult to reconcile with the facts. Trump did not express opposition to the Iraq War prior to the

query: What specific technology does Gillette claim Dollar Shave Club's products infringe upon in their lawsuit?

documents.text:
**Gillette Accuses Dollar Shave Club of Patent Infringement**

Procter & Gamble's (P&G) prominent razor brand, Gillette, has initiated legal proceedings against Dollar Shave Club, an online subscription service for shaving products. The lawsuit, filed in Delaware federal court, asserts that Dollar Shave Club's razors violate a **2004 Gillette patent**. This patent specifically protects a razor blade design incorporating a **"chromium-containing overcoat layer."** Gillette is pursuing financial compensation and a judicial injunction to cease the distribution of the razors in question.

While Dorco, the South Korean entity that manufactures Dollar Shave Club's razors and holds an equity stake in the company, is not named in the suit, P&G spokesperson Damon Jones explained the focus on Dollar Shave Club. Jones stated that the company was targeted because it is **"actually selling the product and bringing it to market."** He further elaborated that P&G routinely engages in **"market surveillance,"** which includes the examination of competitor offerings. This practice led P&G to identify what it perceived as patent-infringing razors within Dollar Shave Club's product line during a recent evaluation. The impetus for this testing was reportedly press coverage highlighting Dollar Shave Club's **"investing new money in product development."**

A representative for Dollar Shave Club has **declined to provide comment** regarding the litigation. The online retailer has successfully attracted substantial investment, securing nearly **$150 million in equity funding** and achieving an investor valuation of approximately **$600 million**. Dollar Shave Club achieved significant market penetration following its **2012 debut**, notably propelled by a viral marketing video featuring its CEO, Michael Dubin. The company has since cultivated a substantial customer base through a consistent strategy of **humorous online videos, television advertisements, and social media engagement**. Earlier this year, Dubin reported that the company served **over two million subscribers** who received regular razor blade deliveries. He also projected **revenue exceeding $140 million for 2015**.

In response to the evolving competitive landscape, Gillette introduced its own subscription service, **Gillette Shave Club, last year**. The brand leveraged celebrity endorsements, including that of Giants quarterback Eli Manning, to promote the service in the period leading up to Father's Day in June.

*Clarification: The patent in question pertains to a razor blade that includes an overcoat layer, not the overcoat layer itself.*

Gillette is taking legal action against Dollar Shave Club, filing a patent infringement lawsuit. This move directly addresses Dollar Shave Club's rapid growth within the U.S. razor and blade market, a sector Gillette has long dominated. In just three years, Dollar Shave Club has captured 8% of the $3 billion market, posing a significant challenge to Gillette's established position.

The lawsuit, filed by Procter & Gamble's (P&G) Gillette division in Delaware, aims to halt Dollar Shave Club's sales of razors that allegedly infringe upon one of Gillette's patents. The complaint specifically references a 2004 patent that covers two coatings designed to improve blade sharpness and longevity. These coatings are currently featured in Gillette's popular Mach 3, Venus, and Fusion product lines.

Key points of the lawsuit and competitive landscape:

*   **Alleged Infringement:** Gillette claims Dollar Shave Club's razors violate a patent for blade coatings that enhance sharpness and durability.
*   **Targeted Products:** The patent in question relates to coatings used in Gillette's Mach 3, Venus, and Fusion lines.
*   **Market Share:** Dollar Shave Club holds 8% of the $3 billion U.S. razor market, while Gillette maintains over 60% of the retail market but only 20% of the online market.
*   **Gillette's Response:** Gillette launched its own subscription service, Gillette Shave Club, to compete in the online space.
*   **Dollar Shave Club's Model:** The company offers a direct-to-consumer model with affordable razor cartridges, appealing to consumers seeking alternatives to higher shaving costs.

A P&G spokesperson, Damon Jones, stated that the company regularly monitors competitor products and had "reason to believe" Dollar Shave Club was modifying its offerings. Following an inspection of Dollar Shave Club's blades, P&G asserts it identified the alleged patent infringement. Dollar Shave Club has not provided a comment on the ongoing litigation.

This legal battle highlights Gillette's efforts to adapt to online competitors. Despite its long history, the company faces challenges in the digital realm. In response to its lower online market share, Gillette introduced its own subscription service, Gillette Shave Club, last year. While P&G has not disclosed subscriber numbers for this service, it reported a four-percentage-point increase in its e-commerce share for blades and razors since the service's relaunch in June.

P&G has a history of using legal strategies to address competitive threats. For instance, in 2014, the company sued Hello Products LLC over claims that their toothpaste was "99% natural." P&G has also defended its Crest Whitestrips product against a private-label manufacturer. "We take every violation of our intellectual property seriously, and when necessary, we take legal action to defend our business as we have in the past and will continue to do in the future," Mr. Jones affirmed.

Dollar Shave Club gained significant attention in 2012 with a viral video featuring founder Michael Dubin. Its direct-to-consumer approach, offering razor cartridges for as little as $1 per month, resonated with consumers looking for more cost-effective shaving solutions. The company now reports 10 million customers purchasing blades at least every other month, with prices ranging from $1 to $9. Mr. Dubin projected $140 million in sales for the current year, including other personal care items. Dollar Shave Club has secured over $90 million in venture funding, with its latest valuation at $630 million, a notable contrast to P&G's market value of $220 billion.

Gillette's subscription service differs from Dollar Shave Club's recommendation of weekly blade changes. P&G estimates that its premium razor blades can cost men approximately $5 per month, considering blade durability and an assumed shaving frequency of three to four times per week. The lawsuit alleges that Dollar Shave Club's razors infringe on Gillette's patent through the use of chromium to adhere an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene on their blades. Dollar Shave Club sources its blades from Dorco, a U.S. division of a South Korean manufacturer that also produces private-label razors for retailers such as Walgreens and Dollar General Corp. Gillette's lawsuit does not include Dorco as a defendant.
