Here's a revised version of the open letter, aiming to meet your quality targets:

**An Open Letter From The Thousands Of Men And Women At Gillette**

Trust in our word is as important as trust in our product – they go hand in hand. Recently, there's been a lot of discussion about Gillette: our claims, our technology, our pricing, and our marketing. Essentially, people are talking about us, the individuals who work here, and what we stand for. We want to speak to you directly about a few things that are incredibly important to us.

First and foremost, **shaving is our passion**. We don't just mean the daily act of shaving; we mean shaving as a **science**. For us, **every single detail matters**. We conduct over 60 precise quality checks, from the raw materials to the final cartridge. Each one **must pass every time**, or it will never leave our facilities. We are confident that **no other razor manufacturer globally adheres to this rigorous standard**. Our team works tirelessly every day to ensure our razors are **superior to any other product available**. Our goal is to provide you with the **absolute best shave possible**, consistently exceeding your expectations. Why is this crucial? Because we want you to start every day with the **confidence that you're using the optimal razor for the job**, without needing to worry about the correct shaving technique or experiencing irritation from inferior razors that are merely rebranded with a fancy name and website.

Secondly, we understand that being the best isn't always enough, and you've told us that **our blades can be too expensive**. We have heard you. **Effective April 1st, the majority of Gillette blade refills in the United States will be available at lower prices** at most retailers, both online and in-store. This is a **genuine price reduction**, not a temporary promotion. You will receive the **exact same high-quality products** you've come to expect – with **zero compromise on quality, period**. Please note that actual retail prices are determined solely by individual retailers, as is standard in the industry. You can find an overview of our products and pricing on our website.

Finally, we are thrilled that **over 200,000 of you have returned to Gillette** in just the past few weeks. Many of you have explored other products, and we are proud that you have come back to the **Gillette quality you know and trust**. It's a significant fact that between November 2013 and October 2015, **more than half of individuals who tried a competitor's product did not make a repeat purchase**. This trend was evident even before we launched our "Welcome Back" offer last year. While some competitors may not welcome this news, ultimately, **the quality of the shave should be the deciding factor**. We are a company with a legacy of over 115 years, built on the core values of **quality, innovation, and trust**. These are principles we have no intention of changing.

Thank you for entrusting us with your faces, every single day.

– The Faces Behind the Blades, the Men and Women of Gillette

The invention relates to improvements to razors and razor blades. A razor blade is typically formed of a suitable substrate material such as stainless steel, and a cutting edge is formed with a wedge-shaped configuration with an ultimate tip having a radius less than about 1000 angstroms, e.g., about 200-300 angstroms. Hard coatings such as diamond, amorphous diamond, diamond-like carbon-(DLC) material, nitrides, carbides, oxides or ceramics are often used to improve strength, corrosion resistance and shaving ability, maintaining needed strength while permitting thinner edges with lower cutting forces to be used. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) outer layer can be used to provide friction reduction. Interlayers of niobium or chromium containing materials can aid in improving the binding between the substrate, typically stainless steel, and hard carbon coatings, such as DLC. Examples of razor blade cutting edge structures and processes of manufacture are described in U.S. Pat. Nos. 5,295,305; 5,232,568; 4,933,058; 5,032,243; 5,497,550; 5,940,975; 5,669,144; EP 0591339; and PCT 92/03330, which are hereby incorporated by reference. In use, the ultimate tip of the edges having hard coatings and polytetrafluoroethylene outer layers can become more rounded after repeated shaves such that there is an increase in the tip radius and a generally perceived decrease in shaving performance. SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION In one aspect, the invention features, in general, a razor blade including a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating on the overcoat layer. In another aspect the invention features, in general, a shaving razor including a handle and a razor head with a blade having a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating on the overcoat layer. Particular embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following features. In particular embodiments, the hard coating material can be made of carbon containing materials (e.g., diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC), nitrides, carbides, oxides or other ceramics. The hard coating layer can have a thickness less than 2,000 angstroms. The overcoat layer can be made of chromium or a chromium containing alloy compatible with polytetrafluoroethylene such as a chromium platinum alloy. The overcoat layer can be between 100 and 500 angstroms thick. The blade can include an interlayer between the substrate and the layer of hard coating. The interlayer can include niobium or a chromium containing material. The polytetrafluoroethylene can be Krytox LW1200 available from DuPont. The PTFE outer layer can be between 100 and 5000 angstroms thick. In another aspect, the invention features, in general, making a razor blade by providing a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, adding a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, adding an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and adding an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating over the overcoat layer. Particular embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following features. In particular embodiments the layers can be added by physical vapor deposition (i.e., sputtering) or by chemical vapor deposition. The chromium containing layer, preferably chromium, can be sputter deposited under conditions that result in a compressively stressed coating. The sputter deposition of chromium containing materials can include applying a DC bias to the target that is more negative than −50 volts, preferably more negative than −200 volts. Alternatively an appropriate RF bias scheme can be used to achieve an equivalent chromium layer. Embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following advantages. The use of a chromium containing overcoat layer provides improved adhesion of the polytetrafluorethylene outer layer to the hard coating layer. The razor blade has improved edge strength provided by hard coating and has reduced tip rounding with repeated shaves. Reduced tip rounding minimizes the increase in cutting force thereby maintaining excellent shaving performance. The razor blade has excellent shaving characteristics from the first shave onwards. Other features and advantages of the invention will be apparent from the following description of a particular embodiment and from the claims. DESCRIPTION OF THE DRAWINGS FIG. 1 is a vertical sectional view of a cutting edge portion of a razor blade. FIG. 2 is a perspective view of a shaving razor including the FIG. 1 razor blade. DESCRIPTION OF A PARTICULAR EMBODIMENT Referring to FIG. 1, there is shown razor blade 10 including substrate 12, interlayer 14, hard coating layer 16, overcoat layer 18, and outer layer 20. The substrate 12 is typically made of stainless steel (though other substrates can be employed) and has an ultimate edge sharpened to a tip radius of less than 1,000 angstroms, preferably 200 to 300 angstroms, and has a profile with side facets 22 at an included angle of between 15 and 30 degrees, preferably about 19 degrees, measured at 40 microns from the tip. Interlayer 14 is used to facilitate bonding of the hard coating layer to the substrate. Examples of suitable interlayer material are niobium and chromium containing material. A particular interlayer is made of niobium greater than 100 angstroms and preferably less than 500 angstroms thick. PCT 92/03330 describes use of a niobium interlayer. Hard coating layer 16 provides improved strength, corrosion resistance and shaving ability and can be made from carbon containing materials (e.g., diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC), nitrides (e.g., boron nitride, niobium nitride or titanium nitride), carbides (e.g., silicon carbide), oxides (e.g., alumina, zirconia) or other ceramic materials. The carbon containing materials can be doped with other elements, such as tungsten, titanium or chromium by including these additives, for example in the target during application by sputtering. The materials can also incorporate hydrogen, e.g., hydrogenated DLC. Preferably coating layer 16 is made of diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC. A particular embodiment includes DLC less than 2,000 angstroms, preferably less than 1,000 angstroms. DLC layers and methods of deposition are described in U.S. Pat. No. 5,232,568. As described in the “Handbook of Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) Processing,” DLC is an amorphous carbon material that exhibits many of the desirable properties of diamond but does not have the crystalline structure of diamond. Overcoat layer 18 is used to reduce the tip rounding of the hard coated edge and to facilitate bonding of the outer layer to the hard coating while still maintaining the benefits of both. Overcoat layer 18 is preferably made of chromium containing material, e.g., chromium or chromium alloys that are compatible with polytetrafluoroethylene, e.g., CrPt. A particular overcoat layer is chromium about 100-200 angstroms thick. Blade 10 has a cutting edge that has less rounding with repeated shaves than it would have without the overcoat layer. Outer layer 20 is used to provide reduced friction and includes polytetrafluoroethylene and is sometimes referred to as a telomer. A particular polytetrafluoroethylene material is Krytox LW 1200 available from DuPont. This material is a nonflammable and stable dry lubricant that consists of small particles that yield stable dispersions. It is furnished as an aqueous dispersion of 20% solids by weight and can be applied by dipping, spraying, or brushing, and can thereafter be air dried or melt coated. The layer is preferably less than 5,000 angstroms and could typically be 1,500 angstroms to 4,000 angstroms, and can be as thin as 100 angstroms, provided that a continuous coating is maintained. Provided that a continuous coating is achieved, reduced telomer coating thickness can provide improved first shave results. U.S. Pat. Nos. 5,263,256 and 5,985,459, which are hereby incorporated by reference, describe techniques which can be used to reduce the thickness of an applied telomer layer. Razor blade 10 is made generally according to the processes described in the above referenced patents. A particular embodiment includes a niobium interlayer 14, DLC hard coating layer 16, chromium overcoat layer 18, and Krytox LW1200 polytetrafluoroethylene outer coat layer 20. Chromium overcoat layer 18 is deposited to a minimum of 100 angstroms and a maximum of 500 angstroms. It is deposited by sputtering using a DC bias (more negative than −50 volts and preferably more negative than −200 volts) and pressure of about 2 millitorr argon. The increased negative bias is believed to promote a compressive stress (as opposed to a tensile stress), in the chromium overcoat layer which is believed to promote improved resistance to tip rounding while maintaining good shaving performance. Blade 10 preferably has a tip radius of about 200-400 angstroms, measured by SEM after application of overcoat layer 18 and before adding outer layer 20. Referring to FIG. 2, blade 10 can be used in shaving razor 110, which includes handle 112 and replaceable shaving cartridge 114. Cartridge 14 includes housing 116, which carries three blades 10, guard 120 and cap 122. Blades 10 are movably mounted, as described, e.g., in U.S. Pat. No. 5,918,369, which is incorporated by reference. Cartridge 114 also includes an interconnect member on which housing 116 is pivotally mounted at two arms 128. The interconnect member includes a base 127 which is replaceably connected to handle 112. Alternatively, blade 10 can be used in other razors having one, two or more than three blades, double-sided blades, and razors that do not have movable blades or pivoting heads where the cartridge is either replaceable or permanently attached to a razor handle. In use, razor blade 10 has excellent shaving characteristics from the first shave onwards. Blade 10 has improved edge strength provided by hard coating and has reduced tip rounding with repeated shaves provided by the overlayer coating while maintaining excellent shave characteristics. Other embodiments of the invention are within the scope of the appended claims.

Gillette, a Procter & Gamble brand, is suing Dollar Shave Club for patent infringement. The lawsuit, filed in Delaware, claims Dollar Shave Club is using Gillette's patented blade coating technology without permission.

This special coating is key to how well Gillette's top razors work, like the Mach3, Venus, and Fusion lines. Gillette, based in Boston, insists it alone owns the patent rights involved in this case.

Dollar Shave Club, located in Venice, California, has received the lawsuit. The company is currently looking into the claims. A Dollar Shave Club representative said they have no further comments on the matter right now.

The lawsuit points to specific Dollar Shave Club razors, such as The Humble Twin, The 4X, and The Executive, as products that allegedly break Gillette's patent rights.

Dollar Shave Club (DSC) has claimed the No. 2 spot in razor cartridge volume sales, surpassing Schick and positioning itself as a significant challenger to established industry giants. This milestone, achieved just over three years after its market debut, is supported by internal data and generally aligns with available third-party market analysis. If confirmed, DSC would trail only Procter & Gamble Co.'s Gillette brand in overall dollar sales and also Schick in that specific metric.

The company's rapid ascent is largely fueled by aggressive marketing strategies and substantial subscriber growth. DSC has seen its subscriber base double to 2.2 million in under ten months, a surge directly linked to a major national television advertising campaign launched in November. Founder and CEO Michael Dubin stated that DSC has been the most significant television advertising spender in the razor category since November, even outspending Gillette. This aggressive push is further bolstered by a $75 million venture capital round closed in June, intended to sustain its television ad initiatives, following a $50 million round nine months prior.

"TV has been a winner for us and a space we're going to stay in," Dubin emphasized, highlighting the brand's commitment to the medium. A new advertising campaign rolling out this month will focus on the distinct advantages of using fresh razor blades. Dubin remarked, "It feels great to shave with a fresh blade. And we feel there are millions more guys out there that have that perception and would enjoy that experience. This campaign is about finding those guys."

The razor cartridge market is the largest and most profitable segment within the shaving industry, representing a high-margin category in packaged goods. Dollar Shave Club reports a 16% volume share of the cartridge market for July, based on its internal sales data. This figure is closely monitored against Nielsen's annual scanner data, which shows Schick with a 15% volume share. However, Nielsen data compiled by Deutsche Bank suggests Schick's cartridge volume share for the four weeks ending August 8th was approximately one point higher than its annual share, potentially placing the two brands in a near tie.

Chris Gough, VP-investor relations at Edgewell, Schick's parent company, declined to comment on Dubin's share claim, citing Dollar Shave Club's private status, lack of publicly audited financials, and operation within an online marketplace with limited third-party sales data. He also noted that Nielsen's coverage does not encompass the entire shaving market.

Conversely, third-party data from Slice Intelligence, which analyzes online purchase receipts from a panel of 2 million consumers, indicates that Dollar Shave Club is either already the No. 2 U.S. razor cartridge brand or will be imminently, even by dollar sales. Slice data corroborates Dollar Shave Club's impressive growth trajectory, showing online sales of $16.3 million in July. This figure more than doubles the $7.7 million recorded in October, prior to the launch of its in-house TV campaign, and nearly triples the brand's sales rate from the previous year.

In contrast, Schick's offline unit sales declined by 6.8% year-over-year for the four weeks ending August 8th, according to Nielsen, with dollar sales falling by 5.5%. While these figures are slightly better than the full-year results, Dollar Shave Club, which focuses on more affordable blades intended for weekly replacement, still trails Schick in cartridge dollar share by a 9% to 16% margin, according to DSC's own Nielsen-aided estimates. Schick also maintains a lead in both volume and dollar sales when considering broader razor product categories, including disposables and handles.

Despite these differences, Dollar Shave Club's ascent is a notable achievement for a three-year-old company challenging an 89-year-old incumbent. If Dollar Shave Club sustains its current monthly sales pace, it is projected to achieve nearly $200 million in sales over the next twelve months, according to Slice data. Even if its current annual growth rate were halved, DSC could overtake Schick in cartridge dollar sales within a year. A hypothetical tripling of its sales, though ambitious, could see the club surpass Schick in total dollar sales. Schick reported $222 million in cartridge sales over the past year, per Nielsen, with an additional estimated $13 million from online sales according to Slice. Schick also generated $115 million in razor handle sales and $239 million in disposable razor sales, a segment where Dollar Shave Club does not compete.

The offline razor market, while larger, is experiencing a decline. Nielsen estimates offline razor cartridge sales at $1.3 billion, razor handles at $427 million, and disposable razors at $1.2 billion, totaling nearly $3 billion. While disposable razor sales saw a modest 1.8% growth over the past year, sales of blades and handles each decreased by over 4%.

Dubin's success has not gone unnoticed by competitors. Gillette launched its "Gillette Shave Club" online sales effort in June, advertising its blades as 50% cheaper than "the other shave club." Nielsen data indicates a continued dip in Gillette's offline sales and market share in recent months, with Dubin reporting no discernible impact on his business from these ads. However, Slice data does show a rebound in Gillette's online share, increasing by over 4 percentage points to 18.6% from June to July, while Dollar Shave Club's share has seen a decline of over 4 percentage points since March from a peak of nearly 54%.

Coincidentally, the new Dollar Shave Club advertisements humorously address the practice of shaving with old, dirty blades to save money. Gillette is also preparing to introduce an improved men's cartridge early next year, along with new theft-resistant packaging designed to make blades more accessible from behind locked store cases – a practice that has been a recurring theme in Dollar Shave Club's advertising.

Dollar Shave Club also faces increasing competition in the affordable blade market, potentially from unexpected sources. Dorco, the South Korean manufacturer widely believed by industry insiders to produce DSC's blades, has been more aggressively marketing its own brand online. According to Slice, Dorco captured an 8.5% share of U.S. online sales last month and also launched value-priced Swipe blades at Costco earlier this year.

The Internet Archive actively crawls the web to save digital content and the pages it links to. This ensures that even if a linked page changes or disappears, a record of its original state is preserved.

The Archive then works to weave these archived links back into the web. The goal is for these preserved links to:

*   Replace broken hyperlinks.
*   Offer additional context for users.

This helps users understand the original intent of web authors. The ultimate aim is to fix broken links across the internet. The Archive specifically targets sites supporting the "No More 404" initiative with its ongoing crawls.

query: What heroic action did Tiana Carruthers take during the Kalamazoo shooting before being shot?

documents.text: The first victim in the Kalamazoo shooting tried to protect children playing outside an apartment complex before being shot multiple times. Tiana Carruthers put herself between a group of kids and gunman Jason Dalton at the beginning of his Saturday night rampage in Michigan, when he killed six and wounded two others. JASON DALTON HAD LONG HISTORY OF DRIVING VIOLATIONS Carruthers was hit three times in the legs and arms, according toWWMT,but is expected to survive after receiving multiple surgeries. The 25-year-old and several children, including at least one of her own, were outside at the Meadows Townhomes when Dalton, a 45-year-old Uber driver, drove up right before 6 p.m. Tiana Carruthers, the first victim of the Kalamazoo shootings, reportedly put herself between gunman Jason Dalton and playing children before she was shot. (Facebook) "Have you seen Mazie?" he asked Joi Coleman, 12. As of Monday night it was unclear who Mazie is, or why Dalton asked about her before beginning the shooting. ACCUSED KALAMAZOO SHOOTER JASON DALTON CHARGED WITH MURDER Coleman said Carruthers asked him if there was anything she could do for him before he opened fire. Several children were outside the apartment complex when Dalton pulled up to them and soon began shooting. (WWMT) “He shot her once and she was still running and then he shot her again and she fell. She was asking 'Joi, help me. Joi, help me,” the young girl recounted. Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller said that the mother "sensed trouble" before sending the kids inside as quickly as possible. KALAMZOO TEEN SQUEEZED MOM'S HAND SURVIVED MASS SHOOTING Dalton had reportedly come to the apartment complex earlier in the day. Dalton, a 45-year-old Uber driver, has admitted to the shooting, though no motive is known. (AP) The gunman would go on to shoot dead Richard Smith and his son Tyler, 17, as the pair was looking at cars around 10 p.m. Shortly after he killed Mary Lou Nye, 60, Mary Joe Nye, 62, Barbara Hawthorne, 68, and Dorothy Brown, 74, at a nearby Cracker Barrel. Fourteen-year-old Abigail Kopf was also shot at the country restaurant and was fighting for her life as of Monday night. Dalton, who had been picking up fares during the night of his attacks, was arrested early Sunday morning and now faces charges of first degree murder. Six people were killed and two, including Carruthers, were injured at three separate shooting sites on Saturday night. (Mark Bugnaski/AP) Carruthers was able to pick Dalton out of a lineup and tell investigators that he left in a silver SUV, according toMLive. Dalton, a former insurance adjuster, also reportedly confessed to the attacks and was denied bail on Monday. With News Wire Services. Sign up for BREAKING NEWS Emails privacy policy Thanks for subscribing!

### Kalamazoo Shootings: A Night of Unspeakable Terror and Witness Accounts

The Kalamazoo shootings, a series of horrific events that unfolded on a Saturday night, left a community in shock and mourning. The events, attributed to Jason Dalton, began with a terrifying encounter at the Meadows Townhomes, where a twelve-year-old witness, Joi Coleman, recounted the harrowing moments. She described holding a woman's hand when a man, later identified as Jason Dalton, approached and allegedly asked, "Have you seen Mazie?" According to Coleman, as the woman offered help, Dalton immediately opened fire, shooting her twice. The young witness vividly described the unfolding horror: "He shot her once and she was still running and then he shot her again and she fell. She was asking 'Joi, help me. Joi, help me.'" Coleman expressed her overwhelming fear, stating, "I knew I was going to get shot too, he didn't stop shooting." Despite the terror, Coleman and the children she was supervising managed to reach safety inside, while the victim's daughter witnessed the event from a window. Police reported Dalton fired 10 rounds, with three bullets striking the woman, who was expected to recover after sustaining injuries to her legs and arm.

Another child present, Meghan Coleman, shared the chilling words of a young girl who ran to the window, crying, "My mom is dead! My mom is dead!" The shooter, described as "the guy," reportedly backed up and resumed shooting, narrowly missing. Joi's mother, who was caring for 11 children, stated that Dalton returned to the scene four times, with Joi recalling him pointing a silver gun directly at their house. Neighbors Travis Gettys and Devin Fletter, who live adjacent to the Colemans, reported seeing Dalton interacting with children in the neighborhood earlier that day, about an hour before the shooting. Fletter commented, "Makes me feel like he was looking for a specific person." Gettys described the surreal experience, noting, "It's such an adrenaline rush, we were just playing video games and everything happened." Joi Coleman also remembered Dalton initially observing them and other children before firing.

The violence escalated just hours after the initial shooting. Shortly after 10 p.m., 17-year-old Tyler Smith and his father, Richard Smith, were fatally shot while looking at cars at Seelye Kia on Stadium Drive. Minutes later, close friends Mary L. Nye, Mary Jo Nye, Judy Brown, and Barbara Hawthorne were killed in their vehicles at the Cracker Barrel parking lot. A 14-year-old who was also in one of the cars remained on life support at Bronson Hospital. Travis Gettys emphasized the direct link between the various incidents, stating, "At this point the connection between the three cases is the defendant. Is Mister Dalton." Gettys expressed the profound shock and grief gripping West Michigan and the nation, stating, "This is just the most awful thing I can think of having happened." When asked about her feelings on Sunday, Joi Coleman simply responded, "Scared."

On Wednesday, the Kalamazoo County Sheriff's Office released a statement regarding the victim from The Meadows Apartments. The family has requested that her name and any related information not be released, as they are not in a condition to speak with the media. They have indicated they will inform Sheriff Richard Fuller when they are ready. The Sheriff's Office urged the media and concerned parties to allow the family the necessary time and space to cope with the situation and focus on recovery. The victim's condition remains serious.

**Uber's Driver Safety Under Fire After Kalamazoo Tragedy**

The tragic mass shooting in Kalamazoo, Michigan, has placed ride-hailing giant Uber under intense scrutiny regarding its driver safety protocols. Jason Dalton, an Uber driver, faces murder charges in connection with the deaths of six people. The rapidly expanding San Francisco-based company has defended its driver screening process, asserting it could not have foreseen Dalton's alleged actions.

Uber maintains its safety procedures are robust and require no modification. However, some experts suggest the company needs more stringent vetting for potential drivers to reassure passengers. These experts acknowledge that Dalton, who had no prior criminal record, likely would not have raised red flags during a standard background check.

Dalton, who had been driving for Uber for less than a month, is accused of carrying out the shootings over a nearly five-hour period on Saturday night. The Kalamazoo County prosecutor indicated that Dalton picked up Uber passengers both before and after the initial shootings.

Key points regarding Uber's driver safety and the Kalamazoo incident include:

*   **Dalton's Background:** Uber's Chief Security Officer, Joe Sullivan, stated Dalton passed a background check and was approved as a driver on January 25th. He had completed over 100 rides with an impressive rating of 4.73 stars out of five.
*   **No Prior Indication:** Sullivan emphasized that, prior to Saturday's events, Uber had no indication of any issues with Dalton's behavior. He commented, "No background check would have flagged and anticipated this situation."
*   **Passenger Pickups:** Dalton is accused of picking up Uber passengers both before and after the initial shootings.

Despite Uber's assurances, this incident has reignited concerns about the company's security measures. Earlier this month, Uber agreed to a $28.5 million settlement to resolve lawsuits alleging deceptive practices regarding safety procedures and fees. Additionally, the company faces a separate lawsuit from district attorneys in San Francisco and Los Angeles, who claim Uber's vetting process failed to prevent the hiring of individuals with felony convictions.

Past incidents have also cast a shadow over Uber's safety record:

*   In Massachusetts, an Uber driver was sentenced to prison for raping a passenger.
*   Around the same time, a teacher working as an Uber driver in South Carolina was accused of raping a passenger.
*   In India, an Uber driver received a life sentence for raping a passenger, leading to Uber's temporary ban in New Delhi.

In response to these legal challenges, Uber has ceased using marketing phrases such as "safer than a taxi" and "industry-leading background checks." Nevertheless, the company insists that riders are protected and highlights its unique feature of allowing passengers to rate drivers. Ed Davis, a former police commissioner and member of Uber's Safety Advisory Board, stated, "The system that Uber has is extremely safe."

However, critics like Matthew Daus, a former New York City Taxi Commissioner and transportation lawyer, argue that Uber's name-based background checks are less effective than fingerprint checks that access the FBI criminal history database. Daus points out that name checks can miss records if individuals use aliases or if their names differ slightly from official records. Uber disputes this, arguing that FBI records can be misleading if charges were dropped. Sullivan stated that Uber reviews court records for up to seven years and conducts in-person checks for non-online records. Both sides agree, however, that Dalton would not have been flagged due to his lack of a criminal record. Daus also expressed concern that Uber controls the background check information, whereas fingerprint-based checks conducted by government agencies are public records.

Sullivan indicated that Uber became aware of issues with Dalton's driving on Saturday, receiving complaints about dangerous and erratic driving from several passengers. He noted that Uber typically suspends drivers accused of violent acts but usually contacts drivers first in cases of erratic driving to hear their side. It remains unclear if Uber contacted Dalton on Saturday night. Uber passenger Matt Mellen reported that he called the police to report Dalton's erratic driving over an hour before the shootings began and also attempted to report him to Uber.

Allen Adamson, founder of Brand Simple Consulting, believes that driver behavior is a critical issue Uber must address as it continues to grow. With millions of rides occurring daily worldwide, Adamson stressed the importance of demonstrating rigorous screening procedures to assure the public that drivers are safe and trustworthy. He warned that while isolated incidents may be overlooked, repeated issues could significantly damage the brand.

Despite these concerns, some customers remain confident in Uber's safety measures. Ian Leavitt, a medical research assistant, stated he will continue using Uber, believing the company is doing all it can as long as drivers are screened for criminal records or links to terrorism. He acknowledged that background checks cannot predict future behavior, especially concerning individuals with mental health issues who have not previously acted out.

Uber is under intense scrutiny following the tragic events in Kalamazoo, Michigan, where driver Jason Dalton's fatal shooting spree claimed six lives. Revelations have surfaced that a complaint detailing Dalton's "dangerous driving" was received by Uber but went unaddressed prior to the attacks. The passenger who filed this critical feedback, Matt Mellen, reported Dalton's erratic and alarming behavior, which prompted Mellen to call 911 after exiting the vehicle. Uber has confirmed that this vital information, received approximately five hours before the first victim was killed, had not been reviewed by the company.

Mellen vividly recounted his terrifying experience, describing how Dalton's driving became dangerously unpredictable after a phone call. "We were kind of driving through medians, driving through the lawn speeding along and then finally, once he came to a stop, I jumped out of the car and ran away," Mellen stated. This explicit report of dangerous driving, a clear indicator of potential risk, was promptly sent to Uber.

A spokesperson for Uber acknowledged the complaint but pointed to the immense challenge of prioritizing feedback from millions of daily rides. "Remember we’re doing 3 million rides a day. How do you prioritize that feedback and how do you think about it?" the spokesperson questioned, as reported by The New York Times. Uber further explained that complaints involving direct threats of violence, such as "the driver assaulted me," result in immediate driver suspension. This policy is in line with recommendations from the NTSB. However, feedback pertaining to general "bad driving" is handled through a different, less immediate process, according to statements made to The Wall Street Journal. The spokesperson clarified that complaints concerning erratic driving are not automatically prioritized for immediate action. This stance has drawn significant criticism from safety advocates. "Because we get a lot of people saying bad driving and there’s two sides to that and sometimes, it’s not fair to deactivate them or not give them a warning," they explained. This particular complaint, unfortunately, "fell into that back bucket." Uber's internal guidelines, reviewed by Reuters, indicate a policy of not immediately deactivating drivers for erratic driving. This is attributed to the sheer volume of such complaints and a stated commitment to avoiding unfair consequences for drivers, a point also emphasized by Uber's Chief Security Officer, Joe Sullivan.

"The reason we do that, just to be clear, is because with something like bad driving, we’re not going to take away someone’s ability to earn a living without talking to them first," the spokesperson stated, a sentiment echoed in a report by The Verge. When directly asked if Uber had reached out to Dalton after receiving the complaint, the spokesperson confirmed, "We had not gotten around to doing that, no." This lack of immediate follow-up has become a central focus of the ongoing investigation. Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller has expressed his concerns regarding this oversight. It has been confirmed that Jason Dalton had successfully passed a background check conducted by Checkr, a third-party vendor utilized by Uber. He had been an Uber driver since January 25th, completing approximately 100 rides. Joe Sullivan, Uber’s chief security officer, asserted in a statement to TechCrunch that the driver's rampage could not have been predicted. He noted Dalton's generally positive rating of 4.73, a metric that has been questioned by critics of ride-sharing safety protocols. Sheriff Richard Fuller confirmed that Uber is cooperating fully with law enforcement, including the FBI, and is expected to provide assistance in filling in timeline gaps. Investigators are particularly keen to examine any communication between Dalton and Uber, as well as with his past customers. This process may involve subpoenas for Uber's internal data, as reported by The Detroit News. The motive behind the attacks remains unclear. Preliminary findings released by the Michigan State Police indicate that the victims had no apparent connection to the gunman or to each other. The series of attacks commenced Saturday evening outside the Meadows apartment complex, where an initial shooting occurred, as detailed in the official police report. Approximately four hours later and 15 miles away, a father and his 17-year-old son were shot while at a car dealership, a location identified by the Kalamazoo Gazette. Just fifteen minutes after that incident, five individuals were shot in the parking lot of a Cracker Barrel restaurant, with four of them tragically succumbing to their injuries, a devastating conclusion to the spree. Uber's policy prohibits both passengers and drivers from possessing any type of firearm within a vehicle, a rule that has been in place since 2014, according to company statements. Individuals found to be in violation of this policy may face prohibition from using or driving for the service. However, an investigation by ProPublica suggests that this policy has been inconsistently enforced.

The Kalamazoo, Michigan, shooting spree on February 20, 2016, tragically claimed six lives and injured several others. The perpetrator, Uber driver Jason Dalton, is accused of a violent rampage that spanned hours and multiple locations. Despite his alleged confession to investigators, the motive behind his seemingly random acts of violence remains a profound mystery, deepening the pain for the victims and their families.

A particularly disturbing aspect of the case is Dalton's alleged continued work for Uber between the shootings. This has ignited serious questions regarding the ride-sharing company's awareness of his erratic behavior and their response to customer complaints. Uber has acknowledged receiving reports of unsafe driving on the day of the attacks but has not confirmed if these concerns were directly relayed to Dalton. This lack of clarity has intensified public scrutiny and added another layer of complexity to an already devastating situation, prompting broader discussions about safety protocols for ride-sharing services.

Jason Dalton, a 45-year-old former insurance adjuster, appeared in court via video link. He faces six murder charges, each carrying a mandatory life sentence. Bail was denied, reflecting the severity of the alleged crimes. Dalton's reported admission, combined with an elusive motive, presents a significant challenge for investigators. Kalamazoo County Prosecutor Jeff Getting highlighted the immense impact: "We've lost six people, we have a 14-year-old girl clinging to life, we have another victim who is recovering, thankfully, ... we have a high school that's lost a classmate. And no one understands why it happened." This bewilderment is deeply felt throughout Kalamazoo.

The day's events began with early erratic driving. Passenger Matt Mellen reported that Dalton, after a phone call, drove recklessly, disregarding stop signs, sideswiping vehicles, and speeding through medians and lawns. Fearing for his safety, Mellen exited the moving vehicle and contacted the police. His fiancée also shared a warning on Facebook. Dalton allegedly continued to drive for Uber between the shootings, raising concerns about the company's oversight. Uber is reportedly cooperating with investigators, expected to provide crucial information and communication records. Dalton had passed a background check and completed approximately 100 rides prior to the attacks.

The confirmed shooting incidents occurred in sequence:

*   **Meadows Apartment Complex:** Tiana Carruthers, 25, survived multiple gunshot wounds. She reportedly shielded children she was supervising, sensing danger. Prosecutor Getting indicated Dalton used a different name to "get her attention," suggesting premeditation.
*   **Car Dealership:** Roughly four hours later and 15 miles away, a father and his 17-year-old son were fatally shot while looking at vehicles.
*   **Cracker Barrel Restaurant:** Fifteen minutes after the dealership incident, a mass shooting in the parking lot resulted in four deaths. Initially reported among the deceased was 14-year-old Abigail Kopf, who sustained a severe head wound. She later showed signs of life and remained in critical condition.

The question of motive and Dalton's mental state remains the most perplexing element. Sheriff Fuller articulated the community's struggle: "In the end, I ask people, because I keep hearing this question of why, 'What would be the answer that would be an acceptable answer for you?' They have to think about it for a moment, and they say, 'Probably nothing.' I have to say, 'You are probably correct.' I can't imagine what the answer would be that would let us go, 'OK, we understand now.'" This sentiment encapsulates the profound bewilderment and the absence of any rational explanation for these horrific acts. The investigation continues, piecing together the events in the hope of offering some answers to a community shattered by inexplicable violence.

This report was compiled with contributions from Associated Press writers Tom Krisher in Saline, Michigan; Dee-Ann Durbin in Ann Arbor, Michigan; Ed White in Detroit; and Don Babwin in Chicago.

### Passenger Recounts Terrifying Encounter with Suspected Kalamazoo Gunman

Hours before the alleged shooting spree that rocked Kalamazoo County, a local man, Matt Mellen, found himself in a terrifying situation as a passenger in the car of suspected gunman Jason Dalton. Dalton, who was reportedly working as an Uber driver before, during, and after the alleged attacks, picked up Mellen on Saturday night. Mellen recounts that from the moment he entered the vehicle, he sensed something was amiss.

Dalton, who introduced himself as "Me-Me" instead of his Uber-listed name, had his dog in the back seat. The ride quickly escalated from unsettling to dangerous as Dalton began driving erratically, reaching speeds of 80 miles per hour down West Main Avenue and sideswiping other vehicles. Mellen describes Dalton driving through medians and lawns, running stop signs, and exhibiting increasingly alarming behavior. "We got a mile from my house and then he got a telephone call and after he got that telephone call he started driving really erratically," Mellen stated. "We were running stop signs."

Fearing for his life, Mellen managed to escape the vehicle when Dalton eventually stopped. He immediately called the police and later informed his fiancée about the harrowing experience. His fiancée then took to Facebook, posting a warning with Dalton's picture to alert friends who might be using Uber that night. Mellen believes his encounter occurred around 4:30 PM, significantly before the first reported shooting by police, which they estimate took place around 6:00 PM. "I'm upset because I tried contacting Uber after I had talked to the police, saying that we needed to get this guy off the road," Mellen expressed.

Tragically, Mellen's warning may not have reached everyone in time. Another Newschannel 3 viewer reported being scheduled for a pickup by Dalton at 11:19 PM, by which time six people had already been shot. Mark Dunton, Scott Dixon, and Kevin Leonard also recounted their unnerving experience of having Dalton pull up to pick them up for an Uber ride on Saturday night. Having heard about the shooting spree and the suspect's vehicle, they were understandably apprehensive when a car matching the description arrived. Dunton recalled a friend questioning Dalton, "this isn't the HHR, you aren't the guy are ya?" to which Dalton reportedly denied it. "It's crazy to think that someone could have gone out and just done all these horrible things and came to work straight-faced -- like he's just coming to work doing his job and just didn't say much at all," Dunton commented. An Indiana couple also shared their story of requesting an Uber that Saturday night and ending up in Dalton's car, even making a lighthearted, yet now chilling, joke to him, "'You're not the shooter are you?'"

Uber released a statement expressing their horror and heartbreak over the "senseless violence" and offered their full cooperation with the police investigation. Dalton was apprehended by police in downtown Kalamazoo around 12:30 AM, shortly after reportedly driving three more passengers to a downtown bar. Mellen emphasized the need for a more rapid response system when Uber drivers exhibit dangerous behavior.

**Abigail Kopf's Fight for Life After Kalamazoo Shootings: Parents Share Update**

Fourteen-year-old Abigail Kopf, a resident of Battle Creek, remains in critical condition following the devastating random shooting attacks that occurred in Kalamazoo. Her parents, Gene and Vicki Kopf, spoke with media on Monday night, sharing poignant details about their daughter's harrowing brush with death and the encouraging progress she has demonstrated since the incident.

The Kopf family, alongside Bronson Children's Hospital Medical Director Dr. Aaron Lane-Davies, held a press conference at Bronson Children's Hospital in downtown Kalamazoo. They have been steadfastly by Abigail's side since the weekend's tragic events. Dr. Lane-Davies confirmed that Abigail is currently on a ventilator and remains in critical condition. While her heart did stop, and the organ and tissue donation program, Gift of Life, was contacted, he emphasized that Abigail was never medically declared brain-dead, despite some media reports to the contrary.

Vicki Kopf shared that Abigail, described as a strong-willed, athletic young girl with a passion for theater and wolves, is actively fighting for her life. "My daughter is not dead," Vicki stated emphatically. "She is alive and she is fighting for her life. I want everybody to understand that. Abigail is strong and she is a vibrant, beautiful, young lady. And she did not deserve this. And neither did her grandmother or those other victims."

Abigail has shown signs of responsiveness, including squeezing hands and attempting to move her arms, as well as wiggling her toes. Dr. Lane-Davies noted, "In spite of the seriousness of injuries, we are encouraged by her relative stability over the last several days and her responsiveness to her parents and our team."

Abigail is the sole survivor of the group shot while sitting in a car at the Cracker Barrel restaurant in Texas Township. Tragically, three other occupants—Mary Jo Nye, 60; Barbara Hawthorne, 68; and Judy Brown, 74, all from Battle Creek—lost their lives. Michigan State Police Lt. Dale Hinz confirmed Abigail was shot once in the head.

Vicki Kopf spoke about the deep connection their family shared with the victims, particularly Barbara Hawthorne, whom Abigail affectionately called "Grandma Barb." Hawthorne, though not a blood relative, was a cherished grandmother figure who had been involved in Abigail's life since she was a baby and shared a love for musicals. "She is not of blood relation, she is just very close to the family," Vicki explained. "She was with us when Abigail was born and she is basically her adopted grandmother. She took both my girls under her wing and has basically raised them both with us."

The series of random shootings on Saturday night in Kalamazoo County resulted in six fatalities and two injuries. Authorities identified Jason Dalton, 45, as the perpetrator. The attack on Abigail and the women at the Cracker Barrel was the final incident in a spree that also included a shooting at an apartment complex and two fatalities at a car dealership. Dalton was reportedly working as an Uber driver at the time of the attacks.

In a statement read by Gene Kopf, the family expressed their condolences to Dalton's family: "Our thoughts are with the Dalton family who also are suffering at this time. We want his family to know that we don't hold anything against the family members."

Dalton was arraigned on Monday, facing six counts of open murder, two counts of assault with intent to murder, and eight counts of felony firearm. He reportedly admitted to taking lives during a preceding probable-cause hearing.

The Kopfs reiterated their plea for privacy, noting that Abigail's 6-year-old sister is still unaware of the full extent of what happened. "We also know Abigail is going to have a very tough road to recovery," Gene Kopf stated. "Which is another reason we didn't want her to be under scrutiny or limelight when she is recovering. It's tough enough being a 14-year-old girl and everything is going well — adding that is hard to comprehend."

Harper Creek Community Schools Superintendent Rob Ridgeway confirmed that Abigail is a seventh-grader at the middle school. "Our hearts are broken that a member of our school family was so gravely injured in the shootings on Saturday in Kalamazoo," he said. "Harper Creek Community Schools has had an emergency crisis team and a team of counselors in place since Monday morning. We will continue to support our students and staff with these services. Please join me and the rest of the Harper Creek community in thoughts and prayers for Abigail and all the victims and their families."

## Kalamazoo Shootings: Suspect Jason Dalton Appears in Court Amidst Community Shock and Bewilderment

Kalamazoo County is reeling from a series of devastating shootings, with the initial court appearance of Jason Brian Dalton, the accused perpetrator, amplifying the pervasive sense of fear. Dalton faces grave charges, including multiple counts of murder and assault with intent to commit murder. Law enforcement officials have described the events as a "worst nightmare" due to the sheer randomness and brutality of the violence, leaving the community struggling to comprehend how such senseless acts could occur and questioning their safety.

### The Alleged Pattern of Calculated Killings

Jason Brian Dalton, identified as a husband and father of two, is accused of systematically taking the lives of eight individuals across three distinct locations on that fateful Saturday evening. Prosecuting Attorney Jeffrey Getting has been explicit, stating the acts were not impulsive but rather "very deliberate killings," underscoring the calculated nature of the alleged crimes. Disturbingly, evidence suggests Dalton continued to operate as an Uber driver between these horrific incidents, even accepting fares from unsuspecting passengers. Getting emphasized the cold, calculated intent, stating chillingly, "He chose people and he shot them simply because he could." This admission highlights the terrifying lack of discernible motive and the seemingly arbitrary selection of victims.

The formal indictment against Dalton is extensive, including six counts of murder, two counts of assault with the intent to commit murder, and eight firearms-related charges. Detectives testified that Dalton admitted to "taking people's lives" after his apprehension. Appearing remotely from jail, Dalton displayed little outward emotion as the charges were read. Bail was unequivocally denied, reflecting the extreme severity of the accusations. The only discernible connection between the victims, beyond Dalton, was the use of a semi-automatic pistol, ballistics of which matched evidence from the crime scenes. This absence of any other apparent link amplifies the sense of random terror that has gripped Kalamazoo, leaving the community grappling with the unsettling reality that victims were seemingly chosen at random.

### Family's Plea and Law Enforcement's Profound Bewilderment

The Dalton family released a statement expressing their deepest condolences to the victims' families and pledging their complete cooperation with the investigation, hoping to shed light on the elusive motive. Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller corroborated the family's commitment but candidly acknowledged the significant void of clear answers regarding the precipitating factors. "My best sense is that it was somebody who was having an issue at the time and for whatever reason they decided to do what they did," Fuller commented to CNN, a statement encapsulating the bewilderment felt by law enforcement in the face of such senseless violence. The lack of a clear motive leaves investigators and the public searching for elusive answers.

### A Timeline of Terror Unfolds

The tragic sequence of events began around 3 p.m. on that fateful Saturday. Dalton visited a local firearms retailer, where he was a known customer. The owner described Dalton's demeanor as unremarkable, noting he purchased a jacket suitable for concealing a compact firearm. His departure was without incident, offering no hint of the violence to follow.

The first confirmed shooting occurred around 5:42 p.m. at an apartment complex. Law enforcement believes Dalton fired multiple times at Tiana Carruthers in the parking lot. Tragically, her children witnessed the attack. However, Carruthers is expected to recover from her injuries.

Approximately four hours later, violence escalated dramatically at a local car dealership. Richard Smith, 53, and his son Tyler, 17, were fatally shot in a brutal and senseless act. Tyler's girlfriend witnessed the attack. Public Safety Director Jeff Hadley characterized this incident as "cold blooded."

Dalton then proceeded to a Cracker Barrel restaurant, unleashing further gunfire in the parking lot. This final attack resulted in the tragic deaths of Dorothy Brown, 74; Barbara Hawthorne, 68; Mary Lou Nye, 62; and Mary Jo Nye, 60. A 14-year-old girl, Abigail Kopf, sustained critical injuries but is showing signs of responsiveness.

### Apprehension and Discovery of an Extensive Arsenal

About two hours after the final shooting, Dalton was apprehended without incident in downtown Kalamazoo. A subsequent search of his residence uncovered eleven rifles, underscoring the extensive arsenal available to the accused and adding another layer of disquiet to the investigation. The sheer number of firearms raises further questions about planning and preparation.

### Witness Accounts and Uber's Troubling Role

Matt Mellen, a passenger in Dalton's vehicle shortly before the shootings, recounted a disturbing experience. Mellen described Dalton receiving a phone call and operating the vehicle erratically, including side-swiping another car and disregarding traffic signals. Mellen implored Dalton to stop so he could exit, noting Dalton's surprising composure. Mellen eventually escaped and contacted 911. In another chilling account, an individual named Derek informed WOOD-TV that Dalton had been his Uber driver. When Derek nervously inquired if Dalton was the shooter, Dalton responded with a dismissive "no" and a head shake, attributing his fatigue to long driving hours.

Uber confirmed Dalton had passed their background checks, and law enforcement stated he had no prior criminal record. "For all intents and purposes, he was your average Joe," Hadley remarked, emphasizing the suspect's seemingly ordinary nature. "This was random," he reiterated, a statement encapsulating the profound shock and unsettling lack of discernible motive that has left the community reeling. The investigation continues to delve into the complex circumstances that may have led to this horrific tragedy, with authorities relentlessly seeking answers. The community remains in shock, struggling to comprehend the senseless loss of life and the terrifying randomness of the attacks.

query: What circumstances led to James Harrison's 2008 arrest, and what were the outcomes of the charges against him?

documents.text: James Harrison believes he has grown since being arrested in 2008, according to Scott Brown of ESPN. Harrison, who recently signed a one-year deal with the Pittsburgh Steelers, was arrested for hitting his girlfriend and charged with simple assault and criminal mischief. Both charges were misdemeanors and were eventually dropped. "It was something that happened, an unfortunate situation, and right or wrong everybody makes mistakes," Harrison said following his first practice since re-signing with the Steelers on Tuesday. "I learned from it and grew from it and I'm where I'm at now." Before the Steelers brought Harrison back into the fold, head coach Mike Tomlin sat down and talked with Harrison on the current state of the league. The NFL has recently been rocked by scandals involving domestic violence, most notably cases including Ray Rice, Greg Hardy and Ray McDonald. Rice is under indefinite suspension and Hardy was placed on the exempt list, allowing him to be paid but not to participate with his team. McDonald is still playing for the San Francisco 49ers, who are waiting for the legal system to play out. Harrison could be active this Sunday against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, although the amount of playing time he might receive is uncertain. Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker James Harrison is sparking a conversation about parenting and achievement, not on the field, but through his recent social media actions. Harrison announced via Instagram his decision to return two participation trophies his sons, James III and Henry, were awarded from a local athletics event. This event was organized by former Steelers quarterback Charlie Batch.

Harrison, a distinguished former NFL Defensive Player of the Year, conveyed his pride in his sons' efforts. However, he firmly stated that these particular trophies would be returned. His core belief is that his children should only be recognized for accomplishments they have genuinely earned through hard work and dedication.

He elaborated on his reasoning, highlighting his commitment to preventing his children from developing a sense of entitlement. Harrison shared his perspective on social media, stating, "I came home to find out that my boys received two trophies for nothing, participation trophies!" He further emphasized his point, adding, "cause sometimes your best is not enough, and that should drive you to want to do better."

This parenting philosophy has resonated with Harrison's Pittsburgh Steelers teammates, who have voiced their strong support. Several players have publicly agreed with his stance. Linebacker Jordan Zumwalt shared with Channel 11’s Bill Phillips that the team room erupted in applause upon seeing Harrison's post, reinforcing the idea that "You don’t get trophies for participating." Zumwalt found Harrison's action entirely in character, remarking, "I laughed and thought it was a very James thing to do." Wide receiver Darius Heyward-Bey also expressed his solidarity, telling Phillips, "He can raise his kids the way he wants to. I kind of agree with him. You know, in this world, you’ve got to earn things."

The Best of the Batch Foundation is a nonprofit organization dedicated to enhancing the lives of children and families in underserved communities. Their mission centers on fostering character development, boosting self-esteem, and instilling a strong appreciation for education.

Currently operating in southwestern Pennsylvania, the foundation undertakes impactful initiatives. These include providing scholarships for college-bound students and organizing an annual back-to-school book bag distribution, known as BatchPacks, for children and educators.

Beyond these core programs, Best of the Batch offers unique educational opportunities that integrate sports with learning. A prime example is their seven-week summer basketball camp. During this camp, young participants engage in study hall sessions where they read and discuss selected books, blending athletic activity with academic enrichment.

Founded in 1999 by NFL quarterback Charlie Batch, the Best of the Batch Foundation is a testament to his personal values of dedication, leadership, and trust. Charlie has consistently utilized his platform as a Pittsburgh Steeler to connect with and inspire young people.

Each program offered by the foundation reinforces Charlie's personal motto, the "5 P's," which he believes are crucial for success in all life endeavors: proper preparation prevents poor performance. His overarching passion is to unlock the inherent potential within children and their families by equipping them with the resources needed to pursue education and empowerment, enabling them to strive for excellence in every aspect of their lives.

Charlie Batch's commitment extends beyond his athletic career. As Dan Rooney of the Pittsburgh Steelers noted, "The great thing about Charlie is that he’s not just a football player but really cares about this community. He established Best of the Batch Foundation to assist local youth and hosts Project C.H.U.C.K, an annual youth basketball league in Homestead. I can vouch for the fact that Charlie loves kids."

For more detailed information, you can download the Best of the Batch Foundation Fact Sheet.
