Such tactics are ineffective and antithetical to our national identity."

The context for Cruz's "New York values" comment emerged during Donald Trump's questioning of Cruz's presidential eligibility due to his Canadian birth, despite his mother's American citizenship. Trump, a Queens native, countered by referencing New York's resilience and response to the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks as a testament to its values. Governor Cuomo supported this assertion on MSNBC's "Morning Joe," remarking, "That is a salient point." He added, "While Trump's timing may have been politically calculated, it resonated with the public. As Cruz propagates anti-New York sentiments and attempts to leverage offensive stereotypes, it's crucial to remember that 9/11 stands as a profound symbol of America's noblest qualities."

Cuomo also highlighted the apparent hypocrisy of Cruz's stance, noting the senator's history of soliciting campaign contributions in Manhattan. "This is profoundly troubling on multiple fronts and demonstrably hypocritical," he asserted on MSNBC. "To criticize New York's financial influence while simultaneously benefiting from it is disingenuous. One can only assume he will now refuse any donations from New Yorkers." During a third interview with NY1, Cuomo further elaborated on the breadth of Cruz's perceived offenses, stating, "He has offended the LGBTQ+ community. He has been offensive to women. He has offended 18 million New Yorkers, and a significant congressional delegation, which is not a viable approach to governance."

Beyond the Governor's office, other New York entities also condemned Cruz's rhetoric. Representative Pete King (R-New York) advised the senator to "retreat." Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams publicly denounced Cruz on Twitter. The New York Daily News featured a provocative image of the Statue of Liberty extending a middle finger towards Senator Cruz.

The phrase "New York values" is often used, especially in political discourse, but its precise meaning remains unclear to many. Drawing from extensive experience across both upstate and downstate New York, this piece aims to provide a more grounded perspective than is often offered by those without firsthand knowledge. My upbringing in Rochester and nearly a decade in New York City have fostered a deep appreciation for the state's diverse character and its dynamic urban center.

Outsiders, such as Ted Cruz, whose background is far removed from New York, frequently discuss these values with a superficial understanding. This approach is like judging a book by its cover; it reveals only a fraction of the story. Cruz, with his Texas origins and elite education, lacks the lived experience to truly grasp the complex fabric of New Yorkers.

Even figures like former Mayor Michael Bloomberg, whose policies sometimes earned him the "nanny-state" label, resist simple categorization. While a proposal to limit large sugary drink sales contributed to this perception, it was ultimately not implemented. New York City possesses a distinct character that sets it apart, a nuance often lost in broad generalizations.

When Cruz referenced Donald Trump's use of "Born in the USA" and suggested Trump would embody "New York values," he missed the mark. His assertion that these values differ from those in Iowa or New Hampshire overlooks significant commonalities. Much of upstate New York shares a landscape, culture, and even prevailing attitudes with New Hampshire. For instance, conservative Republicans upstate often prioritize gun ownership and show less interest in issues like same-sex marriage.

Cruz's perception of "New York values" likely stems from a narrow, stereotypical view, heavily influenced by media portrayals or fleeting glimpses of urban life. This stereotype often includes perceptions of liberal politics, political correctness, and regulations like the soda ban. It also encompasses the vocal opinions found in certain publications, values most prominent in affluent areas often used for political fundraising.

This stereotype also fails to capture Donald Trump's own background. While he is wealthy and resides in a prominent skyscraper, his early life was shaped by the outer boroughs, particularly Queens, and his father's strong business foundation. Trump's defining characteristic is an outsider's drive for success, a stark contrast to the "old money" attitudes found in some of the city's most exclusive neighborhoods, where his more extravagant displays might be met with disapproval.

Most New Yorkers, like people elsewhere, are primarily focused on their personal lives and immediate concerns, not overly preoccupied with politics unless a truly widespread issue arises. Many, myself included, found Bloomberg's proposed soda limit to be a poorly conceived idea. Bloomberg himself, a quintessential New Yorker, was reportedly unbothered by such matters, a trait attributed to his wealth and, more importantly, his inherent New York identity.

The simplistic notion that New Yorkers are solely defined by toughness, rudeness, and a desire to impose their views is illogical. It's contradictory to be both gruff and overly sentimental. I'd playfully invite Mr. Cruz to spend a month living in a modest apartment in an average New York neighborhood, navigating daily life from finding a local bodega to understanding the subway and using local healthcare. If, after such an immersion, he still insists New York's values are significantly more liberal than those in Iowa City, I'll acknowledge his critique, though I won't be overly concerned.

An update from a pro-Cruz Super PAC suggested "NY values" prioritize financial gain over morality and self-importance over humility. This critique is like someone visiting a major tourist hub on a busy holiday and commenting on the perceived lack of quiet contemplation – it's based on isolated, unrepresentative observations rather than a thorough understanding.

The New York Daily News has forcefully responded to Senator Ted Cruz's controversial remarks regarding "New York values." In a striking front-page editorial, the newspaper directly challenged the Texas senator, who had questioned Donald Trump's connection to the city's distinctive character.

The Daily News's provocative cover depicted the Statue of Liberty with an extended middle finger, accompanied by the headline: "Drop Dead Ted." This bold statement echoed the paper's iconic 1975 "Ford to City: Drop Dead" headline. The cover's release followed a Republican presidential debate in South Carolina, preceding the Iowa caucuses.

During the debate, Senator Cruz attempted to question Trump's Republican credentials by stating, "I think most people know exactly what New York values are. Everyone understands that the values in New York City are socially liberal." In a powerful counterpoint, Daily News columnist Mike Lupica shared a personal observation of the city's inherent kindness.

Lupica recounted witnessing a profound act of generosity on a Manhattan subway, where one individual readily offered assistance to another in distress. He further articulated his perspective, asserting, "The best of this city is the best of this country, and always has been. When it was hit in a way that no American city had ever been hit, it came together and rose up together in a moment as thrilling as any America had ever seen."

Donald Trump also reacted strongly during the debate, defending the city's resilience and spirit. "We rebuilt downtown Manhattan and everybody in the world watched and everybody in the world loved New York and loved New Yorkers. And I have to tell you, that was a very insulting statement that Ted made," Trump declared. It is worth noting that the Daily News has also been critical of Trump, notably with a past cover addressing his proposal to ban all Muslims from entering the United States. Current polling indicates Trump's lead among Republican candidates, with a recent NBC News/Wall Street Journal poll showing him as the preferred choice for 33% of potential Republican primary voters, while Cruz garnered 20%.

Ted Cruz has suggested that Donald Trump's ongoing criticisms are a clear indication of concern regarding Cruz's leading position in the presidential race. Cruz interprets these persistent attacks as Trump embodying what he terms "New York values." During an appearance on The Howie Carr Show, Cruz discussed Trump's use of Bruce Springsteen's iconic song "Born in the USA." This song choice has been linked to the ongoing debate surrounding Cruz's eligibility for the presidency, stemming from his birth in Canada.

Cruz speculated that Trump's campaign strategy might pivot to a theme song like Frank Sinatra's "New York, New York," given Trump's own New York origins and his perceived embodiment of those particular values. The Texas senator characterized Trump as being visibly "rattled" by Cruz's increasing prominence and momentum in the campaign. He described Trump's approach as "unloading at every stop with direct attacks." Although Cruz initially stated his intention not to engage in reciprocal attacks, he did subtly counter Trump on two separate occasions. Earlier, he informed reporters that supporters of Hillary Clinton appeared "eager to support Donald Trump and the attacks that are being tossed my direction."

During the radio interview, Cruz reiterated this observation, referencing Hillary Clinton's own comment that Trump had been "a Democrat before he was a Republican." Cruz remarked that Clinton, as a partisan Democrat herself, would possess the expertise to identify fellow Democrats. He also noted that Clinton and Trump are well acquainted. Cruz found it particularly significant that key supporters of Clinton were actively amplifying Trump's criticisms directed at him.

Cruz attributed these Democratic-backed attacks to current polling data. He believes these polls indicate that he would defeat Hillary Clinton, whereas Trump, according to the same data, would lose to her by a considerable margin. This disparity, he explained, likely fuels concern among Clinton's supporters, motivating them to amplify Trump's criticisms against Cruz.

In response to Trump's assertion that evangelicals do not originate from Cuba, Cruz extended an invitation to Trump to attend church services with him. Cruz suggested that attacks targeting an individual's faith often signal nervousness about the current direction of the presidential race. He reiterated his willingness to invite Trump to church, emphasizing that his primary focus remains on the campaign itself, rather than engaging in retaliatory or negative campaigning.

query: What prompted the backlash against Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the owners of Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre?

documents.text:
## Vegan Restaurant Founders' Animal Consumption Sparks Outrage

The founders of the highly regarded vegan restaurant group Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre are currently embroiled in a significant controversy. They are facing intense backlash, including death threats, following the emergence of images that depict Matthew and Terces Engelhart raising, slaughtering, and consuming animals on their farm. This revelation has deeply disturbed many within the vegan community, who have long been patrons of their exclusively plant-based establishments. The very foundation of their brand, built on a message of compassion and animal welfare, has been called into question by these disturbing images.

The Engelharts, who established the first Cafe Gratitude in 2004 and have hosted numerous high-profile figures such as Gwyneth Paltrow and Beyoncé, are now defending their practices at Be Love Farm in Vacaville, California. They characterize this shift in their personal philosophy as an evolution, deeply rooted in a commitment to what they term "regenerative agriculture." The controversy gained significant momentum when activists shared blog posts dating back to 2015, which detailed their "transition" to meat consumption after nearly four decades of dedicated vegetarianism. These posts included stark visual evidence, such as images of a freezer fully stocked with beef and Matthew Engelhart openly consuming a hamburger. Matthew Engelhart himself explained his perspective to the Hollywood Reporter, stating, "We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals." He views the consumption of animals as partaking in an "extreme sacrifice for humanity," and believes that humans can honor this by engaging in this practice, which he refers to as a "sacrament."

While the Engelharts' farm does supply produce to their restaurants, the news of their animal consumption has incensed strict vegans. The online criticism has been particularly harsh, with many leveling accusations of hypocrisy and ill will. Critics argue that the Engelharts deliberately misled their vegan clientele, who supported their businesses with the understanding that they were contributing to a cruelty-free enterprise. These patrons unknowingly supported practices that involved animal slaughter. The couple had meticulously built their brand on a message of peace and love, a philosophy that extended to their sister restaurant, Gracias Madre, which boasts locations in both San Francisco and Los Angeles. Their commitment to this ethos was further solidified through their authorship of books, including "Sacred Commerce: Business as a Path of Awakening." A blog post from Be Love Farm, published in February, described their decision to incorporate meat products as a "necessary and important part of our growth as well as the sustainability of our farm." This post also featured images of their beef alongside idyllic pastoral scenes of their cows, attempting to frame their practices within a broader ecological context.

This is not the first instance of the Engelharts facing public scrutiny. In 2011, they cited "aggressive lawsuits" filed by former employees as the primary reason for closing or selling eight Cafe Gratitude restaurants located in the Bay Area. These legal challenges specifically targeted tip-pooling practices and alleged instances of unpaid overtime. Another lawsuit involved an employee who claimed wrongful termination after refusing to participate in a controversial self-improvement course. Ultimately, the Engelharts settled these disputes, subsequently selling or transferring ownership of some of the restaurants to family members. Terces Engelhart reportedly met Matthew at a Landmark Forum seminar, a personal development program. Her daughter from a previous marriage is now a co-owner of a vegan beer garden and a vegan ice cream parlor situated in Los Angeles. Activist Mary Cummins has put forth the theory that the Engelharts may have been influenced by Allan Savory, an ecologist who advocates for the strategic use of livestock in grasslands as a method to combat climate change. This theory has garnered both fervent support and significant opposition. The long-term ramifications of this current controversy on the Engelhart empire, which serves a substantial number of meals daily and hosts events at their farm, remain uncertain. It is presently unclear whether patrons will continue to engage with the farm, particularly its animal interaction programs, after becoming aware of their meat production practices.

This past week on the farm was truly remarkable, marked by both personal recovery and significant agricultural developments. We were incredibly fortunate to receive a generous gift from our dear friends at Ca Momi in Napa: a selection of their exquisite pizzas and profiteroles. These culinary delights provided a welcome comfort during my recovery from a surgical procedure, and I am deeply grateful for their thoughtfulness and the added rest it afforded me.

Inspired by this act of kindness and the abundance of exceptional farm cream we produce, I felt compelled to create my own profiteroles. The accompanying image showcases this endeavor, with me meticulously piping the delicate choux pastry onto parchment paper, preparing them for the oven. Meanwhile, Matthew diligently continued his essential work of pruning all the trees across the property. Our sons, Jaxon and Jack, contributed significantly by applying mulch around the base of these trees, ensuring their health and vitality.

The visual landscape of the farm is currently breathtaking. The almond trees are in the full splendor of their bloom, and the nectarine blossoms are beginning to unfurl in a beautiful display of soft pink. This period of natural beauty coincides with a profound and transformative shift for our farm: the introduction of our own meat products. This transition follows nearly four decades of dedicated vegetarianism.

We acknowledge that this shift represents a deep internal evolution, a process unfolding within our very beings. However, we recognize its absolute necessity for our continued growth and the long-term sustainability of our agricultural enterprise. It is natural to grapple with the inevitability of mortality, both for ourselves and for the animals under our care. Yet, as we embrace a greater capacity for love and fulfill our role as stewards of these animals, the profound responsibility for their health and well-being rests squarely upon us. This is a complex and ongoing discussion, and we extend an invitation to join us at one of our upcoming farm dinners over the next nine months, where we will delve deeper into this transformative journey.

The accompanying photographs offer further glimpses into our farm's current activities. Here, Matthew is meticulously straining beef broth, a testament to our burgeoning meat production. We also proudly display our freezer stocked with pastured beef, raised with the utmost care. In another image, our cows, replete with the nourishment of spring clover, are captured peacefully chewing their cud in the gentle warmth of the midday spring breeze. We are also delighted to introduce Jack (the blonde young man) and Jaxon, our enthusiastic and energetic apprentices, who bring a welcome youthful dynamism to the farm.

With no immediate prospect of rain, we have commenced irrigation efforts. This practical necessity leads me to a contemplative question for the week: How do you nourish your soul? I encourage you to reflect on this. Wishing you a truly wonderful week ahead.

Love,
Terces

The owners of a popular California-based vegan restaurant chain, known for attracting celebrities, are facing intense backlash from animal rights activists. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the husband-and-wife team behind Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre, have reportedly received death threats after it was revealed they have returned to eating meat and are raising and slaughtering animals on their farm.

This controversy erupted when activists discovered blog posts from the couple's Be Love Farm in northern California. These posts, according to reports to the Hollywood Reporter, documented the Engleharts raising cattle and other animals. "They are duping vegans to support their animal killings," stated activist Chase Avior, who has organized protests at the restaurants.

The Engleharts launched their first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004, expanding to southern California around 2011. Their establishments gained fame for catering to a growing demand for healthy, sustainable food and became a magnet for celebrities such as Emma Stone, Andrew Garfield, Kerry Washington, Jay Z, and Beyonce.

The firestorm ignited when a 14-month-old blog post surfaced, detailing the couple's decision to end 40 years of vegetarianism. They described their new approach as "regenerative agriculture," a concept that includes raising animals. The post featured images of freezers stocked with beef, jars of beef stock, and Matthew Engelhart enjoying a hamburger.

Matthew Engelhart explained their shift to the Hollywood Reporter, stating, "We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals." However, this explanation has not appeased outraged animal rights activists, who have derided their new philosophy as "regenerative bulls--t!" on social media platforms dedicated to boycotting the restaurants and organizing demonstrations.

Protests have occurred outside their establishments, with demonstrators holding signs labeling the restaurateurs "hypocrites" given their prominent role in the vegan community. Other signs have directly referenced the restaurant's name, proclaiming, "no animal is grateful to die." The backlash has also manifested in a flood of one-star Yelp reviews, with one commenter writing, "I’m GRATEFUL that I know they’re s--theads!" Another review suggested a new menu item: "Here's a new dish for Cafe Gratitude - I Am MURDERER!!!" Matthew Engelhart has expressed dismay at the situation, lamenting, "People have taken up the mob mentality."

The owners of the most prominent vegan restaurant chain in Los Angeles were found to be raising and "harvesting" animals at their Northern California farm. Now Hollywood's vegan community is angry, and death threats have been made. "People have taken up the mob mentality," says Cafe Gratitude owner Matthew Engelhart. L.A.'s vegan vortex has angrily turned on the most prominent vegan restaurant group in town this week as word has spread that its owners are not just eating meat but raising and slaughtering animals at the working farm where they live in Northern California. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the husband-and-wife proprietors of favored entertainment industry haunt Cafe Gratitude, tell The Hollywood Reporter they've been receiving death threats as part of a quickly growing, internet-bred campaign against them. It has also spawned a deluge of one-star reviews on their local outposts' respective Yelp pages, a boycott group on Facebook that tallies 571 members at press time and plans now underway for a protest at the Larchmont Village location on Friday at 7 p.m. "People have taken up the mob mentality," says Matthew. "It saddens me that the choices we made in the privacy of our home would lead people to feel so betrayed that it's elevated to threats on our lives. I'm very discouraged." The trouble began last week when animal rights activists discovered and then widely circulated a 14-month-old blog post written by Terces on the Engelharts' Be Love Farm website, which mixed an announcement of their transition back into a meat diet again after nearly 40 years of vegetarianism (they had been vegan since 2003) with posted pictures of strained beef broth and a freezer full of pastured beef from their own dairy cows. Matthew tells THR they have kept chickens on the farm for seven years "for eggs only," along with the cows for five years for milk, cheese and butter that's for sale. (He claims they've "harvested," or slaughtered, several cows in total and never sold the meat, only shared it with "our friends, neighbors and community.") The news has come as a shock to many vegans, who have been regular customers of the restaurants and claim the Engelharts have built their brand on not just serving vegan food but clearly wrapping themselves in the righteousness of the vegan cause — which they argue has now been undermined. "The reason we're so upset is that veganism is a belief system," says Carrie Christianson, who started the Facebook boycott group. "You are patronizing a restaurant that you think has that philosophy, and it turns out it doesn't. Vegans should know that this restaurant has a farm that slaughters animals." The Engelharts launched the first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004. The mini-chain began colonizing Southern California in 2011 with the on-the-ground help of their manager sons from different marriages, Ryland Engelhart and Cary Mosier, beginning with a location in Larchmont Village, followed by outposts in Venice and downtown L.A.'s Arts District (as well as locations in San Diego and Newport Beach). Investors such as musician Jason Mraz have backed them. (A newer sister concept, Gracias Madre, which serves "meatless Mexican" fare, opened in 2014 in West Hollywood; their daughter, Mollie, co-owns Culver City's separate Sage Organic Vegan Bistro — which also purchases from Be Love Farm — with Woody Harrelson.) Cafe Gratitude is known for giving dishes names that are self-affirmations. The most popular include the "I Am Whole" macrobiotic sea-vegetable rice and a curried lentil assemblage called "I Am Humble." The locations have become a solid entertainment industry draw for business lunches, as well as a reliable celebrity nexus. Customers range from Beyonce Knowles and Sacha Baron Cohen to Ariana Grande and Jake Gyllenhaal. The Engelharts frame their return to "sustainable, regenerative" animal consumption as the mindful culmination of years spent laboring on their upstate farm. "We started to observe nature, and what we saw is that nature doesn't exist without animals," says Matthew. "Neither does natural farming. You know, you can't buy organic vegetables that aren't fertilized with animal residue. So that was our discovery. We aren't on a soapbox." Yet while the Engelharts are at pains to paint their decision as a private and personal rural journey separate from their public work at their urban restaurants, their critics find the reasoning specious, believing the enterprises, the figureheads and their choices are inextricably bound. "This is a vegan restaurant company that's been a leader in the vegan community," says Chase Avior, a screenwriter and director who is organizing the Friday protest. "If they're going to go around and say it's not about animal rights, it's not about speaking about the animals horrifically abused, it's just about better nurturing a plant-based lifestyle, that's something different. A lot of vegans have a right to feel betrayed by this." Still, the couple's vegan supporters think the fury is misplaced. "Boycott factory farming — something that will make a real difference to the cause," says Rainbeau Mars, author of The 21-Day SuperStar Cleanse and, along with Ryland Engelhart, a board member at Venice-based healthy-soil nonprofit Kiss the Ground. "And as for their own personal diets, would we stop supporting the Dalai Lama because he eats meat? Or stop supporting the organic farmer who grows your tomatoes because she eats meat?" After that interview, Mars wrote a long follow-up email to THR to clarify her position ("despite needing to go teach a yoga class on the helicopter pad of the Four Seasons this morning and head out of town this evening"). In it, Mars explains how she has worked in "service to greater humanity" and preaches a sort of compassion. "I feel we should support the leaders in our community that are sharing charity and compassion, health and community activism such as the founders of Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre," she says. "I invite us all to not point the finger at others but look at ourselves." Such thinking doesn't sit well with others who have spoken out about the Engelharts' choices at Be Love Farm, which according to the couple these days supplies only a limited amount of fruits and vegetables to the restaurants. One of them is the musician Moby, who opened his own vegan restaurant five months ago in Silver Lake called Little Pine. "I sincerely hope that they discontinue their practice of raising and killing animals for food," he wrote on Facebook. Meanwhile, Cooking Channel personality and raw food chef Jason Wrobel (his clients include Robin Wright and Jeremy Piven) penned a long open letter on his website, echoing many online sentiments: "People feel misled, deliberately lied to and that a business they've so lovingly supported for many years has lost its way. I feel that my hard-earned money has been used for purposes that are unethical, cruel and out-of-alignment with my values. And I strongly feel that a more public announcement from the company should have been made about the practices at the farm, not tucked away in some obscure blog post." For their part, the Engelharts profess to be perplexed by the dissent. "I don't think there's any organization on the planet that's done more to promote a plant-based diet than us," says Matthew. "We've moved it from a dogma to a genre. We serve 28,000 meals a week in all of our enterprises. We've done nothing but a plant-based diet at our restaurants and we're being attacked. It doesn't make sense to me." Their critics aren't satisfied, despite their record. "I eat at non-vegan restaurants all the time," says Elana Lavine, who runs the L.A. healthy lifestyle blog Klean Slate. "Just don't claim to be something you're not. They're slaughtering animals! And there's been a lack of total transparency about it on the restaurant side. It looks kind of shady, like they are hiding things. And there was always that cloud around them when it came to the cult thing." The "cult thing" has left customers leery in the past. The couple, who like to say they practice "sacred commerce," had to close their Northern California locations in 2010 in the midst of lawsuit battles, one related to a policy encouraging allegedly illegal tip-pooling and another stemming from a mandate that their employee get involved with the controversial personal-betterment organization Landmark Forum, a successor to Werner Erhard's 1970s self-help program EST, which centered on a unique style of session therapy that was recently dramatized on FX's period spy show The Americans. Both iterations have been criticized for their aggressive recruitment tactics. The Engelharts, who met while attending Landmark Forum, believe they're being held up to a dogmatic purity standard — one that, in the scheme of their contributions to veganism, is entirely unfair. "Somehow we got made mom and dad of some vegan movement," says Matthew. "We never signed up for that. It's crazy. Do [the protestors] check in on the diets of every other vegan restaurant owner? Do they check in on the diets of the owners of Whole Foods? We are baffled. And believe me there are lots of vegan enterprises where the principals are not vegan, or they have other enterprises that are not vegan." He goes on, "I am allowed to change my mind. And by the way, I never even told them what my mind was. All I told them was it's a vegan restaurant. And it still is! And it always will be!"

Chef Chandra Gilbert, a culinary innovator with **33 years in the industry**, is bringing her unique take on plant-based cuisine to Los Angeles. Her new establishment, **Gracias Madre**, aims to offer a lively blend of **strong tastes** and **ingredients chosen with the seasons in mind**. Gilbert's rich history includes key positions at well-regarded eateries like Alice Waters' Café Fanny, Cowgirl Creamery, and Greens Restaurant. She has been a vital member of the Café Gratitude team since **2004**.

Her time with the famous vegan restaurant group began as Director of Operations in the Bay Area. It was during this period that the idea for Gracias Madre took shape, growing from discussions with Café Gratitude Founders Matthew and Terces Engelhart. Together, they **shaped the restaurant's look and feel** and **developed its creative menu**. This menu drew heavily on the outstanding produce grown at the Engelhart's Be Love Farm in Vacaville.

Gracias Madre first opened its doors in San Francisco in **2009**, realizing Gilbert's concept. Situated in the city's Mission District, the restaurant rapidly earned **praise from critics** for its **energetic and original take on Mexican food**. Now, with the **January 2014 opening of Gracias Madre in West Hollywood**, Gilbert is introducing this acclaimed culinary approach to Los Angeles diners. The Los Angeles menu still features the harvest from Be Love Farm, **further enriched by the freshest produce sourced from local farmers markets**.

A comprehensive crawl of outlinks originating from Wikipedia.org commenced in March 2016. Currently, these collected files are not available for public access. This particular collection possesses several distinct properties.

It has been a considerable period since a similar undertaking was last conducted. For this recent collection, a series of specific adjustments were implemented.

Firstly, duplicate detection mechanisms were deactivated. This decision was made to ensure the completeness of the collection, anticipating a potential future release of the data. Managing shared data that includes pointers to disparate, randomly selected collections presents a significant logistical challenge.

Secondly, this initiative marked the first instance where outlinks from all available Wikipedia wikis were included. Previous crawls were exclusively focused on the English Wikipedia (enwiki). The seed list for this extensive crawl was meticulously constructed from all 865 existing Wikipedia collections.

## Café Gratitude: Nourishing You and the Planet with Plant-Based Cuisine

Café Gratitude invites you on a plant-based culinary journey, offering gourmet dishes designed to nourish both your well-being and the environment. Our fundamental aim is to foster an atmosphere and menu that champions health and ecological responsibility. This dedication is central to our unique "Sacred Commerce" approach, which highlights exceptional service, clear communication, and a profound appreciation for life's abundance.

We envision a world brimming with plenty, celebrating existence through vibrant, delicious food and a committed team. We honor the earth and ourselves by:

*   **Sourcing the finest organic ingredients.**
*   **Supporting local growers.**
*   **Promoting sustainable farming methods.**
*   **Using environmentally conscious products in our operations.**

Every meal is crafted with care, encouraging guests to embrace our warm environment and cultivate feelings of self-love, acceptance, generosity, and gratitude. Discover the deep satisfaction of being nourished, cared for, and revitalized.

Executive Chef Dreux Ellis leads the development and execution of our organic, plant-based menu across all Southern California Café Gratitude locations. Since joining the Venice team in April 2012, Chef Ellis has been key in making healthy, flavorful food accessible to more people. He currently guides the culinary direction for our restaurants in the Arts District, Larchmont Village, Venice, and San Diego, as well as the revitalized Gratitude restaurants in Newport Beach and Beverly Hills.

**Find a Café Gratitude Near You:**

*   **Los Angeles - Larchmont Village:** 639 N Larchmont Blvd., Los Angeles, CA 90004 | (323) 580-6383 | Open Daily: 8 AM - 10 PM
*   **San Diego:** 1980 Kettner Blvd., San Diego, CA 92101 | (619) 736-5077 | Open Daily: 8 AM - 10 PM (Valet parking available Thurs-Sat, 5:30 PM - 11:30 PM)
*   **Newport Beach:** 1617 Westcliff Drive, Newport Beach, CA 92660 | (949) 386-8100 | Weekdays: 8 AM - 10 PM; Weekends: 8 AM - 10 PM (To-Go/Cafe Hours: 7 AM - 10 PM Daily)
*   **Arts District, Los Angeles:** 300 S Santa Fe Ave., Los Angeles, CA 90013 | (213) 929-5580 | Open Daily: 8 AM - 10 PM
*   **Venice:** 512 Rose Ave., Venice, CA 90291 | (424) 231-8000 | Open Daily: 8 AM - 10 PM (90-minute parking available in downstairs garage with validation)
*   **Beverly Hills:** 419 N Canon Drive, Beverly Hills, CA 90210 | (424) 389-1850 | Open Daily: 8 AM - 10 PM (Parking information on-site)

**Stay Connected:**

Explore resources like "Kiss The Ground" to learn more about soil health and regenerative agriculture. Subscribe to our newsletter for updates. For career opportunities or general questions, please reach out to us directly.

This week was a profound period of growth and learning, marked by the exciting launch of a new Cafe Gratitude in downtown Los Angeles' vibrant Arts District. The training of 112 new employees was a significant undertaking, yet it beautifully showcased the dedication of individuals committed to Cafe Gratitude's core mission: cultivating open hearts, expanding love, and fostering ethical livelihoods. A surprise guest during a training lunch added an extra layer of joy to this momentous occasion. Beyond these professional achievements, visits to Los Angeles always bring cherished moments with my grandsons, Valentine, now 6 months old, and Rio, who is 2 months old. The role of grandmother brings me immense joy.

On the farm, we celebrated an extraordinary harvest, with enormous heads of lettuce a clear signal of the land's remarkable fertility. This week also brought a deeply personal turning point: after over four decades of dedicated vegetarianism, we prepared and consumed our first hamburgers. This dietary shift, while certainly challenging, has been profoundly illuminating, approached with deep gratitude for the sacrifices made by our cattle.

Matthew's decision to reintroduce meat into his diet after forty years of vegetarianism stems from a complex understanding of non-violence within the intricate ecological web. He argues that conventional agriculture, particularly practices like plowing and monoculture, represents a destructive force, significantly contributing to species extinction and habitat loss. For instance, the production of margarine from Canadian canola necessitates plowing that devastates fragile prairie ecosystems. Matthew advocates for well-managed grasslands, supported by ruminants like cattle, as a means to preserve biodiversity and prevent land degradation, a concept strongly supported by organizations such as the Savory Institute.

Grasslands, which cover approximately 30% of Earth's surface, fundamentally depend on ruminants for their health and vitality. Their absence often leads to land deterioration and desertification, with consequences that can even fuel regional conflicts. Within this ecological context, cattle emerge as vital partners in restoration efforts. They demonstrably improve soil fertility, ensure essential ground cover, and actively help reverse desertification while simultaneously mitigating climate change. Matthew firmly believes that ethically raised cattle, when integrated thoughtfully into our food system, offer a profound way to participate in a natural sacrament, acknowledging their essential role as a source of sustenance.

This profound perspective on sacrifice is eloquently echoed by Wendell Berry, who stated: "To live we must daily break the body and shed the blood of Creation. When we do it knowingly, lovingly, skillfully, reverently, it is a sacrament. When we do it ignorantly, greedily, clumsily, destructively it is a desecration." This philosophy was further reinforced by an insightful Allan Savory workshop, where a dinner conversation provided a unique glimpse into the preparation of traditional South African "biltong."

While veganism remains a valid and important response to the animal cruelty prevalent in industrial food systems, we also recognize the significant nutritional benefits derived from animals raised in healthy, humane environments. It is crucial, as many food system advocates emphatically emphasize, to foster a deeper connection between consumers and their food sources, embracing the full, natural cycle of life and death. This holistic view is steadily gaining traction and support among proponents of sustainable agriculture.

This week has served as a humbling yet powerful reminder of divine grace and its indispensable importance in all facets of our lives. Reflecting on our journey, from the opening of our very first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco 11 years ago, when cold-brewed coffee and almond milk were considered novelties, to our present day, our progress has been nothing short of extraordinary. This remarkable achievement stands as a testament to the enduring vision of founders like Matthew and Terces.

query: What specific actions led to the dismissal of Harriet Wallberg and Anders Hamsten from the Nobel Assembly?

documents.text:
Here's a revised version of the document, aiming to enhance query similarity and semantic uniqueness while adjusting vocabulary and paragraph structure:

**Astonishing Scientific Discoveries: From Cosmic Phenomena to Ancient Life**

This document explores remarkable scientific discoveries and captivating imagery, offering insights into the cosmos, ancient life, and groundbreaking biological findings.

**Cosmic Wonders and Planetary Mysteries**

NASA has captured stunning images of Jupiter's Great Red Spot, a storm larger than Earth active for over 350 years. Meanwhile, a massive 75,000-mile-wide hole on the Sun's surface, linked to magnetic field interactions, reveals cooler areas. Mars continues to intrigue with evidence of a watery past, such as Echus Chasma. While toxic chemicals on its surface make current life prospects remote, NASA has confirmed flowing water, with hydrated salts indicating dark patches known as Recurring Slope Lineae are caused by liquid water. Our galaxy is observed ejecting comets, potentially increasing Earth's risk of meteor impacts. Scientists suggest orbital disturbances could be more significant than previously thought, with evidence pointing to recent activity indicating passage through a meteor shower. These showers, occurring on an estimated 26-million-year cycle, coincide with mass extinction events over the past 260 million years.

**Paleontological and Archaeological Finds**

Archaeologists in China unearthed remains of 5,000-year-old individuals who were exceptionally tall and robust, with the tallest skeleton measuring 1.9 meters. In Surrey, a 132-million-year-old dinosaur fossil, suspected to be an Iguanodon, was discovered at a factory. This herbivore stood approximately three meters tall and measured ten meters in length. A British scientist unearthed a dog-sized horned dinosaur fossil in North America, dating up to 100 million years old. This jawbone fragment provides crucial evidence for an evolutionary divide between eastern and western dinosaur species on the continent during the Late Cretaceous period. Researchers also discovered a "lost world" of Arctic dinosaurs, with a new species, *Ugrunaaluk kuukpikensis*, challenging existing understandings of dinosaur physiology and survival in extreme environments.

**Biological and Medical Insights**

The Wellcome Image Awards showcase intricate biological imagery, including a detailed 3D reconstruction of an African grey parrot's blood vessel system and the delicate beauty of baby Hawaiian bobtail squid, revealing their ink sac and light organ. Another award-winning image features a close-up of an iris clip, illustrating the implantation of an artificial intraocular lens. Scientists have isolated the distinct scent of human death, identifying specific chemicals emitted by corpses to aid in future body recovery efforts. A comprehensive study challenges the concept of distinct "male" and "female" brains, suggesting that while specific brain regions show sex-based differences, individual brains rarely conform to exclusively "male" or "female" traits. Chinese scientists have also created genetically-modified beagles with double the muscle mass by editing a specific gene, expecting enhanced running abilities and developing them for various applications, including research into human diseases.

**Earth's Natural Wonders and Global Concerns**

Yellowstone National Park mesmerizes with geothermal wonders like the Grand Prismatic Spring and "Old Faithful" geyser. The Syrian civil war necessitated the first-ever withdrawal from the Svalbard Global Seed Vault. Drought-resistant seeds were requested due to damage to a seed bank in Aleppo, marking the initial use of the vault to compensate for regional supply disruptions. Astronomers captured an unprecedented event: a black hole consuming a star and then expelling a portion of it. This observation, tracking a Sun-sized star pulled into a supermassive black hole, revealed a high-speed flare ejected from the black hole's rim, directly linked to the swallowed star. Evidence suggests life on Earth may have emerged hundreds of millions of years earlier than previously understood, potentially as far back as 4.1 billion years ago. Analysis of graphite trapped in ancient crystals indicates that life could have begun almost instantaneously after Earth's formation.

The Swedish government has taken the unprecedented step of dissolving the entire board of the Karolinska Institute, a globally recognized center of medical research and the awarding body for the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. This decisive action is a direct consequence of a comprehensive inquiry into severe mismanagement and profound negligence surrounding the recruitment of surgeon Paolo Macchiarini and his subsequent patient treatments. The escalating medical scandal, now encompassing grave allegations of scientific misconduct and patient fatalities, has inflicted significant damage upon the institute's esteemed reputation.

The controversial appointment of Italian surgeon Paolo Macchiarini in 2010, intended to advance stem cell biology research and provide consultations at the Karolinska University Hospital, ultimately triggered a cascade of high-profile departures. These included the Nobel Committee secretary and the institute's chancellor. Macchiarini himself was dismissed in March, with Karolinska officials citing falsified resume details and scientific negligence, particularly in light of two patient deaths. Prosecutors are currently investigating Macchiarini for gross criminal negligence, though he maintains his innocence.

A pivotal external investigation report revealed the management's "stunning indifference" to negative references concerning Macchiarini, confirming that the institute had contravened established laws, regulations, and ethical standards. Minister for Higher Education and Research Helene Hellmark Knutsson unequivocally stated, "Scandal is indeed the appropriate term," emphasizing that "Individuals have suffered harm due to the actions of the Karolinska Institute and also the Karolinska University Hospital." Consequently, all current board members not already having resigned will be replaced upon the appointment of a new governing body. Harriet Wallberg, the nation's University Chancellor who presided over the institute during Macchiarini's hiring, will also be stepping down.

Further scrutiny has focused on three surgeries conducted between 2011 and 2012, where Macchiarini implanted synthetic tracheas treated with stem cells. This investigation concluded that these procedures were undertaken without sufficient prior research and lacked medical justification as life-saving interventions. The tragic outcomes included the deaths of two patients, with a third remaining hospitalized. In response to the crisis, former head of Karolinska's ethics committee, Bo Risberg, proposed a two-year suspension of the Nobel Prize in Medicine, with the prize money to be reallocated as compensation for the families of Macchiarini's patients.

The Nobel Prize's scientific integrity is facing intense scrutiny due to the Paolo Macchiarini scandal. This deeply troubling affair, involving patient fatalities and damage to the Nobel Prize's reputation, centers on Macchiarini's experimental work with biosynthetic tracheas. The scandal has prompted the resignations of two key figures in the Nobel Assembly.

Here's a breakdown of the key issues:

*   **Experimental Trachea Transplants:** Macchiarini was the first surgeon to attempt an artificial trachea transplant. While promising, the procedure proved tragic for some patients.
*   **Patient Outcomes:** Two of the three patients who received this experimental treatment have died. A third patient has remained in intensive care since their 2012 surgery.
*   **Legal Investigations:** Swedish prosecutors are investigating Macchiarini for potential involuntary manslaughter charges, allegations the surgeon denies.

In light of these devastating outcomes, the Nobel Assembly, responsible for selecting the Nobel laureate in Physiology or Medicine, requested the resignations of:

*   Harriet Wallberg-Henriksson
*   Anders Hamsten

Both are former vice chancellors of the Karolinska Institute, a prominent Swedish medical university linked to the Karolinska University Hospital where Macchiarini performed his controversial surgeries. Further repercussions include Helene Hellmark Knutsson, Sweden's Minister of Higher Education and Research, announcing Wallberg-Henriksson's removal as chancellor for all public universities. Minister Knutsson also called for the resignation of all Karolinska Institute board members who served during Macchiarini's tenure. Reuters reported that any board members who do not step down will be replaced.

The Karolinska Institute terminated Macchiarini's employment in March, following two highly critical reports detailing significant failings in his conduct and the institute's oversight. Minister Knutsson described the situation as a profound "scandal," stating, "People have been harmed because of the actions of the Karolinska Institute and also the Karolinska University Hospital."

Initially, Macchiarini was hailed by the media as a "super-scientist." In 2011, he gained attention for his work in regenerative medicine, performing a groundbreaking procedure implanting a "bioartificial" trachea, made from plastic and stem cells, into Andemariam Beyene. This was presented as a major advancement. Beyene told The New York Times he was initially hesitant, admitting, "I almost refused. It had only been done in pigs. But [Macchiarini] convinced me in a very scientific way." Thomson Reuters BioWorld likened his approach to "science fiction," and The New York Times called it "a first." Tragically, this pioneering spirit was overshadowed by a devastating outcome. In January 2014, the trachea implanted by Macchiarini dislodged, leading to Beyene's death, as reported by the Iceland Review.

Macchiarini's name reappeared in headlines in 2015 due to an independent review revealing he had exaggerated his achievements. The Washington Post noted the investigator's conclusion: Macchiarini engaged in scientific misconduct by omitting or fabricating patient outcomes to portray the procedure as more successful. The scope of his alleged misdeeds was extensive. A report released shortly before highlighted that only one of the three patients had signed a consent form, despite all being capable. This single consent form, the report critically noted, "would not have been approved" as the patient had not consulted an independent medical expert, a crucial ethical safeguard.

Further critical findings from reports include:

*   **Varied Materials:** A different synthetic material was used in each transplant, suggesting a lack of thorough research into material effectiveness and a disturbing lack of readiness for human application. The report concluded: "Too little was known about the material in order for it to be able to begin to be used in patients."
*   **Unauthorized Drugs:** Growth-stimulating drugs were administered in at least two surgeries without authorization from the Swedish Medical Products Agency, a clear violation of protocols.

A separate report, led by Sten Heckscher, former president of Sweden's Supreme Administrative Court, also scrutinized the Karolinska Institute, stating it bore "a certain responsibility for the transplantation." The report noted, "There are many instances of KI employees being involved in the discussions preceding and following up surgery. KI has also, in several contexts, cited the transplantations as part of its own activities. For example, they have been quoted as research successes in KI’s evaluations of how research funding has been utilized." This implied the Karolinska Institute should not have hired Macchiarini, especially given negative references regarding his past. The report stated, "KI received remarkably negative references, including information that Macchiarini had been blocked from a professorship in Italy, that there were doubts surrounding his research and that his CV contained falsehoods."

Administrative oversight was also uncovered: the hospital extended Macchiarini's contract twice, in 2013 and 2015, without evaluating his work. Instead, "the department management asked Macchiarini to describe his work himself." This self-generated description was then used for the contract extension proposal, a process lacking independent verification.

Bo Risberg, professor emeritus of surgery at the University of Gothenburg, told Science that the decision to ask Hamsten and Wallberg-Henriksson to resign was appropriate. Risberg highlighted the Nobel Prize's connection to the Karolinska Institute, calling the situation "the biggest scandal we have ever had in Swedish medicine."

Swedish prosecutors continue to investigate Macchiarini for potential involuntary manslaughter charges related to the deaths of the two patients. Macchiarini has not commented on these latest reports but has consistently disputed all charges, as previously reported by the Associated Press.

The **Nobel Prize for Medicine selection committee** is in **deep trouble**. Two judges are being asked to step down. This is because of how a **scandal** involving surgeon **Dr. Paolo Macchiarini** was handled. Dr. Macchiarini was once a leader in stem cell therapy. The judges, **Anders Hamsten** and **Harriet Wallberg**, are accused of ignoring **warnings about Dr. Macchiarini's bad behavior** when they were in charge at Sweden's Karolinska Institute.

Dr. Macchiarini made **untrue statements** and was praised for **windpipe transplants**. However, he was fired from the Karolinska Institute. This was due to claims of **carelessness, faking research, and lying on his resume**. Now, prosecutors are looking into charges of **involuntary manslaughter and serious negligence** against him. These charges are tied to the **deaths of two patients**. The scandal has already led to Hamsten losing his job as vice-chancellor and Wallberg her position at the Swedish Higher Education Authority. Both are expected to **leave the Nobel Prize for Medicine panel**.

Dr. Macchiarini worked at the Karolinska Institute from 2010 until March. His time there ended after a film showed he **tested new methods on patients**. These were **experimental techniques**. Out of eight patients who got these artificial windpipe transplants, **six passed away**. A Karolinska representative admitted the "very sad results." However, Dr. Macchiarini **says he did nothing wrong**.

This whole affair has led to demands for **big changes**. One idea is to **stop the Nobel Prize for Medicine for two years**. The prize money would then go to **help the families of those who died**. This situation brings up **important issues about how to watch over and hold accountable** top research groups.

The crawl of outlinks from wikipedia.org began in March 2016. These files are not currently public. This collection has unique features and represents a major effort after a long break since our last similar project.

We made several important changes for this specific collection. First, we turned off duplicate detection. This helps make sure the dataset is complete, in case it's released later. It's complicated to share data with links to different, random collections, so we focused on one unified dataset.

Second, this project was the first to include all the different Wikipedia wikis. Before, we only focused on the English Wikipedia (enwiki). For this complete collection, we carefully built the seed list from all 865 available Wikipedia collections. This created a wider and more inclusive data base.

“B—P 4 EVER” Then NBC television producer Benita Alexander and Dr. Paolo Macchiarini glide toward the Doge’s Palace, on the left, and the Bridge of Sighs (visible beyond the pedestrian walkway), Venice, 2013. They met during the making of an NBC News special about the doctor’s work. He’s the doctor who does the seemingly impossible, going where no other has yet dared. —Meredith Vieira I. A Most Interesting Man The first meeting between Benita Alexander, an award-winning producer for NBC News, and Dr. Paolo Macchiarini, the famous transplant surgeon, took place at the bar at Boston’s Mandarin Oriental hotel. It was February 2013, shortly before Macchiarini would have his initial interview with Meredith Vieira for a two-hour NBC special called A Leap of Faith. Macchiarini, 57, is a magnet for superlatives. He is commonly referred to as “world-renowned” and a “super-surgeon.” He is credited with medical miracles, including the world’s first synthetic organ transplant, which involved fashioning a trachea, or windpipe, out of plastic and then coating it with a patient’s own stem cells. That feat, in 2011, appeared to solve two of medicine’s more intractable problems—organ rejection and the lack of donor organs—and brought with it major media exposure for Macchiarini and his employer, Stockholm’s Karolinska Institute, home of the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. Macchiarini was now planning another first: a synthetic-trachea transplant on a child, a two-year-old Korean-Canadian girl named Hannah Warren, who had spent her entire life in a Seoul hospital. Macchiarini had come to Vieira’s attention in September 2012, when she read a front-page New York Times story about the doctor. She turned to Alexander, one of her most seasoned and levelheaded producers, to look into a regenerative-medicine story for television. With blue eyes and raven hair, Alexander seems younger than her 49 years. Though she brims with confidence, friends say she bears the scars of a turbulent childhood in Huntington Woods, Michigan. In her own telling, just shy of her 16th birthday, she returned home from a sleepover to discover that her mother had left the family. Two years later, her father, who by then had married a neighbor, asked her to pack up and leave. Alexander overcame her upbringing and in 1987 graduated magna cum laude from Wayne State University with a degree in journalism. She spent the early 1990s working at a string of local television stations and briefly taught journalism at her alma mater. After she met and married fellow reporter John Noel, the two moved to New York City, where she joined NBC’s Dateline. In 2003, Alexander gave birth to a daughter, Jessina. Alexander and Noel divorced in 2009, and in 2012 she married a ballroom dance instructor named Edson Jeune. Over the years, Alexander has worked with NBC’s top talent—Tom Brokaw, Matt Lauer, and Ann Curry, as well as with Vieira—and earned many accolades, including two Emmys as well as the Edward R. Murrow Award, the Alfred I. duPont-Columbia University Award, and the Society of Professional Journalists’ Sigma Delta Chi Award. Now Alexander sat across from Macchiarini at Bar Boulud, in the Mandarin Oriental. At the time, Alexander’s first husband, Noel, was hospitalized with glioblastoma, an aggressive form of brain cancer, and she would in time begin sharing details about his condition—as well as about her dissatisfaction with her second marriage. “Having worked with so many patients who are terminally ill, Paolo was immensely helpful as far as helping me navigate my complicated emotions,” she explained when I spoke with her this fall. He also suggested ways to talk about the matter with her daughter. “He was an amazing friend to me during that time, and a solid, reliable pillar of strength. He spent hours listening to me talk about it all and offering gentle advice.” (Disclosure: I worked as a producer at NBC News from 2004 to 2009. I did not meet Alexander until I contacted her in 2015.) Photograph by Gina LeVay. When Alexander and Macchiarini found themselves together in Illinois for a period of weeks in the spring of 2013—brought there by the NBC special—they met frequently for quiet dinners. The trachea transplant on Hannah Warren, the Korean-Canadian girl, was being performed at Children’s Hospital of Illinois, in Peoria, and the procedure was fraught with risks, not least because Macchiarini’s technique was still a work in progress even for adults. (Christopher Lyles, an American who became the second person to receive an artificial trachea, died less than four months after his surgery at Karolinska.) “He’s a brilliant scientist and a great technical surgeon,” said Dr. Richard Pearl, who operated alongside Macchiarini in Illinois. Like others, Pearl described his Italian colleague as a Renaissance man, fluent in half a dozen languages. Another person, who would get to know him through Alexander, compared Macchiarini to “the Most Interesting Man in the World,” the character made famous in Dos Equis beer commercials. In Peoria, Macchiarini’s medical magic appeared to have its limitations. Hannah Warren died from post-surgical complications less than three months after the transplant. Her anatomy “was much more challenging than we realized,” Pearl recounted. “Scientifically, the operation itself worked. It was just a shame what happened. When you’re doing something for the first time, you don’t have a textbook. It was the hardest operation I’ve ever scrubbed on.” II. Crossing the Line The personal relationship between Alexander and Macchiarini continued to blossom. In June 2013, they flew to Venice for what Alexander called “an incredibly romantic weekend.” Macchiarini bought her red roses and Venetian-glass earrings and took her on a gondola ride under the Bridge of Sighs. Like a pair of teenagers, they attached love locks to the Ponte dell’Accademia bridge, one of them bearing the inscription “B—P 23/6/13, 4 Ever.” Alexander told me that, “when he took me to Venice, we were still shooting the story … He always paid for everything … gifts, expensive dinners, flowers—the works. When it came to money, he was incredibly generous.” It is a bedrock principle at NBC and every other news organization that journalists must avoid conflicts of interest, real or apparent. Alexander was not oblivious to this. “I knew that I was crossing the line in the sense that it’s a basic and well-understood rule of journalism that you don’t become involved with one of the subjects of your story, because your objectivity could clearly become compromised,” she told me. “I never once thought about him paying for the trip as him ‘buying’ me in some fashion, or potentially using money to influence me, because, from my perspective anyway … that just wasn’t the case. We were just crazy about each other, and I was falling in love.” While Alexander insists that she tried to put the relationship on hold after Venice, she flew to see Macchiarini in Stockholm two weeks later. “Our nights were always spent together, and always romantic in one way or another,” she said. Macchiarini was in Stockholm to attend to Yesim Cetir, a 25-year-old Turkish woman whose artificial trachea had failed. As Swedish television later reported, “It has taken nearly 100 surgeries to support the cell tissue around the airpipes. Her breathing is bad, and to avoid suffocation, her respiratory tract must be cleansed from mucus every fourth hour. She has now been lying in the hospital for nearly 1,000 days.” NBC’s special would come to include skeptical commentary from Dr. Joseph Vacanti, who questioned the sufficiency of Macchiarini’s research, but Cetir’s post-operative complications were not mentioned. By October 2013, when Macchiarini and Alexander flew to Europe for another romantic getaway, she had in her own mind reconciled her personal and professional behavior. “The story was basically done by the time we went to London. It was all little tweaking after that, nothing significant, and so I totally separated Paolo and work in my head,” she explained. “I was in love and because I had made a very personal decision to take a leap of faith for love, I never looked back.” Should she have informed her friend and mentor, Meredith Vieira? “I knew I was crossing the line at work,” Alexander said, “and I made a very conscious decision not to tell anybody else at work what I was doing.” III. Breakthrough in Spain Paolo Macchiarini was born in Switzerland to Italian parents and has spoken of having had a difficult childhood in Basel, where by his own account he felt like a perpetual outsider in school. He attended the University of Pisa, where he would earn a medical degree with a specialization in surgery. During his studies, Alexander recalled Macchiarini telling her, he had received a call from his father, who complained of feeling unwell. The aspiring doctor examined his father but could find nothing wrong and returned to the university. His father died shortly thereafter. It was a moment, Alexander said, that has forever haunted him. In 1986, Macchiarini started a family of his own, marrying an Italian woman, Emanuela Pecchia, with whom he had a daughter and a son. From the collection of Benita Alexander. Over time, Macchiarini developed a certain skepticism about his homeland. “After I had graduated and specialised in thoracic surgery,” he was quoted saying in The Irish Times, in 2008, “I wanted to enter university to continue my studies in that field. I was blocked, I was told not to apply for the job because the result, even before the interviews, had already been decided. There were the usual raccomandati [those with pull] in the queue in front of me.” The Italian system, he told the British medical journal The Lancet in 2012, “favours people who are linked to the politics or are sons of sons but not the merits. I knew that in other countries this was not the fact. So I left.” In 1990 he traded Italy for America, where, according to his curriculum vitae, he did a fellowship in thoracic surgery at the University of Alabama at Birmingham. Macchiarini’s peripatetic studies continued in Besançon, France, where, according to his C.V., he earned a master’s of science and a Ph.D. in organ and tissue transplantation. According to another C.V., he earned a master’s in biostatistics in Alabama and a Ph.D. in life and health science in Besançon. All told, he had a distinguished medical pedigree. He then plunged headlong into academia, starting in France, where according to his C.V.’s he joined the University of Paris—Sud with an “accreditation to Full Professor.” A full professor in Europe is comparable to what Americans call a tenured professor, meaning the individual in question has obtained the highest academic rank at a given institution and has been accorded the job protection and other benefits that go with it. But as Macchiarini told The Lancet in 2012, he was restless: “I think if you stay in a single place for your entire life you restrict your capacity…. In 10 years it came to a point where I was adult, and I needed to go away to express my creativity.” So in 2000 or 2001—depending on which C.V. one consults—he became a full professor at Hannover Medical School, in Germany. Even Germany seemed too confining for Macchiarini, and he moved to Spain, where in 2005, according to one C.V., he became a professore ordinario, a full professor, and where he would continue to maintain a residence. Macchiarini’s wide-ranging academic appointments seemed to prepare him well for his star turn. In June 2008, he performed a trachea transplant using a donor organ seeded with stem cells. The operation in Barcelona on a 30-year-old mother of two, Claudia Lorena Castillo Sanchez, was heralded in the press as the “dawn of the stem-cell revolution.” By replacing cadaverous cells with autologous stem cells (that is, those harvested from the patient’s bone marrow), the technique held out the promise of minimizing organ rejection and reliance on powerful immunosuppressive drugs. Macchiarini himself called the operation “a major achievement in the history of medicine.” From the collection of Benita Alexander. The breakthrough in Spain caught the eye of officials back in Italy, who, concerned that the country was experiencing a brain drain, sought his return. A headline in La Repubblica summarized the turn of events neatly: ROSSI PHONES MACCHIARINI: “COME AND OPERATE WITH US”—referring to Enrico Rossi, then Tuscany’s top health official, who would later become the region’s president. Rossi lured Macchiarini back with a large and prestigious package: a state-sponsored laboratory, the chance to showcase his innovative surgical techniques at Florence’s Careggi Hospital, and a full professorship at the university to which it is connected. Italian law, however, required proof of equivalency: in order to appoint a full professor without an open competition, the university had to show that the candidate had held an equivalent post—that is, a full professorship—at a comparable institution, whether in Italy or abroad. Given his fame, his political connections, and his ample academic credentials, the star surgeon was regarded as a shoo-in, and in late 2009, Dr. Gian Franco Gensini, the dean of the faculty of medicine, assembled a special commission to, in the words of one participant, “rubber-stamp” Macchiarini’s appointment. But in the end, Macchiarini never got the full professorship. He operated at Careggi for a few years and then moved on to posts at the Karolinska Institute and Kuban State Medical University in Russia. IV. Paolo, Putin, and the Pope Macchiarini proposed to Benita Alexander on Christmas Day 2013, Alexander said. In the months leading up to the airing of A Leap of Faith, in June 2014, Macchiarini and Alexander went on trips to the Bahamas, Turkey, Mexico, Greece, and Italy. They went on shopping sprees and ate their way through Michelin-starred restaurants. Macchiarini even took Alexander and her daughter to meet his mother at her home, in Lucca. “She cooked homemade gnocchi,” Alexander recalled. Macchiarini’s mother shared pictures from the family photo album while her son translated. Emanuela Pecchia, the woman whom Macchiarini had married years earlier, lived only a short distance away. When Macchiarini informed Alexander, during a dinner cruise later that summer, that his divorce had finally come through, she recounted, he gave her an engagement ring. Macchiarini could be secretive at times. After his Christmas proposal, he told Alexander that he could not stick around for New Year’s because he was on call for what, she said, he termed an “emergency V.I.P. surgery.” When she pressed him for details, he swore her to silence before telling her, as she recalled, that he was part of a “highly classified group of doctors from around the world who cater to the world’s V.I.P.’s.” She said Macchiarini over time revealed that he had operated on Bill and Hillary Clinton, Emperor Akihito of Japan, and President Obama. People who spent time with the couple said they heard Macchiarini talk about his high-level connections. An NBC colleague, Alisha Cowan-Vieira (no relation to Meredith Vieira), recalled, “I saw a lot of text messages between Benita and Paolo, and she would say, ‘OMG, look what he just told me.’ The texts would say, ‘I just left a meeting with PF [Pope Francis]’ or with Bill Clinton or the Obamas.” Shortly after NBC aired A Leap of Faith, Alexander met Meredith Vieira for lunch at the Modern, an airy restaurant at the Museum of Modern Art. In the dining room overlooking the Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden, with its works by Miró, Matisse, and Picasso, Alexander said, she told her boss for the first time about her relationship with Paolo and that it had begun while the story was still in production. She said that, while she preferred to keep their conversation private, Vieira was taken aback. “I perfectly understand her reaction,” Alexander said. “There’s no two ways about it. I crossed the line.” A source close to Vieira confirmed that Alexander disclosed the relationship at this lunch but, by this account, had assured Vieira that it began only after production wrapped. In the months that followed, the doctor and his fiancée began planning their wedding in earnest. They set a date for July 11, 2015, in Rome. But their desire to marry in the Catholic Church was complicated by the fact that she is Episcopalian and divorced. Divorce would have been an issue for Macchiarini as well. However, Alexander said, Macchiarini insisted that he would fix things by visiting his friend and patient in the Vatican. In October 2014, Alexander recalled, Macchiarini told her that he had met with Pope Francis for four hours and that the Pontiff consented to the couple’s marriage and, in yet another sign of his progressive tenure, vowed to officiate. Alexander said Macchiarini referred to himself as Pope Francis’s “personal doctor” and maintained that in subsequent meetings his patient offered to host the wedding at his summer residence, the Apostolic Palace of Castel Gandolfo. A recommendation letter written by Dr. Mark Holterman—who, along with Dr. Pearl, operated on Hannah Warren in Peoria—suggests that Macchiarini’s Vatican connections were well known: When Pope John Paul II was dying and having trouble breathing from advanced Parkinson’s Disease, Professor Macchiarini was called in to provide an urgent consultation for the pontiff. The decision to not perform an urgent tracheostomy was jointly made between the doctor and his Holy patient. When Professor Macchiarini renders an opinion on all things involving the diseased airway, people listen. He remains among the world’s elite airway surgeons. Over lunch in New York at the restaurant Print, in Hell’s Kitchen, on February 13, 2015, Macchiarini spoke in depth about the wedding plans with Matthew Christopher, a designer who has dressed everyone from Broadway and television star Kristin Chenoweth to World Cup darling Carli Lloyd. He was already hard at work on Benita Alexander’s elaborate wedding gown and three additional dresses for the various functions that were planned. Macchiarini “was totally polished. Very much Mr. Big,” Christopher recalled, referring to the larger-than-life Sex and the City character. According to David Marchi, Christopher’s husband and P.R. chief, who was also at the lunch, “He told us the wedding would take place at the Pope’s summer residence and because of the enormous security—with the Pope and all these heads of state—that the planning between Matthew and Benita had to be precise. The Pope was going to let Benita use his special carriage. We discussed how to get Benita’s dress into this carriage with enough time so that Matthew can get her in, run to the church, and get in the one door before things were locked for security.” Among the V.I.P.’s who Macchiarini said were planning to attend the wedding were Russia’s Vladimir Putin, President and Mrs. Obama, Bill and Hillary Clinton, and France’s Nicolas Sarkozy. Macchiarini also told people that Andrea Bocelli would sing during the service. As for food, Macchiarini was apparently not sparing any expense: Florence’s Enoteca Pinchiorri, with its three Michelin stars, was catering the affair. Toward the end of their February lunch, Macchiarini asked Alexander to leave the table so he could speak privately with Christopher and Marchi. In a hushed tone, the doctor told them that Pope Francis wanted them to participate more fully in the wedding ceremony. “I almost fell off my chair,” recounted Marchi, whose Catholic parents came to the U.S. from Italy. “Growing up, I was always looking for this transformative moment. So when we were told we would take confession and Communion from the Pope as two gay married men, that was it. For me it was almost as if God said, ‘You’ve been waiting for this moment—here it is.’ It was very emotional. Matthew and I started crying.” Alexander and Macchiarini returned from the lunch to her apartment in Brooklyn to find that the wedding invitations had arrived. Sheathed in lambskin and engraved with the initials B&P, the invitations were addressed to, among others, the Obamas, the Clintons, the Putins, the Sarkozys, Andrea Bocelli, Kofi Annan, Russell Crowe, Elton John, John Legend, Kenny Rogers, Meredith Vieira, and His Holiness Pope Francis. By this time, Alexander had met with David Corvo, who as NBC’s senior executive producer for prime-time news was ultimately responsible for A Leap of Faith. Over lunch at Michael’s, Alexander, by her own account, revealed that she and Macchiarini had been together while the story was in production. Alexander recalled that she gave Corvo details about the wedding, including the Pope’s participation. He sent her an e-mail a few weeks later. “Congrats that this is all coming together,” he wrote. “With invitations going out, I need to tell [NBC News president] Deborah Turness. Is everything remaining confidential? She’ll of course want to discuss coverage soonest. (And exclusivity, as you would expect.) Again, congrats.” Alexander says that she and Corvo met again on March 16 and that they discussed how NBC could best cover the wedding. A source close to Corvo confirmed that Alexander told him about the relationship during the lunch at Michael’s but, according to the source, had assured Corvo that it had begun only after the reporting and production were finished. The source added that Corvo was always skeptical about the Pope’s involvement, made no plans to cover the wedding, and did not tell Deborah Turness about the possibility. By May, when according to Alexander her superiors were aware that she, as the producer of A Leap of Faith, had been and continued to be romantically involved with the story’s subject, NBC had submitted the program for an Emmy Award. Sources close to NBC say that Vieira and Corvo were not aware of how early the relationship began until approached by Vanity Fair. NBC News says that, if any new information reported in this story is relevant to its production of A Leap of Faith, it will update it accordingly online, in keeping with its standards practices. In anticipation of a move to Europe, Alexander on May 13 left her job at NBC and notified her daughter’s school that she would not be coming back. She received a glowing video tribute from Vieira: I first met Benita nine years ago. We were asked to cover a story—a heartbreaking story—about a beautiful high-school student who had lost her life in Colorado. And you learn a lot about someone when you’re in the trenches with them doing that kind of story. I learned that Benita is a fabulous producer. I learned that she is a brilliant writer. But most importantly, I learned that she is an incredibly sensitive and wonderful human being who understands others and wants to connect with them in a very deep and profound way. And ever since that story, every time I was asked, “Is there a particular producer you want?,” I would say, “Please, please let me work with Benita.” I love her tremendously. Not just as a professional but also as a dear friend. And anybody given the opportunity to work with her would be crazy to say no. Run to Benita. Don’t walk. Run to Benita. I wish her the best and I know she that she will do extremely well in her new life in Barcelona. From the collection of Benita Alexander. V. The Reckoning The very next day, May 14, Alexander received an e-mail from a friend. The subject line read simply: “The Pope.” It included a link to an article detailing Vatican plans for Pope Francis to visit South America in July—at the very time when he was supposed to be officiating at her wedding. In that instant, the bottom fell out. A few weeks later, Alexander would send an e-mail to invited guests in 17 countries, canceling the wedding. Many had already purchased flights, booked hotels, and bought new clothes for what everyone expected to be a wedding for the ages.
