Alexander recalled that Macchiarini tried to blame the scheduling mix-up on Vatican politics and claimed that he was on his way to Rome to straighten things out. He maintained that her fears were unfounded—that he was acting in good faith and that everything would work out as planned. He said the Pope would be cutting his trip short and returning early. Alexander was unconvinced. She confronted a painful reality. “I just didn’t want to put two and two together,” she said. “I didn’t want Paolo to not be the man I believed him to be. I didn’t want the fairy tale to end.” After canceling the wedding, she e-mailed Macchiarini: “I believed you were exactly who you presented yourself to be, to me, to my friends and family, to the world. Congratulations. You charmed me, and all of us, into la la land. I will never, ever understand how you could have done this to me, or to Jessie. Who the hell are you and what the hell is wrong with you?” As Alexander would discover with the help of a private investigator named Frank Murphy, virtually every detail Macchiarini provided about the wedding was false. A review of public records in Italy would also seem to indicate that Macchiarini remains married to Emanuela Pecchia, his wife of nearly 30 years. Murphy, who spent 15 years as a Pennsylvania State Police detective, told me, “I’ve never in my experience witnessed a fraud like this, with this level of international flair…. The fact that he could keep all the details straight and compartmentalize these different lives and lies is really amazing.” Alexander produced e-mails and WhatsApp chats to support her account of Macchiarini’s claims of a relationship with the Pope.In a statement to Vanity Fair, Father Federico Lombardi, director of the Holy See’s press office, was adamant: “There is no ‘personal doctor’ of the Pope with [the] name ‘Macchiarini.’ The Pope has surely never promised to officiate a wedding of ‘Macchiarini’ and does not know someone with such [a] name. On 11th July the Pope was travelling in Latin America and this was on his agenda long time before July … This is enough.” Dr. Mark Holterman, who had written the recommendation letter citing Macchiarini’s treatment of Pope John Paul II, acknowledged to Vanity Fair that “this was a vignette related to me by Prof. Macchiarini,” adding that he had relied “solely on [Macchiarini’s] word.” Andrea Bocelli’s wife and manager, Veronica Berti, laughed when asked if her husband had agreed to serenade the couple: “He was not booked to sing at a wedding. He doesn’t sing at people’s weddings. Castel Gandolfo? Absolutely not!” Annie Féolde, Enoteca Pinchiorri’s flamboyant co-owner, told me that they were never contacted about, much less contracted for, a wedding on July 11, and that they had never heard of Paolo Macchiarini. To understand why someone of considerable stature could construct such elaborate tales and how he could seemingly make others believe them, I turned to Dr. Ronald Schouten, a Harvard professor who directs the Law and Psychiatry Service at Massachusetts General Hospital. “We’re taught from an early age that when something is too good to be true, it’s not true,” he said. “And yet we ignore the signals. People’s critical judgment gets suspended. In this case, that happened at both the personal and institutional level.” Though he will not diagnose from a distance, Schouten, who is one of the nation’s foremost authorities on psychopathy, observed, “Macchiarini is the extreme form of a con man. He’s clearly bright and has accomplishments, but he can’t contain himself. There’s a void in his personality that he seems to want to fill by conning more and more people.” When I asked how Macchiarini stacks up to, say, Bernie Madoff, he laughed and said, “Madoff was an ordinary con man with a Ponzi scheme. He never claimed to be the chairman of the Federal Reserve. He didn’t suggest he was part of a secret international society of bankers. This guy is really good.” Photograph by Gina LeVay. VI. Behind the Résumé There had been warning signs. In 2012, Macchiarini was charged in Italy with attempted aggravated fraud related to alleged efforts to persuade very ill patients to undergo radical surgery at facilities where Macchiarini had privileges—and where he presumably had a more favorable financial arrangement than at Careggi Hospital. The case is still in the courts, though some of the more serious charges have been dropped. Moreover, in the medical community, it remains a matter of vigorous debate—with claims and counterclaims over the sufficiency and quality of research—as to how well his synthetic tracheas actually work. And then there was the matter of the post which Macchiarini had been offered in Florence, but which for some reason he was never given. What had that been about? During a recent visit to Florence, I spoke with several members of the special commission convened by Careggi Hospital to review Macchiarini’s appointment. The findings had not been made public. They said that things got off to a bad start when one of their colleagues, who had attended the University of Pisa at the same time as Macchiarini, called him out for claiming to have been an associate professor at Pisa when, in fact, he had not been a professor there at all. However, since the “false statement” did not bear on the central question of whether Macchiarini had ever been a full professor—at Pisa or somewhere else—Macchiarini was allowed to submit a corrected C.V. Chaired by a respected general surgeon, the Careggi commission—which included a cardiovascular-and-thoracic surgeon, a pharmacologist, and a stem-cell researcher—tried to verify Macchiarini’s odyssey through academia, scouring his new C.V. and contacting their counterparts in France, Germany, and Spain. While acknowledging that Macchiarini seemed to enjoy a solid reputation as a surgeon and a researcher, one commissioner, Dr. Pietro Tonelli, concluded, “Before coming to Careggi, he had never been a full professor.” Said another commissioner, Dr. Clemente Crisci, “Not only had he never been an ordinary [full] professor in the Italian sense of the term, he had never even been an associate professor in the Italian sense of the term.” When I asked whether this was a matter of semantics, another commissioner with whom I met in Florence said unequivocally, “Absolutely not. He was living in these places for several years. He knew exactly what the titles meant.” Continuing, he told me, “Look at Spain and Italy: ‘professore ordinario’ means ‘full professor.’ ‘Professore associato’ means ‘associate professor.’ This wasn’t a language issue. Same with the other countries. He knew what ‘P.D.’ and ‘H.D.R.’ meant,” referring to Privatdozent and habilitation à diriger la recherche, which one of Macchiarini’s C.V.’s erroneously defines as “accreditation to Full Professor” in German and French, respectively. Vanity Fair contacted many of the schools at which Macchiarini claimed to have either earned a degree or held an academic post. While the University of Pisa confirmed that he indeed received an M.D. and had specialized in surgery, the University of Alabama at Birmingham denied that Macchiarini earned a master’s in biostatistics or that he participated in a two-year fellowship in thoracic surgery. In fact, according to U.A.B. spokesman Bob Shepard, the only record the school has for Macchiarini indicates that he did a six-month non-surgical fellowship in hematology/oncology—which according to the current Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education guidelines is 30 months shy of what is required for a clinical fellowship in that field. The University of Paris—Sud never responded to repeated requests for comment, but Hannover Medical School wrote to say that Macchiarini had been neither a full nor an associate professor there, merely an adjunct. The Careggi commission’s January 2010 conclusion that Macchiarini had misrepresented his professional history put Dean Gensini in a tight spot. Faced with the choice between antagonizing a political master (Rossi) or violating Italian administrative law, commissioners tell me, he chose a middle course. Macchiarini would not receive a professorship and the commission’s full findings would not be released. As a result, over the years, Macchiarini’s stature only grew. (Gensini did not respond to requests for comment.) VII. The Other Shoe

The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine Awarded to 214 Nobel Laureates since 1901 "The said interest shall be divided into five equal parts, which shall be apportioned as follows: /- - -/ one part to the person who shall have made the most important discovery within the domain of physiology or medicine ..." (Excerpt from the will of Alfred Nobel) Alfred Nobel had an active interest in medical research. Through Karolinska Institutet, he came into contact with Swedish physiologist Jöns Johansson around 1890. Johansson worked in Nobel's laboratory in Sevran, France during a brief period the same year. Physiology or medicine was the third prize area Nobel mentioned in his will. The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine is awarded by the Nobel Assembly at Karolinska Institutet, Stockholm, Sweden.

### Nobel Prize Committee Under Fire Amid Transplant Surgeon Scandal

The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, a highly esteemed award presented by Sweden's Karolinska Institute, is currently facing a significant scandal. The controversy stems from the involvement of Nobel committee members with the work of Italian transplant surgeon Paolo Macchiarini. Two members of the Nobel Assembly at the Karolinska Institute have been asked to step down due to their association with Macchiarini's controversial procedures. Reports indicate that any remaining board members who have not yet resigned will be dismissed.

#### The Rise and Fall of Paolo Macchiarini

At the center of this scandal is Paolo Macchiarini, an Italian surgeon once celebrated for developing and implanting bioengineered windpipes using patients' own stem cells. However, his groundbreaking work took a tragic turn when two patients died following his procedures. Macchiarini faces accusations of scientific misconduct, claims he continues to deny.

The two scientists asked to resign, Anders Hamsten and Harriet Wallberg, are believed to have overlooked earlier warnings about Macchiarini's practices. A report from the Karolinska Institute itself highlighted that "the red flags were there from the beginning." These concerns included inaccuracies in Macchiarini's resume, publications with "questionable" data, and a lack of collaboration with other researchers. Hamsten served as vice chancellor of the Karolinska Institute during the initial investigations that cleared Macchiarini of misconduct, while Wallberg was the university's president when he was hired.

Karin Dahlman-Wright, acting vice chancellor of the Karolinska Institute, stated in a news release, "This is a case in which a long chain of poor and absent decisions enabled a visiting professor to contravene the rules and ethical principles."

#### Ethical Breaches and Unstable Science

An external investigation recently released revealed that the three patients who received experimental trachea transplants from Macchiarini at the Karolinska University Hospital were not sufficiently ill to ethically justify such experimental surgeries. Furthermore, the report indicated that the scientific basis for these procedures was too unstable for human subjects, and there were "clear weaknesses in how the informed consent was obtained."

#### A Pattern of Negligence and Renewed Contracts

A second case summary, presented this week, further detailed how the Karolinska Institute hired Macchiarini despite these significant warning signs. His contracts were reportedly renewed twice "without any evaluation of his activities," and the handling of misconduct investigations was severely flawed.

The seriousness of this scandal has prompted Bo Risberg, a former head of ethics at Karolinska, to suggest that no Nobel Prize in medicine should be awarded for at least two years, according to The Telegraph. Risberg proposes that the prize money instead be directed to the families of the patients subjected to Macchiarini's experiments.

STOCKHOLM (AP) — The Nobel Assembly, the esteemed body that chooses the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, is taking decisive steps to confront a scandal linked to a renowned stem cell researcher. In response to the controversy surrounding Dr. Paolo Macchiarini, a disgraced scientist at Stockholm's Karolinska Institute, two members of the Nobel Assembly will be removed.

Nobel Assembly secretary Thomas Perlmann informed the Swedish news agency TT that Harriet Wallberg and Anders Hamsten will be requested to resign from the 50-member committee. This action precedes the assembly's upcoming announcement of the prestigious award next month.

Key developments in this situation include:

*   **Dismissal of Members:** Harriet Wallberg and Anders Hamsten are being asked to step down from the Nobel Assembly.
*   **Previous Departures:** Both Wallberg and Hamsten had already left senior roles at the Karolinska Institute.
*   **Macchiarini Controversy:** Their exits followed significant criticism of the institute's management of scientific misconduct allegations against Dr. Macchiarini.
*   **Researcher's Fall:** Dr. Macchiarini, once celebrated for his work on windpipe transplants, was dismissed after claims of resume falsification and research misrepresentation surfaced.
*   **Ongoing Investigation:** Prosecutors are currently investigating Dr. Macchiarini for involuntary manslaughter, related to the deaths of two patients. He denies all charges and maintains his innocence.

query: What specific criticisms did Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand have regarding President Obama's communication about health care plans affected by the Affordable Care Act?

documents.text: President Barack Obama should have been clearer about what health care plans would be discontinued as part of the Affordable Care Act implementation, Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand said Sunday. Appearing on ABC's "This Week," the New York Democrat offered a stirring defense of the law, if not the administration’s implementation efforts. “He should have just been more specific,” Gillibrand said. “The point is, if you are being offered a terrible health care plan that the minute you get sick you have to go into bankruptcy, those plans should never be offered, he should have just been specific.” (Understanding Obamacare: POLITICO’s guide to the ACA) Yet when asked why 39 House Democrats voted with Republicans to force a legislative fix to legalize existing health care plans, Gillibrand said: “They’re just responding to the worries of their constituents.” Read more about: Kirsten Gillibrand, Obamacare

Wyoming Senate candidate Liz Cheney has again voiced her consistent opposition to same-sex marriage. This stance persists even as her own sister, Mary Cheney, is married to a woman.

On "Fox News Sunday," Liz Cheney remarked, "I love Mary very much, I love her family very much. This is just an issue on which we disagree."

Cheney, whose father is former Vice President Dick Cheney, is currently in a primary contest against Republican Senator Mike Enzi. She reiterated her opposition to a constitutional amendment that would prohibit same-sex marriage.

Despite her views on marriage, Cheney also stated that people should be safeguarded from discrimination due to their sexual orientation. Nevertheless, she firmly holds that marriage should be exclusively between a man and a woman. Cheney concluded, "I believe in the traditional definition of marriage."

Wisconsin Governor Scott Walker has outlined a specific vision for the Republican Party's 2016 presidential nominee. He believes the ideal candidate must be a current or former governor to genuinely represent an "outsider" perspective. Walker emphasizes that this executive experience is vital for a nominee to showcase a history of effective state leadership and a commitment to enacting substantial change.

In a recent interview on ABC's "This Week," Walker expanded on his viewpoint. "I think it's got to be an outsider," he declared, suggesting that both the presidential and vice-presidential nominees should ideally be individuals who have held the office of governor. He further elaborated that these candidates should be known for achieving concrete successes in their respective states and for being prepared to advance the nation.

When asked if this requirement would exclude prominent Republican Senators like Ted Cruz, Rand Paul, and Marco Rubio, Walker confirmed that it would. While recognizing their capabilities, he reiterated his central argument: "All good guys, but it's got to be somebody who is viewed as being exceptionally removed from Washington." This position highlights a strategic inclination towards candidates whose experience in state executive roles provides a clear distinction from those who have spent significant portions of their careers in federal legislative positions.

The public rift between sisters Liz and Mary Cheney has intensified, casting a significant shadow over Liz's United States Senate campaign and starkly highlighting deep familial divisions. Mary Cheney, who is openly gay, and her wife, Heather Poe, have expressed profound hurt and disappointment following statements made by Liz Cheney regarding same-sex marriage. This profound disagreement has led to a complete communication breakdown between the sisters since the summer, potentially impacting former Vice President Dick Cheney's strong desire for Liz to win a United States Senate seat.

The conflict became starkly evident when Liz Cheney, during an appearance on "Fox News Sunday," voiced her opposition to same-sex marriage, describing it as an "area where we disagree" in relation to her sister. This statement deeply affected Mary Cheney, who responded on Facebook, asserting, "Liz — this isn’t just an issue on which we disagree you’re just wrong — and on the wrong side of history." She further elaborated on the personal nature of the disagreement, emphasizing that it was not merely a policy difference but a fundamental moral and historical one.

Heather Poe, Mary's wife, further articulated the emotional toll this has taken on their family, questioning Liz Cheney's decision to relocate to Wyoming for her political ambitions. Poe posted on Facebook, "I can’t help but wonder how Liz would feel if as she moved from state to state, she discovered that her family was protected in one but not the other." She pointedly added, "Yes, Liz, in fifteen states and the District of Columbia you are my sister-in-law." This statement underscored the tangible legal and social realities of their marriage, which Liz's stance seemed to disregard.

This internal family conflict underscores broader tensions within the Republican Party as it grapples with evolving social norms while trying to maintain its conservative base. While Liz Cheney champions her stance against same-sex marriage, her father, Dick Cheney, adopted a more progressive position over a decade ago, publicly disagreeing with President George W. Bush's support for a constitutional amendment banning same-sex unions. By 2011, Dick Cheney had clearly stated, "I certainly don’t have any problem with" same-sex marriage, a stark contrast to his daughter's current position. Liz Cheney, however, is positioning herself to the right of incumbent Republican Senator Michael B. Enzi, aiming to rally conservative and Tea Party voters in her Senate bid, a strategy that appears to necessitate a more traditional stance on social issues.

When asked about her sister and sister-in-law's public statements, Liz Cheney responded via email, "I love my sister and her family and have always tried to be compassionate towards them. I believe that is the Christian way to behave." Sources close to Liz Cheney suggest her displeasure with the public airing of their dispute and her belief that Mary's current position is hypocritical, given Mary's prior work for President Bush, who opposed same-sex marriage. This perspective suggests a belief that Mary should have been more understanding of Liz's political calculus.

The sisters' relationship was once marked by deep closeness, particularly during their father's time as Vice President. After Dick Cheney left office in 2009, the sisters, living near each other in Northern Virginia, continued their tradition of Sunday dinners with their parents, a testament to their once-strong bond. Mary Cheney, 44, mentioned in a phone interview that she had assumed her sister shared their father's views on marriage, a perception that was deeply ingrained. This perception was reinforced by Liz Cheney's consistent support for her relationship with Heather Poe and their two children, including attending their wedding. This assumption was shattered in August when Liz Cheney, shortly after announcing her Senate candidacy, declared her opposition to same-sex marriage rights, a declaration that fundamentally altered Mary's understanding of her sister's values. Mary Cheney now considers reconciliation "impossible" as long as Liz Cheney maintains this stance, viewing it as a betrayal of their shared history and values. "What amazes me is that she says she’s running to be a new generation of leader," Mary Cheney commented, referring to her 47-year-old sister's campaign slogan. "I’m not sure how sticking to the positions of the last 20 or 30 years is the best way to do that."

Mary Cheney revealed that the decision to respond publicly on Facebook was Heather Poe's, indicating the depth of Poe's personal hurt. Poe expressed particular pain that her sister-in-law, who had been a welcoming presence in their home and a supporter of their 2012 marriage, would now publicly disavow their right to marry. "To have her say she doesn’t support our right to marry is offensive to say the least," Poe wrote, highlighting the personal sting of Liz's public pronouncements.

Mary Cheney, a seasoned political consultant, indicated her intention to continue addressing the issue, demonstrating a commitment to standing by her convictions and her wife. When reminded that such criticism could impact her sister's Senate campaign, she offered a concise, "O.K.," followed by a telling silence, suggesting a pragmatic awareness of the potential consequences but an unwavering resolve. While the substance of the same-sex marriage debate may not be a decisive factor in Wyoming, as her opponent also opposes it, the public family drama and questions surrounding Liz Cheney's authenticity could resonate with Wyoming voters, who often value personal connections and may be skeptical of an outsider. Liz Cheney's inaugural campaign advertisement emphasized her family's Wyoming heritage, a move that now appears to be in stark contrast to the public familial discord.

Wyoming, a state known for its vast landscapes and sparse population, is witnessing an unusually high-profile primary, amplified by this family feud. This election cycle has already been marked by a contentious episode involving the sisters' mother, Lynne Cheney, who reportedly confronted former Wyoming Senator Alan Simpson at a charity event, urging him to "just shut up" regarding his concerns about Liz Cheney's challenge to Senator Enzi. Simpson later refuted Lynne Cheney's denial of the exchange, calling it "a damn baldfaced lie," further illustrating the intense and often acrimonious nature of the political and familial dynamics at play.

Former Vice President Dick Cheney remains actively involved in his daughter's Senate campaign, with a scheduled fundraiser in Denver, underscoring his continued support for her political aspirations. Despite early polls indicating Liz Cheney trailing Senator Enzi, her fundraising efforts have been robust, suggesting a dedicated base of support. Mary Cheney noted that during recent family gatherings, discussions about Liz or the campaign are deliberately avoided, a strategy to maintain a semblance of normalcy amidst the turmoil. "They come over for dinner and we don’t talk about Liz or the race," she stated. "There is so much more to talk about." She added that her parents are striving to remain "as neutral as they can," acknowledging their difficult position caught between their two daughters.

Looking ahead to the holidays, Mary Cheney anticipates her parents will join her and Heather Poe for Thanksgiving in Northern Virginia, a traditional family gathering. Liz Cheney is expected to be in Wyoming, focusing on her campaign. The entire Cheney family is slated to convene in Jackson Hole, Wyoming, for Christmas, a planned reunion that highlights the complex emotional landscape. However, Mary Cheney confirmed, "I will not be seeing her," a poignant statement that underscores the deep and perhaps irreparable damage to their sisterly bond.

Nancy Pelosi recently shed light on a crucial element of the Affordable Care Act (ACA), specifically addressing the "grandfathering" provision. This provision, as she clarified, was exclusively designated for health insurance plans that were already established and active prior to the ACA's enactment in 2010.

During an appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press," the prominent California Democrat emphasized that the ACA did not permit the cancellation of any health insurance policy that was in existence before the law's passage in 2010. She further explained that individuals were indeed allowed to retain their pre-existing health insurance plans if they possessed them before this significant legislative development in 2010. Consequently, the grandfathering clause was strictly intended for policies that predated the year 2010.

Pelosi, who was instrumental in the creation of the ACA, acknowledged that President Barack Obama had publicly recognized certain initial difficulties encountered during the law's implementation phase. Notwithstanding these acknowledged challenges, she asserted that the fundamental structure of the ACA did not necessitate substantial modifications.

She remarked that the President's candor in accepting accountability for these implementation issues was commendable. However, she strongly reiterated that this acceptance of responsibility did not imply that the ACA contained any provisions that would have prohibited individuals from continuing their health coverage that was secured before 2010, should they have desired to do so. She further pointed out that the President took ownership of the broader strategic execution of the law, as this aspect of policy rollout often captures public attention.

**Luci Baines Johnson** has shared a **rare and poignant account** of the **profound emotional and logistical hurdles** her family faced when they moved into the White House in the wake of President John F. Kennedy's assassination in November 1963. During a candid conversation, she recalled a **striking moment of marital discord**, remarking, "I overheard my parents arguing. This was highly unusual for them."

She detailed the conversation, during which her mother, **Lady Bird Johnson**, expressed significant reservations. "My mother insisted, ‘No, Lyndon, we cannot. It's absolutely impossible.’" President Johnson, on the other hand, emphasized the **immediate demands of the presidency**. He responded, "And my father, with both understanding and resolve, stated, ‘Bird, we must… We need to move on December 7th, as that date is suitable for Mrs. Kennedy and the Secret Service.’”

At that age, a young Luci Baines Johnson did not fully comprehend the **historical weight of December 7th**. She explained, "I was unaware that December 7th held such a pivotal place in history for their generation. November 22nd had become that defining day for me," referring to the **date of the assassination**.

House Democrats who supported a recent bill to address issues with the Affordable Care Act, often referred to as Obamacare, were primarily focused on shielding themselves from political attacks, according to Assistant Democratic Leader Rep. James Clyburn. He stated on Sunday that many of these lawmakers were "insulating themselves against sound bites."

This legislation, passed on Friday with the support of 39 House Democrats and Republicans, permits insurance companies to continue offering plans that were previously discontinued under the ACA. Clyburn explained that while some representatives genuinely held concerns, approximately 30 voted for the bill as a strategic move to avoid negative public perception stemming from the ongoing challenges with the ACA's implementation.

Clyburn noted that the bill was widely perceived by the public as a measure to allow individuals to retain their existing insurance coverage. Consequently, voting against it could have placed many lawmakers in a difficult political position. He further elaborated on the bill's content, stating, "The fact of the matter is, if you look at the second part of that bill, it allows insurance companies to continue selling what we know to be substandard" plans. He concluded by acknowledging the political realities, saying, "I don’t blame anybody for insulating themselves against these sound bites, because that’s the world that we live in.”

House Democrats are centering their 2014 re-election campaigns on the Affordable Care Act (ACA). This strategy was confirmed by House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi in a "Meet the Press" interview. She stated the party's plan to highlight this healthcare law as a key message for candidates.

Pelosi recognized that predicting election results is hard. However, she stressed the Democratic Party's strong support for the ACA. "While I cannot definitively predict future electoral results," Pelosi said, "I can assure you that Democrats will vigorously advocate for the Affordable Care Act."

Beyond healthcare, Democrats are also focusing on economic issues. They aim to avoid government shutdowns, with the economy as their main campaign theme. On healthcare, Pelosi explained the party's position. "This is an issue that demands attention, and we will not shy away from it simply because it presents political challenges. Its importance to the well-being of Americans is too significant. Our foremost objective is to ensure that individuals receive adequate care and services, rather than solely concentrating on our own prospects for re-election."

President Barack Obama’s mishandling of the Affordable Care Act rollout has been incompetent, Washington Post journalist Bob Woodward said, but it doesn’t rise to past president-dooming scandals. “What this is, it’s a mess, clearly, but what it isn’t, and I think you have to look at the question of motive. And the president’s motive here, even though there were deep problems with the implementation, he wants to do something good for 30 million people and get them health insurance,” Woodward said on “Fox News Sunday.” “So this isn’t Watergate, this isn’t [Bill] Clinton and Monica Lewinsky.” Asked by Fox host Chris Wallace if the Obamacare implementation revealed “rank incompetence,” Woodward agreed. (Understanding Obamacare: POLITICO’s guide to the ACA) “There’s no question about that,” he said. “But you see all of these stories and this frenzy out there, the game over, the presidency is over some people are saying, and I think that’s not the case.” But Woodward did say the ACA rollout is a harbinger of bad things to come for Obama’s White House. (VIDEO: Sunday shows in 90 seconds) “When you go down the road, it’s going to get worse,” he said. “It’s going to blow a hole in the budget when you go two or three months from now… All of a sudden this is going to come on the table and people are going to say, ‘My God it’s going to cost much more money than we thought before. How you disentangle this is now on Obama’s head.” Read more about: Bob Woodward, Monica Lewinsky, Obamacare, Watergate

query: What was the estimated wealth of Mr. Trump’s senior staff and cabinet, according to Bloomberg?

documents.text: Mr. Trump’s administration is considered the most wealthy in American history, with members of his senior staff and cabinet worth an estimated $12 billion, according to a tally by Bloomberg. The Friday filings will add voluminous detail to that top-line figure. The White house chief of staff, Reince Priebus, for example, earned at least $1.18 million — nearly half of which came from the Republican National Committee, which he formerly led. His assets totaled between $604,008 and at least $1.26 million. “I think one of the really interesting things that people are going to see today — and I think it’s something that should be celebrated — is that the president has brought a lot of people into this administration, and this White House in particular, who have been very blessed and very successful,” said Sean Spicer, the White House press secretary. The officials “have given up a lot to come into government by setting aside a lot of assets,” he said. Until January, Mr. Kushner was the chief executive of Kushner Companies, a family-run real estate investment firm with holdings across the country. It is a growing business that has taken part in at least $7 billion of acquisitions over the past decade. Late Friday, the White House released details of the plan devised by his advisers to avoid conflicts of interest between Mr. Kushner’s government role and the wide-ranging business empire he ran with his father. That business depends on foreign investment from undisclosed sources, as well as billions of dollars in loans from the world’s biggest financial services firms. Although Mr. Kushner has stepped down from his management positions at the more than 200 entities that operated aspects of the family real estate business, he will remain a beneficiary of a vast majority of the business he ran for the past decade, through a series of trusts that already owned the various real estate companies. The plan laid out on Friday “is not sufficient,” said Larry Noble, a former general counsel and chief ethics officer for the Federal Election Commission. “While removing himself from the management of the businesses is an important step, he is still financially benefiting from how the businesses do. This presents potential for a conflict of interest. Given his level in the White House and broad portfolio, it’s hard to see how he will recuse himself from everything that may impact his financial interest.” While the filing discloses Mr. Kushner’s personal lenders, it does not provide information on his business partners or lenders to his projects.

**Unpacking the Trump Administration's Financial Disclosures: Potential Conflicts and Key Staffer Holdings**

The Trump administration's approach to releasing financial disclosures for White House staffers presented significant challenges to public transparency, making it difficult to fully assess potential conflicts of interest. These disclosures are crucial for understanding the financial entanglements of individuals advising the President and influencing policy. They detail assets such as stocks, real estate, and business interests, which can reveal ethical concerns and financial ties at the highest levels of government.

Initially, accessing these vital documents for public accountability required individual requests for each staffer's disclosure. Furthermore, the administration withheld the names of those filing, hindering the public and press from identifying who had submitted their financial information. In response to the White House's limited accessibility, organizations like The New York Times and The Associated Press undertook the effort to obtain and compile these filings. These documents, representing nearly all known White House staffers who submitted them, are now available for public review in a shared Google Drive folder. Our analysis of these disclosures is ongoing, and we encourage public participation. Please share any findings that raise concerns via comments on this thread or through our Google Form.

**Key Staffer Holdings and Potential Conflicts:**

*   **Steve Bannon:** As President Trump's chief strategist, Bannon reported over $500,000 in earnings from entities associated with Republican donors Robert and Rebekah Mercer. These entities include Breitbart News Network, Cambridge Analytica, Government Accountability Institute, and Glittering Steel. In accordance with White House ethics counsel, Bannon is divesting his interests in Cambridge Analytica and Glittering Steel. His total reported earnings for the past year ranged from $1.3 million to $2.3 million.

*   **Jared Kushner:** The President's son-in-law and senior White House adviser, Kushner stepped down from leadership roles in 266 business entities to adhere to ethics regulations. His and Ivanka Trump's combined financial disclosures indicate a net worth of up to $741 million, primarily derived from Kushner Companies. Kushner holds over 100 real estate assets, including a Trump-branded rental property in Jersey City that secured millions in investment from Chinese investors through the EB-5 visa program.

*   **Ivanka Trump:** Now holding an official White House position, Ivanka Trump's assets were detailed within her husband Jared Kushner's filing. Her branded companies reported over $5 million in revenue in 2016 and are valued at more than $50 million. Her investment in the Trump International Hotel in Washington, D.C. generated between $1 million and $5 million. To mitigate potential conflicts of interest, she is placing her companies into a trust that she will not manage.

**Additional Financial Profiles of Note:**

*   **Gary Cohn:** The former Goldman Sachs investment banker and Director of the National Economic Council, Cohn possesses assets totaling at least $253 million, including stakes in private companies.

*   **Omarosa Manigault:** A White House communications staffer, Manigault holds a 33% stake in a trust established by her late fiancé, Michael Clarke Duncan. This trust is valued between $1 million and $5 million.

*   **Reed Cordish:** A close family friend of the Trumps and a Maryland real estate developer overseeing White House technology initiatives, Cordish reported assets of at least $197 million, which include interests in Baltimore casinos.

The administration stated that approximately 180 financial disclosures from White House officials were processed, but less than half have been publicly released. These documents are slated for public release as they are received and processed, continuing the administration's stated transparency efforts. The full implications of these financial ties on policy and governance will become more apparent as additional information becomes available.

The White House has unveiled financial disclosures for over 100 senior officials in President Donald Trump's administration. These January filings offer a **clear look at the significant personal wealth** these individuals bring to public service, wealth that is now influencing national policy. While the disclosures detail considerable assets and income, they **don't always fully explain divestments from business interests** that could pose conflicts of interest. It's important to note that President Trump and Vice President Pence are not required to submit new financial disclosures until next year.

A thorough review of these filings by the Associated Press has revealed several key insights into the financial backgrounds of prominent administration figures.

### Navigating Wealth and Business Interests

**Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump: A Real Estate Empire**
Senior Adviser Jared Kushner and his wife, Ivanka Trump, have taken **major steps to separate themselves from their extensive business holdings**. They have officially resigned from all business entities and divested from 58 different assets. Despite these substantial efforts, their financial portfolio remains anchored by significant holdings in their global real estate empire, with reported assets valued at a minimum of **$240 million**. Kushner began divesting from potentially problematic assets shortly after the presidential election, with some transactions occurring even before official disclosure requirements. He sold his stake in a prominent Manhattan skyscraper to a trust overseen by his mother and has strategically organized many remaining assets into trusts where he is the sole beneficiary.

**Gary Cohn: From Wall Street to Economic Advising**
Gary Cohn, formerly an executive at Goldman Sachs, joined the Trump administration as chief economic adviser. His financial disclosure shows an impressive income of at least **$40 million** from his time at Goldman Sachs, including dividends, salary, and bonuses, with approximately half from stock compensation. Cohn's investment portfolio is varied, spanning venture capital funds like Andreessen Horowitz to tangible assets such as self-storage units. Notably, he also reported receiving over **$1 million in income from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China**, an interest the White House indicated he is actively divesting from.

**Steve Bannon: A Multifaceted Financial Profile**
Chief Strategist Steve Bannon's financial disclosures indicate assets ranging between **$13 million and $56 million**. A significant portion, up to **$25 million**, comes from his political consultancy, Bannon Strategic Advisors Inc. Before his prominent role in the Trump campaign, Bannon earned less than **$200,000** as executive director of Breitbart News Network LLC. His professional history also includes a two-year tenure as vice president of the data firm Cambridge Analytica, from which he resigned in August 2016. Bannon's consulting firm received over **$125,000 from Cambridge Analytica** in the preceding year. He also holds a stake in Cambridge Analytica, valued between **$1 million and $5 million**, with an agreement in principle to sell this holding. Additionally, Bannon maintains an investment worth up to **$5 million in Bannon Film Industries Inc.**, an entertainment company known for producing political documentaries.

**Kellyanne Conway: A Strategic Financial Approach**
Counselor to the President Kellyanne Conway possesses assets estimated to be worth up to **$40 million**, primarily from investments and salary from her political consulting firm, "the polling company/WomanTrend." Her firm generated over **$800,000 in business income** during 2016 and is valued between **$1 million and $5 million**. A significant portion of Conway's assets, exceeding **$31 million**, is held in cash or money-market accounts, likely a result of her decision to sell most investments before joining the White House. She continues to hold minor stock holdings, valued at less than **$200,000**, in companies such as Pfizer, Kraft Heinz, Mondelez, Altria, and Philip Morris. Conway has also supplemented her income through paid speeches and consulting services to various conservative organizations and Steven Cohen's firm, Point 72 Asset Management.

### Legal Expertise and Policy Roles

**Don McGahn: Legal Expertise and Political Ties**
White House Counsel Don McGahn earned a substantial income of over **$2.4 million** in the past year from his work at Jones Day, a prominent law firm with strong Republican affiliations. His disclosures list legal services rendered to more than 22 conservative and GOP-affiliated entities, including the NRA and the Republican National Committee. McGahn also reported earning **$4,900 from a music booking firm**.

**Boris Epshteyn: Investment Banking and Media Engagement**
Boris Epshteyn's primary financial asset is a significant stake, valued between **$1 million and $5 million**, in TGP Securities Inc., an investment banking firm where he previously served as a managing director. His income in the past year included **$30,000 earned as a Trump media surrogate** and **$226,000 from TGP**. Epshteyn also received **$240,000 in consulting fees from a health-care firm**. While he joined the White House press office, reports have indicated his departure from that role.

**Reince Priebus: Leadership and Legal Practice**
White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus earned a considerable income exceeding **$500,000** in salary and bonuses from his leadership role within the Republican Party. Additionally, he garnered at least **$750,000 from his former law firm**, Michael Best & Friedrich.

**Julia Hahn: From Journalism to Policy Aide**
Julia Hahn, a former employee of Breitbart News and now a White House aide, possesses assets that could potentially be worth up to **$2.3 million**. Her professional earnings include **$117,217 from her role as a senior investigative reporter at Breitbart News** and **$74,082 as the executive producer of the Laura Ingraham show**. Hahn also holds a stake valued between **$250,001 and $500,000 in Cherrywood Partnership**, a real estate investment company.

**Peter Navarro: Economic Expertise and Diverse Investments**
Peter Navarro, who directs the National Trade Council, previously earned a salary of **$240,000** as an economics professor at the University of California-Irvine. His disclosed assets, including stocks, bonds, and investment property, exceed **$1 million**. A significant portion of these assets is tied to his pension and mortgaged properties, reflecting a diversified investment strategy.

**Jason Greenblatt: Real Estate Law and Financial Markets**
Jason Greenblatt, a former lawyer for the Trump Organization who now advises on Middle East policy, reported assets exceeding **$1.4 million**, primarily concentrated in stock mutual funds. His earnings in the past year from the Trump Organization amounted to **$1.02 million**.

**Keith Kellogg: National Security and Corporate Investments**
National Security Council Chief of Staff Keith Kellogg reported earning **$96,000** in salary and severance from Cubic Corp. His financial disclosures also indicate investments worth at least **$366,000**. Furthermore, he has loans totaling at least **$600,000**, including a mortgage.

**Makan Delrahim: Legal Practice and Entertainment Investment**
Deputy White House Counsel Makan Delrahim earned over **$1 million** in the past year through a combination of salary, stock payouts, and a buyout from his law firm, Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck. His financial interests extend to being a passive investor in the movie "Trash Fire."

This reporting was conducted by Associated Press writers Julie Bykowicz, Chad Day, Stephen Braun, Jack Gillum, Michael Biesecker, Ted Bridis, Bernard Condon, and Ken Sweet.

White House financial disclosures reveal that Steve Bannon, a key advisor to President Donald Trump, earned over half a million dollars last year from entities connected to prominent conservative donors Robert and Rebekah Mercer. Bannon also reported a significant ownership stake, valued at $1 million or more, in a data analytics firm that previously worked for Senator Ted Cruz during his presidential campaign.

In his role as executive chairman of Breitbart News, a conservative media outlet that supported Trump during the Republican primary, Bannon received $191,000 in consulting fees in 2016. **Key Financial Highlights:**

*   **Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump:** Ivanka Trump's investment in the Trump International Hotel in Washington is valued between $5 million and $25 million, yielding $1 million to $5 million in income since its opening. Kushner earned $4.5 million in 2016 from the company that owns the New York Observer, having since sold his stake. *   **Gary Cohn:** The former Goldman Sachs COO, now director of the National Economic Council, reported assets ranging from $200 million to over $1 billion. His income last year was between $48 million and $76 million. While he did not detail the sale of his Goldman Sachs shares, reports indicate he sold them for approximately $285 million. He also reported stock in Bank of America, Facebook, and Twitter, alongside commercial real estate, patents, and a helicopter.

*   **Don McGahn:** The White House counsel previously earned $2.4 million as a Jones Day partner, representing clients including Trump, Senator Mark Kirk, the NRA, i360, and Aaron Schock.

*   **Stephen Miller:** Trump's top policy adviser earned $124,885 in 2016. He also held an unpaid advisory role at his parents' real estate company, Insperity PEO Services, L.P.

*   **Reed Cordish:** A close friend of the Trump family, Cordish holds stakes in companies and holdings worth tens of millions of dollars, including various real estate and hospitality ventures. He is also a beneficiary of a family trust valued between $5 million and $25 million.

*   **Katie Walsh:** The former White House deputy chief of staff earned over $600,000 in the past year, with significant portions from the Republican National Committee and her consulting firm, The Laymont Group.

*   **Sean Spicer:** The press secretary earned $260,000 in his previous role at the Republican National Committee. He owns commercial and rental properties valued between $2.6 million and $5.2 million and holds a family trust worth $500,000 to $1 million, along with stock fund income.

*   **Peter Navarro:** The director of the National Trade Council earned $240,000 as a professor at the University of California-Irvine and an additional $10,500 for a keynote speech.

## Trump Administration Officials Unveil Significant Financial Holdings

The White House has released extensive financial disclosure forms for numerous top administration officials, revealing substantial real estate investments and business entanglements among President Donald Trump's inner circle. These disclosures offer a clear view of the wealth and financial interests held by individuals now serving in key government roles.

Jared Kushner, President Trump's son-in-law and a senior adviser, along with daughter Ivanka Trump, collectively hold at least $240 million in assets. This portfolio includes numerous real estate investments. Kushner has resigned from approximately 260 entities and divested from 58 businesses or investments identified as potential conflicts of interest. However, legal counsel, in consultation with the Office of Government Ethics, has determined that Kushner's real estate holdings, particularly in New York City, are unlikely to require further divestment. An attorney involved in their ethics agreements noted that the remaining conflicts are "pretty narrow and very manageable." Kushner began divesting from problematic assets shortly after the election, with some transactions occurring before disclosure requirements were in effect. For example, he sold his stake in a Manhattan skyscraper to a trust managed by his mother, ensuring neither he nor Ivanka Trump hold any financial interest in it. The Kushner Companies, now managed by his relatives, are reportedly seeking investment partners for a major redevelopment of this building.

Steve Bannon, President Trump's chief strategist, disclosed assets ranging from $13 million to $56 million. This includes his influential political consultancy, Bannon Strategic Advisors Inc., valued at up to $25 million. Bannon also reported earning slightly less than $200,000 in the previous year as executive director of Breitbart News Network LLC before resigning to join the Trump campaign. Further disclosures show he served as vice president of the data firm Cambridge Analytica for over two years, resigning in August 2016 to manage Trump's campaign. Cambridge Analytica was a primary data provider for Senator Ted Cruz during his presidential bid. Bannon's consulting firm received over $125,000 from Cambridge last year, and he holds a stake in the firm valued between $1 million and $5 million, with an "agreement in principle" to sell this investment.

Other high-profile officials also revealed significant financial landscapes. White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus reported receiving over $500,000 in salary and bonuses from the Republican National Committee. Gary Cohn, who left a prominent role at Goldman Sachs to serve as Trump's chief economic adviser, is among the wealthiest officials. His financial disclosure indicates at least $40 million in income from Goldman Sachs-related dividends, interest, salary, and bonuses, with roughly half in stock compensation. Cohn also reported over $1 million in income from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China; the White House has stated he is in the process of divesting from both this bank and his Goldman holdings. White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer characterized the business professionals joining the administration as "very blessed and very successful," asserting that the disclosures demonstrate their commitment to public service.

These financial disclosures, mandated by law, provide a snapshot of officials' financial standing upon entering the White House. While "certificates of divestiture" for employees seeking capital gains tax deferrals on sold assets will eventually be accessible, detailed ethics agreements remain private. Jared Kushner, for example, received such certificates for financial interests in several assets, including funds linked to his brother Joshua Kushner's investment firm, Thrive Capital. Kushner and Ivanka Trump have stepped away from businesses valued at least $50 million each, though they retain financial interests in many of them. Ivanka Trump is set to become a federal employee and will file her own disclosure later.

Jared Kushner's disclosure also reveals tens of millions of dollars in bank debt incurred in 2015 and 2016, including liabilities with several international banks whose interests could intersect with the Trump administration's agenda. Kushner has as much as $25 million in liabilities to Deutsche Bank, a significant lender to Kushner family real estate ventures and a major creditor for Trump Organization-branded projects. Additionally, Kushner and his father, Charles, have liabilities up to $5 million with the U.S. unit of Israel Discount Bank. The Kushner Companies have also acknowledged "long-standing" relationships with two other Israeli banks. Financial information for Cabinet members requiring Senate confirmation has largely been available for weeks through the Office of Government Ethics. President Trump is required to file periodic financial disclosures, with his next due next year.

The financial disclosures submitted by prominent figures within the Trump administration reveal a striking concentration of personal wealth, a reality that seems to stand in contrast to the campaign's "drain the swamp" rhetoric. These official documents highlight that many key individuals in the White House possess considerable financial assets, prompting questions about the interplay between personal fortunes and public service. Among those with substantial holdings are Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner, whose combined financial assets are estimated to surpass an impressive $740 million. Further underscoring this trend, White House Chief Strategist Steve Bannon's assets were reported to be approximately $56 million. Concurrently, White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus disclosed earnings of $1.4 million in 2016, with a significant portion, nearly half of this income, originating from his prior tenure with the Republican National Committee.

These disclosures collectively emphasize the considerable financial holdings of President Trump's closest advisors, a fact that inevitably raises ethical considerations concerning these high-ranking administration members. While the White House had initially indicated plans to release additional disclosures, it was confirmed that those pertaining to President Trump himself and Vice President Mike Pence would not be included in this initial release of information. This selective approach to transparency has attracted particular attention and scrutiny.

In defense of the administration's strategy for releasing these documents, White House press secretary Sean Spicer asserted that the disclosures were "more accessible, more available than in history." When pressed about President Trump's decision not to release his tax returns, Spicer characterized the situation as a matter of "apples and oranges," emphasizing that the financial disclosures are "required by law." He further elaborated that the administration was "going above and beyond what has been done in the past" to ensure public access to this information, suggesting a commitment to establishing a new benchmark for openness.

The collective wealth of the administration has been framed by some as a source of pride. Spicer himself suggested that this significant accumulation of assets should be "celebrated." He expressed a belief that this demonstrates President Trump's capacity to surround himself with "very blessed and very successful" individuals, implying that their financial acumen translates into effective governance. Moreover, he highlighted that their willingness to publicly list their assets and undergo scrutiny, by "set[ting] aside a lot," signifies their profound dedication to the president's vision and agenda, suggesting a personal sacrifice for the sake of public service.

According to reporting by USA Today, the White House was preparing to release a substantial number of financial disclosures, potentially up to 180 documents from its top staffers. These initial releases served to underscore the extraordinary wealth that is demonstrably present within the administration's leadership. Beyond Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner, other prominent figures with substantial assets featured in these disclosures include Kellyanne Conway, who possesses a multimillion-dollar portfolio, and Gary Cohn, the former CEO of Goldman Sachs who assumed the role of heading Trump's National Economic Council. Dina Habib Powell, another former Goldman executive, also appeared on the list of individuals with significant financial holdings.

A particularly noteworthy aspect of these disclosures is the revelation that Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner continue to profit from businesses they officially departed to join the White House. Kushner, for instance, still benefits from his family's extensive and lucrative real estate empire, a significant source of ongoing income. Ivanka Trump, in turn, retains an ownership stake in the Trump International Hotel located in Washington, D.C., a stake valued between $5 million and $25 million, indicating a continued financial connection to the Trump brand.

Further details emerged from the meticulous examination of these financial disclosures. Reince Priebus, the White House Chief of Staff, earned three-quarters of a million dollars from a Milwaukee law firm, a firm from which he had been on leave since 2011, suggesting a substantial financial cushion from his prior professional life.

The financial disclosures also introduced a degree of ambiguity regarding the precise timeline of Steve Bannon's departure from Breitbart. While the disclosures themselves suggested that he resigned in August, which would align with his joining Trump's presidential campaign, Breitbart CEO Larry Solov later stated that Bannon's resignation actually occurred "on or about Nov. 13," a date that falls after the general election. This discrepancy has led to further scrutiny of the timing of his transition from media executive to White House strategist.

The financial disclosures of Trump administration appointees highlight the significant wealth and diverse income streams of individuals in high-level government positions. The White House released approximately 180 financial disclosure forms, underscoring what many have termed the wealthiest administration in U.S. history. This influx of personal wealth into public service raises important questions about potential conflicts of interest and the delicate balance between private financial interests and public duty.

Key figures like Jared Kushner, President Trump's son-in-law and advisor, and his wife, Ivanka Trump, are prominent examples. Their extensive 54-page disclosure details a wide array of assets and debts. While serving in unpaid roles, they navigated anti-nepotism rules, with Kushner gaining clearance in January and Ivanka Trump announcing her position later. Together, their disclosed assets exceed $200 million. Despite their government roles, they remain beneficiaries of a real estate and investment business valued up to $741 million. To mitigate potential conflicts, Kushner divested from dozens of businesses and resigned from over 260 positions. His legal team, in conjunction with the Office of Government Ethics, determined his New York City real estate holdings were unlikely to create conflicts. Ivanka Trump also holds a stake in the Trump International Hotel in Washington, D.C., valued between $5 million and $25 million, from which she earned between $1 million and $5 million in 2016 and early 2017. Other appointees' finances also garnered attention. Steve Bannon, former Breitbart News executive chairman and chief strategist, reported substantial earnings and assets. The Wall Street Journal noted Bannon earned at least $1.4 million before his appointment and held assets between $10.7 million and $48.6 million. His income sources included Breitbart News ($191,000), Cambridge Analytica ($125,333), and the Government Accountability Institute ($61,539), all linked to conservative funders Robert and Rebekah Mercer. Bannon's largest income source, over $493,000, came from Bannon Strategic Advisors, Inc., likely an umbrella entity for fees from various interests, including Breitbart, Cambridge Analytica, and Glittering Steel, which is connected to the "Clinton Cash" documentary.

Gary Cohn, former director of the National Economic Council and ex-president of Goldman Sachs, is among the wealthiest appointees. Bloomberg reported Cohn held at least $254 million in assets and earned at least $48.3 million between 2016 and early 2017. His 2016 Goldman Sachs compensation included a $1.85 million salary and a $5.74 million bonus from 2015. With dividends and interest from Goldman-affiliated assets, Cohn's earnings from the firm reached at least $40 million. Michael Flynn, former national security adviser, also had financial dealings revealed. An initial disclosure on February 11 listed no payments from Russia-linked groups. Flynn resigned two days later after reports of his meeting with Russia's ambassador and misrepresentations to Vice President Pence. A later disclosure on March 31 included "speaking engagements" with RT TV, Kaspersky Government Security Solutions, Inc., and Volga-Dnepr Airlines, with payments exceeding $5,000. These three entities collectively paid Flynn over $50,000. His more recent disclosure shows at least $1.37 million in income, with $827,055 from his consulting firm, Flynn Intel Group LLC, which had been inactive since December.

These public financial disclosures offer a snapshot of appointees' holdings at the start of their government service. Federal ethics law mandates the release of these forms, requiring high-ranking executive branch appointees to reveal their financial holdings and establish formal agreements with ethics officials. These agreements prevent conflicts between personal financial interests and official duties, often requiring asset sales, resignations, or recusal from matters with potential conflicts. The Trump administration announced many appointees before these financial negotiations concluded, and the Office of Government Ethics indicated the White House was slower than the Obama administration in submitting nominees' financial arrangements for review, attributing this to the complex portfolios of its wealthy appointees. Media estimates place the Trump Cabinet's cumulative wealth between $6 billion and $14 billion.

Financial disclosures from several Trump administration officials reveal their reported earnings, assets, and debts. These public filings, mandated by government ethics regulations, provide a glimpse into the financial situations of key personnel.

Sebastian Gorka, who previously served as a deputy assistant to President Trump and was an editor at Breitbart News, reported earning $38,200 in consulting fees from Breitbart. He also disclosed royalties ranging from $50,000 to $100,000 for his book, "Defeating Jihad: The Winnable War," and noted a contract for a subsequent book.

Boris Epshteyn, a former television spokesperson for Mr. Trump during his campaign, continues to owe over $50,000 in college loans taken out more than ten years ago. He recently departed his White House position for reasons that have not been made public.

Jason Greenblatt, an attorney with the Trump Organization involved in Middle East peace initiatives, received $1,025,000 in compensation from the company over the past year.

White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer remarked on these disclosures, suggesting that the financial success of those joining the administration should be acknowledged. He emphasized that President Trump has drawn many accomplished individuals who have made considerable sacrifices by setting aside assets to serve in public office. Spicer indicated that their readiness to reveal all their holdings and face scrutiny demonstrates their commitment to the President's objectives.

The timing of these financial disclosures, released on a Friday evening, aligns with a common practice in Washington D.C. of publishing substantial document releases late in the week, when public attention might be less intense.

Additional individuals who submitted disclosure forms include Stephen K. Bannon, Thomas Bossert, James W. Carroll, and Justin Clark.

query: What were Donald Trump's initial ambitions regarding the health care deal, and how did they ultimately end in failure?

documents.text:
Donald Trump's promised "terrific" healthcare reform effort ended not with legislative success, but a brief phone call. Speaker Paul Ryan delivered the news: the plan had failed. The businessman president, who campaigned on fixing Washington's dysfunction, was outmaneuvered in his first major legislative fight.

Trump acknowledged a learning curve, noting, "We learned a lot about some very arcane rules." The core issue was majority rule. Despite a 22-seat House majority, Trump couldn't gather enough votes for the repeal and replace legislation, a key campaign promise. This failure to advance a bill through his own party's chamber, on a long-prioritized Republican issue, raises questions about Trump's dealmaking skills and the GOP's legislative agenda.

Key issues contributing to the failure included:

*   **Internal Division:** The House Republican conference was deeply divided.
*   **Moderate vs. Conservative:** Concessions to the hardline House Freedom Caucus alienated moderates in the Tuesday Group, and vice versa.
*   **Political vs. Policy:** The White House focused on political pressure, while opposition stemmed from policy concerns, an area where Trump seemed less comfortable.

Scott Reed, chief strategist for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, called it "a black eye for the speaker and the president." The defeat was particularly painful as it came from within Republican ranks, not Democratic opposition. A rebellious faction, similar to the one that ousted Speaker John Boehner, proved to be Trump's undoing. Many new House Republicans struggled to form a unified governing coalition.

Josh Holmes, former chief of staff to Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, observed, "The Republican Party is still operating as an opposition party." He suggested this disunity would create significant challenges for future priorities like tax reform, infrastructure, and immigration.

Trump seemed eager to move past the healthcare debate, while White House officials worried about the long-term damage. One senior official called it a "clear embarrassment." The president's focus appeared to shift to other matters, such as inauguration crowd sizes and legal challenges. "No bullshit, I think he's actually pretty comfortable with the outcome. He wants to move ahead and do taxes," a different senior White House official stated.

However, tax reform, a complex issue that has eluded lawmakers for decades, presents its own obstacles. The Republican Party had hoped that deficit reduction from healthcare reform would free up funds for tax cuts. Speaker Ryan conceded, "Yes, this does make tax reform more difficult."

For weeks, Trump seemed disengaged from the healthcare negotiations. An adviser revealed, "Trump just wanted to get something he could sign. He was over it." He often steered conversations away from the bill.

During a call with Ryan, Trump reportedly disliked the term "buckets" for plan components, preferring "phases." This focus on superficial language echoed his campaign promise of "repeal and replace — with something terrific." Yet, by February, Trump admitted to governors that dismantling Obamacare was proving more intricate, stating, "Nobody knew healthcare could be so complicated."

When Trump visited Capitol Hill to rally support, his remarks lacked substantive healthcare policy. Representative Walter Jones, R-N.C., noted Trump offered "not a whole lot about health care, except to vote for it." Other lawmakers reported Trump was unfamiliar with the bill's specifics, focusing instead on his popularity or the political risks of opposition. The proposed law ultimately garnered less than 20 percent public support, and the president's approval rating fell below 40 percent.

Facing insufficient votes, Trump employed an ultimatum. His lieutenants informed House Republicans that the bill was a take-it-or-leave-it offer. The White House maintained high expectations, with Press Secretary Sean Spicer calling Trump "the closer." Trump pushed for a vote until the last minute, with senior officials insisting they would hold the roll call "to make them vote against the president on live TV."

This approach mirrored Trump's "The Art of the Deal" philosophy, emphasizing boldness. However, this strategy proved less effective in Congress, where time and political capital are limited, and one deal's failure impacts future negotiations.

The setback could also intensify internal White House friction. Blame was cast on various figures, including Jared Kushner, Reince Priebus, and Steve Bannon.

The blame game began early. "This is 100 percent a Ryan failure," asserted one White House official. While Trump reportedly held no ill will towards Ryan, many advisers criticized the Speaker. Ryan himself anticipated bearing the brunt of the blame. Support for the bill eroded significantly, with the chairman of the House Appropriations Committee announcing his opposition.

Speaker Ryan met with Trump to convey the dire situation. Despite initial desires to proceed, concerns grew. Spicer stopped using the term "the closer." GOP leadership pushed to abandon the effort. Trump spoke with Ryan again, who reported being "at least 10 and 15 votes short." Trump agreed to pull the vote. Ryan later explained to the public, "We're going to be living with Obamacare for the foreseeable future."

After the call, Trump contacted reporters to share his perspective before the White House staff was fully briefed. His primary concern throughout the process was media coverage and its reflection on him.

In the Capitol basement, Republican lawmakers gathered to discuss the path forward. The Rolling Stones song Trump often played at rallies, "You can't always get what you want," served as an ironic backdrop.

## Republican Healthcare Overhaul Stalls: AHCA Pulled Amidst Deep Party Divisions

Republicans suffered a significant legislative setback on Friday, withdrawing their proposed American Health Care Act (AHCA) from House consideration. After weeks of intense negotiations aimed at repealing and replacing the Affordable Care Act (ACA), the party conceded failure, unable to garner sufficient internal support for the ambitious overhaul of the U.S. health insurance system. This dramatic turn of events underscored profound divisions within the Republican party and the immense difficulty of legislating complex policy areas like healthcare.

President Donald Trump, speaking from the Oval Office, attributed the bill's collapse to Democrats, characterizing the ACA as "the signature achievement of Barack Obama." He expressed openness to bipartisan collaboration, stating, "If [Democrats] got together with us, and got us a real healthcare bill, I’d be totally OK with that." Trump then squarely blamed Democratic leaders, declaring, "The losers are Nancy Pelosi and Chuck Schumer, because they own Obamacare. They 100% own it." While avoiding criticism of House Speaker Paul Ryan, the President pivoted from health reform discussions to the administration's next legislative focus: tax reform. "We’re probably going to start going very strongly on big tax cuts. Tax reform that will be next," he announced, adding a reflective comment, "We all learned a lot. We learned a lot about loyalty."

Earlier that day, as opposition within the Republican party solidified, Speaker Paul Ryan informed President Trump that the bill lacked the necessary votes for passage. White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer had, until that point, maintained that the vote would proceed as scheduled. He told reporters, "Has the team put everything out there, have we left everything on the field? Absolutely. But at the end of the day this isn’t a dictatorship and we’ve got to expect members to ultimately vote how they will according to what they think." However, the 3:30 PM ET deadline passed without a vote being called. Subsequently, reports indicated that President Trump had requested the bill be pulled. Minutes later, House Republicans officially announced the withdrawal of the legislation.

At a subsequent press conference, Speaker Ryan candidly acknowledged the inherent difficulties of transitioning from an opposition party to a governing party. "Moving from an opposition party to a governing party comes with growing pains and, well, we’re feeling those growing pains today. I will not sugarcoat this: this is a disappointing day for us," he stated. He admitted that after nearly a decade of campaigning against the ACA, Republicans had failed to coalesce around a viable alternative. "We are going to be living with Obamacare for the foreseeable future," Ryan conceded. He explained that he had recommended the bill's withdrawal upon realizing the vote count was insufficient, while also commending President Trump's efforts, describing him as "fantastic." When pressed on how Republicans would address constituents after failing to deliver on their central campaign promise, Ryan responded with a candid, "That’s a really good question. I wish I had a better answer for you."

A reporter from *The Washington Post* later recounted a conversation with President Trump, during which the President indicated that the healthcare bill would not be revisited in the immediate future. Speaker Ryan also confirmed that Republicans would be shifting their focus to other key priorities, including securing the border, rebuilding the military, and enacting tax reform. "Now we’re going on to move on with the rest of our agenda because we have big, ambitious plans to improve people’s lives in this country," Ryan stated.

Despite considerable speculation, the bill's withdrawal came as a surprise to many Republican members. An emergency House Republican Caucus meeting had been convened shortly before the scheduled vote. As this meeting commenced, the House entered a recess, with Democratic members audibly chanting "Vote, vote, vote" in an effort to pressure Republicans. Speaker Ryan then emerged to announce the bill's withdrawal in a brief statement to the press.

A significant factor contributing to the bill's demise was the hesitation of many moderate Republicans representing swing districts. These members expressed concern over the substantial cuts to Medicaid proposed in the AHCA. The non-partisan Congressional Budget Office had estimated that these cuts would lead to approximately 24 million fewer Americans having health insurance over a ten-year period. Conversely, conservative Republicans objected to the bill for what they perceived as its retention of too much of the ACA's existing structure. Despite a concession made on Thursday to remove the federal mandate for essential health benefits, such as maternity and mental healthcare, this adjustment failed to sway enough conservative votes. Nevada Republican Mark Amodei highlighted a critical deficiency, noting a lack of discussion on a "unified Republican vision for what we should do with healthcare in the House." Paul Gosar, a member of the hard-right Freedom Caucus, attributed the setback to issues with White House staff.

The failure to pass the AHCA represented a major political blow to Speaker Paul Ryan, who had spearheaded the legislative effort. It also placed President Trump in a vulnerable position, having made the repeal of the "disaster" of Obamacare a cornerstone of his campaign. Despite his public persona as a skilled dealmaker, Trump's first significant legislative negotiation on Capitol Hill concluded in defeat. House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi hailed the outcome as "a great day for our country" and "a victory for the American people." Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer attributed the bill's failure to "incompetence and broken promises," delivering a sharp critique: "In my life, I have never seen an administration as incompetent as the one occupying the White House today. They can’t write policy that actually makes sense, they can’t implement the policies they do manage to write, they can’t get their stories straight, and today we’ve learned that they can’t close a deal, and they can’t count votes. So much for The Art of the Deal."

Republican members offered varied interpretations of the events. Louie Gohmert of Texas, who had opposed the bill, blamed House leadership for cornering members and the President. "The president didn’t really get involved until after they created this bill and he was fighting for it," Gohmert remarked. Bradley Byrne of Alabama expressed continued willingness to support the AHCA, lamenting that House Republicans "fell just short" and praising the efforts of both Ryan and Trump. Some Republicans voiced uncertainty about the future of healthcare reform. Gohmert suggested that while he would not prioritize healthcare if he were president, as a legislator, the issue still required a correct resolution. Mark Walker, chair of the Republican Study Committee, acknowledged the setback, stating, "I can’t pretend that this is a win for us. I’m sure our friends on the left, this is a good moment for them."

President Donald Trump about the health care overhaul bill, Friday, March 24, 2017, in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. rump says he would be willing to reopen negotiations for a health... (Associated Press) President Donald Trump about the health care overhaul bill, Friday, March 24, 2017, in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. rump says he would be willing to reopen negotiations for a health... (Associated Press) WASHINGTON (AP) — Just two months in, Donald Trump's presidency is perilously adrift. His first major foray into legislating imploded Friday when House Republicans abandoned a White House-backed health care bill, resisting days of cajoling and arm-twisting from Trump himself. Aides who had confidently touted Trump as the deal's "closer" were left bemoaning the limits of the presidency.
