"At the end of the day, you can't force somebody to do something," White House spokesman Sean Spicer said. On its own, the health care bill's collapse was a stunning rejection of a new president by his own party. And for Trump, the defeat comes with an especially strong sting. The president who campaigned by promising "so much winning," has so far been beset by a steady parade of the opposite. With each setback and sidetrack, comes more concern about whether Trump, the outsider turned president, is capable of governing. "You can't just come in and steamroll everybody," said Bruce Miroff, a professor of American politics and the presidency at the State University of New York at Albany. "Most people have a modest understanding of how complicated the presidency is. They think leadership is giving orders and being bold. But the federal government is much more complicated, above all because the Constitution set it up that way." The ambitious agenda Trump vowed to quickly muscle through has now been blocked by both Congress and the courts. Whole weeks of his presidency have been consumed by crises that are often self-inflicted, including his explosive and unverified claim that President Barack Obama wiretapped his New York skyscraper. Earlier this week, the FBI director confirmed that Trump's campaign is being investigated for possible coordination with Russia during the election, an investigation that could hang over the White House for years. Trump's advisers say some of the churn is to be expected from a president with an unconventional style and little regard for Washington convention. They counter the notion of a White House in crisis by pointing to Trump's well-received nomination of Neil Gorsuch to the Supreme Court. They appeal for patience, noting that the administration is indeed in its early days. But early missteps can be difficult to overcome, particularly for a president like Trump, who took office with historically low favorability ratings and has continued to lose support since his inauguration. According to Friday's Gallup daily tracking poll, 54 percent of Americans disapprove of his performance on the job. James Thurber, who founded the Center for Congressional and Presidential Studies at American University, blamed Trump for an apparent "misunderstanding or ignorance of how the separation of powers works" that is hurting him at a time "where he should have much more success." Trump is hardly the first president to stumble through his early days in arguably one of the hardest jobs in the world. Bill Clinton's presidency got off to a chaotic start and was quickly shrouded by an ethics controversy involving the firing of employees at the White House travel office. Jimmy Carter, another Washington outsider, clashed with his own party. Richard Nixon struggled to unite a deeply divided nation. "There have been moments like this in history in terms of where the country is, in terms of the president kind of having a chaotic couple of months," said David Greenberg, a historian at Rutgers University. Still, he said Trump's challenges are exacerbated by his "complete inexperience in the political arena, his personality and style." Indeed, many of Trump's early struggles have been clearly avoidable. His courting of chaos undercuts his campaign promises to bring business efficiency to Washington. Infighting and gamesmanship among top White House aides, sometimes egged on by the president himself, consume a striking amount of attention in the West Wing. His wiretapping allegations against Obama put his advisers in the untenable position of trying to justify an allegation for which there is no evidence. His first, hastily written ban on travel to the U.S. from several countries was blocked by the courts, as was a rewrite, and judges cited his own campaign rhetoric in their rulings. The president's advisers had hoped the health care measure would give the White House a much-needed burst of momentum and prove to wary Republicans that it was worth sticking with Trump in order to accomplish something the party had sought for seven years. Hardly a policy wonk, Trump embraced the plan put forth by House Speaker Paul Ryan and promised Republican leaders he would invest his own political capital into rounding up votes. He did, spending hours working the phones with lawmakers, often early in the morning and late at night. His advisers held bowling and pizza nights for GOP lawmakers at the White House. But Ryan's decision to pull the bill Friday underscored Trump's limitations. His powers of persuasion couldn't overcome the ideological concerns of conservatives who are more popular in their home districts than Trump or the political fears of moderates worried about attaching themselves to an unpopular president. Trump, who has privately lashed out at his staff and publicly berated the media during other rough patches in his young presidency, was surprisingly sanguine in defeat. "We learned a lot about the vote-getting processes," Trump said. "For me, it's been a very interesting experience." ___ EDITOR'S NOTE: Julie Pace has covered the White House and politics for The Associated Press since 2007. Follow her at http://twitter.com/jpaceDC ___ This story has been corrected to change Wednesday to Friday in 2nd paragraph.

The Republican effort to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA) experienced a significant legislative setback this week. The proposed bill, a cornerstone promise of President Donald Trump and Speaker Paul Ryan, was ultimately withdrawn from House consideration on Friday, averting a certain defeat. Speaker Ryan subsequently acknowledged that the ACA, often referred to as "ObamaCare," would likely remain the law of the land "for the foreseeable future," indicating no immediate plans for another legislative push. This outcome represents a pivotal moment for several key political figures, with distinct winners and losers emerging from the ensuing political fallout.

**LOSERS**

**President Donald Trump:** This legislative failure represents a substantial blow to President Trump, whose campaign was built on promises of decisive action and effective deal-making. His inaugural major legislative initiative faltered, despite his direct appeals to wavering Republican members. The precise impact on his broader agenda and the potential for Republican frustration directed at him remain uncertain. While Trump projected an image of resilience, his comment regarding the ACA's persistence – "there’s not much you can do about it" – from a president commanding congressional majorities, underscored a palpable sense of powerlessness. The full ramifications of this setback will continue to unfold over time.

**Speaker Paul Ryan (R-Wis.):** The unsuccessful healthcare bill is as damaging to Speaker Ryan as it is to the President. His inability to shepherd the American Health Care Act through the House constitutes a profound embarrassment. Ryan was deeply invested in the legislation, which encountered formidable opposition from conservative members within his own party and influential interest groups. Some Trump loyalists suggest that Ryan erred strategically by prioritizing healthcare over tax reform. Conservative media figures have begun questioning his leadership capabilities. Despite receiving public support from President Trump, Ryan's defeat highlights a struggle to maintain sufficient party discipline.

**Vice President Pence, Health and Human Services Secretary Tom Price, and OMB Director Mick Mulvaney:** These former House members were anticipated to leverage their insider knowledge to influence the ultra-conservative House Freedom Caucus and other lawmakers. Mick Mulvaney, a founding member of the caucus, was particularly well-positioned to sway votes. However, their collective efforts proved insufficient to secure the necessary support, resulting in a notable political setback for each individual.

**MIXED**

**The House Freedom Caucus:** This conservative bloc successfully blocked the Republican healthcare bill, but the long-term implications of this victory remain uncertain. Under the leadership of Rep. Mark Meadows (R-N.C.), the caucus resisted presidential pressure. Their unified stance was instrumental in the bill's failure, demonstrating their considerable influence. Nevertheless, they now face the challenge of navigating the consequences of opposing a president from their own party, who enjoys strong support among many grassroots Republicans. Furthermore, their strategy of compelling leadership to propose a more accommodating alternative has not yet yielded tangible results.

**WINNERS**

**Former President Barack Obama:** The survival of his signature domestic policy, the ACA, represents a significant victory. This occurred despite unified control of the White House and Congress by those who had pledged to dismantle it. While Trump suggested the ACA would collapse on its own, he did not commit to further repeal efforts, instead indicating an openness to incremental reforms with Democratic collaboration. The ACA's continued existence solidifies Obama's presidential legacy.

**House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.):** Pelosi effectively showcased remarkable party unity, a stark contrast to the evident Republican division. Not a single Democrat broke ranks to support the Republican plan, thereby creating a substantial legislative obstacle. Pelosi, a seasoned political strategist, appeared to relish the Republicans' internal struggles, famously remarking it a "Rookie’s error, Donald Trump" following the initial vote delay.

**Jared Kushner:** Trump's son-in-law and a key advisor, Kushner reportedly opposed the immediate focus on healthcare legislation. His absence from Washington for a significant portion of the week, attending a family ski trip, kept him physically and politically distanced from the contentious negotiations. Consequently, he emerged from this legislative failure relatively unscathed.

**Governors:** Several Republican governors voiced strong criticism of the House replacement plan, citing concerns regarding its potential impact on Medicaid funding. Governors such as John Kasich (Ohio), Brian Sandoval (Nevada), Asa Hutchinson (Arkansas), and Rick Snyder (Michigan) co-signed a letter to congressional leaders expressing their opposition to the bill. Their stance provided crucial political cover for lawmakers in their respective states who were hesitant to endorse the legislation.

**The AARP:** This organization, representing older Americans, actively campaigned against the ACA repeal bill. They characterized a proposed change as an "age tax," highlighted the projected decline in health insurance coverage, and framed the vote as an "accountability vote," signaling their intention to oppose lawmakers who voted against their interests. The AARP's successful advocacy efforts underscored its considerable influence in shaping public opinion and legislative outcomes.

The swift failure of the American Health Care Act provided a significant political lesson for President Trump and his administration. This account is based on insights from anonymous government officials involved in the final discussions.

President Trump described the situation as a crucial learning experience, noting, "We learned a lot about loyalty, and we learned a lot about the vote-getting process." While not initially deeply involved in drafting the bill, he increased his participation, attending rallies and holding meetings to bridge the gap between conservative and moderate groups, whose backing was vital for its approval.

Despite his efforts, the President had previously shown some hesitation about making healthcare reform his primary focus, admitting, "I would have loved to have put it first, I’ll be honest," when discussing tax reform. Nevertheless, he believed the healthcare bill needed his direct support to pass. By Thursday afternoon, just hours before a planned vote, it was clear that the combined efforts of the President, Vice President Pence, and other senior White House staff had not garnered enough votes.

A key meeting in Speaker Paul Ryan's office with the Freedom Caucus underscored the growing disagreement. White House representatives proposed compromises, such as dropping federal requirements for essential health benefits like maternity and wellness care. However, the Freedom Caucus continued to push for more concessions, causing frustration for Speaker Ryan and the President's team.

President Trump's chief strategist, Stephen K. Bannon, and budget director, Mick Mulvaney, told the caucus that talks were over and the President needed a final answer. When asked for their positions, several members hesitated. As the situation grew tense, Representative Mark Meadows stated that the group was not yet ready to commit, indicating the bill would not move forward.

President Donald Trump's approach to repealing and replacing the Affordable Care Act (ACA) took a significant turn on March 24th, 2017. Following the postponement of a crucial vote on the Republican healthcare bill, Trump declared his intention to allow Obamacare to "explode" before engaging further with the media. This strategy aligned with his established persona, which consistently highlighted his unique ability to forge agreements that traditional politicians often struggled to achieve.

Central to Trump's public image, as explored in his book "The Art of the Deal," is a direct confrontation with adversaries, a transparent presentation of his negotiating position, and a demand for reciprocity from others. This philosophy was clearly demonstrated when he urged House Republicans to "put up or shut up" concerning ACA reform. Despite clear indications that sufficient votes were not yet secured, Trump pushed forward, characterizing the moment as a critical, "now or never" juncture. This high-stakes maneuver, a hallmark of Trump's political style and a departure from conventional political tactics, involved forcing a vote on a foundational piece of his legislative agenda with an uncertain outcome.

After extensive deliberations with Speaker Paul D. Ryan at the White House, Trump ultimately made the decision to withdraw the proposed legislation. The lack of adequate support and the potential for considerable embarrassment on the House floor were cited as the primary reasons for this withdrawal. Critics contended that Trump, elected as an outsider challenging the established political order, could have strategically leveraged the situation to compel a public record of support or opposition from all political factions. Hugh Hewitt, echoing this sentiment, expressed regret via Twitter that the President and Speaker Ryan had agreed to pull the bill, suggesting that holding both parties accountable would have been a more beneficial approach.

The aftermath of this decision initiated a period of blame assignment. The White House directed criticism towards Speaker Ryan, suggesting he had prioritized healthcare reform as the initial legislative objective for the Republican administration. Rank-and-file members of Congress indicated that insufficient time had been allocated for a thorough analysis of the bill's provisions prior to its introduction. Establishment Republicans pointed to the House Freedom Caucus for their unwillingness to compromise, while the Freedom Caucus, in turn, faced accusations from some, such as Adam Kinzinger, of single-handedly preserving Obamacare.

Despite these diverse explanations, the ultimate responsibility for the outcome rests with the President. Trump had campaigned on the promise of being the singular individual capable of resolving the nation's most pressing challenges, positioning himself as an unparalleled dealmaker with a proven history of success in high-pressure negotiations. However, when confronted with the critical moment of advancing his legislative agenda to a vote, he ultimately opted for withdrawal. The prevailing narrative that is likely to emerge is that "no deal is better than a bad deal." Yet, Trump's own public statements from that day contradict this assertion. On the very day the vote was scheduled, he tweeted enthusiastically about the legislation, describing it as a "great plan" and a vital opportunity for Republicans to fulfill their campaign pledges.

President Trump is anticipated to frame this outcome as a victory, a common practice for him, and subsequently pivot to other policy priorities. He articulated this strategy to Bob Costa, stating, "The beauty is that they [Democrats] own Obamacare. So when it explodes, they come to us, and we make one beautiful deal for the people." Nevertheless, the undeniable reality is that Trump actively pursued this deal, exerted considerable pressure on his own party, and ultimately retreated when its passage appeared improbable. In essence, Dealmaker Donald engaged in a high-stakes game of chicken but faltered at the decisive moment.

### Republican Health Care Overhaul Bill Pulled from House Floor Amidst Vote Shortage

House Speaker Paul Ryan announced on Friday, March 24, 2017, that the Republican health care overhaul bill was being abruptly withdrawn from the House floor. This decision came as the bill lacked sufficient votes, and leadership sought to avoid a significant and embarrassing defeat for President Donald Trump and Republican leaders. The failure marked a major setback for the GOP's long-standing promise to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA).

For years, House Republicans had passed numerous bills aimed at dismantling President Barack Obama's signature health care law. However, these efforts were largely symbolic, knowing they would not become law with Obama in office. This time, with a Republican president and Congress, the party faced its first genuine opportunity to repeal and replace the ACA. The proposed legislation, intended to eliminate the individual mandate, expand Medicaid, and trim federal subsidies, represented the culmination of seven years of unsuccessful attempts by the GOP to unite behind a replacement plan.

The bill ultimately faltered due to deep divisions within the Republican caucus. Centrist Republicans felt the bill went too far, while conservatives argued it was not aggressive enough in repealing the ACA. This internal discord, combined with unified Democratic opposition, proved insurmountable. Adding to the pressure, nonpartisan congressional analysts projected that the bill would lead to 24 million people losing health coverage within a decade and increase costs for lower-income and older individuals. This assessment was echoed by opposition from various groups, including doctors, hospitals, consumer advocacy organizations, and AARP.

The failure to deliver on a central campaign promise to repeal and replace the ACA presented a significant political challenge for Republicans. For nearly a decade, candidates had pledged to dismantle Obama's law, a commitment that was a cornerstone of President Trump's presidential campaign. Speaker Ryan acknowledged the difficulty of providing a satisfactory answer to constituents, admitting, "I don't know how long it's going to take us to repeal this law."

Democrats, staunch defenders of the ACA, reacted with visible elation. The ACA, credited with extending coverage to 20 million people, mandates that insurers cover essential services and prohibits them from denying coverage to individuals with pre-existing conditions. Top congressional Republicans acknowledged the need to shift focus to other legislative priorities. Some House authors of the bill indicated a desire to move on, with one declaring, "D-O-N-E done. This bill is dead." Republicans stated they would now concentrate on tax reform.

Despite the immediate setback, some expressed hope for alternative solutions. However, the path forward remains complex. Reforming the nation's healthcare system, which constitutes one-sixth of the U.S. economy, involves intricate economic and political considerations. Striking a balance between conservative desires for a free-market approach and the concerns of moderates about coverage losses and increased costs presents a formidable challenge. Ironically, the bill's failure mirrored predictions made by former Speaker John Boehner, who had previously stated that a full repeal and replacement of the ACA was unlikely, noting that Republicans "never ever agree on health care."

Donald Trump had heard enough about policy and process. It was Thursday afternoon and members of the House Freedom Caucus were peppering the president with wonkish concerns about the American Health Care Act—the language that would leave Obamacare’s “essential health benefits” in place, the community rating provision that limited what insurers could charge certain patients, and whether the next two steps of Speaker Paul Ryan’s master plan were even feasible—when Trump decided to cut them off. "Forget about the little shit," Trump said, according to multiple sources in the room. "Let's focus on the big picture here." Story Continued Below The group of roughly 30 House conservatives, gathered around a mammoth, oval-shaped conference table in the Cabinet Room of the White House, exchanged disapproving looks. Trump wanted to emphasize the political ramifications of the bill's defeat; specifically, he said, it would derail his first-term agenda and imperil his prospects for reelection in 2020. The lawmakers nodded and said they understood. And yet they were disturbed by his dismissiveness. For many of the members, the "little shit" meant the policy details that could make or break their support for the bill—and have far-reaching implications for their constituents and the country. "We’re talking about one-fifth of our economy," a member told me afterward. Ultimately, the meeting failed to move any votes. Two Freedom Caucus members—Brian Babin and Ted Poe, both of Texas—told the president that they had switched to yes, but their decisions had already been registered with White House vote-counters prior to sitting down with Trump. (Their colleagues didn't appreciate the gesture, feeling that Babin and Poe were trying to score points with the president at their expense.) Upon returning to Capitol Hill, the Freedom Caucus gathered in a meeting room inside the Rayburn office building, discussed Trump's admonitions to them and took another vote. The tally had not changed: Of the group’s roughly three dozen members, two-thirds remained opposed, with only five or six of those saying they were "soft" in that stance. The president had been working on many of them individually in recent days, typically with what members described as "colorful" phone calls, littered with exaggerations and foul language and hilariously off-topic anecdotes. In some cases, the pressure worked. Jim Bridenstine, a Freedom Caucus member and longtime problem for the Republican leadership, agreed to back the bill after conversations with Trump and other administration officials. (It wasn't necessary to remind Bridenstine that he was a leading candidate to become NASA administrator, and would likely hurt his chances by voting against the president.) But by and large, Trump's first attempt to corral the Republican-controlled Congress—and particularly the Freedom Caucus, a rambunctious, ideologically charged collection of GOP legislators who have long refused to fall in line behind the party's leadership—failed miserably. That failure played a major role in the collapse of the American Health Care Act almost exactly 24 hours after their meeting at the White House, and now, as Trump warned, threatens to paralyze the president's first-year policy agenda and send Republicans into a damaging cycle of intra-party recrimination. By and large, Trump’s first attempt to corral the GOP Congress failed miserably and threatens to paralyze his first-year policy agenda. There will be sufficient blame to go around in the days ahead, and indeed, some Trump loyalists are already pointing the finger at Ryan and his leadership team. The speaker, without question, was clumsy in his strategic rollout and far too presumptuous about the legislation's infallibility. But for a president who pledged to break through the gridlock in Washington—and who promoted himself as a peerless dealmaker—the defeat of Trump's first major policy initiative undermines his take-charge image and emboldens his enemies in both parties. Tom Price, the new Health and Human Services secretary who previously served 12 years in Congress, had assured nervous allies recently that the difference between Ryan's speakership and that of Speaker John Boehner—who was driven from office by the intransigence of the Freedom Caucus—was that unlike his predecessor, Ryan had the backing of a strong Republican president. Mick Mulvaney, Trump's budget director and a former Freedom Caucus member himself, made similar arguments. But faced with his first major test, the president failed—on multiple occasions and on many levels. For starters, Trump kept the GOP health care bill at arm's length for more than a week, offering a smattering of favorable remarks but failing to embrace it in convincing fashion. Ryan's rivals on Capitol Hill got the message: The president was lukewarm about the legislation. According to interviews with officials in all three camps—the White House, the Republican leadership and the Freedom Caucus—conservatives saw a schism between Trump and Ryan, and seized on the perceived opening. Early last week, during a budgetary meeting at the White House, the two leaders of the Freedom Caucus—chairman Mark Meadows and former chairman Jim Jordan—kept diverting the discussion to health care, much to the annoyance of Budget Committee chairwoman Diane Black. When the meeting broke, Meadows and Jordan swiftly sought an audience with the president to discuss Ryan's bill. Trump granted them the meeting, during which the conservatives complained that Ryan was presenting them with a "binary choice"—either vote for the bill that had been introduced, or vote to preserve Obamacare—that was doing the president a disservice. Trump replied that he was open to negotiation and new ideas, and Meadows and Jordan left the White House thinking they had a powerful ally. Ryan’s team was less than thrilled at the narrative of a good cop, bad cop routine. Things went downhill quickly from there. After Meadows visited Trump at Mar-a-Lago, the president’s Florida retreat, that weekend to push for conservative changes—capping a week of futile discussions about significantly altering the House bill—Freedom Caucus members were eager to hear from Trump on Tuesday when he arrived at the Capitol. But when he rose to address the GOP conference, the president made it clear there would be no further modifications, and said he expected Republicans to rally around Ryan's bill. Then Trump made a mistake. After singling out Meadows and asking him to stand up in front of his colleagues, Trump joked that he might "come after" the Freedom Caucus boss if he didn't vote yes, and then added, with a more serious tone: "I think Mark Meadows will get on board." It was a crucial misreading of Meadows, who has been determined to please both the White House and his conservatives colleagues on the Hill. Upon assuming the chairmanship of the Freedom Caucus earlier this year, Meadows was viewed suspiciously by some of his members who worried that the North Carolina congressman is too cozy with Trump and would hesitate to defy him. Meadows campaigned extensively with Trump last fall and struck up a relationship with White House chief strategist Steve Bannon, who communicates with him almost daily by text. Meadows knew the health care fight would be viewed as a test of his independence from Trump, and the moment the president called him out, he was boxed in. "That was the biggest mistake the president could have made," one Freedom Caucus member told me. "Mark desperately wanted to get to yes, and Trump made it impossible for him. If he flipped after that he would look incredibly weak." That was the biggest mistake the president could have made. Mark desperately wanted to get to yes, and Trump made it impossible for him.” Disappointed with the episode, and with the president's apparent inflexibility, Meadows and other Freedom Caucus members back-channeled with the administration and landed what they thought was an invitation to the White House on Wednesday morning. They hoped for a meeting with Trump and an opportunity to negotiate some major policy changes directly with him. Instead, they found themselves hauled into the less-than-inspiring Executive Office Building for a pep rally with Vice President Mike Pence, chief of staff Reince Priebus, Bannon, and other members of Trump's inner circle—but not the president himself. (As an aside, it’s impossible to ignore the failure of Pence, Price and Mulvaney, three former conservative darlings while in the Congress, to sell more of their ideological brethren on this bill.) Members of the Freedom Caucus realized right away that there would be no negotiating. Pence tried to pump up the conservatives, telling them the fight was theirs to win and that they needed to help Trump and Ryan score a victory for the new administration. The plea landed on deaf ears. "Take one for the team" was a phrase repeatedly deployed; at one point, after Bannon used it, Joe Barton, a white-haired conservative from Texas, snapped back in response that Bannon was talking to them like children and he didn't appreciate it. The room filled with uncomfortable silence; Bannon backed down and the meeting went on. (Barton eventually announced his support for the legislation—one of only a few who would eventually switch positions.) After several hours, the members returned to the Capitol feeling frustrated, calling the meeting "a waste of time" and wondering if they had missed their only window for cutting a deal with Trump. That night, however, allies in the White House sent word to the Freedom Caucus that one thing they wanted—reforms to the “essential health benefits” provision under Title I of the Affordable Care Act—could be done. Excitement spread in the group, but there was also confusion; some members felt that would be a big enough concession to win their vote, while others felt it was only a step in the right direction. As they sought to clarify their internal disagreements, there was another meeting scheduled for the next morning, Thursday—this one at the White House and with the president himself. Filled with hope once again, Freedom Caucus members were once again promptly disappointed. This meeting was yet another "take one for the team" seminar. The atmosphere was friendly, and the president had the group laughing with irrelevant riffs and stories of negotiations past, but it became clear, as soon as he made the "little shit" comment, that no serious changes were going to be made, because the president didn't have sufficient command of the policy details to negotiate what would or would not be realistic for Ryan to shepherd through the House. Through charm, force of personality and sheer intimidation, Trump did move some votes into the yes column. But GOP leaders were left wondering why he didn't do more—why he didn't send tweets, travel to congressional districts, put his famed dealmaking skills to work. The answer, to Republicans on both ends of Pennsylvania Avenue, is obvious: Because he lacked familiarity with the legislation itself, and thought it was Ryan’s job to sell the specifics. “Trump is a business executive. When he tells his lieutenants to get something done, he's used to it getting done,” one senior House GOP aide told me. “He’s really not used to getting involved himself.” *** If the bill failed because Trump is a great salesman with a poor grasp of policy, it also failed because Ryan is a poor salesman with a great grasp of policy. The speaker has spent decades straddling the worlds of politics and policy, and is infinitely more comfortable operating in the latter. He has dozens of friends around town in the constellation of conservative think tanks, lobby shops, activist groups and media outlets. Knowing that health care was batting leadoff for the new, unified Republican government, it would seem a no-brainer for the speaker to spend a few days, if not a few weeks, meeting with leading voices on the right to introduce the American Health Care Act, answer their questions, accept their criticisms and, most important, preempt any attacks on the legislation itself. After all, as Democrats love to point out, Ryan had seven years to plan for this moment—first as Budget chairman, then as Ways and Means chairman, then as speaker—and if anyone on the right was ready, it ought to have been him. But Ryan didn’t feel such preventative measures were necessary. After days of drafting the bill in secretive locations at the Capitol—and Sen. Rand Paul, a hard-core Obamacare critic, exposing the absurdity by bringing reporters along as he hunted door-to-door for a copy—the text was leaked, and then unceremoniously released, without any clearly coordinated media strategy between the speaker's office and the White House. Conservatives around Washington, including some of Ryan’s longtime friends, were stunned. “The bill has had the worst rollout of any major piece of legislation in memory,” Rich Lowry, editor of National Review and a longtime Ryan ally, wrote in his Politico Magazine column on March 15. Back in 2013, when the so-called Gang of Eight had authored its comprehensive immigration reform bill, Sen. Marco Rubio spent weeks making the rounds and meeting with top influencers on the right, taking unlimited time to answer every question and consider every criticism. He talked to journalists, grassroots leaders and academics; he offered himself as a human sacrifice to every prominent voice in conservative talk radio, attempting to neutralize opposition to the bill before it materialized. It never became law, but Rubio did everything he could. It passed the Senate, at least, before dying a quick death in the House—and that was in large measure thanks to having a media-savvy Tea Party darling take the lead and work conservative journalists and opinion leaders. There was no such effort on Ryan’s part, and it showed. (Several allies argued he had done some outreach, but they failed to provide any specific examples.) After he unveiled the bill, leading health care experts on the right like Yuval Levin and Avik Roy trashed it as a poorly conceived mess; conservative pressure groups and their media allies immediately branded it as “Obamacare Lite.” Only then did Ryan move aggressively to mitigate the damage. He convened a group of conservative journalists in his office in mid-March and his team began tracking, and publicizing, every media appearance he made, especially promoting his interviews with conservative critics such as Sean Hannity and Laura Ingraham. But it was too little, too late. Loathing of the legislation had already reached a fever pitch and Ryan was powerless to steady the situation. The only thing he could have done to appease the far right—a full repeal of the Affordable Care Act—was not only legislatively impossible but would never pass the lower chamber of Congress. The vast majority of Republicans would have refused to eliminate Obamacare without knowing exactly what would replace it until months or years later. In dissecting Ryan's lackluster marketing of the health care initiative, both his allies and adversaries in the Republican conference reached the same conclusion: He had taken support for granted. After all, this was essentially the same bill written by Price when he was in Congress; it became the de facto proposal of the House GOP in its “Better Way” agenda, which Ryan argued throughout 2016 was a governing document that his colleagues universally supported. "We all ran on this," Ryan said repeatedly over the past several weeks. Except that many House Republicans never saw it that way. In fact, some conservatives spent the past year using "Better Way" as a punch line to tease the speaker for convening wonky groups to dream up big policy proposals—but never hold votes on any of them. If there had been committee hearings and floor votes, some conservatives argue, these differences would have surfaced much sooner. "We ran on these principles, but not on this bill," Meadows told me last week. (Meadows was, however, once a co-sponsor of Price’s bill in Congress.) By Friday morning, there were questions about why Trump had kept a distance from the bill for such a lengthy period of time—and how different the whip count might look if he hadn’t. As the reality of the bill’s likely defeat set in Thursday, and some members of Trump’s team began to assign blame to Ryan—most notably in a New York Times story—the speaker’s allies didn’t stand pat. They began subtly, first by calling Trump “the closer,” and then by emphasizing that the White House felt confident it would deliver the Freedom Caucus. By Friday morning, there were more overt questions about why Trump had kept a distance from the bill for such a lengthy period of time—and how different the whip count might look if he hadn’t. Of course, leadership officials also were eager to blame the Freedom Caucus, claiming the group simply has no interest in voting yes. But the fact is, after Thursday night's impromptu conference meeting—in which Mulvaney delivered Trump's ultimatum that he would move on from health care after Friday's vote—the number of conservatives still opposed to the bill had dwindled significantly. There were 27 Freedom Caucus members voting no at the beginning of the week; by late Thursday, that number appeared to drop below 20. Jordan, worried that conservative opposition might be crumbling, spent Thursday night and Friday morning whipping his comrades to prevent further defections, members said. Trump’s attempt to cajole the group into submission—tweeting Friday morning about the "irony" of its members opposing abortion but voting against a bill that removed Planned Parenthood funding—didn’t work, and probably backfired, just like his singling out of Meadows. I was told the members were slightly irritated but mostly laughed it off as "Trump being Trump," and expressed surprise that he hadn't tried to publicly pressure them ever sooner. *** All this obscures an uncomfortable question for Republicans as they ponder how it is that they control both houses of Congress and the presidency, and yet were unable to get rid of a hated law they spent seven years attempting to destroy. Perhaps the problem isn’t who deserves the blame, so much as how to solve the puzzle that is the fractious GOP majority—the same impossible physics problem that bedeviled and ultimately doomed John Boehner. After all, it wasn’t just conservatives who sank the AHCA. The Freedom Caucus remains a stubborn problem for GOP leadership, but blaming—or crediting—its members for Friday’s defeat ignores the fact that some two dozen moderate and centrist members were also opposed. "There's no natural constituency for this bill," Raul Labrador, a Freedom Caucus co-founder, said throughout the week. He was right. Members care about policy and process, and between the two, there was no clear upside for many of Ryan's members: It left too many people without coverage and failed to drive down premiums; it also was re-written hastily to accommodate changes and felt rushed for no good reason. Ultimately, every concession made to win conservatives, like the amendment that left regulating essential health benefits up to states, was destined to result in the loss of moderates. Perhaps the problem isn’t who deserves the blame, so much as how to solve the puzzle that is the fractious GOP majority. In the corridors surrounding the House chamber, the death knell was felt just before 11 a.m. That's when Rodney Frelinghuysen, chairman of the all-powerful Appropriations Committee, announced he wouldn't be supporting the bill. Republican lawmakers buzzed with disbelief as the news spread across the floor. Losing a committee chairman is never a good sign for leadership; the defection of the Appropriations chairman felt like a nail in the American Health Care Act's coffin. An hour later, two sources—one in the House leadership, one in the Trump administration—confirmed that the whip count was moving in the wrong direction. One of them told me that Ryan was considering pulling the bill from the floor altogether, to prevent a lopsided defeat that would only serve to hand ammunition to Democrats running against Republicans who had been bold enough to vote for the bill anyway. As I processed this, news broke that Ryan was headed the White House. He wanted to show Trump the numbers and consult him before yanking the bill from consideration. Soon enough the decision was made, and Ryan headed back to the Capitol, calling an all-conference meeting in the House basement. Walking toward the tunnel that connects the House office buildings to the Capitol itself, I ran into Mark Walker, the chairman of the Republican Study Committee, a large caucus that was once home to the conservative movement in Congress before being eclipsed in recent years by the more ideologically pure Freedom Caucus. Walker had initially been against the bill, but came on board quickly after some changes, and in doing so validated the critiques of his group by those further to the right. A former minister, Walker is by nature relaxed and genteel, but his face was burning red and his voice trembled as we discussed the bill's defeat. “I’m very bothered. I'm disappointed,” he said, measuring his words. “This was a chance to repeal all the Obamacare taxes. It was a chance to take off the burdensome mandate we've stuck on our employers and individuals who have begged for help. It [has] additional pro-life provisions. It destroys the chance to do the biggest Medicaid revision that we've had in what, 51, 52 years? Yeah, I'm bothered by it.” On the opposite end of the spectrum were Meadows, Jordan, Labrador and Michigan Rep. Justin Amash, arguably the four core members of the Freedom Caucus. Moments before I talked to Walker, I had intercepted the four of them walking toward the meeting room. They hadn't heard the news; when I told them Ryan had pulled the bill, they exchanged glances and tried to suppress grins. Only Meadows looked upset; a southern gentleman and successful businessman, he wants to be liked by everyone, and the episode clearly took an emotional toll on him. He declined to provide a comment. So did Labrador and Amash. But Jordan, the godfather of the House conservatives—he arrived four years prior to the tea party wave of 2010—made clear that he wouldn't go along with Trump's decree that Republicans would abandon health care and move on to tax reform. “We want to see Obamacare repealed,” Jordan told me. “That hasn’t changed.” Ryan, for his part, told reporters in a somber press conference a short while later that he stood with Trump. Obamacare, that great white whale Republicans had long hunted—and hoped to harpoon on its seven-year anniversary Thursday—would remain “the law of the land” due to the GOP's inability to function as a “governing body,” the speaker of the House announced. They had failed at fixing the health-care system; next they would try to overhaul the tax code. If you couldn’t get health care done, how can you get tax reform done?” The improbability of this sequence was not lost on anyone. Earlier, as Ryan's motorcade was zipping toward the White House, I spoke with Kevin Brady, the Ways and Means chairman whose committee sits at the intersection of health care and taxes. I’ve known Brady, one of Congress’s truly decent people and a reliably cheerful spirit, for years; never had I seen him looking so despondent and defeated. Positing that health care was about to die, I asked Brady if re-writing the tax code would be any easier. “Tax reform is the hardest lift in a generation,” he told me, shaking his head. “So that would be a big challenge.” “If you couldn’t get health care done,” I ask him, “how can you get tax reform done?” Brady thought for a moment. “Every Republican is all-in on tax reform. We still have a lot of work. But it’s just a natural issue for us in a very positive way.” But every Republican was all-in on repealing and replacing Obamacare, too, I told him. “Won't the devil be in the details?” Brady stared back at me. “It always is,” he said. “It always is.” Tim Alberta is national political reporter at Politico Magazine.

query: What specific accusations has Donald Trump made regarding the integrity of the election process in Pennsylvania?

documents.text: To ensure election vigilance, law enforcement, including sheriffs and police chiefs, must be involved. This is vital because any threat to the election's integrity, particularly if it jeopardizes a win in Pennsylvania, a state of great importance, would render the results unfair. Given the current circumstances, it is improbable that she can surmount these obstacles.

This analysis delves into the August 10, 2016, NBC News/Wall Street Journal/Marist Poll, focusing on the electoral landscape in three pivotal battleground states: Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania. These states collectively hold 44 electoral votes, making their outcomes critical to the presidential race. The poll reveals a closely contested race in Iowa, while Ohio shows a slight Democratic advantage, and Pennsylvania offers a more substantial lead for the Democratic ticket.

In Iowa, the poll indicates a tight contest between Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine, and Donald Trump and Mike Pence. In a two-way matchup, Clinton leads Trump by a narrow 4-point margin, 41% to 37%. This represents a slight shift from the previous poll. When third-party candidates Gary Johnson and Jill Stein are included, Clinton and Trump are statistically tied at 35%. Notably, Gary Johnson garners significant support in Iowa, capturing 12% of registered voters, while Jill Stein secures 6%, her strongest performance among the three states.

Ohio's electorate presents a slightly different picture, with Clinton holding a 5-point lead over Trump, 43% to 38%. This marks an increase from the prior month, when both candidates were tied. Clinton maintains a similar advantage when Johnson and Stein are factored in. Johnson also achieves double-digit support in Ohio with 12%, while Stein garners 4%.

Pennsylvania demonstrates the widest margin among the three states, with Clinton leading Trump by 11 points, 48% to 37%. This expanded lead is an increase from the previous poll. The inclusion of third-party candidates does not significantly alter the dynamics in Pennsylvania. Across these battleground states, Clinton has seen gains among moderates. Her lead over Trump among this demographic has increased in Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania, with the most pronounced shift occurring in Ohio.

Dr. Lee M. Miringoff, Director of The Marist College Institute for Public Opinion, noted that despite the national conventions, neither Clinton nor Trump has significantly improved voters' positive impressions of them. Both candidates face substantial unfavorability ratings in these key states, with figures consistently high across Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania. These unfavorability ratings remain consistent with the July poll.

The vice-presidential candidates, Tim Kaine and Mike Pence, exhibit low name recognition across all three states. A significant portion of voters in Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania are unfamiliar with both Kaine and Pence, with varying degrees of favorable and unfavorable opinions among those who do recognize them.

In the U.S. Senate races, incumbent Republican Chuck Grassley maintains a lead in Iowa. In Ohio, incumbent Republican Rob Portman holds a 5-point advantage, a shift from the previous month's tie. Pennsylvania's Senate race shows Democrat Katie McGinty with a slight edge over incumbent Republican Pat Toomey.

President Barack Obama's job approval ratings show variation by state. He performs best in Pennsylvania, with consistent approval ratings. Iowa shows a slight shift in approval and disapproval, while Ohio indicates an increase in approval from the previous month.

This analysis is based on the complete August 10, 2016, NBC News/Wall Street Journal/Marist Poll releases for Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania, including detailed methodologies and sample information for each state.

Donald Trump has intensified his claims that the upcoming presidential election could be rigged to favor Hillary Clinton. He has specifically stated that the only way he could lose Pennsylvania is "if cheating goes on." While Trump frequently cited opinion polls during his successful primary campaign, his current deficit in national surveys has led him to question the integrity of the electoral process even before voting begins.

During a rally in Altoona, Pennsylvania, Trump declared, "We’re gonna watch Pennsylvania. Go down to certain areas and watch and study and make sure other people don’t come in and vote five times. The only way we can lose, in my opinion – and I really mean this, Pennsylvania – is if cheating goes on. I really believe it." He further explained his concerns, stating, "I looked over Pennsylvania and I’m studying it. And we have some great people here. Some great leaders here of the Republican Party, and they’re very concerned about that, and that’s the way we can lose the state, and we have to call up law enforcement, and we have to have the sheriffs and the police chiefs and everybody watching. Because if we get cheated out of this election, if we get cheated out of a win in Pennsylvania, which is such a vital state, especially when I know what’s happening here, folks. I know. She can’t beat what’s happening here."

It is worth noting that a Pennsylvania judge invalidated a law requiring state-approved photo identification for voting in 2014. George H.W. Bush was the last Republican presidential candidate to win Pennsylvania in 1988. Trump is attempting to defeat Clinton by appealing to the state's significant blue-collar population. He asserted, "The only way they can beat it in my opinion – and I mean this 100% – if in certain sections of the state they cheat, OK? So I hope you people can sort of not just vote on the 8th, go around and look and watch other polling places and make sure that it’s 100% fine, because without voter identification – which is shocking, shocking that you don’t have it."

Current polling data from a Quinnipiac survey released Tuesday shows Trump trailing Clinton by 52% to 42% in Pennsylvania. This places the state among several key battlegrounds where Trump's support has declined since the party conventions and following a series of public relations challenges. As his presidential prospects appear to dim, Trump has increasingly adopted an aggressive stance against perceived biases in the electoral system, a tactic rarely employed by other candidates in recent history. Earlier this month, he told Fox News, "You don’t have to have voter ID to now go in and vote, and it’s a little bit scary, and I’ve heard a lot of bad things. I mean, people are going to walk in, they are going to vote 10 times maybe." He also told the Washington Post, "If the election is rigged, I would not be surprised." Last week, while campaigning in Ohio, he expressed his concern that "the election is going to be rigged," without offering any supporting evidence. Interviews with Trump supporters at a Florida rally revealed a sentiment that any potential loss would be attributed solely to electoral irregularities.

Studies consistently indicate that instances of voter fraud are exceptionally rare. President Barack Obama publicly refuted Trump's claims, stating, "Of course the elections will not be rigged." He elaborated, "What does that mean? The federal government doesn’t run the election process. States and cities and communities all across the country, they are the ones who set up the voting systems and the voting booths. And if Mr Trump is suggesting that there is a conspiracy theory that is being propagated across the country, including in places like Texas, where typically it’s not Democrats who are in charge of voting booths, that’s ridiculous. That doesn’t make any sense. And I don’t think anybody would take that seriously." The President further commented, "This will be an election like every other election. And I think all of us at some points in our lives have played sports or maybe just played in a schoolyard or a sandbox. And sometimes folks, if they lose, they start complaining that they got cheated. But I’ve never heard of somebody complaining about being cheated before the game was over, or before the score is even tallied. So my suggestion would be go out there and try to win the election. If Mr Trump is up 10 or 15 points on Election Day and ends up losing, then maybe he can raise some questions. That doesn’t seem to be the case at the moment."

During the Altoona rally, Trump also alleged that the National Security Agency possesses Hillary Clinton's missing emails and called for their public release. He was joined in Pennsylvania by Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus, amid speculation of internal party divisions. Meanwhile, Bill Clinton was campaigning in Las Vegas, Nevada. Addressing a forum for Asian American voters, the former president framed the election as a reflection of America's core identity as a nation of immigrants, rather than solely a policy choice. Speaking to a large, diverse audience at Caesars Palace, Clinton remarked that "the metaphor of this election may be walls or bridges." He asked the crowd, predominantly comprising voters from a rapidly growing demographic, "Are we stronger together or stronger apart?" He urged them to "remind people that e pluribus unum -- out of many, one -- is not just a slogan, it’s a way of life. You remind people that the eternal effort of making our union more perfect means expanding our definition of us and shrinking our definition of them, not the other way around."

Donald Trump's assertion that President Barack Obama founded ISIS has followed a complex and contradictory trajectory, with the Republican nominee appearing to retract and then reaffirm his statement within a matter of hours. Initially, Trump suggested his accusation was merely "sarcasm," a position he quickly abandoned, seemingly reinforcing the inflammatory rhetoric.

### The "Founder of ISIS" Claim Emerges

The controversy began when Trump first voiced the provocative accusation during a rally in Sunrise, Florida, on Wednesday evening. "ISIS is honoring President Obama. He is the founder of ISIS," Trump declared. "He is the founder of ISIS, OK? He’s the founder. He founded ISIS. And I would say the co-founder would be crooked Hillary Clinton."

His rhetoric intensified on Thursday. During a speech to the National Association of Home Builders in Miami, he asserted, "Our government isn’t giving us good protection. Our government has unleashed ISIS." He reiterated, "I call President Obama and Hillary Clinton the founders of ISIS. They’re the founders. In fact, I think we’ll give Hillary Clinton the — you know, if you’re on a sports team, most valuable player, MVP, you get the MVP award — ISIS will hand her the most valuable player award. Her only competition is Barack Obama."

These remarks followed interviews on CNBC and with conservative radio host Hugh Hewitt on Thursday morning. On "Squawk Box," Trump stated that Obama and Clinton deserved ISIS's most valuable player award. When Hewitt offered an opportunity to clarify, suggesting Trump meant Obama created a vacuum that allowed ISIS to emerge, Trump responded, "No, I meant he’s the founder of ISIS. I do."

### Shifting Explanations and Defenses

The situation shifted Friday morning when Trump tweeted, "Ratings challenged @CNN reports so seriously that I call President Obama (and Clinton) ‘the founder’ of ISIS, & MVP,” adding, “THEY DON'T GET SARCASM?” He appeared to enjoy the ensuing media confusion, tweeting approximately 90 minutes later about the inability of pundits to understand his intentions. "I love watching these poor, pathetic people (pundits) on television working so hard and so seriously to try and figure me out. They can't!" he declared.

However, during an afternoon rally in Erie, Pennsylvania, Trump softened his "sarcasm" defense. "So I said the founder of ISIS," he recalled to the crowd, after criticizing Obama's leadership. "Obviously I’m being sarcastic. Then — but not that sarcastic, to be honest with you."

Trump's campaign defended his remarks. Senior adviser Sarah Huckabee Sanders identified the term "founder" as the "point of sarcasm" but maintained that the rise of ISIS was a consequence of Obama and Clinton's policies. "And that can't be disputed. ISIS didn’t exist before the Obama presidency," she stated on CNN, despite historical accounts tracing the group's origins to before 2009. Sanders insisted Trump's intention was to elevate the issue in the campaign, which she argued he achieved. "I think the point that he’s trying to make is the contrast that exists, and he’s trying to bring out a really important issue and make everybody talk about it, and that's exactly what we're doing right now," she said. "I bet that you guys will spend all day talking about whether or not Obama played a role in the formation of ISIS."

Trump special counsel Michael Cohen repeatedly declined to explain Trump's rhetoric in a separate CNN interview with Chris Cuomo, insisting Trump was the best person to clarify. However, Cohen eventually offered an explanation, stating, "Mr. Trump is claiming that, you know, President Obama and Secretary Clinton are the founders of ISIS. What he's referring to, and he’s talked about it so many times, is of course the fact that ISIS grew and grew out of control and is now a threat to our national security."

### Reactions and Context

This wavering attempt at damage control followed Trump's repeated insistence on the "founder" claim throughout Thursday. The situation also unfolded ahead of a critical meeting between Trump's campaign and Republican Party officials in Orlando, Florida. This gathering occurred as numerous prominent Republicans urged the Republican National Committee to withdraw support for Trump and reallocate funds to bolster Republican congressional majorities.

Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich, a Trump surrogate, expressed frustration with Trump's "imprecise language" and tendency towards "shorthand." Gingrich told Fox News that Trump "sometimes uses three words when he needs 10." He suggested that if Trump had explained that the withdrawal from Iraq created the vacuum for ISIS, his statement would have been "100 percent accurate." Gingrich cautioned, "When you instead comprise them into ‘Obama created ISIS’ — I know what Trump has in his mind, but that’s not what people hear." He advised Trump to "learn to use language that has been thought through and that is clear to everybody and to stick to that language because otherwise the mainstream media is gonna take every possible excuse to pile on him."

Massachusetts Rep. Seth Moulton, a Clinton supporter and Iraq War veteran, characterized Trump as a candidate with a "clueless" and "hazardous temperament." Moulton told CNN's "New Day," "I mean, what happens when he presses the nuclear button and then the next day says, ‘Oh, I’m sorry, I was just being sarcastic’? It’s ridiculous that we’re even talking about someone like this being our commander in chief, and it’s dangerous for our troops."

RNC Chairman Reince Priebus, who introduced Trump at his Friday rally, appeared to offer support. Despite strained relations between Trump and the party, Priebus told the crowd, "Don’t believe the garbage you read. Let me tell you something: Donald Trump, the Republican Party, all of you, we’re gonna put him in the White House and save this country together."

This "founder" comment is the latest in a series of controversies surrounding Trump since securing the GOP nomination. These include a public dispute with a Gold Star family and remarks concerning "Second Amendment people" and their potential to prevent Hillary Clinton from appointing liberal Supreme Court justices.

Donald Trump (AP Photo/Evan Vucci) Update: This article originally ran when Donald Trump made comments about voter fraud in Pennsylvania in August. On a Saturday night rally in the state, he repeated the claim, so we're republishing it with some updated numbers. Donald Trump held a rally in Altoona, Pa., on Friday night, during which he told the audience that the only way Hillary Clinton could win the state was if "in certain sections of the state they cheat." CBS's Sopan Deb transcribed Trump's comments. We're gonna watch Pennsylvania. Go down to certain areas and watch and study and make sure other people don't come in and vote five times. ... The only way we can lose, in my opinion -- and I really mean this, Pennsylvania -- is if cheating goes on. I really believe it. Because I looked at Erie and it was the same thing as this. ... [L]et me just tell you, I looked over Pennsylvania. And I'm studying it. And we have some great people here. Some great leaders here of the Republican Party, and they're very concerned about that. And that's the way we can lose the state. And we have to call up law enforcement. And we have to have the sheriffs and the police chiefs and everybody watching. Because if we get cheated out of this election, if we get cheated out of a win in Pennsylvania, which is such a vital state, especially when I know what's happening here, folks. I know. She can't beat what's happening here. The only way they can beat it in my opinion -- and I mean this 100 percent -- if in certain sections of the state they cheat, OK? So I hope you people can sort of not just vote on the 8th, go around and look and watch other polling places and make sure that it's 100 percent fine, because without voter identification -- which is shocking, shocking that you don't have it. There is almost no actual in-person voter fraud. In a survey of 1 billion ballots cast between 2000 and 2014, 241 possible -- possible! -- fraudulent ballots were found. Several of those ballots were cast in elections in Pennsylvania where a man named "Joseph Cheeseboro" and another named "Joseph J. Cheeseborough" each cast a ballot. That's all that was uncovered in Pennsylvania. The "certain sections of the state" to which Trump is referring is almost certainly are a reference to a long-standing conspiracy theory involving the results in Philadelphia in 2012, where, in some places Mitt Romney got zero votes. Trump ally Sean Hannity raised it during a dispute with CNN's Brian Stelter. Calling us unpatriotic? 59 districts not one Romney vote. So you are voting for the liar HRC. Did DNC hurt Bernie? https://t.co/Wuf5Sv9nCR — Sean Hannity (@seanhannity) August 7, 2016 The Philadelphia Inquirer wrote about the absence of Romney votes after the election, but included a key phrase: "The unanimous support for [President] Obama in these Philadelphia neighborhoods -- clustered in almost exclusively black sections of West and North Philadelphia -- fertilizes fears of fraud, despite little hard evidence." Little hard evidence -- and the voting was clustered in areas with a high percentage of black voters. Obama won 93 percent of the black vote in 2012. But you don't have to take my word for it. An inspector of elections in Philadelphia, Ryan Godfrey, replied to Hannity on Twitter. Some excerpts: 1. I'm an inspector of elections for a Philly voting division. Independent but was a Republican as recently as June. https://t.co/pd82mOkEKh — Ryan Godfrey (@rgodfrey) August 7, 2016 Claim that 59 divisions in Philadelphia engaged in electoral fraud in 2012 because no votes for Romney is absurd & personally insulting. First, there's absolutely no way to erase votes from the machines we use in this city. ... Next, we get a paper tally at the end of the night that we match against physical count of voters who used machines (like an odometer). We match that against the count of the individual names of voters who have signed our rolls (and whose names we also recorded in books). ... So, # of votes corresponds with # of voters, & can't be tampered with after fact, but what about having machines change R votes to D? ... Why would they ever change *ALL* R votes to D votes, when anybody who voted R could easily refute the results just by saying they had? He continues -- but that last point is key, and he reiterates it: 18. The Philadelphia Inquirer couldn't find anyone who cast a vote for Romney. Anyone. https://t.co/5hmtwwP1wi — Ryan Godfrey (@rgodfrey) August 7, 2016 Let's move to the bigger picture. In July 2012, the state stipulated that there had been no in-person voter fraud in the state. There have been instances of other types of fraud, as Godfrey notes, including an incident in which election workers in Philadelphia tampered with voting machines to add six votes. There have also been many instances in which voter rolls in different counties had two people that appeared to be identical, like Mr. Cheeseboro -- but few instances in which both of those people have cast ballots and raised concerns. Usually, such dual ballots "melt down to one or two if any at all," a researcher from MIT told the Pittsburgh Tribune‑Review. Why? In part because they are usually data errors or people with similar names. But also because there are a lot of protections, as Godfrey outlines. (Those protections, in fact, made it hard for Trump to vote in 2004.) 1 of 60 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × What Donald Trump is doing on the campaign trail View Photos The GOP presidential nominee is out on the trail ahead of the general election in November. Caption The GOP presidential nominee is pressing his case ahead of Election Day. Nov. 7, 2016 Donald Trump speaks during a campaign event at SNHU Arena in Manchester, N.H. Jabin Botsford/The Washington Post Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. What's above is a tally of rare incidents. In 2012, 5.5 million presidential ballots were cast in Pennsylvania. There's no evidence that thousands of fraudulent votes were cast -- or hundreds, or dozen, or even a handful. [Rigging an election is a lot harder than you might think] Perhaps more importantly, Trump is also already losing Pennsylvania. The RealClearPolitics polling average in the state shows Clinton with a lead of more than nine points. This, of course, doesn't include Trump's alleged, unproven voter fraud. It's an average of a number of polls in the state. (Trump has claimed that polls under-represent his support; there's no evidence of that.) Trump is convinced that he will win Pennsylvania, it seems, because of the support he sees in the state. Fair enough. But thousands of people at a rally doesn't compare to millions of voters. Why is Trump likely to lose Pennsylvania? Half the state thinks Obama is doing a good job. Thirteen percent of the electorate in 2012 was black; 93 percent of those voters supported Obama. In a new NBC/Marist poll, Clinton gets 94 percent of the black vote. Clinton and Trump are tied among white voters, thanks to Clinton winning women by 25 points. Obama won them in 2012 by 13. Update: As the national race has tightened, so have polls in Pennsylvania. The most recent was conducted in late September, near the closest point of the Trump-Clinton race nationally. Clinton has a 2.4-point lead as it stands -- a figure that includes polls conducted after Trump's closing much of the national gap but before the first debate. What's more disconcerting than Trump's baseless assertion that Clinton can only win by cheating is his suggestion that his supporters -- and law enforcement -- police the polls. Even the Inquirer, which raised the question in 2012, notes that voter ID laws wouldn't somehow erase votes for Romney. What Trump is encouraging is vigilante citizens harassing voters at polling places, asking that they prove they are who they say they are. He's asking for intimidation, explicitly: Challenge suspect voters. This is a recipe for tension, if not violence -- and the lack of voter fraud incidents reveals that there's no purpose to it. Political campaigns often have staffers at polling places track who has voted for turnout efforts. Trump, ironically, has no real get-out-the-vote effort, which could help him police this imaginary threat. So he suggests he will turn to untrained supporters. American politics succeeds because the transfer of power is gracious. Trump's comments once again seem to anticipate that his response to what increasingly looks like it will be a loss will not fall into that category.

Donald Trump faces a significant challenge in securing undecided voters, particularly in crucial swing states like Pennsylvania. Republican strategist Frank Luntz emphasizes that Trump's high disapproval ratings necessitate capturing a substantial portion of the remaining electorate. Events following the Republican National Convention have not demonstrably improved his electoral standing. Pennsylvania, a state that has favored Democrats in the last six presidential elections, is vital for Trump's victory. His campaign identifies potential support in the state's conservative western regions, believing his stances on trade, immigration, and foreign policy could resonate with independents and blue-collar Democrats. However, his path to winning remains difficult.

During a rally in Erie, Trump underscored Pennsylvania's importance, declaring, "We must win Pennsylvania. If we win Pennsylvania, we will win it." Electoral analyses, however, suggest Trump must overcome a considerable deficit in the Philadelphia suburbs to carry the state. In 2012, President Obama won these suburbs by approximately nine percentage points over Mitt Romney, and Obama himself carried Pennsylvania by roughly five points. Current polling from an NBC News/Wall Street Journal/Marist College survey indicates Clinton holds a significant lead in these suburban districts, with 52 percent to Trump's 26 percent. G. Terry Madonna, director of the Franklin & Marshall College poll, explains the predicament: "There is no scenario where Trump wins Pennsylvania unless he can significantly broaden his appeal and overcome his substantial deficit in the suburbs." Madonna notes that while Trump connects with white working-class voters, their numbers alone are insufficient for a statewide victory. He also expresses doubt about Trump's ability to maintain focus on his core political messages.

Despite these reservations, Trump's advisors express confidence in their strategy and question the accuracy of public opinion polls. While acknowledging potential difficulties in appealing to women and minority voters, they believe Trump has opportunities to gain support among first-time voters, white men, and independents. They anticipate these groups will become more engaged once the presidential debates commence in late September. Paul Manafort, Trump's campaign chairman, views the debates as critical for influencing voter sentiment. He also points to increased Clinton campaign spending on television ads in Pennsylvania and Ohio as evidence of the electoral threat Trump poses in these states. Manafort stated, "We anticipate performing very well in the suburbs, which will help us carry Pennsylvania. The demographics are highly favorable for Donald Trump, and the issue agenda benefits Donald Trump in Pennsylvania."

While a Clinton campaign spokesperson declined to comment, many advisors and allies are optimistic about her prospects in Pennsylvania and other Rust Belt states. Candidates often adjust their policy positions during general elections to broaden their appeal, a strategy Trump has largely eschewed. Both Trump and Clinton face historically high unfavorable ratings and low favorability among undecided voters. However, Clinton has concentrated her campaign efforts on undecided voters in swing states since the party conventions, while Trump has indicated he will adhere to his current campaign approach, even if it leads to defeat.

In Bucks County, some Republican-leaning voters voiced disappointment with Trump's perceived attempts to alienate them. They concur with his policy proposals on repealing the Affordable Care Act and enacting tax cuts but are deterred by his rhetoric concerning women, Mexicans, and Muslims, as well as his mockery of a disabled reporter and his confrontational responses, such as his exchange with Khizr Khan, the father of a fallen soldier. Adam Woldow, a dermatologist from Richboro, Pa., who supported Romney in 2012 and McCain in 2008, remarked, "America’s role in the world is important to me, and I do not want a president who yells at other people." He added, "He carries so much baggage at this point, all the things he continues to say. I feel he is even somewhat racist." Woldow is currently leaning towards the Libertarian candidate, Gary Johnson.

Penny Clendaniel-Bullaro, an independent voter from Morrisville, Pa., who also voted for Romney, expressed dismay at Trump's insinuation that Khizr Khan's wife, Ghazala, was not "allowed" to speak at the Democratic convention. "Who is Trump to make these judgments? Trump speaks constantly with his own wife by his side, silent," she commented. "He should be discussing issues if he truly wants to win people over." Conversely, Mariann Davies, a lawyer from Doylestown who supports Trump, believes his popularity will grow among voters who oppose the continuation of the Obama administration's policies. "He is addressing the issues everyone is avoiding," Davies stated. "I think he could attract more voters if he would simply focus on trade policy and jobs because people in this area care deeply about those matters."

For most political figures, a direct communication style presents drawbacks, as alienating voters or appearing impulsive can lead to electoral failure. Trump, however, believes voters facing economic hardship will perceive him as an authentic voice. In the traditionally Democratic blue-collar areas of northeastern Pennsylvania, where Trump needs to perform strongly, some voters expressed skepticism about his leadership, despite sharing his concerns about immigration and national security. Ody Draklellis, a Republican diner owner in Allentown, observed that while constituents are open to Trump due to a lack of trust in Clinton and dissatisfaction with Obama's policies, "Mr. Trump himself" is his greatest obstacle. Draklellis pondered, "Could Trump be an effective president? Likely. But he might involve us in a war, so the risk is too high." He added, "One would think he would be entirely focused on all of Hillary’s weaknesses. He could grow in the polls that way. Instead, he just speaks carelessly."

Politicians frequently bend the meaning of words, a phenomenon exemplified by Donald Trump's evolving rhetoric. The word "literally," once strictly denoting something taken at face value, saw its definition expand by 2013 to include metaphorical usage, a shift acknowledged by major dictionaries. Trump has consistently tested the boundaries of language, often to underscore a point, but increasingly to retract or reframe damaging statements.

This pattern was evident when Trump, on a Wednesday, leveled accusations against President Barack Obama and Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton, labeling Obama the "founder" of ISIS and Clinton its "co-founder." Despite opportunities to clarify his remarks as hyperbolic or metaphorical, Trump initially doubled down.

During a Thursday interview with conservative radio host Hugh Hewitt, Trump was pressed to narrow his definition. Hewitt suggested Trump meant Obama "created the vacuum" or "lost the peace" in Iraq, leading to ISIS's rise. However, Trump insisted, "No, I meant he's the founder of ISIS. I do." He further elaborated, stating Obama was the "most valuable player" in its founding, a designation he also extended to Clinton.

Hewitt attempted to steer Trump toward a more conventional interpretation, noting Obama's opposition to ISIS. Trump remained steadfast, asserting, "He was the founder. His, the way he got out of Iraq was that that was the founding of ISIS, okay?" When Hewitt suggested that proper management of the Iraq withdrawal would have prevented ISIS, Trump agreed, concluding, "Therefore, he was the founder of ISIS."

The subsequent fact-checking was unequivocal: President Obama was not the founder of ISIS. Historical accounts attribute the group's origins to Abu Musab Al-Zarqawi, who led al Qaeda in Iraq. The organization later rebranded as ISIS under Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, exploiting regional instability and governmental failures in both the George W. Bush and Obama administrations, alongside the Syrian civil war, to establish a global terror network.

By Friday morning, facing significant backlash, Trump initiated a familiar "walk-back." In a tweet, he attributed his earlier statements to sarcasm, questioning if his critics at CNN understood the concept. "Ratings challenged @CNN reports so seriously that I call President Obama (and Clinton) 'the founder' of ISIS, & MVP," he wrote. "THEY DON'T GET SARCASM?"

This explanation was met with skepticism. Rep. Seth Moulton, an Iraq War veteran, stated on CNN's "New Day," "Donald Trump has shown that he lies every single day and then the next day he just tries to claim it was sarcasm." Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich, an ally, commented on "Fox and Friends" that Trump's "imprecise language" is frustrating, suggesting he "sometimes uses three words when he needs 10."

However, the definition of sarcasm is precise: the use of irony to express contempt or to make a point by saying the opposite of what is meant. Interpreting Trump's remarks as sarcastic would imply he was mocking his own words, a less likely scenario given the context.

Later Friday, Trump tweeted further criticism of the press, stating, "I love watching these poor, pathetic people (pundits) on television working so hard and so seriously to try and figure me out. They can't!"

Donald Trump's presidential campaign is actively recruiting volunteers to act as "observers" for the November election. The campaign's stated goal is to ensure election integrity and counter concerns that Democratic candidate Hillary Clinton may try to manipulate the outcome.

A dedicated section on the official campaign website, titled "Help Me Stop Crooked Hillary From Rigging This Election!", functions as a recruitment hub for these volunteer Trump Election Observers. Those interested are encouraged to fill out a form to get more information about how they can participate.

After submitting their contact details, volunteers are offered the chance to donate to the campaign. Registered observers will then receive an email. This communication will emphasize the campaign's commitment to using all legal means to prevent alleged election rigging by Hillary Clinton.

Key steps for volunteers involve:

*   Registering through the official campaign website.
*   Sharing contact information for further details.
*   Considering a donation to support the campaign.
*   Receiving an email outlining the campaign's stance on election integrity.

**Volunteer as a Trump Election Observer to Protect Election Integrity**
