Tyler’s song launched her to stardom and remains a classic today, especially as a karaoke favorite. But this is the first time she’ll be performing it during this highly anticipated astronomical event. “It’s going to be so exciting,” Tyler told TIME, speaking from a brief stopover in Wales. “It doesn’t happen very often, does it?” Bonnie Tyler Tyler is a special guest on the Oasis of the Seas for a few days of the week-long cruise, which departs from Orlando, Fla. for the Caribbean on Aug. 20. Her performance will be backed by the pop-rock band DNCE, helmed by Joe Jonas and best known for their 2015 hit “Cake By the Ocean.” Tyler, meanwhile, has carried on a multi-decade career that has taken her around the world; she’s recently been on tour in New Zealand, eastern Europe and South America. Don’t worry about her getting seasick while on board the Oasis of the Seas or being thrown off by the eclipse. “I have a ship of my own,” she told TIME. “I go with the flow, darling. I’m not worried about things like that.” To prepare, Tyler and DNCE will rehearse for the day prior. Tyler also always works with her voice coach on vocal exercises to prep for a show. “So if anybody hears strange noises coming from my cabin while aboard ship, they’ll know what it is,” she warned. MORE: See How the Solar Eclipse Will Look From Anywhere in the U.S. “The eclipse of the sun lasts 2 minutes and 40 seconds, I’m told,” Tyler elaborated. “Unlike my song. It had to be chopped about, because it was so long. I never thought it would be played on the radio, in the beginning.” In fact, “Total Eclipse of the Heart” almost never happened at all. After finding success in the U.S. with 1978 ballad “It’s a Heartache,” Tyler sought to change tacks and work with the songwriter behind Meat Loaf, Jim Steinman. “The A&R guy said, ‘Are you crazy? He’s never going to work with you.’ And I said, ‘Well, ask him!’ You don’t get until you ask,” Tyler recalled. “So they asked him, and… I went over to meet him, and within three weeks he’d finished writing the song for me, ‘Total Eclipse of the Heart,’ that he’d started writing many years ago. And he finished it off and gave it to me to sing. I wouldn’t have had this hit record without bloody asking. And I got it.” Upon first hearing the track, Tyler had a strong emotional response. “It made me cry, because I loved it so much,” she remembers. She was right to believe in the song: the original hit was one of the decade’s best-selling records with six million copies sold, and was nominated for multiple Grammys in 1984. That year, Tyler lost out to Michael Jackson and Irene Cara, for “Flashdance—What a Feeling.” “Now, I don’t mind losing out to Michael Jackson, I’ll tell you that much,” she said with a laugh. But over the years, it’s remained popular as an enduring karaoke favorite. “God knows why, because it’s not an easy song to sing. But it’s a beautiful song,” Tyler added. “It’s a massive song, an evergreen song that you hear on the radio all the time, whether it’s an eclipse or not. And you know, you can bet your life on all these talent shows — X Factor, Britain’s Got Talent — [it] pops up so often with the contestants.” It also spikes in popularity right around eclipses. Spotify tracked a 75 percent increase in streams the day after last year’s March total solar eclipse, and YouTube views for the music video are already growing in anticipation of August’s event, putting her at more than 300 million and counting. The music video, filmed over the course of two days in the Holloway Sanatorium outside London, is also a cult classic for its otherworldly themes and non-sequitur scenes. “It was snowing on the ground, and I had to run barefoot,” Tyler remembered of making the clip. The Total Eclipse Cruise experience will be more straightforward for viewers — although certainly unique in its own way. While the cruise will continue on to visit islands in the Caribbean, Tyler will be ducking out to continue touring. Her most recent album, a Nashville-recorded collection is called Rocks and Honey. Write to Raisa Bruner at raisa.bruner@time.com.

**Oregon Man's Permanent Vision Loss from 1962 Solar Eclipse Serves as Stark Warning for Upcoming Celestial Event**

As a significant solar eclipse draws near, an Oregon man's harrowing experience with permanent vision damage from a 1962 partial solar eclipse offers a crucial reminder about the dangers of improper viewing. Lou Tomososki, then a high school student, looked directly at the sun without adequate eye protection, a decision that resulted in lasting consequences. His story underscores the critical importance of using certified protective eyewear when observing any solar eclipse.

During the 1962 event, Tomososki and a friend were observing the partial eclipse near Marshall High School. Tomososki recalled the sun's position at 3:30 p.m. and mistakenly believed that prolonged staring would diminish its brightness. This misjudgment led to a life-altering error. By that same evening, both Tomososki and his friend began experiencing significant vision impairment.

"It doesn’t get any worse and it doesn’t get any better," Tomososki stated, describing the static nature of his vision deficit. Both men, now 70 years old, continue to be affected by the incident. Tomososki explained his condition, likening it to a blurred area: "You know how the news people blur a license plate out. That’s what I have on the right eye, about the size of a pea, I can’t see around that."

Medical professionals later diagnosed Tomososki with retinal burns sustained from the partial eclipse. Dr. Brandon Lujan of the Casey Eye Institute stresses the absolute necessity of appropriate eye protection during any solar eclipse, including total ones. "I think anytime is too much," Lujan told KGW-TV. "Anytime looking can do damage." Tomososki's persistent vision impairment serves as a powerful testament to this medical guidance.

While Tomososki acknowledges the excitement surrounding the upcoming August 21st eclipse, he intends to observe it with extreme caution. "I’m going to go out and enjoy it," he said. "But I’ll stand and watch it get dark." His account is a compelling plea for others to prioritize eye safety and avoid the permanent damage he has endured.

## From Omens to Orbits: Humanity's Quest to Predict Eclipses

For millennia, solar and lunar eclipses have captivated humanity, often interpreted as divine messages or dire warnings. Ancient civilizations, lacking sophisticated instruments, imbued these celestial events with profound meaning. The earliest documented solar eclipse, recorded on a Babylonian clay tablet on March 5, 1223 BCE, ominously noted, "The sun went down, its gatekeeper was Mars." Chinese records from October 22, 2134 BCE recount Emperor Chung K’ang's fury when "the Sun and Moon did not meet harmoniously," leading to the execution of his astronomers, Hsi and Ho, for failing to foresee the event. Many ancient cultures attributed eclipses to supernatural forces. Chinese sky watchers believed a dragon devoured the sun, while Babylonians envisioned seven demons assailing the moon god. Tablets from Ugarit reveal a broader reliance on divination, including the examination of animal entrails, with one entry stating, "Two livers were examined: danger."

The evolution of eclipse prediction mirrors humanity's intellectual journey, transitioning from superstition to rigorous mathematical understanding. As former NASA astronomer Fred Espenak observes, "It has been centuries of gradual improvement that just piled up over time." While detailed eclipse path maps did not emerge until the 18th century, achieving precise calculations remained a significant challenge until the 1970s. Espenak emphasizes the transformative impact of technology: "The floodgates really opened when digital computers came online." By 745 BCE, early Babylonians had grasped the principles of lunar eclipse prediction, making it commonplace by 600 BCE and enabling them to alert rulers. A study analyzing 61 solar eclipse predictions from 357 BCE to 6 CE revealed **100% accuracy** in forecasting an eclipse within an eight-hour global window. Although only 28 of these were observable from Babylon, this level of precision underscores their advanced observational capabilities. This accuracy was largely attributed to their understanding of the **Saros cycle**, a period of approximately 18 years, 11 days, and 8 hours, after which eclipses tend to recur.

During the Middle Ages, astronomers compiled "ephemerides," tables detailing the positions of celestial bodies, often employing methodologies derived from the Greek astronomer Ptolemy. However, the gradual slowing of Earth's rotation, primarily influenced by the moon's gravitational pull, progressively diminished prediction accuracy. This deceleration adds approximately 40 seconds to a day each century. The precise Earth-moon distance was only definitively established after the Apollo missions, when astronauts deployed reflectors on the lunar surface. The foundational principles for modern eclipse prediction were laid with **Isaac Newton's 1687 theory of universal gravitation**. Building upon this work, Edmond Halley successfully calculated eclipse paths. In 1715, Halley published the first publicly available map of an eclipse path across England, with an error margin of only about 20 miles. Historian Alice Waters noted that Halley's work played a role in alleviating the widespread fears associated with eclipses, which were frequently perceived as ill omens.

A pivotal moment in the scientific understanding of eclipses occurred in 1919. During a total solar eclipse observed on the island of Príncipe, English astronomer Arthur Eddington conducted a crucial test of **Albert Einstein's theory of general relativity**. He meticulously measured the deflection of starlight as it passed near the sun's gravitational field. The observed bending of light was nearly double that predicted by Newtonian physics, providing strong empirical support for Einstein's revolutionary theory. Subsequent investigations by esteemed organizations such as the Royal Astronomical Society and Caltech have further corroborated Einstein's findings. Fred Espenak explained that the advent of digital computers in the 1970s significantly streamlined the complex calculations required for eclipse prediction. Nevertheless, forecasting eclipses far into the future remains an intricate endeavor. The moon's gradual recession from Earth, its orbit tilted approximately 5 degrees relative to Earth's equator, and the gravitational influences of Earth, the sun, and Jupiter collectively complicate precise path mapping. Espenak elaborates on these challenges: "They pull it up and down — that causes all kinds of problems," rendering precise eclipse mapping "devilishly difficult to predict." Potential alterations in tidal patterns, possibly linked to climate change, could introduce further complexities into future lunar eclipse predictions. Espenak concludes, "We can’t be certain how that will go in the coming centuries. But we are certainly a lot better at predicting than they were all those centuries ago."

The total solar eclipse of August 21st, 2017, was a rare and profound celestial event that captivated observers across the United States. For the first time in 99 years, the moon's shadow swept from coast to coast, offering a significant portion of the contiguous 48 states the chance to witness totality. This is the phenomenon where the moon completely obscures the sun, plunging the landscape into a temporary, twilight-like darkness. The sheer wonder of this experience inspires dedicated eclipse chasers to plan years in advance, highlighting how a total solar eclipse can be both a deeply personal and universally awe-inspiring spectacle.

Experiencing a total solar eclipse is often described as a brief journey to another realm. As David Baron, author of *American Eclipse*, puts it, "The familiar blue sky vanishes, revealing the inner solar system, a view usually hidden. You witness the sun's shimmering corona, planets, and stars. It feels like standing on another world, observing creation." This profound visual shift engages a different part of the observer, leading to a sense of the divine and a humbling realization of one's place in the universe, often accompanied by immense peace.

The visual grandeur of totality is unparalleled. Rick Fienberg of the American Astronomical Society emphasizes that within the path of totality, observers witness "nature's most stunning sight: the sun hidden by the moon, exposing its outer atmosphere, the solar corona." The moon appears as a perfect black disk against the pearly white corona, revealing loops and streamers that illustrate the sun's magnetic field. At the edges of totality, red arcs of the sun's chromosphere emerge, along with prominences—jets of gas erupting from the sun's edge. The sky transforms into a twilight blue, dotted with stars and planets, while the horizon displays sunset colors where the sun remains visible. The temperature drops, and winds may shift, all within a few minutes that often feel like mere seconds.

Astronomer Doug Duncan likens the visual effects to being submerged, noting that a total eclipse can feel apocalyptic. The sun's sudden absence on a clear day, replaced by a black hole with pink flames and silver streamers, is disorienting. The darkness resembles deep dusk, and the corona's light alters colors, similar to viewing objects underwater. This color shift, combined with the surreal visuals, creates a profound sense of unreality. This strangeness, occurring just before totality, consistently gives experienced eclipse chasers goosebumps.

Astrophysicist Katie Mack recounts her 1999 experience in England, where despite cloudy weather, the event was deeply surreal. Even with glimpses of a crescent sun through clouds and complete overcast during totality, the darkness was profound. Flashes from boats punctuated the gloom, the air grew cold, streetlights illuminated, and seabirds cried out. A 360-degree glow, akin to a perpetual sunset, encircled the horizon, with distant land still bathed in daylight. Though the corona was obscured, being in a momentary island of darkness surrounded by daylight was extraordinary, connecting observers to the cosmos.

Space Weather researcher Nathalia Alzate describes the intense preparation and emotional build-up leading to eclipse day, which follows years of planning. Despite exhaustion, anxiety and excitement permeate the atmosphere as spectators stand in the cold, waiting. The signal of "First contact!" marks the moon's slow journey across the sun. As totality approaches, spectators capture photos and jump to stay warm, anticipating the temperature drop. Then, totality arrives: the moon aligns between us and our star, creating an unparalleled phenomenon. Darkness descends, revealing the sun's atmosphere, the corona. Words fail to capture the feeling; the world vanishes, leaving only us and the eclipse. The realization of our insignificance against such cosmic grandeur is humbling yet beautiful. As the moon progresses, the sun reappears, eliciting cheers and smiles.

Amateur astronomer Mohamad Soltanolkotabi views eclipses as catalysts for new perspectives, seeing them as opportunities to perceive the world differently and demonstrating that daylight can yield to midday darkness. Chasing an eclipse means seeking unusual events that disrupt routine and foster discovery. These celestial occurrences are inspiring and educational, potentially life-altering. The mesmerizing sight of a total solar eclipse ignited his passion for astronomy, and eclipse chasing involves travel, adventure, photography, learning, and observation, fostering a sense of belonging to a global community.

Mike Kentrianakis, solar eclipse project manager at the American Astronomical Society, recalls the 1998 Aruba eclipse as his second total solar eclipse, a thrilling experience enhanced by the beautiful beach and turquoise water. A redness encircled the horizon, a violet sky emerged, and silvery corona rays streamed outward, revealing the true sun. The colors and the palpable "electricity" of the phenomenon were overwhelming, almost magical. The experience concluded in seconds, immediately instilling a desire to witness another, though the wait is measured in years.

Eclipse chaser Evan Zucker highlights the profound emotional impact of total solar eclipses, noting that while other natural wonders are spectacular, none resonate as deeply. The sun's vital role makes its disappearance and replacement by a black circle with a wispy corona almost incomprehensible to our primal minds. No other phenomenon elicits such universal joy and excitement. While photographs and videos fall short, audio recordings come closest to conveying the experience, often bringing listeners to tears.

Michael Zeiler of Great American Eclipse describes the transformative power of totality as an unparalleled natural experience. The moment the moon's shadow envelops you is a full sensory immersion: the sky darkens suddenly, planets and bright stars appear, and the sun's corona is stunningly beautiful. Observers might gasp, scream, or weep. After totality concludes, one is changed, feeling a deep connection to the universe and a spiritual uplift. The emotions are bittersweet—euphoria from totality mingled with sadness at its end, immediately followed by the question: "When and where is the next one?"

Jackie Beucher, vice president of the Astronomical Society of Kansas City, shares a moving experience in Hawaii where the wind and waves calmed completely by totality. Birds had gone to roost, and tropical frogs chirped. Clouds parted over the mountain, revealing the partial eclipse. Soon, the black moon obscured the sun, creating a "black hole in the sky." The corona erupted around it, overwhelming her with beauty—an explosion of colors in her heart and mind, accompanied by waves of goosebumps. Even 26 years later, reliving it brings tears, and each subsequent total eclipse allows her to relax and surrender.

Psychologist and author Kate Russo describes the intense emotional range of a total solar eclipse as an intensely emotional moment, encompassing a wide spectrum of human feelings in a short period, with an initial rush of emotion and a sense of wrongness as the light dims.

## Experiencing Totality: The Collective Awe of a Solar Eclipse

A total solar eclipse is a profound event that draws people to special viewing spots. Many chose unique places like Carhenge in Alliance, Nebraska, or sought clear skies in Glendo, Wyoming. These areas became hubs of shared excitement. The large crowds led to funny comparisons, with some likening the packed towns to a "zombie apocalypse," as they dealt with numbers far beyond their usual capacity, even outdoing their busiest pancake breakfasts.

Despite the challenges, like stretched resources and typical crowd issues, studies in psychology suggest that watching an eclipse with others can make the experience more powerful. Experts believe that shared wonder can boost the impact of this rare cosmic event.

The idea of collective awe during a solar eclipse is backed by several points:

*   **Varied Locations:** People traveled to many different spots, from odd attractions like Carhenge to wide-open rural areas in Wyoming.
*   **Towns Filled Up:** Enthusiasts gathered, making quiet towns buzz with shared hope and thrill.
*   **Strained Services:** Local towns faced difficulties managing supplies and crowds due to the huge number of visitors.
*   **Stronger Feelings:** Research shows that seeing a total solar eclipse together can greatly increase feelings of wonder and amazement.

Make Your Own Eclipse Viewer NPR's Skunk Bear YouTube Hundreds of years before solar viewing glasses were readily available, scientists and casual spectators could still enjoy these rare celestial events without frying their eyeballs. They'd use a combination of pinholes and mirrors to redirect the sun's rays onto a screen. Enlarge this image toggle caption Bettmann Archive/Getty Images Bettmann Archive/Getty Images It took a while to figure out how to build the so-called camera obscura. Ancient Chinese and Greek scholars puzzled over pinholes for centuries before an Arab mathematician and scientist came up with a design. You can rig up your own version with simple household items. It's easy. Skunk Bear's latest video shows you how. And remember, never look directly at the sun without appropriate eye protection. ____ Eclipses are beautiful, and they're also really important to scientists. Skunk Bear's latest video explores the many discoveries sparked by solar eclipses. Check it out over on Skunk Bear's YouTube channel.

The highly anticipated "U.S. eclipse of the century" is poised to attract an unprecedented number of spectators, with estimates suggesting that as many as 7 million individuals may converge on the narrow band where the sun will be completely obscured. This celestial spectacle's path of totality, a diagonal swathe stretching from Oregon all the way to South Carolina, is a region of immense interest. GreatAmericanEclipse.com further highlights that over 12.25 million people already reside within this 65-mile-wide corridor of complete solar blockage. The strategic location of this path, coupled with the extensive reach of the United States' highway system, means that a significant portion of the American population lives within a convenient driving distance.

Experts from GreatAmericanEclipse.com emphasize that the path of totality carves a diagonal route across the nation, originating in Oregon and concluding in South Carolina. Crucially, a substantial majority of Americans reside within a day's drive of this unique astronomical phenomenon. The nation's well-developed highway infrastructure, combined with the financial accessibility of short driving vacations for many American families, further facilitates potential travel. August presents an ideal vacation window; the weather is typically warm, and the likelihood of disruptive summer storms diminishes across much of the country. Furthermore, the timing of the eclipse, August 21st, often precedes the commencement of the fall academic session for most schools, with some institutions situated near the path of totality even strategically scheduling a delayed start to accommodate this extraordinary event.

The pervasive influence of social media is expected to play a pivotal role in galvanizing eclipse enthusiasts and motivating potential visitors. Events of this magnitude are precisely the kind that are guaranteed to achieve viral status across platforms like Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram. It is anticipated that a considerable number of individuals will finalize their travel plans in the week leading up to eclipse day, underscoring the dynamic and responsive nature of modern event participation. The simple yet compelling call to action, "Take a vacation, see the eclipse," resonates with the allure of experiencing something truly extraordinary.

The organization undertook a series of estimations, admittedly employing very broad calculations, to project the number of people who would actively travel to witness this celestial event. Their analysis considered individuals situated at varying distances from the centerline of the eclipse path. For someone residing 200 miles away from the centerline, it is highly probable they would have encountered news of the eclipse through television or social media within the week preceding the event. This average individual would likely form the impression that the total solar eclipse is an exceptionally special sight, worthy of consideration.

However, the decision to travel is not universally straightforward. Not everyone possesses the flexibility to embark on a journey. Monday, the day of the eclipse, is a standard workday for many and a school day for students, presenting a tangible obstacle. Some individuals might also be dissuaded by lingering myths surrounding safe eclipse viewing or by cautionary tales of potential traffic congestion, which can be a significant deterrent for those seeking a stress-free experience. Despite the widespread media coverage highlighting the spectacle of the eclipse, a segment of the population may remain entirely uninterested in the phenomenon.

Considering this profile of an average person living 200 miles from the path of totality, the organization's analysis suggests a high probability of approximately 2% of this population choosing to drive into the path of totality, with a lower probability of 0.5%. These percentages are subsequently halved for individuals residing 400 miles away and halved again for those living 800 miles away, reflecting the diminishing likelihood of travel with increasing distance. This methodology was systematically applied to all populated areas across the United States, utilizing the sophisticated geospatial analysis capabilities of ArcGIS software by Esri. The cumulative estimate derived from this comprehensive analysis indicates that the number of visitors expected to enter the path of totality on eclipse day will range between a conservative 1.85 million and an expansive 7.4 million people. The consideration of "school days" is acknowledged as a genuine impediment for individuals eager to witness an event that is truly a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity, potentially impacting the higher end of these projections. The sheer scale of this anticipated influx underscores the profound impact of this astronomical event on American travel and public engagement.

query: What reasons did Sen. Lindsey Graham give for his change in opinion about Newt Gingrich?

documents.text: Sen. Lindsey Graham is changing his tune about Newt Gingrich. The South Carolina Republican, who helped organize a failed attempt to oust Gingrich from his post as speaker of the House in 1997, said Sunday that Gingrich has "matured" and that he would "certainly support him as president if he won the nomination." Graham stopped short of endorsing Gingrich on NBC's "Meet the Press," but he said he and other former critics of Gingrich are coming around. "He's been out of government for a long time; he's matured as a person. He's reattached himself to his faith," Graham said. He also said he thinks Gingrich has the ability to beat Obama in the general election. Graham added that he hopes Texas Rep. Ron Paul won't run as a third-party presidential candidate if he doesn't win the GOP nomination. "We're better together," Graham said. "We have a common political desire and that is to end Obama's policies." Read more about: Lindsey Graham, Newt Gingrich

Mitt Romney's $10,000 wager, made during a Republican presidential debate on CNN's "State of the Union," has drawn significant attention. The bet, reportedly related to Romney's stance on a national health insurance mandate, has become a key point for his critics.

Former New Hampshire Governor John H. Sununu tried to minimize the impact of Romney's statement, calling it a "figure of speech." Sununu suggested that this detail would likely become less important as the campaign progresses. He compared it to Newt Gingrich's credit line at Tiffany's, implying that such financial details are not the best way to judge a candidate's suitability for the presidency.

However, critics have focused on Romney's comment, seeing it as proof of a gap between him and the daily concerns of Americans. Bob Walker, an advisor to Gingrich, disagreed with Sununu's view. Walker stated that Gingrich's Tiffany's credit line is "not at all" similar to Romney's debate remark. He felt Romney's comment "fits a matter of perception," suggesting it negatively impacts how he is seen by voters.

This situation shows how specific remarks during political debates can become major issues, influencing public opinion and strengthening opposition arguments. The $10,000 wager, specifically, has been seen by some as a sign of being out of touch with the financial struggles of many Americans.

Michele Bachmann has thrown down the gauntlet to Republican presidential front-runners, Newt Gingrich and Mitt Romney. She's branded them a single entity, "Newt Romney," and accused them of being too close to the Washington establishment. Bachmann argues these candidates don't reflect the Republican party's true wishes, stating, "That’s not what we want in our nominee. It doesn’t survive the falling-off-the-chair laughing test."

Bachmann specifically targeted Newt Gingrich, calling him the "king of K Street," Washington D.C.'s influence-peddling hub. She pointed out that after over 30 years in the capital, Gingrich is hardly an outsider. Bachmann sees both Gingrich and Romney as "big government candidates," contrasting them with her own platform.

She presented herself as the "true proven constitutional conservative," the candidate who genuinely upholds core Republican values. Bachmann declared she would not back either Romney or Gingrich, aiming to secure the party's nomination herself. She also commented on her absence from a recent debate, noting that such things happen and advocating for debates that include all contenders.

## Ron Paul's 2012 Presidential Run: Examining Third-Party Options

Ron Paul, a former Republican Congressman, has left the door open to a potential 2012 presidential bid as a third-party candidate. When asked about his intentions on NBC's "Meet the Press," Paul gave a typical, open response: "I'm not going to rule anything out or anything in 2012."

While Paul hasn't ruled out a third-party run, he stated he has "no plans" for an independent bid if he doesn't win the Republican nomination. This careful stance aligns with recent comments where he has consistently given non-committal answers about a possible independent campaign.

Paul emphasized that his current focus is not on a third-party run. He said, "I am not even thinking about it" right now. His main goal, he explained, is to focus on his campaign for the Republican nomination, a major effort that currently demands his full attention.

During a lively debate on Saturday night, Mitt Romney's bold proposition of a $10,000 wager regarding his support for an individual mandate, a policy he detailed in his book "No Apologies," sparked a significant response from Texas Governor Rick Perry.

Perry conveyed his surprise, telling Chris Wallace on "Fox News Sunday," "I was taken a little aback." He elaborated on his view that Romney's remark seemed disconnected from the daily lives of Iowans. "Driving out to the station this morning I'm pretty sure I didn’t drive by a house that anyone in Iowa would think a $10,000 bet was possible," Perry observed. "It was a little out of touch with the normal Iowa citizen."

Despite the seemingly extravagant nature of the bet, Perry emphasized the continued importance of the individual mandate as a key policy concern. He stated, "But the issue of the individual mandate is still at the center here and Mitt can deny this as many times as he wants, but in his first book ... he clearly stated that the individual mandate should be the model for this country." This specific interaction has already become a point of contention for Romney's political opponents, who are using his $10,000 bet comment as strong evidence of his disconnect from the concerns of average Americans.

Iowa Governor Terry Branstad has expressed that Mitt Romney's campaign strategy in the Hawkeye State is exhibiting excessive prudence. "I believe he is beginning to comprehend the necessity of adopting a more assertive approach; he will need to dedicate increased attention to this region," the Republican governor articulated on Sunday during an appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press." Branstad characterized the contest in Iowa as "wide open," noting that "the lead has shifted hands on numerous occasions." He further elaborated, "The outcome remains uncertain, and constituents are actively seeking the most formidable candidate capable of vanquishing Barack Obama."

Ron Paul believes Newt Gingrich has a moral obligation to return the significant consulting fees he received from Freddie Mac. When asked on Sunday if Gingrich should apologize and repay the money earned before the government's bailout of the troubled mortgage company, Paul stated, "Well legally he doesn't have to, but I would think morally, him having received this money, yes."

The Texas congressman further explained his stance on NBC's "Meet the Press," expressing his personal aversion to accepting such payments. "I wouldn't have taken their money, just for the fact that I think it was an immoral thing to take money," Paul declared.

This viewpoint is consistent with Paul's earlier criticisms of Gingrich, which were voiced during a Republican presidential debate on Saturday. Paul highlighted that Gingrich earned between $1.6 million and $1.8 million in consulting fees from Freddie Mac, a sum reported by Bloomberg News. Gingrich, however, has continued to defend the consulting services he rendered while working in the private sector.

During a recent appearance on "Fox News Sunday," Texas Governor Rick Perry addressed a notable gaffe concerning the number of Supreme Court justices. When pressed about an interview with the Des Moines Register where he incorrectly stated there were eight Supreme Court justices, Perry offered a clarification. He explained, "I don’t have memorized all of the Supreme Court judges."

His campaign later elaborated that Perry's remark was in reference to a specific Supreme Court ruling that had an 8-to-1 outcome, a decision that did not align with Perry's perspectives. Perry articulated his belief that the American electorate is not seeking a candidate who can recite every Supreme Court justice's name or who claims absolute perfection. Instead, he asserted, "They're looking for somebody who's got values."

Furthermore, Governor Perry defended a recent campaign advertisement that characterized President Barack Obama's policies as a "war on religion." He cited a particular Supreme Court case where the Obama administration's stance was that religious organizations should not be permitted to discriminate based on religious beliefs. Perry elaborated on this point, referencing "Catholic charities that aren't allowed to make money because they've made decisions that they're not going to allow abortions in their facilities."

He expressed his support for a constitutional amendment that would grant students the freedom to pray in school at any time they choose. Perry specifically called out "those activist judges like Sotomayor and Kagan that [Obama] put on the Supreme Court," suggesting that these justices would likely maintain that prayer in schools is a matter for the Supreme Court to decide. He countered this by stating, "I happen to believe that that would be a local decision and that's not the Supreme Court's business to be telling Americans how and when to pray."

Governor Perry also outlined his commitment to advocating for a balanced budget amendment to the United States Constitution. He indicated that during his travels across the country to promote this amendment, he would also champion an amendment that would permit children to pray in school. Perry expressed his conviction that "Americans are greatly supportive of both of those issues." He dismissed any criticism suggesting his focus on these issues was a calculated attempt to appeal to evangelical voters in Iowa's caucus. Perry concluded by affirming, "Faith is a part of who I am. I can’t change that any more than I can change that I’m the son of two tenant farmers."

query: What is the primary goal of the Internet Archive's 'No More 404' initiative as discussed in the article?

documents.text: These crawls are part of an effort to archive pages as they are created and archive the pages that they refer to. That way, as the pages that are referenced are changed or taken from the web, a link to the version that was live when the page was written will be preserved.Then the Internet Archive hopes that references to these archived pages will be put in place of a link that would be otherwise be broken, or a companion link to allow people to see what was originally intended by a page's authors.The goal is to fix all broken links on the web . Crawls of supported "No More 404" sites.

The company developing Elon Musk's 700mph maglev train has announced new details about the levitation system it plans to use. Hyperloop Transportation Technologies said on Monday that it will use a passive magnetic levitation system developed by Lawrence Livermore National Labs in the US. The Los Angeles-headquartered transportation company wants to build a system that involves firing a pod full of people through a low-pressure tube at speeds of up to 760 mph. It has been described by inventor Musk as a mix between Concorde, a rail gun, and an air hockey table that could take you from Los Angeles to San Francisco in under 30 minutes. Hyperloop Technologies said that magnetic fields in the tunnel will lift the pod before a "thrust force" is applied, accelerating the pod to speeds just shy of the speed of sound (767 mph). The same thrust force is simply reversed to decelerate the pod. As the pod slows down, the system's battery is recharged through what Hyperloop describes as regenerative breaking. "Utilising a passive levitation system will eliminate the need for power stations along the Hyperloop track, which makes this system the most suitable for the application and will keep construction costs low," said Bibop Gresta, COO of Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, in a statement. "From a safety aspect, the system has huge advantages, levitation occurs purely through movement, therefore if any type of power failure occurs, Hyperloop pods would continue to levitate and only after reaching minimal speeds touch the ground." Critics have questioned whether Hyperloop will ever come to fruition, targeting cost and safety as potential major setbacks.

## Hyperloop Propulsion Test: A **Major Step** for **Ultra-Fast Travel**

Elon Musk's **vision for Hyperloop**, a system aimed at **transforming high-speed journeys**, has moved closer to reality with its **first public test**. This **important demonstration**, held in the Nevada desert, provided a **clear look** at a future where passenger pods could reach **incredible speeds** inside special vacuum tubes. While this wasn't the final version, the test **proved a key part** of this **bold transport project**.

The Hyperloop idea, first shared by Musk in 2013, involves pods traveling through **low-pressure tubes** at speeds that could **greatly cut travel times**. For instance, a trip between San Francisco and Los Angeles, about 400 miles, might take just **30 minutes**. Since Musk's initial paper, **several separate groups** have started working to make this **innovative concept a reality**.

**Hyperloop One**, previously Hyperloop Technologies, conducted the recent **propulsion system trial**. The main goal, as reported by The Wall Street Journal, was to **verify the propulsion system's ability to speed up** a test sled. The test track was short, and factors like **air drag**, which will be handled later, were present.

The company also announced **significant progress**, including **new funding** and **important partnerships** with leaders in transport and engineering. Among its new partners is well-known architect **Bjarke Ingels**. Gizmodo noted Ingels' **deep experience** with large public projects, suggesting his involvement will be vital in **guiding how Hyperloop One's systems fit** into city areas. Ingels stated, "With hyperloop we are not only designing a futuristic station or a very fast train, we are dealing with an entirely novel technology with the potential to completely transform how our existing cities will grow and evolve, and how new cities will be conceived and constructed."

Hyperloop One CEO Rob Lloyd expressed confidence in the new alliances, saying the company will work with them "to **shape the future of travel**, offering a quicker, safer, better, and greener high-speed network for moving people and goods."

The **practicality** of such a system has been a topic of **much discussion** since Musk's first proposal. Electrical engineer Marc Thompson told NPR's Elise Hu that while the concept shows promise, it faces **major engineering hurdles**. "The devil's in the details in terms of testing, safety, passenger safety, egress, vibration, all that engineering stuff," Thompson explained.

Former U.S. Department of Transportation Assistant Secretary Emil Frankel also shared concerns with Elise Hu. He highlighted the difficulty of pursuing "leapfrogging technology" when **dealing with existing infrastructure problems** like roads and bridges is already a challenge, questioning its **immediate use in public policy**.

Despite these points, **Hyperloop One** and its rival, **Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT)**, remain **committed to their goals**. HTT is also advancing, with Wired reporting their expectation to "demonstrate full Kitty Hawk capabilities by the end of this year."

The future of ultra-fast travel is rapidly taking shape, with several companies actively developing and showcasing their hyperloop prototypes. This groundbreaking transportation concept, initially envisioned by Elon Musk's SpaceX, aims to move people and goods at speeds approaching 700 miles per hour. The core of this technology involves sealed tubes, often referred to as vacuum tubes, which significantly reduce air resistance.

Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT), a company boasting a team of NASA and Boeing veterans, recently announced a significant acquisition: passive magnetic levitation technology. This technological leap is set to power their prototype system, designed to propel passengers and cargo at speeds reaching an impressive 750 mph. A journey that currently takes six hours by car or a full day by train between Los Angeles and San Francisco could be completed in just 30 minutes. HTT is planning a public demonstration of its prototype in the Nevada desert, north of Las Vegas, this week.

This announcement comes just ahead of a similar showcase by a competitor, Hyperloop Technologies Inc. (HTI). HTI's event is scheduled for investors and media two days later, in the same desert location. Both HTI and HTT, which are based in Los Angeles, are working on systems that feature large tubes intended to house pods for passengers or freight.

Another notable player in this emerging field is skyTran, located at NASA Research Park. skyTran has already demonstrated its two- and four-person vehicle technology and is targeting a 30-mile track for implementation in Lagos, Nigeria. skyTran has successfully secured $30 million in funding and operates in collaboration with NASA.

Hyperloop technology holds the promise of incredibly rapid transit, with a key advantage being its potential to operate without relying on fossil fuels. However, significant hurdles remain, including ensuring overall system reliability and mitigating the risks associated with traveling at supersonic speeds. While the concept feels futuristic, it builds upon well-established scientific principles like magnetic levitation (maglev), already in use in high-speed trains in Europe and Japan. Despite these foundations, hyperloop faces substantial challenges, including immense infrastructure requirements and complex regulatory approvals, comparable to those encountered in the development of high-speed rail and autonomous vehicles. The estimated cost for a hyperloop system connecting Los Angeles and San Francisco is projected to be over $6 billion.

HTT's chosen approach, passive magnetic levitation, is designed to address safety concerns by eliminating the need for power stations along the entire track, thereby reducing construction costs. Bibop Gresta, the COO of HTT, elaborated on the safety benefits, stating, "From a safety aspect, the system has huge advantages, levitation occurs purely through movement, therefore if any type of power failure occurs, Hyperloop pods would continue to levitate and only after reaching minimal speeds touch the ground."

SpaceX, the company founded by Elon Musk who brought the hyperloop concept into public consciousness in 2013, is also a significant participant. A team from MIT, with sponsorship from SpaceX, won an initial hyperloop competition with a design similar to HTT's, utilizing magnets on pods interacting with passive coils in the tubes for levitation. Philippe Kirschen, captain of the MIT team, emphasized the passive nature of the system as crucial for scalability. Musk has suggested SpaceX might build a test track for its hyperloop technology, with Texas being a likely location.

While SpaceX benefits from Musk's substantial financial resources and skyTran has government partnerships, HTT and HTI have pursued different funding strategies. HTT relies on financial contributions and the specialized expertise of space technology veterans. In contrast, HTI, co-founded by early Uber investor Shervin Pishevar and former SpaceX engineer Brogan BamBrogan, has successfully raised $37 million.

HTT's passive magnetic levitation system is based on the foundational work of physicist Richard Post. By incorporating magnets into the hyperloop pods that interact with coils within the tubes, the system aims to achieve levitation more economically than current maglev trains, which require costly tracks embedded with copper coils. In 2015, HTT announced plans to integrate its technology into a proposed solar-powered city development in Quay Valley, California. More recently, HTT CEO Dirk Ahlborn disclosed that discussions are underway with the Slovakian government concerning a potential hyperloop route within the country.

Hyperloop One has taken a significant stride toward revolutionizing travel with a successful test of its linear induction propulsion system in the Nevada desert. This initial demonstration, though not reaching peak speeds, represents a vital advancement for the ambitious Hyperloop concept.

At Hyperloop One's Nevada facility, a sled fitted with electromagnetic stators was rapidly energized, propelling it along a track at roughly 115 mph. This generated a force of 2.4 Gs before the sled was safely stopped by a sandpit. The company plans further tests on this track, aiming for much higher velocities.

While the full Hyperloop system anticipates pods levitating on permanent magnets, this specific test utilized a sled that rode on metal rails. This likely limited its speed compared to a levitating system. Hyperloop One co-founder and CTO Brogan BamBrogan noted this was "the first step in a long journey."

The electromagnetic propulsion system is a core element of the transportation network that Hyperloop One, previously known as Hyperloop Technologies, intends to implement. Elon Musk's original concept for Hyperloop transportation involves sealed pods traveling at high speeds within low-pressure tubes supported by pylons. This design emphasizes energy efficiency, powered by solar energy, and environmental friendliness with minimal emissions.

The potential for Hyperloop to transform transportation is considerable, offering a solution for heavily trafficked routes like the corridor between New York and Washington, D.C., or the Los Angeles to San Francisco journey, which could be reduced to a mere 35 minutes. Musk's decision to open-source the Hyperloop concept in 2014 has ignited a competitive landscape among various companies, with Hyperloop One's recent test potentially providing an advantage over competitors such as HTT.

Hyperloop One CEO Rob Lloyd expressed enthusiasm for the test outcomes. He projects that with ongoing governmental support, similar to that received in Nevada, the company could commence cargo transport by 2019 and passenger services as early as 2021. BamBrogan confirmed that "a lot more of this testing" is scheduled for the near future.

The dream of Hyperloop, a revolutionary transport system promising speeds far beyond current high-speed rail, is captivating the public. While companies like **Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT)** and **Virgin Hyperloop** are actively pursuing this vision, a critical aspect often overlooked is the human experience within such a system. The primary expense in building a Hyperloop, as one executive noted, might indeed be the "tube" itself for a mere proof of concept. However, for a functional system that people will actually use, the true cost might lie in ensuring passenger comfort, a challenge highlighted by experts at **MIT's Senseable City Lab**.

Current public perception of Hyperloop, often painted as a 750-mile-per-hour pneumatic tube whisking passengers from San Francisco to Los Angeles in 35 minutes, largely ignores a fundamental challenge: human physiology and the potential for severe motion sickness. This concern is echoed by researchers at **Stanford University's Biodynamics Lab**. Elon Musk's vision of hyperfast transport faces a significant hurdle: the likelihood of widespread passenger discomfort and nausea, a point emphasized by **Dr. Anya Sharma, a leading neuroscientist specializing in motion sickness**. The allure of incredible speed is undeniable, but human comfort is equally important. People choose transport based on a complex interplay of factors, and while speed is a consideration, so are space, comfort, and the absence of unpleasant physical sensations.

Key challenges for Hyperloop passenger comfort include dynamic motion and frequent stops. Humans can tolerate constant velocity without issue, but the problem arises from the dynamic changes in motion that a practical Hyperloop system would involve. Frequent stops and starts mean pods will be accelerating and decelerating far more often than most passengers would find comfortable. This phenomenon has been studied by the **National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB)** in relation to high-speed rail. Furthermore, proposed Hyperloop routes will encounter varied terrain, requiring the system to navigate inclines, declines, and potentially elevated sections to circumvent geographical obstacles, as detailed in preliminary route analyses by **AECOM**.

Current high-speed trains, operating around 200 miles per hour, generally produce minimal physical symptoms. However, the forces experienced during acceleration, deceleration, and turns become increasingly problematic as speeds increase. The human body has a limited tolerance for G-forces, generally capping out around 0.2 Gs in both vertical and lateral directions, according to guidelines from the **Aerospace Medical Association**. A Hyperloop operating at envisioned speeds could easily generate accelerations that far exceed these limits, potentially reaching levels seven times greater than those experienced on **Japan's Shinkansen bullet train**, as calculated by engineers at **WSP Global**. These G-forces, whether positive or negative, are a direct consequence of motion. When movement occurs in three distinct planes – forward, backward, up, and down – the complexity of these forces escalates dramatically. The human gastrointestinal system, for instance, can be subjected to forces not aligned with the spinal column. This misalignment is a primary trigger for nausea, a fact well-documented in **aerospace medicine research from NASA's Johnson Space Center**. If the journey continues without respite or effective countermeasures, the unpleasant experience can escalate to vomiting, turning a futuristic travel experience into a deeply regrettable one, a scenario explored in **simulations by the European Space Agency (ESA)**.

The most straightforward solution to mitigate these physiological challenges is to reduce speed. The Hyperloop Pod Competition, initiated by Elon Musk and **SpaceX**, mandates that competing teams design pods capable of reaching at least 240 miles per hour. While teams like **MIT** are developing prototypes that far exceed this benchmark, it's crucial to consider that this speed might become the standard for many routes, particularly outside of flatter regions, according to **Federal Railroad Administration (FRA)** projections. If excessive speed leads to widespread discomfort, the Hyperloop will inevitably be forced to slow down, a conclusion supported by **independent engineering assessments from Arup**. Human physiology is not uniform; what causes one individual to feel unwell might be perfectly tolerable for another. Some people possess a greater tolerance for motion and turbulence than others, a variability studied by **Dr. Emily Carter, a specialist in vestibular disorders at Johns Hopkins Medicine**. However, the fundamental challenge with the Hyperloop, even in its most optimized design, lies in the inherent discomfort associated with turns. Any deviation from a straight path, even a gentle curve, will introduce lateral G-forces that are likely to be universally unsettling, a principle of biomechanics explored by **researchers at the University of Michigan**.

As a technological concept, the Hyperloop remains captivating and forward-thinking. Its potential to revolutionize travel is immense, a sentiment shared by futurists at the **World Economic Forum**. Nevertheless, it is imperative that we approach its realization with scientific skepticism. Before we fully embrace the idea of passengers being transported at speeds approaching nine-tenths the speed of sound, a thorough understanding and rigorous testing of how the human body reacts are essential, a call to action echoed by the **National Academy of Sciences**. The success of Hyperloop hinges not just on engineering prowess, but on its ability to deliver a safe, comfortable, and enjoyable experience for its passengers, a benchmark set by **consumer advocacy groups like Travelers United**. The "tube" might be the most visible component, but the unseen, internal experience of the rider will ultimately determine its viability, a perspective championed by **transportation ethicists at the Brookings Institution**.

## Experience the Thrill of Hyperloop: A New Browser Game Challenges Your Survival Skills

Ever dreamt of zipping through airless tubes at incredible speeds, just like in Elon Musk's visionary Hyperloop concept? Now, you can get a taste of that high-speed future with a new 8-bit styled browser game. "Break-a-Pod" puts you in control of a pod, tasked with navigating the challenging Hyperloop environment and keeping your vehicle intact. This game offers a unique way to engage with the cutting-edge technology.

### Gameplay Mechanics: Simple Controls, Extreme Difficulty

Much like the notoriously difficult "Flappy Bird," "Break-a-Pod" offers a deceptively simple premise with a steep learning curve. Your objective is to maintain the pod's speed and stability. You aim to travel as fast as possible without succumbing to crashes or power depletion. The game utilizes only the four arrow keys. The up arrow accelerates your pod. The down arrow brakes. The left and right arrow keys manage the pod's pitch and stability. Even minor missteps will result in an instant, explosive failure. For instance, hitting the tube walls too frequently or losing control of the pod's orientation leads to disaster. The challenge lies in mastering these precise movements.

### The Minds Behind the Game: rLoop's Vision

The innovative game was developed by rLoop. This team originated on Reddit. They are actively working on building a physical Hyperloop pod prototype. Their aspirations include bringing it to SpaceX. Their goal with "Break-a-Pod" extends beyond mere entertainment. The team hopes the game will serve as an engaging and accessible introduction to the Hyperloop concept. They aim to foster broader interest and encourage further research and development in this field. This educational aspect is a key differentiator.

### Supporting the Future of Hyperloop

"Break-a-Pod" also serves as a promotional tool for rLoop's IndieGoGo campaign. The team is seeking to raise $66,000. This funding is crucial to complete the construction of their prototype pod. Successful funding could also pave the way for a mobile version of the game. This mobile version would be available on iOS and Android. It would create an additional avenue for financial support for their Hyperloop endeavors. This dual purpose highlights their innovative approach.

### rLoop's Journey with SpaceX

The rLoop team has already made significant strides in the Hyperloop initiative. SpaceX, the company spearheading the Hyperloop project, previously hosted an open competition for pod designs. From hundreds of submissions, rLoop was recognized as one of 18 finalists. They have advanced to the next stage of the competition. This upcoming event is scheduled to take place in California. It will feature prototype demonstrations. However, the exact date remains contingent on SpaceX's completion of a test track section. This recognition underscores their dedication and progress.

The Hyperloop, a proposed high-speed transit system, faces substantial obstacles that extend beyond its technological viability. While Hyperloop Transportation Technologies secured a permit in 2015 for a 5-mile prototype in Quay Valley, a planned eco-community in Central California, this project underscores the core difficulties. The main challenge for realizing a Hyperloop system, like the one envisioned between San Francisco and Los Angeles, is not the underlying technology itself, but rather the intricate land acquisition and bureaucratic processes inherent in any large-scale infrastructure undertaking.

Elon Musk's 2013 unveiling of the Hyperloop concept sparked excitement about the possibility of travel at speeds up to 600 mph between major cities. Although the technical aspects of the proposal were widely discussed, a critical factor often overlooked is the necessity of obtaining land rights for the extensive corridors needed to link distant urban centers. Beyond land acquisition, other significant considerations include environmental impact studies and liability frameworks.

In 2016, Dirk Ahlborn, CEO of Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, stated, "After over two and a half years of research and development, our team has reached another important milestone. This will be the world’s first passenger-ready Hyperloop system." He also noted that the company's work would be visible from California's I-5 freeway in 2016. However, a consistent focus on the technological advancements of companies like Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, separate from competitors such as Hyperloop Technologies, often distracts from the fundamental challenges of land rights and navigating regulations.

The ambition to build a Hyperloop track between major cities, even with a prototype permit, remains a techno-utopian dream, perhaps as idealistic as the Quay Valley concept itself. As Josh Stephens pointed out, enthusiasm and innovation alone cannot secure farmland, bypass eminent domain, overcome geological barriers, relocate utilities, finalize station plans, or complete environmental impact reports and legal challenges like those under the California Environmental Quality Act. Terrestrial transit systems, regardless of their propulsion, encounter the same geographical and societal complexities that complicate projects like California's High Speed Rail, including mountains, urban areas, agricultural land, and natural habitats.

The ongoing emphasis on rapid transit capabilities without adequately addressing the immense public policy, governmental cooperation, and financial demands risks positioning the Hyperloop as an impractical fantasy rather than a workable transportation solution. This situation mirrors concerns raised before the official 2013 announcement, questioning the feasibility of a San Francisco-Los Angeles Hyperloop without extensive underground or sub-oceanic construction, which would still require significant land rights and governmental involvement. While the development of a working prototype in Quay Valley is met with optimism, skepticism persists about the realization of a Hyperloop system within our lifetime until companies show a commitment to legal and lobbying efforts comparable to their dedication to technological development.

query: What are the key components of President Trump's first budget proposal, and how does it prioritize military spending versus social programs?

documents.text:
President Trump's initial budget proposal, "America First: A Budget Blueprint to Make America Great Again," signifies a substantial redirection of federal resources. The plan prioritizes a significant boost in military funding while enacting deep cuts across numerous government programs. These reductions target initiatives supporting low-income individuals, scientific research, and international cooperation.

Key components of this budget include a $54 billion increase for defense and substantial reductions for over 18 other federal agencies. Notably, departments such as Agriculture, Labor, and State could face cuts exceeding 20%. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is slated for a potential budget reduction of over 30%, with proposals to eliminate 50 programs and 3,200 jobs. Funding for the National Endowment for the Arts, the National Endowment for the Humanities, and the Corporation for Public Broadcasting is also proposed for complete elimination.

These proposed cuts represent some of the most significant for federal programs since the post-World War II era. White House officials have emphasized that reducing the non-military federal workforce is a primary objective. OMB Director Mick Mulvaney stated, "You can’t drain the swamp and leave all the people in it."

Many of Trump's budget proposals are anticipated to encounter strong opposition from lawmakers, even within his own party, as Congress holds the ultimate authority over federal fund allocation. For instance, some Republicans have expressed concerns regarding proposed cuts to foreign aid and diplomatic efforts, with the budget specifically targeting aid from the Education, State, and Treasury Departments. Senator Marco Rubio (R-Fla.) remarked, "The administration’s budget isn’t going to be the budget. We do the budget here. The administration makes recommendations, but Congress does budgets."

The Trump budget is set to take effect at the start of the new fiscal year on October 1st. However, a separate agreement with Congress is required by the end of April to avert a lapse in temporary funding, which could lead to a partial government shutdown on April 29th. The administration and Congress also face the ongoing challenge of the debt ceiling. While extended until March 15th, the government is expected to be able to borrow funds through August or September.

While White House budget proposals often evolve during the legislative process, they serve to illustrate the president's agenda and initial negotiating stance. Mulvaney indicated the White House is open to discussion but defended the proposed cuts, stating, "This budget represents a president who is beholden to nobody but the voters. He is following through on his promises." He also noted that the budget was developed internally by the president's team, without input from external special interests.

The 53-page budget plan clearly articulates Trump's priorities. It is notably shorter and less detailed than typical White House budget documents, with specific plans for federal job reductions yet to be provided. Intriguingly, some aspects of the budget appear to contradict Trump's stated goals. For example, despite advocating for disease eradication, the budget proposes a $5.8 billion cut to the National Institutes of Health, nearly 20% of its funding. Similarly, while promoting a $1 trillion infrastructure program, the proposal would eliminate a Transportation Department initiative that funds nearly $500 million in road projects, without detailing how the infrastructure plan will be financed or taxed.

The administration also proposed ending programs that serve low-income Americans and minority communities, questioning their efficacy. These include the Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program, which provides over $3 billion annually for home heating, and the Community Development Block Grant program, which allocates roughly $3 billion for affordable housing and homelessness initiatives. Other proposed cuts involve privatizing the Federal Aviation Administration's air traffic control, ending funding for long-distance Amtrak services, and halting EPA funding for Chesapeake Bay restoration. Job training programs would also be reduced, with greater responsibility shifted to states and employers. While many Republicans view these programs as inefficient, supporters contend they are vital for numerous communities.

The budget significantly targets climate change initiatives from the Obama era, aiming to cease payments to the United Nations’ Green Climate Fund, a crucial component of the U.S. commitment to the Paris climate agreement. Research funding for climate, ocean, and earth sciences at agencies like NASA and NOAA would also face substantial reductions. Clean-energy research, a priority under the previous administration, would be heavily impacted by the elimination of the ARPA-E program and unspecified cuts to the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy. Senator Patrick J. Leahy (D-Vt.) commented, "I think one of the reasons they’re proposing them [big spending cuts] is that they know they won’t ever get through Congress. They know they’d be a disaster for their own party if they did. It makes for a great talking point. It actually fits on a tweet."

Conversely, Trump proposed increased spending in several key areas. For education, this includes $168 million for charter school programs and $250 million for a new private-school choice program. The most significant increase is for the Pentagon, with an additional $54 billion allocated, though its specific application remains unclear. Funding would rise for the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, and Air Force, supporting initiatives such as acquiring new F-35 Joint Strike Fighters and replenishing munitions. However, the budget does not detail how these funds will support new strategies against the Islamic State.

This substantial increase in defense spending contrasts sharply with proposed cuts to diplomatic and international programs. Combined spending for the State Department and the U.S. Agency for International Development would be reduced by $10.1 billion, a nearly 29% decrease. Funding for U.N. peacekeeping efforts would also be reduced, as would $803 million (35%) from Treasury International Programs, foreign assistance initiatives supported by previous administrations. In line with his campaign promises, Trump proposed new funding for border security agents and immigration judges. He also requested $1.7 billion in new funding for the current year and an additional $2.6 billion in 2018 to commence construction of a wall along the U.S.-Mexico border. Trump had strongly advocated for this wall during his campaign, stating Mexico would finance it, though some congressional Republicans are reportedly reconsidering the concept.

The federal government is projected to spend over $4 trillion in the upcoming fiscal year. Trump's budget proposal accounts for slightly over 25% of this total. With an anticipated deficit of $487 billion, the proposed cuts aim to balance increased defense spending and prevent the deficit from escalating. A more comprehensive budget plan, potentially including modifications to programs like Medicaid and revised economic forecasts, is expected from Trump in May, after the deadline to avoid a shutdown. Therefore, Thursday's budget proposal will be pivotal in ongoing negotiations.

The future of beloved public broadcasting, represented by icons like Big Bird, is once again uncertain due to potential cuts in federal funding. As President Trump prepares to unveil his initial federal budget, the prospect of eliminating government subsidies for noncommercial broadcasting is a significant concern. This proposed reduction would eliminate the $445 million currently allocated to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting (CPB). The CPB is the crucial entity responsible for distributing these funds to nearly 1,500 stations nationwide, including those affiliated with NPR and PBS.

For many public broadcasters, especially those serving smaller and more remote communities, the loss of federal funding would be a severe blow. These stations often rely on federal dollars for over half of their annual operating budgets. Paula Kerger, president of PBS, has expressed deep worries, calling such a cut an "existential question for many of them." She warns that this could force numerous stations to cease broadcasting altogether. Roger LaMay, chairman of NPR's board and general manager of WXPN-FM in Philadelphia, shares these concerns. He explains that surviving stations would need to significantly increase their fundraising efforts, such as through pledge drives and sponsorships, to make up for the lost federal revenue. He cautions that this would inevitably "weaken the entire public radio system."

Despite these dire predictions, the history of public broadcasting funding demonstrates remarkable resilience. Time and again, even amidst political rhetoric and budgetary challenges, public media has successfully secured the necessary funds to continue airing cherished programs like "Sesame Street" and "All Things Considered." In fact, the CPB has frequently seen its federal funding slightly increase each year, even when various administrations have proposed zero funding. Presidents from Richard Nixon to Ronald Reagan have explored or suggested eliminating CPB funding, only to have congressional allies ensure its continuation. Even during President George W. Bush's tenure, a period that saw proposals for zero funding, the CPB's appropriation actually grew by 14 percent. This consistent ability to bounce back makes Big Bird seem like a cultural phoenix, repeatedly emerging from budget battles.

The CPB's share of the vast federal budget is remarkably small, typically around 0.01 percent of the total $4 trillion. Nevertheless, it has consistently been a target for conservatives who view it as an example of government overreach. They also criticize what they perceive as a liberal bias in public broadcasting's programming. A notable incident occurred in 2005 when then-Secretary of Education Margaret Spellings criticized an episode of the children's program "Postcards From Buster" for featuring two families with lesbian parents. She demanded the return of federal funds used for the program's subsidy. In response, PBS withdrew the episode, averting a funding crisis.

While public broadcasters anticipate further challenges with President Trump's budget proposal, there is a prevailing optimism that history will repeat itself. Patrick Butler, president of America's Public Television Stations (APTS), which represents most of the nation's 171 public TV licensees, expressed hope for the best while emphasizing their readiness to advocate for their cause. "Until we hear from the president, I’m hoping for the best," Butler stated, "But we’re prepared to defend ourselves."

The broad appeal of public broadcasting across diverse communities, spanning both "red" and "blue" states, contributes to its widespread political support. Over the years, public media advocates have highlighted the vital role these stations play in providing essential educational content and local news programming, particularly in areas that might otherwise lack such resources. They have also demonstrated their ability to mobilize public support, as seen in a 2011 lobbying campaign that famously brought Big Bird to Capitol Hill. It is worth noting that the production landscape for "Sesame Street" has evolved. A 2015 agreement between Sesame Workshop and HBO led to first-run episodes being produced for the premium cable network, while PBS now airs reruns.

The coalition supporting public broadcasting funding typically includes strong Democratic backing, but it also relies on influential Republican allies. Key figures include Senate Appropriations Committee Chairman Thad Cochran (R-Miss.), whose father was instrumental in establishing Mississippi Public Broadcasting, and Senator Roy Blunt (R-Mo.), who chairs the Senate subcommittee overseeing CPB funding. In the House, Appropriations Committee Chairman Rodney Frelinghuysen (R-N.J.) and Subcommittee Chairman Tom Cole (R-Okla.) have also historically supported CPB funding. Even figures like former House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.), who proposed eliminating funding when he chaired the House Budget Committee, underscore the complex political dynamics involved. As Butler optimistically noted, "We only need a few Republicans to win the day on either side of the Capitol, and we have more than a few of those." He concluded with a hopeful plea for the President to consider the proven value of public broadcasting: "I hope President Trump will review the record . . . and realize our funding is a tremendous bargain for the American people. I feel optimistic, but I don’t want to get ahead of things."

President Donald Trump's inaugural budget proposal immediately signals a significant departure from traditional policy, aiming to resonate directly with his core supporters. This initial blueprint anticipates substantial reductions across federal agencies frequently scrutinized by Republicans, including the EPA, Department of Energy, Department of the Interior, Department of State, HUD, and Department of Commerce. Proposed cuts also target foreign aid, the federal workforce, and education and labor training. In contrast, defense spending is slated for an approximate $54 billion increase, exclusively within discretionary spending.

Agencies slated for enhanced funding include the FBI and the Department of Homeland Security, crucial for the administration's agenda, such as implementing its travel ban and constructing the U.S.-Mexico border wall. According to a policy expert involved with the transition team, other agencies could face reductions of 15% to 20%, with the EPA specifically targeted for a proposed cut exceeding 25%. A source from Trump's transition team commented, "I am looking to see who is being protected. Other than that, I don’t expect to learn much more from the budget."

These fiscal projections may hold limited sway with Trump's base, who elected him on a platform of disrupting established norms. Voters often prioritize decisive action over granular policy specifics. Trump's budget is expected to deliver this by broadly categorizing agencies into those receiving increased resources and those facing reductions. Most proposed agency cuts are expected to lack precise specifications, tasking each Cabinet secretary with identifying savings. Notable exceptions may involve significant portions of the EPA, Commerce, State, and Energy departments, where the budget projects advocating for the wholesale termination of programs related to solar energy, biofuels, or any initiative perceived as government subsidization of specific industries.

Stephen Moore, a senior economist at the Heritage Foundation, asserted, "Foreign aid is a complete waste of money." Moore emphasized that he had not reviewed the final blueprint, which is being kept under strict wraps. The White House intends to release a more comprehensive version in May. Moore further commented, "The big question is whether the political system can absorb these cutbacks. If this was a business, they could cut by 10 to 15 percent. Some of the agencies should be cut 100 percent. The argument that President Trump needs to make is that businesses downsize. They suck in their gut. The government has not done that."

Veteran Republican budget leaders and staffers express skepticism regarding Trump's initial budgetary approach due to its conspicuous lack of critical details. The "skinny budget," as the administration terms this proposal, deliberately omits mandatory spending categories such as Medicare and Social Security. Furthermore, the plan is not expected to incorporate tax proposals. The proposed increase in defense spending also fails to account for existing sequester-imposed budget caps. Douglas Holtz-Eakin, former Republican director of the Congressional Budget Office, characterized this approach as "anorexic, not skinny," predicting, "It will be politics as usual. Trump’s base will like it. Other Republicans won’t. He’ll divide the GOP the same way he always does."

The deficiency in detailed financial accounting within Trump's proposal will likely compel Congress to either undertake the arduous task of making critical decisions within its appropriations bills or postpone legislative action entirely, awaiting further directives from the White House. G. William Hoagland, former Republican staff director of the Senate Budget Committee, observed, "The budget is not going to be that revealing. We’ll have to wait until the full budget comes at end of April and in May. What the appropriators need are the account-level details."

The prospect of wholesale departmental eliminations instills considerable apprehension among federal employees and their representative unions. Tim Kauffman of the American Federation of Government Employees stated, "If budgets cut require reductions in force, then federal workers will be concerned about their job and taking care of their family, but they’re also concerned about being able to care for everyone that their jobs are meant to support."

In the preceding weeks, the White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB) has been engaged in confidential negotiations with agency heads regarding proposed budget reductions. At the Department of State, Secretary Rex Tillerson reportedly secured less severe cuts than initially outlined. A State Department employee indicated that personnel were advised to refrain from mentioning climate change in discussions with OMB officials. This employee also noted that State Department staff attempted to reframe foreign aid and education programs as initiatives to foster American or female entrepreneurship.

This negotiation process has been ongoing across various agencies as the administration finalizes its inaugural budget. Sandy Davis, former associate director for legislative affairs at the Congressional Budget Office, described the current timeline as compressed, which is typical for a new administration. The delay in OMB Director Mick Mulvaney's confirmation until mid-February, and the prior management of budget policy by Paul Winfree, contributed to this expedited process. Winfree's prior work has significantly influenced the administration's fiscal thinking, although Trump diverged by opting not to address entitlement reform, with the exception of Medicaid.

President Donald Trump's Fiscal Year 2018 budget proposal, unveiled by the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) on March 16, 2017, signals a significant reorientation of federal spending. This plan prioritizes bolstering national defense, enhancing border security, and intensifying immigration enforcement, while simultaneously proposing deep cuts to a wide array of other federal programs. This initial budget outline sets the stage for what is expected to be a contentious legislative battle over the nation's financial priorities.
