After a near-fatal incident involving Brussels sprouts, Aykroyd performed the Heimlich maneuver. "He saved my life, and then he asked me to marry him," Fisher recounted. "And I thought... wow, what if that happens again? I should probably marry him."

### Meryl Streep Portrayed Her

Achieving a level of recognition where Meryl Streep plays you in a film is a significant feat. While it was a cinematic adaptation of Fisher's autobiography, "Postcards from the Edge," the fact that the legendary Meryl Streep embodied her on screen speaks volumes about Fisher's impact.

### Her Emoji Game Was Legendary

Fisher had a unique talent for communication, often expressing herself through emojis on social media. She demonstrated that words weren't always necessary to convey a message, showcasing a modern and engaging way to connect with her audience.

### A Canine Companion of Note

Her beloved dog, Gary, was more than just a pet; he was a celebrity in his own right. Gary frequently accompanied Fisher on red carpets and movie sets, often stealing the spotlight. His presence was a constant source of joy for Fisher, and his social media account offered a poignant glimpse into their bond, even posting a heartbreaking message after her passing.

### A Complex Role Model

Fisher openly discussed her struggles with addiction and bipolar disorder, offering a raw and honest portrayal of life's challenges. Despite these battles, she was an incredibly talented writer and comedian. She embraced her imperfections, famously stating, "If my life wasn't funny, it would just be true, and that is unacceptable." She embodied a powerful form of "Grrl Power," inspiring many with her resilience and humor.

### The Truth About Underwear in Space

Fisher shared a humorous anecdote from the set of "Star Wars" where director George Lucas explained the practicalities of space travel. He reportedly told her that in zero gravity, bodies expand, but undergarments do not, leading to a rather uncomfortable scenario: "You go into space and you become weightless. Then your body expands but your bra doesn't, so you get strangled by your own underwear."

query: What marked the significance of Alibaba's IPO on September 19, 2014, in relation to previous IPOs like Facebook's and General Motors'?

documents.text: By Rana Foroohar When the opening bell of the New York Stock Exchange rang out on Sept. 19, it marked the biggest IPO the world had ever seen — bigger than Facebook’s or General Motors’. This new Wall Street juggernaut, based 7,000 miles away in Hangzhou, China, is Alibaba. Its founder and chairman, 50-year-old Jack Ma, is now challenging some of the most powerful companies on the Internet, including Amazon, eBay and PayPal. And he is doing so by melding Western entrepreneurship with a canny—and sometimes controversial—sense of how to profit in the world’s most populous nation. As with so many Chinese enterprises, comprehending the enormity of Ma’s creation requires thinking on a different scale. Alibaba is used by more than a third of China—some 500 million people—making it easily one of the largest e-commerce companies in the world. Those customers come to Alibaba to shop in the electronic malls it operates, where some 8.5 million merchants, large and small, ply their goods. Increasingly, Chinese pay for those goods—and conduct all kinds of other transactions—using Alipay, a PayPal-like service affiliated with Alibaba that is now one of China’s most important financial entities. In fiscal year 2014, Alibaba generated $8.5 billion in revenue, nearly half of it profit. And Alibaba’s $25 billion IPO made Ma one of the world’s wealthiest individuals. (Read More: Behind the Person of the Year Images) But even those figures obscure the significance of Alibaba’s rise and the extent to which it has been propelled by Ma’s deft understanding of how to do business in his homeland. Since it began in 1999 by linking Chinese manufacturers with foreign and domestic buyers, Alibaba has played a crucial role in China’s digital opening to the West. Having previously worked in the Ministry of Trade, Ma was able to ensure support from the Communist Party government and take on bigger competitors in China’s private sector. His innovations were not just technical but also diplomatic and strategic. Like many Chinese, Ma comes from almost nothing: he is the son of traditional storytelling performers who taught himself English by tagging alongside tourists at a local hotel. Later he became a teacher and traveled to the U.S. Searching the World Wide Web for information on Chinese beer and coming up dry, he vowed to bring China online, at a time when only 1% of the population was on the web. A few years later, Ma started Alibaba in his apartment with 17 friends. His first employee was his wife, who now looks after the couple’s two children. Zhang Yin has claimed that she fell for Ma despite his unexceptional looks because he had the capacity to do things other men couldn’t. Friends have also been drawn to that ambition. “Jack had the biggest dreams of anyone I’ve ever known,�? says Porter Erisman, an American adman who left his job at Ogilvy & Mather in Beijing to work for Ma early on. “You’d come up with a goal, and he’d immediately ask you to triple it.�? A consummate spinner of corporate narrative, Ma is full of Shaolin-monk-style sound bites. “eBay is a shark in the ocean. We are a crocodile in the Yangtze,�? he famously proclaimed. “If we fight in the ocean, we will lose. But if we fight in the river, we will win.�? Ma claims to base his management technique on Chinese martial-arts teachings, encouraging employees to adopt nicknames from kung fu novels. (His own, Feng Qingyang, is a nod to an aggressive master swordsman.) Like the best martial artists, he is capable of turning weakness into strength. In the years before Alibaba went public, for instance, Ma leveraged his freedom from Wall Street pressure on quarterly earnings to keep his site free for a long time, building market share. Obliged to show shareholders progress on profit margins, eBay watched as Alibaba scooped up business.

The **2014 Ebola epidemic** served as a stark reminder that effectively combating infectious diseases transcends mere technological advancement; it fundamentally relies on the **human resilience and courage** demonstrated by those on the front lines. While historically Ebola outbreaks were largely confined to remote African regions, their infrequent appearances in Western nations often ignited sensationalized media narratives, fixated on the virus's **horrific symptoms** and the profound despair experienced by medical staff. The year 2014 marked a **devastating turning point**, escalating a regional outbreak into a full-blown epidemic, a crisis amplified by the very global advancements that had fostered economic progress and interconnectedness.

This virulent disease spread with alarming speed through **densely populated urban centers** across Liberia, Guinea, and Sierra Leone. Its reach extended to Nigeria and Mali, and tragically, it even crossed the Atlantic. The epidemic inflicted a heavy toll on **healthcare workers**, overwhelming already fragile public health systems. The **World Health Organization (WHO)** documented dire circumstances, such as in Liberia, where overcrowded hospitals meant that in August, six pregnant women were unable to receive necessary care. The ever-present risk of Ebola virus disease (EVD) transmission posed a **constant threat** to caregivers, a harsh reality faced by organizations like **Doctors Without Borders/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF)**.

With national governments ill-prepared and international bodies slow to mobilize, the immense burden fell squarely on the shoulders of **frontline responders**. Despite facing accusations of alarmism, teams from MSF, Samaritan's Purse, and numerous other medical professionals **remained steadfastly committed**. Their unwavering dedication was fueled by deep-seated **religious conviction**, an unshakeable sense of **civic duty**, and an **intrinsic drive to confront danger head-on**. Liberian nurse Iris Martor voiced her concerns about the disparity in international aid, while Foday Gallah, an ambulance driver who survived EVD, powerfully stated, "**I want to give my blood so a lot of people can be saved. I am going to fight Ebola with all of my might.**"

Salome Karwah, an MSF nurse’s assistant, became a profound symbol of dedication. She continued to care for patients even after losing both her parents to the virus and surviving it herself. "**It looked like God gave me a second chance to help others**," she reflected. The epidemic inflicted immense suffering, leaving countless children orphaned and families separated from dying loved ones by the sterile barrier of personal protective equipment (PPE). MSF president Dr. Joanne Liu poignantly lamented, "**You should not die alone with space-suit men.**" Individuals afflicted with Ebola endured **excruciating symptoms**. Karwah vividly described the pain as feeling like "**they are busting your head with an ax**." Dr. Kent Brantly, a survivor and medical missionary with **Samaritan's Purse**, characterized his harrowing experience as an "**incredible opportunity for redemption**."

The **critical lessons learned** from this devastating epidemic remain profoundly important. Global preparedness for future pandemics was demonstrably **inadequate**, a fact underscored by the **Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)**. The crisis exposed governmental corruption, pervasive complacency, and damaging inter-agency rivalries that significantly hampered effective response efforts. Challenges abounded, including the difficulty of securing borders and the urgent need to develop treatments for diseases disproportionately affecting impoverished nations. The death of Thomas Eric Duncan in Dallas and the subsequent infection of two nurses eroded public confidence in U.S. hospitals' capacity to manage such threats, consequently fueling widespread fear globally. Incidents such as the euthanasia of a dog in Spain and the confinement of hotel guests in Macedonia illustrated how fear could eclipse the necessity for rational, evidence-based solutions, a point astutely noted by the **World Economic Forum**.

As the world urgently required more medical volunteers, the **risks and logistical barriers** to their deployment escalated. The quarantine of nurse Kaci Hickox, who returned from Sierra Leone asymptomatic, ignited a crucial debate concerning public health policy and individual liberties. Hickox herself argued, "**It is crazy we are spending so much time having this debate about how to safely monitor people coming back from Ebola-endemic countries, when the one thing we can do to protect the population is to stop the outbreak in West Africa.**" The Ebola epidemic represented not only a devastating conflict but also a global warning. The existing global health infrastructure proved insufficiently robust to prevent the spread of new infectious diseases, a conclusion corroborated by a **RAND Corporation** report. This pervasive threat impacts **all populations**. Ultimately, the world's security hinged on a **dedicated cohort willing to confront the pathogen** directly, often at immense personal risk. For their extraordinary courage, profound compassion, and unwavering dedication, the Ebola fighters were rightfully recognized as TIME's 2014 Person of the Year.

**Nurses on the Ebola Frontline: Courage, Fear, and the Fight Against a Deadly Virus**

The Ebola epidemic in West Africa thrust nurses into an unprecedented battle, demanding immense courage and resilience. These healthcare heroes faced not only the direct threat of a deadly virus but also the societal impact of fear, stigma, and quarantine. This document explores the personal experiences of three nurses – Kaci Hickox, Iris Martor, and Nina Pham – to highlight their profound journeys and the broader implications of the epidemic.

**Kaci Hickox: Navigating Quarantine and Stigma After Serving in Sierra Leone**

Kaci Hickox, a nurse with Doctors Without Borders (MSF), became a focal point for quarantine debates in the U.S. after her deployment to Sierra Leone. In Bo, she managed a 35-bed Ebola Case Management Center. Her work involved grueling 12-to-14-hour shifts in extreme heat, wearing non-breathable Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). This gear, while vital, limited direct patient interaction and compassionate communication.

Hickox explained that Ebola care in West Africa prioritized meticulous hygiene and encouraging fluid and food intake, rather than solely intensive care. The emotional burden was significant. A particularly difficult moment involved informing a friend of his positive diagnosis from a distance, while in her regular clothes, to maintain eye contact. Despite his youth, he did not survive. However, moments of hope, such as celebrating patient recoveries with chlorine showers and applause, provided solace.

Upon returning to Newark Liberty International Airport, Hickox, expecting monitoring, was directed to a CDC quarantine office after informing an official of her work. Despite having no symptoms and wearing full PPE, she was told a quarantine was pending. She disputed a reported fever, attributing her flushed appearance to frustration. After evaluation and testing negative for Ebola, she was eventually released to a home quarantine in Maine, which a judge later overturned. Hickox's experience underscored the pervasive stigma driven by fear and her hope for compassion and knowledge to guide future responses. She remains willing to return to West Africa.

**Iris Martor: Providing Essential Community Care in Monrovia's Slums**

Iris Martor, a nurse at the More Than Me Academy in Monrovia's West Point slum, initially met skepticism about Ebola. The outbreak's reality became clear in June when Redemption Hospital reported cases, including healthcare workers, leading to widespread fear and avoidance of medical facilities. As hospitals closed, Ebola Treatment Units (ETUs) became the only option, but their capacity was quickly overwhelmed.

Martor saw the urgent need for intervention as people began dying from treatable illnesses. In August, her team started home-care visits to address a rise in child and adult deaths from malaria and other conditions. They assessed patients, arranged transports for suspected Ebola cases to ETUs, and investigated other illnesses. Her role shifted from school nursing to community safety, visiting students' homes and extending care to any household with a sick person.

Home care carried risks due to unknown environments. Martor emphasized treating any sick person as a suspect, maintaining distance, and avoiding contact. Her initial fear was overcome by her nursing oath and the dedication of international aid workers, motivating her as a Liberian to lead the effort for her people. She warned against complacency, noting that Ebola's continued presence in neighboring countries meant Liberia was not truly safe. Martor advocates for strong contingency plans based on lessons learned from the crisis.

**Nina Pham: A Nurse's Personal Encounter with Ebola**

Nina Pham, a nurse at Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital, learned about Ebola through media reports. Despite her fear, she accepted the assignment to care for patient Thomas Duncan, viewing nursing as a calling. She strictly followed PPE protocols during high-risk procedures, especially in the early days of his care.

Pham was surprised when her Chief Nursing Officer and a CDC officer, both in full protective gear, arrived, confirming the seriousness of the situation. Initially in disbelief, she contacted her family. The memory of Mr. Duncan's worsening illness intensified her fear. Pham hopes her experience will highlight the ongoing severity of the Ebola crisis in West Africa and the importance of global solidarity, advocating for mutual support over border closures.

“To have an African doctor, who grew up in a shantytown in probably one of the most disadvantaged countries in the world, on the cover of TIME magazine’s Person of the Year is the right thing to do,” says Jackie Nickerson, the fine-art photographer who shot the cover of TIME’s Person of the Year. This year, TIME chose to highlight the incredible work Ebola fighters are doing to bring to a stop an epidemic that has killed more than 6,000 people. TIME commissioned Nickerson and U.S. photographer Bryan Schutmaat to shoot more than 20 portraits in 12 locations around the world—from London to Geneva, Boston to Dallas, and all the way to Monrovia, Liberia. “I was working on a job in Paris when Kira Pollack and Paul Moakley [the director and deputy director of photography at TIME] called me,” says Nickerson. “They told me they had this assignment for me and asked if I could go to Monrovia. I immediately knew this was an once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to bear witness to this moment in history. There was no doubt in my mind that it was something I wanted to do.” Nickerson was surprised. “I had actually never worked for TIME before,” she says. “I had no idea what the commission process was, so this call came out of the blue.” Meanwhile, Schutmaat was in Amsterdam attending World Press Photo’s Joop Swart Masterclass when he received his phone call. “My schedule was very busy, and I thought [being in Europe wouldn’t work], but photo editor Natalie Matutschovsky, who’s been championing my work at TIME, just said: ‘That’s good, because two of the subjects we need you to shoot are in Europe already.’” The following week, he was on a flight to Geneva. Both photographers were selected for their strong sense of aesthetics, which come from their fine-art backgrounds. “They are celebrated artists,” says Pollack. “There is a heartbeat to Nickerson's portraits that lent itself to just the right mood for this project. She’s spent a considerable amount of time working throughout Africa. She is agile and informed on how to photograph in the harsh African light, and her portraits are honest and beautiful. Schutmaat's studied portraits have an almost painterly quality. There is something very telling about capturing a body posture or a simple gesture.” For weeks, TIME had been preparing for Nickerson’s assignment. “We talked to NGO workers, journalists and photographers who had been in the field before we decided to go ahead do this,” says Moakley. “We talked with the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and we packed everything we needed—including Personal Protective Equipment suits for all of us.” TIME’s Africa Bureau Chief Aryn Baker, who spent the summer covering the Ebola epidemic, met Nickerson and Moakley in Monrovia. “Aryn did a lot of the work ahead of time as well,” says Nickerson. “With Paul, she had selected the sitters, so we had a very good idea of who we were going to shoot, so that made things incredibly easy for me.” Few people would know Dr. Jerry Brown’s name. The Liberian doctor, who is featured on the first of five covers shot for TIME’s Person of the Year issue, opened his country’s first Ebola treatment center in early 2014, at a time when many of his colleagues failed to react to the growing epidemic. “There was a kind of gravity to the way Dr. Brown and his staff were working,” says Nickerson. “When we met [him], we had the idea to do something very simple against a plain color, something of a more formal portrait. And then, he invited us to go into the reception area where he gets dressed. It was a very simple, bare room. It had a single light bulb, and I just thought it captured the atmosphere and gravity of what they were doing.” The photograph is not a staged shot: It’s a portrait that was caught in the middle of Brown’s regular dressing procedure. Over four days, Nickerson, Moakley and Baker witnessed the commitment of dozens of health workers and body-retrieval teams. “Sometimes, we would be waiting to get access to someone and we’d be chatting to other people with incredible stories,” says the photographer. “It just never stopped. Their stories really touch you—the self-sacrifices that people are making. They are doing such a brilliant job.” In the U.S., meanwhile, Schutmaat was meeting with Dr. Kent Brantly. The 33-year-old physician with Samaritan’s Purse was the first American citizen to be diagnosed with Ebola while working in Monrovia. “Kent was doing a lot of hard, selfless work to help people out,” says Schutmaat.“I met him at his church in Fort Worth, Texas. TIME’s photo editors and I felt that since he was a man of faith and since he was guided by that faith, it would be good to photograph him in there.” Schutmaat had no idea then that it would be featured as part of the magazine’s Person of the Year franchise. “I just thought I was doing a big story on Ebola that would end up somewhere inside the magazine,” he says. “I didn’t think it would be such a huge deal.” Nickerson was similarly in the dark: “Kira Pollack had said it could be an important story, and I knew that Person of the Year happened around this time of the year, but I didn’t dare to hope because I think there had always been people of status on the cover, and I couldn’t believe it was going to be a non-famous, African doctor on the cover.” It was only when TIME’s photo editors ordered the final, high-resolution images that both photographers found out the real purpose of their work. “It’s probably the biggest privilege of my professional career,” says Nickerson. “There’s no question about it. Doing this whole story was a privilege.” “I’m honored,” adds Schutmaat. “And to know that the editors at TIME would think that my photos would stand up next to Nickerson’s is pretty awesome.” A sentiment Nickerson reciprocates. “Bryan’s a great photographer. I love his work. I’m really happy to be sharing this story with him.” Jackie Nickerson is a fine-art photographer based in Dublin, Ireland. She is represented by Jack Shainman Gallery . TIME last featured her work, Terrain , on LightBox . Bryan Schutmaat is a Austin-based photographer. He is represented by Sasha Wolf Gallery . Olivier Laurent is the editor of TIME LightBox. Follow him on Twitter and Instagram @olivierclaurent. Paul Moakley , TIME's deputy director of photography, and Natalie Matutschovsky , a senior photo editor at TIME, edited this photo essay.

Massoud Barzani, a prominent leader in Kurdish history, embodies the enduring image of Kurds as a resilient yet perpetually marginalized ethnic group—the world's largest without a sovereign nation. His father, Mustafa Barzani, a revolutionary who dedicated sixty years to the pursuit of a Kurdish state, briefly realized this ambition in 1946, the year Massoud was born. This short-lived independence was soon reabsorbed by Iran, compelling Mustafa's forces to recommence their protracted struggle for self-determination. Massoud's own inaugural visit to the White House in 2005, distinguished by his traditional Kurdish attire, mirrored that of his father, featuring a unique jumpsuit cinched below the chest, adorned with epaulets and a swirling red-and-white turban.

Within Iraq, a de facto state has remarkably emerged. In the rugged northern regions, the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) wields considerable authority, possessing its own military, legislature, border controls, and foreign policy, all overseen by President Massoud Barzani. His life story is intrinsically linked to the narrative of a people whose historical moment appears to be dawning. Kurdistan, the ancestral homeland, exists conceptually on maps and through the vibrant flags displayed by Kurds. The fact that a dependable ally in the campaign against ISIS is, in essence, an imaginary country, speaks volumes.

As ISIS rapidly advanced in June 2014, conquering Iraq's second-largest city and then turning towards Kurdish territories, a critical juncture was reached. Iraqi Kurdistan, despite possessing a functional military and a surging economy, found its stability suddenly imperiled. The Kurds, predominantly Sunni Muslims, adhere to a tolerant tradition that stands in stark contrast to the fundamentalist ideology of ISIS. Initially, the Kurdish fighters, known as peshmerga, were pushed back, outmatched by the heavily armed extremists. It was not until August 6, with ISIS artillery mere miles from the regional capital of Erbil, that Barzani issued a resolute command to "fight the terrorists to the last breath." By this time, the Pentagon had begun to provide crucial support, initiating U.S. airstrikes the following day. The stakes were immense; Barzani, leading a stateless people, found himself in a position to influence the future trajectory of Iraq and the broader Middle East.

Barzani's leadership has been a testament to navigating complex geopolitical fault lines. He abandoned formal education at sixteen to join the armed struggle, yet possesses fluency in Arabic and Farsi, alongside his native Kurdish, and a grasp of English. His traditional attire, while reflecting a deep connection to his heritage, has sometimes been perceived by reformists as indicative of a more feudal approach. The KRG's internal structure, with a nephew as Prime Minister and his son as intelligence chief, is a reality not lost on ordinary Kurds, who often speak with caution. Ayub Nuri, editor of the Kurdish news site Rudaw, observes that while Barzani faces criticism as a tribal leader, this characteristic is crucial for maintaining unity and ensuring survival within a tribal society.

The autonomy enjoyed by Iraqi Kurds since 1991, following the U.S.-imposed no-fly zone, has been a significant factor in their development. During the turbulent decade after the 2003 U.S.-led invasion, Barzani's government grew in strength as Baghdad's authority waned. The crisis of 2014 marked a new threshold. As Iraqi federal forces retreated from ISIS advances, Barzani directed his peshmerga to secure Kirkuk, an oil-rich city long considered Kurdish but contested by Sunni Arabs. This strategic move protected Kirkuk from ISIS and presented Baghdad with a challenge. Under the new Iraqi Prime Minister Haider al-Abadi, and with U.S. air support, Baghdad has begun to rally against ISIS. However, the Kurds remain at the forefront of the fight, a role that aligns with their distinct aspirations.

While ISIS leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi seeks to establish a caliphate, Barzani and his people ardently desire their own sovereign nation. Two days after al-Baghdadi proclaimed himself Caliph Ibrahim, Barzani announced that Iraqi Kurds would hold a referendum on independence within months, stating, "From now on, we won’t hide that that’s our goal." The potential emergence of a new Kurdish state, however, carries the risk of destabilizing the Middle East. For the present, the Kurds appear inclined to leverage their current position to enhance their standing within a unified Iraq, deferring the immediate pursuit of full independence. A significant development occurred on December 2, when Barzani approved a pact with the new Iraqi government, strengthening ties with Baghdad and resolving a dispute over oil exports. This agreement, strongly advocated by the U.S., highlights both the constraints faced by Barzani's government and his adeptness at navigating them.

The leadership of Iraqi Kurdistan possesses a history as challenging as its rugged terrain, with a people dispersed across four nations. Despite repeated betrayals by the international community, they now count the U.S., Israel, and Iran among their allies. Barzani has even cultivated a relationship with Turkey, home to a substantial Kurdish population, a country that has historically viewed its Kurds as a threat to national sovereignty. However, Turkey has increasingly recognized Iraqi Kurdistan as a valuable market, with annual trade reaching approximately $8 billion. In September 2012, Barzani solidified this alliance by addressing the convention of Turkey's ruling party, notably wearing a suit and tie. Two years later, he extended his reach to support Syrian Kurds defending Kobani. As Nuri reflects, "When I was a kid, it was forbidden in our house to say anything critical of his father. It’s not a blind love. We need a leader." Massoud Barzani inherited his leadership role by birthright, and through his strategic partnership with the U.S., he may ultimately fulfill a pivotal role not only for his own people but also for the broader region.

The **Ferguson uprising**, ignited by the August 2014 death of Michael Brown, fundamentally reshaped the **national conversation on racial injustice and policing**. What began as a local protest against the fatal shooting of an unarmed Black teenager by a white police officer rapidly evolved into a **significant social movement** that garnered international attention. This document explores the **origins of the Ferguson uprising**, its **profound impact on national discourse**, and the **diverse individuals who fueled its momentum**.

The initial gatherings on sidewalks, witnessing the immediate aftermath of the shooting, became potent symbols of grief and frustration. Johnetta Elzie, then 25, found her planned return to college postponed by the urgent need to document the unfolding events. She actively participated in marches, enduring tear gas and rubber bullets, and used social media to broadcast the situation in Ferguson globally. "I was just hoping someone would care," she recalled. While the recurring tragedy of unarmed Black men killed by police often resulted in fleeting public outrage, Ferguson marked a **turning point**. Despite the grand jury's decision not to indict Officer Darren Wilson, the protests coalesced into a movement that revitalized the national conversation on racial inequality and the justice system. "We made the world pay attention," Elzie stated, recognizing this as a **significant achievement in itself**.

These incidents, which might have otherwise remained localized, began to reveal a disturbing pattern. In late November, protests erupted in Cleveland after police fatally shot Tamir Rice, a 12-year-old Black boy, within moments of their encounter, mistaking his toy gun for a real weapon. Shortly thereafter, a New York City grand jury's decision not to indict the officer who fatally choked Eric Garner, an unarmed Black father of six suspected of selling loose cigarettes, ignited nationwide demonstrations. The chants and hashtags originating in Ferguson started to connect these disparate incidents, weaving them into a **cohesive narrative of systemic injustice**. Erica Garner, Eric Garner's daughter, declared, "This is not a black-and-white issue. This is a national crisis."

Protest emerged as a powerful mechanism for making the invisible visible. Thousands participated in demonstrations across the country and internationally. Protesters disrupted freeway traffic in Miami and Chicago, carried symbolic coffins across the Brooklyn Bridge, and engaged in confrontations with law enforcement in Berkeley, California. Students organized "die-ins" on college campuses from Boston to Baton Rouge to underscore the value of Black lives. The St. Louis Rams football team famously took the field with their hands raised in solidarity, a gesture that resonated even within Congress. President Barack Obama, often perceived as hesitant to address racial issues, was compelled to engage, meeting with Ferguson protest leaders and announcing initiatives aimed at enhancing police accountability. Senator Rand Paul also spoke out against the disproportionate incarceration rates of African Americans, advocating for criminal justice reform. Following the Garner grand jury decision, New York City Mayor Bill de Blasio emphasized, "People need to know that black lives and brown lives matter as much as white lives."

This outcry demonstrated a more focused intensity than the Occupy movement and a broader scope than the reaction to the Trayvon Martin case, yet it shared a similar raw and authentic fervor. The initial feelings of powerlessness experienced on a Missouri sidewalk soon inspired hope among those in positions of influence. Attorney General Eric Holder told TIME that Ferguson "could be one of those **seminal moments that transform the nation**."

**The Genesis of an Uprising**

Ferguson's history is characterized by racial stratification. In the mid-20th century, it was predominantly white, with a physical barrier separating it from the adjacent Black suburb. Decades of "white flight" altered the demographics, and by the time of the uprising, Ferguson was approximately 70% Black, featuring a diverse housing stock. Many long-term white residents remained unaware of the underlying racial tensions. Former Mayor Brian Fletcher expressed difficulty comprehending the protesters' grievances, stating, "They say they hate the police now, but I never heard about any racial problems, even from our African-American officials."

However, statistical data revealed a pattern of racial profiling that had long angered residents, even preceding the fatal shooting of Michael Brown and the heavily militarized police response. St. Louis alderman Antonio French, a community leader, highlighted the police response as a critical factor: "Every opportunity in those first 48 hours to be compassionate, they escalated the situation instead."

The protesters themselves played a role in the unfolding events. Some, like Dhoruba Shakur, a 24-year-old jazz drummer, viewed the shooting and subsequent police actions as a justification for unrest. He described the looting as "kind of beautiful," suggesting it was a response to the government failing its citizens. The ambiguities surrounding the shooting did little to quell the sense of injustice that fueled the confrontations. While Michael Brown had been involved in a theft and a physical altercation with an officer, the grand jury testimony offered no definitive account of the fatal encounter. Nevertheless, the fact that an unarmed Black man was killed by a white officer remained a **central grievance**. For many, the anger was difficult to reconcile with the destruction of businesses and police vehicles, and the use of Molotov cocktails.

Law enforcement officers face a challenging and dangerous profession, and in Ferguson, they were tasked with maintaining order in a volatile environment where any misstep could ignite further unrest. The demonstrations polarized the nation, with debates centering on whether blame lay with the officers or the protesters. As the immediate chaos subsided, the sustained effort to continue the struggle fell to activists like Johnetta Elzie. This group comprised a diverse array of individuals – poets, paralegals, students, professionals, affluent and unemployed, Black and white. They organized crowdfunding campaigns, community meetings, and even a mock trial at the courthouse where Dred Scott once sought his freedom. They continued to advocate through social media platforms like Twitter, which became a **vital tool for disseminating their message**.

Leaders like DeRay Mckesson, a 29-year-old educator from Minneapolis, emerged from outside St. Louis. After observing the initial protests from afar, he took a week's vacation to join the movement. He arrived with no connections but has since become a key figure, co-running a movement newsletter with Elzie that reaches 11,000 subscribers daily. "We will not be silent anymore in the face of oppression," Mckesson declared. "Silence can so easily look like comfort."

Dr. Kent Brantly, 33 Physician with Samaritan’s Purse Bryan Schutmaat for TIME I’ve thought a lot about the moment when I was infected with Ebola. I will never know for certain when it happened, but I do remember one overnight shift, about nine days before I got sick, a woman came into the ER with her daughter. When I went to check on her, she was very sick. At one point, the daughter took her mother into the bathroom because she had diarrhea. We needed to get her out of the ER and into our Ebola treatment unit, but her daughter was incredibly distrustful of the situation and of us. I had to counsel her extensively to reassure her that we were trying to do what was best for her mother. To have that conversation, I took off my mask, gloves and apron. I probably held her hands or put my arm around her shoulder, as I often do. I don’t think I was infected by her mother, but since the daughter had taken her to the bathroom, there’s a chance she didn’t wash her hands after helping her mom. The mother died by the morning, and a postmortem test showed she did indeed have Ebola. A little more than a week later, I woke up feeling a little warm. My temperature was 100.0—higher than normal but not too concerning. I took a rapid malaria test; it was negative—not a good sign. I called our team leader, who sent physician colleagues to my home in full protective gear. After two more negative malaria tests, I knew I would be in isolation for at least three more days. In the meantime, I grew sicker. My fever hit 104.9. We all hoped it could be dengue fever. On the fourth day the team leader came to my bedroom window with news. “Kent, buddy, we have your results. It’s positive for Ebola.” I didn’t know what to think. I just asked, “So what’s our plan?” This was July, and I’d been in Monrovia since October 2013. The first time I heard about the Ebola outbreak was at the end of March 2014, at a picnic for expatriates living in the area. Someone asked if I had heard about the outbreak in Guinea. I had not, but within a couple of months I was one of only two doctors in Monrovia treating Ebola patients—and at that point we had only one survivor. My wife Amber and I were both at the disadvantage of knowing how this illness can end. But even with the bad news, I felt strangely at peace. God blessed me with a peace that surpasses understanding. At some point, I was told about an experimental drug called ZMapp. It had worked on monkeys but had never been tested in humans. I agreed to receive it but decided that Nancy Writebol, a medical aide I worked with, should get it first, since she was sicker. I wasn’t trying to be a hero; I was making a rational decision as a doctor. Over the next couple of days, though, my condition worsened. The doctor decided to give me the drug too, and within an hour my body stabilized a bit. It was enough improvement for me to be safely evacuated to Emory University Hospital in Atlanta. Shortly after I arrived at Emory, Amber called me from a phone outside my room. I don’t really remember that conversation, I was so delirious, but she said to me, “We watched you walk off that ambulance.” I said, “You were watching me?” and she said, “Oh, Kent. The whole world was watching you.” I’ve had time to reflect on what happened to me. Am I the same person I was before Ebola? In a lot of ways, yes. I don’t live every moment with a conscious awareness of what I’ve been through. I still have the same flaws I did before. But I think whenever we go through a devastating experience, it’s not about there being some inherent redemptive narrative, but it is an incredible opportunity for the redemption of something. We can say, “How can I be better now because of what I’ve been through?” To not do that is kind of a shame. When I thank God for saving my life, I am not unique in that. If you watch videos of survivors in Liberia, so many of them thank God for saving their lives. I chose a career in medicine because I wanted a tangible skill with which to serve people. And so my role as a physician is my attempt to do that. I’ll probably get tired of talking about my experience some day, but I went to Liberia because I long felt it was my vocation to spend my career as medical missionary. Deep in the core of my heart, I still think that’s my calling. I don’t want to go on with life and forget this.—as told to Alexandra Sifferlin Dr. Jerry Brown, 46 Medical director and general surgeon at the Eternal Love Winning Africa [ELWA] Hospital in Monrovia and director of the ELWA 2 Ebola treatment center Jackie Nickerson for TIME I first heard about Ebola in March, when I was listening to the radio. That was late March. We had patients with Ebola presenting in Lofa County. In the counties that border with Guinea. So it was then that we really started panicking and thinking, Now that one of our counties is involved, what next? Sooner or later it might reach us, we thought. So from then on we started thinking about Ebola. The very first challenge I had as medical director at that time was that if we started having Ebola patients show up in our emergency room, well, “Where do we keep them, doctor?” The only place that came through my mind was our chapel, because we didn’t have the financial capability to construct something. Our intent was not to create a treatment unit but instead to transform the chapel into a holding unit such that whenever we had a patient in the emergency room and we had the suspicion of the person being a probable or suspected case, the person could be kept in the holding unit until the patient would be transported or transferred to the treatment unit. So that was our goal for having the chapel used initially. It was June 12, in the late evening after surgery. I had just left the OR when I saw a series of missed calls on the phone [from the Deputy Minister of Health] and decided to call her back. She said, “There are two patients that we have, they are Sierra Leoneans, and they visited one of the hospitals already, Redemption Hospital. A couple of their staff have already encountered them. But their presentation, their signs and symptoms, and from where they have traveled, tell me that they are probable cases and they most likely have Ebola. And the only place I thought about that we could keep them until we can have an investigation done is at your center.” The very first thing I felt was, this is going to be challenging to have those patients brought here. We had not attended to such patients before, so in the back of my mind I kept thinking, What would it be like, to have those patients here? From that day onward, I always had the fear of myself or one of my staff getting infected, and what would become of me thereafter. The first two patients were brought in an ambulance, with two of their relatives, in the back of the ambulance, along with the patients. The front of the ambulance had the driver and two health workers; one nurse and one physician assistant. What was shocking was that the nurse and the physician assistant never had any personal protective equipment. The only thing they had was gloves, and dressed in their ordinary scrubs. That was frightening. The two patients in the back of the ambulance, one had died on the way. And the other was lying there. And then a boy about the age of 13 and his brother, maybe around his late 20s, were both in the back of the ambulance. We could tell that they were Sierra Leoneans by their accents and the way they spoke. We were shocked. First question was why the health workers allowed the patients’ caregiver to ride in the ambulance with them. It all boils down to people not knowing what a disease Ebola actually was. So no one knew what was happening. And I tell you, if we had not done some training initially, and spoke about Ebola repeatedly during our regular devotions, it would have been a greater disaster in the ELWA hospital. Through the training a few persons had some knowledge of what Ebola was all about: the mode of transportation, the means of prevention and the signs and symptoms related to the disease. So the bulk of the workers at this institution knew a lot about it. So it was a pity to see those workers in the ambulance dressed as they were. One of them is said to have died of the disease later, the nurse. So initially, that is the situation we had. Dr. Kent Brantly and Dr. Debbie Eisenhut were the first two who were willing to dress in full PPE and took the patient down to the unit. It was very challenging to get the nurses on board the first day, though they had been trained. The patient died a few days later. Before the end of June, the chapel, where we only had six beds, could no longer take additional patients because it was full to capacity. The initial intent of the unit in the chapel soon changed from a holding unit to a treatment unit. The chapel then became the first treatment unit in Monrovia. So all the patients that were presenting with the symptoms [of Ebola] were all rushed here, to the chapel. [They] were dying every three, four, five days. Because of the rapid rate at which people were dying, you could easily find places to put someone else. By then the government in the middle of July created another ETU at JFK Hospital that became the second unit in Monserrado County, in Monrovia. When the chapel became full and we could no longer take additional patients, and more people were getting ill and dying, the ELWA management decided to turn over our newly built kitchen and laundry for the new hospital under construction to be transformed into a treatment unit. Thus ELWA 2. Within a week it was filled. We had 20 beds. Within one week we had more than 20 patients. Patients were now in the corridors and under the eaves of the building, and still patients kept pouring in daily. The outpatient department of the hospital was then turned over to be used as an extension of the unit, shutting down outpatient services to the public. When it all started, and we had a unit set up, my wife did warn me not to enter the unit. So the first time I did enter I didn’t tell her I had entered. But she knew I had been trained. I went home and said nothing to her. And the very first time she had a suspicion of me going to the unit was when Dr. Abraham Borbor, who was one of my mentors, got ill. And I had to go in and attend to him. I had been in the unit for two weeks without her knowing. And I went home one evening and started discussing a few things about Dr. Borbor. Borbor was my role model, especially when I was in medical school. How he was now ill, and how I was feeling so sorry for him, and how his condition was not improving despite what we were doing. And she paused, and asked, “You are not working in the unit. How do you know these things?” and I caught myself. And tried to change the topic. It went to something else, and she never noticed. But then she started having suspicions. I remember her saying, “I hope you are not being stubborn. I told you not to go to the ETU.” So I said, “Well, I will do my best.” I formed some excuse and we just abandoned the topic. A few days later she noticed changes in the color of my boxers from the bleach or chlorine solution used for disinfection when leaving the unit. And so she said, “Ah, what is this?” and so I had no option but to finally confess. It did not go down well with her. I apologized, and we kind of talked about it for some time, and then she accepted. What she said was, “I can’t stop you. I realize this is something you like, so I am not going to stop you. But just be careful. So when you are back home, change all your clothes in the garage before you enter the house. I do not want my children and I to get infected.” But before she said this, I never took any of my outfits back home. I had special scrubs that I kept at the hospital. Once I got to the hospital I changed completely into my scrubs. At the end of each work day, I changed back to my ordinary wear and went back home. It was challenging to have her to accept this. She was afraid of me getting infected. She later realized that I was safe, and we kept trusting God to remain safe prayerfully. —as told to Aryn Baker Dr. Mosoka Fallah, 44 An American-educated Liberian infectious-disease expert who returned to his country last year to help establish a school of public health and now leads the effort to find, monitor and isolate the contacts of Ebola victims Jackie Nickerson for TIME As an infectious-disease student [at Harvard] I studied emerging and re-emerging infectious disease, and Ebola was always what we were talking about. At the time I had come back to Liberia to work on a USAID project. It was March. Ebola was the only thing on my mind. It was already in Guinea. I understood the interaction between the people on the border, between Guinea and Liberia. And I knew that there was definitely going to be Ebola coming to Liberia. It was one thing reading about a disease and then being in a disease. Maybe I could have raised the alarm, but I didn’t. I regret that now. I regret that we didn’t stop it in Guinea. Then the cases came to Liberia from Guinea. It was March, the 24th or 25th. I called [Assistant Health Minister] Mr. Tolbert Nyenswah: “I’m hearing about this Ebola, how can I help?” Everyone was coming in and thinking about how to mount an effective response. It was a task force. It became much more personal for me, because one of the relatives of the [first person to be killed in the outbreak] in Lofa County had taken a taxi to Monrovia. And she slept in the Chicken Soup Factory [neighborhood]. I grew up there. My mom is there, all of my relatives are there. I said, “We’ve got to get our boots on the ground. We’ve got to go to Chicken Soup Factory.” And they said to me, “This is the task force. We construct policies and strategy.” And I said, “O.K., I’m going to go down to the county [level] where we can discuss boots on the ground.” I led a team to Chicken Soup Factory. The index case had already left. But there were contacts like the taxi driver. I talked to him. He was frightened. He said, “I tried to help this old lady. If I do have the disease, I am going to spread it.” We told the townspeople to keep an eye on him. [We said], “Don’t stigmatize him. Don’t let him on the street.” Our contacts officer would go and take his temperature every day. And after 21 days, he did not come down with the disease. That case had me running around recruiting and training people. And passing awareness. Then [in] April we saw the situation slow down, the number of cases drop. By the end of April, we weren’t getting any more cases. But I was a little bit worried. Given the fluid nature of our borders and the nature of Ebola, I was worried. I remember sending an email to a friend. “Very soon it will be in Liberia again,” I said, thinking of the increasing cases in Sierra Leone. Because when it hit Sierra Leone, it hit with so much force and ferocity. The cases mounted at an alarming rate. I didn’t know that was prophetic when I wrote that line to my friend. On June 27 we had a new case. I got a call from the Ministry of Health. And it was in New Kru Town [one of Monrovia’s most crowded slums]. The population density and the denial allowed the disease to escalate. A landlady we approached [to do contact tracing] said, “If anyone says they have Ebola in this house, I will give you a slap.” I just walked out. There was so much resistance, so much denial. As we tracked case by case, we got to know that the first index patient who died had gone to Redemption Hospital. He had come into contact with all the nurses. So we had to consider every nurse and doctor at Redemption a possible contact. Actually, I followed about 45 people that were listed as contacts. This nurse, Esther, had touched him, and she became infected. And Dr. Sam [Samuel Muhumuza Mutooro] from Uganda—he treated him, and he became infected. And then the man who took the index patient in the back of his car died, and the sister died. What we knew was only the tip of the iceberg. Even before the ambulance had gone [to take the first case for treatment], six people had died. There is a communal bathroom that all the houses use. It just blew our minds. All of a sudden we have this situation in New Kru Town, of all places. We were worried that very soon it would spread to West Point [another congested slum]. I was working to mobilize tracers, to train tracers. And there were contacts that we could not find. Contacts were becoming symptomatic and moving and exposing other contacts. Twenty-six people got infected by one person. And then the cases hit us so hard. We had contacts everywhere becoming symptomatic and generating more contacts. That’s when the tension was on. Seven days a week, 10 hours a day. And we were getting from 50 to 200 phone calls. The phone calls would come until midnight and start at 5 a.m. I couldn’t afford to turn the phone off. All through Ebola, I think I turned it off once or twice, and only when a friend said to me, “Go and rest. You look like a dead body right now. We don’t want you around reminding us of death.” And I slept for two days. Very soon I realized that the response is not a single piece. It has to be holistic. There has to be enough ambulances. There has to be enough case investigation teams. There has to be enough burial teams. The sick will be waiting for someone to die [in the Ebola treatment centers] so we can have space. Everything we did on Ebola was dependent on the ETUs [Ebola treatment units]. And we didn’t have enough. August and July were quite tough for us. People would die, and we were helpless. We just couldn’t do anything. I would have a contact tracer follow a family. The mother died. The sister died. The maid died. The wife died. The father died. And she [the contact tracer] would go there every day to do contact tracing on the symptomatic people. She would encourage them, but every time she came back, there would be one more body. We were left alone. I have to say that. I always say that in July and August we lost a good window of opportunity. If they had come in with the ambulances and the tents, we could have averted a lot of death. If everyone had rushed in and built another [treatment unit]. They took such a long time to be built. Meanwhile, people were dying. The bodies would accumulate in the street. The burial teams would go someplace for burial and they would be chased out. I was always afraid that Ebola would be in West Point. The unsanitary conditions. The population density. The fluid nature of the population. It was Aug. 12 that I got a call. [A volunteer] called me and said, “Mosoka, you better come to West Point.” He said, “There is one dead in the house with the door locked. And the other is sick and vomiting and toileting in the street.” I left everything and I ran to West Point. Nobody wanted to talk, nobody wanted to explain what was happening. I called a couple of leaders [from West Point], and they began to reveal to us that for the past two weeks there had been massive secret burial going on. They would take [the bodies] to an island across the bay and they would bury them. There was no investigation or contact tracing. That night I called the WHO Country Representative. I said, “Our worst nightmare has happened. West Point has Ebola.” The decision to open a [Ebola] holding center in West Point was a desperate decision. West Point is congested. There is no way you can walk in West Point without touching. You have the sick walking around, vomiting and toileting. I said, “The best we can do for West Point is to get the sick from the population.” The community didn’t understand. And then it was Aug. 19, the worst day of my life. It was a rainy day. That day alone we took six dead bodies from West Point. I think there was a lot of misinformation. There was still a lot of denial. And then [rioters] broke into the West Point [Ebola] center. They took mattresses. Where were those contacts? Where did they take the mattresses? There was a very big panic. The government had to deal with people running around with infected mattresses. They also knew that they had the secret burials. So the government quarantined West Point. Later, the government lifted the quarantine. The lesson we have to learn is that the government, before taking action, should consult with the community. I’m cautious about the declining number of cases because with Ebola, a single case is an epidemic. Before we were fighting a big war. Now it’s a guerrilla war and we’re fighting little pockets. If it comes back, it’s going to be even more intense. It only takes one case, and then all our achievements are reversed. On Tuesday, Nov. 11, we got a call that a prayer leader had died from Ebola. Thursday morning, they brought a casket to carry the body. There were people there rubbing oil on her and praying for her. Forty people. Children as young as 3, 4. All of them under a tent. So as I speak to you, three of the people that were in that room are in the [treatment unit] today. This is in New Kru Town, where it all started. That’s what I am trying to say. It came back. —as told to Aryn Baker Dr. Philip Ireland, 44 Liberian doctor at John F. Kennedy Medical Center in Monrovia, Liberia’s largest hospital Jackie Nickerson for TIME We had several cases of clinicians at the John F. Kennedy hospital who have come down with Ebola. Dr. [Samuel] Brisbane [and] physician’s assistant Stephen Vincent. And then I came down next. It was in a meeting [on July 24] when I had this splitting headache. I saw flashes of lightning. I have never had a headache like this in my entire life. And I knew something was very, very wrong. I did my temperature; it was 38.1 Centigrade [100.58 Fahrenheit]. And I said this sounds like, smells like Ebola. This can’t be malaria because I’ve had malaria a thousand times. And I know how it is. This is completely different. I called the chief medical officer, told him I wasn’t feeling so well. I told him, “Doc, I think I have been exposed to Ebola, and I want to be tested.” And he was instrumental on the next day to have me tested. The guy came in the house dressed in the entire space suit, into my room. They did the test. The next day we found out that I was Ebola positive. By day seven I could not feel my radial pulse. I was in early shock. I was really sick. Very, very ill. My colleagues said that if we don’t get him out of here [his home] today, he’s going to pass. And I felt that way for the first time on day seven. I felt I was in shock. If I didn’t get IV lines going I was probably going to die that day. So my fellow doctors, a pediatric resident at the hospital, and an ob-gyn resident, and also my wife and her brother, all of them were calling to see if they could get an ambulance. But in Monrovia there were no ambulances [available] at the time. So they found this ambulance that was having some parts replaced at a garage. They were repairing the breaks. And [one of the doctors] literally sat there and waited until this ambulance was repaired and brought to the house. Then we got to the hospital, to the Ebola treatment unit. I collapsed on the bed and passed out. I woke up and found out that they have given me three liters of IV fluids, saline. And I felt a little better. That night was the worst night at ETU. I had 46 episodes of diarrhea and 26 episodes of vomiting. I was in a sea of mess. The next day there was this physician’s assistant—I will never forget him. His act of love towards me, to wash me, was so much so that I will never forget it in my entire life. He cleaned me totally. He dressed me, put me in a clean bed. And I felt that was so, so, so nice. I really appreciated that. I felt so relieved. The very first night I woke up to some loud music. They had turned the SUV around and opened the doors and turned the music up very loud. Gospel music. And all the artists were singing, “You’re going to make it, you’re not going to die.” At that time I already had headaches; I felt like a nightmare. Like you had the speakers right [next] to my windows and boom, boom, boom. And then they had a mixture of different kind of people, like some people were in there with fever or malaria. We weren’t all Ebola patients. Some people had strength, and those who had the strength will have prayer services of what we call vigils. And there was singing and praise God the entire night. They were singing and praying the entire night. And that was tiring. They would sing and pray the entire night. After one night of that, [two infected doctors] said, “No, no more praying to God. If you want to talk to God, do it silently in your heart.” So they stopped the vigils. By day three, the morning of day three, I started to do some terrible, terrible hiccups [a symptom of advanced Ebola]. That was when the clinicians taking care of me thought I was going to die. And they communicated that with one another. In fact, at one time I was hiccupping with every breath. So they thought I wasn’t going make it. They were even discussing whether I would be cremated or buried. And the news circulated that selfsame day that I had passed. I was in bad shape at that time. But after that day, day four in the ETU, I started to get better. When they came in to check on me the next morning they met me standing. And they were very surprised. I stayed there getting better, getting better. I was actually in the Ebola Treatment Unit for 14 days. And by day 10 in that place, which was like 17 days of illness, I was feeling much better. There was no more diarrhea, I didn’t have fever. Now I did have complications. I had pneumonia, I had hallucinations of all kinds of different things. Besides the acute renal failure. So by day 17 I was much better. So the guy did another test and the test results came back negative. And then came day 14 [in the ETU]. I was told 20 minutes before I left the Ebola Center that I would leave the Ebola Center. They said, “Dr. Ireland, you have to take a chlorine bath.” And I said, “Is the water warm or hot?” And they said, “No, it’s cold.” And then I said, “I’m staying. I’m not going anywhere.” Where I was, you have mosquitoes flying all over the place and cockroaches. So I said, man, let me get out of here. So in the evening I mustered up enough courage to do the cold chlorine water bath. They had me strip in front of all the nurses and the physician’s assistants. And the male physician’s assistant had to ask them to leave to give me some privacy. I had two buckets of the chlorine water. Very cold, icy water. And then I put on this T-shirt, sweatpants. And I had to go to the spray where I got sprayed thoroughly. In my ears, in my face, with my clothes on. When I left I was so wet. I was drenched with chlorine water. And I came outside. Now you have to bear in mind I am very, very fragile and weak. I’m still sick. I’m just glad I made it, that’s all. And I walk out and there are people singing all around me. The nurses were singing with their beautiful—they were singing and so happy. I was being released at the same time as a nurse. Nurse Barbara. And we had people from JFK there, my family, my elder brother was there, my wife was there. We had a lot of other doctors that were there. We had members of the press. A lot of people had turned out. And I felt like Nelson Mandela. I always use that description. It felt like the Long Walk to Freedom. And even though it was a short walk, for me, because the energy I had to expend because I was so weak, it was like a walk to freedom. And I walk up and I raised my hands to heaven, thanking God for saving my life. And then I noticed something also. There were a lot of crying people, people happy to see me. And when I got close to anybody, they actually backed away. —as told to Aryn Baker

## Putin's Crimea Annexation: A Symbol of Russian Resurgence and Imperial Ambition

In 2014, Vladimir Putin's annexation of Crimea from Ukraine marked a pivotal moment, representing Russia's first significant territorial gain since the Soviet Union's dissolution. This action resonated deeply with many Russians, tapping into a desire for renewed national pride and echoing historical ambitions of territorial expansion that shaped Russia into the world's largest nation.

For Putin and a substantial segment of the Russian population, the collapse of the Soviet Union was viewed not as liberation, but as a profound national tragedy. Putin himself expressed this sentiment, stating, "Millions of Russians went to sleep in one country and awoke in another." This abrupt shift diminished Russia's superpower status, reducing it to what he described as a "corrupt petrostate." The annexation of Crimea, a peninsula comparable in size to Massachusetts, provided Putin a means to restore a measure of Russia's former global standing. As observed by Gorbachev, by acting unilaterally, free from U.S. dominance and international legal norms, Putin managed to reclaim a portion of Russia's honor. This perspective was widely shared by the majority of Russians, reflected in Putin's approval ratings, which climbed to 88% following the annexation.

The acquisition of Crimea, a region slated for over $18 billion in Russian development over the subsequent six years, seemed to elevate Putin to an almost mythical status among his people. Domestic opposition diminished, while thousands of supporters enthusiastically purchased Putin-themed merchandise in Red Square. The very name "Crimea" became a potent symbol of national resurgence in Russia, reigniting aspirations for imperial glory. Prominent Russian sociologist Lev Gudkov commented, "It had been extremely painful. Only with the annexation of Crimea did people start to feel that our great-power status was restored."

However, Putin's assertive efforts to reassert Moscow's influence over Ukraine in 2014 came at a considerable cost to Russia and the international community. The downing of Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 over eastern Ukraine on July 17, which claimed nearly 300 lives, was almost certainly linked to poorly trained militias supported by Russia. Already ostracized from the G-8 in March due to the Crimea annexation, Putin faced further isolation at the G-20 summit in Australia. Canadian Prime Minister Stephen Harper directly confronted Putin, stating, "I guess I’ll shake your hand, but I have only one thing to say to you: You need to get out of Ukraine."

Consequently, Russia has grappled with a surge in inflation, and the ruble has depreciated by 40% against the dollar since the beginning of the year. This economic strain has forced Russians to reduce spending on Western goods and international travel, with the government forecasting a recession for the upcoming year. In early December, Putin was compelled to abandon the South Stream natural gas pipeline project, a key initiative intended to supply Europe, citing pressure from E.U. regulators. "If Europe doesn’t want to do this, well, then it means this won’t be done," Putin stated, adding, "We will steer the flow of our energy resources to other regions of the world." The central question remains: was Putin's pursuit of imperial ambition worth the detriment to Russian prosperity? For those who harbor historical grievances, the answer appears to be affirmative.

query: What significant discovery did the New Horizons spacecraft make about Pluto's size compared to previous estimates?

documents.text:
The NASA spacecraft, New Horizons, is poised to make a historic flyby of Pluto, hurtling past the dwarf planet at an astonishing speed exceeding 45,000 kilometers per hour. This pioneering mission, the first to reach Pluto on the distant fringes of our solar system, has already delivered groundbreaking revelations. Fresh measurements transmitted from New Horizons indicate that Pluto is significantly larger than previously estimated by scientists. The dwarf planet now measures 2,370 kilometers in diameter, a size comparable to approximately two-thirds of Earth’s moon.

Dr. Alan Stern, the principal investigator for the ambitious $700 million mission, explained that these newly acquired dimensions suggest Pluto likely possesses a greater abundance of ice and a lesser proportion of rock beneath its surface than anticipated by researchers. Accurately measuring Pluto from Earth has historically presented considerable challenges due to its immense distance and the atmospheric distortions that can create optical illusions for ground-based telescopes. Complementing these size revelations, other sophisticated instruments aboard New Horizons have definitively confirmed the presence of an icy cap adorning Pluto’s north pole.

The most recent data transmitted to Earth from the intrepid probe has successfully identified distinct chemical signatures of methane and nitrogen ice within this polar cap. An initial image, received by NASA just last week, presented Pluto as a captivating, orangey sphere distinguished by a prominent, luminous spot remarkably shaped like a heart. Subsequent, more detailed images have unveiled a dramatic landscape featuring imposing cliffs, extensive craters, and vast chasms that dwarf even the Grand Canyon. Dr. Stern expressed his profound excitement, stating, "The scientific discoveries we have already achieved are incredibly tantalizing. The Pluto system is utterly captivating in its profound strangeness and its truly alien beauty."

New Horizons is scheduled to execute its momentous flyby at precisely 12:49 PM BST on Tuesday. However, the scientific community will need to exercise patience, as confirmation of the probe's survival and the initial data transmission will not occur until 2:00 AM BST on Wednesday, when contact is re-established with Earth. The primary threats to New Horizons during its encounter are minuscule dust particles, which have been dislodged from Pluto's moons by meteorite impacts and are now trapped in orbit. While a collision with a dust particle as small as a grain of rice could potentially incapacitate the spacecraft, the probability of such a catastrophic event is remarkably low, estimated at approximately one in 10,000.

The New Horizons spacecraft embarked on its extraordinary nine-year, 4.8 billion-kilometer odyssey to Pluto in January 2006. This journey culminated in reaching the last known world in our solar system to be visited by a spacecraft. Onboard this remarkable vessel are seven highly advanced scientific instruments, alongside the ashes of Clyde Tombaugh, the dedicated astronomer who first discovered Pluto in 1930.

It is noteworthy that when the probe initially launched, Pluto was classified as the ninth planet in our solar system. However, a mere seven months later, astronomers at the International Astronomical Union reclassified the icy celestial body as a dwarf planet. This reclassification was based on the criterion that Pluto does not sufficiently dominate its orbital region in the same manner as the other major planets.

As the spacecraft navigates its rapid trajectory past Pluto and its five recognized moons – Charon, Hydra, Nix, STyx, and Kerberos – it will capture an extensive array of photographs. These images will provide humanity with its very first close-up perspective of the mountains, valleys, and the delicate atmosphere of this enigmatic world, particularly as the sun rises and sets behind it. It is even conceivable that the instruments aboard New Horizons might detect evidence of snowfall on this diminutive world. NASA officials anticipate the release of the initial images from the flyby on Wednesday evening. These snapshots, captured by the onboard cameras, will resolve details as small as 100 meters across, representing a monumental leap in clarity compared to the images obtained during the approach phase, which could only discern features approximately 15 kilometers in size.

Nevertheless, scientists face an extended period of anticipation to fully comprehend the wealth of information that New Horizons will gather. The sheer volume of data collected during the Pluto encounter will necessitate a lengthy 16-month transmission period to relay it all back to Earth. This mission signifies the culmination of NASA's ambitious endeavor to explore every planet within our solar system, an undertaking that commenced with the exploration of Venus in 1962. Intriguingly, Tuesday's historic flyby coincides with the 50th anniversary of the Mariner 4 probe's pioneering fly-by of Mars.

The **New Horizons spacecraft** is poised to achieve a **historic milestone**: a close flyby of **Pluto**, the dwarf planet it has journeyed to for over nine years. This **NASA probe** represents the **first spacecraft ever to visit Pluto**, with its closest approach scheduled for tomorrow, July 14th, at 7:49 a.m. EDT. At its nearest point, New Horizons will be a mere **7,800 miles from Pluto's surface**. Even from its vast distance of 4.67 billion miles, this mission promises to **reveal secrets relevant to our own planet, Earth**. By examining celestial bodies like Pluto, scientists can gain **crucial insights into Earth's formation and evolution**. Here are five key ways studying Pluto can deepen our understanding of Earth:

### Unlocking Secrets of Early Planetary Formation

Earth and its planetary neighbors likely formed through the **collision and merging of smaller celestial bodies**. Early in Earth's history, a massive impact event, possibly involving a Mars-sized object, is thought to have nearly destroyed our planet, with debris eventually forming the Moon. The inner solar system, around 4 billion years ago, was a highly chaotic region, with Jupiter's gravity flinging asteroids and comets inward. In contrast, the Kuiper Belt, where Pluto resides, provided a more **stable environment for icy and rocky materials**. Observing Pluto and similar Kuiper Belt Objects offers a glimpse into the solar system's ancient conditions, helping us understand the processes that shaped our own planet.

### Investigating the Origins of Earth's Water and Life

Recent findings challenge the long-held belief that comets were the main source of Earth's water and essential life molecules. Data from the Rosetta mission suggested that water on Comet 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko had a different deuterium-to-hydrogen ratio than terrestrial water, pointing to an **alternative origin for Earth's water**. The solar system contains numerous icy bodies, including moons, dwarf planets, and parts of Mars. Scientists hypothesize that Pluto may also hold significant ice deposits, and **analyzing Pluto's ice could refine our models of water distribution** across the solar system. Furthermore, carbon is a **vital element for the emergence and sustenance of life on Earth**. The potential discovery of **organic molecules in other extraterrestrial locations** is a significant scientific pursuit. While not all organic compounds are biological, they are considered the building blocks of life. The question of whether organic molecules exist on Pluto's cold, seemingly barren surface is particularly intriguing. Organic compounds have been found in challenging environments like Mercury and Comet 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko, suggesting their possible presence on Pluto. The existence or absence of organic material on Pluto could offer **critical clues about the initial development of life on Earth**.

### Deciphering Atmospheric Dynamics and Solar Activity

Pluto possesses a **unique atmospheric structure**. Due to its weak gravity, Pluto's thin atmosphere extends much farther into space than Earth's. Some researchers propose that Pluto's atmosphere might even freeze and settle on its surface during colder periods. Currently, as Pluto orbits closer to the Sun, its atmospheric gases are expanding. By studying the atmospheric processes of other celestial bodies, astronomers can **improve their understanding of similar phenomena on Earth**. For instance, Venus's extreme surface temperature, driven by a runaway greenhouse effect, has provided invaluable data for comprehending global warming on Earth. Life on Earth fundamentally relies on the Sun, our closest star, which is the **primary source of our planet's heat**. The Sun's influence reaches Earth's upper atmosphere, where solar flares release charged particles (coronal mass ejections) that interact with atmospheric molecules, creating auroras. While auroras are harmless, the Sun's radiation can also disrupt terrestrial power grids and satellite operations during geomagnetic storms. Scientists are eager to observe how the Sun interacts with atmospheres throughout the solar system, including at Pluto. These observations will **enhance our ability to predict the impacts of intense solar activity**, from the aftermath of solar flares to the Sun's broader influence on Earth's climate.

The New Horizons spacecraft is on the cusp of a historic milestone in our Solar System's exploration: a monumental encounter with Pluto. This advanced probe is entering the most critical and thrilling phase of its mission to the distant dwarf planet. Mission controllers have confirmed the spacecraft's readiness, with its antenna precisely aimed at its targets. This strategic positioning temporarily halts direct communication with Earth, enabling New Horizons to dedicate all its observational power to this pivotal moment. This period of silent data acquisition is set to conclude shortly after midnight GMT on Wednesday. Following this, mission control will eagerly await a signal from the spacecraft, which is nearly five billion kilometers away.

This flyby of Pluto, a dwarf planet with a diameter of approximately 2,370 kilometers, marks a profound achievement. Its successful completion will mean that all nine "classical" planets in our Solar System have now been visited by a human-made probe. This signifies that every major celestial body, from Mercury to Pluto, will have been explored by our technology. While New Horizons has already transmitted fascinating information about Pluto and its system, this close encounter promises an unprecedented wealth of observational data, far exceeding anything gathered previously. The scientific community anticipates a deluge of new insights into this enigmatic world.

The Pluto flyby involves several key details that highlight the mission's precision and ambition. The probe will pass a mere 12,500 kilometers from Pluto's surface, offering an incredibly close perspective. The moment of closest approach is scheduled for 11:50 GMT. Beyond Pluto itself, the mission also aims to thoroughly study Pluto's five moons. These include the substantial moon Charon, which is nearly half the size of Pluto, as well as the smaller moons Styx, Nix, Kerberos, and Hydra. New Horizons will execute a series of rapid, intricate maneuvers designed to capture high-resolution images and gather a diverse array of scientific measurements.

Alan Stern, the principal investigator for New Horizons, eloquently expressed the team's profound anticipation. "I can't wait to get into the data and really start making sense of it," he stated. "Right now, we're just standing under the waterfall and enjoying it." This sentiment captures the excitement and awe surrounding the culmination of years of planning and travel.

The precise positioning of the spacecraft for this encounter represents an immense engineering feat. The entire observation sequence is automated, requiring New Horizons to adhere to a pre-programmed, highly accurate trajectory. The spacecraft is aimed at a minuscule "keyhole" in space, measuring approximately 100 by 150 kilometers. Achieving this specific point within a narrow 100-second window was absolutely essential for the mission's success. This remarkable precision was accomplished after an arduous nine-and-a-half-year journey across the vast expanse of the Solar System.

The mission team will be anxiously awaiting confirmation of New Horizons' reconnection with Earth, scheduled for 00:53 GMT on Wednesday. This initial contact will provide a crucial status update on the probe's condition, allowing controllers to verify the successful completion of the flyby.
